Thanks once more to everyone that bought the book and helped send a strong signal to the world that Rails is real.
posts.key_insight: The rapid commercial success of the first Rails book served as early mainstream validation that the framework had real momentum beyond the early-adopter community.

posts.summary: DHH praises fellow Dane Rasmus Lerdorf's views on immediacy in technology, specifically his comparison of REST vs SOAP web services. Lerdorf argues that REST's simplicity and directness make it far superior to SOAP's painful complexity. DHH connects this to why HTML succeeded: immediacy should be a prime directive for any standard seeking wide adoption. The post is a brief endorsement of simplicity over enterprise complexity, framed through national kinship with the PHP creator.
key_points: REST's simplicity lets you test queries directly in a browser, while SOAP adds painful layers of complexity
Immediacy should be a prime directive for any standard or platform seeking wide adoption
The same quality of immediacy that made HTML flourish applies to REST over SOAP
DHH maintains respect for Rasmus Lerdorf and PHP despite having personally moved away from the language
money_quotes: SOAP always reminds me of being stuck behind the guy in a hat driving a Lincoln Towncar. You eventually get to where you want to go, but the journey is painful.
With REST you can just toss your query into your browser and have a look at the returned XML.
This is the sort of stuff that makes my brain hurt.
Immediacy should be a prime directive for any standard and platform that seeks wide adoption.
Rasmus is the king of immediacy.
posts.key_insight: Technologies that prioritize immediacy — letting developers see results quickly and directly — are the ones that achieve wide adoption, which is why REST triumphed over SOAP.

posts.summary: DHH shares news of a full-page portrait in the Danish Computerworld magazine that tells his origin story: transitioning from gaming journalism to web development through 37signals. He connects his own journey to Chad Fowler's concept of 'love it or leave it' from the book 'My Job Went To India,' describing how passionate obsession with a craft—not career ambition—drives meaningful work. He endorses Fowler's book as codifying the instinctual techniques that shaped his own path.
key_points: DHH's career trajectory went from gaming journalism to web development after getting involved with 37signals
The 'love it or leave it' philosophy frames passion-driven work as addiction in a good way—you pursue it because you can't not
Chad Fowler's transition from classical music to programming mirrors DHH's own experience of creative obsession transferring between domains
The reward is the work itself, not career advancement or external validation
money_quotes: That's how it becomes all worth it. The long nights, the single-track focus, and eyes on the work itself as the prize.
I was in it because I couldn't not be.
My drive to create had been ignited in much the same way that it had when I started writing classical music or playing improvisational jazz.
posts.key_insight: The most meaningful work comes not from career ambition but from an irresistible creative obsession where the work itself is the reward.

posts.summary: DHH announces his appearance on the Danish newz.dk podcast where he discussed winning the Best Hacker award, explained Ruby on Rails, and shared future plans. The post is a brief link-sharing announcement with no substantive argument or analysis. It simply directs readers to the audio in MP3 or OGG format, noting the conversation is entirely in Danish.
key_points: DHH won a Best Hacker award and discussed it on the podcast
The interview covered the what and why of Ruby on Rails
The conversation was conducted entirely in Danish
money_quotes: I had a chat with Peter Mønnike from newz.dk yesterday about winning the Best Hacker award, the what and why of Ruby on Rails, future plans, and more.
posts.key_insight: A brief announcement post linking to a Danish-language podcast interview about DHH's Best Hacker award and Ruby on Rails.

posts.summary: DHH argues that open source contributions provide a far superior hiring signal than traditional interviews, CVs, and credentials. He describes conventional hiring as a 'crap shot' where companies bet on abstract indicators and biased bios. Using 37signals' hire of Jamis Buck as an example, he shows how following someone's open source work reveals their actual craftsmanship over time. He concludes that open source benefits both employers (reducing hiring risk) and programmers (letting work speak for itself).
key_points: Traditional hiring relies on unreliable signals like CVs, short interviews, and credentials, making it an unnecessarily risky gamble
Open source contributions let employers evaluate candidates over extended periods using real work output
37signals hired Jamis Buck based entirely on his open source releases and Ruby community participation, ignoring traditional credentials
Secondary factors like GPA, location, and years of experience become irrelevant when actual work is available to evaluate
Open source creates a two-sided advantage: companies pick better and programmers can prove themselves through craft rather than credentials
money_quotes: In the wake of open source, traditional hiring practices seem like an unnecessarily risky way to hire new employees.
Why bet the composure of your collective on abstract indicators, hearsay, and a biased bio?
It's a lot easier to hire someone than to fire them, so most companies would just keep the outcome of their crap shot.
I can't imagine hiring someone that I didn't know through open source.
I would consider it irresponsible to endanger the composition of 37signals by bringing someone on in the same manner I have personally been hired a good number of times.
Who cared about his GPA (or if he even went to college)?
Stop optimizing the secondary factors and focus on what it's all supposed to be about: the craftsmanship.
posts.key_insight: Open source contributions are the most reliable hiring signal because they let you evaluate a candidate's actual craftsmanship over time, making traditional credentials and interviews obsolete.

posts.summary: DHH announces 37signals' forthcoming book 'Getting Real,' sharing its table of contents. He frames the book not as a formal methodology but as a collection of guiding stories, values, and short essays about how 37signals approaches software and business development. The post manages expectations by emphasizing the book's informal, practical nature. He closes with a satirical jab at business jargon, mocking the kind of buzzword-laden descriptions the book explicitly avoids.
key_points: Getting Real is being published as a book after being discussed as an approach for some time
The book is explicitly not a methodology or formal process — it's a collection of essays and values
DHH actively sets expectations low and practical rather than overpromising
The satirical closing mocks corporate buzzword culture, implicitly positioning 37signals as its antithesis
money_quotes: This is not a methodology or even much of a process.
Getting Real is merely a set of guiding stories, values, and thoughts on software and business development.
It's an attempt to cram a lot of short essays of advice into a big pot that'll hopefully convey a somewhat coherent image of how, why, and when we do things at 37signals.
In short, it's for holistic managers of tomorrow's gazelle sprinters in the new economy who aims to attain shoutability, best-practice patterns, and value-enhancing B2C leaps.
Just kidding.
posts.key_insight: Getting Real is positioned as the anti-methodology — practical stories and values over formal process, delivered with self-aware humor that rejects corporate buzzword culture.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates eBay's $2.6 billion acquisition of Skype, particularly because his friend Malthe, Skype's head of design, holds equity in the company. He expresses excitement at personally knowing someone involved in such a massive buyout. The post ends with a tongue-in-cheek reference to wanting a similarly inflated valuation for his own ventures.
key_points: eBay acquired Skype for $2.6 billion, which DHH considers extraordinary
DHH's friend Malthe, a fellow Dane, was Skype's head of design with equity
The post reflects the mid-2000s tech acquisition boom and startup culture excitement
DHH humorously hints at wanting similar valuations for his own work (37signals/Rails)
money_quotes: But even more exciting is that Malthe is riding that windfall as head of design with equity.
I've never known someone personally that had a share in such an incredible buyout.
Now where's my 371 P/E evaluation...
posts.key_insight: The thrill of a massive tech acquisition becomes personal when someone you know is riding the windfall.

posts.summary: DHH responds to Mena Trott of Six Apart's defense of building big companies, arguing that the internet age has made it possible for small teams to accomplish extraordinary things. He rejects the framing that entrepreneurs must choose between a lifestyle business and a VC-funded large operation. While acknowledging Mena's right to choose scale, he insists that bigness is now optional, not required, for making a significant impact.
key_points: The internet, cheap infrastructure, and productive development tools have given small teams unprecedented power to build big things
The false dichotomy between 'lifestyle business' and 'VC-powered large team' is frustrating and misleading
Being big to do big is now optional, not necessary — Six Apart chose scale but didn't have to
Entrepreneurs and customers alike should reject the assumption that size equals impact
money_quotes: Never before in the history of the world has the potential to do extraordinary things been this big for the smallest of teams.

The reach of the net, the low/no-cost of infrastructure, and rise of fiercely-productive environments have empowered those with passion near superhuman strength in business, by last-century standards.
It's no longer necessary to be big to do big. It's optional.
Don't buy it if you're a customer. Don't buy it if you're an entrepreneur in the making.
Mena and crew chose to do so, but they had every opportunity to have done it differently.
posts.key_insight: The internet economy has made scale optional — small teams can now achieve outsized impact without VC funding or large headcounts.

posts.summary: DHH observes that very few interesting web startups come from Europe despite the continent's strong social safety nets, which should theoretically encourage risk-taking. He uses Rails adoption numbers as a concrete example, noting France has only 5 professional Rails users versus 130+ in the US. His provocative hypothesis is that Europe's generous welfare state actually breeds complacency rather than entrepreneurship. He argues that since modern web startups are driven by passion rather than capital, Europe's problem is motivational, not financial.
key_points: Europe's strong social safety nets paradoxically discourage entrepreneurial risk-taking rather than enabling it
Rails adoption numbers reveal a stark US-Europe gap in web technology innovation
The primary driver of web startups is passion, not capital — and Europe lacks that entrepreneurial fire
Comfort and stability can be enemies of ambition and innovation
money_quotes: Europe affords its citizens an impressive social security net, which you should think would make people more willing to take a risk of doing it on their own. But its not.
We have it all too cozy.
There's not a lot of incentive to "get out of the ghetto" when the governmental care is so good.
It's too easy to remain complacent in a stable job at a stable company that's not your own.
Europeans simply lack the motivation to reach higher for themselves.
It doesn't help much that the requirement for capital expenditure is plummeting when the primary driver of web startups is passion.
Europe needs to wake up from the haze, ignite the passion, and make it more desirable to reach for the stars.
posts.key_insight: Europe's generous social safety nets, counterintuitively, may suppress entrepreneurial ambition by making comfortable employment too easy to settle for.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates a backhanded milestone: Ruby on Rails appearing as a bullet point on a corporate job listing. The job posting absurdly lumps Rails alongside J2EE and Websphere, demanding 5 years experience with 'the latest and greatest tools' and the ability to construct 'Enterprise wide Java solutions from whitepapers.' DHH finds it hilariously ironic that Rails has been adopted by the very corporate recruiting culture it stands against. He treats this as proof that Rails has broken through the mainstream awareness barrier.
key_points: Rails appearing on corporate job listings signals mainstream technology adoption, even if the context is absurd
The job posting perfectly captures clueless enterprise recruiting: demanding experience with contradictory technologies and buzzword soup
The phrase 'constructing Enterprise wide Java solutions from whitepapers' epitomizes the bloated enterprise culture Rails was built to reject
True technology awareness is reached when even people who don't understand a tool start requiring it
money_quotes: On the ladder of corporate acceptance, few achievements rank higher than to bestowed the honor of becoming a bullet point on the recruiting check list.
Should have experience constructing Enterprise wide Java solutions from whitepapers.
Rails has truly broken through the awareness barrier when its included as part of a job application that's the antithesis of everything Rails is about.
Clueless recruiters of the world, I salute you!
Should have ideally 5 years experience with all of the latest and greatest tools out there. The more the better.
posts.key_insight: A technology has truly gone mainstream when it gets mindlessly listed on corporate job postings by recruiters who have no idea what it actually represents.

posts.summary: DHH criticizes Microsoft's Atlas Ajax framework, announced at PDC 2005, as a late and inferior clone of the open-source Prototype and script.aculo.us libraries already bundled with Rails. He highlights that a two-line Rails auto-completion feature required a 23-page tutorial in Atlas, and that the framework couldn't even handle workarounds for Microsoft's own browser. He argues Microsoft should have contributed to the existing MIT-licensed open-source projects rather than poorly reinventing them. The post positions Rails' Ajax ecosystem as the de-facto standard that everyone else, including .NET's MonoRail, had already adopted.
key_points: Microsoft's Atlas framework is dismissed as a late, verbose, buggy imitation of Rails' existing Ajax libraries
A two-line auto-completion in Rails required a 23-page tutorial in Atlas, illustrating the complexity gap
Prototype and script.aculo.us are MIT-licensed, making Microsoft's decision to clone rather than contribute nonsensical
Other frameworks including .NET's own MonoRail had already adopted the Rails Ajax libraries, further isolating Atlas
money_quotes: Atlas is a late, verbose, buggy, and under-whelming stab at catching up to yesterday.
While Microsoft may have gotten the ball rolling back in the day, they certainly can't keep up with it any more.
Why go through the trouble of recreating these two de-facto standards for Ajax development when you could have been part of the originals? They're even under the MIT license, for crying out loud!
Every Rails-inspired framework under the sun have already joined up behind the path we set.
If it wasn't so funny, I'd be worried.
Not even .NET developers deserve this cruel and unusual punishment.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's attempt to build a proprietary Ajax framework was pointless when superior open-source alternatives already existed under permissive licenses and had become the industry standard through Rails.

posts.summary: DHH announces that RubyConf '05 has sold out at 195 attendees, more than tripling the previous year's turnout. He frames the sellout as evidence of Ruby's rapidly growing popularity and momentum. The post closes with a bold prediction that RubyConf '06 will draw 800 attendees, signaling his confidence in Ruby's explosive trajectory.
key_points: RubyConf '05 sold out at 195 attendees, tripling the prior year's numbers
DHH frames the sellout as proof that Ruby is 'burning-red hot' as a technology
He makes a bold prediction of 800 attendees at the next year's conference, quadrupling again
money_quotes: There's certainly no mistaking that Ruby is burning-red hot.
I lavishly predict that we'll see 800 people at RubyConf '06. Watch it happen.
posts.key_insight: The tripling of RubyConf attendance to 195 signaled the beginning of Ruby's breakout moment, and DHH wasn't shy about predicting even more explosive growth ahead.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the shift from proprietary to open source software requires a corresponding cultural shift in how people seek support. Since open source maintainers are volunteers with no contractual obligation to help, users must replace the entitlement of paid customer relationships with basic courtesy and kindness. He acknowledges the trade-off: you often get better information from passionate volunteers, but lose the leverage of being a paying customer. The post frames politeness as the new currency of the support transaction.
key_points: Open source support is a samaritan transaction, not a commercial one — the leverage dynamics are fundamentally different
Users often get better information from passionate volunteers than from paid vendor support
Being rude or entitled in open source communities is self-defeating because maintainers have zero obligation to help
Kindness and courtesy are more effective than money at unlocking help from open source communities
money_quotes: It's a trade-off where you often get better-than-before information, but have to give up your I-can-scream-at-you-because-I-pay privileges.
It seems obvious that you can't invoke the same instincts in a samaritan transaction as you would in a commercial transaction.
The people you are asking for help have no external obligations to assist you.
Don't expect anyone to help you if you're acting like an ass.
Unlike the commercial world, you have to at least act like a decent human being when you ask the open source community for help.
Since you can't say please with dollar bills, try words. You'd be surprised how well and often they work ever better than green.
posts.key_insight: Open source replaces commercial leverage with social leverage, making kindness and courtesy the most effective currency for getting help.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates RubyGems reaching one million packages served, noting that nearly half came from Rails downloads. He highlights Rails reaching almost 130K total downloads in its first year. While pleased with the growth, he expresses frustration that RubyGems still isn't bundled with Ruby's core distribution, requiring an extra installation step. He looks forward to RubyGems being included in Ruby 1.8.3 or 1.8.4 to simplify the Rails onboarding experience.
key_points: RubyGems hit one million packages served, validating it as Ruby's packaging standard
Rails accounted for nearly half of all gem downloads, with 130K total downloads in its first year
RubyGems not being part of Ruby core remained a friction point for new Rails adoption
Reducing setup friction was a key priority for growing the Rails ecosystem
money_quotes: Whenever I'm drawn back into the dark world of a three-step setup.rb, I shudder.
We all have to start somewhere, but I'm thrilled that we don't have to stay there.
Not too shabby for a 1-year old.
I still can't just type "gem install rails" on a fresh Ruby installation and expect it to work.
Can't wait to cut that step out of the How To Get On Rails message.
posts.key_insight: Rails' explosive early growth was driving the entire Ruby packaging ecosystem, but DHH understood that reducing even small friction in developer onboarding was critical to sustaining adoption.

posts.summary: DHH announces his upcoming appearance at the JAOO conference in Århus, Denmark, where he'll deliver a double-session on Ruby on Rails to an audience primarily composed of Java and .NET developers. He frames it as an 'interesting' challenge to present Rails to this enterprise-oriented crowd. The first session will be a live demo, while the second will address common skeptical questions. He sarcastically references the perennial 'But does it scale?' question.
key_points: DHH was actively evangelizing Rails at enterprise-focused conferences dominated by Java and .NET developers
The 'But does it scale?' question was already a recurring challenge Rails faced from the enterprise community in 2005
Live demos were a key part of DHH's Rails advocacy strategy, showing rather than telling
money_quotes: a double-session Ruby on Rails cocktail
It's going to be an interesting audience to present in front.
we'll be answering such marvelous questions as "But does it scale?"
posts.key_insight: Even in 2005, DHH was taking Rails directly into enterprise territory, meeting Java and .NET skepticism head-on with live demos and a confident, slightly combative attitude toward the inevitable scalability questions.

posts.summary: DHH responds to criticism that Active Record is limited by MySQL's lack of advanced database features. He argues this isn't a constraint but a deliberate design opinion: business logic belongs in the domain model, not the database. He rejects stored procedures and database constraints as violations of object-oriented encapsulation. He distinguishes between application and integration databases, noting his stance applies to the former, and warns that expecting Rails to embrace enterprise database features is a losing bet.
key_points: Active Record's simplicity is an intentional design opinion, not a limitation imposed by MySQL
Stored procedures and database constraints destroy coherence by splitting business logic across layers
Software should have a single layer of cleverness — the domain model — rather than distributing logic into the database
The distinction between application databases and integration databases is critical to understanding this position
Rails will not change course to embrace enterprise database features regardless of MySQL's evolution
money_quotes: I don't want my database to be clever!
I consider stored procedures and constraints vile and reckless destroyers of coherence.
No, Mr. Database, you can not have my business logic. Your procedural ambitions will bear no fruit and you'll have to pry that logic from my dead, cold object-oriented hands.
I want a single layer of cleverness: My domain model.
Letting it sieve half ways through to the database is a terrible violation of those fine intentions.
Doing integration through the database belongs in a time where Beverly Hills 90210 was a hit show on TV.
posts.key_insight: Good software architecture demands a single layer of cleverness — the domain model — and letting business logic leak into the database destroys the coherence that object-orientation was designed to provide.

posts.summary: DHH reports from the 2005 JAOO conference that Java's perceived dominance in web development is cracking. He cites prominent voices like Dave Thomas, Dave Geary, and Jason Hunter who argue that Java and J2EE are grossly overused for web applications, with estimates that 70-80% of Java projects would be better served by Ruby on Rails. He frames this as a classic innovator's dilemma where Java's complexity tax is too high for most applications. The post concludes that Java isn't going away but should be reserved for the minority of cases where its power is truly needed.
key_points: Java has lost its aura of invincibility at developer conferences, with criticism no longer considered blasphemy
Multiple prominent technologists estimate 70-80% of Java web applications would be better built with simpler tools like Rails
The problem is framed as an innovator's dilemma: Java's complexity price is too high for the value it delivers in most applications
ThoughtWorks, once skeptical of alternatives to Java in enterprise, had shifted to working on commercial Rails projects
DHH positions this not as Java's death but as a correction — the sledgehammer should stop being used to crack nuts
money_quotes: Java has lost its aura of invincibility.
Using the full might of a J2EE stack to write a small stand-alone application is using a sledgehammer to crack a nut.
I keep hearing the sound of nuts being pulverized as developers seem to think that using anything other than J2EE is somehow unprofessional.
His estimate would be that 70-80% of the applications currently being developed with these technologies would be better off using Ruby on Rails.
Java and its stacks are grossly over-serving the majority of web applications that are built today.
The price of complexity in the Java world is far too high for the value it delivers in a large portion of applications.
It just requires enough hope and conviction that things can change.
As an industry, we should merely realize that the sledgehammer is grossly over-used.
posts.key_insight: Java's dominance in web development was never a technical inevitability but a cultural default, and the growing chorus of prominent voices questioning it signals that simpler tools like Rails can and should replace it for the majority of applications.

posts.summary: DHH announces that approximately 4,700 copies of 'Agile Web Development with Rails' shipped in a single week after a prolonged shortage from the previous printing. The book had been out of stock for a while, leaving buyers at events like JAOO empty-handed. The new shipment went to direct customers, Amazon, and local bookstores. He reflects on the impressive number with a lighthearted tone.
key_points: 4,700 copies of Agile Web Development with Rails shipped in one week after a supply shortage
Demand had built up significantly during the out-of-stock period, with even conference attendees unable to buy copies
The Rails book's commercial success signals strong early adoption of the framework in 2005
money_quotes: Boy, there was a lot of pent up demand after running short on the last printing.
They didn't even have any copies of the book at JAOO — despite having lots of willing buyers. What a shame.
4,700 copies in one week. Glad I didn't have to lick the stamps on all of those.
posts.key_insight: The rapid sellout and massive restock of the Rails book demonstrated the explosive early demand for Ruby on Rails in 2005.

posts.summary: DHH teases the upcoming launch of Writeboard, a project from 37signals that had been delayed twice by the releases of Ta-da List and Backpack. He acknowledges it's not a massive product but calls it their most polished work yet. The post builds anticipation by promising a public release the following week while keeping details minimal.
key_points: Writeboard was repeatedly delayed as other 37signals products (Ta-da List, Backpack) shipped first
DHH emphasizes polish and simple usefulness over size or feature count
The teaser format reflects 37signals' approach of building anticipation through transparency
money_quotes: No, it's not a huge thing. But quite possibly the most polished one we've done so far.
And simply useful.
posts.key_insight: A product doesn't need to be big to be valuable — polish and simple usefulness matter more.

posts.summary: DHH announces an upcoming conference travel itinerary covering Web 2.0, RubyConf, and EuroOSCON. He'll be attending Jason Fried's keynote at Web 2.0, speaking at RubyConf, and keynoting at EuroOSCON in the Netherlands. The brief post captures the early excitement of Ruby on Rails' rising prominence on the conference circuit in 2005.
key_points: DHH was embedded in the Web 2.0 conference scene at its peak hype moment in 2005
Rails had enough momentum that DHH was keynoting at O'Reilly's European open source conference
The post reflects the tight Basecamp/Rails connection, with Jason Fried keynoting Web 2.0 while DHH attended
money_quotes: Tomorrow morning at the ungodly hour of six, I'll be boarding a plane to travel the world. Or, rather a few good conferences around the world. Or, really, just the US and the Netherlands.
posts.key_insight: A brief travel announcement that captures the moment in 2005 when Rails was ascending rapidly on the global conference stage.

posts.summary: DHH announces the launch of Writeboard, a free collaborative text tool with version control and difference tracking. He acknowledges that critics will find it too simple, just as they did with Basecamp, Ta-da List, and Backpack. Rather than apologizing for minimal features, he frames deliberate simplicity as 37signals' core philosophy and competitive advantage. The post is a brief product launch that doubles as a manifesto for doing less.
key_points: Writeboard launches as a deliberately minimal collaborative writing tool with version control and commenting
Every 37signals product launch has been met with criticism for being too simple, which DHH views as validation
The company's explicit brand promise is simplicity, not feature richness
Products should help people get work done better today, not impress them with feature counts
money_quotes: It's also a whole lot less software than many people probably expected.
All our subsequent applications have been billed as too simple and too little by a choir of pundits at their launch.
We've never been in the business of wowing people with an amazing wealth of features.
So while we'll continue to proudly underwhelm people looking for more software and more features.
We'll be equally proud to please those who find the less we do, just enough to get their work done better today than yesterday.
posts.key_insight: Consistent criticism for being too simple is not a failure of product strategy but confirmation that you're staying true to a philosophy of deliberate minimalism.

posts.summary: DHH announces he has secured an O-1 'Extraordinary Alien' visa to live and work in the United States, settling in Chicago. After narrowly missing the H1B quota by two days, he obtained the more prestigious O-1 visa with help from prominent tech figures who wrote recommendation letters. He and his partner Marianne plan to live in the Bucktown neighborhood, with her studying psychology at Loyola. The post serves as both an announcement and a thank-you to the many people who helped make the visa happen.
key_points: DHH secured an O-1 'Extraordinary Alien' visa after missing the H1B quota by just two days
Prominent tech figures including Tim O'Reilly, Martin Fowler, and Bruce Perens wrote recommendation letters supporting his application
He would be joining Jason Fried and Ryan at 37signals headquarters in Chicago's Bucktown neighborhood
The publication of his Rails book and the Best Hacker award were key evidence for demonstrating 'extraordinary ability'
money_quotes: After missing the H1B quota by a mere two days back in August, it's such a relief that it finally happened.
I'm now legal to stay, work, and pay taxes in the US of A for 3 years.
It all played a vital part of demonstrating to the INS in Nebraska that I qualified as an 'extraordinary individual'.
She put the application together, wrote a cover letter so endorsing my ears were burning, and always answered our questions fast and comprehensively.
So. With that acceptance speech out of the way, I look very much forward to moving over in November.
posts.key_insight: DHH's early Rails success — the book, awards, and endorsements from tech luminaries — was substantial enough to qualify him for the elite O-1 visa, marking a pivotal personal milestone as he relocated to join 37signals in Chicago.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the rapid growth and momentum of Ruby on Rails, noting that the framework has taken on a life of its own. He marvels at the constant stream of announcements and developments coming from both expected and unexpected sources. The post serves as a brief celebration of how far Rails has come in just one year, positioning it as a technology that has truly arrived.
key_points: Rails had developed momentum beyond DHH's direct control, with the community driving growth independently
New announcements and developments were arriving daily from diverse and unexpected sources
DHH frames Rails as having definitively 'arrived' as a mainstream technology in just one year
money_quotes: The beast has certainly taken on a life of its own.
Ruby on Rails has really arrived.
I can't believe how far we've come in just a year.
posts.key_insight: By late 2005, Ruby on Rails had crossed the threshold from a niche framework to a self-sustaining ecosystem with its own momentum and community-driven growth.

posts.summary: DHH announces Writeboard integration with Basecamp, using it to illustrate 37signals' 'app-less app' design philosophy. A single Writeboard codebase powers a standalone application at writeboard.com plus integrated features in both Backpack and Basecamp. The architecture uses REST and an observer pattern where Writeboards notify 'folder' URLs when updated, keeping indexes current without expensive lookups. DHH frames this as a force multiplier for small teams, getting three products from one codebase at roughly a third of the cost.
key_points: A single Writeboard codebase serves triple duty: standalone app, Backpack feature, and Basecamp feature, each with slightly different UI configuration
The architecture uses a REST-based observer pattern where Writeboards ping registered folder URLs on updates, avoiding expensive per-request lookups
The 'app-less app' or 'document/single-url' design pattern lets small teams scale their product suite at a fraction of normal cost
37signals was already planning to apply the same pattern to a second application under development
money_quotes: This is interesting because it shows how the 'app-less app' design allows us to reap the benefits of development thrice.

From a single code base, we've provided a stand-alone application at writeboard.com and decorated both Backpack and Basecamp with Writeboard-powered 'features' integrated deeply into each of the applications.
Behind the scenes its all a simple REST-powered architecture following a simple observer pattern.
Programming-wise, it's been trivial to implement. But functionally it's a big win and a great way for a small team like us to scale our suite of applications at near 1/3 of the cost.
Develop once, launch many times.
posts.key_insight: Designing applications as self-contained, single-URL documents with REST interfaces lets small teams multiply their product surface from a single codebase at a fraction of the development cost.

posts.summary: DHH contrasts two bloggers' approaches to retracting public criticisms. Dave Winer blasted Evan Williams then silently deleted the post without acknowledgment, while Russell Beattie openly retracted his criticism in the comments. DHH argues that transparent apologies earn more empathy than silent retractions. He admits to his own history of knee-jerk reactions, making the point universal rather than self-righteous.
key_points: Silent retractions without acknowledgment are worse than the original offense
Public, transparent apologies actually increase empathy and goodwill toward the person who erred
Everyone has knee-jerk reactions — what matters is how you handle the aftermath
Accountability in public discourse builds trust; erasure destroys it
money_quotes: It's not the incident, but how you deal with it that matters
I came out empathizing more with Russell than before the entire incident, I came out empathizing less with Winer than after he had posted the original blast
posts.key_insight: How you handle retracting a mistake in public — transparently or silently — matters far more than the mistake itself.

posts.summary: DHH argues that general-purpose content management systems are a pipe dream whose days are numbered. As the cost of creating custom software approaches zero, the appeal of generalized solutions diminishes. He points to blogs and wikis as examples of specialized tools that already serve narrower needs better than generic CMS platforms. The post concludes that 'content' is a meaningless abstraction—people produce specific things like reviews, news, articles, and photos, not generic 'content.'
key_points: As software creation costs decrease, the allure of generalized solutions loses its justification
Blogs and wikis already prove that specialized tools outperform general-purpose CMS for specific use cases
The concept of 'content' is itself a harmful abstraction that obscures what people actually produce
General-purpose CMS belongs in the same graveyard as model-driven architecture and CASE tools
money_quotes: The more expensive it is to create fresh software, the more appealing the mirage of generalization will appear.
Don't accept the label of content. Nobody produces content. People write reviews, people write news, people write articles, people exhibits photos.
Try to realize, there is no content.
I believe the time has come to mark a date in the not too distant future for celebrating the death of the general-purpose content management system.
While it'll never reach zero, t is aiming enough in that direction to expose the fraud of ultimate generalization.
posts.key_insight: The general-purpose CMS is a fantasy born from expensive software development; as creation costs drop, specialized tools will always win over the false promise of generalization.

posts.summary: DHH discusses how Rails has prepared technical defenses against Google Web Accelerator's destructive pre-fetching behavior, which triggers state-altering GET requests and can wipe user data. Rails now ships with button_to and link_to helpers that convert actions to POST requests, plus controller-level verify guards. However, DHH argues that the real problem isn't whether frameworks can protect against GWA—it's that Google knowingly released software that wreaks havoc on the vast existing web of applications using state-altering GETs. He condemns the reckless cost-benefit tradeoff of marginally faster browsing versus potential data destruction.
key_points: Google Web Accelerator returned without reporting pre-fetching intentions, making it impossible to detect and block
Rails added button_to, POST-generating link_to, and controller verify guards to protect against destructive GET pre-fetching
The technical ability to protect against GWA is beside the point—the real web is full of state-altering GETs that won't change overnight
Google's cost-benefit calculation is completely wrong: marginal speed gains don't justify the risk of wiping user data across countless applications
money_quotes: It no longer reports its pre-fetching intentions, so now you can't detect whether its either or. In other words, you can't block the fucker.
A complete package to Do The Right Thing And Look Pretty While You Do It.
The problem with the GWA is not that you can protect against it. It's not whether Rails makes it easy or not to do so. It's the fact that we know the Real Web doesn't behave uniformly like this.
To willfully release an application that wrecks havoc, potentially wiping data left and right, is malicious.
Your web experience may or may not get a little bit faster. Are you kidding?!
The sense of proportions, of gain vs risk, of price vs reward, is completely and utterly out of whack.
posts.key_insight: Having a technical defense against bad behavior doesn't excuse the bad behavior—Google's Web Accelerator was reckless because the real web is full of applications that will never conform to pure REST conventions.

posts.summary: DHH updates his criticism of Google Web Accelerator (GWA) after learning that the missing X-moz:prefetch header was a bug, not a deliberate removal. Google's product manager Othman Laraki contacted DHH directly to explain the issue and confirm a fix was being rolled out. While DHH acknowledges this is good news, he maintains his broader criticism of the GWA concept itself. He uses the new contact to push for more answers about Google's motives.
key_points: The missing prefetch header in GWA requests was confirmed as a bug by Google's product manager, not intentional removal
Google began rolling out a fix immediately after DHH's post brought attention to the issue
DHH maintains that GWA is fundamentally problematic even with the bug fixed
Open dialogue between developers and product teams can lead to quick resolutions
money_quotes: So while I still consider the GWA to be pretty evil stuff, it's at least not double evil by intention.
Pretty nasty bug, but shit happens every where.
Now this naturally doesn't exonerate the vanilla evilness of the GWA concept, but its definitely a step in the right direction.
posts.key_insight: A missing HTTP header that broke web application safeguards against Google Web Accelerator turned out to be a bug rather than malice, illustrating that assuming incompetence over evil intent is usually the right call.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the most valuable lesson from his three years at Copenhagen Business School: learning to price things based on their value rather than their cost. He argues that the inability to separate value from cost is what prevents good businesses from becoming great ones and turns potential entrepreneurs into 'starved artists.' He connects this pricing mindset to his broader philosophy of programming and product development, where he seeks maximum value from minimum effort. The post pushes back against the moral stigma attached to value-based pricing.
key_points: The guilt people feel about charging more than cost is the biggest barrier between nice businesses and great businesses
Society creates a false moral divide between 'compassionate' people and 'cold business people' over value-based pricing
The ability to discern value is DHH's most important skill for programming — always asking 'Worth it?'
Simple problems with high value should always be solved before hard problems — it's about maximizing the value-to-effort ratio
money_quotes: I spent three years at the Copenhagen Business School to largely acquire one skill: The ability to price something at its value, not cost, and feel damn good about it.

The inability to price at value instead of cost is what separates nice (or niche) businesses from great businesses, what fizzles good ideas, and what turns would-be entrepreneurs into 'starved artists'.
People who charge at value are often labeled as cynical or price gaugers or downright inhuman.
It creates a false split between 'the compassionate' and 'the cold business people'.
The ability to discern value and make calls based on it: Worth it?
What's the least amount of work I can do that ends up having big value?
Before I've cleared all the simple problems of high value, it's just not for me to engage in solving hard problems. Too much risk, not enough ratio.
posts.key_insight: The most important skill in both business and programming is learning to evaluate things by their value rather than their cost, then ruthlessly prioritizing high-value, low-effort work.

posts.summary: DHH shares a News.com article that bills Ruby on Rails as 'High Impact' and notes the framework's growing mainstream recognition. He acknowledges the article is somewhat ego-stroking but appreciates how the journalist captured his excitement. He points to Sun CEO Jonathan Schwartz linking to Rails as further evidence of mainstream adoption, and teases the upcoming Rails 1.0 release as the next milestone.
key_points: News.com featured Ruby on Rails as 'High Impact,' comparing its trajectory to Linux's early days
Sun Microsystems CEO Jonathan Schwartz publicly acknowledged Rails, signaling enterprise-level awareness
Rails was approaching its 1.0 release, which DHH positioned as the next inflection point for adoption
money_quotes: So it's a little bit more of an ego-wank than what might have been desired, but I can cope.
This is exactly how things like Linux got started
Ruby on Rails is certainly making its way into the mainstream.
Now to take it all up a notch with the bang of 1.0.
posts.key_insight: By late 2005, Ruby on Rails had crossed from niche developer tool to mainstream tech press coverage and enterprise CEO attention, validating its rapid rise ahead of the 1.0 launch.

posts.summary: DHH addresses early criticism that Rails was merely a 'code generation' framework akin to the bloated wizards of the past. Critics dismissed Rails generators as boilerplate factories that would create unmaintainable code. He argues this comparison is fundamentally flawed because Rails generators produce structure—directories, files, and naming conventions—rather than opaque, incomprehensible code blocks. He reframes the concept as 'Structure Generation' to distinguish it from the bad reputation of traditional code generation.
key_points: Early Rails critics used the 'code generation is bad' syllogism to dismiss the entire framework
Traditional code wizards deserved their bad reputation because they produced opaque, unmaintainable code
Rails generators are fundamentally different: they create structure and conventions, not incomprehensible boilerplate
The term 'code generation' is tainted and doesn't accurately describe what Rails does
money_quotes: Rails is all code generation, code generation is bad, therefore, Rails is bad. Only one problem: What Rails is doing with its generators lie far enough away from the horror stories of the past to go under the same name.
The problem is that the code-spewing wizards gave the whole field of code generation a bad name.
What we do in Rails is a lot less about generating lines of code and a lot more about generating structure.
Herding the programmer down the path of conventions. Creating the right directories, the right files, and calling them the right names. It's conventions on rails.
posts.key_insight: Rails generators aren't code generation in the traditional sense—they're structure generation, guiding developers into conventions rather than producing opaque boilerplate.

posts.summary: DHH responds to Robert Scoble's analysis of why Microsoft's development tools fall short for web application development. He uses Rails' difficulty retaining a SQL Server adapter maintainer as evidence that developers are fleeing the Microsoft ecosystem. He argues that Microsoft has become entirely optional across the full web stack, which fundamentally changes their competitive position. DHH predicts Microsoft will never regain dominance in web development tools and suggests they should follow Apple's example of moving to higher ground.
key_points: Rails struggles to retain SQL Server adapter maintainers because developers only need it temporarily while migrating away from Microsoft
Microsoft has become entirely optional for web development — no part of the stack requires it on client or server
Competing as one choice among many is fundamentally harder than being the only option, and Microsoft hasn't adapted
DHH predicts Microsoft will not acknowledge the loss of their web development toolchain dominance until it's too late
money_quotes: None of the developers that have been or are working on it are in it for the long term. It's just that they need it while they either migrate their shop over to open-source alternatives or find a new job that doesn't force them to use Microsoft tools.
Microsoft is now entirely optional. No part of the stack needs Microsoft. Not on the client, not on the server.
You have to compete very differently when you're just one choice out of many instead of the only show in town.
To be frank, I don't ever see the good times coming back for them.
There is no dominant future for the Microsoft tool chain for web development in sight. But I doubt the company will acknowledge that before it's game over.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's shift from necessity to option in web development is an existential competitive challenge they are unlikely to overcome.

posts.summary: DHH shares links to two recent interviews: one with eWEEK about programmer happiness and the secrets of Ruby on Rails, and another with Jarkko Laine at EuroOSCON for a Master's thesis on open source and small businesses. He self-deprecatingly acknowledges that he keeps covering the same topics and jokes about needing new material.
key_points: Rails was gaining enough mainstream attention to warrant coverage in eWEEK, a major enterprise tech publication
DHH's core message at this time centered on programmer happiness as a key Rails selling point
The intersection of open source and small business was already a research-worthy academic topic in 2005
DHH shows self-awareness about his tendency to repeat the same talking points
money_quotes: I definitely need to come up with a new pony soon.
I apologies in advance if you're tired of hearing me recycle the same old subjects.
posts.key_insight: By late 2005, Rails had grown prominent enough to attract enterprise press coverage and academic research, even as DHH was conscious of repeating his core evangelism message.

posts.summary: DHH responds to Paul Graham's essay 'The Venture Capital Squeeze,' which cited him as a model for the future of startups. He argues that choosing more powerful, simpler technologies like Ruby on Rails gives small teams a genuine competitive edge over large companies that throw resources at problems. He expresses ambivalence about Rails going mainstream, suggesting that its exclusivity to small, agile teams is part of what makes it a strategic advantage. The post frames technology choice as a competitive weapon rather than a mere preference.
key_points: Choosing a more powerful language can be more effective than outsourcing to cheaper labor or raising more venture capital
A single founder-programmer is more than 10x cheaper than a team of ten due to zero meeting overhead and willingness to work for equity
Rails remaining niche could be a sustainable competitive advantage for small teams against large incumbents
Competitors doubling down on heavyweight frameworks and outsourcing actually makes the agile team's edge stronger
money_quotes: The future of software is not to accept that status quo and just throw more resources at it — neither labor from India or capital from the VCs.
You can gain even more productivity by simply routing around the mainstream technology choices and go for something simpler.
Some part of me actually enjoys the vision that Rails would never become mainstream.
That it would remain available only to the smaller, agile teams, and thus give them a sustainable advantage to compete against the big guys with.
The further your competitors go down that route, the easier it will be to use your edge to win big.
posts.key_insight: Choosing a radically simpler technology stack is a legitimate competitive strategy that lets small teams outmaneuver large, resource-heavy competitors.

posts.summary: DHH announces his departure from Denmark to Chicago for an indefinite period. His friends in the Danish internet and startup scene are throwing him a farewell party. The event will feature a debrief on Rails, 37signals, and making an impact. It's a brief, personal post marking a major life transition as he leaves his home country to pursue his work in the US.
key_points: DHH is relocating from Denmark to Chicago for an unknown duration, marking a significant personal and professional move
The Danish startup community organized a farewell event reflecting DHH's standing in the local tech scene
The event agenda—Rails, 37signals, making an impact—captures the themes DHH was already known for in 2005
The post reveals DHH's rootedness in a tight-knit Danish internet community before his global fame
money_quotes: SK0943 takes me to Chicago for period unknown.
I'll be debriefed on Rails, 37signals, how to make an impact, create a stir, and see it all through with a smile.
It'll be free and there will be beer.
I am very honored.
posts.key_insight: A brief personal farewell captures the moment DHH left Denmark for Chicago, transitioning from local startup figure to global tech personality.

posts.summary: DHH responds to a claim that Ruby on Rails won't replace Java for web applications by agreeing—but for reasons that damn Java's dominance rather than defend it. He argues Java persists because the industry wrongly assumes implementation is low-value commodity work that can be easily outsourced. DHH rejects these assumptions, arguing that specification and implementation cannot be meaningfully separated, and that implementation is neither trivial nor mechanical. He notes that technical excellence rarely wins in large corporations, and provocatively says he hopes it stays that way—because becoming the safe default choice brings a world of distractions and suffering.
key_points: Java's dominance rests on two flawed assumptions: that implementation is low-value in the software value chain, and that it can be easily outsourced
You cannot meaningfully separate specification from implementation—implementation is the bottleneck and the bulk of real work
Technical excellence has an inverse correlation to the size and age of corporations
DHH hopes Rails never becomes the 'nobody ever got fired for' choice, because that safe-default status brings unwanted distractions and suffering
money_quotes: I don't believe that you can meaningfully separate specification and implementation and I most certainly don't believe that implementation is either trivial or mechanical.
Technical excellence seem to have a inverse correlation to the size and age of most corporations.
Nobody ever got fired for... Right now that sentence ends in Java.
I actually hope it stays that way. I'd really hate to become the end of that sentence.
Because with it comes a world of distractions (and hurt and suffering) that just isn't a place that I want to be.
If you work at Mega Corp Inc. under five layers of management and need to play the corporate politics game, then implementation is very unlikely to be your prime concern.
In many environments, I'd wager that they rarely win.
posts.key_insight: Java dominates not because it's technically superior but because large organizations treat implementation as commodity work—an assumption that guarantees mediocrity and ensures the best tools rarely win.

posts.summary: DHH posts a brief classified ad in Danish selling his Mac Mini for 3,500 DKK (originally ~5,000 DKK). He lists the specs—1.25GHz processor, 40GB storage, 512MB RAM, Airport Extreme, Bluetooth, and combo drive—and notes it still has a couple months of Apple warranty remaining. He asks interested buyers to contact him before Tuesday morning when he leaves for the US. This is a simple personal listing with no broader argument or commentary.
key_points: DHH is selling his Mac Mini at a ~30% discount from its original price
The machine was purchased in January 2005 and still has remaining Apple warranty
He frames the offer as a chance for someone considering switching to Mac to try it affordably
The post is entirely in Danish, reflecting his early Danish-language blogging before switching to English
money_quotes: Hvis du overvejer at switche, eller i det mindste bare snuse til det grønne græs på den anden side, så har jeg lige tilbudet.
posts.key_insight: A brief Danish-language classified ad from DHH's early blogging days selling his Mac Mini, notable only as a historical artifact with no substantive argument or insight.

posts.summary: DHH posts a brief travel update announcing his arrival in the United States. He mentions a goodbye gathering before departing and notes that despite frequent transatlantic trips, jet lag still affects him. The post is essentially a quick check-in with readers to confirm he made it safely.
key_points: DHH has arrived in the US after a goodbye gathering
Despite frequent transatlantic travel, he still struggles with the 7-hour time zone change
The post is a casual, personal check-in rather than a substantive essay
money_quotes: It's cool and odd at the same time.
You should think that after all these trips that my body would have grown comfortable with the 7-hour time changes. Not so. Hurting.
posts.key_insight: A brief personal travel update with no substantive technical or cultural commentary.

posts.summary: DHH announces the first RailsConf, scheduled for June 2006, projecting around 400 attendees at $350-400 per ticket. He frames the conference as a milestone that makes the Rails ecosystem feel tangible and larger than any individual contributor. Beyond community building, he positions it as a proof of concept to demonstrate market viability to potential vendors and companies showing interest in Rails. He expresses excitement about moving beyond introductory-level content to serious, in-depth technical conversations.
key_points: The first RailsConf is announced for June 22-25, 2006, just before Rails' two-year anniversary
DHH sees the conference as validation that Rails has grown beyond any single person into a real ecosystem
Getting 400 paying attendees at $350-400 each would serve as a commercial proof of concept for vendors eyeing the Rails market
DHH is eager to move past introductory 'how to make a weblog' content toward deeper technical discussions
money_quotes: Announcing RailsConf makes the ecosystem seem so real.
This whole Rails thing is bigger than anyone person, bigger than me, bigger than the core team, bigger than the circle of people that I know doing it.
That's a great feeling. It's the feeling of creation.
If we can actually get some 400 people together who are willing to shell out around $350-400, that's a strong signal to potential vendors that there is a market here.
Going beyond the bland introduction of 'how to make a weblog' should provide for a welcome change of scenery.
posts.key_insight: A community conference serves as both an emotional milestone proving an open source project has transcended its creator and a commercial signal proving market viability to the broader industry.

posts.summary: DHH announces the relaunch of 37signals' workshop series, rebranded from 'Building of Basecamp' to 'The Getting Real Workshop.' The workshop covers the same core philosophy but draws from a broader range of examples across all 37signals applications. Capped at 50 attendees for an intimate format, it sold out in just 12 hours—14 times faster than the previous workshop.
key_points: 37signals rebranded their workshop from 'Building of Basecamp' to 'Getting Real,' broadening the scope beyond a single product
The 50-person cap is a deliberate choice to foster intimate discussion and audience interaction
Selling out in 12 hours (vs. one week previously) signals rapidly growing demand for 37signals' philosophy
money_quotes: The 50-person cap creates a intimate atmosphere with lots of great questions and interactions.
Sold out. All 50 seats where gone in 12 hours. That's about 14 times as fast as last time.
posts.key_insight: The explosive demand for 37signals' Getting Real workshop reflected a growing appetite in the industry for their contrarian, lean approach to building software products.

posts.summary: DHH critiques the Zend Framework's approach to building an official PHP framework, arguing that their process-driven methodology of review, testing, and committee input won't achieve the 'extreme simplicity' they seek. He contends that genuine simplicity only comes from solving real problems, not from design-by-committee or foresight-based planning. Drawing from his experience with Rails, he asserts that frameworks must be extracted from real applications rather than designed in the abstract. He warns that optimizing for sample applications and imagined use cases leads to solutions that fail when applied to real-world problems.
key_points: Zend's bid to create an official PHP framework is undermined by a process-heavy, committee-driven design approach
Extreme simplicity cannot be achieved through review processes and documentation alone — it requires solving real problems
Frameworks should be extractions from real applications, not designed through foresight or imagined use cases
Optimizing for sample applications produces solutions that are often the opposite of what's needed in practice
Simulation and abstraction are poor substitutes for building against real-world constraints
money_quotes: I've found that the only way to achieve even mediocre simplicity is by solving real problems.
Frameworks are extractions. If they are anything else, it's usually.. well.. crap.
This sounds like a fancy rewriting of design-by-committee. Or rather, design without cause.
When your anchor is sample applications or imagined use cases, you usually end up optimizing for just that.
Simulation can only take you so far.
Which more often than not is exactly the opposite of what is really needed when the rubber meets the road.
posts.key_insight: Genuine framework simplicity can only be extracted from solving real problems in real applications, never designed in the abstract through committee processes and imagined use cases.

posts.summary: DHH shares praise from Jim Dunnigan, a former Microsoft Project product manager, who called Basecamp the first truly accessible project management tool. DHH reflects on how user appreciation fuels motivation and keeps the team focused. He frames this particular endorsement as especially meaningful given Dunnigan's deep experience in the project management software industry. The post is a brief celebration of external validation from an industry insider.
key_points: User appreciation is a powerful motivator that helps developers push through bugs and ship features faster
Endorsement from a 15-year veteran of project management software validates Basecamp's approach to simplicity
Basecamp's value proposition is making project management accessible to everyone, not just specialists
Public products benefit from a feedback loop of buzz and appreciation that private tools never get
money_quotes: One of the most rewarding aspects of doing a public application is the appreciation that'll trickle in from passionate users.
They can make that bug you're working on seem so much less of a problem, ignite your motivation for getting that next feature done today rather than tomorrow.
Basecamp is the first product I have seen that is truly project management for everyone. It is nice to see someone finally figured it out.
posts.key_insight: Validation from an industry veteran of complex project management software confirms that simplicity and accessibility can be more valuable than feature richness.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Tim O'Reilly's BookScan data showing Ruby book sales surging 1,552% year-over-year, surpassing Python for the first time. He attributes this explosive growth directly to Ruby on Rails driving interest in the language. He notes that Python and Perl are stagnant or declining by comparison, while Ruby achieved this with only two books on the market. DHH frames this as just the beginning, with more titles and announcements in the pipeline.
key_points: Ruby book sales grew 1,552% year-over-year and surpassed Python book sales for the first time
Rails is identified as the singular driver of Ruby's surge, a language that had existed for years without mainstream traction
Ruby achieved this market position with only two books available, compared to extensive libraries for Python, Perl, and Java
Python grew modestly at 20% while Perl and Java declined, suggesting dynamic languages were ascendant but Ruby was in a class of its own
money_quotes: Ruby book sales have exploded. In total, Ruby is up 1,552% over last year in the same period.
Ruby books are now selling better than Python books. Which of course is the first time that ever happened.
So its no wonder we have a feeling that the duo of modern, dynamic languages are taking off. It's because they are!
Naturally, I'm radiating with pride of having had the opportunity to bring the most beautiful programming language I know to the top of mind share.
There's just a single book out on Ruby on Rails right now. And only one recent Ruby book covering 1.8. Compare that to the wealth of titles available for Python, Perl, and Java.
Ruby has just started its ascend. Sure, it's a rocket take-off, but we haven't even left the atmosphere yet.
posts.key_insight: A single killer framework can catapult an obscure programming language past established rivals almost overnight, and Rails did exactly that for Ruby.

posts.summary: DHH highlights a blog post from Hani Suleiman's BileBlog, a notorious Java community blog known for profanity-laden takedowns, which attacked Web 2.0 and Basecamp specifically. DHH quotes Suleiman's claim that everyone he knows who tried Basecamp had an awful experience and considers it the worst project management tool they've encountered. Rather than mounting a serious defense, DHH dismisses the criticism as tragically funny and pitiable. He then pivots to linking posts about civility in tech discourse, acknowledging he could improve in that area himself.
key_points: The BileBlog's Hani Suleiman, known for hostile tech commentary, was paradoxically voted onto the Java Community Process Executive Committee
Suleiman attacked Basecamp as universally terrible and dismissed the 'simpler means higher quality' philosophy
DHH's response strategy is to treat the criticism as self-evidently absurd rather than engage with it substantively
DHH links to posts about online civility while admitting he could be more civil himself, showing some self-awareness
money_quotes: This is awesome. In the sense of awesome meaning funny in a sad, tragic way where you feel sorry for the person.
Hani disparages everything and everyone with profanities, toilet references, and insults of a variety that's usually reserved for the worst trolls of the internet.
I could definitely learn to be more civil at times too.
posts.key_insight: DHH treats a hostile, profanity-laden attack on Basecamp as self-discrediting entertainment rather than legitimate criticism worth addressing, while nodding toward the broader question of civility in tech discourse.

posts.summary: DHH shares that Jason Fried was nominated for Entrepreneur of the Year by Venture Voice podcast. He acknowledges the strong competition from notable tech entrepreneurs including Scott Heiferman, Joel Spolsky, and Evan Williams. The brief post serves as a signal boost encouraging support for Jason's nomination, noting that phoned-in votes carry 5x weight.
key_points: Jason Fried was nominated for Venture Voice's Entrepreneur of the Year 2005
Competition included notable figures like Joel Spolsky, Evan Williams, and Scott Heiferman
Phone-in votes carried 5x weight over other voting methods
money_quotes: Pretty cool, but also pretty stiff competition with guys like Scott Heiferman of Meetup, Joel Spolsky of Fog Creek Software, Evan Williams of Odeo, and others in the running.
posts.key_insight: A brief endorsement of Jason Fried's entrepreneurial recognition among a notable cohort of mid-2000s tech founders.

posts.summary: DHH announces the release of Ruby on Rails 1.0, reflecting on his journey from PHP and Java to Ruby over the preceding two years. He celebrates the milestone alongside the launch of a redesigned rubyonrails.org site created by Jason Fried. The post highlights new screencasts, a showcase of Rails applications in production, the core team, and endorsement quotes. He frames the 1.0 release not as a finish line but as a great beginning.
key_points: Ruby on Rails officially reached version 1.0 after roughly two years of development
DHH's transition from PHP and Java to Ruby catalyzed and gave direction to his ideas about web development
The 1.0 launch was accompanied by a redesigned website by Jason Fried showcasing the growing Rails ecosystem
DHH frames 1.0 as a beginning rather than a culmination, expressing excitement for what comes next
money_quotes: I can't believe that its been only two years since I started working with Ruby.
Ruby gave a thousand thoughts a direction.
So it's a big day. A great beginning. I can't wait to see where this will all go in 2006.
posts.key_insight: The Rails 1.0 release marked the moment DHH's vision for a better way to build web applications crystallized into a real, community-backed framework.

posts.summary: DHH calls out Bruce Eckel for hypocrisy in criticizing Bruce Tate's book 'Beyond Java' for quick dismissals of programming languages, when Eckel himself had previously dismissed Ruby as 'a bad ripoff of Python' without serious examination. DHH traces the evolution of Eckel's Ruby criticisms from 'bad Python clone' to 'better Perl with OO,' pointing out the contradictions. He argues Eckel's real issue is simply that he doesn't like Ruby or the attention it's receiving, and suggests Eckel should just be honest about that rather than dressing it up in shifting technical comparisons.
key_points: Bruce Eckel criticized Bruce Tate for quick dismissals in 'Beyond Java' while having done exactly the same thing to Ruby years earlier
Eckel's characterization of Ruby shifted from 'bad Python clone' to 'better Perl with OO,' revealing the comparisons as ill-conceived and inconsistent
DHH argues Eckel's real motivation is disliking Ruby's growing popularity and attention, not genuine technical critique
The post highlights how established language advocates often mask emotional reactions to new languages behind shifting technical rationalizations
money_quotes: Hold on. That reminds me of someone. Oh, I know, it's Mr. Eckel himself.

I'm losing track of the ill-conceived comparisons, but I do know what's astoundingly clear: Bruce Eckel doesn't like Ruby, he doesn't like the attention its getting, and he doesn't like people such as Bruce Tate fueling that attention.
No beef, that's cool. But why not just say it like that?
You could even have presented yourself as the polar opposite to the so-called hyper-enthusiasts: A hyper-detractor!
The label comes complete with a cape, an evil smirk, and long tirades about how the other side is no match for your master plan.
Does that mean that Perl is a bad clone of Python just missing the OO?
posts.key_insight: Critics who dismiss new languages with shifting technical rationalizations should just be honest that they don't like the competition, rather than pretending their objections are principled.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the reaction to Rails 1.0's honest release statement that it 'works mostly right, most of the time, for most of the people.' The Slashdot community was divided, with many taking offense at what they saw as reckless honesty for a 1.0 release. DHH argues the statement was simply truthful expectation-setting that every framework could make but none dare to. He embraces the controversy as a useful cultural filter, arguing that those offended by such honesty are self-selecting out of the Rails community.
key_points: Rails 1.0's honest release statement provoked outrage on Slashdot, revealing a cultural divide in how programmers relate to marketing vs. truthfulness
Critics worried that 'careless honesty' from an open source project could damage the broader F/OSS community's market perception
DHH sees divisive statements as features, not bugs — they efficiently filter out people who aren't a cultural fit for the project
The software industry norm of never acknowledging imperfection is a form of emperor-has-no-clothes self-deception
money_quotes: There simply is no sexy way to articulate the truth.
Such careless honesty from such a visible project may inadvertently damage overall market perception of the f/oss community.
As if we were about to bring down the kingdom of software development by pointing out that the emperor has no clothes.
I like it because it's a dividing issue. One which can garner as much love and appreciation as it can cause outrage and despair.
It exposed a set of deep cultural lines that in my mind separated the wheat from the chaff.
If that statement rubbed you the wrong way, it's an early warning signal that Ruby on Rails wouldn't be for you.
It's kinda like applying the principle of 'fail fast' to tech stack selection.
posts.key_insight: Radical honesty about software's limitations serves as a valuable cultural filter, efficiently sorting people who share your values from those who don't.

posts.summary: DHH dismisses the pedantic objection that Rails cannot be compared to Java or PHP because one is a framework and the others are languages. He argues this technical distinction is irrelevant because all three are solutions to the same problem: building web applications. The real comparison should be about which tool delivers the most valuable software fastest. He characterizes the pedantry as a convenient dodge to avoid confronting uncomfortable competitive realities.
key_points: The 'you can't compare a framework to a language' objection is technically correct but practically irrelevant
What matters is which tool solves the web problem most effectively and efficiently
Pedantic categorization is a defense mechanism to avoid engaging with competitive threats
Comparisons should be pragmatic and outcome-oriented, not taxonomically pure
money_quotes: Yes, the pedantry is technically correct, but also completely irrelevant.
In addition to being framework or language, they are all possible solutions to the web problem — requiring more or less water added to the mix.
Within your context, which is getting you the most valuable software in the shortest amount of time?
It's much easier to save the good tone of the dinner party by declaring them different species altogether.
We can then continue on with our lives undisturbed. Knowing that we need not care or worry too much about what goes on outside the window.
Most convenient.
posts.key_insight: Dismissing comparisons between competing tools on taxonomic grounds is intellectual cowardice disguised as rigor — what matters is which solution best solves your actual problem.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates TextMate reaching version 1.5 after 15 months, reflecting on how the text editor nearly didn't exist. He recounts how he got involved in Spring 2004 when creator Allan Odgaard was on his fourth or fifth rewrite and considering giving up. DHH helped by prioritizing features into iterations and pushing Allan to finish. The editor became essential to the Ruby on Rails community and even a reason for developers to switch to Mac.
key_points: TextMate's explosive growth made its creator Allan Odgaard wealthy, which DHH considers well-deserved
The editor nearly died before launch — Allan had done multiple complete rewrites and considered abandoning it for a 'real job'
DHH played a product management role, helping prioritize features into iterations and pushing development forward
TextMate became a gateway drug to Mac adoption, especially for the Ruby on Rails community
money_quotes: TextMate is without a doubt the most important application in my dock.
It's become a reason to get a Mac for many — especially the growing Ruby on Rails community.
And to think it almost didn't happen!
He was on his fourth or fifth complete rewrite with no end in sight. There was even talk about chalking it all up as a learning experience and get a 'real job'.

We had a good handful of meetings to discuss all the features on the plate, then we chopped them up into three large iterations with priorities and then Allan got to work with me as the monkey on his back.
OS X has the best text editor I've ever used on any system.
posts.key_insight: Great software often nearly dies before launch, and sometimes what's needed isn't more coding but someone to prioritize ruthlessly and push the creator to ship.

posts.summary: DHH highlights Denmark's unusually low Firefox adoption rates compared to the rest of Europe. He attributes this to systemic bias toward Internet Explorer in Danish public institutions, banks, and government web projects. Examples include a university application portal, his own bank, and the public TV station all building IE-exclusive or IE-favored experiences. He sarcastically notes Denmark must be one of Microsoft's favorite nations as a result.
key_points: Denmark's Firefox market share significantly trails the rest of Europe
Danish government, banking, and public broadcasting websites are built with Internet Explorer bias or exclusivity
Institutional decision-makers perpetuate browser monoculture through their platform choices
Public web projects discriminate against users of alternative browsers
money_quotes: The last couple of years is fraught with embarrassments of discrimination and bias from public web projects.
Internet Explorer continues to reign supreme or exclusive in the mind of decision makers.
It must surely be one of the favorite nations on the globe in Redmond. What an accomplishment.
posts.key_insight: Institutional bias toward Internet Explorer in Danish public web projects has suppressed browser competition and kept Denmark at the bottom of Firefox adoption in Europe.

posts.summary: DHH argues that code reuse is overvalued in software development, particularly for business logic. He contends that reuse works well only when instances are nearly identical, as with infrastructure frameworks like Rails, but fails for domain-specific components like authentication. He attributes Rails' success to its coherent single-vision design rather than assembled reusable parts, and argues that dynamic languages like Ruby make writing fresh code so cheap that reuse often costs more than starting from scratch.
key_points: Context-specific code beats consistent reusable abstractions in most business logic scenarios
Rails succeeds because of coherent vision, not because it's assembled from reusable parts — forced reuse creates 'frankenstein' systems
Dynamic languages lower the cost of writing new code so much that reuse must clear a higher bar to justify itself
Reuse works at the infrastructure level but rarely at the business logic or component level
money_quotes: Context beats consistency.
Reuse only works well when the particular instances are so similar that you're willing to trade the small differences for the increased productivity.

When you try to hard to reuse, you'll often end up with a frankenstein of slightly different approaches and philosophies that creates rough, unpolished surfaces that simply can't make you happy because it can't be beautiful.
I believe Rails works so well because it was all created with a coherent vision.
Since the cost of producing new, original code is so much lower, the demands on reuse are that much greater.
And usually that means it'll take more time to reuse something than it'll take to create it from scratch. Leaving you with something that's both more expensive and uglier.
posts.key_insight: Code reuse is often more expensive and uglier than writing fresh, context-specific code — especially when dynamic languages make original code cheap to produce.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his experience speaking about Ruby on Rails at Amazon's internal developers conference. He notes that many Amazon departments are already using Rails and that the audience was receptive. He positions Ruby as a natural evolution from Amazon's Perl-heavy front-end stack, especially given skepticism about Perl 6.
