He also describes Amazon's decentralized, polyglot architecture as fertile ground for Rails adoption.
key_points: Multiple Amazon departments were already using Rails internally by late 2005/early 2006
Amazon's Perl-heavy front-end made Ruby a natural transition, especially with low confidence in Perl 6
Amazon's decentralized, service-oriented architecture allowed different teams to choose their own tools
The reception from non-Rails teams was open-minded and interested
money_quotes: I was surprised to learn how many departments inside Amazon are already using Rails for various systems.
With the low confidence in Perl 6 ever panning out, I certainly felt an interest in exploring alternatives.
They already embrace diversity big time.
It's very decentralized with different groups responsible for different services that are then weaved together to produce the final pages.
Ruby is not that big of a leap.
posts.key_insight: Amazon's decentralized, polyglot engineering culture and waning confidence in Perl 6 created a natural opening for Ruby on Rails adoption across the company.

posts.summary: DHH announces the return of 37signals' workshop series, now rebranded from 'Building of Basecamp' to 'Getting Real.' He notes a significant shift in audience attitude: early attendees were skeptical of 37signals' approach, while current ones have already bought in and want practical advice. The March 2006 Chicago workshop sold out in 19 hours at $895/person, demonstrating strong demand for their philosophy.
key_points: The workshop evolved from 'Building of Basecamp' to 'Getting Real,' covering topics from beautiful code to hiring to running a web business
Audience shifted from skeptics questioning 'why' to believers asking 'how' — indicating mainstream acceptance of 37signals' approach
The workshop sold out in 19 hours at $895/person, showing strong commercial demand for their unconventional business philosophy
money_quotes: Where the first workshop was full of people who thought we were obviously not living in the real world, Getting Real was full of people who had stepped into this world and just wanted to learn how to make the most of it.
More questions about the how than about the why.
Basically everything we have strong opinions on at 37signals.
posts.key_insight: The shift from skeptical audiences to eager practitioners showed that 37signals' contrarian approach to building web businesses was gaining real traction.

posts.summary: DHH announces the first official RailsConf conference, opening registration with early-bird pricing and a lineup of notable keynote speakers including Martin Fowler, Paul Graham, and Dave Thomas. He urges quick registration due to limited seats and offers incentives like a charity-linked pre-conference tutorial day. The post was later updated to note that all 400 tickets sold out in roughly one week, confirming the explosive growth of the Rails community in 2006.
key_points: The first official RailsConf featured heavyweight keynotes from Martin Fowler, Paul Graham, Dave Thomas, and DHH himself
Early-bird pricing started at $400 with tiered increases, though DHH doubted any seats would remain at the highest tier
A charitable donation of $40 unlocked free access to a pre-conference Rails tutorial day for the first 100 registrants
All 400 tickets sold out in approximately one week, demonstrating Rails' rapid rise in developer mindshare
money_quotes: We got Martin Fowler, Paul Graham, Dave Thomas, and yours truly all lined up to deliver anchors of blast.
Three days of happy programming for people who love beautiful code.
It took just about a week and all 400 tickets to RailsConf has been sold out!
Nothing like that first-conference atmosphere.
I doubt we'll have any seats left to sell at $550, though.
posts.key_insight: The first RailsConf selling out in a week signaled that Rails had crossed from niche open-source project to a legitimate movement in web development.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Why the Lucky Stiff will perform at the first RailsConf, calling it the event's 'killer feature' above even keynotes from Martin Fowler, Paul Graham, and Dave Thomas. He recounts seeing _why perform at OSCON with his band the Thirsty Cups, highlighting a hilarious song about install.rb. The post promotes _why's new show and recommends his Poignant Guide to Ruby. Updates note that 150 additional seats sold out in under 24 hours, bringing attendance to 550.
key_points: Why the Lucky Stiff's performance is positioned as more exciting than keynotes from industry luminaries like Fowler, Graham, and Thomas
The early Rails community valued creativity, humor, and artistic expression alongside technical content
RailsConf sold out rapidly, with 150 additional seats gone in under 24 hours, showing intense early demand for Rails community events
_why's Poignant Guide to Ruby is praised as one of the funniest and weirdest programming language introductions ever written
money_quotes: Forget all about the keynotes from Martin Fowler, Paul Graham, Dave Thomas, and myself. This is the killer feature.
Him and his band, the thirsty cups, delivered an absolutely fantastic show.
A song about install.rb that was so freaking funny we were all at the brink of tears in laughter.
Unquestionably one of the funniest (and weirdest) introductions to a programming language ever.
The additional 150 seats have been sold in less than 24 hours. So we're all full and ready to rock with 550 Railers in the house.
posts.key_insight: The early Rails community distinguished itself by celebrating artistic and comedic expression as central to its culture, not just technical achievement.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates MeasureMap's acquisition by Google, noting it as the first Ruby on Rails application to be acquired in a Web 2.0-era buyout. He finds it particularly amusing that the product was bought before it even officially launched, joking about selling futures on ideas he hasn't even had yet. He congratulates the team, which includes Rails core member Nicholas Seckar and designer Jeffrey Veen, and praises the product's approach to statistics. He closes with a nudge toward launching and embracing the 'Getting Real' philosophy.
key_points: MeasureMap was the first Ruby on Rails application acquired in a Web 2.0 buyout
The product was bought by Google before it even publicly launched, highlighting the speculative nature of Web 2.0 acquisitions
The team included notable figures like Rails core member Nicholas Seckar and Jeffrey Veen
DHH uses the acquisition to subtly validate Rails as a serious technology for building acquirable products
money_quotes: I believe that's the first Ruby on Rails application to be picked up in a Web 2.0'ish buyout.
And it didn't even have to launch, take that Yahoo!
I'm now having a sale of futures in ideas for apps that I haven't even thought of. Who's bidding?
Statistics that goes beyond the numbers.
Now let's see some Getting Real and a launch!
posts.key_insight: The acquisition of a pre-launch Rails app by Google served as early validation that Ruby on Rails was a serious technology platform, even as DHH poked fun at the absurdity of Web 2.0 buyout culture.

posts.summary: DHH announces the launch of Campfire, 37signals' fifth product — a browser-based group chat application for businesses. He highlights it as their most advanced Ajax application to date, noting the technical challenges of real-time polling. The post emphasizes their philosophy of building products they need themselves and celebrates the contributions of new team members Sam Stephenson and Marcel Molina.
key_points: Campfire is a browser-based group chat tool that was 37signals' most technically ambitious Ajax application at the time
The real-time polling architecture (every 3 seconds per active user) created significant resource challenges they were actively optimizing
Building products they personally need ('dogfooding') remains their core product development philosophy
The launch doubled as a proving ground for new hires Sam Stephenson and Marcel Molina
money_quotes: Group chat for businesses in the browser.
It's by far the most advanced Ajax application we've done, but also the most resource intensive.
Campfire feels as natural as a web-page and IRC at the same time.
I've found that's been the best way for us to do something interesting: Just need it!
So I'm naturally very proud of both of them and happy to share a "ship it!" moment with them.
posts.key_insight: The best products come from building what you genuinely need yourself, even when the technical challenges are significant.

posts.summary: DHH responds to Russell Beattie's claim that building web apps is 'moronic' and that startups should focus on mobile instead. DHH argues that Campfire was deliberately built as a web app because its use case—long-running text-based business chat—is poorly suited to mobile devices. He notes that despite being a self-described mobile enthusiast, he finds phone-based chat impractical compared to a full keyboard. He warns against treating mobile as the primary platform for most applications and suggests Yahoo would suffer if it followed such advice.
key_points: Beattie's claim that web apps are 'last decade's technology' and mobile is the 'real revolution' is misguided for most use cases
Campfire was intentionally designed for desktop because its core use case—extended business discussion and decision-making—requires a full keyboard
Despite being an avid mobile fan, DHH finds phone-based chat apps are mostly 'party favors' rather than practical tools
Only a handful of apps have a perfect fit for mobile-first; most business software does not
Following mobile-first dogma uncritically would be a strategic mistake for companies like Yahoo
money_quotes: Stages of reaction: 1) WTF?, 2) Is he serious?, 3) What is he talking about?
Making Campfire primarily for phones would have been a horrible idea.
Discussing at length over most phones simply sucks.
I have both AIM and IRC applications on my phone, but they've rarely served as anything but party favors. As in 'look what you can do with a mobile these days!'
For practical use, it's very rare that I can't hold whether I'm interested in saying until I get in front of a real keyboard.
I caution anyone to take Beattie's hyper excitement for the mobile as platform as sound business advice.
I also hope Yahoo is not listening too much to this. I'd hate to see them follow this advice and treat the computer as a second-rank device after the mobile.
posts.key_insight: Building for the right platform matters more than chasing the next platform hype—most business software is better served by the web than by mobile.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates the arrival of his new MacBook Pro with Intel Core Duo processor, which he received early due to an Apple ordering bug. He benchmarks it by running the Active Record test suite against MySQL, finding it 3.3 times faster than his PowerBook G4. More impressively, he notes the MacBook Pro maintains its performance under heavy multitasking loads, while the G4 slows dramatically. The post captures the excitement of the Intel transition era for Mac users.
key_points: Active Record's 705 tests ran in 11 seconds on the MacBook Pro vs 36 seconds on a PowerBook G4, a 3.3x speedup
Real-world multitasking performance is where the MacBook Pro truly shines over the G4, which 'croaks' under load
Lab-coat benchmarks don't capture the feel of everyday performance under real working conditions
An Apple ordering system bug resulted in delivery just five days after ordering
money_quotes: There's never a bug so bad that it doesn't benefit someone, so yay for computer fallacies.
The dots were racing across the screen and in just 11 seconds, it was done, telling me that 705 tests and 2307 assertions exhibited 0 failures and 0 errors.

The most impressive part of the MacBook Pro is not its peak performance against a G4 when both machines just run a single application. No, the dazzle hits when you actually use your machine for real work.
That's the kind of stuff lab-coat benchmarks rarely pin down and something that takes more of a feel than a stopwatch.
The MacBook just feels freaking fast.
posts.key_insight: Real-world multitasking performance matters more than single-application benchmarks, and the Intel MacBook Pro's ability to maintain speed under load was its true breakthrough over the G4.

posts.summary: DHH announces that the 'Agile Web Development with Rails' book has been translated into French, German, and Japanese, expanding Rails' reach beyond English-speaking programmers. He highlights the translators' contributions, particularly noting Shugo Maeda's involvement with the Japanese version given his prominence in the Ruby community. The post frames these translations as evidence of Rails becoming a global phenomenon and expresses gratitude toward the translators for making the framework more accessible internationally.
key_points: The Rails book has been translated into French (by Laurent Julliard and Richard Piacentini), German (by Hanser), and Japanese (with help from Shugo Maeda)
The translations signal Rails' growth from an English-language tool into a global programming phenomenon
DHH emphasizes the importance of making technical resources available in non-English languages to broaden adoption
money_quotes: The Agile Web Development with Rails book is reaching beyond the tens of thousands of English speaking programmers who picked it up.
These three books goes a long way to show what a global phenomenon Ruby on Rails has become.
I'm very grateful to all the translators for making Rails even more accessible in countries where English is not the primary language for programming.
posts.key_insight: The translation of the Rails book into three major languages marked an early milestone in Rails' transformation from an English-language project into a global development framework.

posts.summary: DHH humorously announces 'Emo Programming' as a satirical new methodology created with Kathy Sierra. The post parodies the software development industry's obsession with branded methodologies like Agile and Pragmatic Programming. It playfully suggests that emotional satisfaction and happiness should be the primary goals of programming, complete with a mock manifesto. The tongue-in-cheek piece pokes fun at how developers rally around movement labels while making a genuine underlying point about passion in coding.
key_points: Satirizes the software industry's tendency to brand programming philosophies as movements with manifestos and trademarks
Playfully argues that emotional fulfillment and happiness in programming matter more than purely rational tool selection
Mocks common developer mantras like 'best tool for the job' and 'getting things done' as insufficient
Collaboration with Kathy Sierra (known for 'Creating Passionate Users') signals a genuine belief in passion-driven development underneath the humor
money_quotes: Forget agile, forget pragmatic, no, the new way is: The Emo Programmer™ — a practitioner of Emotional Programming™.
This is not merely a label, nay, it's a new way of life for programmers seeking a higher state of satisfaction and fulfillment in their symbioses with code.
It's the pursuit of happiness put at the forefront and given turbo.
This is a unique chance to escape such silly constraints as 'the best tool for the job' or simply be about 'getting things done'.
You too can be happy, you too can be Emo™.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's endless cycle of branded methodologies is ripe for parody, but the underlying desire for programmers to find genuine happiness and emotional fulfillment in their work is real.

posts.summary: DHH shares 30-day sales results for 37signals' self-published book Getting Real, which earned $120,000 on roughly 5,750-10,000 copies. He highlights that this is 12 times more revenue than their traditionally published Defensive Design book, despite fewer units sold. The post makes a strong case for self-publishing by showing that the traditional publishing model would have required writing 12 books to match the same revenue. DHH acknowledges Getting Real may not be typical but urges others with viable ideas to consider self-publishing.
key_points: Getting Real earned 12x more revenue than Defensive Design despite selling fewer copies
Self-publishing fundamentally changes the economics by cutting out the traditional publisher's take
Even 'best seller' status in traditional publishing (5,000 copies) can mean relatively modest revenue
DHH encourages others to self-publish and share their numbers to see if the model is replicable
money_quotes: Less units sold, 12x more revenue.
We would have had to write 12 books through the traditional publishing model to earn this kind of money on the same number of sales.
That's just an entirely different scale and game.
Apparently 5,000 copies count as a "best seller", despite bringing in 1/12th of the Getting Real book.
If you think you have an idea with enough steam to carry through sales of just a thousand units or more, I urge you to consider self-publishing.
posts.key_insight: Self-publishing can generate dramatically more revenue per unit sold than traditional publishing, making it a far better economic model for authors with an existing audience.

posts.summary: Responding to a prediction that Ruby on Rails won't become mainstream, DHH argues that mainstream adoption is actually overrated. He contends that mainstream means reaching people who don't care, which brings more downsides than benefits for an open source project. Instead, he values having a sustainable ecosystem with critical mass — enough people and businesses to ensure long-term survival. He urges the Rails community to optimize for passionate, like-minded developers rather than watering down the project to chase broad adoption.
key_points: Mainstream adoption primarily means reaching people who don't care about your project, which brings significant downsides
A sustainable ecosystem with critical mass is more valuable than mainstream status for open source projects
Going mainstream usually means slowing progress to a crawl and dealing with secondary concerns that dilute the original vision
Open source communities should optimize for passionate, self-selecting contributors rather than chasing the lowest common denominator
money_quotes: I believe that the mainstream is overrated.
To me, mainstream is mostly about reaching people who just don't care.
Solve our own problems, not be a vendor, and all that.
Would you rather be part of the mainstream or a smaller community with critical mass?
Going mainstream usually means slowing progress until it resembles a complete stop and dealing with a whole other level of secondary concerns.
Optimize for those who sympathize with our cultural leanings instead of trying to water it down by reaching for the lowest common denominator of enterprisy glitter and mainstream blandness.
Let the willing and the able self-select out of the mainstream and into the upstream.
posts.key_insight: An open source project should prioritize a passionate, sustainable community with critical mass over chasing mainstream adoption, which dilutes culture and slows progress.

posts.summary: DHH highlights and endorses blog posts by Chris Petrilli and James Robertson that critique enterprise software culture. The post amplifies their arguments that big IT budgets reflect architectural bloat and turf wars rather than success, and that most applications are far simpler than the enterprise industry wants people to believe. DHH frames complexity addiction as a disease spread by enterprise jargon and consulting culture.
key_points: Large IT budgets typically signal runaway architecture and organizational dysfunction, not project success
Most IT budgets could be cut 30-70% by simply saying 'no' to unnecessary complexity
Most applications are not nearly as complicated as analysts, vendors, and 'propeller heads' claim
Enterprise complexity is self-perpetuating, driven by those who profit from it
money_quotes: The addiction to complexity is a disease and the enterprisey babble is its vector.
Huge budgets are usually indicative of run-away architecture and absurd turf-wars and protectionism, not actual success.
Most IT budgets I've been around could be shaved 30-70% by the judicious use of the word 'no,' and the refusal to accept stock answers from astronauts and certification-wielding automatons.
Most applications just aren't that complicated. The propeller heads would like you to think they are - too many analysts want you to think they are - and too many vendors want you to think they are.
Chris, James, and others are doing a great job helping to fight the contamination.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise software complexity is largely artificial, sustained by vendors, consultants, and architects who profit from making simple problems appear difficult.

posts.summary: DHH endorses Kathy Sierra's research on how exposure to negative people can structurally and chemically degrade your brain. He argues that toxic environments can be identified by their disproportionate intensity over minor issues. He advocates for the 'It Just Doesn't Matter' technique as a tool for maintaining perspective, comparing it to the economic concept of sunk costs. He concludes that maintaining mental well-being requires deliberate effort and applauds decisions to limit negativity in online spaces.
key_points: Exposure to unhappy people makes you unhappy — treat toxic environments like biohazards and limit your time in them
Disproportionate intensity over minor issues is a red flag for a contaminated environment
The 'It Just Doesn't Matter' technique provides distance and perspective, similar to ignoring sunk costs in decision-making
Being a happy person requires deliberate intent and action, not passive hope
Preserving your mental health is more important than allowing unrestricted negative commentary on your platform
money_quotes: Your brain is a precious piece of work. Allowing it to degrade structurally and chemically by over-exposing it to bad influences is obviously not a good idea.
When an inordinate amount of intensity is allocated to addressing seemingly minor priority, it's a red flag for contamination.
Given incident A, do I choose to respond with X or Y? The incident has already occurred, no way to take that back, so all your response needs to consider is 'am I making it better or worse?'.
Applying a healthy dose It Just Doesn't Matter gives you distance to the incident and perspective for the time after your reaction.
When deciding what to do today, don't consider what resources you spent yesterday. Those are already gone.
It's hard work to be a happy shiny person. There are techniques out there to help you, but it requires intent and action.
Preserving a healthy chemical balance in your brain is indefinitely more important than allowing some random slob to sling his malice on your domain.
posts.key_insight: Protecting your mental environment from disproportionate negativity is an act of self-preservation that requires conscious technique, not just good intentions.

posts.summary: DHH announces the release of 'Getting Real,' a book by 37signals compiling their philosophy on building web applications. The book is released as PDF-only, deliberately avoiding traditional paper publishing which they view as inefficient. It contains ninety essays covering their approach to software development, serving as a complement to their Getting Real workshops. The book sold over 1,750 copies in its first day.
key_points: 37signals chose PDF-only distribution to avoid the inefficiencies of traditional paper publishing
The book is a collection of 90 short essays designed to be read in small increments, embodying their 'say less' philosophy
The book serves as an accessible alternative to their Getting Real workshops which frequently sell out
The content focuses on software philosophy rather than trendy technologies like Ajax or Web 2.0
money_quotes: When you live and breathe in the web space, the world of paper publishing is a scary place fraught with inefficiencies and legacy ways.
Since we're all about saying less, we thought instead just to write a short book that's really just a collection of essays.
Despite being hip and youthful, the book doesn't talk a lot about Ajax and other Web 2.0 snazziness.
posts.key_insight: 37signals practices what it preaches by applying its 'less is more' product philosophy to the book itself — choosing PDF over print, essays over chapters, and principles over trendy technology topics.

posts.summary: DHH shares a quote from Java creator James Gosling dismissing PHP and Ruby as mere 'scripting languages' that 'just generate web pages' and have 'serious scaling and performance problems.' Rather than mounting a detailed rebuttal, DHH lets the quote speak for itself, implying Gosling's characterization is so obviously wrong or outdated that it needs no commentary. The post is a brief, confident dismissal of establishment criticism from a Java luminary.
key_points: James Gosling publicly dismissed Ruby and PHP as limited scripting languages at a Sun conference
DHH responds not with argument but with amused incredulity, suggesting the criticism is self-evidently absurd
The post reflects the recurring tension between enterprise Java culture and dynamic language communities in the mid-2000s
money_quotes: I love quotes like that. You don't even have to add commentary. Just imagine a puzzled face with raised eyebrows.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best rebuttal to establishment dismissal is to simply let the critic's own words reveal how out of touch they are.

posts.summary: DHH describes the final sprint before the Rails 1.1 release, noting that contrary to expectations, the most significant evolution happens near the end rather than just polish work. He observes that closing on a release paradoxically generates the most new ideas and additions. The post serves as a teaser for upcoming detailed writeups on new features, while linking to community members' documentation of the changes.
key_points: The final sprint before a software release generates the most creative evolution, not just polish
Rails 1.1 gained significant new features during its closing phase, including a new integration testing framework
The Rails community was actively documenting and showcasing changes through guides and changelog commentary
money_quotes: The document that is Rails always evolves the most around closing and this release has been no different.
One would think this would lead to the final commas being placed, the font size being adjusted, and proper kerning of the headline being ensured. Not so.
posts.key_insight: Software projects experience their most creative evolution during the pressure of an imminent release, not during the long middle stretch of development.

posts.summary: DHH responds to James Gosling's comments comparing Java's power to Ruby/Rails by arguing Gosling conflates 'power' with 'versatility.' He contends that versatility—being able to do everything—comes at the cost of simplicity and productivity for specific tasks. Rails deliberately trades away versatility (like 'interplanetary navigation') to achieve greater power, productivity, and happiness for web applications. He argues that if you don't need a do-everything tool, choosing a focused one is the rational choice.
key_points: Gosling's claim about Java's 'power' over Ruby/Rails is really about versatility, not effectiveness at specific tasks
Greater versatility requires higher abstraction, which makes tools less useful for specific domains
The 'one tool for everything' mindset only makes sense if you assume programmers can't or won't learn multiple platforms
Rails intentionally trades versatility for simplicity, productivity, and programmer happiness
If your needs are modest and specific, choosing a focused tool over a versatile one is the rational decision
money_quotes: The greater the versatility, the higher the abstraction, the less useful for the specifics.
Saying you'll be everything to everyone, from 'web presentations' to 'interplanetary navigation' as Gosling puts it, is not free. You have to give up other desirable attributes to get that.
Rails achieves great power, productivity, and programmer happiness by trading in the option to 'interplanetary navigation'.
If you don't desire versatility for the task at hand, wouldn't you rather trade it in for simplicity, productivity, and programmer happiness?
If your model of programmers is that they're too busy/uninterested/dumb to ever learn more than one platform.
posts.key_insight: True power for a specific task comes from focus and trade-offs, not from a tool's ability to do everything—versatility is not the same as effectiveness.

posts.summary: DHH introduces a series on leveraging the HTTP Accept header in Rails to eliminate repetition when serving different clients (mobile, API, web) from the same controller logic. He argues that while Rails has done well with DRY principles in models and views, controller-level code reuse across different output formats has relied on hacks. He points to Backpack as a real-world example where 80% of actions are shared across clients, and frames the Accept header as the proper, standards-based solution to formalize this pattern.
key_points: Rails has successfully institutionalized DRY in domain models but controller-level client handling remained repetitive
Backpack already shared ~80% of controller actions across mobile, API, and web clients through ad-hoc hacks
The HTTP Accept header provides a standards-based mechanism to replace those hacks with a proper convention
This represents Rails extending its convention-over-configuration philosophy to content negotiation
money_quotes: Ruby on Rails is all about eliminating repetition through convention over configuration and any other DRY-hack we can come up with.
How DRY are we in handling different clients desiring different output from the same controller logic? Up until recently, not very.
It's those hacks and ideas that are now able to blossom through the (re-)discovery of the wonderful Accept header in the HTTP specification.
posts.key_insight: The HTTP Accept header offers a clean, standards-based way for Rails to apply DRY principles to serving multiple client types from a single controller action.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Ruby on Rails winning two Jolt Awards in 2006 — one for the 'Agile Web Development with Rails' book in technical books and another for Rails 1.0 in Web Development Tools. He notes the framework beat established competitors like JBoss, Coldfusion, and Zend Studio. He frames the wins as industry validation for a young open source project from Denmark and a small company, expressing gratitude to CMP Media and the judges while lamenting he and Dave Thomas couldn't attend the ceremony.
key_points: Rails 1.0 won the Jolt Award for Web Development Tools, beating JBoss 4.x, Backbase, Coldfusion, and Zend Studio
The companion book 'Agile Web Development with Rails' won the Jolt in technical books
DHH frames industry recognition as crucial for an underdog open source framework competing against established players
The post reflects early Rails-era optimism and DHH's confidence in disrupting the status quo
money_quotes: We won, we won! Not once, but twice.

Industry recognition from venerable organizations like CMP Media will no doubt help us break down the barriers that this young open source framework, born in little Denmark, and out of a small company still face against the big boys.
We want to, can, and will change the world.
posts.key_insight: Early industry recognition like the Jolt Awards provided crucial legitimacy for Rails as a scrappy open source underdog challenging established enterprise tools.

posts.summary: DHH describes breaking his cycle of buying the latest smart phones by switching to a simple Motorola PEBL. He explains that his enthusiasm for each new smart phone would last only weeks before he reverted to basic functions: calls, texts, and voicemail. He admits he simply doesn't encounter enough situations requiring a pocket computer, and finds freedom in accepting that reality rather than chasing features he won't use.
key_points: Smart phone excitement follows a predictable cycle: explore features for weeks, then revert to basic phone functions
The gap between what smart phones can do and what most people actually need them for is enormous
Self-awareness about actual usage patterns is more valuable than having the latest technology
There's liberation in choosing a 'dumb' device that does fewer things well rather than many things unnecessarily
money_quotes: After years following and buying the latest in smart phones, I've stepped out of the race, confessed to myself, and bought a Motorola PEBL.
Yes, it's a design masterpiece, but also just about the dumbest phone around these days.
See, my smart phone kicks only ever lasted three or four weeks. Just enough time to explore all the features, download all the interesting applications, and get all the hacks running.
Then, after all was explored, I would just make phone calls. And receive phone calls. And write text messages. And check my voice mail.
You know, stuff the phone has been capable of doing before it had a multitasking operating system and more power than my first three PCs.
I simply don't have enough situations available where I need the power of a computer in the palm of my hand.
I've admitted that to myself now, which means I can admire the Nokia N80 without desiring it.
posts.key_insight: Honestly assessing what you actually use versus what you think you want frees you from the endless cycle of chasing the latest technology.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the WS-* stack of web services standards (SOAP, WSDL, WS-Transfer) promoted by Microsoft and IBM is needlessly complex and losing relevance to simpler REST/HTTP/XML approaches. He notes that 37signals never considered SOAP for Basecamp's API and that web-native developers increasingly view WS-* as absurd. He cites Tim Bray's argument that REST is actually more standards-based than the unratified WS-* specs. The post concludes that if big vendors won't embrace web simplicity, smaller players will route around them.
key_points: The gap between enterprise 'Web Services' (WS-*) and simple web services (REST/HTTP) is widening, with HTTP awareness rising
37signals chose REST over SOAP for Basecamp's API and wouldn't consider WS-* for any product
WS-* specs are mostly unratified vendor proposals from IBM and Microsoft, while HTTP and XML have been stable international standards for a decade
Big vendors like Sun acknowledge REST's superiority privately while maintaining enterprise WS-* commitments publicly
The web's simple building blocks will win regardless of whether large vendors embrace them
money_quotes: The gulf between Web Services™ and web services have been widening as of late.
I couldn't dream of going down that path for any products we're working on at 37signals.
For the web native, interest has turned to bafflement over HTTP over XML over HTTP and other WS-* silliness coming out of the Microsoft and IBM lead 'standards' committees.
There's only one option available today that's actually standards-based.
If the big vendors can't or won't take advantage of the simplicity that the core building blocks of the web are providing us, then rest assured that others will.
WS-* appears to be yet another ivory tower going up in a place no sane native would want to live.
The village of the web is already plenty strong to withstand the distraction. Complex lights, flickering buzzwords, and long roadmaps to nowhere notwithstanding.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise web service standards from big vendors are doomed ivory towers because the web's simple building blocks — HTTP, XML, and REST — already provide everything developers actually need.

posts.summary: DHH notes that Rails, 37signals, and himself are featured in both Wired and BusinessWeek on the same day. While flattered, he finds the experience surreal and reflects on the contrast between print media's heavy production process and the simplicity of blogging. He marvels at how much effort goes into short print pieces, including multiple photo sessions lasting hours.
key_points: Rails and 37signals received simultaneous coverage in two major publications, marking a milestone in mainstream recognition
Print media's wide distribution gives coverage a sense of gravitas that feels 'puffy' compared to online content
The production overhead of print magazines—hours of photo sessions for short articles—contrasts sharply with the immediacy of blogging
money_quotes: When it rains, it pours.
There's just something about paper and wide distribution that makes it all seem so puffy.
Imagine that they have to do all that work for so many small pieces.
Certainly a different world from just jotting your notes down on a blog and possibly slapping on a half-assed photo.
posts.key_insight: The heavy production process of print media feels absurdly disproportionate compared to the effortless immediacy of blogging.

posts.summary: DHH mocks James McGovern's critique that Ruby isn't 'enterprise ready' by highlighting the absurd density of the word 'enterprise' in McGovern's credentials and writings. Rather than engaging with the technical arguments, DHH uses McGovern as a case study for everything wrong with enterprise culture — the self-important titles, the convoluted writing, and the gatekeeping mentality. The post is a brief, dismissive takedown that treats the critique as self-refuting by virtue of its source.
key_points: The word 'enterprise' has become so overused and hollow that stacking credentials full of it undermines rather than supports credibility
DHH dismisses McGovern's Ruby critique not on technical merits but by attacking the enterprise culture it represents
Self-proclaimed 'thought leaders' with elaborate titles are treated as symptoms of enterprise dysfunction rather than authorities
money_quotes: The word 'enterprise' has long been in bad shape, but I think James McGovern just drove the final stake through its ambiguous heart.
You may not have the patience to finish through his tirade.
posts.key_insight: When someone's entire identity is built around the word 'enterprise,' their critique of something not being 'enterprise ready' says more about enterprise culture than about the technology in question.

posts.summary: DHH rallies against enterprise software complexity, building on a blogosphere debate sparked by James McGovern's rant. He approvingly cites Dare Obasanjo's argument that the complexity pushed by enterprise architects and consulting firms is unnecessary bullshit. DHH argues that the web's transparent simplicity has exposed the fraud of over-engineered enterprise solutions. He calls on developers not to abandon big business to the 'enterprise astronauts' but to actively help companies escape needless complexity.
key_points: Enterprise architects, vendors, and big consulting firms push unnecessary complexity that the web has proven is not needed
The web's success as a global distributed system disproves the claim that business problems require enormously complex solutions
Rather than smugly watching enterprise approaches fail, developers should actively promote simpler alternatives
Big business does not inherently require the painful, bloated solutions that enterprise vendors sell
money_quotes: That's the wonder of the web. It's so much harder to sell black holes of complexity when transparent examples of easier, simpler, and less are everywhere.
You can only peddle a recipe of more software, more layers, more consultants, and more vendors for so long. The success of the web has stamped this strategy with an expiration date.

We could cherish moments of smug self-satisfaction as the towers of ineptness crumble to the hand of competition at some point in the future. But it is nothing against the joy we could have helping good businesses escape this morass of bad intentions.
Strike down the assumptions that big business and big money necessitates the world of hurt that enterprise astronauts carry in their vendor-branded laptop bags.
So let's keep up the heat. Increase the awareness of alternatives.
posts.key_insight: The web's success as a simple, global-scale distributed system proves that enterprise software complexity is manufactured by vendors and consultants, not demanded by actual business problems.

posts.summary: DHH announces the first release candidate for Rails 1.1 after three months of development. The release includes roughly 500 new features, fixes, and changes. While highlighting major features like a Ruby-to-Javascript compiler and polymorphic associations, he emphasizes that the hundreds of small community-contributed patches are what truly make the release special. He credits the low barrier to contributing and the talented community for the deep polish Rails has achieved.
key_points: Rails 1.1 RC1 includes approximately 500 changes across features, fixes, and improvements
Major features include a Ruby-to-Javascript compiler, polymorphic associations, join associations, and integration testing
The real strength of the release lies in hundreds of small patches from contributors worldwide
Low barriers to contribution and a talented community enable deep polish that no single team could achieve
money_quotes: When you live on Edge Rails, it's quick to forget just how much has changed since the last public release.
What I really like about Rails 1.1 are the hundreds of tiny patches. Wrinkles ironed out by software developers from all over the world.
People who found something ugly and made it beautiful.
The kind of deep polish you can only get when the barrier to entry for patching Rails is as low as it is and when you have such an incredible talent pool to draw the best insights from.
I am more proud than ever of what we have been able to accomplish together.
posts.key_insight: The true power of open source lies not in headline features but in the hundreds of small improvements contributed by a global community with low barriers to participation.

posts.summary: DHH criticizes the standard deals offered by traditional tech book publishers, arguing that authors are getting exploited. He highlights the typical arrangement: 10% of profits with months-long payment delays, forced use of Word for writing, and a 3+ month production cycle. In return, publishers provide minimal marketing while expecting authors to handle much of the editing. He pushes back against the conventional wisdom that tech books shouldn't be expected to make money, arguing that low earnings are a direct result of the exploitative profit split rather than an inherent truth about tech publishing.
key_points: Standard tech book deals give authors only ~10% of profits with 4-6 month payment delays
Publishers provide minimal value (some editing, cover design, distribution) while taking 90% of profits
The industry normalizes poor author compensation by framing tech books as being about fame rather than money
Low author earnings are a structural problem caused by the profit split, not an inherent feature of the market
money_quotes: I've been talking to a lot of friends and acquaintances who are writing tech books for a wide variety of old-school publishers and I can't believe the deals they're taking.

So you get some editing, a cover/layout, and the distribution done for you. Is that worth 90% of the profits and the torture of writing a book in Word and then bouncing versioned documents back and forth?
The standard sugar coating of this setup is that you should not expect to make money writing a tech book.
Why on earth would you want to accept the premise that writing a book is not going to be worth it for the money?!
Of course it's not going to be for the money when you only land 1/10th of the crumbs that trickle back to the publishers from Amazon and independent retailers.
posts.key_insight: The conventional wisdom that tech authors shouldn't expect to make money is a self-fulfilling prophecy created by publishers who take 90% of the profits while providing minimal value in return.

posts.summary: DHH announces the second edition of 'Agile Web Development with Rails,' which is essentially a complete rewrite covering new features and best practices from the past year. Despite the book selling over 40,000 copies, they chose to overhaul it rather than rest on its success. The beta version is available immediately as a PDF, with a hybrid format mixing new and old chapters that will be progressively updated. He notes that even Rails core team members routinely discover features they missed, justifying the new edition.
key_points: The first edition sold over 40,000 copies, making it one of the best-selling programming books of its period
The second edition is a near-complete rewrite covering RJS, join models, migrations, RESTful interfaces, and Capistrano
The beta book ships as a hybrid package with new chapters clearly marked alongside old content, updated progressively
Rails 1.1 alone had over 500 changes, meaning even core team members missed features
money_quotes: That makes it one of the best selling computer programming books in its period, which would probably make the conventional wisdom "don't screw with the cash cow". But how much fun would that be?
It's more or less a complete rewrite that'll include the latest approaches and best practices developed in the Rails community over the past year.
With the more than 500 changes launched in Rails 1.1 alone, I can almost guarantee you that you missed something.
Even members of the core team are routinely pleasantly surprised learning about new nuggets they didn't catch on commit.
We're cycling through the metaphors. Now we've done trains and tracks, skating and bars, so you just know that the 3rd edition will be feature white lines of powder on a mirror.
posts.key_insight: A successful technical book should be aggressively rewritten to match the pace of framework evolution rather than milked as a static cash cow.

posts.summary: DHH announces RailsConf Europe, scheduled for September 14-15 in London, as a follow-up to the sold-out RailsConf Chicago. The Chicago event sold all 550 seats before the program was even finalized, leaving many without access. The London conference will feature prominent speakers including Dave Thomas, Kathy Sierra, and DHH himself. Tickets range from £400 to £575, with proceeds going to the non-profit RubyCentral rather than to speakers or organizers. The post urges people to act fast given limited capacity of roughly 500 spots.
key_points: RailsConf Chicago sold out 550 seats in under a week, before the program was announced, demonstrating explosive Rails community growth
RailsConf Europe in London is positioned as a remedy for those who missed Chicago, especially Europeans
All speakers are volunteering their time, and profits go to the non-profit RubyCentral to fund Ruby hackathons
Ticket prices of £400-£575 reflect the cost of flying US speakers to central London
money_quotes: 550 seats were snatched up before the program was even ready or a week had passed.
None of the speakers are taking home a dime and Chad Fowler has been working his ass off for free through RubyCentral, which is the non-profit organization putting on this show.
So at least you know your quid isn't being blown on white powdery rails. All the hype is 100% home-grown.
When they're gone, they're gone. If you want to go to an official Rails conference this year, meet and hear the core team among others speak, this is the last chance.
posts.key_insight: The Rails community's explosive growth in 2006 was so intense that a single sold-out conference couldn't contain the demand, necessitating a European expansion built entirely on volunteer labor and non-profit principles.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates making the cover of Linux Journal, which dedicated an entire issue to Ruby and Rails. He notes this tops his earlier Wired feature and highlights the impressive lineup of Ruby/Rails content in the issue. He reflects on how far Rails has come in just 22 months since version 0.5, pointing to additional press coverage in Dr. Dobb's as further validation. The post serves as an excited lead-up to the upcoming RailsConf.
key_points: Linux Journal dedicated an entire issue to Ruby and Rails, featuring DHH on the cover
The issue included an interview, Ruby introduction, RubyGems tutorial, enterprise Ruby usage, and a Rails Ajax tutorial
Rails had grown from version 0.5 to major press recognition in just 22 months
Dr. Dobb's Journal also featured Rails coverage in the same month
money_quotes: A cover, dammit!
Even better than my smug face on glossy paper is the dedication of this issue to Ruby and Rails.
I'm so proud to see this mark in big letters how far we've come over the last 22 months or so since Rails 0.5 saw the light of day for the first time.
posts.key_insight: Rails achieved mainstream technical press recognition remarkably quickly, going from initial release to Linux Journal cover story in under two years.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates being named #34 on Business 2.0's '50 People Who Matter' list, ranking ahead of Oprah Winfrey at #38. He playfully notes the irony of being honored by the same magazine whose startup guide he had previously criticized. The post is a brief, tongue-in-cheek victory lap that pokes fun at the arbitrariness of such rankings while clearly enjoying the recognition.
key_points: DHH was ranked #34 on Business 2.0's '50 People Who Matter' list, ahead of Oprah Winfrey at #38
He jokes that influence rankings matter more than personal fortunes in the billions
He highlights the irony that the magazine honored him despite his previous public criticism of their startup guide
He satirizes the credibility of media rankings by declaring Business 2.0 a 'complete authority' only after they listed him
money_quotes: Who needs a personal fortune in the billions when you can snub those who do on a list like Business 2.0's 50 People Who Matter.
Now that they put my name on a list, they're of course a completely authority. And you should listen!
posts.key_insight: DHH uses a magazine ranking to humorously illustrate how media credibility is subjective and self-serving, while openly enjoying the recognition.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his experience at RubyKaigi in Japan, where he was surprised by Rails' massive popularity in the Japanese Ruby community. He describes the enthusiastic reception including laptop signings and constant photography. He then transitions to excitement about the upcoming RailsConf in Chicago, marveling at 550 attendees gathering just two years after Rails' creation.
key_points: Rails had unexpectedly large adoption in Japan, with nearly every RubyKaigi session mentioning it
The Japanese Ruby community had developed Japan-specific plugins like ActiveHeart that DHH wasn't aware of
RailsConf was about to launch with 550 attendees, a milestone for a two-year-old framework
DHH expresses genuine humility at the rapid growth of the Rails community
money_quotes: I really had no idea that Rails was so big in Japan.
Pretty much all of the sessions at the conference that I attended mentioned Rails in one way or the other.
550(!) people gathered for four days of Rails.
Who would have thought we'd come this far in just under two years?
I'm humbled again and again (even if my one tough-guy face doesn't reveal it in recent photo shots ;)).
posts.key_insight: Rails' rapid international adoption, particularly its unexpected popularity in Japan, demonstrated that the framework had grown far beyond its origins into a global movement in just two years.

posts.summary: DHH describes his discovery that applying CRUD constraints and HTTP resource-oriented thinking to Rails controllers has profoundly simplified his application development. Working on Sunrise, the next 37signals app, he found that the biggest benefit wasn't REST itself but the second-order effect on domain modeling. He presented these ideas at RubyKaigi and RailsConf, acknowledging some audience anxiety about further constraining Rails conventions. He frames the resource-oriented approach as voluntary but transformative, promising simpler controllers, free REST APIs, and richer domain models.
key_points: CRUD-constrained controllers lead to profound simplification in Rails development, with extractions already emerging from the Sunrise project
The most significant benefit of resource-oriented design is not HTTP/REST itself but its second-order impact on domain modeling
The insight parallels TDD: just as TDD is more about design than tests, resources are more about modeling than protocol compliance
DHH acknowledges community concern about tightening design constraints but emphasizes the approach is voluntary, not mandated
money_quotes: What if the whole world could be modeled under the constraints of CRUD?

The most profound benefit I'm witnessing of this renaissance for HTTP has not been related to first-order glories of the protocol itself. But rather to the second-order impact it has on domain modeling.
It's somewhat similar to the notion that test-driven development is less about tests and more about design.
Is DHH trying to tighten the belts on the straight jacket of design once more?
If you choose to partake in the renaissance, be prepared to raise your game to a new level of delight.

There's a rejuvenation awaiting you. One that'll simplify your controllers, bless your application with an almost-free REST API, and elevate your domain model even further away from the depths of anemic.
posts.key_insight: Constraining controllers to CRUD operations on resources yields its greatest benefit not in REST compliance but in forcing cleaner, richer domain models.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Rails turning two years old by reflecting on its journey from a niche idea to a global phenomenon. He notes Ruby's explosive growth in book sales, surpassing both Perl and Python. He pushes back against skeptics who dismissed Rails as a passing fad, arguing that its appeal to beauty, happiness, and developer satisfaction represents a lasting trend rather than transient hype. He salutes early adopters who endured ridicule and declares that Rails has moved past infatuation into lasting love.
key_points: Ruby book sales jumped ~700% on top of 1500% the prior year, surpassing Perl and Python
Rails proved wrong the skeptics who gave it weeks or months before developers moved on to the next shiny thing
Rails bridged the gap between quick prototyping and clean code, attracting smart developers from mainstream camps
Developer happiness and passion are legitimate values, not embarrassing fads
The framework's sustained growth signals a lasting trend rather than hype
money_quotes: One of my guilty pleasures is proving people wrong.
Few things get me more fired up to achieve than hearing how inappropriate or idealistic or unrealistic the idea that I'm pushing is.
Rails did its part to bridge the worlds of quick and clean. Bridge dazzle and sensibility.
Attracting smart people from the mainstream camps and giving them a story to believe in: "It doesn't have to suck".
That story simply isn't a fad, it's a trend.
A story that allows for words like passion and enthusiasm to be part of the sanctioned vocabulary of developers without the need to make excuses for yourself.
We're past the point of infatuation, this is love, and love is inclusive.
posts.key_insight: Rails' sustained growth proved that developer happiness and aesthetic pleasure in tools are lasting values, not passing fads.

posts.summary: DHH congratulates Allan Odgaard on TextMate winning the Apple Design Award for Best Mac OS X Developer Tool. He reflects on helping Allan get TextMate released two years prior, motivated by his own frustration with Mac programming editors. DHH considers TextMate the most important OS X application on his machine, arguing it stands in a class of its own with no worthy alternatives. He credits TextMate as a significant factor in Ruby on Rails programmers adopting Macs.
key_points: TextMate won the Apple Design Award for Best Mac OS X Developer Tool in 2006
DHH helped Allan Odgaard get TextMate released, motivated by frustration with existing Mac programming editors
TextMate became uniquely important in the Mac ecosystem with no real alternatives
TextMate played a significant role in driving Ruby on Rails developers to adopt Macs
money_quotes: I must admit that I was acting on entirely selfish reasons. I was dreading the choices for a programming editor on the Mac and saw TextMate as the chance to escape the under-whelming offerings.
TextMate is today without a doubt the most important OS X application on my machine.
Most everything else has worthy alternatives, but TextMate is in a class of its own.
It's a big part of the reason that Ruby on Rails programmers are picking up Macs in droves.
posts.key_insight: Great developer tools emerge when frustrated developers take matters into their own hands, and a single indispensable application can drive platform adoption for an entire programming community.

posts.summary: DHH dissects Joel Spolsky's 'Language Wars' article as a textbook example of Fear, Uncertainty, and Doubt (FUD) against Ruby on Rails. He breaks down Spolsky's arguments into classic FUD categories: fear of scaling problems, uncertainty about enterprise readiness, and doubt about mission-critical reliability. DHH then delivers his knockout punch by revealing that Spolsky's own product, FogBugz, is built on Wasabi — a proprietary, in-house programming language that compiles to VBScript, JavaScript, and PHP — making Spolsky's warnings about choosing 'safe' and 'mainstream' technologies deeply hypocritical.
key_points: Spolsky's critique of Ruby on Rails follows the classic FUD pattern: fear of scaling, uncertainty about enterprise readiness, and doubt about mission-critical use
DHH notes Spolsky was late to the anti-Rails FUD party, having previously endorsed VBScript and criticized exceptions
The central irony is that Spolsky built FogBugz on Wasabi, a private one-off language, while warning others against using anything non-mainstream
DHH frames the hypocrisy as disqualifying: advising others to pick 'safe' technologies while building your own business on an in-house language no one else uses
money_quotes: It's one of the purest forms of FUD I've ever seen against Ruby and Rails. Not even the best of the Java guys can keep up with Joel on this.
It wasn't too long ago Joel thought VBScript was a reasonable language for web development.
So Joel and friends invented their own language, which has to reasonably compile to three and a half different ones.
Yes, they're building their Serious Business Stuff application on a 1-off, closed language. So please do as I say, not as I do, dammit.
I swear I couldn't make this stuff up even if I tried. Joel, you're my new hero of irony.
And pick something mainstream and 'safe'.
posts.key_insight: Critics who warn against adopting new technologies as too risky often build their own businesses on far riskier, less proven foundations — exposing their arguments as FUD rather than genuine technical assessment.

posts.summary: DHH investigates whether Joel Spolsky's custom programming language 'Wasabi' — a VBScript compiler that emits PHP — is a joke or real. After recalling a year-old interview where Joel advocated building on technology you control, DHH concludes it's genuine. Joel confirms directly that Wasabi is real, arguing custom languages work for installable multi-platform software while enterprises should stick with mainstream languages to avoid career risk. DHH finds this worldview depressing and disputes Joel's FUD about alternative frameworks like Rails.
key_points: Joel Spolsky built a custom compiler for VBScript that targets PHP (and potentially other platforms), eventually called Wasabi, to maintain platform portability for FogBugz
Joel's philosophy is that companies should build on technology they control, even if it means creating their own language — a position DHH finds misguided
Joel draws a distinction between his own context (installable software) and enterprise contexts where the 'prime directive is not to get fired'
DHH argues Rails is fast enough for the vast majority of web applications, preferring developer productivity over raw performance
DHH sees Joel's enterprise advice as FUD that doesn't hold up under scrutiny
money_quotes: The benefit of the doubt would have made me err on the side of sanity.
So for Joel, portability is so important that you'll want to stay with a language like VBScript and just consider all other platforms a compile target.
By god, I wish it were, but I don't think so.
His claim goes that making your own language is great for his environment, but that an enterprisey context needs The Big Three because the prime directive is not to get fired.
I think that's a depressing world-view and it doesn't explain the FUD.
I just happen to care much more about free developer cycles and am willing to trade the former for the latter.
posts.key_insight: Building your own programming language to maintain platform control is a misguided form of NIH syndrome when mature, productive frameworks already exist — and optimizing for developer productivity beats optimizing for theoretical portability.

posts.summary: DHH argues that performance concerns shouldn't dictate your choice of programming language for an entire application. He demonstrates how Basecamp and Campfire use Ruby for 90% of their code while outsourcing performance-critical bottlenecks to C libraries or small C extensions. The post responds to critics who claim Ruby is too slow for real applications by showing that polyglot approaches are standard practice even at Yahoo and Amazon. He frames this selective optimization as smart engineering rather than cheating, and accuses those who let bottleneck fears drive technology choices of premature optimization.
key_points: Different programming languages excel at different tasks, and using multiple languages within a single application is standard practice at companies like Yahoo and Amazon
Ruby applications can delegate performance-intensive operations to C libraries (like RMagick for image resizing) without sacrificing productivity for the 90% of code that doesn't need raw speed
Campfire rewrote only its polling bottleneck (100 lines of Ruby to 300 lines of C) while keeping the rest of the application in Ruby — writing the whole app in C would have been madness
Most performance concerns are imaginary rather than real, and letting bottlenecks dictate your entire development environment is textbook premature optimization
The 'scripting language' ecosystem thrives precisely because it builds on top of fast C libraries rather than reinventing everything from scratch
money_quotes: I can hear it already: "But that's cheating!" Of course it is. Cheating is good, cheating works.
The era of islands is over for most development scenarios. You don't have to make one definitive choice.
So please don't let bottlenecks (real or imaginary, usually the latter) dictate your choice of software development environment.
Writing the entire application in C just so we could get the maximum performance from the polling service would have been madness, so I'm glad we didn't have to choose.
I don't hear a lot of people claiming that operating systems should be written in PHP. Or that guest books should be written in C.
You get to hog all the productivity you can for the common cases, then outsource the bottlenecks to existing packages in faster languages.
posts.key_insight: Smart engineering means writing 90% of your application in a productive high-level language and selectively outsourcing only the proven bottlenecks to faster languages, rather than letting performance anxiety dictate your entire technology choice.

posts.summary: DHH recaps RailsConf Europe 2006, expressing enthusiasm about the quality of talks, community energy, and impressive technical demonstrations. He briefly covers his own presentation on Active Resource and Simply Helpful, but dedicates most of the post to praising community contributions like HAML, UJS, JRuby, and Capistrano. He highlights keynotes from Kathy Sierra, _why, and Dave Thomas as standout moments. The post celebrates the rapid growth of the Rails community and looks ahead to future conferences co-organized with O'Reilly.
key_points: Community contributions like HAML, UJS, JRuby, and Capistrano impressed DHH more than his own presentation, showing Rails' maturing ecosystem
JRuby running Rails on Java demonstrated Rails' expanding reach beyond its Ruby origins, appealing to enterprise shops
The conference reflected a strong shared aesthetic and values despite diverse viewpoints within the community
DHH positions Rails' growth as astonishing — filling two multi-day, multi-track conferences within months of each other
Kathy Sierra, _why, and Dave Thomas represented the community's blend of user empathy, creative programming, and inspirational vision
money_quotes: In the overall scheme of things, my presentation was rather inconsequential. It was far more interesting to see all the other fantastic stuff being demonstrated from people in the community at large.
I can't help but have respect for a Canadian who manages to swear more than I did during my vendoritis rant and drink beer at the same time.
It's one of those things were I count my lucky stars that I won't ever need, but it's incredibly cool for those who happen to be saddled with a significant Java investment.
If you manage more than one server, you should run, not walk, to get hold of Capistrano 1.2.
Despite lots of minor disagreements and different view points, we all seemed to share a great many underlying values and appreciation for the same aesthetics.
And it's still hard to believe that we've come so far, so fast. That we have so many extremely talented developers working together.
posts.key_insight: The strength of Rails lies not in its creator's contributions but in the passionate, diverse community building impressive tools and pushing the framework in unexpected directions.

posts.summary: DHH argues that while criticism drives progress, it can also destroy motivation and creative output. He uses the metaphor of a pearl versus a diamond to illustrate how creators can protect themselves by narrowly scoping their work and rejecting criticism that falls outside that scope. He observes that many developers have withdrawn from public development because they couldn't handle criticism. He concludes that shielding against criticism is preferable to abandoning creation entirely.
key_points: Criticism is powerful for progress but can be destructive when it overwhelms a creator's ability to cope
Creators can protect motivation by narrowly defining what their work is — a pearl, not a diamond — and rejecting out-of-scope criticism
Not everyone has the emotional resilience to absorb criticism productively, and that's okay
The world benefits more from continued creation than from creators being fully open to all criticism
Shielding against criticism is a valid short-term survival strategy for maintaining creative output
money_quotes: Criticism is one of the most powerful creators of progress in every facet of life. People change, laws change, governments change in the face of sufficient criticism. But that doesn't mean it's always a net benefit.
If this is a pearl, I can reject criticism arising from the desire to get a diamond.
I've seen more than a fair share of developers withdraw from public development because of criticism they either didn't shield against or weren't strong enough to absorb.
It's not a skill worth pursuing at the expense of creation.
If you have to choose, at least in the short term, I hope you pick to shield rather than to run.
Mark your work a pearl, reject requests for diamonds. I desire your creation more than I care for the privilege to criticize it.
posts.key_insight: When criticism threatens to destroy a creator's motivation entirely, it's better to shield against it and keep creating than to absorb it all and stop making things.

posts.summary: DHH introduces the concept of 'syntactic vinegar' as the counterpart to syntactic sugar in framework design. Using a Rails form_tag refactoring as his example, he shows how making the preferred approach beautiful (block syntax) while making the discouraged approach ugly (asymmetric non-block syntax) guides developers without forbidding anything. The core philosophy is that framework designers should encourage and discourage rather than allow and forbid, since application developers will always know their specific needs better than framework authors can anticipate.
key_points: Good design requires both promoting proper patterns (syntactic sugar) and discouraging improper ones (syntactic vinegar)
Framework designers should guide through aesthetics rather than enforce through prohibition, since they cannot foresee all valid use cases
The Rails philosophy favors 'encourage and discourage' over 'allow and forbid'
Making edge-case code paths ugly but functional respects developer autonomy while signaling trade-offs
Appealing to a programmer's sense of aesthetics is the most effective way to promote good patterns
money_quotes: Good design lies not only in emphasizing the proper, but de-emphasizing the improper too.
Just as we can sprinkle syntactic sugar across a certain style or approach to promote its use, we can add syntactic vinegar to discourage it as well.
The perfect tarmac of preferred approach is traded in for the rocky gravel of the possible approach.
Never assume that you as a language or framework designer is an eternal oracle of insight who can foresee all possible use cases.
The programmer working on a real application using your work for their assistance will almost always know more about her application-specific issues than you possibly can.
It's rarely your role to outright deny certain behaviors. Your role is to guide, to inform, and to highlight the trade-offs.

Make that which is common, that which is preferred, into a beautiful structure. Sprinkle syntactic sugar to encourage. Then, make that which is uncommon, that which is suspicious, into an ugly structure.
If the programmer truly needs it, the bitter taste of the vinegar will not be a deterrent.
posts.key_insight: Framework designers should use aesthetic appeal as a steering mechanism — making good patterns beautiful and edge cases ugly — rather than imposing hard restrictions that assume they know better than application developers.

posts.summary: DHH declares that the WS-* (Web Services) stack of enterprise standards is headed for inevitable defeat, drawing a Star Wars analogy to the Death Star's destruction. He argues that REST, championed by a 'small band of renegade hackers,' will triumph over the bloated complexity of SOAP and WS-* standards. He points to the pattern of previous enterprise technologies like EJB and CORBA that seemed invincible before collapsing. Rails 1.2's built-in REST support, he argues, will make REST so natural that developers will find SOAP requests absurd.
key_points: The WS-* enterprise web services stack is doomed despite its institutional momentum and committee-driven standardization
Enterprise IT follows a recurring pattern: complex standards feel invincible until simpler alternatives destroy them, as happened with EJB and CORBA
The IT industry perpetuates a cycle of bottomless complexity that serves consulting houses and tooling vendors rather than developers
Rails 1.2's baked-in REST conventions will make simplicity the default, winning over the vast middle of developers who don't care about the standards debate
money_quotes: We have committees to oversee the committees.
I'm sure the pushers of EJB and Corba felt equally invincible long after their rebels had captured the blueprints for their destruction.
There must be a new frontier of bottom-less complexity available to get lost in. Something that needs tooling, big consulting houses, five-year mission statements, and barriers of entry and exit.
The response to a request for a SOAP interface will soon be a common 'you want me to do what?!?!'.
The standardization of the standards is progressing full-speed ahead.
REST is already easy. Throw a little help, guidance, and baked-in convention into the mix and the response to a request for a SOAP interface will soon be a common 'you want me to do what?!?!'.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise complexity stacks like WS-*/SOAP are doomed to fall to simpler alternatives like REST, following the same pattern that killed EJB and CORBA, because the IT industry's addiction to complexity always eventually loses to pragmatic simplicity.

posts.summary: DHH announces the 2nd edition of 'Agile Web Development with Rails' (AWDwR), timed to coincide with the Rails 1.2 release. He praises publisher Pragmatic Bookshelf and author Dave Thomas for the extraordinary effort of keeping the book synchronized with Rails' rapid evolution. The book covers new features like migrations, polymorphic associations, respond_to, RJS, and resources. DHH highlights the extensive beta testing process and thanks first-edition buyers for helping put Rails on the map.
key_points: The 2nd edition of AWDwR is coordinated to ship alongside Rails 1.2, covering all major new features from the past year
Coordinating a book with a fast-moving open source project like Rails is extraordinarily difficult, requiring constant rewrites as the framework evolves
The book underwent months of beta testing by hundreds of developers, resulting in exceptional polish
The first edition's commercial success helped build the Rails ecosystem and economic momentum
The Pragmatic Bookshelf's innovative publishing model (PDF now, paper later) suited Rails' rapid pace
money_quotes: Coordinating a book with any open source project is notoriously difficult, coordinating one with Rails almost inhumanly so.
Regular book cycles can easily spend a year from idea to print. Considering the pace of evolution in the Rails world, that obviously wouldn't work.
Every time we reverted our opinion, change defaults, or introduced new API elements that deprecated old ones, Dave has been there to rewrite and update sections of the book to match.
This is truly the one book that every Rails developer and every aspiring Rails developer should have in their possession.
I'm really proud to see this level of documentation available for this little collection of Ruby scripts I started just over three years ago.
Its phenomenal success has really helped put Rails on the map.
posts.key_insight: The rapid evolution of Rails demanded a radically different approach to technical publishing, and the Pragmatic Bookshelf's agile process proved that books could keep pace with fast-moving open source projects.

posts.summary: DHH highlights Nic Williams' Magic Multi-Connections plugin, which solved Rails' lack of multi-database support in under 75 lines of Ruby code. The plugin was released the same day the issue blew up around Twitter's scaling problems. DHH uses this as evidence that extending Rails is far simpler than critics assume, and that the high-level nature of Ruby makes such fixes trivial compared to patching lower-level systems. He celebrates the open source ecosystem while gently suggesting that complainers could try fixing things themselves.
key_points: Nic Williams released Magic Multi-Connections, enabling Rails apps to use multiple read/write databases for scaling
The entire solution was implemented in fewer than 75 lines of Ruby as a Rails plugin, turning around within a single day
DHH argues this demonstrates that extending Rails is far easier than critics assume, especially compared to patching lower-level tools like Apache or MySQL
He pointedly notes Twitter could have built this themselves rather than complaining about Rails' limitations
The episode illustrates both the power of open source community response and the low barrier to contribution in Ruby/Rails
money_quotes: Williams let code be his reply to the discussion of Twitter's woes on scaling the database.

How could Nic fix such an apparent "critical flaw", as others have billed the lack of this facility in Rails, in such a short time? Simple, he did it in less than 75 lines of Ruby as a plugin for Rails. Less than 75 lines.
Extending Rails to do what you want is often much simpler than you think.
You can't compare extending a high-level framework written in a language like Ruby to, say, patching Apache or MySQL. The barriers of entry are simply not in the same sport.
Some times you can just ask and you will receive.
Maybe next time, I could just have a look at how hard it would be to fix myself.
posts.key_insight: The perceived 'critical flaw' in Rails' database scaling was solved in 75 lines of Ruby, proving that extending a high-level framework is vastly easier than critics assume.

posts.summary: DHH recounts returning to fencing after more than a decade away from the sport. He reflects on how fencing foil prioritizes technique over raw physical attributes, a lesson reinforced when a 7-year-old girl outperformed him. He appreciates that fencing accommodates a wide range of ages and body types, making it uniquely inclusive. The post is a lighthearted personal vignette about rediscovering a former hobby and dealing with the physical aftermath of long-dormant muscles.
key_points: Fencing foil is a sport where technique dominates over strength, stamina, and agility
Returning after a decade revealed how much technique had been lost despite retained muscle memory for footwork
Fencing is uniquely inclusive, allowing participants from age 7 to 50+ to compete together
Physical soreness from unused muscles served as a humbling reminder of time away from the sport
money_quotes: Fencing foil is so much more about technique. A small rotation of the wrist is all it takes to parry an attack and you're all set for riposte.
The hard part was realizing just how much technique I had lost as I was getting schooled by a 7 year-old girl.
It allows for such a wide range of physical attributes to enjoy it together.
Nothing like exercising muscles that have laid dormant since Jurassic Park was a box office hit.
posts.key_insight: Technique and skill trump raw physical attributes in fencing — and returning to a long-abandoned discipline is both humbling and rewarding.

posts.summary: DHH's blog Loud Thinking is experiencing server failure after six years of uptime. He's working to recover archives, with content from June 2005 and earlier already restored. He frames the potential data loss philosophically as 'forced Spring cleaning' and notes that emails sent to his loudthinking address in recent days may have been lost.
key_points: His long-running blog server failed after six years of service
He's recovering archives but faces potential data loss for newer content
He reframes potential data loss positively as clearing out cruft
Email sent to his address during the outage may have been lost
money_quotes: Loud Thinking is down for the count while the server that held it up for the past six years is in ICU.
Nothing like a little forced Spring cleaning to get the cruft moved out.
posts.key_insight: DHH takes a characteristically unfazed approach to server failure and potential data loss, treating it as an opportunity to clean house rather than a crisis.
