If you have so little confidence in the locality of your changes, the tests are indeed telling you that the system has overly high coupling.
Don't pervert your architecture in order to prematurely optimize for the performance characteristics of the mid-nineties.
Embrace the awesome power of modern computers, and revel in the clarity of a code base unharmed by test-induced design damage.
posts.key_insight: The TDD orthodoxy of avoiding database access in tests is a fossilized heuristic from slow hardware eras, and following it today inflicts unnecessary architectural damage on your codebase.

posts.summary: DHH explains why he quit Facebook in 2011, arguing that its mission to connect everyone is fundamentally toxic. He rejects Zuckerberg's idea that people should have one consistent identity, arguing that personal growth requires the freedom to grow apart from past social circles. Facebook disrupted the natural process of losing touch by making permanent connection the default, requiring active effort to sever ties. He found that the platform's persistent audience from every life stage inhibited authentic self-expression and experimentation, so he deleted it entirely and found it liberating.
key_points: Zuckerberg's philosophy that people should have one authentic self across all contexts is toxic and stifles personal evolution
Growing apart and losing touch with people is a natural, healthy process that enables personal growth and reinvention
Facebook reversed the default: staying connected became automatic while disconnecting required deliberate, socially awkward action
The persistent audience of past acquaintances moderates what people share and inhibits experimentation with new identities
Real-life transitions like leaving university naturally sever connections, and this is accepted as normal — Facebook broke that
money_quotes: I quit Facebook back in 2011 for a lot of reasons, but perhaps the most crucial was to rebel against its core mission: Connecting the world.
Zuckerberg has repeatedly doubled down on the toxic idea that we should only have one self, one persona. That we should be the same person in all social circles, lest we be "frauds".
I'm happy to be a fraud under that definition.
What allowed me to change and prosper was the freedom to grow apart and lose touch with people.
There's a gravitational force that pulls you into repeating the same circular pattern over and over again. Breaking out of that takes tremendous force.
Facebook changed that. It stunted this natural, gentle process of growing apart and losing touch.

Becoming someone else entails experimenting and failing with new styles and ideas. Not a lot of people are so keen to premiere such vulnerable stages of their evolution in front of an audience that expects them to be that same person they always were forever.
Connected in the sense that an anchor is to a boat. A literal drag.
posts.key_insight: Personal growth depends on the freedom to shed old identities and social connections, a natural process that Facebook's permanent connectivity fundamentally disrupts.

posts.summary: DHH describes Basecamp's decision to temporarily remove the 'claps' (likes/applause) feature from their own internal Basecamp account to better understand its impact. He noticed personal anxiety around the feature — worrying about whether selectively applauding some updates but not others sent unintended signals about favoritism or dissatisfaction. Rather than endlessly debating improvements, they simply killed the feature for a six-week cycle. The experiment immediately surfaced strong opinions from the team and revealed that people started writing more substantive comments without the easy out of a quick clap.
key_points: Likes and claps in collaboration tools create subtle anxiety about perceived favoritism and implicit judgment when not applied equally
Rather than brainstorming the perfect solution to a fuzzy problem, sometimes the best approach is to simply remove the feature and observe what happens
Removing claps led to more substantive commenting — the easy-out reaction had been suppressing deeper engagement
Dogfooding product changes on your own team before deciding for customers provides real insight that abstract discussion cannot
money_quotes: If I applauded an update from Sam yesterday, but don't today, does that mean I'm expressing discontent with the most recent work?
Frequently my answer to this question was: Applaud equally for everyone or no one. An easy out for anxiety, but not actually very helpful.
So instead of trying ever-harder to think up the perfect solution, we grabbed the hammer instead and decided to whack the feature.
No big discussion, no brainstorming, no endless contemplation. Just a big whack, and let's see where this goes.
Hey, isn't it interesting that we're all commenting with a lot more depth now that the easy out of a clap isn't there any more?
When in doubt, give it a whack.
posts.key_insight: When a feature creates more anxiety than value, the fastest path to clarity is removing it entirely and observing what changes, rather than endlessly debating improvements.

posts.summary: DHH announces Stimulus 1.0, a lightweight JavaScript framework designed to enhance server-rendered HTML rather than replace it with client-side rendering. He argues that the mainstream approach of building single-page applications with frameworks like React is unnecessarily complex for most use cases. Basecamp's architecture combines Turbolinks (for fast page transitions) with Stimulus (for fine-grained page interactions), achieving modern responsiveness while keeping server-side rendering at the core. The framework revolves around three concepts—controllers, actions, and targets—and stores state in HTML rather than JavaScript objects. DHH positions this as a productivity-multiplying approach that lets small teams compete with much larger ones using heavyweight frameworks.
key_points: Stimulus enhances existing server-rendered HTML rather than generating DOM from JSON, storing state in HTML instead of JavaScript objects
The Turbolinks + Stimulus combination delivers single-page-app responsiveness (~3x faster page transitions) while maintaining the simplicity of server-side rendering
Most applications don't warrant the complexity of client-side MVC frameworks, and adopting them represents a regression in simplicity and productivity
The approach is explicitly designed for small teams to compete on fidelity and reach against larger teams using more laborious mainstream frameworks
Stimulus doesn't require an all-or-nothing commitment—it works alongside heavier approaches for the portions of an app that truly need them
money_quotes: All our applications have server-side rendered HTML at their core, then add sprinkles of JavaScript to make them sparkle.
It allows us to party with productivity like days of yore. A throwback to when a single programmer could make rapacious progress without getting stuck in layers of indirection or distributed systems.
If you're happy with the complexity and effort it takes to maintain an application within the maelstrom of, say, React + Redux, then Turbolinks + Stimulus will not appeal to you.

In fact, it feels like the same kind of secret sauce we had at Basecamp when we developed Ruby on Rails. The sense that contemporary mainstream approaches are needlessly convoluted, and that we can do more, faster, with far less.
Above all, it's a toolkit for small teams who want to compete on fidelity and reach with much larger teams using more laborious, mainstream approaches.
A time before everyone thought the holy grail was to confine their server-side application to producing JSON for a JavaScript-based client application.
This set of alternative frameworks is about avoiding the heavy lifting as much as possible.
posts.key_insight: Most web applications don't need the complexity of single-page JavaScript frameworks—server-rendered HTML with modest JavaScript enhancements delivers modern responsiveness while preserving the productivity advantages of small teams and simple architectures.

posts.summary: DHH announces that despite Basecamp having its best revenue year ever, the company is implementing a hiring freeze — not out of financial distress, but as a deliberate choice to resist growth-driven momentum. He argues that the entrepreneurial culture's obsession with 'what's next' and constant expansion inevitably pulls founders away from the work they love. By capping headcount at around 50 people, Basecamp embraces constraints that force the team to do different, more effective work rather than simply more work. The post frames this counterintuitive move as a way to preserve the satisfaction of doing meaningful work over owning an ever-expanding empire.
key_points: Basecamp's best financial year ever is being met with a hiring freeze, inverting the typical growth playbook
Growth momentum silently takes control if you don't actively resist it, filling your time with obligations rather than craft
The entrepreneurial culture's expectation of constant conquest is a trap that pulls founders away from designing, programming, and writing
Constraints on headcount force creative prioritization — doing different work rather than more work
The company's independence from investors enables choices that optimize for satisfaction over scale
money_quotes: Sure, it's nice to see numbers tick ever higher, but we passed enough many years ago.
It's so ingrained in our entrepreneurial culture that you must always be on a conquest. Once a set of territories have been subdued, you're honor-bound to push further north.
The momentum of growth assumes control of the ship quickly, if you don't dare wrestle back the wheel.
Before you know it there's no longer time to do your favorite things. Now all the things that simply have to be done fill first your weeks, then your months, and then finally the whole year.
Hiring freezes are usually for companies that are struggling. Trying desperately to cut costs to stay afloat. And here we are, doing better than ever, pulling that same move? Yes.
The constraint of having the same team means that you also only get to do the same amount of work. But you don't have to do the same actual work, you can do different work.
Basecamp has never sought to conquer the world or the markets. We do not have to win a total victory from a total assault to be fulfilled.
posts.key_insight: The greatest act of business discipline isn't scaling up when things are going well — it's choosing not to, so you can keep doing the work that made things good in the first place.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the entrepreneurial world is too obsessed with exponential, hockey-stick growth and needs more companies that embrace modest, linear growth instead. He uses Basecamp's 14 years of steady growth as proof that sustainable businesses can thrive without venture capital or network effects. He contends that the obsession with exponential growth feeds monopolistic concentration by Facebook, Google, and others, which poisons markets and society. He calls for entrepreneurs to consciously choose linear growth as a sign of strength and independence, not weakness, and to make that choice early before their company's trajectory is locked in.
key_points: Linear growth is the natural result of good products sold at reasonable prices spreading through word of mouth, without artificial growth hormones from investors
The obsession with exponential growth creates monopolistic concentration that corrupts both business and society, as seen with Facebook and Google capturing all advertising value
The business press restricts entrepreneurial imagination by constantly asking who will be the next Google or Facebook instead of celebrating smaller sustainable businesses
The choice between exponential and linear growth is made early and locks a company's course — cap tables and investor demands are nearly impossible to undo later
Basecamp's 14 years of linear growth has served millions of users, employed fifty-odd people, and provided deep meaning to its founders
money_quotes: Exponential growth gets all the glory. Every startup story that lands on the cover of a magazine has a hockey-stick chart to flaunt.
If everyone wants to be the foundation, then there's nobody left to serve as the beams or cladding or tiles. That's a recipe for a concrete and corporate wasteland.
It's creative destruction without creative regeneration. More black hole, less forest-fire cleanse.
Capitalism as a system is prone to all manners of dysfunction, but few are as fatal as that of monopolies backed by exponential growth.
To eschew the exponential demands of investors is a sign of strength, not a mark of weakness. To be content with linear growth is a streak of independence, not absence of vision.
You can't move a tree by blowing at it softly once the roots are down. But you can radically change where a seed will land by doing the same.
Choosing to chase the exponential is just that, a choice. Which also means that choosing the linear is a choice too.
We are in dire need of such reprogramming of the entrepreneurial boot loader.
posts.key_insight: Choosing modest, linear growth over exponential expansion is not a concession but a deliberate act of independence that produces more sustainable businesses and a healthier economy.

posts.summary: DHH argues that most disagreements are driven by hidden context rather than the surface-level issues being debated. The emotional temperature of a conflict reveals how much unspoken history and compounding frustrations are at play. He explains that people often fail to recognize when they're fighting a proxy war over deeper dynamics. This is why online arguments between strangers rarely go anywhere productive — without knowing someone's context, you can't address the real sources of friction. The solution lies in investing time to understand the other person's circumstances and experiences, which naturally cools the conflict enough for resolution.
key_points: Most conflicts are shaped more by accumulated history and interpersonal dynamics than by the specific issue at hand
Disproportionately heated tone is a reliable signal that the real conflict lies beneath the surface
Online arguments between strangers fail because participants lack access to each other's context and experiences
Investing in understanding someone's circumstances is both the path to resolution and a natural cooling mechanism
money_quotes: Most disagreements aren't just about the cards on the table. They're just as much about who's at the table.
It's no wonder the game ends up making little sense to folks who think everything they need to know to make the winning argument is reading the cards facing up.
If it's unreasonably, disproportionately hot, then it's probably not just about the current specifics.
People get worked up by compounding circumstances and repeating dynamics.
It's not at all uncommon for the context to dwarf the particulars. But it's exceedingly common for people to be oblivious to the fact.
This is one of the reasons why it's so rare for online arguments between strangers to go anywhere productive.
By the time you've bothered to dive that deep, the whole thing has probably cooled off enough for gentle hands to touch.
posts.key_insight: Conflicts escalate not because of what's being argued, but because of the accumulated context and unspoken dynamics that neither side is addressing.

posts.summary: DHH announces a new YouTube series called 'On Writing Software Well' where he explores the real Basecamp production codebase to discuss programming decisions and trade-offs. He argues that most software debates happen in the abstract without looking at real code, leading to pointless disagreements rooted in different contexts. By showing production code used by millions, he aims to ground programming discussions in reality rather than idealized examples. He frames the series as a battle against complexity and a search for beautiful code.
key_points: Software debates without real code devolve into illusions of disagreement because people argue from different contexts
Production codebases contain the real wisdom because they force actual trade-offs between competing patterns
Example code in programming arguments is idealized and Platonic — it doesn't account for real-world constraints
Few people are willing to show their production code publicly, which DHH sees as a missed opportunity for the industry
money_quotes: When you debate without code, you're likely to get sucked into illusions of disagreement.
Yet somehow arguments grounded in production code are rare. Few people seem willing to lift the curtain on such codebases, which is a damn shame.
Because that's where the real wisdom is buried. That's where people have been forced to make actual trade-offs between competing patterns and practices.
Programming arguments based in example code is most often stylized and idealized. It's Platonic shadows on the cave wall.
So easy to dig in and defend a technique when you don't actually have to worry about guarding the flanks, setting up camp, and getting your supply lines in order.
So that's my mission: To take you into the trenches and have you fight by my side in the battle against complexity and in the search for beautiful code.
posts.key_insight: Software debates are meaningless without production code because real wisdom lives in the actual trade-offs developers are forced to make, not in idealized examples.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Silicon Valley's obsession with 'changing the world' is a delusional coping mechanism that most companies use to justify grueling work cultures. He points out that companies which actually changed the world (Twitter, Facebook) started with mundane goals, not grand missions. Using HealthIQ's career page as a case study, he mocks a life insurance company claiming to 'heal the world' and argues this cognitive dissonance is an existential cry for help. He concludes that embracing the mundane nature of most work is healthier than carrying the impossible burden of world-changing ambitions.
key_points: Companies that actually changed the world (Twitter, Facebook) started with modest, unglamorous goals — not missions to reshape civilization
The 'changing the world' rhetoric is a coping mechanism to justify brutal work conditions that wouldn't be warranted by the company's actual product
Steve Jobs's 'sugar water' pitch to Scully has been distorted into a toxic belief that all work must have cosmic significance to have meaning
Most people's legacy will be the impressions they leave on friends, family, and colleagues — and that's cause for celebration, not despair
The cognitive dissonance between mundane products and grandiose missions eventually cracks, and the harder you invest in the delusion, the harder you shatter
money_quotes: If everyone in Silicon Valley who proclaimed to be on a mission to change the world actually did, our collective regret would be far deeper.
What on earth would prompt someone hawking life insurance — LIFE INSURANCE! — to posture, and quite possibly believe, that they're Changing The World™?

If you're going to toil your life away on a literal treadmill with meetings at 9pm every night, you're going to need to tell yourself something different than 'I do this such that I can save privileged joggers and kale eaters 10% on life insurance'.
The idea that work isn't worth living unless it entails the prospect of upsetting the world order.

Carrying the weight of Changing The World on your shoulders is a tremendous burden. Look at the characters who actually managed it. Most of them weren't exactly living envious lives outside the scope of their work.
You can't outrun existential angst. Even if you put actual fucking treadmills in the middle of your office.
Set out to do good work. Set out to be fair in your dealings with customers, employees, and reality.
Chances are you won't, and if you do, it's not going to be because you said you would.
posts.key_insight: The grandiose 'changing the world' rhetoric in Silicon Valley is a coping mechanism that masks mundane reality, and embracing the ordinary nature of most work leads to a healthier, more honest existence.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Highrise, a CRM product spun off from Basecamp as a separate company in 2014, is being reabsorbed back into Basecamp. The spin-off experiment under CEO Nathan Kontny and a team of seven is ending, though the technical operations were always handled by Basecamp's team. DHH emphasizes continuity for customers, noting the original builders are still at Basecamp and the product will be supported indefinitely. The post frames the move as a natural homecoming rather than a failure, and hints at new future directions for the product.
key_points: Highrise operated as a separate company for about four years but its infrastructure was always run by Basecamp's team
The spin-off is characterized as an experiment that had highlights but ultimately led to a different strategic decision
Basecamp commits to supporting Highrise 'until the end of the internet' for its 10,000+ customers
The transition is framed as nearly invisible to customers since the original team never really left
money_quotes: Spinning Highrise off into a separate company with a separate team and CEO was always a bit of an experiment.
For customers of Highrise, we at Basecamp are committed to supporting the product until the end of the internet!
Highrise is home to over 10,000 customers who've trusted their data and their business to us.
In short, this transition back to the Basecamp team is going to be nearly invisible to Highrise customers.
Highrise is returning to the team that originally built the software, and it's being operated on the technical side by the same team that always did so.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best outcome of a business experiment is recognizing when to bring things back home, prioritizing customer continuity over organizational novelty.

posts.summary: DHH argues that ORMs like Active Record have compressed the conceptual overhead of SQL to the point where beginners no longer need to learn it before building database-backed applications. He draws a parallel to memory management, which also shifted from essential knowledge to background abstraction. Basecamp 3's 42,000 lines of code contain zero raw SQL statements while serving millions of users. He contends that experienced programmers who insist beginners must learn SQL first are falling into the trap of romanticizing their own suffering. The post frames this as natural progress: as abstractions improve, formerly fundamental concepts become appliances, lowering barriers to entry and empowering generalists.
key_points: ORMs have matured enough that production applications serving millions can operate without a single raw SQL statement
Conceptual compression follows the same pattern as memory management — once essential, now safely abstracted away for most developers
Experienced programmers who demand beginners learn historical fundamentals are romanticizing their own trials rather than celebrating progress
Databases becoming 'appliances' is an honor, not a dig — it means the technology and abstractions have become that good
Conceptual compression is essential to fight specialization and preserve the power of individual generalists to build complete applications
money_quotes: Basecamp 3 has about 42,000 lines of code, and not a single fully formed SQL statement as part of application logic!

This is one of the key pitfalls of experience. Falling in love with the trials and tribulations you had to suffer, rather than rejoicing in the fact that the next generation doesn't have to endure them.

The relegation to 'appliance status' isn't a dig, but an honor. It means we've finally made the technology and the abstraction so good that many, if not most, programmers don't even have to think about it on a daily basis.
The more effectively we compress the concepts of yesterday, the lower the barriers to entry become.
We need such conceptual compression more than ever to starve off specialization, and to preserve the power of individual generalists to make complete applications.
You can't learn everything. You can't hold every concept fully expanded in your head. And moreover, you shouldn't.
Don't be a fogey. Don't fight the conceptual compression. Help make it happen.
posts.key_insight: As abstractions mature, formerly essential low-level knowledge like SQL becomes an appliance you can learn later, and insisting beginners master it first mistakes nostalgia for wisdom.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Basecamp is ending its homegrown SMS-based two-factor authentication and switching to Google's 2FA system, effective July 2nd, 2018. The change is motivated by security experts no longer recommending SMS as a second factor. Users who want 2FA will need to use Google Sign-In, with options including Google's authenticator app, 1Password, or a physical Yubikey. The post urges users to adopt 2FA across all their online accounts.
key_points: SMS-based two-factor authentication is no longer recommended by security experts
Basecamp is replacing its homegrown SMS verification with Google Sign-In paired with Google's 2FA system
Users can authenticate via Google's app, 1Password, or a physical Yubikey
The post is a practical security announcement rather than an opinion piece, urging users to adopt 2FA broadly
money_quotes: Using SMS as a second security factor for signing into web applications is no longer recommended by security experts.
It's never been more important to take serious precautions to guard your security online.
Hacks are common, and failing to protect your online accounts with a second factor makes it so much easier to become a victim.
Good security is like good backups. It's a bit of a hassle to setup, but the regret you'll feel if you don't have it when you need it dwarfs that inconvenience.
Don't procrastinate.
posts.key_insight: Basecamp is migrating from homegrown SMS verification to Google's 2FA system because SMS is no longer considered a secure second factor, and users should adopt 2FA everywhere.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Silicon Valley's toxic startup culture stems from founders and employees who plan short tenures followed by big exits. When you don't plan to stick around, it's easy to mistreat employees, customers, and society without regret. He contrasts this with Basecamp's long-term mindset, where decisions are made with decades of consequences in mind. The post frames permanence as a moral incentive that naturally produces better behavior.
key_points: The 'loot and leave' mentality explains why startup culture tolerates harmful behavior — short tenures make villainy bearable
Planning to stay for decades fundamentally changes how you make decisions about employees, customers, and technology
Permanence functions as an intrinsic moral incentive, similar to how people treat their own neighborhood better than one they're visiting
The worst startup behaviors are concentrated below the titan level, where there's no empire to justify the means
money_quotes: If you don't plan to stick around, who cares how you leave the things behind?
Employees, customers, regulations, and, hell, even society at large, is much easier to screw over without regret if you don't have to stick around for all that long.
A few years of being the villain or the asshole is probably something a lot more people can imagine tolerating than if it was the condemnation of a whole career.
If I'm going to be here for the next 10–20–30 years, what's the right move that I won't regret over the coming decades?
That perspective of permanence gives you a completely different outlook on your actions and your overall strategy.
It's like how most people end up treating a neighborhood they live in with a different kind of respect than one they're just visiting.
How would things be different for you if you couldn't just loot'n'leave?
posts.key_insight: The short-term exit mentality of startup culture removes the natural moral incentive of permanence, making it easy to exploit people and cut corners when you won't be around to face the consequences.

posts.summary: DHH argues that open-plan offices are fundamentally hostile to productive work, benefiting only managers and photo ops while harming the vast majority of workers. He cites a Royal Society study showing that open offices actually decrease face-to-face collaboration — the opposite of their stated purpose — as people retreat into headphone cocoons and rely on messaging instead. Drawing from personal experience at early-2000s tech companies, he claims he never could have created Ruby on Rails in an open office environment. He presents a continuum from worst (mixed departments in one room) to least bad (small team rooms under six people), while arguing the entire concept remains an unquestioned default that squanders human health and potential.
key_points: The vast majority of workers dislike or hate open offices, yet they persist because managers — who don't need long uninterrupted focus time — are the ones designing office layouts
Research shows open offices actually decrease face-to-face collaboration, directly contradicting the primary justification for their existence
Open offices exist on a continuum of badness: mixed departments together is worst, same-function groups are less bad, and small teams under six people are least bad
The open office persists as an almost unquestioned default in tech despite an endless stream of studies showing it causes stress, conflict, and turnover
The real beneficiaries of open offices are investor tours, journalist interviews, and photo shoots — not the workers
money_quotes: Open offices are more appealing to people in management because they needn't protect their own time and attention as much.
Converting traditional offices with walls and doors and separation into open-plan offices causes face-to-face interaction to plummet, not rise.
People try to shield their attention (and sanity!) by retreating into headphone-clad cocoons, and instead rely on instant messaging or email to interact.
I feel quite comfortable stating that I would never have been able to create Ruby on Rails or any of my other software or creative achievements in such an environment.
It was a tyranny of interruption, distraction, and stress.
We're squandering human health and potential on an epic scale by forcing the vast majority of people who dislike or hate the open office into that configuration.

There's been an endless stream of studies showing that the open-plan office is a source of stress, conflict, and turnover. And yet it's still the default in tech. An almost unquestioned default. That's a fucking travesty.
posts.key_insight: Open-plan offices persist not because they work but because the people who choose them — managers — don't suffer the consequences, while the workers who do suffer lack the power to change them.

posts.summary: DHH argues that managers who brag about their extreme work hours and exhaustion are driven by insecurity, not genuine productivity. He traces this behavior to guilt over the historically leisurely managerial lifestyle, leading to performative overwork as compensation. The media amplifies this toxic pattern by lionizing workaholics as heroic figures. DHH calls for an end to celebrating exhaustion culture, arguing it trickles down and harms everyone. Instead of making everyone overworked, he advocates spreading the privilege of reasonable hours and flexibility to all workers.
key_points: Managerial overwork bragging stems from deep insecurity about whether their constant efforts are truly needed or desired
Media fawning over workaholic executives normalizes and amplifies toxic work culture that trickles down to employees
Rather than turning everyone into overworked peons, society should strive to give everyone the flexibility managers once enjoyed
Performative exhaustion deserves pity and sometimes ridicule, not admiration
The goal should be calm management in calm companies, not glorified martyrdom for The Mission
money_quotes: When you deep down know that your 24/7 efforts just aren't as important — or even desired! — as you'd like to believe, the quickest way to quell those nagging thoughts is to doubling down on the bravado.
Stop lionizing toxic work habits as inspiration for new entrepreneurs and managers.
Masochistic managers can continue their self-immolation for The Mission and The Company, but the rest of us and the media do not need to bang the tambourine while they do it.
Instead we should look with pity on such a narrow existence.
Instead of turning everyone into the overworked peon of the early 20th century, we should be striving for everyone to be able to take off early on Friday for golf.
It's hard work to dance with dissonance, cognitive or otherwise.
Let's return to a time where being a harried mess of a human wasn't the high managerial calling it is today.
posts.key_insight: Managers who perform exhaustion as a badge of honor are compensating for insecurity about their own relevance, and society should stop applauding this toxic pattern and instead work toward spreading reasonable work-life balance to everyone.

posts.summary: Basecamp is hiring a data analyst in a posting that doubles as a manifesto for the company's work philosophy. DHH emphasizes that while data informs decisions, Basecamp is not 'data-driven' — they drive with their heart. The role spans A/B testing, revenue forecasting, security forensics, and usage analysis, but the posting spends equal time selling Basecamp's culture: 40-hour weeks, remote work, generous benefits, and no office perks designed to keep people at work longer. Pay is benchmarked to San Francisco top-10% rates regardless of location, and the company explicitly prioritizes diversity and encourages 'imposters' to apply.
key_points: Basecamp uses data to inform decisions but rejects being 'data-driven' — data clears the head, but heart drives the company
The role is unusually broad: A/B testing, security forensics, sales tax analysis, cloud cost forecasting, and performance debugging
Remote-first with San Francisco-benchmarked pay regardless of location, rejecting the silicon valley office-perk model
The job posting is as much a cultural statement as a hiring ad, emphasizing calm work, 40-hour weeks, and sustainable pace
Basecamp explicitly seeks diversity and puts extra weight on candidates from underrepresented groups
money_quotes: Data is there to clear the head, but ultimately we drive the company with our heart.
You won't be looking for ways to squeeze the last sour drop out of the lemon at Basecamp.
This means the job isn't about maximizing revenue or minimizing costs. Yes, we want to make money and we don't want to be wasteful, but we also want to be kind, considerate, fair, flexible, and calm.

While we have a lovely office in Chicago, it's not where you'll find foosball tables constantly spinning, paid lunches, or any of the other trappings that companies use to lure employees into staying ever longer at work.
Work can wait.
Nobody hits the ground running.
posts.key_insight: A job posting can be a vehicle for company values — Basecamp uses hiring as an opportunity to articulate its rejection of data-obsession, overwork culture, and Silicon Valley office perks in favor of calm, heart-driven decision-making.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on WhatsApp co-founder Brian Acton's regret after selling to Facebook for $19 billion, arguing that the deal stripped Acton of purpose and power despite making him fabulously wealthy. He draws on a Forbes interview where Acton struggles to rationalize his decision as Facebook systematically dismantles WhatsApp's principles. DHH argues this should be treated as a cautionary tale rather than a success story, and calls for a cultural shift that celebrates turning down massive acquisition offers. The post frames selling out as a fundamentally human temptation that leads to predictable despair.
key_points: Acton traded all his power and purpose for wealth, becoming 'unfathomably small and impotent' under Zuckerberg
The 'offer too good to refuse' framing is a rationalization that absolves founders of agency and guilt
WhatsApp's anti-ads ethos will inevitably be destroyed because Facebook needs to fill a $20 billion balance sheet hole
The tech industry needs to rewrite cultural incentives so that selling out is seen as tragedy rather than victory
Billionaire regret is real and shouldn't be dismissed—purpose matters more than a 10-digit bank balance
money_quotes: Zuckerberg made Acton unfathomably rich. He also made him unfathomably small and impotent.
They say becoming a billionaire isn't about the money, it's about the power. If so, clearly this was a deal gone wrong.
Acton became a billionaire by giving up all his power and handing it to Zuckerberg.
The utterly human temptation towards unfathomable wealth with the equally human suspicion that purpose is so much more than 10-digit bank balance.
The 'no ads, no games, no gimmicks' ethos will complete its transition into 'all ads, all deceit, all extraction' soon enough.
We need to rewrite the cultural incentives around selling out. Acton predicament as the prototypical tragedy rather than victory. Replace envy with pity. Inspiration with rejection.
Let's make it cool to turn down twenty billion dollars.
posts.key_insight: Selling your company to Big Tech is a Faustian bargain—you gain billions but lose the power, purpose, and principles that made the work meaningful in the first place.

posts.summary: DHH recounts the disastrous launch of 'It Doesn't Have To Be Crazy At Work,' where publisher Harper Business botched nearly every aspect of the release. Despite paying mid six figures for the rights, Harper printed only 14,000 copies, Amazon sold out in five days, and it took a full month to restock. The cover design was poorly executed, and Harper offered a parade of excuses including tariffs and paper shortages. The irony of a chaotic launch for a book about calm workplaces is not lost on DHH. He reflects on the bad assumptions they made trusting a major publisher and the lessons learned about verifying every detail yourself.
key_points: Harper Business printed only 14,000 copies despite knowing Amazon accounts for 70-90% of sales, allocating just 3,300 to Amazon
The planning department's incentives were broken: they were judged on not printing too many books, not on having enough to meet demand
Amazon sold out in 5 days and was out of stock for a month, killing all sales momentum during peak publicity
DHH and Jason assumed a major publisher paying top dollar would handle logistics competently, revealing dangerous assumptions built on past good experiences with Crown
The book missed all major bestseller lists not because of lack of demand but because there were literally no books to sell
money_quotes: It shouldn't have to be this crazy at work, especially when work is publishing a book called It Doesn't Have To Be Crazy At Work.
You had one job, Harper. One job. Print the books, get them to book stores.
The planning department is judged not on having enough books, but only if they print too many, and end up with unsold inventory.
What good is having a wonderful launch campaign, if you have no books to sell?
Harper spent less than $30,000 to print books, because their planning department didn't want to risk sitting with $10,000 worth of unsold inventory if the book should bomb.

When you work with people who know what they're doing, it's so easy to assume that this is totally normal. That everyone is going to be at this level, because that's the baseline you see. But it's not normal.
We went for the publisher who bid the highest, and we assumed this meant they had real skin in the game.
If you're not going to make the lists, let it be for a good reason like, not enough people buy your book, not that you don't have books for all the people who want to buy.
posts.key_insight: A high bid from a major publisher doesn't guarantee competence — misaligned internal incentives and bureaucratic dysfunction can sabotage even the most promising launches, and trusting 'the professionals' without verification is a costly assumption.

posts.summary: DHH provides a real-time incident report on Basecamp 3's nearly five-hour outage caused by an integer overflow in a database tracking table. The column was configured as a regular integer (max ~2.1 billion) instead of a big integer, and when IDs were exhausted, the system was forced into read-only mode. The fix required a database migration across 8 databases in two datacenters, followed by careful verification. DHH candidly admits the root cause was a known issue that Rails itself had fixed two years prior by changing its default to big integer, making it an avoidable failure. He takes full personal responsibility and promises a detailed postmortem.
key_points: An integer column overflow at 2,147,483,647 forced Basecamp 3 into read-only mode for nearly five hours
The fix required migrating a very large production table from integer to big integer across 8 databases in two datacenters
Rails, which DHH's team created, had already changed its default from integer to big integer two years earlier
DHH takes full personal responsibility for not heeding a known issue in their own framework
The outage was their worst in roughly a decade, against a normal uptime of 99.998%
money_quotes: That's the most catastrophic failure we've had a Basecamp in maybe as much as decade, and we could not be more sorry.
We've let you down on an avoidable issue that we should have been on top of.
The integer runs out of numbers at 2147483647. The big integer can grow until 9223372036854775807.
Changing a production database is serious business, so we had to test this fix on a staging database to make sure it was safe.
We need to be absolutely certain that all the data is in proper sync before we can go back online. It's looking good, but 99% sure isn't good enough. Need 100%.
It's embarrassing to admit, but the root cause of this issue with running out of integers has been a known problem in our technical community.
We use the development framework Rails (which we created!), and the default setting for that framework move from integer to big integer two years ago.
I accept full responsibility for failing to heed that warning, and by extension for causing the multi-hour outage today.
posts.key_insight: Even the creators of a framework can be bitten by not adopting their own improvements, and the right response is radical transparency and personal accountability.

posts.summary: DHH writes a transparent postmortem about Basecamp 3's worst outage in a decade: nearly five hours of read-only mode caused by hitting the 32-bit integer ceiling (2,147,483,647) on their events table. The fix had already been implemented as a default in Rails 5.1 a year earlier, making it an avoidable failure. DHH accepts full personal responsibility as CTO and Rails creator, noting they were actively working on the fix but didn't complete it in time. The post emphasizes accountability over spin, refusing to downplay the outage as 'partial' despite read-only access remaining available.
key_points: Basecamp's events table hit the 32-bit integer max (2,147,483,647), freezing all write operations for nearly five hours
Rails 5.1 had already solved this exact problem with 64-bit integer defaults a year earlier, but Basecamp hadn't migrated its own schema
DHH refuses to minimize the incident as a 'partial outage' despite read-only access remaining available, taking the full hit to their uptime record
The outage dropped Basecamp 3's uptime from 99.998% to 99.978%, which DHH frames as a humbling reminder rather than still-impressive stat
money_quotes: The root cause was that our database hit the ceiling of 2,147,483,647 on our very busy events table.
It's bad enough that we had the worst outage at Basecamp in probably 10 years, but to know that it was avoidable is hard to swallow.
We pride ourselves at Basecamp on being "boring software" because it just works and it's always available.
Some companies might choose to weasel around an outage like ours by claiming that it was only a "partial outage". But that's not what we're going to do at Basecamp.
We're going to take the scar in our uptime record as a reminder to do better.
As the CTO of Basecamp and the creator of Ruby on Rails, I accept full responsibility for our failures.
We can't promise to be perfect, but we promise always to keep you informed in a timely and completely transparent manner.
posts.key_insight: True accountability means refusing to minimize avoidable failures and accepting the full weight of responsibility, especially when the fix was already known and within reach.

posts.summary: DHH writes an open letter to Jeff Bezos, criticizing Amazon's HQ2 selection process as demeaning to cities and arguing that Bezos, as the world's richest man, doesn't need to extract billions in subsidies. He urges Bezos to consider his legacy beyond conquest and extraction, warning that public sentiment will eventually turn against big tech monopolies. DHH discloses that Bezos is a minority owner in Basecamp and admits feeling a tinge of regret about that relationship. The letter frames magnanimity and responsibility as both morally right and strategically smart.
key_points: Amazon's HQ2 process forced cities to grovel for a company that didn't need the subsidies, demeaning local politicians and communities
Bezos personally owns Amazon's decisions — he can't hide behind committees or shareholders
Public trust in Amazon is a lagging indicator, and antitrust sentiment is building from the margins toward the mainstream
DHH uses Bezos's own regret minimization framework against him, arguing these extractive decisions will become regrets
DHH publicly distances himself and Basecamp from Bezos despite the investment relationship
money_quotes: You could blow up the extractive motive if you wanted to.
The losers ended up worse than where they started, and even the winners may well too.
I think you'll be able to catch the optics that the richest man in the world asking for tribute like this is an ugly one.
Amazon is Jeff Bezos. You can't cover decisions behind committees or other shareholders.
Rather than keep asking what cities and countries can do for Amazon, maybe start asking what Amazon can do for them.

At some point people are going to have had enough, and when they figure out a way to channel that discontent into political action, they're going to come looking for the heads of those that did them the most egregious wrongs.
It starts with a few pioneers calling for antitrust action, and then one day you wake up, and that's what the whole world wants.
It's hard to be proud of having you as a minority owner in Basecamp right now. Maybe there's even a tinge of regret.
posts.key_insight: The richest man in the world extracting subsidies from desperate cities is not just morally wrong but strategically foolish, as public backlash and antitrust action are inevitable.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his complicated relationship with Instagram, describing how he returned to the platform in 2015 and found it transformed into a curated showcase of perfect lives. Despite being self-aware about the highlight-reel nature of the platform, he found himself trapped in addictive patterns of posting and checking for validation. He acknowledges his own complicity in projecting an unrealistic lifestyle of race cars, travel, and glamour. Prompted by ongoing Facebook controversies, he decided to leave again, recognizing that what you consume visually colors your thoughts and ultimately your soul.
key_points: Instagram evolved from casual phone photos into an intoxicating stream of curated perfection that leaves users perpetually hungry for more
Self-awareness about Instagram's unreality provides no real defense against its addictive feedback loops of posting and seeking validation
DHH admits complicity in feeding an aesthetic that devours unfulfilled aspirations and produces alienation
The messenger's 'last active' timestamps revealed that most people on his list were similarly hooked, shattering the illusion of personal weakness
Paraphrasing Marcus Aurelius: what you watch colors your soul, making visual consumption a matter of spiritual hygiene
money_quotes: You don't really realize how cooked you're getting while sitting in the stew. But something important is dissolving.
Chasing affection from people I mostly didn't know, so I could please an ego that vowed not to care.

I've contributed to an utterly unrealistic and unhealthy depiction of success or even just The Good Life. I've feed an aesthetic that devours unfulfilled aspirations and spits out alienation and resignation.
The aesthetic becomes the essence. And the feedback loop sends you in circles. It's addictive. Even when you're aware of it, even when you have your guard up.
You'd think there'd only be so many cars or watches or beaches or sunsets or whatever you could take in before you're full. But somehow Instagram always just left me hungry for more.
The main ingredients in credibility is time and commitment. Otherwise it easily just comes off as obnoxious.
You probably have a completely healthy relationship with the lifestyle projections and Good Life aesthetics you're consuming on Instagram. Right? Of course you do.
posts.key_insight: Self-awareness about social media's curated unreality offers no real protection against its addictive pull, because the aesthetic seeps in and colors your soul regardless of your intellectual defenses.

posts.summary: DHH pushes back against the pervasive entrepreneurial narrative that anyone can make it to the top if they just want it enough. He argues this message is mathematically cruel, since there isn't room at the top for everyone, leaving masses of people disillusioned and self-blaming. He proposes an alternative: focus on personal mastery and the satisfaction of doing good work rather than competing against others for status. Even those who reach the top often find the satisfaction shallow or the cost too high.
key_points: The 'anyone can make it' success narrative is statistically false and emotionally cruel to the vast majority who won't
The top is filled with people who hate what they became to get there or find the achievement hollow
You have limited control over external success but full control over personal growth and craft satisfaction
The most fulfilling part of success is the 'becoming good' journey, not the status it confers
Stop playing games where you can't set the rules — focus on the ones where you can
money_quotes: Odds are overwhelmingly that you will not make it to the top.
A shit track indeed. To give simultaneous false hope and a false idol to so many when you know, PER MATHS, that it just isn't going to happen for the vast, vast majority.
The top is full of people who hate what they had to do or who they had to become to get there.
Even for the people who get there with a clean conscience often end up disappointed by how shallow the satisfaction really is.
You only have limited influence on whether you're going to succeed at whatever you put your mind to. It's by no means within your exclusive sphere of control.
That's a game worth winning: The one played with yourself for your own betterment. Not the one played against others, measured against them. Screw that game.
Stop playing games where you can't set the rules. Start winning the ones where you can.
posts.key_insight: The pursuit of 'making it to the top' is a rigged game — true fulfillment comes from the craft of becoming good, not from winning a status competition you can't control.

posts.summary: DHH pushes back against the entrepreneurial success narrative that tells people they can reach the top if they just want it enough. He argues this message is statistically cruel, creating masses of disillusioned people who blame themselves for inevitable failure. Even those who reach the top often hate what they became or find the satisfaction shallow. Instead, he advocates focusing on personal mastery and the intrinsic satisfaction of getting better at your craft, a game where you set the rules.
key_points: The 'anyone can make it to the top' narrative is mathematically false and creates far more disillusioned people than winners
Success at the top is largely outside your control — too many factors must align, and you only own a couple of them
Many who reach the top hate what they had to do to get there or find the rewards disappointingly shallow
The real game worth playing is personal mastery and self-improvement, measured by your own satisfaction in the work
Focusing on 'becoming good' rather than 'becoming successful' is where the actual fulfillment lives
money_quotes: Odds are overwhelmingly that you will not make it to the top.
To give simultaneous false hope and a false idol to so many when you know, PER MATHS, that it just isn't going to happen for the vast, vast majority. That's not just a shit track, it's cruel.
The top is full of people who hate what they had to do or who they had to become to get there.
You only have limited influence on whether you're going to succeed at whatever you put your mind to. It's by no means within your exclusive sphere of control.
You do have control over whether you're doing a good job, as measured by your personal sense of satisfaction in the work.
That's a game worth winning: The one played with yourself for your own betterment. Not the one played against others, measured against them. Screw that game.
Stop playing games where you can't set the rules. Start winning the ones where you can.
posts.key_insight: Instead of chasing the statistically improbable goal of reaching the top, focus on the game of personal mastery — the only game where you control the rules and the rewards.

posts.summary: DHH argues that while tech luminaries like Musk and Hawking worry about future AI threats like Skynet, AI-powered algorithms are already causing real harm today. Social media platforms optimize for addiction and anxiety, airlines use algorithms to split families for profit, and ride-sharing companies exploit cognitive biases. He focuses his sharpest criticism on Predictim, a startup that claims to use AI to vet babysitters by analyzing their social media accounts, calling it dangerous snake oil that doesn't work but still invades privacy. He concludes that we should focus on fighting today's algorithmic abuses rather than hypothetical future ones.
key_points: Worrying about hypothetical future AI threats distracts from the real algorithmic harms happening right now
Social media algorithms are optimized to exploit psychological vulnerabilities for engagement and ad revenue
Predictim exemplifies the worst of AI snake oil: promising safety through social media surveillance while disclaiming all responsibility
Consumers are disturbingly eager to accept invasive AI-branded tools, partly because hype about superintelligent AI makes them overestimate current capabilities
We have the power to reject, regulate, and ridicule harmful AI applications today, unlike a hypothetical future Skynet scenario
money_quotes: We don't need to wait for the singularity before artificial intelligence becomes capable of turning the world into a dystopian nightmare.
We're made increasingly miserable because connect-the-world imperialists are unleashing machine learning on our most vulnerable and base impulses.
There's no way this even fucking works! Simply no way.
This is that infamous Silicon Valley style of promising the world yet accepting zero responsibility for the consequences of its actions.

When you have Musk and Hawking waving their hands wildly about how The Great AI is going to render us all slaves so soon that we need emergency legislation about it, I can't fault people for thinking that it's already good enough to assess the personality of a babysitter.
Chances are good that when we do, we see it's just two rubber bands of questionable ethics holding together four cans of bullshit.
We can chose to reject, regulate, and ridicule when AI-branded algorithms turn against our better nature.
posts.key_insight: The real AI threat isn't a future superintelligence but today's algorithms that exploit human psychology for profit while hiding behind disclaimers and hype about capabilities they don't have.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Basecamp has published all its company policies on GitHub under a Creative Commons license, inviting public collaboration. The policies are written in plain language without legalese, and every revision is tracked and date-stamped. The move was inspired by the positive reception to open-sourcing their employee handbook, which helped many businesses create their own. DHH argues that company policies are organizational code that should be open source, forkable, and improvable by anyone.
key_points: Basecamp published all company policies on GitHub with full version history and Creative Commons licensing for reuse
The initiative was inspired by the success of open-sourcing their employee handbook, which many businesses adopted
DHH calls out Google specifically for failing to honor legacy systems and abandoning users
Company policies should be treated like code: open source, version-controlled, and collaboratively improved
Plain-language policies written 'by humans, for humans' are a competitive advantage and trust builder
money_quotes: We try hard to write good policies at Basecamp. Make them plain and easy to understand. Without out all the dreaded legalese. By humans, for humans.
Sometimes our policies may decay over time. Terms that are or become unreasonable linger on.
If more companies — AND YES I'M LOOKING AT YOU GOOGLE — would honor their legacy systems, and not willy-nilly kick users to the curb, we'd all gain from the level-up in trust.
Policies are part of the organizational code of a company. Not only should that code be open source, it should be tinkered with, improved, critiqued, forked, and refactored.
If more companies would adopt a no-nonsense refund policy, we'd all be better off.
posts.key_insight: Company policies are organizational code that deserves the same open-source treatment as software — version-controlled, publicly reviewable, and freely forkable to raise the bar for all businesses.

posts.summary: DHH attacks the glorification of CEOs who wake up at 4am to get work done, arguing this is a symptom of broken workplace culture rather than admirable discipline. He contends that if a CEO can't get deep work done during normal hours, it's their own fault for creating a dysfunctional environment full of meetings, interruptions, and status reports. The post calls on leaders to fix organizational dysfunction instead of fleeing from it, and champions the idea that a calm 9-to-5 workday is not only possible but preferable.
key_points: The 4am CEO routine is a symptom of workplace dysfunction, not a badge of honor
CEOs who can't get work done during normal hours have only themselves to blame for creating that environment
Organizational chaos stems from executive habits: excessive meetings, interruptions, and status reports
Business media enables the problem by fawning over extreme morning routines instead of questioning them
A normal 9-to-5 workday with time for exercise and family should be the aspirational standard
money_quotes: If you're the CEO, and you can't get work done at work, you only have one person to blame for it: Yourself.
It's your job to fix the damn workplace, not run away from it.
Work isn't crazy because of the nature of its being. Work is crazy because you're making it crazy!
Having to get up at 4am to get real work done is broken. Busted. Kaput.
You know what's cool? Getting to work at 9, putting in eight solid hours, and then being done by 5.
There's no prize for being the first to rise. You're not a fucking bird and there ain't no fucking worm.
Stop playing calendar Tetris with a whole organization.
posts.key_insight: Waking up at 4am to work isn't discipline — it's an admission that you've failed to create a workplace where real work can happen during normal hours.

posts.summary: DHH launches a 'Facebook-Free Business' campaign, urging companies to completely sever ties with Facebook and its subsidiaries. He argues that Facebook's repeated privacy scandals prove the company can't be trusted with user data. The initiative establishes three clear rules: no advertising on Facebook properties, no using them for business communication or promotion, and no assisting Facebook's data collection through Like buttons or login integrations. Basecamp pledges to go Facebook Free immediately and invites other businesses to join, offering a Creative Commons badge for participants.
key_points: Facebook has demonstrated it cannot be trusted with user data through endless privacy scandals involving selling, bartering, and abusing data
The Facebook-Free Business pledge covers three areas: no ads, no business presence or communication, and no data collection assistance like Like buttons or Facebook logins
Businesses vary in Facebook dependence, so adoption will follow a natural curve from innovators to late majority
Companies don't need to solve the general problem of corporate ethics before taking action against companies that have clearly crossed the line
money_quotes: If Facebook's endless privacy scandals have shown one thing, it's that the company has far too much data on its users, and that they can't be trusted not to sell, barter, or abuse that data whether for profit, growth, or negligence.
Being a Facebook-Free Business means your customers can trust that you aren't collaborators with the Facebook machine.
When you spend your money with a Facebook-Free Business, none of that money will find its way back to Facebook's coffers.
We do not assist Facebook in its data collection regime through use of Facebook social Like buttons or by offering Facebook logins.
We don't have to solve that riddle before we take action against companies that have clearly crossed the line, wherever that may be drawn.
Apologies for the Facebook Like button on this Medium page. We're just about ready to get off Medium as well, so that'll take care of that!
posts.key_insight: Businesses have an ethical obligation to stop funding and enabling Facebook's data exploitation machine, and they should publicly commit to severing all ties with the platform.

posts.summary: DHH argues against the false dichotomy of either total apathy or total revolt when it comes to Big Tech dependence. He acknowledges that most people have accepted Big Tech companies are more interested in devouring the world than helping it, but notes that quitting cold turkey is unrealistic for most. Instead, he advocates for incremental reduction in usage, comparing it to gradually cutting back on cigarettes. He points out that these companies walk a precarious tightrope of expectations and even small collective reductions in engagement can have meaningful impact.
key_points: The apathy vs. revolt framing is a false dichotomy that paralyzes people into inaction
Big Tech companies operate on such thin margins of expectation that even small collective reductions matter
Incremental reduction in usage is more sustainable and realistic than going cold turkey
You can mix and match strategies — quit some services entirely while just reducing others
Big Tech companies are experts at helping you not think about their stranglehold on your life
money_quotes: We've almost all come to accept the fact that Big Tech is here less to help the world and more to devour it.
These companies are experts at helping you not think about the structure and stranglehold of their businesses.
You don't have to either resign yourself to your utter insignificance or don a cape while shouting in the wind. There's power in the margins. Tremendous power.

All of these companies are already walking a precarious tightrope of towering expectations. They don't need to miss a quarter by more than a few percent before it's a calamity that'll get everyone's attention.
So here's what you can do: A little bit. It helps. Really.
Every day it'll get a little easier, but you gotta do it. That's the hard part. But it gets easier.
It's not all or nothing. Something counts. Something works.
posts.key_insight: You don't need to completely abandon Big Tech to make a difference — incremental reduction in usage is both more achievable and collectively powerful enough to matter.

posts.summary: DHH announces Signal v Noise's departure from Medium after three years, returning to self-hosted WordPress. The move is driven by Medium's shift toward a paid membership/aggregation model that doesn't align with SvN's non-commercial writing philosophy. DHH frames this as part of a broader pendulum swing back toward independent blogs and away from centralized platforms. The post also signals intent to reduce dependence on Twitter by making SvN the primary home for all content.
key_points: Medium's pivot from attracting publishers to selling aggregated subscriptions changed the value proposition from mutual benefit to extractive
The internet is swinging back toward decentralization as the costs of platform centralization become apparent
Writing should serve communication, not monetization — SvN writes to share ideas, not to generate revenue from content
Owning your own domain proved critical — it allowed a seamless migration because all links still worked
Reducing dependency on Twitter alongside Medium signals a broader commitment to platform independence
money_quotes: Writing for us is not a business, in any direct sense of the word. We write because we have something to say, not to make money off page views, advertisements, or subscriptions.

Traditional blogs might have swung out of favor, as we all discovered the benefits of social media and aggregating platforms, but we think they're about to swing back in style, as we all discover the real costs and problems brought by such centralization.
These days there's less of a "what Medium is doing for us", and a whole lot more of "what we're doing for Medium".
We've grown ever more aware of the problems with centralizing the internet.
The intention is to lessen our dependency on Twitter too, and simply turn Signal v Noise into the independent home for all our thoughts and ideas – big or small.
Thanks to the fact that we kept our own domain when we moved to Medium, all the articles and links still work.
posts.key_insight: Platform centralization inevitably shifts the value equation from serving publishers to extracting from them, making independent ownership of your content and domain essential.

posts.summary: DHH argues that privacy scandals are fundamentally caused by the economic value of personal information in ad targeting. Drawing an analogy to ivory poaching, he contends that eliminating demand for targeted advertising would eliminate the incentive to harvest personal data. He points out that advertising worked fine before personal targeting existed, relying on contextual placement instead. He calls for regulation banning ad targeting by personal information, noting precedents in tobacco and children's advertising restrictions.
key_points: Privacy scandals are demand-driven: personal data is harvested because ad targeting makes it valuable, just as elephants are poached because ivory has a market
Contextual advertising (placing ads based on content rather than personal data) worked before and may be equally effective, citing the NYT's post-GDPR experience
The societal cost of allowing personally targeted ads far outweighs any efficiency gains
Advertising regulation has clear precedent (tobacco, children's advertising) and works effectively
money_quotes: Elephants wouldn't be killed for their tusks if there wasn't a demand for ivory.
Personal information is valuable because we use it to target ads.
But what if you couldn't do that? Then the personal information would cease to have value, and the flood of privacy scandals would stop.
The world of commerce spun around just fine in the era before ads could be targeted by personal information.
The societal price we pay for allowing ads to be targeted is far too high.
Just try to imagine that world without ad targeting. It's hard to imagine that it wouldn't be a better one.
posts.key_insight: The root cause of privacy scandals isn't bad actors but the economic incentive created by ad targeting — ban targeted advertising and the reason to harvest personal data disappears.

posts.summary: DHH argues that web designers should work directly with HTML and CSS rather than creating mockups in tools like Photoshop that get handed off to developers. Drawing from his experience in the late 90s dotcom era and at Basecamp, he contends that designers who work with real web materials produce better results because the feedback loop is shorter and more authentic. He pushes back against the modern JavaScript ecosystem's complexity, which he sees as scaring designers away from implementing their own designs. At Basecamp, designers write HTML, CSS, and often first-pass JavaScript and Rails code, enabled by their server-side rendering approach with Turbolinks and Stimulus.
key_points: Designers who work directly with HTML/CSS produce designs that feel 'of the web' rather than merely 'put on the web', because the real-world feedback loop is shorter and more honest
The JavaScript ecosystem's growing complexity is regressing the industry back toward the old designer-developer handoff model, scaring designers away from implementation
Basecamp's server-side rendering approach with Rails, Turbolinks, and Stimulus deliberately keeps the stack approachable enough for designers to work independently
The notion that 'full-stack' is a point of derision rather than self-sufficiency reflects a harmful trend toward narrow specialization
Basecamp's designers routinely ship their own JavaScript and Rails code, proving this isn't a unicorn scenario but a matter of expectations and tooling choices
money_quotes: Not only would the designs feel like they were of the web, not merely put on the web, but they'd always be better. Less about what it looked like, more about what it worked like.
The towering demands inherent in certain ways of working with JavaScript are rightfully scaring some designers off from implementing their ideas at all. That's a travesty.

The calamity of complexity that the current industry direction on JavaScript is unleashing upon designers is of human choice and design. It's possible to make different choices and arrive at different designs.
Not going back to the dark ages of designers incapable of making their designs work on their own, incapable of making direct changes, and shipping them too!
Also not interested in retreating into the idea that you need a whole team of narrow specialists to make anything work.
Designing for the modern web in a way that pleases users with great, fast designs needn't be this maze of impenetrable complexity. We're making it that! It's possible not to.
posts.key_insight: The modern JavaScript ecosystem's complexity is an avoidable human choice that needlessly pushes designers away from working directly with the web's native materials, and simpler server-side approaches prove there's a better way.

posts.summary: DHH argues that View Source—the ability to inspect how websites are built—is a foundational feature of the web that's being eroded by modern build tools and frameworks. He credits View Source with launching his career and many others', and laments that transpilers, bundlers, and minification have made web source code unreadable. He announces that Basecamp is now shipping source maps in production so anyone can read their JavaScript, and that Rails 6 will ship source maps by default. He frames this as paying tribute to the web's heritage and calls on developers to protect View Source as a civic duty to the platform.
key_points: View Source was a formative learning tool that launched many developers' careers, including DHH's own
Modern JavaScript tooling (Babel, Webpack, TypeScript, minification) has made View Source practically useless for learning
Source maps can restore readability but are typically treated as dev-only tools rather than shipped to production
Basecamp is now shipping source maps in production and Rails 6 will do so by default
Developers have a responsibility to give back to the web platform that enabled them, akin to a Right to Repair ethos
money_quotes: The web isn't just another software platform. It's the greatest software platform the world has ever seen.
Seeing how something real is built puts the individual pieces of the puzzle together in a way that sample code or abstract lessons just don't.
Progress needn't be the enemy of learning.
It's akin to the Right To Repair. Don't glue your application together in such a way that it won't come apart for people who're inclined to inspect what's under the hood.
I like to think of Basecamp as a teaching hospital. The care of our users is our first priority, but it's not the only one.
Some times it's worth taking a step back and ask yourself: What can I do for the web? One answer: Protect and promote View Source.
The web is a better place when we allow others to learn from our work.
posts.key_insight: Developers who built their careers thanks to the open, inspectable web have a duty to preserve that openness by shipping readable source code—including source maps—in production.

posts.summary: DHH announces Jane Yang as Basecamp's new data analyst, hired from over 700 applicants. He uses the announcement to reinforce Basecamp's philosophy on hiring rarely, valuing diverse backgrounds, and using data ethically. Jane's non-profit background aligns with Basecamp's growing charitable initiatives. The post reiterates that Basecamp uses data to get wiser, not to chase growth or maximize extraction.
key_points: Basecamp received 700+ applications but hires rarely, making each new hire significant
Diversity in backgrounds and perspectives leads to better decisions and software
Jane's non-profit experience complements Basecamp's growing charitable commitments inspired by Patagonia's 1% for the Planet
Data at Basecamp is meant to inform and educate, not to drive growth-obsessed metric optimization
The company explicitly rejects exponential growth, domination, and shareholder-value-driven decision making
money_quotes: Running the numbers at Basecamp is not about squeezing every metric until it squeals more, more, MORE.
Data informs us, educates us, but it does not run us.
We make decisions at Basecamp on a much broader base than just 'shareholder value'.
We already have enough. We're not chasing exponential growth.
There's no quest to dominate, capture, or own everything and everyone.
Data analysis is here to help us get better, wiser, kinder. Not greedier, not more extractive.
When people of different backgrounds and perspectives work together, we make better decisions and better software.
posts.key_insight: Data analysis should serve a company's values and wisdom, not fuel an endless appetite for growth and extraction.

posts.summary: DHH shares a brief example of how Basecamp iteratively refines its internal processes, treating the company itself as its most important product. The post highlights a specific process change around 'kickoffs' that was documented in Basecamp's public employee handbook on GitHub. It illustrates Basecamp's philosophy of continuously tweaking organizational practices and transparently codifying them.
key_points: Basecamp treats the company itself as its most important product, subject to continuous improvement
Process changes are tested internally and then codified in their public employee handbook
Organizational practices deserve the same iterative refinement as software products
Transparency is practiced by sharing internal process changes publicly via GitHub
money_quotes: Since our company is itself our most important product, we keep tweaking, experimenting, and – hopefully! – improving the organizational software that makes it run.
posts.key_insight: A company should treat its own organizational processes as a product worthy of continuous iteration, experimentation, and transparent documentation.

posts.summary: DHH reports a credential stuffing attack on Basecamp where over 30,000 login attempts were made using stolen username/password combinations from major data breaches. Of those attempts, 124 accounts were successfully accessed, though no actions were taken within the compromised accounts. The ops team quickly detected the attack, blocked offending IPs, enabled CAPTCHA, and reset passwords for affected accounts. DHH uses the incident as a call to action for users to adopt password managers, breach notification services, and two-factor authentication. A follow-up attack the next day was even larger, using 5,000 IP addresses, but was similarly contained.
key_points: Credential stuffing attacks exploit password reuse — all 124 compromised accounts used credentials found in known breaches like Collection #1
Detection speed matters: the ops team identified the attack within minutes and contained it before any account data was actually exploited
CAPTCHA, while imperfect and sometimes annoying, proved effective at stopping automated credential testing at scale
No amount of server-side defense can fully protect users who reuse passwords and don't enable two-factor authentication
The attack escalated from 30,000 attempts to 5,000+ IP addresses the next day, showing attackers adapt their tactics quickly
money_quotes: All of the unauthorized access was gained using the correct username and password for the account.
It seemed like the attack focused on first validating which accounts were vulnerable, perhaps with a plan to later exploit these vulnerable accounts.
You should never share the same password between multiple services.
If someone has your username and password, and you don't have 2FA protection, there are limits to how effective this protection can be.
CAPTCHA isn't perfect, and some times it's annoying, but it has provided effective protection against this wave of attack.
posts.key_insight: When attackers have your correct credentials from other breaches, the only reliable defenses are unique passwords and two-factor authentication — no amount of server-side protection can substitute for basic personal security hygiene.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Signal v Noise, Basecamp's long-running blog, is leaving Medium after three years and returning to self-hosted WordPress. He explains that Medium's shift toward a paid membership/aggregation model no longer aligns with their goals of writing to share ideas rather than monetize content. He frames the move as part of a broader trend away from centralized platforms and back toward independent blogs. The new site also aims to reduce their dependency on Twitter by becoming the primary home for all their thoughts.
key_points: Medium's pivot from attracting publishers to selling memberships changed the value proposition for Signal v Noise
Centralization of the internet through platforms like Medium carries real costs that are becoming increasingly apparent
Traditional independent blogs are poised to come back as people recognize the downsides of platform dependency
