The relationship with Medium shifted from mutual benefit to one-sided — SvN was doing more for Medium than Medium was doing for them
Owning your own domain and platform is essential for long-term independence
money_quotes: Writing for us is not a business, in any direct sense of the word. We write because we have something to say, not to make money off page views, advertisements, or subscriptions.
We've grown ever more aware of the problems with centralizing the internet.

Traditional blogs might have swung out of favor, as we all discovered the benefits of social media and aggregating platforms, but we think they're about to swing back in style, as we all discover the real costs and problems brought by such centralization.
These days there's less of a 'what Medium is doing for us', and a whole lot more of 'what we're doing for Medium'.
The intention is to lessen our dependency on Twitter too, and simply turn Signal v Noise into the independent home for all our thoughts and ideas — big or small.
posts.key_insight: Platform centralization inevitably shifts the value exchange against content creators, making independent publishing the only sustainable long-term strategy.

posts.summary: DHH argues that passion should not be treated as a prerequisite for starting a business. He pushes back against the romantic notion that entrepreneurs must wait for a perfect, passion-driven idea before launching. Using his own experience at Basecamp, he explains that after nearly twenty years, his motivation comes not from the domain itself but from the craft and practices surrounding the work. Most business is mundane, and that's perfectly fine.
key_points: Passion is helpful but not a requirement for starting or sustaining a business
The romantic idea of waiting for the perfect passion-driven opportunity is a mirage
Most of business is mundane — DHH doesn't spring out of bed yearning to improve project management
What sustains long-term work is enjoying the craft and practices (writing Ruby, sharing lessons, building culture), not the domain itself
The same enjoyable practices can be pursued across many different business domains
money_quotes: There are a lot of hoops to jump and obstacles to climb before starting a new business, but a lack of an all-burning passion for the pursuit shouldn't be one of them.
This romantic idea that there's the perfect opportunity just waiting out there for you to discover it is a mirage.
Most of business, most of the time, is pretty mundane!
I'm not still working at Basecamp because, after nearly twenty years, I just spring out of bed every morning yearning to improve todos, events, messages, or project management in general.
The domain itself isn't a burning bush of passion.
There are lots of reasons for why you'd want to start and run a business. Passion isn't a permit you need to acquire before setting off.
posts.key_insight: Passion for the domain is overrated as a business prerequisite — what matters more is enjoying the craft and practices that the work enables you to pursue.

posts.summary: DHH provides a detailed outage report for Basecamp 2 and Basecamp 3's search feature, which were mostly offline for over 8 hours due to catastrophic network failures at their cloud provider. The outage took down primary, backup, and multiple ad-hoc network connections between critical services. DHH takes full ownership of the failure despite it originating with the cloud provider, arguing that architecture decisions are always within Basecamp's control. The post includes a minute-by-minute timeline showing repeated cycles of restoration and failure as the team tried to route around the damage.
key_points: A cloud provider network failure took down both primary and backup network links, causing an 8+ hour outage affecting Basecamp 2 entirely and Basecamp 3's search feature
DHH takes full responsibility despite the root cause being external, arguing that system architecture choices are always within their control
The team attempted multiple workarounds including secondary and tertiary backup connections, all of which were repeatedly disrupted
The incident exposed limitations of redundant and double-redundant backup connections as a reliability strategy
DHH commits to changing how Basecamp works with cloud providers and architects systems to prevent similar outages
money_quotes: Even in this new world of cloud services, it's still always our fault when Basecamp isn't available.
Whatever the underlying problem for an outage, there's always something you could have done to prevent it.

While this incident may have been triggered by network issues outside of our immediate control, it's always within our control how we architect our systems, how we prepare for disasters, and how we ensure something like this never has the power to inflict such a traumatic outage.
We've learned some hard lessons about network availability, the limitations of redundant, and double redundant backup connections.
So I want to make absolutely clear that this is our failure.
We won't rest until it's all sorted and stable.
posts.key_insight: Even when outages originate with third-party cloud providers, the responsibility lies with your own architectural decisions and disaster preparedness — it's always your fault when your service goes down.

posts.summary: DHH documents Basecamp's second major outage in a week, caused by a sustained cloud storage provider failure that took Basecamp 3 mostly offline for about two hours. He expresses genuine shame and frustration, acknowledging that the repeated failures have exposed deep organizational unpreparedness despite their confidence otherwise. The company is halting all major product development to address single points of failure and pulling back from their cloud migration until they can build a multi-region, multi-provider setup. The post includes a detailed play-by-play timeline of the incident and recovery.
key_points: Basecamp suffered its second major outage in one week due to a cloud storage provider failure, dropping from aspirations of 99.999% uptime to struggling for 99%
DHH acknowledges the outages have called into question Basecamp's entire risk management and operational structure
All major product development is being halted to focus on eliminating single points of failure
Basecamp is pulling back from cloud migration until they can commit to a multi-region, multi-provider architecture
The post serves as both a public apology and a transparent incident report with timestamped updates
money_quotes: This goes much deeper than just a few lessons. It has called into question our entire risk management and operational structure at Basecamp.
From reaching for 99.999% in uptime – the hallowed five nines! – we're now scrambling for two of them. From riches of reliance to rags of shambles.
I'm not going to bother you with platitudes about 'lessons to be learnt', because I've already done that just a few days ago.
We're stopping all major product development at Basecamp for the moment, and dedicating all our attention to fixing these single points of failure that the recent cloud outages have revealed.
To say this is humbling is an epic understatement.

That's incredibly painful. Both because it's because we're failing our customers for the second time in a week, but also because it's showing us just how unprepared we've been as an organization to deal with these cloud challenges, despite our belief otherwise.
posts.key_insight: Repeated cloud outages forced Basecamp to confront that their risk management was fundamentally inadequate, leading them to halt product development and rethink their entire cloud migration strategy.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Apple's MacBook keyboard failure rate is far higher than Apple acknowledges, because most affected users never contact Apple for repairs. He presents data from Basecamp showing 30% of MacBook users currently have keyboard issues, and a Twitter poll of 7,000+ respondents showing a 63% failure rate. He notes the 3rd generation membrane keyboard fix didn't solve the problem, with nearly half of 2018+ MacBooks at Basecamp having failed keyboards. He frames this as a disaster that threatens decades of Apple's brand goodwill.
key_points: Apple's official failure statistics are misleading because most users never report keyboard problems, instead living with broken keys or switching to external keyboards
30% of MacBook users at Basecamp are currently dealing with keyboard issues, and the 3rd generation membrane keyboard made things worse, not better
A Twitter poll of 7,000+ respondents showed 63% failure rate, with only 11% of affected users actually contacting Apple
The real test of Apple's health is not the keyboard crisis itself but whether they accept responsibility and respond appropriately
money_quotes: Apple keep insisting that only a 'small number of customers have problems' with the MacBook keyboards. That's bollocks.
The fact is that many people simply do not contact Apple when their MacBook keyboards fail. They just live with an S key that stutters or a spacebar that intermittently gives double.
Out of the 47 people using MacBooks at the company, a staggering 30% are dealing with keyboard issues right now!!

Worth noting here is that the 3rd generation membrane keyboard did nothing to fix the issues. Six out of thirteen – nearly half!! – of the 2018+ MacBooks we have at the company have a failed keyboard.
This is a disaster. A complete unmitigated disaster.
In a time of crisis, the event itself is less indicative of the health of a company than the response.
Is Apple going to accept that they're currently alienating and undermining decades of goodwill by shipping broken computers in mass quantities?
posts.key_insight: Apple's reliance on support contact data to measure keyboard failure rates creates a massive blind spot, as most users simply endure broken keyboards rather than seeking repair.

posts.summary: DHH pushes back against the common interview question asking for 'the one thing' that leads to success. He argues there is no single secret or principle that unlocks professional or entrepreneurial achievement. Instead, success comes from an interwoven tapestry of many practices and insights working together. He points to his own books with Jason Fried as evidence — collections of dozens of separate essays rather than one unifying narrative. The quest for a silver bullet actually prevents people from absorbing the compound wisdom that really matters.
key_points: There is no single secret or principle that guarantees professional or entrepreneurial success
The obsession with finding 'the one thing' prevents people from doing the harder work of absorbing many lessons
Success comes from compound wisdom — many interwoven practices contributing together
Books like REWORK (88 essays) and It Doesn't Have To Be Crazy At Work (66 essays) reflect this reality of many small insights over one big revelation
money_quotes: Wouldn't it be great if there really was just one secret you had to know, and all your professional or entrepreneurial dreams would come true?
The boring truth is that the big leaps are all the result of an interwoven tapestry of practices, each contributing a strand of progress or insight.
If you pull just one, all you have is that thin, disconnected strand. It's only together the colors come alive.

As long as you're stuck on a quest for that one super-power practice or North Star principle, you're not going to make space in your brain for the fact that no individual secret is going to make the difference.
Only compound wisdom will.
posts.key_insight: There is no single secret to success — only the compound effect of many interwoven practices and insights creates meaningful progress.

posts.summary: DHH responds to Jason Calacanis's argument that Americans must work harder to compete with China, calling it fear-mongering rooted in Cold War rhetoric. He rejects the premise that matching China's exploitative 996 work culture is a path to 'winning.' DHH argues that accepting your opponent's worst practices is a strategic mistake, not a competitive advantage. He points to Europe's quality of life as evidence that growth-at-all-costs isn't the only measure of success. The post concludes that doubling down on exploitation accelerates capitalism's ethical decay rather than saving it.
key_points: Using nationalist fear to justify overwork is a desperate, outdated tactic reminiscent of Cold War propaganda
Adopting your competitor's worst practices is a strategic mistake, not a competitive advantage
Defining 'winning' solely as greater growth ignores quality-of-life metrics where Europe outperforms the US
The global backlash against overwork culture — from Japan's karoshi to China's 996 movement — signals a growing rejection of workaholism
Racing to the bottom on labor exploitation accelerates capitalism's ethical problems rather than solving them
money_quotes: Those who reject the wisdom of overwork is really helping the ENEMY. This is democracy vs communism!! What is this, 1950?
This is at best a lateral move from 'work harder or the kitten gets it'. A trope that's meant to be a punchline, not a policy recommendation.
Accepting the terms of engagement by your so-called opponent is a basic, rookie mistake in any form of strategic out-maneuvering.
You're not going to 'beat' the Chinese by one-upping 996 with 997.
If you define winning solely as 'who has the greater growth', you've already lost.
If you dismiss the standard of living enjoyed in Europe as a 'retirement society', you're the one who deserves to be dismissed.
The ideological underpinnings of capitalism are already in an advanced state of ethical decay.
Racing to the bottom just gets you there faster.
posts.key_insight: Competing with exploitative work cultures by adopting their worst practices isn't winning — it's surrendering your own values while racing to the bottom.

posts.summary: Basecamp is hiring a Director of Operations to manage their technical infrastructure team of seven, overseeing a mix of owned data-center servers and cloud deployments on Google Cloud and AWS. The role combines hands-on technical work with team management, emphasizing reliability, risk mitigation, and building a resilience culture. DHH outlines Basecamp's management philosophy: no micromanagement, systems thinking over individual blame, and calm, sustainable work practices. The post doubles as a manifesto for how Basecamp thinks about operations — favoring prevention over heroics and sound engineering over firefighting. Compensation is $280K-$350K based on San Francisco rates regardless of location, reflecting their remote-first approach.
key_points: Operations leadership should prioritize prevention and sound practices over heroic firefighting — they want someone who follows building codes, not someone who puts out five fires at once
Basecamp uses a hybrid infrastructure model (owned servers plus cloud) and needs leadership to navigate trade-offs between the two
The role demands both technical depth and management skill — the director must participate in on-call rotations and get hands dirty
Basecamp's management philosophy centers on systems thinking: when something goes wrong, ask 'how was this possible' rather than 'who's to blame'
Pay is pegged to San Francisco top-10% rates regardless of location, and benefits are designed to support life outside work rather than trap people at the office
money_quotes: If our applications aren't available, it doesn't matter how well we've designed them, how clever we are at marketing them, or how good our support team is. Nobody wants to buy flaky.
To manage the work at Basecamp, you need to know the work and do the work.
Someone who reads a story about the intern dropping the production database and wonders not 'what an idiot' but 'how was this possible in the first place?'.

What we're not looking for is a superhero who might be able to put out five fires burning at once by working 80+ hours/week, but a candidate who avoids the drama by following sound building codes, performing regular inspections, and responding to incidents with a tight routine.
Our benefits are all aimed at supporting a life well lived away from work. None are about trapping people at the office or cajoling them into endless overtime.
We value people who can take a stand yet commit even when they disagree.
posts.key_insight: Great operations leadership is about building systems that prevent disasters through disciplined practices, not about hiring heroes who can survive them.

posts.summary: Basecamp has raised its minimum pay to $70,000 for all employees, regardless of role or location. The company benchmarks salaries at the top 10% of San Francisco market rates, even though no employees live there. DHH explains that while this benchmark works well for programmers and designers, it didn't adequately lift pay for roles like customer support where tech hasn't raised market rates equally. The new floor creates a roughly 5x ratio between highest and lowest paid employees, which DHH frames as more egalitarian than typical tech companies. He positions this as an example for other companies while cautioning them to grow into such policies sustainably.
key_points: Basecamp pays SF top-10% rates to all employees regardless of location, making them extremely competitive in lower cost-of-living areas
Tech industry market rates disproportionately benefit programmers and designers while leaving support and admin roles behind
The $70,000 minimum breaks the market-rate peg for roles where benchmarks don't reflect adequate compensation
The entire company salary range is roughly 5x from lowest to highest paid, which is more compressed than typical tech companies
Sustainable profitability over 15 years enabled these policies — DHH warns against companies overextending to match venture-funded competitors
money_quotes: We pay at the top 10% of San Francisco market rates for salary + bonus at Basecamp. Those rates apply to everyone here, regardless of the role or where they actually live.
While we might be top 10% for San Francisco, I'm pretty sure we're in the top 1% for, say, Fenwick, Ontario or Spokane, Washington or Madrid, Spain.
Where before we'd have roles that started in the $50,000s, our new minimum wage at Basecamp is now $70,000, regardless of what the benchmarks say.
We're choosing to link 'minimum pay' not simply with what's the least we can get away with, but rather as literally just the lowest pay that we offer at Basecamp.
We can't fix all the problems in technology, whether around pay or otherwise. But we can set one example.
Like having the pay range of the entire company, not just the most sought-after positions, feel the rising wealth of technology.
Not because we're putting ourselves in a bind trying to keep up with Mr Venture and Mrs Capital.
posts.key_insight: Tech companies that benchmark only to market rates perpetuate inequality across roles — profitable companies should deliberately break that peg to ensure all employees share in the industry's wealth.

posts.summary: DHH argues that open source software, particularly under the MIT license, should be understood outside market-based frameworks of debt, obligation, and scarcity. Drawing on anthropologist David Graeber's work on pre-monetary economies, he contends that the 'tragedy of the commons' is a conceptual misappropriation for software since it has zero marginal cost — freeloaders are literally free. He critiques both Gates' proprietary model and Stallman's GPL as rooted in the same scarcity-based, libertarian property-rights paradigm. Using Rails as his case study, he reframes open source as a path to Maslovian self-transcendence and existential meaning, arguing that the 'sustainable open source' crisis narrative is overblown and that treating contributions as market transactions destroys the very meaning that makes the work worthwhile.
key_points: The 'tragedy of the commons' doesn't apply to open source software because software has zero marginal cost — there is no over-grazing problem and freeloaders cost nothing

Both Bill Gates and Richard Stallman built their worldviews on scarcity-based thinking and strong property rights enforced through licenses, making them more ideologically similar than commonly assumed
The MIT license represents a radical departure from both proprietary and copyleft models — it's an 'anti-license' that rejects transactional obligations entirely
Open source work provides meaning through Maslow's hierarchy: autonomy, mastery, and purpose leading to self-transcendence, but only when base needs are met through other means
The 'sustainable open source' narrative and inflated startup mission statements both stem from the same crisis of meaning in a market-soaked tech industry
money_quotes: The magic of software is that there is virtually no marginal cost! The freeloaders are free! There is no practical scarcity to worry about.

Both Gates and Stallman built their life's work on the back of copyright law. One with the right to extract gobs of money from his proprietary software monopoly. The other with the right to extract contributions and distribution concessions from users of his open source software.

When I look at the literally billions of dollars in business that's been done on the basis of this thing I started, I don't look at that with envy or an open mouth. I don't think 'I should have had some of that'. I think what a wonderful world!
For fuck's sake, Dropbox. You host files. You make the files appear on all my computers.
If you dress up like a salesman, it's a little disingenuous to be surprised when people think they're buying a product.
Working on open source, outside of the context of the marketplace and its expectations, was a lifeline.

Open source, as seen through the altruistic lens of the MIT gift license, has the power to break us free from this overly rational cost-benefit analysis bullshit that's impoverishing our lives in so many other ways.
Contribute to the Rails community because it brings meaning to your life. Because writing Ruby sparks joy. Don't participate if it doesn't.
posts.key_insight: Open source software exists beyond the economics of scarcity, and treating it as a market transaction destroys the existential meaning that makes it sustainable in the first place.

posts.summary: DHH argues against the 'spray and pray' approach to job applications, comparing it to buying lottery tickets. He acknowledges that job searching is painful on both sides—applicants face black-hole processes while employers wade through unqualified candidates. The core problem is the mindset that applying to everything maximizes your chances, when in reality repeated rejection from stretch positions erodes your confidence and dilutes the quality of your applications. He distinguishes this from imposter syndrome, endorsing encouragement for qualified people who hold back, while cautioning against treating hiring like a numbers game.
key_points: The job application process is broken on both sides—applicants face black holes, employers face floods of low-effort applications
The 'just apply everywhere' mindset treats hiring like a lottery, but qualifications matter in ways that lottery odds don't
Repeated rejection from unrealistic applications gradually erodes confidence and application quality
Encouraging qualified people with imposter syndrome to apply is good; encouraging unqualified mass-applications is harmful
Applicants should be strategic and realistic, stretching slightly rather than swinging wildly
money_quotes: Sure, you can't hit if you don't swing, but it doesn't matter how many times I swing, I'm not going to hit a homerun against a Yankees pitcher.
Every time you don't hear back, you can lose a tiny little something of yourself.
If you keep applying for unlikely-to-get jobs, you might eventually water yourself down, and dilute your application, until it's a very thin cup of tea indeed.
Let's stop pretending that the hiring process is a lottery. That sending out the most resumes is how you win it.
Boosting that confidence amongst the qualified with encouragement is ace. Let's keep doing that.
Apply when you have a real shot. Stretch a little, but not too much. Save yourself and your ego from the lottery trap.
posts.key_insight: Mass-applying to jobs you're unqualified for doesn't improve your odds—it erodes your confidence and application quality through accumulated rejection, making you worse at landing the jobs you actually could get.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the cultural obsession with celebrating 'hard work' is misguided and counterproductive. He points out survivorship bias — countless people work extremely hard without achieving success — while many successful people work reasonable hours. He contends that effort alone is not accomplishment, and that the fetishization of hard work stems from a pessimistic view of human nature as inherently lazy. Instead, he advocates celebrating creativity, insights, and breakthroughs over mere effort.
key_points: Survivorship bias undermines the 'hard work equals success' narrative — most hard workers never achieve celebrated success
Many successful people work normal 40-hour weeks but still feel obligated to pay tribute to the 'hard work' mythology
Effort is not accomplishment — repeating the same lesson endlessly is inferior to advancing through diverse challenges
The hard work cult assumes humans are naturally lazy, when creativity and drive are actually innate
Celebrating 'hard work' primarily serves to cajole people into pursuing alienating goals that benefit others
money_quotes: Effort is not accomplishment.
The world is full of people who work very hard, in that literal, long-hours sort of way, and yet only a tiny minority of those end up with fawning fans celebrating that oh-so-hard work.
If you repeat the same lesson a hundred times over, you'll be left behind on the path to insight by the person who advances through a hundred different lessons.
This obsession with 'hard work' is founded in a pessimistic view of natural state of humanity being lazy loafers.
The virtuous label of 'hard work' is only necessary when you seek to cajole people into doing a lot of what they intrinsically do not want to do.
The drive for creativity and creation is innate.
Why am I celebrating mere effort? Celebrate creativity, insights, breakthroughs, rebellions, anything but mere effort.
posts.key_insight: Celebrating 'hard work' mistakes effort for achievement and serves to normalize exploitation, when what actually deserves praise is creativity, insight, and meaningful output.

posts.summary: DHH pushes back against the trend of dismissing work-life balance as outdated or naive. He argues that critics who claim you need 'integration' instead of balance, or that balance is impossible, are engaging in circular logic. The post defends balance as simply ensuring each part of life has sustainable weight. He emphasizes that enjoying work and appreciating time away from it are not contradictory, and that limiting hours and taking vacations is a straightforward pursuit of balance.
key_points: Work-life balance has become a fashionable concept to dismiss, with critics pushing 'integration' as a replacement
The semantic argument that 'work is part of life, so balance is wrong' is circular and pedantic
Loving your work and valuing time away from it are fully compatible
Regular breaks, limited hours, and vacations are practical, not exotic arrangements
money_quotes: Balance simple means that each portion of the system has a sustainable weight which keeps the composition in harmony.
Going down a pedantic, semantic rabbit hole of "well, actually, work is part of life, ergo seeking balance is wrong" is some perfidy circular logic.
It's possible to enjoy, like, or even love work, and yet also appreciate life away from work.
Appreciate the breaks and the distance from work that rewards us with perspective.
So let's take a break from worksplaining the concept of work-life balance into a negation.
Balance is neither bad nor impossible. It's the cornerstone of a healthy, productive, and sustainable state of being.
posts.key_insight: Work-life balance is a straightforward, achievable goal that critics have needlessly complicated with semantic games and performative workaholism.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the disconnect between people who admire Basecamp's philosophy, books, and Rails but have never actually tried the Basecamp product. He acknowledges this is partly their own failure to draw the connection and ask directly. Rather than relying on surveillance-capitalism-style ad targeting, Basecamp has historically relied on goodwill and word of mouth, but DHH admits they've taken that for granted. The post ends with a direct, humble ask for readers to try Basecamp.
key_points: People who love your philosophy won't automatically try your product — you have to explicitly ask them
Longevity breeds a false assumption that awareness equals adoption
Basecamp rejects invasive ad targeting but hasn't adequately activated the goodwill they've built
There's an unsolved tension between authentic promotion and coming off as a self-promoting shill
money_quotes: If you want people to give your product a chance, you gotta ask them.
We've failed to do the work; failed to draw the connection.
Part of it is the lazy assumption that since you've been around for a long time, then of course people who like your philosophy or perspective would know what you make, and would have given it a try.
They ask a bunch of strangers to try their product that they've targeted through the mechanisms of surveillance capitalism.
You have to activate that goodwill, if you want it to translate into sales that sustain all that making, sharing, and teaching.
How do you ask people who might not be your friends, but don't feel like strangers either, if they'd try your product?
posts.key_insight: Building an audience through philosophy and goodwill doesn't automatically convert to product adoption — you still have to directly ask people to try what you make.

posts.summary: DHH criticizes Silicon Valley's defensive reaction when successful startups face scrutiny, using Facebook and Superhuman's privacy-violating read receipts as examples. He argues that scrutiny naturally scales with a company's impact and user base, calling it 'a feature, not a bug.' He pushes back against the Valley tribalism that frames criticism as attacks on makers rather than legitimate accountability. DHH contends that the entire Silicon Valley ecosystem needs ethical recalibration, and that companies should welcome scrutiny as an opportunity to course-correct early.
key_points: Silicon Valley insiders reflexively defend startups against even mild scrutiny, framing critics as conspiracy theorists trying to hold down makers
Scrutiny should scale proportionally with a company's success and user base — more users means greater potential for harm
Superhuman's abusive read receipts feature illustrates how small early ethical decisions set a company's trajectory
Valley tribalism becomes dangerous when it turns into apologism for genuine privacy abuses
The entire Silicon Valley ecosystem's ethical trajectory needs recalibration, one company at a time
money_quotes: The scope of a startup's impact and influence is correlated to the amount of scrutiny it receives. This is a feature, not a bug!
It's like the thinking goes that success does not attract scrutiny because of the broader impact it affords, but simply because conspiracy theorists are trying to hold down the makers!
When that affection becomes a set of blinders or an apologist shield, you can't be surprised when the broader community raises an eyebrow and is encouraged to dig deeper.
There's an ethical trajectory of a whole ecosystem, and the one in Silicon Valley is in need of some serious recalibration.
Springing to the defense of appalling privacy abuses with excuses like 'well, everyone else does it' only reveals just how dire the need is for that recalibration.
The more people are using something, the greater the potential for harm (and good). This isn't rocket science.
Successful startups (and their boosters) should celebrate the scrutiny when it arrives by listening and making changes in actions and culture.
posts.key_insight: Scrutiny is the natural and healthy consequence of a startup's growing impact, and Silicon Valley's reflexive defensiveness against it reveals an ecosystem in need of serious ethical recalibration.

posts.summary: DHH argues that most companies' hiring processes are broken, with generic job postings written by people too far removed from the actual work. He criticizes the common claim of 'hiring the best' as nonsense, arguing that most people can do great or mediocre work depending on their environment. Instead, companies should invest time in crafting honest, specific job postings that attract complementary candidates. He shares Basecamp's approach of having team leaders write postings with input from future coworkers.
key_points: Job postings are too often written by HR or executives disconnected from the actual role, resulting in generic template-driven listings
The notion of 'hiring the best' in absolute terms is nonsense — most people's performance depends heavily on their environment
Good job postings require significant time and effort to honestly describe the role, work, and organization
The goal should be expanding the candidate pool by attracting complementary people who might not otherwise apply
Hiring postings should be written by team leaders and reviewed by future coworkers, not outsourced to HR
money_quotes: The disconnect between how many companies claim that they only hire the best and how they try to actually do that is perverse.
The world is full of people who are stuck doing mediocre work in a shitty environment or blessed to do stellar work by virtue of an elevating one. Most people are well capable of doing both!
The only thing that makes sense is to hire the best – defined as most complementary to the organization – person out of the candidates who apply.
Writing a good job posting is hard because it requires you to actually think about what the position entails and how to realistically portray the organization it's within.
The vast majority of potential candidates in this world are not going to apply to your position in any case. The aim of a great job posting is to expand the pool in awareness of that fact.
It's a banal statement that hiring is some of the most important work that an organization does. But that doesn't make it any less true.
posts.key_insight: Great hiring starts with honest, carefully crafted job postings written by the people closest to the work, not with the delusion that you're selecting from an absolute ranking of talent.

posts.summary: DHH addresses the uncomfortable reality that Basecamp asks job applicants to invest significant time in applications but cannot provide personalized rejection feedback. With 400+ applications for a single programmer role, spending just 20 minutes per rejection would consume nearly 7 weeks of work. He acknowledges this is unfair to applicants who invest hours in tailored cover letters and CVs, and commits to being more transparent about the process upfront so candidates can make informed decisions about whether to apply.
key_points: Basecamp's application process demands substantial time investment from candidates including custom cover letters and detailed CVs
The math of providing individual feedback at scale is prohibitive — 400 applications at 20 minutes each equals nearly 7 weeks of work
Applicants who invest hours deserve feedback but realistically will only get a generic rejection email
The solution isn't to fix the feedback problem but to be honest upfront that no feedback will be given
money_quotes: It can easily take hours to apply to a job at Basecamp.
So it's not an unreasonable expectation to hope for some detailed feedback if the application isn't successful.
And yet the math simply makes that impossible for us.

It's easily the worst part of hiring to reject hundreds of applicants who've put in a lot of their time with a generic "thank you for applying, but unfortunately we've moved forward with other candidates!".
So the least we can do is to be honest that this is the process.
Hiring is hard.
posts.key_insight: When you demand high effort from job applicants but can't reciprocate with personalized feedback, the least you owe them is upfront honesty about that asymmetry.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Basecamp is removing all pixel tracking from its emails, prompted by the Superhuman email tracking scandal. While Basecamp's tracking was relatively innocuous (aggregate open rates and diagnostics), they concluded no use case justified tracking everyone's emails all the time. They killed diagnostics tracking, turned off mailing list tracking, and plan to remove Google Analytics next. DHH frames this as part of a broader reckoning the tech industry must have about data collection.
key_points: The Superhuman tracking scandal prompted Basecamp to examine its own email tracking practices, even though theirs were far less invasive
Reading an email shouldn't leave a data trail regardless of whether that trail is used innocuously or deviously
The only remaining email 'tracking' marks messages as read within Basecamp itself, serving the recipient rather than the sender
The tech industry has been so accustomed to capturing data that it forgot to ask whether it should
Privacy is framed as both an ethical imperative and a business advantage
money_quotes: Reading an email shouldn't leave a long data trail, regardless of whether that trail is used in fairly innocuous ways, like an aggregate open-rate calculation, or in its most devious, like spying on whether a personal email has been seen and from where.
The tech industry has been so used to capturing whatever data it could for so long that it has almost forgotten to ask whether it should.
This gluttonous collection of data must stop.
Privacy isn't just the right thing to do, it's also better business.
A feature in service of the recipient, not the sender, not us.
Discerning customers are already demanding it, and everyone else will too soon enough.
posts.key_insight: Even innocuous data collection should be eliminated unless it strictly serves the customer, because the tech industry's default posture of capturing everything it can is fundamentally wrong.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the internet has been colonized by a small number of tech companies, and that individuals have the power to fight back through their daily choices. He advocates for switching to alternatives like DuckDuckGo, even if they're not perfect replacements. The core argument is that alternatives offering 95% of the utility with none of the ethical baggage are worth adopting. He challenges people who complain about Big Tech dominance but won't make even small sacrifices to support alternatives.
key_points: The internet has been colonized and exploited by a small cabal of tech companies
You don't need to go cold turkey on Big Tech — every small switch helps
Alternatives don't need to be 100% as good to be worth using
Refusing to sacrifice even 5% of convenience while lamenting the lack of alternatives is hypocritical
money_quotes: The internet is hurting. It's been colonized and exploited by a small cabal of tech companies.
You don't have to go cold turkey on everything Big Tech. That's almost impossible at the moment. Such is the stronghold.
The world is full of alternatives to the Big Tech offerings that give you 95% of the utility for 0% of the regret.
If you can't even be bothered to give up 5% to help an alternative along, you also can't be surprised when the alternatives are so few and far between.
You can heal the internet. One choice – one search! – at the time.
posts.key_insight: Supporting imperfect alternatives to Big Tech is both a moral obligation and a practical necessity — waiting for a perfect replacement guarantees none will ever arrive.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Basecamp removed the email address requirement from their free ebook 'Getting Real,' following the same approach they took with 'Shape Up.' He argues that requiring email addresses in exchange for free content is a sleazy, coercive practice. He criticizes the broader content marketing industry for manufacturing insincere content purely to harvest contact information. The post advocates for sharing valuable things freely without transactional strings attached.
key_points: Gating free content behind email collection is coercive — if your mailing list were worth joining, you wouldn't need to trick people into it
Content marketing as an industry is built on packaging manufactured 'pink slime' as valuable content to trade for contact information
This transactional approach to sharing is the same insincere logic behind influencer marketing — disguising sales with purchased credibility
The pervasive A/B-optimized manipulation is why people are so skeptical and cynical today
Not everything needs to be tracked or pay off — sharing things freely because they're helpful or fun is perfectly valid
money_quotes: If you have a mailing list that's worth signing up for, you don't need to trick, cajole, or bribe people in other to get them on board.
There's a whole cottage industry of bullshit around how CONTENT MARKETING is supposed to work.
Don't invent things to say or to share just such that you can package up that pink slime as a golden nugget of truth to trade for someone's contact information.
It's all about disguising the sale with a thin, flimsy layer of purchased credibility.
No wonder we're all so skeptical and cynical these days. Because we have a million good A/B-optimized reasons to be.
Not everything needs to be tracked. Not everything needs to pay off.
posts.key_insight: Requiring email addresses in exchange for free content is a coercive marketing tactic that erodes trust — share things freely because they're worth sharing, not as bait for contact harvesting.

posts.summary: Basecamp replaced its Google Sign-In-based two-factor authentication with a self-built TOTP-based system, eliminating the requirement for users to have a Google account. After initially panicking when SMS-based 2FA was deemed insecure, they had outsourced 2FA to Google as a quick fix but found requiring a Google account to be an uncomfortable privacy compromise. The new system works with any TOTP-compatible app like Authy, 1Password, or Duo. DHH frames this as part of a broader effort to disentangle Basecamp from Big Tech dependencies, including having already dropped Google Analytics.
key_points: SMS-based 2FA was insecure, and Basecamp initially outsourced the problem to Google Sign-In rather than building a proper replacement
Requiring a Google account for 2FA security was an uncomfortable privacy tradeoff given Google's data collection business model
The new TOTP-based system with backup codes works with any compatible authentication app across all Basecamp products
This is part of a deliberate strategy to remove Big Tech dependencies from Basecamp's business, including dropping Google Analytics
money_quotes: There are about a million good reasons for why you wouldn't want Google to know everything about when you log into apps all over the internet.

Google's business is literally based on collecting as much data as possible, so it can use it all against you for ad targeting. That's just not a regime we feel comfortable encouraging, let alone requiring.
It feels good to have one additional area of the business free from Big Tech entanglement.
We'll continue the work to untangle ourselves from Google and the rest of the industry behemoths. It's a long slog, it's unlikely ever to be fully complete, but every little bit helps.
So I'm thrilled to announce that we got our shit together and built our own, wonderful, and secure 2FA login protection for Basecamp.
posts.key_insight: Companies should invest in building their own critical infrastructure rather than outsourcing it to Big Tech, even when the DIY approach seems daunting, because depending on surveillance-capitalism companies creates uncomfortable compromises for your users.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his week-long experiment switching to a Surface Laptop 3 after years of frustration with Apple's butterfly keyboards. Despite enjoying the hardware and initial setup, he was disappointed by Windows' poor font rendering, the immature state of WSL for Unix development, and numerous friction points like needing a Windows Pro upgrade for Docker. He returned the laptop after five days, concluding that macOS remains far superior for developer workflows, but argues Apple needs more competition to improve.
key_points: Apple's broken butterfly keyboards drove DHH to seriously consider Windows as an alternative after twenty years on Mac
Windows font rendering remains noticeably inferior to macOS, which matters for developers who stare at text all day
WSL is not yet ready for seamless Unix-style development — terrible filesystem performance and requiring alpha software make it impractical
The experiment made DHH appreciate macOS more, particularly its font rendering, native Unix tooling, and overall polish
Apple needs real competition from Windows to stay motivated, and DHH encourages others to make the switch even though he won't
money_quotes: Apple's stubborn four-year refusal to fix the terminally broken butterfly keyboard design led me to a crazy experiment last week: Giving Windows a try for the first time in twenty years.
The font rendering in Windows remains excruciatingly poor to my eyes. It just looks bad.

I accept that this is a personal failure of sorts. The Windows font rendering does not prevent you from using the device. It's not like you can't read the text. It's just that I don't enjoy it, and I don't want to.
It feels like my toes are so stubbed and bloody by the end of the walk that I almost forgot why I started on this journey in the first place.
I kept looking for things I liked about Windows, and I kept realizing that I just fell back on rationalizations like 'I guess this isn't SO bad?'.
What this experiment taught me, though, was just how much I actually like OSX. How much satisfaction I derive from its font rendering. How lovely my code looks in TextMate 2.
Apple needs the competition. We need to feel like there are real alternatives that not only are technically possible, but a joy to use.
Also, Apple, please just fix those fucking keyboards. Provide proper restitution for the people who bought your broken shit.
posts.key_insight: Despite Apple's maddening hardware failures, macOS remains so superior for developer experience that even a week of genuine effort with Windows couldn't overcome the gap — but Apple desperately needs the competitive pressure.

posts.summary: DHH breaks down Basecamp's $3 million annual operations budget to show that only 15% ($450,000) is spent on actually running Ruby on Rails app and job servers. The vast majority goes to infrastructure like file storage, databases, search, and caching that would be needed regardless of programming language. Even if a hypothetical technology were twice as fast, the savings ($225,000) would be trivial compared to developer salaries. He argues that making the 15-person developer team even 15% less productive by switching languages would cost more than the entire Ruby/Rails compute budget. The conclusion is that for most SaaS companies, choosing a programming language based on raw performance is economically irrational.
key_points: File storage is Basecamp's largest operations cost at $1M/year for 4.5 petabytes, dwarfing compute costs
Most infrastructure spend (databases, search, caching, storage) is language-agnostic and would exist regardless of tech stack
Even a 2x performance improvement would only save $225,000/year — less than Basecamp's annual employee Christmas gift
Developer productivity matters far more than compute efficiency: a 15% productivity loss would exceed the entire Ruby/Rails compute budget
For most SaaS companies, the programming language and framework represent a small minority of total operations cost
money_quotes: So against a $3 million budget, it's about $450,000. That's it.
We spend more money than that on the Xmas gift we give employees at Basecamp every year.
That reduction wouldn't even pay for two developers at our average all-in cost at Basecamp!
If we make that team just 15% less productive, it'll cost us more than everything we spend to run Ruby and Rails at Basecamp!

For a company like Basecamp, you'd be mad to make your choice of programming language and web framework on anything but a determination of what'll make your programmers the most motivated, happy, and productive.
Whatever the cost, it's worth it. It's worth it on a pure cost/benefit, but, more importantly, it's worth it in terms of human happiness and potential.
This is why we run Ruby. This is why we run Rails. It's a complete bargain.
posts.key_insight: For most SaaS companies, developer happiness and productivity dwarf compute costs, making programming language performance benchmarks economically irrelevant to the tech stack decision.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Basecamp has removed the last third-party tracking pixel from basecamp.com, continuing their push to reduce data collection and protect user privacy. He frames the decision as an obligation in a post-2010 world where collective naiveté about data exploitation has been shattered. Basecamp replaced Google Analytics with homegrown tools that track only basics like page visits and signup rates. He acknowledges the journey isn't complete—Google Sign-In and some third-party processors remain—but argues the calculus has shifted: convenience no longer justifies sending user data to outside parties. He encourages others to take similar incremental steps toward reducing their data footprint.
key_points: Basecamp removed all third-party tracking pixels from their marketing site, serving everything from their own domains
Their homegrown analytics deliberately tracks only basics (visits, signups, A/B results) and doesn't try to match Google Analytics' sophistication
The bar for using third-party data processors has fundamentally changed—slight convenience or cost savings no longer justifies sharing customer data
Privacy improvement is incremental work: they still use Google Sign-In and Customer.io but plan to phase these out
Untangling from the old data paradigm is neither quick, easy, nor free, but worth doing step by step
money_quotes: Can you believe we used to willingly tell Google about every single visitor to basecamp.com by way of Google Analytics?
The internet needs to know less about us, not more. Just because it's possible to track someone doesn't mean we should.
In this new world, it feels like an obligation to make sure we're not aiding and abetting those who seek to exploit our data.
Those who hoard every little clue in order to piece of together a puzzle that'll ultimately reveal all our weakest points and moments, then sell that picture to the highest bidder.
Now when you visit our marketing page, you only have to trust that we won't abuse that data – not a laundry list of third parties you have no reasonable chance of vetting.
Basic stuff doesn't require overly sophisticated tooling, so it's fine that our homegrown package isn't nearly as fancy or as piercing as offerings like Google Analytics. It doesn't need to be.
It's no longer enough for something to be slightly more convenient or slightly cheaper for us to send data out of the house.
Untangling yourself from the old paradigm of data is neither quick, easy, nor free. But it's worth doing, even if you can only do it one step at the time.
posts.key_insight: Companies have a moral obligation to reduce their dependence on third-party tracking, and the convenience of tools like Google Analytics no longer justifies the privacy cost to users.

posts.summary: DHH testifies before the House Antitrust Subcommittee about how Google, Apple, and Facebook have transformed the once-open internet into a marketplace controlled by monopolies. He argues Google extorts businesses by selling their trademarked names to competitors, Apple exploits its App Store duopoly with a 30% fee and draconian review processes, and Facebook's data vacuuming creates an inescapable advertising machine. He calls on Congress to intervene, arguing small companies have no agency to resist these forces and that the promise of a middleman-free internet has been broken.
key_points: Google leverages its search monopoly to force businesses to pay for their own trademarked brand names, while protecting its own trademarks automatically
Apple and Google's App Store duopoly sustains a 30% fee versus 2% in competitive credit-card markets, with draconian enforcement against alternatives
Facebook and Google captured 99% of internet ad growth by 2016, creating an inescapable surveillance advertising machine
Big tech's relentless expansion into every industry means all businesses will eventually compete against them
The acquisitions of Instagram and WhatsApp should never have been approved and need to be undone
money_quotes: The internet has been colonized by a handful of big tech companies that wield their monopoly power without restraint.
The promise that the internet was going to cut out the middleman has been broken.
We're all left to accept that these companies can and do alter the deal, any deal, however they please.
Google uses this monopoly to extort businesses like ours to pay for the privilege that consumers who search for our trademarked brand name can find us.
Google would never have been able to capture a monopoly in search by acting like this from the start.
Then you'll be stuck in an appeals process that would make Kafka blush.
Not even Putin would dare brag of an approval rating that high!
Help us, congress. You're our only hope.
posts.key_insight: The open internet that enabled small companies to compete has been captured by a handful of monopolists who now serve as inescapable, exploitative middlemen — exactly what the internet was supposed to eliminate.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the microservices trend of the past five years has been a misguided attempt to solve problems rooted in poor coding discipline, not architectural constraints. He contends that developers embraced microservices as an absolution for messy codebases rather than addressing the real issue: weak habits and lack of competence. The post advocates for integrated systems built by integrated (full-stack) programmers who can hold an entire application in their head. DHH argues the real work is shrinking an application's conceptual surface area through compression, good patterns, and productive tooling. He dismisses the frontend/backend specialization divide and calls for developers who can work across the whole stack simultaneously.
key_points: Microservices became popular not because monoliths were genuinely too complex, but because developers wanted to externalize blame for messy codebases
Breaking dirty code into microservices doesn't clean it up — it just distributes the mess while adding coordination complexity
The real solution is conceptual compression: shrinking an application's surface area until a competent programmer can reason about the whole thing
Specialization into frontend/backend roles creates arbitrary boundaries that harm productivity — integrated developers who build whole features are more effective
The web hasn't fundamentally changed; what changed was developers adopting increasingly astronautic abstractions that made simple things seem impossible
money_quotes: We're gonna break it up and somehow find the engineering discipline we never had in the first place.

You don't want to hear that the reason your monolith is a spaghetti monster is because you let it become that way, one commit at the time, due to weak habits, pressurized deadlines, or simply sheer lack of competence.
Microservices as an architectural gold rush appealed to developers for the same reason TDD appeals to developers: it's the pseudoscientific promise of a diet.
Integrated systems are good. Integrated developers are good. Being able to wrap your mind around the whole application, and have developers who are able to make whole features, is good!
The road to madness and despair lays in specialization and compartmentalization.
Forget frontend or backend. The answer is all of it. At the same time. In the same mind.
The web is actually much the same as it always was. What changed was us. And mostly not in ways for the better.
posts.key_insight: Microservices are a symptom of developers avoiding the hard work of writing clean, well-structured monoliths — the real solution is conceptual compression and full-stack integrated programmers who can reason about entire applications.

posts.summary: DHH argues that email tracking pixels are a form of spying that violates basic privacy expectations and should be eliminated by default in mailing list software. He shares that Basecamp stopped using tracking pixels months earlier with no negative consequences. He contends that regardless of their marketing utility, spy pixels are invasive, non-consensual, and violate what normal people expect from email. He proposes two simple reforms: mailing list software should disable tracking pixels by default, and senders who want open tracking should obtain explicit opt-in consent from recipients.
key_points: Most normal people are shocked to learn that simply opening an email can trigger tracking, violating basic expectations about email privacy
Basecamp stopped using tracking pixels and found it was actually a relief, proving they aren't necessary
The dictionary definition of 'spy' fits email tracking pixels perfectly: secretly collecting and reporting information without knowledge or consent
The reform is simple: disable tracking by default and require explicit opt-in consent when senders want open tracking
Link tracking with URL parameters is an acceptable alternative since clicking links is an explicit action users understand involves some tracking
money_quotes: The internet is finally coming out of its long haze on privacy, but it's with one hell of a hangover.
Not being able to track open rates, and fret over whether that meant our subject lines weren't providing just the right HOOK, has actually been a relief.
They're invasive, they're extracted without consent, and they break the basic assumptions most people have about email.
Just about every normal person (i.e. someone not working in internet marketing) has been surprised, pissed, or at least dismayed when I tell them about spy pixels in emails.
A lot of them have taken offense to the term 'spy pixels'. Affixing the spying label made a lot of them uncomfortable, because they were just trying to help!
Here's the dictionary definition of a spy: 'a person who secretly collects and reports information on the activities, movements, and plans'. That fits pretty well to a spy pixel.
Don't do it by default, ask for informed consent if you must. Being respectful of someone's privacy isn't rocket science.
And if we keep making things a little better, we'll eventually wake up to a world that's a lot better.
posts.key_insight: Email tracking pixels meet the literal definition of spying and should require explicit opt-in consent rather than being silently enabled by default in mailing list software.

posts.summary: DHH shares his 2019 reading list of roughly 20 books spanning philosophy, history, economics, climate science, and management. The list reveals a deep engagement with Erich Fromm's humanistic psychology, critical examinations of neoliberal economics, and historical accounts of societal fragility. He is particularly drawn to works that challenge mainstream narratives about progress, capitalism, and human nature. Several management and business books disappoint him as ideas that should have been blog posts rather than full-length works.
key_points: Erich Fromm emerges as DHH's most influential thinker, with five books read, praising his ability to diagnose modern materialism and critique both capitalism and communism
A strong thread of skepticism toward neoliberal economics runs through the list, with books like The Divide and Winners Take All challenging narratives about global development and elite philanthropy
DHH is impatient with modern business books that stretch a single insight to book length, dismissing both Radical Candor and The Tyranny of Metrics as ideas that should have been blog posts
Historical accounts of societal collapse (the plague, the Troubles, climate projections) reinforce a worldview that human civilization is fragile and progress is not inevitable
Snowden's memoir earns strong praise, with DHH calling for a presidential pardon and describing mass surveillance capabilities as 'almost required reading'
money_quotes: Things got the way they are through an endless series of historical accidents, unlikely events, and forks in the road. Not through some deterministic path.
An enjoyable reminder that there is little new under the sun, and that much of what we must do is to continue repackaging eternal truths for a new context.
Just the fact that HALF of all the greenhouse gasses that are warming the earth were released since Seinfeld aired on TV. That's my life time!
May a future president see the courage, wisdom, and self-sacrifice Snowden committed to and give him the pardon and the parade that he so rightly deserves.
This should have been a blog post.
Is the worker doing mindless assembly work at a factory really any better off whether the plant is owned by a capitalist or the state?
Radical Candor isn't just brutal honesty. It's emphatic honesty. Telling people where they stand from a position of care, not disinterest.
Disneyland is presented as imaginary in order to make us believe that the rest is real.
posts.key_insight: DHH's reading reveals a consistent worldview: mainstream narratives about progress, capitalism, and management are largely self-serving myths, and genuine understanding requires engaging with thinkers who challenge comfortable assumptions.

posts.summary: DHH explains why 37signals delayed the launch of HEY, their new email service, due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Despite being a fully remote company for twenty years, he argues that the crisis demanded more than just business continuity — it required acknowledging that employees with kids at home, heightened stress, and a world in turmoil couldn't give 100% to a product launch. He frames the decision as responsible leadership: deadlines are made up and can be changed when circumstances demand it. The team will continue improving HEY and Basecamp while waiting for the world to stabilize.
key_points: Being remote-ready doesn't mean business-as-usual during a crisis — the issue isn't capability but capacity and responsibility
Employees with kids doing remote learning and everyone dealing with pandemic stress can't give 100% to a high-stakes launch
Almost all deadlines are artificial and should be flexible when real-world conditions change dramatically
The work continues but bends to accommodate reality — they'll keep improving HEY and Basecamp without forcing a launch date
Companies have an obligation to help employees through crises, not push them harder
money_quotes: A lack of excitement, though, you could push through. The prospect of a stressful launch alongside the reality of a stressful life? No.
What's going on right now is about more than just whether work can happen, but to which degree it should.
You can't put in 100% at work when life asks for 150%.
Launching a new product in the midst of that just wasn't the responsible thing to do, so we won't.
Remember, almost all deadlines are made up. You can change your mind when the world changes around you.
The work doesn't stop, it just bends.
posts.key_insight: Responsible leadership means recognizing that almost all deadlines are made up and being willing to bend them when your people's wellbeing demands it.

posts.summary: DHH introduces 'The Citadel' as an architectural pattern that extends his earlier 'Majestic Monolith' concept. The Citadel keeps the monolith at the center but allows small 'Outposts' to handle specific responsibilities that diverge in performance or organizational needs. He uses Basecamp's Campfire chat polling service as a concrete example—a tiny service rewritten in multiple languages over the years while the rest of the app stayed in Rails. He argues this is the natural evolution for the rare monolith that genuinely needs to scale beyond its original constraints, rather than jumping to full microservices.
key_points: Most web apps should be monoliths for their entire lifespan—the scaling limits are much higher than architects imagine
The Citadel pattern keeps the monolith central while extracting only specific, divergent responsibilities into small Outposts
Basecamp's Campfire polling service exemplified a perfect Outpost: 99% of traffic but only ~20 lines of Ruby logic
The first instinct when hitting limits should always be to improve the monolith, not decompose it
The microservices trend is a blind alley, and the pendulum is swinging back toward monolithic architectures
money_quotes: The vast majority of web applications should start life as a Majestic Monolith: A single codebase that does everything the application needs to do.
The limits upon which this pattern is constrained are high. Much higher than most people like to imagine when they fantasize about being capital-a Architects.
Your first instinct should be to improve the Majestic Monolith until it can cope, but, having done that and failed, you may look to the next step.
We didn't try to carve the entire app up into little services, each written in a different language. No, we just extracted a single Outpost.
As more and more people come to realize that the chase for microservices ended in a blind alley, the pendulum is going to swing back.
The Majestic Monolith is here waiting for microservice refugees.
posts.key_insight: When a monolith genuinely hits its limits, the answer isn't microservices but a Citadel—a monolith supported by a few small, purpose-built outposts that handle only the responsibilities that truly diverge.

posts.summary: DHH explains Basecamp's approach to hiring programmers using take-home coding assignments instead of whiteboard interviews or brain teasers. Candidates are whittled down to a small percentage (1-3%) through cover letters before receiving a 3-5 hour practical assignment based on real problems the team faces. He shares two concrete examples: a rack-ratelimit feature and a datalist autocomplete enhancement, along with the successful submissions from Jorge Manrubia (hired from Spain) and Nabeelah Ali (hired from Norway). DHH emphasizes that the assignment is necessary but insufficient — great code alone won't get you hired, but you can't get hired without demonstrating coding ability.
key_points: Basecamp rejects whiteboard interviews and brain teasers in favor of small take-home assignments modeled on real work problems, estimated at 3-5 hours
Only 1-3% of applicants reach the take-home stage — aggressive filtering happens first through cover letters, making the unpaid work commitment fair
Completing the programming assignment is necessary but insufficient; it merely unlocks further interviews with the team
The alternative to take-home tests isn't 'nothing' — it's often worse: travel, whiteboard grilling, or abstract puzzles that don't reflect actual work
Hiring without looking at actual code risks defaulting to bias, credentialism, and 'culture fit' as proxies for competence
money_quotes: There's no perfect process for hiring great programmers, but there are plenty of terrible ways to screw it up.

If you only consider candidates from the open source community, you're going to miss out on plenty of great programmers who just don't have the time or the inclination to contribute code to open source.
Asking someone for forty hours of work product, without pay, which might well go nowhere, is not what we do or advocate at Basecamp.
You can't get hired without demonstrating a core competency in writing great code, but you also won't get hired just because you can write great code alone.
It wasn't some Tower of Hanoi abstract, computer-sciency test that you can look up a million solutions to, and which favor recent grads of CS algorithm courses.
I would do that any day over a doing a live coding challenge.
Hiring without looking at actual code they wrote often risks leading down a path of bias (or, as we call it today, 'fit'), credentialism, whiteboard puzzles, and brainteasers.
posts.key_insight: A short, practical take-home coding assignment based on real work problems is the fairest way to evaluate programmers — it levels the playing field while avoiding the biases of whiteboard interviews, brain teasers, and credential-based hiring.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Basecamp has banned employee surveillance software vendors from integrating with their platform or using their logo. Prompted by COVID-19 driving mass remote work adoption, he argues that surveillance tools represent a 'cancer of mistrust and control' that must be resisted before they become normalized. Basecamp updated its API and logo policies to explicitly prohibit integrations that monitor user activity beyond time tracking. DHH advocates for evaluating work outputs rather than surveilling inputs, arguing that trust produces better results than invasive monitoring. He contends that capable managers can assess work quality directly without resorting to screenshots and activity tracking.
key_points: Basecamp changed its integration policies to explicitly ban employee surveillance vendors from using its API or logo
COVID-19's forced remote work shift turbocharged demand for surveillance tools, making resistance urgent before normalization sets in
Employers should monitor outputs (work product) rather than inputs (keystrokes, screenshots), which creates healthier relationships
Surveillance regimes are counterproductive — they motivate employees to game the system rather than do good work
When work quality slips, managers should investigate root causes rather than default to surveillance as a solution
money_quotes: We've decided it's our obligation to resist the normalization of employee surveillance software. It is not right, it is not human, and unless we speak up now, we might well contribute to this cancer of mistrust and control spreading even after the COVID-19 crisis is behind us.

Employers are always free to – and should! – evaluate the work product produced by employees. But they don't have to surveil someone's every move or screenshot their computer every five minutes to do so.
That's monitoring the inputs. Monitor the outputs instead, and you'll have a much healthier, saner relationship.
If you hire smart, capable people and trust them to do good work – surprise-surprise – people will return the sentiment deliver just that!
The irony of setting up these invasive surveillance regimes is that they end up causing the motivation to goof off to beat the very systems that were setup to catch such behavior.
There are a million reasons more likely than 'employee wanted to defraud their employer' when the work isn't where it needs to be.
No surveillance is necessary to make that determination.
Just say no to employee surveillance.
posts.key_insight: Employee surveillance software is a counterproductive cancer of mistrust that should be actively resisted — good management means evaluating work outputs, not monitoring every input.

posts.summary: DHH recounts how Apple threatened to remove HEY, Basecamp's new email service, from the App Store on its launch day unless they paid Apple's 30% revenue cut through in-app purchases. Despite HEY following the same model as Gmail, Netflix, and other apps that sell subscriptions outside the App Store, Apple singled them out. After a viral public campaign, Apple partially relented but forced HEY to create an unnecessary free tier. DHH argues Apple wields monopoly power over the digital economy since 90% of HEY's paying customers use Apple devices, making App Store access existential. He calls on Congress to force Apple to allow alternative payment processing and restore competition.
key_points: Apple threatened to remove HEY from the App Store on launch day despite approving it days earlier, demanding 30% of subscription revenue through in-app purchases
Apple enforces its rules selectively — Gmail, Netflix, and banks follow the same subscription model HEY originally used but face no consequences
90% of HEY's paying customers use Apple devices, making App Store access an existential business requirement rather than an optional distribution channel
The disparity is stark: Facebook pays Apple $99/year while HEY would owe $300,000/year on $1M in revenue, punishing paid services while rewarding surveillance-based free ones
DHH's proposed fix is forcing Apple to allow alternative payment processors and the freedom to tell customers about them
money_quotes: Apple controls who can and cannot compete in virtually all sectors of the digital economy through the monopoly power they wield capriciously with the App Store.
Apple isn't just a dominant player in this market, they are the market. Without access to the iPhone, we're dead.

We were sitting on a rocket ship of a successful new service, eager to hire more people, but were at the same time essentially paralyzed from making any investments, because what if Apple wouldn't give us the permission to exist?

A company like Facebook, that makes tens of billions of dollars every year from their iOS app, simply has to pay Apple the $99/year developer license. But our HEY service would have to pay Apple $300,000/year, if we made but a million dollars?
What other small software company is going to invest years of effort and millions of dollars in a new service, if Apple might kill them dead on their birthday.
This is how you stifle entrepreneurship, depress innovation, and prevent the next generation of makers from ever having a chance to compete.
Imagine if the telcos demanded a cut of company revenues, since they provide the phone line that connects customers, in the heyday of their monopoly?
posts.key_insight: Apple's monopoly control over app distribution lets it selectively enforce rules to extract rent from small competitors while exempting big tech incumbents, effectively functioning as a gatekeeper that stifles entrepreneurship and innovation.

posts.summary: DHH reports that spy-pixel tracking services are escalating their tactics to defeat HEY's privacy protections, much like malware evolves to defeat antivirus software. GMass, a Gmail plugin, published a blog post bragging about bypassing recipient protections, which prompted HEY's team to spend a day developing new detection methods. DHH frames email tracking as a security arms race and calls on Google to stop enabling this kind of surveillance on Gmail. He commits to ongoing work identifying and calling out bad actors in the email tracking space.
key_points: Spy-pixel services are actively evolving their techniques to defeat privacy protections, mirroring the malware arms race
GMass publicly bragged about defeating tracking protections, which backfired by putting them on HEY's radar for name-and-shame treatment
HEY treats email tracking defense as an ongoing security problem requiring continuous adaptation
Google is implicitly criticized for tolerating privacy-abusing plugins on Gmail while blocking spam and phishing
money_quotes: We knew that spy-pixel pushers might go down the rabbit hole of escalation once we gave HEY users the power to defend themselves.
Thanks to a new blog post where they brag about defeating protections that recipients might take to defend themselves, they came onto our radar.
Internet security is a constantly moving target.
We can hope that Google will soon stop being a conduit for this kind of privacy abuse on the Gmail platform. Just like they don't tolerate being used for spamming or phishing.

We'll continue to do the work both on a general level to protect against all forms of privacy attacks against HEY users, but also specifically to identity bad actors, and to call out the users who employ their software for spying.
posts.key_insight: Email spy-pixel tracking is evolving like malware, requiring the same arms-race mentality and continuous adaptation that security software demands.

posts.summary: DHH argues that web developers don't need JavaScript-heavy single-page application frameworks to build modern, responsive web apps. Instead, generating HTML on the server and sending it directly to the browser—what he calls 'HTML Over The Wire'—offers a radically simpler approach. He contends that the JavaScript-everything trend of the past decade has created unnecessary complexity that particularly burdens small teams. The post introduces Hotwire as a concrete implementation of this philosophy, requiring only small JavaScript libraries rather than full client-side MVC frameworks.
key_points: Server-generated HTML delivered directly to the browser can replace JSON APIs and client-side MVC frameworks for most web applications
The JavaScript-everything approach creates incidental complexity that disproportionately hurts small teams, even if large companies can absorb it
HTML Over The Wire restores language diversity on the server side, freeing developers from writing everything in JavaScript
A small amount of JavaScript is still needed for polish and interactivity, but it doesn't need to be the center of the architecture
This approach lowers the barrier to entry for newcomers overwhelmed by modern frontend toolchain complexity
money_quotes: You can write fast, modern, responsive web applications by generating your HTML on the server, and delivering that (with a little help) directly to the browser.
But just because you can, doesn't mean you should.
The price for pursuing JavaScript for everything has been a monstrosity of modern complexity.
But incidental complexity can absolutely break your back if you're a small team where everyone has to do a lot.
The tools and techniques forged in the belly of a huge company is often the exact opposite of what you need to make progress at your scale.
When we embrace HTML as the format to send across the wire, we liberate ourselves from having to write all the code that creates that HTML in JavaScript.
A throwback to a time where you could view source and make sense of it.
posts.key_insight: Most web applications don't need JavaScript-heavy architectures; sending server-rendered HTML directly to the browser eliminates incidental complexity and returns the web to its simpler, more diverse roots.

posts.summary: DHH testifies before the North Dakota Senate committee in support of SB 2333, a bill targeting app store duopoly abuses by Apple and Google. He recounts Basecamp's own near-death experience launching HEY email, when Apple demanded 30% of revenues through in-app payments or threatened removal. He argues that federal action is too slow and praises North Dakota for drafting concise, effective legislation.
