His core argument is that developers need the choice to use alternative payment systems, not discounted rates from monopolists, and that state-level action can chart a new path for the entire country.
key_points: Apple approved HEY for the App Store then reversed course days after public launch, demanding 30% of revenue through in-app payments or threatening removal
Federal legislative relief is not materializing fast enough — no bills are currently under consideration — making state-level action like North Dakota's SB 2333 essential
The core demand is choice in payment systems, not lower rates, because only competition can drive fees down to market levels like the ~2% credit card processing norm
Apple and Google claim credit for the innovation built on their platforms, but they are infrastructure operators, not the creators of the value they tax
The 30% fee is applied selectively — small companies pay while giants like Facebook pay nothing — making the system both extortive and inequitable
money_quotes: If your house is on fire, and you call for help, it's no good if the red engine arrives after daffodils have started growing where your house once was.
We're not interested in a slightly lower rate on their obscene payment processing fees. We're interested in choice.
It's like if a shipping company wanted credit for all the products inside the containers it carried by rail or sea.

It's simply obscene that a small software company that makes $1,000,000 dollars in revenue has to send a $300,000 check to Cupertino or Mountain View, rather than invest in growing their business, while Facebook makes billions off those same app stores without paying any cut of their revenues whatsoever.
The free market depends on legislative guardrails that prevent monopolies from exploiting their outsized power and preying on smaller competitors.
Help us, Congress. You're our only hope.
posts.key_insight: Small software companies need legislative intervention to gain payment system choice on app stores, because the duopoly's selective 30% tax and threat of retaliation cannot be corrected by market forces alone.

posts.summary: DHH testifies before the Arizona House of Representatives in support of HB2005, a bill granting app developers the right to choose their own payment processors. He argues Apple's 30% commission is an unfair tax applied inconsistently, with billion-dollar companies like Uber paying only $99/year while mid-sized companies pay millions. He frames Apple's App Store as a monopoly tollbooth that harms both developers and consumers, comparing it to Standard Oil's anticompetitive practices. The bill passed committee 7-6.
key_points: Apple's 30% cut is over five times Arizona's sales tax and fifteen times typical payment processing fees, yet is applied inconsistently across developers
Billion-dollar companies like Uber and Facebook pay only $99/year to Apple while mid-sized companies making $10M/year must pay $3M, creating a deeply unfair two-tier system
Apple's expansion into credit cards, TV, fitness, news, gaming, music, and potentially cars represents classic tying and bundling behavior that would make Standard Oil envious
Passing the bill would make Arizona the most desirable place to start a software company by offering lower costs and protection from platform retaliation
money_quotes: Apple's tax of 30% on developers is more than five times the rate of the Arizona state sales tax, and more than fifteen times the rate of other payment processing services.
Apple has an iron grip on the market, and if they say 'you now have to pay', all developers can reply is 'how much'.

How is it fair that billion-dollar companies that use thousands of times more of Apple's App Store infrastructure get away with paying $99/year, while a mid-sized software company making $10m/year has to pay $3m?
Apple operates a tollbooth on the only road to distribution of mobile software for the dominant iPhone platform.
When Apple tried to shake down our company for the 30% cut of revenues, they explicitly encouraged us simply to pass on the cost to consumers.
Standard Oil would have been green with envy at how Apple has used their monopoly in one area to further their business interests in every other.
Giving us at least a fighting chance by enforcing a choice in payment processing is the right thing to do.
posts.key_insight: Apple's App Store commission is an inconsistently applied monopoly tax that punishes mid-sized developers while exempting the largest companies, and legislative action is needed to restore fair competition.

posts.summary: DHH argues that software makers are the only champions of 'less software' because customers always ask for more features. He uses HEY World as a case study — a blogging tool deliberately stripped of tracking, analytics, cookies, JavaScript, comments, likes, and monetization. The post contrasts HEY World's simplicity with the gamified pressure of modern publishing platforms that turn writers into 'CONTENT PRODUCERS.' DHH positions this minimalism as liberating, making writing less intimidating and more joyful. The core philosophy: show customers what less looks like rather than asking them what to remove.
key_points: Customers always want more features, but the sum of everyone's wants produces too much software — only the maker can champion less
HEY World deliberately omits tracking pixels, analytics, cookies, JavaScript, comments, likes, social sharing, and monetization
Modern publishing platforms gamify writing with follower counts and engagement metrics, creating pressure and intimidation
You can't ask customers how much less they'd use — you can only show them
Writing for the joy of it, even with few readers, is a valid and sufficient goal
money_quotes: Less software only has one champion: you, the maker.
You can't ask customers for just how much less they'd actually use, you can only show them.
Everyone usually wants a different thing. So the total sum of everyone's wants is a lot of software! Very often, too much software.
In return, you get a simple typewriter with which to reach the world.
One that is far less intimidating than most social publishing platforms that turn you into a gamified CONTENT PRODUCER from day one.
Where you're supposed to constantly be hustling to get people to LIKE, SUBSCRIBE, AND HIT THAT BELL ICON.
Writing for the sheer joy of it. Even if nobody else, or just a few, might read it. That's enough.
posts.key_insight: The maker is the only advocate for simplicity because every customer wants something different, and the sum of all those wants is always too much software.

posts.summary: DHH draws a parallel between the organic food movement and the growing privacy movement. He argues that just as organic food was once dismissed as a niche concern for hippies in the 1990s before becoming mainstream, privacy awareness is currently at a similar inflection point. He notes that public sentiment toward Facebook and Google has already shifted from gratitude to reluctant tolerance. He predicts that as consumer awareness grows, viable privacy-respecting alternatives will emerge and improve, just as organic food became widely accessible.
key_points: Privacy concerns today occupy the same cultural position that organic food did in the 1990s — niche but on the verge of mainstream adoption
Public sentiment toward big tech has already shifted from enthusiastic gratitude to reluctant defense, mirroring early stages of the organic food awakening
Rising consumer awareness creates market demand that improves access and selection of alternatives over time
Mainstream adoption won't solve all problems overnight but creates a meaningful counter-force against exploitative business models
money_quotes: Organic food, was, at the time and in my head, something for hippies. Kind, caring people, who perhaps were a little strange. In a good way, but not in my way.
I think caring about privacy today is where caring about organic food was in the 90s.
Wow! They're giving these amazing services away for FREE??? THANK YOU BENEVOLENT TECH MASTERS!

That's just not a sentiment you'll find many people expressing today. Usually, you'll hear a reluctant defense for why it's actually a pain to switch, or that the alternatives are just as bad, but very few are coming out with an enthusiastic defense of these business models now.
Facebook and Google are like Tyson Foods: Mass producers of the digital pink slime.
Billions of people turned into a gob of eyeballs sold at monopoly rates to advertisers around the world.
How long do you want to continue being an ingredient in that?
posts.key_insight: Privacy awareness is at the same tipping point organic food was in the 1990s — still niche but rapidly approaching mainstream adoption that will reshape the digital economy.

posts.summary: DHH argues that charging customers directly is a viable and superior business model to the 'free' internet services that monetize through surveillance and data selling. He recounts Basecamp's two-decade history of charging money directly, including a painful 2005 experience with consumer product Backpack that soured him on B2C. When co-founder Jason Fried proposed launching HEY email as a consumer product, DHH was skeptical but trusted him. HEY proved wildly successful, signing tens of thousands of paying customers in its first six months, demonstrating that consumers are now willing to pay for privacy-respecting services.
key_points: The internet industry has a terrible track record of offering 'free' services that ultimately exploit users through data selling, acquisitions, or shutdowns
Basecamp's 2005 consumer product Backpack was profitable but painful — individual consumers were more demanding and support-intensive than business customers
Consumer willingness to pay has fundamentally shifted since 2005, driven by growing awareness of surveillance practices like Gmail receipt tracking and spy pixels
HEY became Basecamp's fastest-growing product ever by charging ~$8.25/month directly to consumers
Business truths have a half-life — what didn't work in 2005 can work now, and leaders must resist letting past experiences calcify into permanent assumptions
money_quotes: Monetize by charging money. That's it. That's the business plan.
The honeymoon consumers afforded "the free internet" is over.
Tons of them have realized that they'd rather pay for a product with cash money than with their privacy, their data, or their attention.
I always try to remind myself about the dangers of having been in business for two decades. You can easily convince yourself that what didn't work in 2005 won't work now or vice versa.
One time a guy literally called me up at 2am in the morning because we had dared raise the price from $5/month to $7/month.
Either way, I'm thrilled to have been wrong.
People are rightfully skeptical about new apps and services on the internet. The industry has a terrible record of conning folks into using something new "for free".
posts.key_insight: The era of consumers tolerating 'free' surveillance-funded services is ending, and straightforward business models built on charging money are newly viable in the consumer market.

posts.summary: DHH examines why major email providers haven't addressed spy pixels (tracking pixels in emails) despite their prevalence. He argues Apple hasn't acted due to its monopoly position removing competitive pressure, not philosophical opposition. Google won't act because it profits from the same kind of surveillance. He predicts spy pixels will ultimately die through GDPR enforcement rather than voluntary industry action, with email service providers like Mailchimp potentially facing liability for enabling violations.
key_points: Apple's monopoly in iOS Mail removed competitive pressure to innovate on privacy, despite Apple's philosophical alignment with anti-tracking
Google has no incentive to block spy pixels because it already mines Gmail data extensively for advertising purposes
GDPR already outlaws spy pixels without informed consent, but lack of enforcement has let the practice continue unchecked
Bulk email providers like Mailchimp could face legal liability for knowingly enabling GDPR violations by their customers
Real change will come from regulation and enforcement, not from dominant email providers voluntarily acting against their interests
money_quotes: Most people still don't know they're being spied upon when opening emails, and they're shocked when they learn that they are!
The reason Apple hasn't done anything is because iOS Mail has simply not been under any real competitive pressure for a very long time.
In short, Apple's monopoly position has halted innovation.
Asking Google to do something about spy pixels would be like asking the mob to help stop larceny.
Verizon gives you "free" email such that they can sell you as a product to advertisers. You'll wait until the end of the earth for Verizon to stand up to spy pixels.
The real change will come from regulation, enforcement, and culture.
Corporate compliance departments will tell corporate marketing departments "cut that shit out", and that'll be that for big companies.
I'm convinced that spy pixels are already dead trackers walking. It's just going to take a couple of years for reality to catch up with this historical inevitability.
posts.key_insight: The dominant email providers won't voluntarily stop spy pixels because monopoly removes competitive pressure and surveillance is their business model — only regulation will force change.

posts.summary: DHH argues that antitrust enforcement against big tech has reached a tipping point after years of inaction. He points to simultaneous developments in the US House Antitrust Subcommittee, the Arizona state legislature (HB2005), and Dutch competition authorities as evidence that change is accelerating. The Arizona bill is particularly exciting to him because it would prohibit dominant app stores from forcing developers to use their payment processing and pay the 30% commission. He reveals that Basecamp filed complaints with multiple European competition authorities and is even considering relocating to Arizona to benefit from the proposed law. Despite some puzzling Democratic opposition at the state level, he's optimistic that the momentum is unstoppable.
key_points: Multiple antitrust actions against big tech are happening simultaneously across US federal, US state, and European jurisdictions
Arizona's HB2005 would prohibit dominant app stores from coercing developers into using their payment processing or retaliating against them
Basecamp is seriously considering relocating from Illinois to Arizona to benefit from the proposed antitrust protections
State-level legislation can move far faster than federal action, potentially changing the landscape overnight
Basecamp has filed competition complaints with multiple European authorities beyond just the Dutch ACM
money_quotes: For basically my entire career, big tech has gotten away with whatever they wanted, whenever they wanted.
The monopoly question is no longer one of "are they/are they not", but about what should we do with the fact that they definitely are.

It would prohibit dominant app stores on general-purpose computing platforms from coercing app developers into paying the exorbitant 30% tax on payment processing, and prevent the platforms from retaliating against developers.
I try not to get too excited, but it's hard, because this is very, very exciting indeed!
Awareness of big tech monopolies and the harm they cause is still not evenly distributed.
The wheels of justice may turn slowly, but once they get going, watch out!
posts.key_insight: After two decades of unchecked big tech dominance, simultaneous antitrust actions at federal, state, and international levels signal that meaningful regulation is finally arriving — and state legislatures can move faster than anyone expected.

posts.summary: DHH condemns the rise of remote-work surveillance software, using RemoteDesk as a particularly egregious example that openly markets 'obedience monitoring' with AI-powered features like 'Facial Suspicion' detection. He argues that such tools treat employees as prisoners, not people, and that the common retort of 'just quit' ignores economic reality. He calls for legal regulation to ban or severely limit these tools, pointing to European labor protections as a model.
key_points: Remote work surveillance software has grown bolder, dropping euphemisms and openly marketing 'obedience monitoring' with dystopian features like facial suspicion detection
The biggest problem with remote work is usually employees working too much, not too little, making surveillance counterproductive
The 'just quit' argument is victim-blaming that ignores economic coercion — workers need legal protections because markets don't self-correct on humane timescales
AI-powered surveillance is worse than Orwell's 1984 because it records everything continuously without human monitoring limitations
Legislative action like Europe's right-to-disconnect laws is needed, but the surveillance threat requires even more urgent regulation
money_quotes: The biggest problem with working remotely is usually not that employees work too little, but that they work too much.
Obedience monitoring?? Are we talking about employees or prisoners here?
All of it is based on the same bankrupt ideology. That workers have sold their time for wages, and therefore their privacy and dignity has been bought in full.
Employee-surveillance software is employee-abuse software. Plain and simple.
The reason we have laws and regulations protecting workers from abusive hours, terms, or environments is exactly because the holy market does not correct itself.
Far too many managers are addicted to the power, if not sadism, that employee-surveillance software tickles.
Employees are not prisoners. War upon them is not peace. Obedience is not productivity. Surveillance is not justified.
posts.key_insight: Remote-work surveillance software is fundamentally dehumanizing and markets won't correct the abuse — only legal regulation can protect workers from this digital panopticon.

posts.summary: DHH describes how Apple locked a user out of their entire digital life—apps, music, calendar, iCloud—for missing a single Apple Card payment to Goldman Sachs. He argues this represents the dangerous intersection of big tech conglomerate power and financial services, where Apple weaponizes its monopoly position in computing to act as a debt collector for a bank. The Apple Card is really a Goldman Sachs credit product with Apple branding, yet Apple uses platform control as collection leverage. DHH calls for Glass-Steagall-style regulation for big tech, arguing that companies operating essential digital platforms should be barred from using that position to enforce financial product compliance.
key_points: Apple disabled a user's access to apps, music, calendar, and iCloud services because of a late Apple Card payment—essentially bricking their computer as a debt collection tactic
The Apple Card is fundamentally a Goldman Sachs credit product with Apple branding, yet Apple acts as enforcer using its platform monopoly power
Big tech conglomerate power is distinct from monopoly power—having fingers in computing, finance, entertainment, and services creates dangerous cross-leverage between unrelated businesses
The same repo tactics could extend to future Apple products like cars, and already mirror predatory practices in subprime auto lending
DHH calls for Glass-Steagall-style legislation that would prevent essential digital platform operators from also acting as financial debt collectors
money_quotes: Apple basically bricked his computer as a debt-collection tactic.
To hold your digital life hostage until you pay off a credit card bill is grotesquely disproportionate.
Apple is acting as a debt collector on behalf of another company, and telling you that until you sort out your payments with them, you're going to be shut out of your digital life.
Apple didn't design a credit card, they simply used their branding, and their monopoly position with the iPhone, to push a financial product by Goldman Sachs.
It's hard to imagine repo tactics more effective than holding your digital life ransom until you pay.

If you're operating an essential digital services company, like Apple is with the iPhone, you can't also be the muscle for a loanshark business that charges 22% APR, and kick people off your dominant platform if they miss a payment.
So you miss a payment to Goldman Sachs, and now your car is going to be bricked by the side of the road?
posts.key_insight: When a tech company controls both essential digital infrastructure and financial products, it can weaponize platform access as a debt collection tool—a form of conglomerate abuse that demands Glass-Steagall-style separation of powers.

posts.summary: DHH explains how Basecamp has thought through the abuse potential of HEY World, their new newsletter/blogging tool. He describes existing HEY protections like the screener and shield team that protect users from mail-bombing attacks. He argues that HEY World is fundamentally a tool (like a typewriter) rather than a platform (like a social network), which limits its potential for amplifying abuse. The post details protections in both directions: preventing HEY users from abusing others and protecting HEY World writers from receiving abuse. He also offers to comp dedicated addresses for writers with specific abuse concerns.
key_points: HEY's screener and shield team provide depth of protection against mail-bombing that no other service replicates, including a two-hour emergency response promise
HEY World is positioned as a 'typewriter, not a newspaper' — a tool rather than a platform, which categorically limits its potential for amplifying harm
Being a paid product with credit card requirements naturally deters abuse, as enforcement has only ever been needed against anonymous trial accounts
HEY World newsletters are sent from known addresses with reply capability, a deliberate design choice that trades some anonymity for accountability
For the 2% of writers with specific abuse fears, Basecamp offers to manually comp a dedicated quarantine address
money_quotes: The internet can be a pretty grim place, and if you're building software here, you better think about how it can be abused, because odds are that it will.
We've been thinking about HEY World as a typewriter as opposed to a newspaper.
What we're offering here is a tool, not a stage.
We're more like a web hosting service than a social network.
There's a categorical difference in the potential harm that HEY World could amplify or accelerate within its own space.
The internet already has a lot of places where you can post anonymously, or at least without a return address. HEY World isn't that.
It'd be lovely if the internet wasn't such a grim place at times, and you didn't even have to consider this, but it can be and you need to.
posts.key_insight: Building responsible software means thinking deeply about abuse in both directions — protecting users from the world and protecting the world from users — and the distinction between offering a tool versus operating a platform fundamentally changes the abuse calculus.

posts.summary: DHH enthusiastically reviews Jason Hickel's book 'Less Is More,' which argues that capitalism's need for never-ending growth is destroying the planet. He traces his discovery of Hickel through the Citations Needed podcast and his earlier book 'The Divide,' praising the author's accessible writing style. The review summarizes the book's arc: establishing ecological stakes with visceral examples, providing a revisionist history of capitalism from feudalism through industrialization, and arguing that GDP growth beyond a certain point doesn't improve human wellbeing. DHH calls it imperative reading and urges action before it's too late.
key_points: Capitalism's requirement for perpetual growth is causing ecological destruction observable within a single generation
Life before and outside capitalism wasn't the 'short and brutish' misery commonly depicted — life expectancy actually fell during early industrialization
Societies with far lower GDP per capita than the US deliver longer-living, happier citizens, undermining the growth-equals-progress narrative
The common framing of the global south as 'developing countries' relies on accounting shenanigans to maintain the illusion that world poverty is declining
Hickel's writing is praised as plain, accessible, and flowing compared to denser works like Piketty's Capital
money_quotes: Capitalism's need for never-ending growth is killing the planet.
If you're even just a forty-something like myself, you can actually remember when the earth was different. That's... heavy.
Life prior to capitalism wasn't the 'short and brutish' misery it's commonly depicted as, in fact much of the time it is and was the opposite, particularly at the start of the industrial revolution.
The never-ending pursuits of capitalism are not making us live healthier, happier lives once we've reached a certain point of maturity.
There are societies around the world that make due with a small slice of the GDP-per-capita that, say, the US musters, yet deliver far longer living, happier citizens.
The constant search for growth is killing the planet, and we're watching it happen in real time.
It debunked myths I'd left unquestioned since attending business school.
This is imperative reading for anyone living, working, or succeeding under capitalism as we know it today.
posts.key_insight: The relentless pursuit of economic growth has passed the point of improving human lives and is now actively destroying the planet, making degrowth thinking urgent and necessary.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates the Arizona House passing HB2005, an anti-monopoly bill that would prevent Apple and Google from forcing developers to use their payment processing systems. He argues this is the most important big tech antitrust victory in twenty years. He dismisses fears that Apple would retaliate by pulling services from Arizona, noting the political and reputational costs would be too high. He points out that Apple has to win in every state while developers only need one victory to crack the dam, and predicts Apple will eventually concede rather than damage its brand.
key_points: Arizona HB2005 passed the House, preventing Apple and Google from mandating their payment processing for app developers
Fears of Apple retaliating by pulling platforms from Arizona are overblown given the political climate and reputational risks
The asymmetry favors developers: Apple must win in all fifty states, but developers only need one state victory to set precedent
Federal antitrust momentum with bipartisan support makes aggressive retaliation even riskier for Apple
The Arizona bill has inspired a growing list of states to pursue similar legislation
money_quotes: The passing of this bill represents the most important victory for big tech antitrust in twenty years.
People assuming that these big tech companies are now so powerful that they don't have to abide by state laws any more.
Are they really going to take kindly to the idea that laws enacted by their elected officials would be met with such a punitive response? By America's most valuable company?
Breaking them up is a mainstream idea with supporters on both sides.
Apple and Google shouldn't be able to put a 30% tax on the digital economy!
Apple has to win potentially fifty times, if all fifty states eventually take a swing at this, whereas app developers just need to win one state to crack the dam.
Whereas Apple used to be able to largely dodge out of the Big Bad Tech conversation, they're now smack in the thick of it.
posts.key_insight: The asymmetric nature of state-by-state antitrust battles means Apple must win everywhere while developers only need one breakthrough, making the eventual end of the 30% app store tax increasingly likely.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Google suffers from a 'digital petro curse' analogous to the resource curse afflicting oil-rich nations. Because Google derives overwhelming profits from its advertising monopoly (~$40 billion/year from US alone), it has no structural incentive to nurture new products long-term. This explains the company's notorious graveyard of abandoned services like Stadia and Google Pay redesigns. Executives launch new initiatives to feel like innovators rather than 'middle managers in a petro state,' but inevitably abandon them because nothing can compete with the ad revenue machine. DHH concludes Google won't recapture its early innovative spirit until this fundamental dynamic changes.
key_points: Google's ~30% share of US online advertising creates a resource-curse dynamic that disincentivizes sustained investment in new products
The company's graveyard of killed services is a structural outcome, not a management failure — nothing can match ad revenue returns
Google executives launch new products to avoid seeing themselves as stewards of a data-extraction monopoly, but abandon them within 18 months
Paraphrasing Steve Jobs: when a monopoly makes product success irrelevant to company success, product people lose influence to the revenue machine operators
money_quotes: Why bother investing in other industries when you can make so much money from shit that's literally spouting out the ground?

It's almost impossible for anything else to get even close to matching the riches available from mining people's data, processing their eyeballs like pink slime, and selling it all as targeted advertisement.
What self-respecting executive really wants to internalize that they're just the middle manager in a petro state, exploiting a search monopoly for outsize data rents?
No, you want to dream! Dream that you're really changing the world, actually. Or organizing the world's information. Or whatever other fairytale you're selling yourself in the mirror that morning.
When your company is no longer influenced much by the success of your new products, the people who end up running the show are the ads and data extraction people.
Google won't get back to the mojo that gave us search, gmail, and maps before the petro curse is cured.
posts.key_insight: Google's advertising monopoly functions like a petro state's oil wealth — it structurally corrupts the company's ability to sustain innovation because no new product can compete with the effortless riches of data extraction.

posts.summary: DHH draws a parallel between the historical European enclosure movement—where public commons were privatized by lords—and Big Tech's enclosure of the open internet. He argues that the period from roughly 1995-2010 represented a brief commons era where software flowed freely from makers to customers. The rise of smartphones, mislabeled as 'phones' rather than pocket computers, gave Apple and Google the rhetorical cover to impose app store monopolies and 30% taxes on software distribution. This forced developers into unsavory business models like ads and tracking. He frames the fight against app store monopolies as part of a broader struggle to preserve open internet commons, urging people to choose open platforms over enclosed ones.
key_points: The open internet era of 1995-2010 was a brief commons period, analogous to the post-plague era of European commoner prosperity before enclosure
Calling smartphones 'phones' instead of pocket computers gave Apple rhetorical cover to compare app distribution to the old feature phone model rather than the open Mac software market of 2005
Apple's 30% app store tax parallels how lords enclosed formerly free commons, squeezing developers into running faster, capping growth, or pivoting to ad-driven models
Facebook, Apple, and Google fight not over whether to enclose the internet but over who gets to do it and who gets the spoils
The antidote is choosing open platforms—newsletters over Twitter, podcasts over Facebook, direct distribution over app stores
money_quotes: This glorious phase of freedom unfortunately did not even last the 150 years that Hickel examined between the plague and the capitalist acceleration of the European enclosure movement, but rather a brief 15 or so.

That's the time Big Tech companies like Apple would like you to forget about. Those glorious fifteen years where most software flowed freely and unencumbered across an unenclosed internet, directly from makers to customers.

This misnomer, that the computer I carry in my pocket should be summarized by the feature most of us use the least, has allowed Apple the rhetorical opening to compare today's mobile software distribution to that of feature phones.

Apple and Google are simply acting like the lords that in the early capitalist era decided that what used to be free or at least cheap — distributing software over the internet — now suddenly needed to be taxed at 30%.
This naturally caused the commoners, app developers, to become squeezed in the process. To either run a third faster, cap the capacity to grown by a third, or turn to other unsavory business models.

Facebook and Apple and Google might bicker amongst themselves from time to time, just like I'm sure the lords of the enclosure movement did back in the 1600s and 1700s, but in their essence, they're more alike than they are different.
Fighting not over whether or not to enclose the internet commons, but about who gets to do it, and what portion of the spoils should go to which set of shareholders.
But it's part of the same struggle that wins when people dump Twitter for a newsletter or Facebook for a podcast. Open pastures on internet commons, not enclosed or captured by any one company.
posts.key_insight: Big Tech's app store monopolies and platform dominance are a modern enclosure movement, privatizing what was once an open internet commons and taxing developers who formerly distributed software freely.

posts.summary: DHH describes the planning process for HEY's next development cycle, arguing that the team must deliberately prioritize weird, different, and fun features alongside practical ones. He acknowledges they're late on promised features like custom domains for personal accounts. But he pushes back against users who only want their wishlists fulfilled, arguing that nobody asks for weird or delightful — so it's the team's duty to champion it. The post is a short manifesto for product development that resists being driven purely by feature requests.
key_points: HEY's planning process includes an explicit directive to 'keep it weird' alongside practical feature work
Users ask for practical and configurable features but never ask for weird, different, or fun — so the team must advocate for those qualities themselves
Backfill features (the stuff you're 'supposed to do') are necessary but insufficient for a great product
Customer pushback on unexpected features like HEY World doesn't mean the team was wrong to build them
money_quotes: Keep HEY weird. Keep it different. Keep it FUN!
But people don't ask for weird. They don't ask for different. They even rarely ask for fun.
Life is more than just that, though. And HEY has to be.
That's why its our duty to stand up for weird/different/fun. Give it a seat at the planning table.
We've never been short on our own ideas, we've never been short on feature requests. That's software development!
posts.key_insight: Great products require the team to deliberately champion weird and delightful features that users would never think to request.

posts.summary: DHH recounts the origin story of his partnership with Jason Fried at 37signals, which began with an email in October 2001. After years of telling a slightly inaccurate version of the story, a $1,000 internet-sleuth bounty uncovered the original blog post, revealing that Jason's question was about regular expressions, not pagination as DHH had always claimed. The email was sent on October 27, 2001, when DHH was 22, from Copenhagen to Chicago. He finds poetic symmetry in the fact that their partnership began with email, and they now build HEY, an email product.
key_points: DHH's 20-year partnership with Jason Fried started from a single email responding to a blog post about a programming problem
The oft-told origin story turned out to be wrong — the question was about regular expressions for scraping Amazon book metadata, not pagination in PHP
Their first collaboration was Singlefile, an early SaaS product for book tracking at $19.95/year
There's a narrative symmetry in an email-born partnership culminating in building HEY, an email product
money_quotes: And now we know our origin story, as repeatedly told, was a lie!
There's some real poetic beauty in the fact that Jason and I started working together on account of an email. Sent from Copenhagen, Denmark to Chicago, Illinois. Twenty fucking years ago.
Yes, we really are the grand old men of software as a service
I wrote Jason the very next day, so the infamous email that started it all was sent on October 27th, 2001. Just twelve days after my 22nd birthday.
And now, here we are, spending our days honoring the majestic awesomeness that is email with HEY
posts.key_insight: The most consequential professional partnerships can begin from the smallest, most serendipitous interactions — a single email answering a stranger's programming question can launch a twenty-year collaboration.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on why he loves watches, framing them not as productivity tools but as memento mori—reminders of mortality that encourage spending time well. He draws on Stoicism and existentialism to argue that life's finite constraint is what gives it meaning, rejecting the 'time equals money' mentality as spiritually bankrupt. He hopes immortality is never discovered, believing the hundred-year limit is part of what makes life worthwhile. The post pivots to a lighthearted appreciation of a cheap Casio digital watch, celebrating its nostalgic design and practical simplicity as a perfect daily mortality reminder.
key_points: Watches serve as personal memento mori devices, reminding the wearer of mortality rather than productivity
The 'TIME = MONEY' productivity mindset is spiritually bankrupt—spending time well means living with satisfaction, not maximizing returns
Life's finite constraint gives it meaning; immortality would diminish rather than enhance existence
Stoicism and existentialism converge on the idea that embracing life's shortness is both morbid and liberating
A $20 Casio can be as meaningful and interesting as a mechanical watch when viewed through the right philosophical lens
money_quotes: I really like watches. Not so much because I need to precisely tell time all that often – most of my days, the calendar is pretty empty – but because they remind me that I'm going to die.
The notion that TIME = MONEY – squandered unless invested with a great return! – is spiritually bankrupt.
Life is long enough if you spend it well, but spending it well requires embracing life's shortness.
So much of what we fool ourselves into obsession over is trivial. But also that we could spend our time on things that are not trivial.
It's for the same reason I hope we never do discover immortality. A hundred years, give or take a decade or two, is enough. The constraint is part of what gives the duration its meaning.
What a lovely way to count down to the last day.
posts.key_insight: The awareness of mortality isn't morbid but liberating—it's the finite constraint of life that gives time its meaning and frees us from trivial obsessions.

posts.summary: DHH criticizes tech CEOs like Spotify's Daniel Ek and Netflix's Reed Hastings for viewing all human activity—including sleep—as competition for their platforms' attention. He argues this totalitarian approach to the attention economy treats users as mere batteries to be drained of every minute. The post highlights how even subscription-based companies that don't earn per-minute still obsessively pursue total attention capture out of paranoia. DHH draws parallels to addictive industries like tobacco and opioids, noting that Silicon Valley uniquely lionizes addiction under the euphemism of 'engagement.' He calls for a new ethical framework that treats binge consumption as the excessive indulgence it literally is.
key_points: Tech CEOs now define their competition as all human activity—not just rival services, but sleep, socializing, and every waking minute
Even subscription-based companies like Spotify and Netflix obsess over per-minute engagement despite not earning on a per-minute basis, driven by paranoia about churn
Facebook's ad-driven model makes the attention war even more literal—every captured minute directly monetizes
Silicon Valley uniquely celebrates addiction as 'engagement' and 'stickiness,' unlike tobacco and pharma companies that at least tried to deny their products' addictive qualities
The binge-watching culture Netflix promotes has real consequences, including drowsy driving that exceeds alcohol-related accidents
money_quotes: Every minute is a minute you could have spent listening to audio on Spotify. How depressing is that. How obsessive is that.
Just think about that for a second. This is the CEO of the world's premiere streaming service, musing with investors about how he might capture more service minutes if he can steal your sleep.
That is a pathological degree of neediness.
Is that really what we've been reduced to? A set of eyeballs (or earballs??) to be squeezed until every last tear of attention is drained.
The image of human batteries powering the simulation is barely a metaphor at this point.
Silicon Valley, on the other hand, lionizes addiction under the banner of engagement.
We don't have to live like this. You're not a bushel of waken hours. You're a goddamn human being.
posts.key_insight: Tech companies have adopted a totalitarian view of the attention economy where every human minute—including sleep—is territory to conquer, and Silicon Valley uniquely celebrates this addiction-driven approach as virtuous engagement.

posts.summary: DHH argues that reading habits should be private, drawing a parallel between the FBI's 1973 Library Awareness Program and today's Big Tech surveillance of what people read, follow, and subscribe to. He notes that Americans once deeply valued the principle that reading habits are nobody's business, yet now Twitter followers, Amazon purchases, and social media engagement are publicly visible and increasingly used to judge people. He describes how retweeting writers like Glenn Greenwald or Matt Taibbi now invites social condemnation, creating a chilling effect on intellectual curiosity. He calls for custodians of reading data — from librarians to social media platforms — to treat reading habits as private by default.
key_points: The FBI's Library Awareness Program once scandalized Americans, yet today Big Tech tracks reading habits far more invasively with little outcry
Social media platforms like Twitter make following lists public, turning reading choices into acts of social transgression subject to mob judgment
The acceleration of cancel culture creates expanding 'rings of incrimination' where not just writing but reading subversive content becomes punishable
Email newsletters (outside Gmail) remain one of the few private reading channels, which DHH holds up as the correct model
DHH acknowledges the complexity of content moderation questions while arguing the chilling effect on reading diversity is a serious problem
money_quotes: What you read is none of anyone's business.
We're well into the phase where the rings of incrimination are spreading. It's bad to be the original author of subversive writings, but it's increasingly bad to be a reader as well.

How bizarre it would have seemed back then to learn that in the future your reading habits would not only be tracked forever by Big Tech, and thus available to any agent with a subpoena, but broadcast to the world in a variety of uncomfortable ways.

It's bizarre you can't easily make your reading habits private on Twitter. You either have to hack the system with private lists, turn your account private, or keep a separate burner account with your subversive reading list. That's backwards.
But it has given me a renewed vigor to seek out subversive, alternative voices. To broaden my reading habits further, rather than shrink them down.

We need to bring back the idea that custodians of your reading habits, be they librarians or social media operators or online book sellers, should take a professional oath to keep your reading habits private.
These are big, difficult questions. And it's clear that we don't have big, easy answers.
posts.key_insight: The principle that reading habits should be private — once so strongly held it triggered congressional hearings — has been quietly abandoned in the age of Big Tech surveillance and social media mob judgment, and we need to reclaim it.

posts.summary: DHH examines whether Apple's commitment to privacy is genuine or merely convenient by focusing on the emerging conflict with Chinese tech giants. While Apple's privacy stance against Facebook costs nothing due to their dominant position, Chinese companies Tencent and ByteDance, backed by the Chinese government, are developing workarounds (CAID) to circumvent iOS 14.5's tracking protections. With China generating over $20 billion per quarter for Apple, the company faces a real test: enforce privacy principles and risk losing the Chinese market, or compromise and undermine its credibility. DHH frames this as Apple's first true privacy test where principles actually carry a price tag.
key_points: Apple's privacy stance against Facebook is essentially free since Apple holds all the leverage in that relationship
Chinese tech giants backed by the state have developed CAID to bypass iOS 14.5 tracking protections, forcing Apple into a genuine dilemma
China represents 20% of Apple's revenue, but Apple is replaceable there by local competitors like Huawei and Xiaomi
Letting Chinese companies skirt privacy rules while enforcing them on Facebook creates an antitrust vulnerability and credibility crisis
Most corporate principles are untested because they never conflict with profit—Apple's are about to be tested for real
money_quotes: It's easy to commit to principles when they don't cost you anything. That's why most mission statements ring so hollow.
The flowery mission statement is usually meant as a fig leaf over the real mission: grow as fast as possible / make as much money as possible.
Standing for something is a risk, so it takes courage and conviction.
Apple has been able to own the principle of privacy for free.

Chinese tech giants, with the explicit backing of the Chinese government, are telling Apple that they're not going to comply with the new privacy controls, and basically daring 'and what are you going to do about it??'
Apple will be forced to weigh the value of its commitment to privacy against the value of billions in Chinese revenue.
Cook already demonstrated with his pandering to the Trump administration that he has no qualms about moral compromises if it means an economic advantage for Apple.
So what's it gonna be, Apple? The purse or the principles?
posts.key_insight: Corporate principles only mean something when they cost you money, and Apple's privacy commitment is about to face its first real test in China where billions of dollars hang in the balance.

posts.summary: DHH critiques Google's decision to match Apple's reduced 15% App Store commission on the first million in revenue, framing it as a coordinated duopoly move rather than genuine concession. He imagines Machiavelli advising Apple on the original scheme as a cheap political play to split opposition while costing almost nothing. Google's copycat move, he argues, actually undermines the illusion of meaningful competition between the two platforms. However, he sees a silver lining: the fact that both companies felt compelled to act validates the strength of antitrust pressure being applied.
key_points: Apple's 15% reduced commission on the first million was a calculated political move to appear developer-friendly at negligible cost
Google copying the scheme exposes the lack of meaningful competition and reinforces the appearance of duopoly collusion
The concessions are cosmetic and do nothing to address the core antitrust arguments about app store dominance
The fact that both companies made these moves signals they are feeling genuine pressure from antitrust scrutiny
Google's follower position is strategically weaker — it looks sheepish without the political upside Apple gained
money_quotes: What if y'all just lowered the totally obscene 30% cut of revenues to a merely utterly obscene 15%, but then only for the first million in revenue? It would cost you bupkis, but the plebs might buy it!
It's a beautiful political play, meant to split an opposition at the lowest possible cost. Classic Machiavelli!

Google wasn't going to let Apple get away with appearing – and appearance was indeed all this was! – more benevolent, so they'd rather make both of them look like bandits than sit there with the lone bad hand.
Zero chance either Apple or Google would have announced these show-pony programs out of the goodness of their heart without the pressure.
So that validates the strength of the pressure.

This is good news for the antitrust momentum. Not because these crumbs do anything to counter the core argument, because they most certainly do not, but because it shows the sweating on the other side.
posts.key_insight: When dominant platforms make token concessions in lockstep, it doesn't prove competition exists — it proves antitrust pressure is working.

posts.summary: DHH argues against the crypto movement's vision of a trustless society, contending that trust is a fundamentally good thing worth preserving. He draws on his conversation with Small Technology Foundation's Aral Balkan to illustrate how small companies can engender personal trust in ways large corporations cannot. Using his experience with Braintree's CEO Bryan Johnson as an example, he shows how trust is personal and non-transferable — once Braintree was acquired by PayPal, the trust evaporated. He concludes that the world would be better with more human-scale companies where genuine trust relationships can form.
key_points: The crypto utopia of trustless transactions is a regression, not progress — removing trust from human dealings makes them worse
Trust is personal and doesn't scale: you can trust people but you can't trust corporations
Trust cannot be commodified or transferred in an acquisition — it evaporates when the trusted person leaves
Small, human-scale companies have a structural advantage in building trust that large corporations can never replicate
Skepticism about how companies treat user data is warranted, but the answer is better companies, not eliminating trust entirely
money_quotes: I don't think the world is better off by erasing the need to trust in our transactional counterparts, so turning these transactions into pure computing always struck me as a regression.
Trust is good, but trust doesn't scale. You can trust people, you can't trust corporations.
I didn't have a relationship with Braintree the company. And now that they're owned by a faceless corporation, the trust is gone. We're still there because of inertia, not allegiance.
Trust isn't a commodity. It can't be transferred in a sale, and as I said, it doesn't scale. Trust is personal. This is a feature not a bug!
The world would be better off if more companies were kept at a more human scale where trust can happen.
You can actually get to know and trust the folks who run them. This makes the transactions human, and therefore better. There's no minting that.
posts.key_insight: Trust is inherently personal and human-scale, which means the push toward trustless systems and faceless megacorporations represents a loss, not a gain, for how we transact with each other.

posts.summary: DHH argues that social media platforms are fundamentally broken and we don't need more of them. Content moderation fails at scale, and algorithmic amplification is harmful at any scale. He advocates returning to Web 1.0 principles — tools that let individuals publish without algorithmic amplification or viral mechanics. The solution is small-scale, human-paced publishing: newsletters, podcasts, and forums.
key_points: Content moderation is broken at moderate scale, and algorithmic amplification is broken at any scale
Virality is a bug, not a feature — it makes ideas burn bright but brief, preventing deep engagement
The constant stream of content from hundreds or thousands of people daily is unnatural and unhealthy for humans
Web 1.0 tools that enable publishing without amplification restore a natural, human scale to communication
Newsletters, podcasts, and small-scale forums are the right model for online discourse
money_quotes: We have enough social media platforms, and they are all broken.
Content moderation is bust at even moderate scale, and algorithmic amplification is broken at any scale.
Virality is a bug not a feature.
Ideas improve when they build slowly.
This constant quest for virality might make good ideas burn 10x as bright, but also 10x as short.
Everything from everyone all the time is too much. It's unnatural and it's unhealthy.
Tools that let individuals publish, but do not seek to amplify them or force them viral, give us that natural, human scale.
posts.key_insight: The internet needs fewer platforms and more simple publishing tools — virality and algorithmic amplification are fundamentally incompatible with healthy human discourse.

posts.summary: DHH critiques Andrew Wilkinson's post-mortem of his failed $10M to-do app Flow, arguing that Wilkinson drew the wrong lessons from his own failure. Wilkinson claimed he should have raised venture capital to compete with Asana, but DHH points out that Wilkinson never actually followed the bootstrapping principles he claimed to be inspired by from Basecamp. Instead of staying profitable, building a differentiated product, and living within his means, Wilkinson chased billionaire dreams with a bootstrapper's budget. DHH argues that Flow could have been a successful small business making millions per year, but Wilkinson's VC-style ambitions without VC funding doomed the company. The real lesson is that if you want unicorn outcomes, take VC money and accept the odds; if you want a sustainable business, actually follow bootstrapping principles.
key_points: Wilkinson claimed inspiration from Basecamp's model but violated every core principle: he overspent, chased growth, copied competitors, and spread the product too thin
Running a company with VC ambitions but without VC funding is the worst of both worlds — you get all the reckless spending with none of the cushion
Defining success as beating a well-funded competitor on their terms is a losing strategy; under-doing the competition and building a differentiated product is more viable
Todoist and Basecamp prove that bootstrapped to-do apps can succeed — Flow's failure was about strategy, not about lacking venture capital
A $1-3M/year company employing 10 people and generating profits for years is not a failure — it's a success most entrepreneurs would envy
money_quotes: If you run your company like it was VC funded without the venture capital, yeah, you're going to wish you had just taken other people's money.
It's so easy to fall in love with one of those infinite alternate universes where you just did that one thing differently and everything worked out.
If you define your success by whether you're beating a competitor or you're winning some arbitrarily-defined market, you'll be sucked into playing a game where they set the rules.
This romantic dream that Flow would have beat Asana if only it had more money, more people, more ads, more clients, more everything is just that... a dream.
Most people should be ever so grateful to have failures like that!
It's the venture capital mission without other people's money.
You'll almost certainly fail, because those are just the odds of the venture game, but at least you'll be spending other people's money to do so.
posts.key_insight: Drawing lessons from your own failures is treacherous because you'll romanticize the path not taken rather than recognizing you were playing the wrong game entirely.

posts.summary: DHH argues that business leaders who refer to what their company makes as 'the product' reveal they're in it for the business mechanics rather than the craft. He contends this language reduces everything to interchangeable commodities and serves as a shield for executives who lack domain expertise. He points to research showing employees are happier under technically competent bosses. The commodifying mindset eventually degrades quality as bean counters drain the substance from what a company makes.
key_points: Calling what you make 'the product' is a tell that leadership prioritizes business mechanics over craft
Abstract managerial science pretends domain expertise is irrelevant, but it deeply matters
Commodity language insulates executives from accountability for not understanding their own business
Research shows employees are happier working for bosses with technical competence in their domain
Customers eventually notice when a company is run by people who don't care about the particulars
money_quotes: "Product" reduces everything to a tradable commodity. A box that could really be any box, as long as it sells.
No. It does fucking matter.
That's probably why they adopt this managerial language of commodifying bullshit! To insulate against not having a clue about the business they're in.
To attempt to narrow the scope of competence to the business of business, so being ignorant about the particulars becomes irrelevant.
It might take a while, or even a long while, for the bean counters to slowly drain the soup of all the beans, but usually that's eventually what happens.
Make a car. Make a game. Make a camera. Just for the ever love of it don't make a "product".
posts.key_insight: The word 'product' is a shield for executives who lack domain expertise, and companies led by people who don't care about the particulars of what they make will eventually have nothing worth making.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Apple's App Store review process is fundamentally broken by design, functioning as security theater rather than genuine protection. Evidence from the Epic v. Apple lawsuit reveals reviewers check 50-100 apps daily with no technical background, while multi-million dollar scams persist unchecked. He contends the real purpose of App Store review is business model enforcement and competitive leverage, not user safety. DHH points to macOS as proof that system-level protections work without gatekeeping, and calls on Apple to adopt common carrier principles and net neutrality for native apps.
key_points: App reviewers spend as little as one minute per app with no technical background, making meaningful security review impossible at scale
Apple's own senior engineer compared App Store defenses to 'bringing a plastic butter knife to a gunfight'
The vague App Store guidelines serve as cover for arbitrary enforcement rather than consistent security review
macOS proves system-level protections work without a gatekeeping review process, undermining Apple's justification for iOS restrictions
Apple should embrace common carrier and net neutrality principles, treating all app makers equally regardless of business model
money_quotes: Apple's App Store was never designed to work. At least not in the way the company purports that it does.
There's a term for a practice like this: security theater.
Nobody could possibly provide real security reviews with a workload like that.
The key benefit Apple derives from using overworked and under-qualified personnel to review apps is business model enforcement and competitive leverage.

App developers are the ones suffering from the inconsistent, capricious, and infuriating decisions made by reviewers who might at most spend a few minutes reviewing the work that took months or years to complete.
They're meant to give Apple the cover to do whatever they want, whenever they want, to whomever they please. It's the essence of absolutist rule.
Apple needs to admit that they can't have millions of apps and project a picture of perfect safety.
The tragedy of Darth Apple can still end in redemption. There's good underneath that monopoly mask. Take it off, Tim.
posts.key_insight: Apple's App Store review process is security theater that exists not to protect users but to enforce business model compliance and competitive advantage, and should be replaced with the same system-level protections that already work on macOS.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the iPhone represented both a milestone and a fundamental regression: a general-purpose computer that only runs Apple-approved software. He traces how Apple's 'Pivot to Services' around 2017 transformed latent control into active monopoly exploitation, using the App Store as a tollbooth to extract rents and crush competitors. Drawing on his own experience with HEY.com and Epic's lawsuit, he contends that Apple's security justification is theater, with unqualified reviewers spending minutes on apps while scams flourish. He calls for the end of Apple's monopoly tollbooth as lawmakers worldwide begin to act.
key_points: The iPhone was a general-purpose computer locked down unlike any mainstream computer in history, including Apple's own Mac
Apple's 'Pivot to Services' circa 2017 turned latent platform control into active monopoly exploitation as iPhone growth peaked
Apple uses the App Store as a competitive weapon to gain unfair advantages in gaming, news, streaming, fitness, and payments
Apple's security justification is undermined by unqualified reviewers and years of unchecked scam apps
Regulatory and legislative action against the App Store is accelerating in the US and Europe
money_quotes: They called it a phone, but the revolution was shrinking a general-purpose computer to fit in your pocket. That was the progress.
It was a general-purpose computer that was only allowed to run software specifically approved by Apple, unlike any other mainstream general-purpose computer in history.
Like this wasn't a computer at all, but a gaming console or carrier-locked flip phone.
Apple's new strategy for a peak iPhone world where new growth had to come from making more money off existing customers rather than finding new ones.
The App Store tollbooth during that time not only expanded who were due to pay, but served as a competitive cudgel to beat competitors like Spotify.
We went from a bear story of peak iPhone to Apple surpassing two trillion dollars in market cap, or four times the valuation from 2017, off the prospects and profits of these monopoly rents.
I lived through hell for two weeks last year when Apple threatened to destroy our new email service HEY.com, unless we yielded 30% of our revenues.
The App Store is classic monopoly tollbooth that Apple is trying to justify by operating a security theater.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone's locked-down App Store was a fundamental regression disguised as progress, and Apple's 'Pivot to Services' exposed it as a monopoly tollbooth justified by security theater.

posts.summary: DHH reacts to a Verge exposé revealing Apple's failure to address widespread App Store scams despite years of warnings. He argues Apple is effectively an accomplice to fraud, knowingly profiting from scam apps through its 30% commission while doing nothing to stop them. He highlights the terrible timing for Apple's Chief Compliance Officer, who must defend App Store safety before a Senate committee on the same day the story breaks. DHH frames Apple's inaction as a product of unchecked success breeding arrogance and invincibility.
key_points: Apple knowingly allowed scam apps to persist on the App Store despite specific, repeated warnings, making them complicit in consumer fraud
Apple profited directly from these scams through its 30% cut of in-app payments, creating a financial incentive to look the other way
Apple's legal exposure could mirror that of banks knowingly aiding money laundering — potentially criminal, not just civil
The timing devastates Apple's position before a Senate antitrust hearing where they must defend App Store policies
Apple's years of unchallenged dominance bred a dangerous belief in their own invincibility
money_quotes: Now the problem is that Apple is defacto an accomplice to fraud. They knowingly aided and abetted scams that preyed on consumers and cost them millions.
Every scam that ran through the in-app payment system paid Apple a 30% cut of the take.
All the money went into PR, lawyers, and lobbyists. Oh, and obscene returns to investors.
This has to be pretty close to the same level of legal exposure a bank would have by knowingly aiding and abetting money laundering?
Apple has been winning so much for so long that they've deluded themselves into thinking they're invincible.
Apple has been putting on a security theater with the App Store, but have hired the worst actors, let the set fall apart, and accepted a director that is nowhere to be found.
It's no longer a tragedy, it's a farce. Time to drop the curtain.
posts.key_insight: Apple's claim that its App Store monopoly exists to protect consumers collapses when the company knowingly profits from the very scams it claims to prevent.

posts.summary: DHH announces a new email recycling feature coming to HEY, arguing that Gmail trained people to hoard emails forever primarily to mine that data for advertising and machine learning. He frames email deletion as both an ecological and personal liberation issue, noting that newsletters, notifications, and routine correspondence have no reason to persist indefinitely. The feature allows users to set recycling schedules per sender or domain, with newsletters being the easiest win. He draws a parallel to Signal's disappearing messages and the psychological lightness of not maintaining a permanent digital trail.
key_points: Gmail's 'save everything' model exists to serve Google's data mining, not users' actual needs
Storing unread emails indefinitely wastes real physical resources in data centers
HEY's recycling center lets users auto-delete emails by sender, domain, or category like newsletters
There's a psychological benefit to ephemeral communication, similar to Signal's disappearing messages
Corporate retention policies are crude and compliance-driven; HEY's approach is user-motivated and granular
money_quotes: Gmail taught us to save every email forever so they'd have an endless data trove to mine for purchases, behaviors, and connections.
Endless fields for machine learning to roam wild, sowed by the anxiety of WHAT IF I NEED IT ONE DAY.
Storing all those emails that are only ever going to be read again by machines is something we should stop doing.
There are entire data centers dedicated to storing all this crap forever. Soaking up resources to build, maintain, and power. Why?
There's such liberation in knowing that not everything you ever said is going to live in a hoard of data forever. It's a lightness that's hard to articulate.
We really all do need to think about what we can do, big and small, to squander fewer resources.
posts.key_insight: The default of saving every email forever serves surveillance capitalism, not users, and we should reclaim the freedom of impermanence.

posts.summary: DHH announces the end of Signal v Noise, the influential blog that connected him with Jason Fried in 2001 and spawned REWORK and other books. After 22 years, the energy for writing on the company blog faded, perhaps due to Twitter's pull or the weight of running a larger company. Both he and Jason have found renewed writing energy on HEY World, where they write from personal perspectives and it feels effortless again. DHH rejects nostalgia as a trap that romanticizes the past at the expense of the future, preferring to view SvN as milestones in the rearview mirror while keeping forward momentum.
key_points: Signal v Noise, running since 1999, is being retired after 22 years as a pioneering tech blog
The company blog format lost its energy — possibly due to Twitter siphoning ideas into ephemeral form, or the weight of a growing company
HEY World revived their writing by making it personal and effortless again, producing more output in two months than the prior year
DHH draws a sharp distinction between legacy (useful milestones) and nostalgia (a romanticizing trap that holds you back)
money_quotes: I'm just not a nostalgic person. In fact, I'm deeply skeptical of nostalgia. It too often feels like a trap to romanticize the past at the expense of the future.
If you fall in love with who you once were, it's too easy to forget to keep going. I want to keep going.
I enjoy the idea of a legacy. But as milestones in the rearview mirror. There to remind you of where you were, but without any obligation to stop and linger and fawn over every one.
That's when I enjoy writing the most. When it's effortless. When the words just pour out of my fingers, and I can hit send a few edits later.
When it stops feeling like that, I stop writing. And instead end up taking the ideas to a cheap, ephemeral medium like Twitter, only to waste those thoughts on the void.
This isn't a wake. It's a celebration!
posts.key_insight: Legacy is best honored not through nostalgia but by keeping the spirit alive in new forms — when the old vessel stops serving the energy, move on without sentimentality.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Basecamp is banning societal political discussions from its internal work communication platforms. He argues that these debates create stress and strife without changing minds, and that the company workplace is not the right forum for them. Employees are encouraged to engage in political advocacy on personal platforms and outside of work. The company will still engage on politics directly related to its business, like antitrust and privacy. DHH addresses the Twitter backlash, clarifying that personal political expression is still encouraged—just not on internal company tools. He frames the policy as etiquette rather than zero-tolerance enforcement.
key_points: Internal political discussions at work create strife without productive outcomes or changed minds
The policy applies equally to founders and employees—DHH and Jason Fried included
Basecamp will still take political stances on issues directly related to its business like antitrust and privacy
Employees are actively encouraged to engage in political advocacy on personal platforms outside work
The policy is framed as etiquette with gentle reminders, not zero-tolerance enforcement
money_quotes: There are many places to be involved, exposed, and engaged in those conversations. Basecamp shouldn't be one of those places.
You shouldn't have to wonder if staying out of it means you're complicit, or stepping into it means you're a target.
No comment thread on Basecamp is going to close the gap on fundamental philosophical and political differences. And we're left worse for wear when we try.
We also like to tell ourselves that having these discussions with the whole company is "healthy". I used to think that too, but I no longer do.
By trying to have the debates around such incredibly sensitive societal politics inside the company, we're setting ourselves up for strife, with little chance of actually changing anyone's mind.
It's none of Basecamp's business how or whether you choose to spend your time, money, or voice to support charities, causes, or political action groups.
posts.key_insight: Workplace political discussions create more stress and division than understanding, and companies serve employees better by keeping internal forums focused on work while encouraging personal political engagement elsewhere.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how Basecamp repeated a mistake they've warned against for over a decade: promising a feature (custom domains for HEY) before shipping it. Despite knowing that roadmaps create an 'illusion of agreement' where customers project their own expectations onto vague promises, they still pre-announced the feature and faced inevitable disappointment from customers whose expectations didn't match reality. He argues that even meeting expectations produces only a muted 'finally!' rather than excitement. The post is a reminder to his future self that knowing something intellectually isn't enough — you have to live the lesson.
key_points: Roadmaps and feature promises create an 'illusion of agreement' where vague descriptions let everyone imagine different outcomes, guaranteeing disappointment for some
Even when you meet customer expectations from a pre-announced feature, the reaction is merely 'finally!' — not excitement or delight
Knowing something intellectually is insufficient; lessons must be lived and recalled in the moment to actually change behavior
The pressure of endless customer requests can push teams into making promises they know are counterproductive
money_quotes: Don't promise, just ship.
This. Always. Happens!
It's not enough to know something, you have to live it. It's not enough to have learned something, you have to recall it.
When all we have to agree on is a slogan, like 'Meetings Tab, 4th Quarter', it becomes an empty imagination box that'll fit wildly different expectations.
Disappointment, however, is sure to ensue when only one set of expectations can actually be met.
But that just meant we met their expectations! Nothing too exciting, just, you know, finally!
posts.key_insight: Pre-announcing features turns a potential moment of delight into guaranteed disappointment for some and mere relief for the rest, because vague promises let every customer imagine a different product.

posts.summary: DHH argues that every person holds a unique mosaic of political and philosophical positions that defy neat ideological categorization. He illustrates this by listing his own positions that span liberal and conservative camps, showing how the overlap of people who agree with all of them shrinks rapidly. He warns against adopting ideological 'package deals' that offer belonging but breed rigidity and intolerance. The post also defends keeping contentious political debates out of the workplace, given the power dynamics involved. He concludes that accumulating too many ideological red lines constrains your ability to coexist with others.
key_points: Everyone holds a unique combination of positions that don't fit neatly into liberal or conservative boxes
Adopting an ideological package deal provides belonging but leads to increasing rigidity and tribal intolerance
The workplace is the wrong venue for identity-level political debates due to inherent power imbalances
The more ideological red lines you accumulate, the less tolerant you become of coexisting with others
Feeling betrayed when allies hold one heterodox position is a symptom of package-deal thinking
money_quotes: If you learn enough about someone, you'll eventually be disappointed or dismayed. This is nature, this is normal.
How many people do you think would agree with me on everything I've ever written? Nobody, that's how many. (Not even me!).
We need vigorous debate in a free society. But as the joke goes, 'Sir, this is a Wendy's'. Not every place is the right place. Not every time is the right time.
Not only will the ideological package deal keep evolving, usually without affording you much control or say in the matter, but it'll also stoke all the tribal proclivities you're hardwired for.
The more lines you accumulate, the more cross you're going to get. The more constrained your tolerance for coexisting with others.
If I end up in a debate with an employee at Basecamp that hits identity bedrock, we're both going to be put in a hard situation.
posts.key_insight: Intellectual honesty requires holding positions that form a unique mosaic rather than accepting a tribal package deal, and recognizing that not every venue — especially the workplace — is appropriate for debating identity-level disagreements.

posts.summary: DHH publicly shares Basecamp's internal communications about the 'Best Names Ever' list controversy after details were leaked to The Verge. He details how a list of funny customer names, maintained by employees over years, was discovered and addressed, but the internal discussion escalated when employees connected it to genocide frameworks, colonial atrocities, and the Atlanta shootings. His attempt to de-escalate by arguing for proportional response triggered HR complaints alleging discrimination and harassment, which external lawyers found unsubstantiated. The repeated acrimonious internal debates over societal politics ultimately led to Basecamp's controversial policy banning political discussions in company workspaces, with severance packages offered to dissenting employees.
key_points: The 'Best Names Ever' customer list was wrong and inappropriate, but connecting it to genocide pyramids and colonial atrocities was disproportionate and counterproductive
High-stakes framing of workplace mistakes (linking them to systemic oppression) makes people less willing to admit errors and more likely to hide transgressions
A genuinely diverse workforce will inevitably disagree on contentious societal issues, and forcing those debates onto company-wide stages creates acrimony rather than resolution
Some employees privately felt unable to push back against the dominant framing of these discussions for fear of being labeled, revealing a chilling effect on dissent
Basecamp offered generous severance to anyone who couldn't accept the new direction, acknowledging this is a matter of personal principles
money_quotes: If the stakes for any kind of bad judgement in this area is a potential link to a ladder that ends in genocide, we're off on a wrong turn.
People make mistakes. Some times repeatedly. And some times they're blind to those mistakes until times or sensibilities change. That does not mean they're irredeemable people.
The key to working with other people of different ideological persuasions is to find common cause in the work, in the relations with customers, in the good we can do in the industry.

If you do indeed strive to have a diverse workforce both ideologically and identity wise, you're not going to find unison on all these difficult, contentious issues. If you did, you'd both be revealing an intellectual monoculture.

Feeling unable to speak up out of fear that they'd have an accusatory label affixed to their person for refusing to accept the predominant framing of the issues presented by other more vocal employees.
Demanding that it also has to play out in our shared workspaces isn't going to lead anywhere good, in my opinion.

We should in my opinion also be humble enough to recognize that if we participated in acts just a few years ago that we now consider deplorable, it's possible for others now to be where we were then without being irredeemable people.
posts.key_insight: Workplace mistakes deserve proportional responses, and importing maximalist societal-political frameworks into internal company debates creates an environment where people hide errors rather than correct them.
