Rails 7 seeks to be the wormhole that folds the time-learning-shipping-continuum, and allows you to travel grand distances without knowing all the physics of interstellar travel.

The key engine powering this assault is conceptual compression. Like a video codec that throws away irrelevant details such that you might download the film in real-time rather than buffer for an hour.
There are so many vested interests in the subdivision of expertise now. Specialisms are getting ever more narrow. And this is trying to go in the opposite direction.
It's been well over twenty years for me now, and a big release like this still makes me giddy like nothing else.
posts.key_insight: Rails 7 aims to reverse the industry's march toward ever-narrower specialization by compressing enough complexity into one framework that a single person can build and ship competitive modern web applications.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on Steve Jobs' motto 'Real artists ship' and argues that actually delivering products to users matters far more than impressive demos or future promises. He points to Apple's discipline in only showing what's ready to ship at keynotes, contrasting it with the temptation to demo vaporware. He admits Basecamp has occasionally fallen into the trap of making promises they couldn't keep. The core message is that the authentic feeling of shipping real improvements to real people is one of the best experiences in business.
key_points: Tech demos and future promises provide artificial excitement — 'empty calories' that often lead to broken commitments
Apple's discipline of only showcasing shippable products at keynotes exemplifies the 'real artists ship' ethos
Even disciplined teams like Basecamp occasionally succumb to the temptation of premature promises
The authentic satisfaction of shipping real improvements to real users is addictive and worth pursuing over demo culture
money_quotes: Real artists ship.
A tech demo isn't shipping. And shipping is what counts.
It's so tempting to bask in the excitement of a demo showing things that (might) come later. But it's an artificial feeling. Largely empty calories.
It often comes back to bite you in the ass when reality proves more troublesome than the imagination, and fulfilling the promises become hard or impossible.
The authentic shipping feeling is one of the best in the business. Getting real improvements, real progress, into the hands of real people.
Be a real artist. Ship.
posts.key_insight: The discipline of shipping real products to real users, rather than basking in the artificial excitement of demos and promises, is what separates authentic creators from pretenders.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the cultural dominance of 'woke' ideology has likely peaked, citing John McWhorter's ability to promote his book 'Woke Racism' on mainstream platforms that would have been hostile to such arguments a year prior. He points to the practical failures of 'defund the police' policies — rising murders, carjackings, and retail theft in major cities — as accelerating the backlash. He notes both cultural and political shifts, including the Virginia governor's race and San Francisco's mayor reversing course. DHH hopes the pendulum doesn't swing to the opposite extreme, advocating instead for moderation and a return to a world where not everything is filtered through political ideology.
key_points: McWhorter's mainstream media acceptance for 'Woke Racism' signals a rapid shift in what's culturally permissible to argue
The 'defund the police' movement has collided with rising crime in Philadelphia, Chicago, and San Francisco, undermining its theoretical appeal
Political consequences are materializing, as seen in Virginia's gubernatorial election and San Francisco's mayor reversing progressive stances
The counter to one form of extremism should be moderation, not an equal swing to the opposite extreme
The politicization of every topic is unsustainable and culturally stunting
money_quotes: The abstract moral motivation behind defunding the police has met the concrete experience of lawlessness and violence.
It's hard to maintain your theoretical ideals if they're in conflict with the practice of living in a deteriorating city. The theory is bound to yield for most people.
The counter to one kind of extremism isn't its negation, but its moderation.
We can enjoy all manner of interests without having to rationalize them through some ideological lens.
The world is made better when some attention is spent some of the time attending to these issues. But it can't be all of it, everywhere, all of the time.
If we've passed peak 'woke', and I sure hope we have, may it be followed soon thereafter with peak politics too.
It's not only is it unsustainable, it's also stunting.
posts.key_insight: When ideological movements collide with deteriorating real-world conditions, the resulting disillusionment should drive society toward moderation rather than an equal and opposite extremism.

posts.summary: DHH argues that email, not social media, is the true medium for meaningful discourse on the internet. He highlights how his media diet has shifted toward newsletters from independent writers, and how direct email exchanges with strangers have produced far more respectful and insightful debates than a decade of Twitter. He contends that social media's performative dynamics — dunks, retweets, likes — poison discourse, while email strips away the audience and restores humanity. The experience reminds him of the early internet's promise, which he argues is still accessible, just not widely adopted.
key_points: Social media is not the internet — it's one distorted expression of it, and other modes of connection still work
Newsletters from independent writers have become the majority of DHH's media diet, replacing social media feeds
Email debates with strangers are far more respectful and insightful because they remove the performative audience
The concept of 'going viral' is aptly named — social media spreads like a collective brain virus eating our humanity
The early internet's promise of meaningful exchange still exists through email; it's just not widely distributed
money_quotes: Social media is not the internet. It's merely one distorted expression of it.

Without the stage of social media, there's just none of the performative bullshit. No one to impress with your superlative dunks. No retweets or likes to egg you on. Just two people engaging directly, humanely.
It's solidly reassuring that the whole world has indeed not gone mad (either in general or at each other), and that the internet can still facilitate meaningful exchange.

It's funny how intrinsically linked the concept of 'going viral' has become with social media. Because it's an eerily apt description of what most of the time seems like a collective brain virus eating our humanity.
The antidote is here. It's just not widely distributed.
posts.key_insight: Email removes the performative audience that poisons social media discourse, restoring the internet's original promise of meaningful human exchange.

posts.summary: DHH explains how Basecamp manages programming projects without tightly linking todos to code commits. He argues that the pull request already provides sufficient connection between planning and implementation, making micro-linking unnecessary overhead. Using the Out of Office feature as a case study, he shows how they split work between Basecamp (scope, decisions, trade-offs) and GitHub (code, reviews, CI). He contrasts this approach with Jira-style ticket factories, positioning Basecamp's method as part of their broader Shape Up philosophy.
key_points: You don't need to tie every todo to every commit — pull requests already provide the right level of granularity for connecting planning to code
Most features at Basecamp have a 1-1 relationship between a Basecamp project and a pull request, keeping things simple
Project management is split cleanly: Basecamp handles scope, decisions, and trade-offs while GitHub handles code, reviews, and CI
Managing projects is mostly about communication and human context, not procedural linking of artifacts
The Jira ticket-pulling assembly-line mindset is fundamentally incompatible with how Basecamp thinks about work
money_quotes: Micromanaging that level of detail has the appeal of superficial neatness, but without much if any payoff in additional ease or insight in practical terms.

The entire story is told in two parts: The Basecamp project that sets the scope, manages the outstanding work, documents the trade-offs and the considerations. The GitHub repository that holds the code, the per-line review notes, and the results of continuous integration testing.
Managing projects, be they code or otherwise, is mostly about communication.

Making decisions about how things should work, doing the trade-offs to ship within the time allotted. That's the kind of work that benefits from narratives, selective highlighting, and human context far more than it does from some procedural idea of Tying It All Together.
Programmers are trained in consistency and coherence. They're key properties of writing good code. But sometimes chasing those properties outside the code just don't offset the cost of doing so.
I get that this might feel somewhat alien if you're working at a feature factory pulling tickets from a Jira backlog.
posts.key_insight: Project management doesn't need granular commit-to-ticket traceability — a single pull request per feature and clear narrative communication provide all the context teams actually need.

posts.summary: DHH contrasts Denmark's calm, respectful public debate over childhood COVID vaccination with America's polarized, tribal approach to the same question. He notes that despite Denmark having one of the highest adult vaccination rates in the world, only a small fraction of children 5-12 were fully vaccinated, with parents freely making the choice without coercion. He presents data showing vaccines did little to prevent infection spread and that children faced extremely low risk from COVID compared to other causes. The post ultimately argues that Denmark's ability to tolerate dissent and maintain good-faith discussion is a sign of societal strength that America should emulate.
key_points: Denmark maintains a respectful, non-coercive public debate on childhood vaccination despite high adult vaccination rates and low child uptake
Data showed vaccinated and unvaccinated people in Denmark were being infected at similar rates, undermining the spread-prevention argument for child vaccination
Children face far lower COVID mortality risk than from flu or drowning, making parental hesitancy a reasonable position
America's vaxx vs anti-vaxx trench warfare contrasts sharply with Denmark's approach of treating both sides as legitimate
High public trust in health authorities may be sustained precisely by tolerating dissent rather than demanding compliance
money_quotes: Being eager to vaccinate kids is one reasonable stance, being hesitant to vaccinate kids is another reasonable stance.
Tolerating dissent and discussion is a sign of strength, not weakness.
What a contrast to the American public dialogue! Which seems to have dug into the trenches of vaxx v anti-vaxx.
What fascinates me the most is that Denmark is able to have this sober public debate at all while America largely can't.
How we conduct the public debate is not a given!
If Denmark can have a reasonable debate on a highly contentious topic like whether to vaccinate your kids or not, why can't America?
The societal debate is full of admonishments to ensure that schools don't end up making unvaccinated kids feel different.
posts.key_insight: A society's ability to hold good-faith debate on contentious issues, tolerating dissent without demonization, is itself a marker of institutional health and public trust.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Basecamp's exceptional uptime record in 2021, with all applications achieving at least 99.99% uptime and Basecamp 2 hitting five nines (99.999%). He attributes this to years of infrastructure investment including active-active dual data center deployments and redundant S3 setups across independent AWS regions. The post also notes their migration away from Google Cloud Platform after reliability issues. DHH argues that operational excellence deserves public celebration despite the superstition that bragging about uptime invites disaster.
key_points: Every Basecamp application achieved at least 99.99% uptime in 2021, with Basecamp 2 reaching five nines — their best reliability record in nearly 20 years
Active-active double data center deployments allow major upgrades with ease and instant failover when issues hit a single data center
They fully migrated off Google Cloud Platform after being burned by storage outages in 2019, replacing it with redundant multi-region S3 setups
Applications are strategically distributed across AWS regions so that a single region outage doesn't take down everything simultaneously
Operational excellence deserves celebration, not superstitious silence
money_quotes: It was a very loud year, 2021. Which makes the satisfying silence of technical incidents at Basecamp all the more of a celebration.
Basecamp 2 hit the mythical five nines with 99.999% uptime!
We finally shut down the last of our Google Cloud Platform usage, after having been burned so badly in 2019 by their file storage outages in particular.

Now I know that superstition demands that if you celebrate your uptime, you're due a calamity of failures shortly thereafter. And while I don't believe in that kind of mumbo jumbo, it totally could happen.
I don't think having a fear of celebrating the crowning achievements of technical operational excellence is wise.

Keeping the lights on for a (very broad!) set of applications with hundreds of thousands of users, petabytes of data, across on-premise and cloud, with software legacies stretching back to the dawn of this millennium? That's not easy!
posts.key_insight: Achieving near-perfect uptime across a diverse application portfolio requires years of deliberate infrastructure investment — and that operational excellence deserves to be celebrated, not hidden behind superstition.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the childhood lesson of putting setbacks in perspective by remembering things could always be worse. He connects this mindset to Stoic philosophy, particularly negative visualization and the idea that we suffer more in our minds than in reality. He argues that resilience comes from controlling our reactions to events rather than the events themselves. He closes by wishing modern culture embraced more of this perspective instead of catastrophizing.
key_points: The 'it could be worse' mindset is a practical form of Stoic negative visualization applied after setbacks occur
We suffer more in our minds than in reality — our reactions to events cause more pain than the events themselves
Resilience is built by recognizing that our reactions are not fixed and can be consciously changed
Modern culture trends toward catastrophizing ('this is the worst thing ever') rather than maintaining perspective
money_quotes: The way to make those setbacks and the inevitable suffering in life bearable was to constantly remind yourself that... it could be worse!
The underlying premise is the essence of Stoicism: We suffer more in our mind than in reality.
The ultimate resilience to face the struggles of living lies in controlling our reactions to the things that happen.
Our reactions are not given. They can be changed.
I wish there was more of that today. More "it could be worse". Less "this is the worst thing ever".
posts.key_insight: Resilience comes not from avoiding hardship but from training yourself to put suffering in perspective — a Stoic practice modern culture has largely abandoned in favor of catastrophizing.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Basecamp is hiring a Director of Engineering as the company rapidly expands its technical teams, aiming to double its programming staff. He reflects on how eight years earlier they made the opposite choice, shrinking to focus on one product to avoid growing beyond 40-60 people. Now consciously pushing through that ceiling, they're embracing middle management for the first time. The core challenge: hiring managers is fundamentally harder than hiring individual contributors because there's no equivalent of a take-home coding test for management roles.
key_points: Basecamp is reversing its 2014 decision to stay small, now growing rapidly to support multiple products including HEY
Between 40-60 employees there's a tipping point where you can no longer avoid full-time middle management
Hiring managers is much harder than hiring ICs because you can't evaluate them through realistic work samples
The COO hiring process required 50+ hours of interviews with a dozen candidates due to the lack of practical assessment methods
After nearly 20 years in business, Basecamp is on track to cross 100 employees for the first time
money_quotes: Wild to think that after almost twenty years in business, we're suddenly on a fast track to crack a hundred employees.
That still feels like it was the right decision at the time, even if we're making the opposite one now.
Between 40-60 people, we felt we could get away with having essentially no full-time managers.
But hiring managers is hard! Much harder than hiring individual contributors who can be evaluated in large part on the strength of their individual work.
It's incredible how much it helps the hiring process to be able to see someone's actual, real work on a realistic assignment that mirrors what they'd be doing if hired.
But what's a take-home assignment for a director of engineering? Or for any manager?
We spent fifty-plus hours in interviews with a little over a dozen candidates for the COO position before we hired Elaine. That was incredibly exhausting.
posts.key_insight: There's a fundamental asymmetry in hiring: individual contributors can be evaluated through realistic work samples, but no equivalent practical test exists for managers, making those hires slower, harder, and riskier.

posts.summary: DHH observes that Basecamp's director of engineering opening attracted a flood of applicants who didn't read the basic requirements or follow application instructions. He questions why people treat job applications like lottery tickets, speculating that disillusionment with AI-driven hiring processes has led candidates to stop putting in effort. He pushes back on this approach, noting that Basecamp has humans reviewing every application. He argues that spray-and-pray applications create a negative psychological feedback loop for the applicants themselves.
key_points: Most applicants to Basecamp openings don't meet basic requirements or follow application instructions
Candidates may assume AI screens applications, making effort feel pointless, but DHH is skeptical this is true for most roles
Basecamp reviews applications with humans who won't spend time on non-compliant submissions
Low-effort mass applications create a psychological toll through terrible callback ratios, worsening a negative feedback loop
Putting in genuine effort on fewer applications is a better strategy than spamming resumes
money_quotes: Virtually none of whom seem to have bothered reading the basic requirements for the opening or comply with the instructions on how to make a successful application.
Do people think this is a lottery?
Presumably this approach must work for some people, some of the time, right? Otherwise why would you keep operating like this?

There's a human on the other side, and that human is not going to spend five minutes pondering whether you might be a fit for the role, if you don't comply with the basic requirements, like including a cover letter.
I think there's a cost to that kind of job applications which show up in a psychological toll from a terrible applications-to-callbacks ratio.
I guess what I'm saying is make the effort or don't bother. We're not drawing names out of a hat here.
posts.key_insight: Treating job applications as a numbers game hurts applicants more than it helps, creating a demoralizing feedback loop that rewards no one.

posts.summary: DHH defends merit-based hiring practices, arguing that direct assessment of programming ability should be the primary criterion for hiring programmers. He pushes back against criticisms that meritocracy is inherently unjust when it fails to produce desired diversity outcomes. Drawing parallels to blind orchestra auditions and standardized college admissions tests, he argues that placing people in roles beyond their competence hurts everyone involved. He advocates for Basecamp's take-home assignment approach as the most fair and direct way to evaluate candidates.
key_points: Direct assessment of merit (actual programming ability) is more reliable and fair than indirect proxies like pedigree, brainteasers, or years of experience
Diversity has value but should not displace core competency as the primary hiring criterion
Placing people in roles beyond their current ability hurts both the individual and the team, citing McWhorter's argument about matching students to appropriate schools
Take-home assignments are superior to traditional interviews because they test real skills rather than rewarding smooth talkers
The broader cultural push against meritocracy in hiring, education, and the arts is misguided and undermines equality of opportunity
money_quotes: It doesn't matter what else is true about you, if your programming chops aren't up to par, there's not going to be a job offer.
Diversity certainly can be of merit in itself, as the team acquires a broader perspective and more closely resembles its customer base, but not as a displacement for core competency.
If the gap is too big, it'll eventually swallow up the best intentions, the broadest patience, and the deepest empathy.
The majority of front-ends-on-a-database work that employs millions today doesn't exactly ask for geniuses.
The alternative to direct assessments of merit is usually indirect assessments of merit! Like those pedigree gates. Or those brainteasers. Isn't that worse?
Merit is not a dirty word. Letting everyone have an equal shot is equality of opportunity. Once we veer off that path, we're into the wilderness.
May the best candidate get the spot, the admission, or the job.
posts.key_insight: Direct assessment of merit through real-world work samples is the fairest hiring practice, and attempts to subordinate competency to other criteria ultimately harm everyone involved.

posts.summary: DHH describes the intellectual thrill of changing long-held beliefs, using his shift on nuclear power as the primary example. Drawing on Michael Shellenberger's arguments, he outlines why wind and solar alone cannot solve climate change due to reliability, storage, and ecological issues. He makes the case that nuclear power is a superior energy source when trade-offs are weighed objectively, noting that historical opposition from environmentalists now looks like a catastrophic error. The post ultimately argues that progress on any topic requires willingness to revisit assumptions and embrace better arguments over tribal loyalties.
key_points: Wind and solar are unreliable and require dirty backup sources like coal, making exclusive reliance on them counterproductive
Nuclear power emits no greenhouse gases, provides abundant predictable power, and has caused far fewer deaths than commonly feared
The spectacle of nuclear disasters like Chernobyl and Fukushima has massively overstated their risks relative to everyday energy sources
Germany serves as a cautionary tale of over-investing in renewables while becoming dependent on Russian natural gas
Intellectual progress requires the willingness to change your mind when presented with better arguments and data
money_quotes: I've changed my mind on a lot of topics over the last few years, and it's frankly been exhilarating.
If you over-invest in wind and solar, you may end up with a power grid that's simultaneously expensive, unreliable, dirty, and without sovereignty.
The nuclear waste generated can be stored on-site, and the waste needed for a single person's lifetime energy consumption can fit in a coke can.
Their impact has largely been drawn from the spectacle of disaster. And as a result been massively overstated.
When we weigh the trade offs objectively, nuclear emerges as shining star. Not because there are no risks, but because they are far less than the alternatives.
The historical opposition from environmentalists now looks like a catastrophic error in hindsight.
Fall in love with the thrill of accepting the better argument – in contrast to mere social pressure – and you'll help pave the path to that better world.
posts.key_insight: Progress comes not from grand solutions but from the willingness to change your mind when better arguments emerge, trading tribal loyalty for honest evaluation of trade-offs.

posts.summary: DHH contrasts Denmark's open, non-judgmental public debate about childhood COVID vaccines with the polarized, shame-driven approaches in France, Australia, and the US. He notes that despite high trust in government, only 40% of Danish parents vaccinated their 5-11 year-olds, and this hesitancy is discussed seriously rather than condemned. He references mainstream Danish media featuring vaccine researchers questioning the ethics of recommending vaccines for young children. DHH admits he doesn't have answers for why countries diverge so sharply but warns against easy conspiratorial explanations, suggesting deeper psychological and sociological forces are at play.
key_points: Denmark allows open, mainstream debate about childhood vaccine pros and cons without political tribalism or shaming
Only 40% of Danish parents vaccinated their 5-11 year-olds despite strong government recommendations and high institutional trust
Other countries like France, Australia, and the US have adopted punitive or tribal approaches to vaccine hesitancy
Easy conspiratorial answers about money and corruption don't explain why different democracies diverge so sharply
The societal impulse to 'other' the unvaccinated varies dramatically by country for reasons we don't yet understand
money_quotes: While the French president is talking about "pissing off the unvaccinated", the Australians are trying to a martyr of Djokovic, and the Americans continue to render everything virus through the basic two-way political tribal lens, Denmark is doing something else.

It's the fact that this vaccine hesitancy amongst parents of small children is taken seriously! Discussed widely, without reaching to the knee-jerk modes of condemnation that is characterizing so many other countries at the moment.
It is unethical to recommend the vaccine to 5-11 year olds because we lack solid proof on the advantages of doing so for this age group.
I don't bring this up because I have an answer here. I really don't know why some countries break one way and others another. I don't think anyone else really knows either.
In the absence of such an answer, it's easy to go down the path of "well, it's gotta be the money... it's gotta be corruption?".
There are some deep, dark questions of human psychology and sociology at the roots of all this.
The zeal to "other" and hunt the unvaccinated in certain societies, but not so much in others.
posts.key_insight: Denmark's ability to debate vaccine policy openly without tribal condemnation reveals that the politicization of COVID responses in other countries is a cultural and psychological phenomenon, not an inevitable one.

posts.summary: DHH examines an ILSR report revealing that Amazon's average cut from independent retailers has risen from 19% to 30% in five years, mirroring Apple and Google's marketplace fees. He argues this is a universal big tech monopoly pattern: once a company controls a tollbooth that businesses must pass through, it inevitably raises rates and bundles overpriced services. Drawing parallels to Apple's App Store practices and his own experiences with HEY and book sales, he contends that competition cannot function when one player dominates 90% of a market. He concludes that regulatory intervention—utility-like standards and forced spinoffs—is the only viable check on monopoly power.
key_points: Amazon's all-in fees for independent retailers rose from 19% to 30% in five years by tying storage, shipping, and advertising to its platform
Two-thirds of Amazon marketplace sales come from sellers who started in the last three years, because the squeeze churns through businesses faster than they can sustain themselves
The monopoly tollbooth dynamic is identical across Amazon, Apple, and Google—once businesses depend on you, raising rates is just turning a knob
Apple internally explored expanding its 30% cut to physical-goods apps and even considered raising rates to 40%, demonstrating the irresistible pull of monopoly extraction
DHH advocates for public utility-style regulation and mandatory spinoffs of dominant platform divisions
money_quotes: Once you capture a tollbooth like this, and you know the vast majority of those who pass through simply have to pass through, you won't be able to resist hiking rates and expanding the number of businesses who must submit.
There's no easier money made once you have a monopoly tollbooth than to grab a bigger cut for more goods and services from more dependent businesses.
It has none of the complexity or expense involved with speculative innovation, new products, making things. It's simply a knob to turn.

Competition is supposed to keep such wild increases in check! If you don't like it here, you get to go over there. But that basic market mechanism breaks down once someone like Amazon accrues this dominant level of market power.
Gmail's dominance had lead to one of the most important pieces of the internet to be suspended from innovation. Why bother?
If they squeeze hard enough, and they do, eventually there's nothing left by resentment.
Two-thirds of sales on the Amazon marketplace come from sellers who've started in the last three years, because it's difficult to make a long-term business happen on these terms.
posts.key_insight: Big tech monopolies follow an identical, inevitable playbook: once they control a tollbooth businesses must pass through, they relentlessly raise rates and bundle overpriced services, and only regulatory intervention can break the cycle.

posts.summary: DHH announces that HEY, 37signals' email service, has successfully migrated to import maps for JavaScript loading in production. He frames this as a major validation of the bundler-less JavaScript approach that became the default in Rails 7. After months of testing and adaptation—particularly around Turbo Native integration and module loading timing—HEY's nearly 50,000-line codebase with tens of thousands of paying customers runs smoothly without traditional JavaScript bundling. DHH argues this proves import maps work for serious production applications, not just toy projects, and suggests the success is changing minds across the industry.
key_points: HEY has run on import maps in production for two weeks, eliminating an entire class of JavaScript tooling problems
Import maps represent rare 'conceptual compression'—progress that simplifies rather than adds complexity
HEY served as the real-world testbed that validated import maps as the default path for Rails 7
The migration required careful work around module loading timing differences, especially for Turbo Native integration across seven platforms
DHH claims the success is influencing broader industry adoption, potentially accelerating native browser support in Firefox and Safari
money_quotes: Usually progress means more to learn, more to master. It's not often we get to celebrate the progress of true conceptual compression.
An entire class of problems flushed from the daily development experience. WOOSH.
This is the benefit of using new tech in anger: You discover all the gaps between exuberant theory and actual practice.
If we can make HEY work well with import maps, you can make the vast majority of all apps work well with import maps.
I'm not interested in pursuing an approach that only works for some small subsection of modern apps.
Having people realize that the slow boil of complexity they'd been steaming in wasn't inherent to doing great, modern web applications.
Going with import maps by default felt like it might be a huge gamble when I started the work in early 2021.
posts.key_insight: Import maps prove that a decade of JavaScript bundling complexity was never inherent to building modern web applications—it was an accidental burden that can be eliminated entirely.

posts.summary: DHH draws on Matt Taibbi's thesis that the political left has adopted tactics once associated with the right, particularly censorship and moral panic. He focuses on the campaign to remove Joe Rogan from Spotify over his interview with vaccine skeptic Dr. Robert Malone, arguing it mirrors the conservative moral panics of the 80s and 90s. DHH contends that science requires adversarial debate and that platforming controversial voices is a tradition of great interviewers. He notes the irony that the same liberals who mocked conservative censorship campaigns now deploy identical tactics, and predicts the outrage will only boost Rogan's audience, just as moral panics historically amplified what they sought to suppress.
key_points: The political left has adopted censorship tactics (deplatforming, moral outrage) that were once hallmarks of the religious right in the 80s and 90s
Science depends on adversarial debate, and shutting down dissenting voices undermines the scientific process rather than protecting it
Rogan's long-form interview style follows a legitimate tradition of interviewers like William Buckley who let intellectual adversaries present their arguments
Outrage campaigns against content creators function as free advertising, just as moral panics boosted Eminem, NWA, and Dungeons & Dragons
There are signs of pushback against this trend, such as McWhorter's mainstream acceptance and the Virginia political upset
money_quotes: What is science, if not an adversarial process by which we seek an ever-closer understanding of the truth through critical review from all sides?
How can we follow The Science, if we do not subject it to such pressures?
It was not only coming from the other side, but it was laughed at by liberals and progressives as preposterous.
Things have shifted. Drastically and quickly.
I can't help but chuckle at how this outrage is performing the same function for Rogan that picketing parents did for Eminem: MAKE A LOT OF PEOPLE WANT TO LISTEN!
The more you tell people how dangerous this music/game/interview is, the more appealing it'll appear to many. It's basic human nature!
posts.key_insight: The political left has become the new moral majority, deploying the same censorship tactics it once ridiculed the right for using, and with the same counterproductive results.

posts.summary: DHH examines a viral anonymous Twitter thread by 'Hazard Harrington' claiming to expose woke excesses at a Big Tech company, using it as a case study in motivated reasoning. Rather than taking sides on the thread's veracity, he points out that people become strikingly gullible when stories confirm their existing beliefs. He draws a parallel to the debunked Oklahoma ivermectin hospital story to show this tendency cuts across political lines. He recommends Julia Galef's 'The Scout Mindset' as a framework for recognizing when you're defending existing beliefs versus genuinely seeking truth.
key_points: Anonymous, unverifiable accounts go viral because they confirm what people already want to believe, regardless of political orientation
Motivated reasoning makes people strikingly gullible when a story fits their preferred narrative
Julia Galef's Scout vs Soldier framework offers a useful tool for intellectual self-examination
The inability to verify anonymous claims should make us skeptical regardless of which side the claims support
Personal intellectual growth comes from embracing the scout mindset and welcoming being wrong
money_quotes: When a story fits a contentious narrative someone already believes, they tend to become strikingly gullible. There's a name for this tendency: motivated reasoning.
This is an anonymous account of an unnamed company detailing personal but unverifiable experiences.
Could be true applies to all manner of accounts that turn out not to be.
Am I being a scout or a soldier right now? That's something you can ask yourself when you watch your own reaction.
I too am liable to fall into soldier thinking, but my most satisfying intellectual moments are when the inner scout is in charge.
posts.key_insight: Before accepting or rejecting any viral anonymous account, ask yourself whether you're evaluating the evidence like a scout seeking truth or a soldier defending what you already believe.

posts.summary: DHH traces Apple's escalating contempt for legislative oversight of its App Store monopoly, from relatively sober technical testimony in North Dakota to outright fearmongering about malware affecting 'millions of Americans' in its letter to Congress. He highlights how Apple and Google have made a mockery of laws passed in South Korea and the Netherlands by technically complying while maintaining their extractive fees under different names. The post argues that this brazen defiance of democratic institutions is a classic warning sign of unchecked monopoly power capturing not just economics but politics itself. DHH concludes with cautious optimism that the escalating contempt signals the fight is nearing a turning point.
key_points: Apple's anti-regulation arguments have escalated from technical privacy claims to outright fearmongering about millions of malware victims
In South Korea and the Netherlands, Apple technically complied with new laws while gutting their intent by renaming fees and retaining control
Monopoly power that can mock democratic institutions has metastasized beyond economic capture into political capture
Apple and Google successfully bullied state legislatures in North Dakota and Arizona into backing down through lobbying
The escalating contempt paradoxically signals the fight is progressing, like harder bosses in a video game meaning you're closer to the end
money_quotes: Now that Apple is facing a concrete antitrust bill in Congress, they've sent their biggest baller of bullshit yet to dribble with the legislature.

Millions of Americans are going to be subject to malware if the iPhone must work like the Mac? Allowing the buyers of thousand-dollar smartphones to install the software of their choice, like they do on their laptop and desktop computers, would be a novel catastrophe of computing?
It's a clear sign that monopoly power has metastasized when it feels this confident in its ability to mock and embarrass the democratic institutions that it in theory must answer to.

The practice of trillion-dollar might is that it'll yield political rights too. Which of course is the classical warning about letting monopoly power grow unchecked: Eventually it's not just about the capture of economics, but soon too the capture of politics.
As with oil, railroads, trusts, and telecoms, the fight against monopoly capture is the fight against a many-headed hydra.
Developers extraordinarily end up worse than where they were, if they try to take advantage of the statutory relief they were granted by lawmakers and regulators.
But as with any video game, when the bosses get harder, it means you're closer to the end.
posts.key_insight: Apple's escalating contempt for antitrust regulation — from sober testimony to brazen fearmongering and malicious compliance — reveals how unchecked monopoly power inevitably captures not just markets but democratic institutions themselves.

posts.summary: Google is ending free Gmail on custom domains, now charging $500+/year for what was previously free. DHH argues that while charging for email is reasonable, the 'switcheroo' of offering something free to build lock-in and then demanding payment is manipulative. The original 'free' service was never truly free—users paid with their data and attention. He advocates for paying with actual money from the start rather than accepting free services that inevitably come with hidden costs.
key_points: Google's free Gmail on custom domains killed email innovation by undercutting paid competitors, then reversed course to charge $500+/year
The 'free' service was never truly free—users paid through data harvesting, purchase receipt tracking, and promoted emails
The bait-and-switch feels especially predatory from a monopolist who used free services to capture dominant market share
Users with a decade of locked-in data have little leverage when the price suddenly changes
Paying with real money from the start is more honest and gives consumers actual voting power over what survives
money_quotes: When you give something away for free for years, and then suddenly show up with a demand for payment, people are rightfully going to feel like it's a stickup.
It wasn't ever free FREE, of course, but the price in data and attention wasn't obvious to most before they got hooked.
Oh, we were just the stepping stone for you to get the dominant marketshare, and now that you've captured it, you return for the loot?
The long deep freeze of innovation in email came as a result of Google giving away free service.
Email wasn't ever free to offer. Especially not the unlimited kind with gigabytes of storage that gave Gmail its original splash.
How's that for a New Year's resolution: Paying for things with money, and skipping the ones that aren't worth it.
posts.key_insight: Free services from monopolists are a long con—they destroy competition, create lock-in, and eventually extract payment from users who have no easy way out.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the Mac's decades-long track record of safe software installation outside the App Store completely undermines Apple's claims that opening the iPhone to sideloading would cause security catastrophes. He points out that Apple simultaneously brags about Mac security while lobbying Congress that the same open model would be dangerous on iPhone. The post systematically dismantles Apple's three main counterarguments: that iPhones hold more sensitive data, that phone users are less sophisticated, and that iPhones aren't really computers. DHH contends that the iPhone's technical security measures (sandboxing, kill switches) already provide stronger protection than the Mac, and that App Store review exists to protect Apple's revenue stream, not users. He concludes that Apple resorts to security arguments only because defending a 30% tax on a trillion-dollar monopoly would generate zero public sympathy.
key_points: The Mac has nearly forty years of proof that computers don't need an App Store to be safe, undermining Apple's iPhone sideloading security claims
Apple's ecosystem syncs all data across devices, so the argument that iPhones hold more sensitive data than Macs is contradictory
App Store reviewers lack technical security qualifications because their real purpose is protecting Apple's revenue, not user security
The iPhone already has stronger technical security measures than the Mac through tighter sandboxing and more locked-down architecture
Apple's security argument is the only one available because openly defending monopoly rents from a trillion-dollar company would be indefensible
money_quotes: With the Mac, we have almost forty years of proof that computers don't need an App Store to be safe.
Apple continues to brag about this solid security record for the Mac when they try to sell you a new computer while their lobbyists argue the opposite on Capitol Hill.
This cognitive dissonance forces them into some bizarre logical contortions as to why this proven model of choice can't work for the iPhone.
Who are these unsophisticated rubes who'll be exploited if they're given access to a platform with the same application installation process as every other computer ever made?
The only thing these technical defenses can't guard against is business model threats.
They're not there to uncover security threats, only threats to the faucet of monopoly rents.
Even when they fail to detect a scam, Apple still takes a cut. It's win-win for Apple, lose-lose for consumers and developers.
Trillion-dollar monopolists just don't garner support from a naked attempt to pick the pockets of independent developers.
posts.key_insight: Apple's own Mac platform, with its decades of safe open software installation, is the strongest evidence against Apple's claim that iPhone sideloading would be a security disaster.

posts.summary: DHH criticizes Apple for refusing to comply with Dutch competition authorities' requirements on App Store policies, choosing instead to absorb a €5 million weekly fine. He argues that fines are ineffective against trillion-dollar companies because they simply become a cost-benefit calculation. The post frames Apple's defiance as a form of business crime where the penalty is too small to deter the behavior. DHH suggests that while Dutch authorities face an impossible task fining a company that makes a billion dollars daily, Apple's contemptuous stance may provoke a much larger response from the European Union.
key_points: Apple chose to ignore Dutch requirements allowing dating apps to link to external payment methods, treating the fine as an acceptable cost
Fines become meaningless deterrents when they're trivial relative to a company's revenue — €50 million is spare change for a company earning $1 billion per day
There's a perverse rational calculus where breaking the law is the economically optimal choice when fines are smaller than the profits from violations
National regulators are structurally outmatched when dealing with trillion-dollar global companies
Apple's defiance of Dutch sovereignty may trigger a larger EU-level regulatory response
money_quotes: This is just the cost of doing business [crimes]!

They put a price on criminal behavior, and if the gains from committing those crimes exceed the cost of the fines, there's a base hyper-rational calculus that says the company should continue to break the law.
The normal rules of commerce, competition, and regulation just don't apply when you're dealing with a trillion-dollar global behemoth like Apple.
€50 million is "spare-change-under-the-sofa" money for Apple, a company that literally makes a billion dollars per day in revenue.
Fines should be sufficient to deter violations (and these surely are not).
If Apple willfully decides to flip off the Dutch and their national sovereignty, I think we might just see the leviathan that is the European Union awaken.
posts.key_insight: When fines for anticompetitive behavior are trivially small relative to a company's revenue, they don't deter violations — they just put a price tag on breaking the law.

posts.summary: DHH reports that Denmark is lifting virtually all pandemic restrictions despite record-high infection rates, because ICU admissions due to Covid have plummeted and the virus is no longer considered a threat to societal functioning. He contrasts Denmark's approach — keeping schools open, avoiding child mask mandates, tolerating vaccine dissent without tribal warfare — with America's more restrictive and divisive handling. He praises Danish society for factoring in children's mental well-being and learning, rejecting safetyism, and showing institutional courage to reverse mandates when data changed. The post argues that Denmark's model of accepting risk while maintaining social cohesion is what many Americans now want.
key_points: Denmark lifted all restrictions despite all-time record infection rates because ICU admissions from Covid had plummeted
Danish schools stayed mostly open for young children throughout the pandemic, without mask mandates for kids or teachers
A majority of Danish parents chose not to vaccinate children aged 5-11, against official recommendations, without social ostracism
Denmark maintained open debate about pandemic trade-offs without it devolving into tribal warfare
The institutional willingness to reverse mandates when data showed vaccines barely protected against spread is presented as a model of good governance
money_quotes: What's striking about this decision is that it's been taken against a backdrop of all-time record infection rates.
An ever-growing share of those in the hospitals with Covid are not there because of Covid.

To live in a society that allows a debate broad enough to factor in the mental well-being of kids, their in-class/no-mask learning opportunities, and even dissent against official recommendations on vaccinations for young kids. Without any of that turning into a tribal fight.
A society that has largely rejected safetyism, and accepted that an open society will mean deaths and long Covid and other regrettable consequences. That we can't live in lockdown forever.

The institutional courage to back down from mandates and restrictions that made sense when we thought vaccines protected against spread, and to change the approach after the data now show it barely does.
The patience and restraint to give those who were offered a vaccine but declined a dignified way to continue to live, work, and interact in society.
posts.key_insight: Denmark's willingness to lift all pandemic restrictions despite record infections — by distinguishing between infection rates and actual societal threat — represents a model of evidence-based governance and social cohesion that America's tribal approach failed to achieve.

posts.summary: DHH argues that critics demanding Spotify cancel Joe Rogan over COVID-related content operate under a flawed assumption: that Spotify secretly agrees Rogan is harmful but keeps him out of fear. He contends this is a moral blindness that prevents people from considering Spotify might genuinely disagree with the premise. DHH frames cancellation campaigns as counterproductive religious rituals performed by loud minorities, comparing the Rogan situation to the failed attempt to cancel Dave Chappelle at Netflix. He argues these failed campaigns actually strengthen their targets through the Streisand effect while weakening future cancellation attempts.
key_points: Critics assume Spotify must agree Rogan is harmful, unable to conceive of genuine disagreement with that premise
Cancellation campaigns are driven by small, loud minorities with inflated sense of purpose, fueled by moral righteousness
Failed cancellation attempts like Chappelle and Rogan weaken future efforts against less resilient targets — a basic strategic blunder
These campaigns function more as religious rituals to bless the condemners than as political movements seeking practical victories
The Streisand effect turns each failed cancellation into free marketing, drawing new audiences to the targeted figure
money_quotes: The only reason they're not yanking him is fear. Fear of losing subscribers, money, marketshare, whatever. It can't possibly be due to a genuine disagreement with the menace-to-society charge.
What we can't beat with compelling counter speech, we will threaten, extort, or slander our way to exorcising!
The 'we' in this sense is virtually always a small, loud minority with a completely inflated sense of purpose and power.

If you're going to take on the number one music or video streaming service together with the number one podcaster or comedian, you damn well better come packing more heat than I Don't Like What They're Saying!
Every loss you take in the forum of public opinion sets you up worse for the next attempt.

These cancelation attempts are not the product of political movements trying to mobilize mass coalitions on the basis of compelling shared goals. They're religious incantations meant to bless those who condemn.
Every swing at the king that misses only serves to highlight his glory.
posts.key_insight: Cancellation campaigns against major figures are self-defeating religious rituals that strengthen their targets through the Streisand effect while eroding the credibility of future attempts.

posts.summary: DHH argues that social media platforms have engineered away all friction from human interaction, creating a toxic environment where every fleeting impulse gets broadcast directly into others' consciousness. After hitting a personal breaking point on Twitter in April 2021, he quit engaging with strangers there and replaced it with friction-mediated connections: email newsletters and open source collaboration on GitHub. He found that the friction inherent in these older internet formats serves as a high-grade filter, producing authentic, good-faith exchanges. He questions whether a social media platform designed around friction could ever compete with frictionless alternatives, while concluding that friction is essential for healthy human connection.
key_points: Social media has systematically removed all friction between emotional impulse and public expression, creating a toxic feedback loop that degrades discourse
After quitting Twitter engagement, DHH found that email and open source collaboration restore faith in stranger-to-stranger connection because friction filters out the frivolous and malevolent
Friction is a feature, not a bug — historically, the effort required to contact someone served as a natural quality filter on communication
Tinder's two-way matching and HEY's Screener are examples of products that embrace friction as a design principle
Content moderation and fact-checking have failed as solutions; the answer lies in redesigning how connections are initiated
money_quotes: The distance from emotion to reaction has been whittled away one A/B test at the time, and there's barely any left.
We need others enough that even if they become these warped beings, pulled apart by the black hole, they're still preferable to the cold emptiness of being alone.
Giving voice to every fleeting emotion or thought is a pathology. Listening to all these voices sure to eventually develop into one.
Friction is freedom.
Whittling it all away such that every little impulse of every little person is connected to an outlet of expression is perhaps the worst crime committed by the modern internet.
Democratizing the power to force your way into someone's head is intoxicating.
We really are all better off when creating a connection to another human being is gated by a modicum of friction.
Work can unite what politics so often divide.
posts.key_insight: Friction in communication is not an obstacle to overcome but a vital filter that produces authentic human connection, and social media's relentless removal of it has degraded public discourse.

posts.summary: DHH praises Spotify's COVID content moderation policy for its clarity and narrow focus on genuinely dangerous claims, contrasting it with vague 'misinformation' labels favored by critics. He argues that The Verge's frustration with the policy reveals a desire for broader censorship rather than reasonable guardrails. He supports his position with concrete examples: AstraZeneca vaccine deaths in Denmark and Norway, evolving mask guidance, and the premature banning of the lab leak theory. He concludes that open discussion of legitimate health concerns, even uncomfortable ones, serves the public better than suppression.
key_points: Spotify's moderation policy is effective because it focuses on concrete, falsifiable claims rather than subjective 'misinformation' labels
Critics are frustrated precisely because the policy's clarity makes it hard to stretch into broader censorship
Real-world examples from Denmark and Norway show that vaccines did cause rare deaths, making blanket bans on such discussion factually unjustifiable
The premature banning of the lab leak theory on social media demonstrates the danger of vague content moderation
Open public debate about health risks, as happened in Denmark with AstraZeneca, ultimately serves public trust better than suppression
money_quotes: What an impressively clear and concise policy! Focused primarily on the concrete "imminent threat" principle. Thus making it clear whether a violation has happened or not.
Terms that have been stretched so far that all manner of reasonable speech can be included when it serves the political mood or moment.
First, vaccines have caused deaths!
It didn't turn out well when the lab leak theory was banned from social media, only to be allowed once it became clear that the "conspiracy theory" label was applied prematurely.
Pushing on by downplaying these deaths might well have harmed the overall trust in vaccines even more.
Would Danes really have been better off if the discussion of rare but real vaccine deaths were banned from the media? That seems very hard to believe.
posts.key_insight: Content moderation policies work best when they target specific, falsifiable dangerous claims rather than relying on subjective labels like 'misinformation' that can be weaponized to suppress legitimate debate.

posts.summary: DHH argues that content moderation based on 'truth' is fundamentally impossible because truth is always in dispute at the edges of discourse. He distinguishes between moderating direct threats and trying to police 'misinformation,' calling the latter a form of censorship. He criticizes the notion that social media platforms can serve as arbiters of truth, pointing out that even experts frequently get things wrong. The post concludes that the fluidity of truth should make everyone hesitant to grant censorship powers to any authority, since those powers can easily change hands.
key_points: Truth at the vanguard is always in dispute and emerges faster when opposing inquiries are pursued simultaneously
Moderating threats and doxxing is feasible, but conflating that with policing 'misinformation' is a rhetorical sleight of hand
Platform companies cannot be trusted as oracles of truth — the machinery of censorship easily changes hands
The distinction between 'I think this is wrong' and 'nobody should be allowed to hear it' is the line between discourse and censorship
Recent years of rapidly shifting consensus should have made people more cautious about demanding platform intervention, not less
money_quotes: Moderating content on the basis of "truth" is an impossible task at all but the dullest edges of discourse.
The vanguard of truth is always in dispute. Out there on the edge, truth is a process, and it emerges faster when opposing inquiries are pursued simultaneously.
Even our best and brightest can't call it consistently (and those asked to make the calls at these platforms are rarely our best or our brightest).
A gang of private social media companies endowed with the ultimate authority to sift truth from trash.
Don't build censorship regimes that you wouldn't want your worst political enemy to control.
Note the difference between "I think this is wrong, and you shouldn't listen to it" and "I think this is wrong, AND NOBODY SHOULD BE ALLOWED TO HEAR IT".
It's not a crime to be wrong. You're wrong all the time about all sorts of things.

There's no amount of subservient tribute we can pay to the authorities of today to change this. They will never become all-knowing, all-seeing oracles that we can entrust with the final arbitration of the truth.
posts.key_insight: No platform or authority can reliably arbitrate truth, so building censorship infrastructure in pursuit of that goal is both futile and dangerous — the machinery will inevitably change hands.

posts.summary: DHH comments on the backlash against Joe Rogan's podcast popularity, finding amusement in critics who are infuriated that Rogan's casual, unscripted approach commands such a massive audience. He argues that Rogan's spontaneity is precisely what makes the show appealing, not a deficiency. DHH contends that critics confuse effort with value, a novice mistake. Listeners choose Rogan freely in an open market of infinite podcast options, and their preference for authentic conversation over polished preparation is a legitimate choice.
key_points: Rogan's off-the-cuff spontaneity is the core appeal of his show, not a flaw to be corrected
Critics who prepare more but attract fewer listeners are making the novice mistake of conflating effort with value
Podcast listeners operate in a near-perfect market of choice with unlimited alternatives, making Rogan's popularity a genuine signal
There is a meaningful distinction between conversation and deposition — both have their place, but audiences clearly prefer the former at scale
money_quotes: This is the confusion of the novice. Conflating effort with value.
Nobody is listening to your show because of how much effort you put into it. They listen because they find it funny, interesting, illuminating, or otherwise valuable.
Sometimes those things come as a product of studious effort, but other times they do not.
Millions of listeners have chosen that they like these conversations. Not in spite of the off-the-cuff approach, but because of it.
They're voting with their attention in an almost perfect market of choice.
This is a spontaneity that relies on intuition, flow, and open mindedness. Not studious preparation. And herein lies the crime.
posts.key_insight: Audiences reward authenticity and spontaneity, not preparation effort — conflating hard work with value is a novice mistake that explains why polished but sterile content loses to genuine conversation.

posts.summary: DHH argues that collaborative work is one of the most powerful unifying forces in society, bringing together people of diverse backgrounds around shared creative purpose. He contends that focusing on what people contribute to a task rather than their beliefs or political affiliations enables broader collaboration. He invokes Mark Twain's etiquette about avoiding politics in polite company, noting that social media has made it harder to remain ignorant of colleagues' political and religious views. He concludes that deliberately choosing not to know—or to forget—others' political stances is worth the effort to preserve the creative, unifying power of work.
key_points: Collaborative work unites people by focusing on shared purpose and mastery rather than beliefs or identity
Politeness and deliberate avoidance of divisive topics oils the gears of productive collaboration
Social media has destroyed the natural separation between personal beliefs and professional collaboration
Choosing ignorance of colleagues' political views is a deliberate, worthwhile act that preserves creative unity
money_quotes: Is there a more powerful, communal force than making stuff together?
It pulls the shared humanity right out of people when they collaborate on making something greater than what they could make themselves.
When you're focused on making, you focus on what people bring to the task, not what they believe, how they pray, or which political sect they belong to.
It's the politeness that oils the gears of collaboration.
All this used to be so much easier, because we never had to know.
Today social media has spoiled that for millions.
But it's worth taking the effort not to know, and if you do, to forget.
posts.key_insight: Work's power to unite people across divides depends on deliberately keeping politics out of professional collaboration—a discipline that social media has made harder but more important than ever.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how social media produces stress even without active participation. After years as an 'active combatant' in online arguments, he retired from engaging but discovered that passive scrolling still triggers a stress response. He argues that taking a break and returning makes the toxicity shockingly apparent, and that the real danger is the tolerance we build up that masks the constant cortisol exposure. He compares second-hand social media stress to second-hand smoke in its potential health impact.
key_points: Passive social media consumption produces stress responses similar to active engagement and arguments
Taking a break from social media reveals how much tolerance you've built up to toxic content, and returning is a shock to the system
The tolerance we build to social media stress is the real problem because it masks ongoing physiological damage
Second-hand social media stress may be comparable to or worse than second-hand smoke exposure in developed countries
money_quotes: I'd been an active combatant in the arena of social media for so long that I thought the baseline stress it produced was mainly due to the direct involvement.

The tolerance you built up from years of exposure to these rivers of unhappy, resentful, angry exchanges wanes remarkably quickly, and when you throw yourself back in without that tolerance, it's a shock to the system.
That shock isn't the problem, though. It's the solution. It's your body telling you just how unhealthy soaking your mind in these flows actually is.
The problem is the tolerance because it allows you to cope with the constant exposure.
I wouldn't be surprised if the effects of second-hand stress from social media is worse than the second-hand stress from smoking in many developed countries.
posts.key_insight: The tolerance we build to social media's constant stream of negativity is more dangerous than the stress itself, because it masks the ongoing physiological damage of chronic cortisol exposure.

posts.summary: Apple responded to a €5 million Dutch antitrust fine by creating an alternative payment framework for dating apps that DHH argues is even more punitive than the original system. The new scheme still charges 27% commission, demands monthly sales reports, grants Apple audit rights over developers' books, and threatens App Store removal for non-compliance. DHH argues this isn't just Apple giving Dutch regulators the middle finger—it's a roadmap for extending this tax regime globally to any business with an app. He envisions Apple potentially applying this coercive reporting-and-invoice model to all apps worldwide, extracting 27% of economic activity from companies like Facebook under threat of platform expulsion.
key_points: Apple's 'compliance' with the Dutch order still charges 27% commission while adding burdensome reporting requirements, audit rights, and threatening punishments
The framework reveals Apple doesn't need technical payment integration to extract its tax—coerced reporting and monthly invoices work just as well
The audit rights and App Store expulsion threats create a coercive regime that no rational developer would voluntarily adopt
This Dutch-specific response could serve as a template for Apple to extend its commission model globally to all app-dependent businesses
The potential scope is staggering—if applied broadly, Apple could invoice companies like Facebook for 27% of all iPhone-derived economic activity
money_quotes: Apple has revealed an even more draconian, invasive, and frankly, terrifying scheme to collect their App Store tax from dating-app developers operating in The Netherlands.
By granting themselves audit rights 'pursuant to the entitlement's terms and conditions', they've shown how they can apply their 27% tax to any developer's economic activity at will.
Under the threat of App Store expulsion – with the subsequent ruin of any platform-dependent business – they don't even need a technical solution to collection.
Coerced reporting requirements together with monthly invoices might be old school, but it'll work just fine.
It's a small hop from there to amending the App Store guidelines globally such that this new tax regime will apply to all apps, from all businesses.
Apple has, perhaps inadvertently, just revealed to the world how they might go from a three-trillion dollar company to a hundred-trillion dollar company.
Just send every business dependent on an app a demand to audit their books, then mail them an invoice for 27% of their economic activity.
posts.key_insight: Apple's malicious compliance with Dutch antitrust orders inadvertently reveals a scalable blueprint for taxing 27% of all app-dependent economic activity worldwide through coerced reporting and platform expulsion threats.

posts.summary: DHH argues that GoFundMe's decision to block and redirect donations intended for protesting Canadian truckers is a dangerous politicization of financial platforms. He details how GoFundMe first attempted to reroute funds to other charities, then settled on blocking donations with delayed refunds, all at the behest of Ottawa police without a court order. He draws parallels to government pressure on tech platforms for censorship and contrasts the treatment with how BLM fundraising was handled. Despite being a longtime crypto skeptic, he concedes that such actions make a compelling case for apolitical money transfer protocols.
key_points: GoFundMe acted as a voluntary enforcement arm of local government by blocking trucker protest donations without a court order
The selective application of financial platform power reveals dangerous political bias — contrasting treatment of trucker donations vs. BLM fundraising
Government circumventing constitutional protections by pressuring private companies to act on their behalf is a growing pattern
Even a crypto skeptic can see the case for apolitical financial protocols when existing channels are vulnerable to political capture
Expanding terms like 'domestic terrorists' to broader groups enables sanctions originally meant for extreme cases to be applied to ordinary political activity
money_quotes: You couldn't have planned a better advertising campaign for crypto than Gofundme's ham-fisted cancelation of donations intended for the protesting Canadian truckers.
Having the freedom to support political organizations of your choice with your own money seems like a fundamental one in a democracy.
Actions like these from Gofundme make a very strong case for why we need an apolitical protocol of money transfer when the existing channels are so liable to petty political capture.
You don't have to agree with any of these causes or organizations to understand that politicizing our basic platforms for charity or finance is a terrible idea.
It's one thing to take down someone's words, quite another to take their money.
The government can't circumvent the 1st amendment by having private actors carry out the censorship on their behalf.
This is the slope we're sliding down when terms like 'domestic terrorists' are expanded to include Canadian truckers and parents at school board meetings.
posts.key_insight: When financial platforms become willing enforcers of political agendas without court orders, even crypto skeptics must acknowledge the need for apolitical money transfer systems.

posts.summary: DHH defends the Canadian trucker convoy's protest against vaccine mandates, arguing their peaceful demonstration is a legitimate exercise of democratic rights. He criticizes progressive commentators for condemning peaceful truckers while having cheered violent 2020 protests, and accuses Canadian politicians of refusing to engage with the substantive argument. He points to Denmark's approach of rejecting vaccine mandates as evidence the truckers' position is reasonable. He argues that efforts to cut off donations, seize fuel, and label protesters as extremists are designed to provoke escalation that would justify a crackdown.
key_points: Progressive commentators are hypocritical for condemning peaceful trucker protests while having supported violent 2020 protests
Canadian politicians refuse to engage with the merits of the anti-mandate argument, instead resorting to labels like white supremacist and insurrectionist
Denmark's health authority rejected vaccine mandates, showing the truckers' position aligns with mainstream international policy
Efforts to cut off donations and fuel appear designed to provoke desperation and escalation to justify a crackdown
Over 90% of Canadian truckers are already vaccinated, making the mandate both punitive and ineffective given omicron's spread patterns
money_quotes: How dare these truckers continue to exercise their basic political rights to freedom of assembly, association, and speech?!
How does one square the condemnation of peaceful political protests on the one hand with cheering on violent political protests on the other? By any means necessary, of course!
What's fascinating about the Canadian trucker protest is the unwillingness of politicians to engage with the merits of the argument.
Forcing vaccines upon truckers needing to cross the border for work is a gross overreach of state power, and utterly unwarranted by the state of the pandemic.
Without support to sustain the peaceful protests, maybe the truckers will turn desperate, and we can have the escalation needed to justify the hammer?
The right to peaceful protest does not have an expiration date.
A healthy democracy will engage with protesters to hear their claims and consider their voice within the guardrails of peaceful expression.
posts.key_insight: Democratic governments that refuse to engage with the substance of peaceful protests and instead try to delegitimize protesters through labels and resource deprivation are undermining the very democratic norms they claim to defend.

posts.summary: DHH reports on Denmark's experience two weeks after dropping all COVID restrictions, arguing it validates the decision. Despite high infection rates and 30% test positivity, Danish society has moved on, with health authorities viewing Omicron as a blessing that builds natural immunity. He contrasts Denmark's approach favorably against countries like the US that maintain restrictions, particularly around children in schools. He acknowledges uncertainties about future variants but argues Denmark is responding to current science rather than fear.
key_points: Denmark dropped all COVID restrictions and society functioned normally despite high Omicron infection rates, with ICU admissions and deaths-from-COVID remaining stable

Danish health authorities reconsidered vaccinating young children and acknowledged that vaccinated and unvaccinated contract Omicron equally, reducing the vaccine argument to individual protection against serious illness
The distinction between hospitalized-with-COVID versus hospitalized-because-of-COVID is critical and was being misread by international commentators
Other countries ignored Denmark's data-rich example, much like they ignored Italy's early outbreak data at the pandemic's start
US restrictions on children, including school masking and outdoor distanced eating even after vaccination, are contrasted unfavorably with Denmark's lighter approach
money_quotes: Omicron is basically heralded as a blessing. The ticket out of the pandemic.

Now that it's clear that vaccinated and unvaccinated people contract the virus in equal proportion, the pro-vaccine argument is reduced to the fact that it offers good protection for adult individuals against serious illness.

The facts that are emerging from that intense level of inquiry into the virus show clearly that omicron's spread is not mitigated by vaccinations, that the spread is unstoppable outside of extreme, draconian lockdowns.
On a broad, societal level we no longer need to fear this variant to a degree that it dictates how we live.
They've chosen to react to The Science of today, and have stopped fearing omicron as a disease that requires society-wide restrictions, and get on with life.
At the fear of hubris, it seems the future of the pandemic is already here. It's just not evenly distributed.
posts.key_insight: Denmark's data-driven decision to drop all COVID restrictions amid Omicron proved successful, exposing other countries' continued restrictions as driven more by fear than by science.

posts.summary: DHH introduces Propshaft, a new asset pipeline library for Rails that replaces the aging Sprockets with a dramatically simpler alternative. Made possible by HTTP/2, browser-native ES6, and import maps, Propshaft handles only what's needed in modern web development: configurable load paths, digest stamping, and URL rewriting. Sprockets grew large trying to handle transpiling, bundling, minifying, and compression all by itself, but modern standalone tools now handle those responsibilities. HEY has already shipped to production with Propshaft, though it won't become the Rails default until at least Rails 8 due to gem ecosystem dependencies on Sprockets.
key_points: Propshaft is enabled by the same trifecta powering Rails 7: HTTP/2, browser-run ES6, and import maps
Unlike Sprockets, Propshaft only handles load paths, digest stamping, and URL rewriting — leaving transpiling and bundling to standalone tools like esbuild, Dart Sass, and Tailwind CSS
Sprockets was forged for HTTP/1 and ES3 constraints that no longer apply, accumulating unused pipelines and underdeveloped features
HEY is already running Propshaft in production, but the default switch from Sprockets won't happen until Rails 8 due to gem ecosystem dependencies
Simplifying one part of Rails (removing Webpacker) cascaded into simplifying another (replacing Sprockets with Propshaft)
money_quotes: After a decade's worth of front-end progress kept demanding ever more complicated setups, we're finally moving in the opposite direction.
Like with expanding complexity, where one complication often leads to another, compressing complexity also cascades.
It was written on a clean sheet of paper to carry only what we need in the year of our lord 2022.

It's hard to keep up with the state of the art on all these fronts at once, and while valiant attempts were made, it kept falling behind on everything from Sass compilation to understanding ESM dependencies.
When we manage to make one aspect of Rails dramatically simpler, we can often make another part much simpler too.
The battle against complexity in web development is a constant tug of war. We give a little to get something new, we take it back to make it simpler.
Progress is good. Complexity is a bridge. Simplicity is the destination.
posts.key_insight: Modern browser capabilities and standalone tooling have made the monolithic asset pipeline obsolete, enabling Rails to replace Sprockets with a dramatically simpler library that only handles what the ecosystem no longer provides.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how the medium of communication fundamentally shapes the quality of debate, drawing from his recent email exchanges about the Canadian trucker protests. He observes that the same people who would be hostile on Twitter engage in civil, good-faith discussions over email or in person. He attributes this to Twitter's algorithmic optimization and audience dynamics that reward aggression, while one-on-one formats self-correct toward civility. Rather than believing humanity is broken, he argues we're trapped in bad platforms producing bad outcomes, and a change of venue can restore productive discourse.
key_points: The same people behave completely differently on Twitter versus email or in-person — the platform shapes the behavior, not the person
Twitter's audience dynamics and algorithmic optimization incentivize hostility by providing dopamine reinforcement for dunks and attacks
One-on-one communication is self-correcting: hostile tactics cause people to simply stop replying, with no audience to cheer the aggression
