The current online political climate obsessively polices who can speak about what, which may be the natural end state of social media as designed
Good-faith debates outside toxic platforms act as seeds that change your perspective of what happens inside them
money_quotes: It's not that there's a new subspecies of homo sapiens inhabiting Twitter, and then another living on email. It's the same people.
We are currently trapped in some bad platforms producing bad outcomes, but the people participating often just need a change of venue for the dark clouds to clear.
You start hurling Twitter dunks at someone on email, and they'll just stop replying. There's no audience to cheer the attack, just the silence of a cut connection, and thus no dopamine reinforcement.
The bigger the topic, the more exhilarating a good-faith debate can be.

Eventually the algorithmic optimization will optimize us all to be these resentful, angry people online. Not even through executive malice, but because these systems run on autopilot when exploiting our all too human flaws.
Every such exchange is a seed planted in your heart. Who knows what'll grow over time.
posts.key_insight: The toxicity of online discourse is a product of platform design, not human nature — the same people who are hostile on Twitter become civil and curious when the venue changes to email or in-person conversation.

posts.summary: DHH draws a parallel between Apple's current adversarial relationship with developers and Microsoft's dominance in the 1990s. He recounts how developers fled Microsoft for Apple's OS X in the early 2000s, seeing it as liberation from a bullying monopolist. Now Apple has become the same kind of villain, fighting developers in court and defying regulators to protect its app store monopoly. With antitrust actions mounting worldwide, DHH argues a tectonic shift may be coming that lets developers finally break free from Apple, just as the web and OS X freed them from Microsoft.
key_points: Developer goodwill is a critical but unmeasurable asset that Apple is systematically destroying through legal aggression and regulatory defiance
Apple's trajectory mirrors Microsoft's 1990s monopoly playbook, but Apple is behaving even more ruthlessly than Microsoft did
The early 2000s switch from Microsoft to Apple felt like rebellion and release, and a similar shift away from Apple may be imminent
Global antitrust momentum against Apple far exceeds what Microsoft faced from the DOJ, but Apple is also a larger adversary
Emerging platforms like web3 or a Microsoft comeback could provide the escape hatch developers are waiting for
money_quotes: There's nothing symbiotic about Apple's current relationship with its developers.

They can win every battle in court, lobby around every hearing in the house, and pay all the trifling regulatory fines, and still ultimately end up losing something far more long-term important than a monopoly tax rake.
I guess you either die a hero (salute Commodore) or you live long enough to become the villain (hello Apple).
Apple has planted all the same seeds of discord with developers as Microsoft did in the 90s. But somehow even more ruthlessly and greedy than the boss that came before it.
It felt like a release and a rebellion at once. A way to finally put action behind all those years of gritted teeth. A way to finally say: Fuck you, Microsoft.

What's the goodwill of developers worth to a platform operator? It's a hard question, because it can't be answered by the traditional economic models used by bean counters to populate the balance sheet.
And when that happens, if it happens, there'll be thousands upon thousands of developers ready. With their gritted teeth. Who had been bidding their time.
posts.key_insight: Platform monopolists inevitably alienate the developers who made them powerful, and Apple is repeating Microsoft's fatal mistake of prioritizing control over the symbiotic relationship that sustains its ecosystem.

posts.summary: DHH reverses his long-held skepticism of cryptocurrency after watching the Canadian government invoke emergency powers to freeze bank accounts of trucker protest donors. He acknowledges all of crypto's real problems — energy consumption, fraud, lack of decentralization — but argues the Canadian response proved that even stable Western democracies can weaponize financial systems against peaceful protesters. He compares the shock to the Snowden revelations: unbelievable until undeniable. The fundamental freedom to transact, he concludes, is worth protecting even if the current crypto ecosystem is deeply flawed.
key_points: Canada's use of emergency powers to freeze bank accounts of protest donors demonstrated that financial authoritarianism isn't limited to failing states
DHH's prior dismissal of crypto's relevance to Western democracies was fueled by a lack of imagination about how governments could weaponize banking
The fact that this authoritarianism came from Canada rather than Trump's America makes it harder for Westerners to dismiss as an anomaly
The situation parallels the Snowden revelations — seeming hyperbolic until proven undeniable
Crypto's many real flaws don't negate its fundamental value as protection for the freedom to transact
money_quotes: Just because Bitcoin's most virtuous argument was presented – in if not bad-faith then in fig-leaf-faith – by get-rich-quick boosters, doesn't mean it isn't true!
I still can't believe that this is the protest that would prove every Bitcoin crank a prophet.
You might as well have told me that it was really the Care Bears who ran Abu Ghraib.
This is crazy. Absolutely bonkers. Terrifying.

I'm sitting with that same feeling here. Even just a few months ago, I would not have found it credible if you said a three-week peaceful protest in Canada could have lead to martial law, frozen bank accounts, and terrorist-financing laws being used to hunt protest donors.
I don't think we have any idea what kind of radicalizing seeds have been planted by Trudeau with these actions.
In a weird way, I'm glad we all got this warning from Trudeau in Canada and not Trump in America.
You don't need laser eyes or an NFT avatar to appreciate that.
posts.key_insight: The Canadian government's use of emergency financial powers against peaceful protesters proved that the freedom to transact — crypto's core promise — is essential even in Western democracies.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on Baudrillard's concept of simulacra — the idea that fictional constructs like Disneyland exist to make us believe everything else is real — and how this insight resurfaced during the Canadian trucker protest and government response to frozen bank accounts. He connects Baudrillard's thinking to Harari's and Graeber's arguments that human institutions are fundamentally make-believe. The Canadian government's actions felt like a 'tear in reality' that made traditional banking seem like the fictional construct, leading him to reconsider cryptocurrency. He acknowledges post-modern philosophy can be 'obnoxiously pompous' but argues small doses can produce genuine insight.
key_points: Baudrillard's insight that Disneyland exists to make the rest of reality seem real applies broadly to human institutions
The Canadian trucker protest bank freezes made traditional financial systems suddenly feel like the fictional construct rather than the stable reality
Existential insights about the make-believe nature of institutions are hard to hold in constant consciousness — they dissolve into daily life until events force them back
Post-modern philosophy contains genuine insights but requires careful dosing to avoid getting lost in nonsense
This realization drove DHH's conversion to seeing value in Bitcoin and crypto as alternatives to traditional finance
money_quotes: Human institutions are all, broadly speaking, make believe.
It's the kind of insight that's almost too much to bear on a constant conscious basis.
Disneyland suddenly appears more real than the rest. Our contract with what we thought it meant to live in reality is reneged upon.
Like a tear in reality. Like the upside down version of maple leaf.
The difference between poison and elixir is indeed often just the dose. And a drop of Baudrillard can really stir the mental waters.
When the traditional money and the bank accounts suddenly seemed like the carton cut-out version of reality.
posts.key_insight: When governments freeze bank accounts over political protest, the supposedly solid institutions of traditional finance start to look more like make-believe than the crypto alternatives.

posts.summary: DHH defends his characterization of the Canadian trucker protest as 'peaceful' against critics who labeled it violent. He argues that expanding the definition of 'violence' to include hateful rhetoric, honking, or the backgrounds of participants is a deliberate rhetorical strategy to justify disproportionate government responses. He contends that once a protest is labeled violent, it unlocks draconian crackdowns that would be illegitimate against peaceful demonstrators. He criticizes the Canadian government for choosing alienation and persecution over engagement and concession.
key_points: Labeling a protest 'violent' is a rhetorical move that justifies maximum-severity government responses, which is why the designation is so fiercely contested
Expanding the definition of violence to include speech, rhetoric, or the backgrounds of participants conflates distinct concepts to serve a political agenda
Peaceful protest derives its power precisely from denying legitimacy to severe crackdowns — stripping that label away is the first step toward authoritarian response
A healthy democracy would engage with protestors and offer reasonable concessions rather than demonize and persecute them
The Canadian government's punitive response risks radicalizing the very people it seeks to suppress
money_quotes: Once you've claimed something to be violent, you've justified the maximum severity of a response.
Actions that would never seem reasonable against 'peaceful protestors' are now suddenly warranted.
Every despot under the sun knows to legitimize their disproportionate intervention by claiming to protect the security of the people.
If no credible threat to such security exist, they manufacture it, even if just rhetorically.
Disputing that assessment was and always is the key to unlocking a draconian response.
Instead the response appears designed to radicalize further. To alienate. To ostracize. And to persecute.
Violence is violence. Everything else is people shouting.
posts.key_insight: Redefining 'violence' to encompass speech and disruption is not a semantic debate — it's the rhetorical mechanism that legitimizes authoritarian crackdowns on dissent.

posts.summary: DHH argues against the industry convention of keeping pull requests small and short-lived. At Basecamp, pull requests run as long as needed to encompass a complete feature—sometimes up to six weeks under their Shape Up methodology. He acknowledges the trade-off of merge conflicts but contends the value of reviewing complete features in context far outweighs the cost. The core argument is that chopping features into small PRs degrades design standards and architectural cohesion by preventing reviewers from seeing the big picture.
key_points: Large PRs let reviewers see all trade-offs, design decisions, and changes in context together, preserving architectural cohesion
Merge conflicts from long-running branches can be mitigated by frequently merging main into the feature branch
The industry debate over PR size focuses too much on costs and not enough on the value of holistic code review
Small, disjointed PRs create negative externalities: missed simplifications, deferred cleanup that never happens, and code that's too shipped to change
Code review is just as powerful as a self-review tool—programmers benefit from seeing the complete diff of their own substantial work
money_quotes: My favorite part of doing code reviews is to see all the trade-offs, design decisions, and changes in context together.
That's what frustrates me a little about the debate over how long to let your pull requests run. It focuses mostly on the cost, not on the value.
High design standards and a cohesive architecture bank the rivers of clear code.
Code that didn't embrace a key insight to simplify the whole thing when viewed as a whole from 30,000 feet.
Code that doesn't leave the campsite in a better condition than it found it, because doing the cleanup is something for another pull request, on another day (by no one ever).
We often think of the code review as something done by others, but it's frequently just as powerful a method for a programmer to do to themselves.
Please don't chop up your pull requests until the cost of doing so definitively outpaces the value.
posts.key_insight: Splitting features into small pull requests optimizes for reducing merge conflicts at the expense of the far more valuable ability to review a complete feature's design and architecture as a cohesive whole.

posts.summary: DHH praises Russell Brand's YouTube channel for pushing heterodox views while avoiding the backlash that Joe Rogan has faced. He argues that Brand's hippie, poetic persona makes it impossible to dismiss him with the usual right-wing or 'dude bro' labels. DHH highlights Brand's ability to tackle contentious topics like financial deplatforming, corporate home-buying, and pandemic polarization with humor and artistry. He sees Brand as someone who defies the culture war's false dichotomy, which is precisely what makes him effective at reaching people who would otherwise dismiss the message.
key_points: Brand's persona — hippie, poetic, peace-and-love — shields him from the right-wing accusations that have been leveled at Joe Rogan
In an age of identitarianism, presentation and identity matter as much as the argument itself for determining who gets condemned
Brand's commentary style is a unique blend of comedy, poetry, and political analysis that makes difficult topics accessible
People who defy easy categorization in the culture war binary have more power to reach across ideological divides
money_quotes: While Joe Rogan has served as a magnet for all the wagging fingers of the world, Russell Brand has somehow so far managed to build a five-million strong channel pushing equally or more heretic views without much consternation.
In this age of identitarianism, this presentation is not to be dismissed.
Brand has a way of articulating his position that makes it feel like you're listening to a mashup of poetry slam and the evening news. It's a novel and beautiful art form.
He really does not fit in neatly with the false dichotomy of the culture wars.
You can't squint and see his silhouette in a MAGA hat. Which really is deeply problematic to any attempt of putting him in one of the two boxes.
Which then again is key to his power to reach those who would have dismissed his message from any other.
posts.key_insight: People who defy easy ideological categorization are uniquely powerful communicators because they can't be dismissed through identity-based attacks.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his latest appearance on Jason Calacanis' podcast, noting how the conversation felt unusually dangerous despite being honest and open. He observes that public discourse on controversial topics has become increasingly risky, leading people to self-censor or retreat to private channels. Jason Calacanis offered the optimistic insight that these conversations aren't disappearing but migrating to private group chats. DHH sees this pattern everywhere in his own life and wonders if abandoning social media for smaller private groups might be the next cultural shift. He values those who still engage in difficult public debates as a signal to others that nuanced thinking exists beyond partisan binaries.
key_points: Public conversations on controversial topics now feel dangerous in ways they didn't a decade ago, requiring careful self-censorship
Twitter's repressive partisan environment is driving honest discourse into private group chats rather than eliminating it entirely
The migration to private conversations may represent a healthy correction away from toxic social media dynamics
Public debates between thoughtful people still serve a purpose: showing the 'tribe-less' that more than two positions exist
The next cultural wave may be younger generations abandoning social media altogether in favor of smaller, private communities
money_quotes: Like every argument ought to be hidden in an armored helmet from inside a protective trench.
The conversations were always good because they weren't burdened by two tons of offense-deafening hedges.
The repressive, partisan nature of Twitter in particular isn't only leading to large amounts of self-censorship, but also fueling a new interest in private chats.
Refuge is being sought in privacy.
Might the next wave be abandoning social media altogether, and finding meaning in smaller groups again?
Part of the appeal with these debates is to show those who are left shell-shocked and tribe-less from the daily partisan skirmishes that there are more than two ways to think.
posts.key_insight: The increasing hostility of public social media discourse isn't killing honest conversation — it's driving it underground into private channels, which may ultimately be a healthier model for genuine debate.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on social media's role during the Russian invasion of Ukraine, arguing that despite its well-documented problems, it has proven uniquely powerful in humanizing the conflict and galvanizing Western support. He contrasts Ukraine's effective social media campaign—featuring direct, personal content from politicians and President Zelenskyy—with Russia's outdated, choreographed propaganda. He argues this media prowess has been instrumental in expediting Western sanctions, military aid, and public solidarity. He acknowledges the uneven empathy Europeans show toward Ukraine versus other conflicts, but frames this as basic human nature rather than something to intellectualize away. He closes with characteristic ambivalence, noting social media is simultaneously good and bad.
key_points: Ukraine's social media strategy—mixing personal moments with wartime updates—has been far more effective than Russia's old-school propaganda approach
The depth of public support activated through social media has directly expedited political action like SWIFT sanctions, German military spending, and airspace closures
The stronger European empathy toward Ukraine compared to Yemen or Syria reflects natural human psychology, not something to be ashamed of
Even a persistent critic of social media must acknowledge its unique capacity to give direct voice to people during a crisis
The long-term implications remain unknowable, and social media remains a mix of truth, fabrication, and fog of war
money_quotes: Being able to read, in real time, about members of the Ukrainian parliament taking up arms, mixed together with pictures of walking the dog, is a deeply humanizing experience.
It feels like a 20th century war propaganda machine firing mortars at a 21st century social media machine shooting lasers.
I think their invaders dramatically underestimated the power of that social media prowess to galvanize support from the West.
Being upset about the basic human graduation of empathy – that makes us care more about that which is closer and familiar – is an intellectualization.
As someone who rarely holds back any criticism of social media in general, and Twitter in particular, it's worth taking a moment to appreciate the difference it's making here and now.
Good, not good. Bad, not bad. Neither, both.
posts.key_insight: Social media's capacity to humanize conflict through direct, unfiltered personal narratives can galvanize political action faster than any traditional propaganda, even as it remains a deeply flawed medium.

posts.summary: DHH argues that investing all your sense of purpose and happiness into a single area of life — whether work, hobbies, or friendships — leaves you dangerously exposed when that area inevitably hits trouble. He draws an analogy to financial diversification, suggesting people should spread their emotional investment across multiple pillars. The post warns against survivorship bias, noting that for every person who succeeds by going all-in, ninety-nine others crash and burn unseen. He advocates for resilience through balance, not necessarily equal distribution, but enough variety that no single setback can collapse your entire sense of self.
key_points: Staking all your happiness and drive on one thing — work, a hobby, or a social circle — makes you fragile when that thing fails
Life pillars can be weighted unevenly, but extreme concentration like 90/5/5 leaves you unprepared for inevitable setbacks
Survivorship bias distorts our view: we see the highlight reels of those who bet everything and won, never the majority who lost
Diversification isn't about equal distribution but about having enough backup pillars to stay standing when one crumbles
money_quotes: Betting your drive to get up in the morning on a single path will leave you completely stuck if that one road is blocked.
Don't bet all your happiness and purpose on a single square.
Diversification is resilience in action.
And planning for that by packing a bag with more than just hammers.
You'll only ever see their highlight reel, and you'll never see the other ninety-nine who crashed and burned.
It's fine to lean on one pillar or another at different times in your life, but you'd do well to make sure there's a few others holding up the roof if the first one crumbles.
posts.key_insight: Resilience comes from distributing your sense of purpose across multiple areas of life so that no single setback can take everything down.

posts.summary: DHH recounts an extraordinarily productive 2021 for Rails, detailing his work on import maps, Hotwire integration, jsbundling-rails, cssbundling-rails, Kredis, Propshaft, and more that made Rails 7 a landmark release. He then reveals that Ruby Central's RailsConf organizers effectively disinvited him from the opening keynote, citing he'd been 'mostly offline' — despite the massive output he just catalogued. DHH frames this as politically motivated retaliation, connecting it to broader ideological gatekeeping he sees in the Rails community. He pledges his enthusiasm for Rails remains undimmed and expresses confidence the community will eventually move past what he calls fractious politicization.
key_points: Rails 7 delivered a complete, compelling frontend story: Node-free defaults via import maps, Hotwire as standard, plus first-class support for traditional JS bundling
DHH shipped an enormous volume of work in 2021 including importmap-rails, turbo-rails, stimulus-rails, jsbundling-rails, cssbundling-rails, Kredis, Propshaft, tailwindcss-rails, and dartsass-rails
Ruby Central effectively removed DHH from the RailsConf keynote while claiming he'd been 'mostly offline,' which he views as a transparent pretext for ideological exclusion
DHH sees a pattern of partisan litmus tests in the Rails community, citing the web3 track controversy as another example of ideological gatekeeping
He warns that politicized conference organizing could discourage companies from participating if they fear retaliation for ideological transgressions
money_quotes: Between getting rid of Node, forging a part with import maps, and committing to Hotwire, the default frontend story for Rails became complete and compelling unlike anything we've ever had before.

It's a real shame that this is the world we find ourselves in now. One so sharply divided by politics and ideology that we can't even share the love of Ruby on Rails together at a conference without a need to settle scores.
What does 'you having been mostly offline the last year' even mean? Is there a pious tweet minimum?
Trying to deduce an inner logic of an obvious pretext is a fool's errand.

Should companies that support RailsConf by sending employees, speakers, or sponsorship money expect retaliation or exclusion if they end up transgressing against whatever shrinking ideological ring surround the organizers or program committee?
It's a perilous time to reveal preferences, so I fully understand why many choose to duck instead.
I'm confident we'll be able to eventually route around the fractious politicization that's been leaking into our proceedings.
posts.key_insight: DHH uses an exhaustive catalog of his 2021 Rails contributions to frame his removal from the RailsConf keynote as ideologically motivated exclusion rather than a legitimate reflection of his involvement.

posts.summary: DHH explores the tension between tribal allegiance and principled reasoning, using Jonathan Haidt's elephant-and-rider metaphor as a framework. He argues that most people unconsciously filter their principles through tribal loyalty, conveniently leaning on whichever principles serve their side. He holds up Glenn Greenwald as an exemplar of applying universal principles consistently regardless of which political tribe benefits. DHH suggests that principled figures are uniquely infuriating because they expose the cognitive dissonance of tribal reasoning. While pessimistic that tribalism is humanity's base nature, he maintains that cultivating more people with strong allegiance to universal principles remains a noble pursuit.
key_points: Most reasoning is post-hoc tribal justification: people select whichever principles conveniently support their side without consciously realizing it
Figures who apply principles universally become targets of both sides, because consistent principle-application creates cognitive dissonance for tribal thinkers
Glenn Greenwald exemplifies this pattern — celebrated by the left during Bush years, then vilified when he applied the same scrutiny to Obama and Clinton
Opposing principles often coexist, making it easy to selectively invoke whichever one serves the moment
Despite believing tribalism is hardwired, DHH argues the world needs 'unreasonably reasonable people' who model principled consistency
money_quotes: We launder our arguments and reasoning through the tribal wash.
The most infuriating, polarizing figures are those who do not succumb to this tribal-first reasoning.
The same principles that drew applause when they were directed against the right suddenly invoked the most indignant contempt when they were applied to the left.

The fact that he applied universal principles caused such severe cognitive-dissonance hangovers amongst former supporters that he now seemed worse than even the other side. He became a traitor to the team.
The world needs unreasonably reasonable people.
We're hard-wired to be of weak principles and strong tribal allegiance.
Minting more scouts with stronger allegiance to universal principles is still a noble cause.
posts.key_insight: People instinctively subordinate principles to tribal loyalty, which is why those who apply principles universally across partisan lines become uniquely despised by all sides.

posts.summary: DHH argues that persuasion is a long-term process, not an instant conversion event. Most arguments don't immediately change minds but leave cracks in people's cognitive defenses that widen over time. He draws on two decades of writing and debating online to observe that patient argumentation is a powerful force. He also notes that the act of changing someone's mind can forge deep friendships, but only if the conversion is met with grace rather than scorn.
key_points: Arguments should not be judged by whether they immediately convert someone — there's a long tail of persuasion
Even the best arguments rarely breach mental walls instantly, but they leave cracks that time and reflection can widen
Changing minds is painful, but it creates opportunities for enduring bonds and friendships
Grace in receiving someone's changed perspective is essential — gloating or contempt makes any conversion brittle
money_quotes: Even when the best arguments fail to breach the mental walls, they often leave cracks. Cracks which time, new observations, and deeper consideration can open.
"Right now, right here" is a bad timeframe to measure the success of your arguments on. There's a long tail of conversion.
Patient persuasion is none the less a mighty force that does move minds.
Most of my best friends are those who've changed my mind, and I theirs.
The grace of simply being happy that someone heard what you had to say, and eventually found it compelling. Who cares how long it took, just be happy that they're here now.
Any conversion in perspective will be brittle if it's greeted with scorn and contempt. Whether I TOLD YOU SO or WHAT TOOK YOU SO LONG.
posts.key_insight: Persuasion works on a long timeline — arguments leave cracks that eventually crumble walls, and the process deserves patience and grace rather than triumphalism.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how returning to in-person social interactions after Denmark's pandemic restrictions revealed the profound contrast between face-to-face conversation and social media discourse. He describes the subtle 'conversational ballet' of facial expressions, posture, and social graces that naturally moderates disagreement in person — something entirely absent online. He argues that pandemic isolation functioned as a form of microdosed torture that drove people to self-medicate with social media, creating a feedback loop of tribalism and anger. The antidote, he concludes, is simply spending more time with real people in person.
key_points: Face-to-face interaction is deeply nourishing even for introverts, and the contrast with online discourse became vivid after prolonged deprivation
In-person conversations have a natural 'ballet of social graces' — facial expressions, posture, pauses — that effortlessly moderates disagreement in ways social media cannot
Pandemic isolation created a feedback loop: loneliness leads to alienation, desperate virtual bonds, tribalism, and anger
Social media grew angrier during the pandemic not just because the world was harder, but because people lost the ability to process challenges together in person
DHH acknowledges the irony of a remote-work advocate arguing for the irreplaceable value of in-person human connection
money_quotes: The first revelation was that no matter how introvert you are, spending time face-to-face with others after prolonged periods of isolation is nourishing in a profound and human way (even if it's also exhausting).

The second revelation was just how normal and calm all these interactions and conversations were. No matter the topic. In stark contrast to what appears to be a continued deterioration of social media's ability to do the same.
Like if you cut out sugar for a month, then take your first bite of a frosted cake, the experience is vivid and detailed.
If everyone in your life has been reduced to an avatar for too long, the world does become very flat and two dimensional.
Where loneliness leads to alienation leads to despair leads to forming desperate virtual bonds leads to tribalism, hate, and a miserable quest for meaning.
There's a reason subjecting prisoners to total isolation for even a few days is considered torture. Even the most introverted among us need human connection. We're hardwired for it.
I think we may well look back upon these social dark ages of the pandemic as a grand social experiment of microdosing a huge swath of the population with this unique form of isolation torture.
But the antidote is there. It's waiting. It's other people, in flesh and blood, without a mask, without an agenda.
posts.key_insight: The pandemic revealed that social media's growing toxicity is less about the platforms themselves and more about the isolation that drives people to them — and the cure is simply being with other humans in person.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how social media has dramatically raised the cost of changing one's mind by encouraging people to publicly commit to positions on complex issues. He uses his own viral reversal on crypto as an example of how rare and notable public mind-changing has become. He argues this dynamic is catastrophic for society's ability to adapt to evolving reality, particularly visible during the pandemic. He advocates for epistemic humility, treating science as method rather than settled conclusion, and finding ways to lower the social cost of being wrong.
key_points: Social media forces public commitment to positions, making reversals socially expensive and rare
The cost of changing your mind scales with how publicly you've committed — from private thoughts (free) to social timelines (very expensive)
The pandemic exemplified how poorly society handles evolving knowledge when people have dug into public positions
Science's chief contribution is a method, not settled conclusions — models should be discarded when they fail
Society needs to invest in credibility through modesty about certainties and tolerance of acknowledged ignorance
money_quotes: By enticing people around the world to publicly commit to one side or another on a million hard questions every day via social media, we've dramatically raised the cost for society to collectively change its mind about anything.
This is nothing short of a catastrophe, and it's making societies less responsive to adjusting to reality as it evolves.
Opinions you hold in your head are easily malleable. There's no social price to pay for changing your mind on ideas that never left that mind in the first place.
Even the smartest people – be they scientists, journalists, or activists – get reality wrong all the time.
The chief contribution of science is a method not a conclusion.
We build the best models we have given the data we got, and then unceremoniously discard them when they fail to explain or predict.
We need to make the long-term investments in credibility with modesty around our certainties, and tolerate the short-term cost of acknowledged ignorance.
The deeper you've dug your position, the harder it is to advance.
posts.key_insight: Social media has made changing your mind prohibitively expensive by turning every opinion into a public commitment, and this rigidity is making society dangerously unable to adapt to new information.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Twitter and cable news create a distorted picture of public opinion, making loud activist minorities appear to represent mainstream views. He points to several cases — Virginia's gubernatorial election, San Francisco's school board recall, and the Netflix/Spotify controversies — where the silent majority eventually expressed opposing preferences through votes and purchasing decisions. The post contends that most Americans are 'decent and normal' independents who don't engage in online discourse but ultimately wield decisive power. DHH sees this pattern repeating across politics, entertainment, and tech, where brief outrage cycles on social media are followed by the silent majority's contradictory reply through ballots and wallets.
key_points: 75% of Americans never tweet, and independents would be the largest political party, meaning Twitter dramatically misrepresents public opinion
Independent media like Joe Rogan's podcast vastly outperforms cable news, suggesting mainstream media also misreads the public
The Virginia governor's race and San Francisco school board recall showed voters rejecting policies that appeared popular on Twitter
Netflix and Spotify saw no real subscriber losses despite intense online outrage campaigns over Chappelle and Rogan
In competitive markets with low switching costs, consumer behavior is the truest measure of public sentiment — and it consistently contradicts Twitter outrage
money_quotes: Most people you meet in everyday life — at work, in the neighborhood — are decent and normal. Even nice. But hit Twitter or watch the news, and you'd think we were all nuts and nasty.

This reality of 'decent and normal' people not expressing their preferences on the big topics online means they appear silent. Until suddenly they reveal their big stick as a belated reply, and the commentariat is surprised by the force.
In politics they vote with their ballot, in commerce they vote with their wallet.
The loud minority loom impossibly large for a brief moment in history, and then change or not comes on the basis of the silent majority's reply.
Streaming music and streaming video? Two of the most competitive arenas on the internet! Scarcely any friction in hopping from Spotify to Apple Music or from Netflix to Amazon Video.
How many developers moved their projects in protest to GitLab or some other source repository? I can't even recall one.
posts.key_insight: Twitter outrage cycles consistently misrepresent public opinion, and the silent majority's actual reply — delivered through votes and purchasing decisions — repeatedly contradicts the loud online consensus.

posts.summary: DHH announces his return to competitive racing at the 24 Hours of Le Mans after a pandemic-era hiatus. He describes how burnout and loss of the flow state led him to consider retiring from racing after the 2020 Sebring race. After a break, watching Le Mans 2021 reignited his passion, and he secured a seat in an LMP2 car for the 2022 season. He reflects on how the track at Le Mans has always been the core draw of his racing career, and how pre-season testing confirmed the flow state was still accessible after all.
key_points: After a decade of high-level racing without pause, DHH experienced burnout and lost access to the flow state that made racing worthwhile
Sometimes the cure for burnout is simply a break — watching Le Mans 2021 from the outside reignited his desire to compete
Le Mans holds a singular place in his racing life, with close to a thousand laps over the years and an undiminished pull
Pre-season testing initially felt like a mistake, but the brain quickly acclimatized and the flow state returned within hours
He returns with a strong record (class win in 2014, four podiums in eight appearances) but frames 2022 as having nothing to prove — which paradoxically fuels the competitive drive
money_quotes: I actually thought I was going to hang up the helmet after the pandemic.
That the portal to the pure state of flow had been closed. That shutting the cockpit door no longer kept the world out and the focus in.
But sometimes all you need is a break.
It may sound glamorous, but the reality is that you rarely get to see much outside the track when traveling for racing.
After close to a thousand laps over the years, it still pulls like nothing else.
There's nothing to prove. Which of course means we'll be throwing everything at it to win!
After the first ten laps, I thought, shit, I've made a mistake. I've totally lost the feel. But then snap, the brain acclimates.
The world had once again been completely isolated to outside the cockpit, the flow state was constant, and the smile couldn't be wider.
posts.key_insight: Burnout from a passionate pursuit doesn't mean the passion is gone — sometimes all it takes is a real break to rediscover the flow state that made it worthwhile.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his experience upgrading from an M1 MacBook Air to an M1 Max MacBook Pro, only to discover he couldn't perceive any meaningful performance difference for his web development work. After nearly ordering a new Mac Studio with the M1 Ultra, he swapped back to his 2020 M1 MacBook Air and found it handled everything just as well. He praises Apple's chip team for making CPUs interesting again while noting that the M1's original leap was so significant that subsequent upgrades offer diminishing returns for most developers. He concludes that a $999 M1 laptop is all web developers really need, celebrating deflationary progress in computing.
key_points: Apple's chip team made CPUs interesting again after a decade of Intel stagnation, and the original M1 was a genuinely noticeable leap forward
For web development workflows, there is no perceptible difference between the base M1 and the far more expensive M1 Max or Ultra machines
Big leaps in computing are rare and remarkable precisely because they stand out from ordinary incremental improvements that most people don't feel
Once a machine crosses the 'fast enough' threshold, qualities like slimness and lightness become more valuable than raw power
DHH separates admiration for Apple's engineering from criticism of their business practices, praising the chip team while opposing App Store monopoly abuses
money_quotes: I don't have a problem in the slighest with admiring the engineers while skewering the business operators.

But the reason we call it a big leap is because it stands out from all the ordinary leaps. Those regular yearly incremental improvements that most people just don't feel. It's remarkable because it's rare.
The 2020 M1 MacBook runs everything that I do more or less exactly as well as the three times more expensive specced-out MacBook Pro.
Once a machine is Fast Enough and the battery lasts Long Enough, making it lighter and slimmer really does count for more.
Maybe Ive was right all along – minus that awful keyboard! – he just didn't have the chips to fulfill his vision.
It's a marvel that a $999 M1 laptop is now all web developers really ever need. Hurrah for deflationary progress!
posts.key_insight: The original M1 chip crossed a 'fast enough' threshold for web development that makes expensive upgrades to Max and Ultra variants an exercise in spending money for imperceptible gains.

posts.summary: DHH argues that when people are ready to change their minds on ideological positions, the smart move is to welcome them gracefully rather than punish them for past beliefs. He illustrates this through multiple examples: Western sanctions that trap dissenting Russians, wokeism's take-no-prisoners approach to ideological purity, MAGA's loyalty tests, and technical community gatekeeping. He contrasts these with the Bitcoin community's welcoming reception when he changed his stance on crypto. The core argument is that effective persuasion requires lowering the cost of changing one's mind.
key_points: Blocking graceful exits from ideological positions pushes doubters back into the very doctrines they want to leave
Western sanctions on Russia risk trapping potential dissenters by offering no realistic path to defection other than martyrdom
Both progressive wokeism and MAGA enforce purity tests that repel potential converts and undermine coalition building
The Bitcoin community's welcoming response to DHH's change of heart exemplifies the right approach to ideological converts
Effective persuasion requires lowering the price of admission for those willing to change their minds
money_quotes: It's never been easier to dig yourself into an ideological hole.
The internet today is nothing if not a credence reinforcement program.

Few humans can weather the world without companions. Most will choose comfort among compatriots, even if internal opposition to the shared ideology has crept in, rather than be hazed by new allegiances.
We shouldn't be surprised if they eventually accept and embrace whatever modicum of comfort they might find in life as a supporter of the only regime that'll have them.
We should all seek to lower the price for those who wish to be convinced by our arguments, if we care to be effective persuaders.
It's hard enough for someone to give up their ideological moorings on the sunniest of days. It's virtually impossible if there's a jeering crowd waiting to greet them on the new shore.
Open your arms and your mind to those who've come to see it your way, and forget quickly where they came from. This is the mercy of a winning argument.
posts.key_insight: If you want to win people over to your side, you must make it easy and safe for them to change their minds rather than punishing them for having once disagreed.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on Basecamp's recovery one year after the controversial decision to ban political discussions at work, which led to roughly a third of employees departing. He frames the crisis as ultimately beneficial, arguing the company emerged stronger and more aligned after the turbulent period. Writing from a company meetup in Miami, he describes a reinvigorated team with better morale and shared values. He credits the concept of antifragility and argues that the public backlash was far less consequential than feared in the moment.
key_points: Basecamp's post-crisis recovery exceeded expectations, with the company now bigger and on a better trajectory than before the political speech policy controversy
Real crises provide clarity by forcing you to question fundamentals and choose a new direction rather than defaulting to the status quo
The concept of antifragility applies to organizations — bouncing back stronger from breakage is better than being strong but brittle
Most customers and prospective employees care about the product and workplace fit, not your temporary controversies
Basecamp now includes an explicit statement about its no-politics-at-work policy in all job listings, filtering for cultural alignment upfront
money_quotes: When things are difficult, it's tempting to lose hope, regret your principles, and shrivel from the occasion. But rarely is the outlook as dim as you imagine, and rarely are the consequences as harsh as you envision.
The gift of a real crisis is the clarity it brings by forcing you to question the fundamentals.
You can be strong yet brittle. Capable of withstanding immense pressure, but shattering in a thousand pieces if your limits are exceeded. Or you can be antifragile.
The vast majority of customers care about you delivering a great service at a reasonable price above all else.
The vast majority of prospective employees care about whether they can see themselves at your company, not how others couldn't.
The uncertainty of what might happened replaced by the certainty of what did happen. And the relief that not only wasn't it as bad as feared, but it turned out better than we dared hope.
The world is full of great people who not only can but embrace working with others of different political backgrounds without litigating their differences at work.
posts.key_insight: Organizational crises that force you to stand by your principles can ultimately strengthen a company by filtering for cultural alignment and providing clarity of purpose.

posts.summary: DHH applies Kierkegaard's either/or philosophy to Twitter's potential acquisition by Elon Musk. He argues that both outcomes—selling or not selling—will lead to regret. Under current management, Twitter will continue increasing censorship and policing dissenting opinions. Under Musk, the platform might become even more toxic by maximizing engagement through algorithmic manipulation. DHH sees this as a lose-lose scenario with no good options.
key_points: Kierkegaard's either/or framework applies perfectly to the Musk-Twitter acquisition dilemma
Current Twitter management trends toward increasing censorship and suppression of dissenting views
Musk's Twitter would likely maximize engagement, amplifying algorithmic toxicity and division
Removing overt political content moderation won't make the platform less political
money_quotes: Sell Twitter to Musk, and you'll regret it. Don't sell Twitter to Musk, and you'll regret that too.
Many of life's grand dilemmas have only regrettable choices. Yet we spend much of our time trying to argue and act as if otherwise.
More bans, more suppression, more policing of thought divergent from The Consensus Opinion.
Twitter made in Musk's image might well "unlock the potential" of the site, and thus infect even more people with the algorithmic darkness and division that maximizing for engagement brings.
Less overt political gerrymandering of the Overton window will not make for a less political site.
Either way, we'll all regret it.
posts.key_insight: The Twitter acquisition debate is a false choice between two bad outcomes—continued censorship under current management or amplified algorithmic toxicity under Musk—and neither path leads anywhere good.

posts.summary: DHH contrasts the visible independence of children in Copenhagen with the heavily supervised childhood experience in America. He observes that Danish kids routinely take public transit alone, run errands, and play unsupervised from young ages — freedoms that have largely vanished for American children. Drawing on Jonathan Haidt's work, he links this loss of autonomy to broader social problems in the US. He argues that Denmark's example should be more relatable to Americans than Japan's, since the societies share many values, and urges American parents to actively seek opportunities to grant their children independence.
key_points: Danish children have visible autonomy in daily life — taking metro alone, biking in traffic, running errands — that would be unthinkable for most American kids
America actually has slightly more children per capita than Denmark, but kids are far less visible in American cities because their lives are so heavily supervised
This level of childhood independence was normal in America in the 1980s but has been systematically eroded
Denmark's example should be more actionable for Americans than Japan's because the societies are culturally similar
The loss of childhood autonomy may contribute to growing authoritarianism and social problems, per Jonathan Haidt's research
money_quotes: All these observations stick out, but none trigger the culture shock like seeing kids everywhere.
It's the fact that this prevalence of kids everywhere means they have real autonomy and independence. Freedoms that have been rapidly disappearing for many American kids.
In the US, virtually all interactions outside of school for our elementary school kids were not just scheduled, but supervised and chaperone by adults.
There's such a glow of accomplishment on the face of our boys when they push another boundary of independence.
You'd immediately be pursued by social services or the police in the US if you let a three-year old run around the city by themselves.
It's not a force of nature that's continually curtailing the autonomy and independence of American kids.
What gift of independence might I be able to bestow my kid today? I guarantee that the value of receiving such gifts is universal.
posts.key_insight: The disappearance of childhood independence in America is not inevitable but a cultural choice, and Denmark demonstrates that similar Western societies can preserve children's autonomy with positive results.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the failed campaign to cancel Joe Rogan over COVID misinformation on Spotify represents the peak of cancel culture. Despite weeks of media outrage, open letters, and a trending #CancelSpotify hashtag, Spotify grew to 182 million subscribers and Rogan gained 2 million new listeners — a textbook Streisand effect. DHH contends that when the public examined the actual evidence, the charges seemed overblown, which permanently damaged the credibility of those making them. He predicts this dynamic is already carrying over to the media reaction against Elon Musk's Twitter acquisition.
key_points: Spotify's Q1 numbers showed unimpeded growth despite the cancel campaign, proving the listening public rejected the outrage
Overblown charges against a popular figure backfire via the Streisand effect, indicting the accusers rather than the accused
Each failed cancel attempt increases the public's 'discount factor' on future moral outrage from the same voices
The cycle of outrage moves so fast that each crisis feels ancient within months, undermining its own seriousness
The Rogan episode's failure is already affecting public reception of similar outrage directed at Elon Musk
money_quotes: The listening public has spoken about the grave dangers posed by the man with the microphone: It doesn't give a shit.

That's the problem with bringing out the big guns. When you trump up charges of crimes against humanity against a mild-mannered, broadly intellectually curious, and all-round popular podcast host, it's a big gamble!

When folks then examine the evidence for themselves, and actually listen to an episode of Rogan, and they fail to find even the most basic substantiation of the charges? Well, it naturally indicts the indicter rather than the indictee.
It also leaves a permanent mark of 'wait, we were freaking the fuck out over this???' in the public psyche.
When the same segment invokes the same moral outrage in the same shrill tones, the discount factor applied to the message is rapidly increasing.
The Current Thing is at once both the most important topic in the world and a memory hole to be forgotten five minutes later.

You always have to be careful not to tip so far that the mere presence of the shrill voices mean you take the other side out of reflex, but it's fair to say that you can easily turn the volume on them down.
posts.key_insight: When cancel campaigns massively overstate their case against popular figures, the public backlash permanently erodes the credibility of future outrage from the same voices.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his journey of becoming addicted to daily news consumption around 2010 and his recent decision to break free from it. After the pandemic news cycle ended and seamlessly transitioned into war coverage, he recognized the futility of obsessive news following and cut his consumption by over 90%. He found that the major events still reach him without active seeking, while the reduction freed him from phone addiction and redirected his attention toward books. The core realization is that the payoff isn't productivity gains but simply a calmer mind and a greater sense of time.
key_points: There's a crucial difference between actively following the news and passively being exposed to it — the big events find you regardless
Obsessive news consumption creates an illusion of doing something worthwhile by being 'informed,' but the residual knowledge and wisdom gained is negligible
Breaking the news habit had a cascading effect: less phone use, less scrolling, and more time for books and deeper reading
The pandemic-to-war coverage transition revealed how breathless news cycles are interchangeable and self-perpetuating
The real benefit isn't the productivity substitutes but a fundamentally calmer mind and greater emotional distance from events you can't affect
money_quotes: Following the news often leaves you with this sense that you're doing something worthwhile. You're informed! But are you really?
The difference between following the news and being exposed to the news is huge.
I've essentially given up on social media too, the news was the last big trap of unlimited scrolling that black mirror contained.
What difference did it make to know all the daily infection numbers? Reading every damn long-form piece on what the pandemic was doing, and what we were all doing because of it?
It's a calmer mind. A sense of time slowing down. A greater distance from the woes of the world you can't affect either way.
You really do have to retreat to a cave in the mountains to fully avoid The Slap, Musk buying Twitter, or The War.
When you don't have a never-ending stream of news, commentary, and think pieces to consume, you're all that much more likely to spend time reading actual damn books.
posts.key_insight: Obsessive news consumption creates an illusion of informed engagement while actually delivering little wisdom, and breaking the habit yields not productivity but a fundamentally calmer mind.

posts.summary: DHH argues that social media has destroyed the ability to feel unique within small social circles, creating a global competition for distinctiveness that has unhealthy consequences. In pre-internet times, minor quirks or interests were enough to make someone 'something' within their small group. Now, constant exposure to the entire world's talent and uniqueness dilutes individual distinctiveness. He suggests this drives people toward adopting diagnoses and oppression narratives as identity markers, and calls for a return to smaller, isolated circles where ordinary variations can feel meaningful.
key_points: Before the internet, uniqueness only required standing out within a small social circle — liking sausage or comic books was enough
Social media collapsed all local hierarchies into one global competition for distinctiveness, making ordinary uniqueness feel worthless
The drive for uniqueness may be fueling the trend of adopting mental health diagnoses and oppression identities as personal branding
The Instagram critique of unrealistic achievement highlight reels may have been preferable to the current search for uniqueness through affliction
We need to find ways to restore isolated circles of belonging where being a slight variation on ordinary is enough
money_quotes: This scene pins the deep fear in most people that the worst you can possibly be is ordinary.
When your comparison group was mainly a small group of friends, or maybe your class, or at most your school. It wasn't the whole damn world.
The best of everything, the most unique species of every subcategory, bubble to the top. Thus diluting whatever would have made you special in your little group in an instant.
One way to stand out as unique is to embrace and accentuate every quirk of personality, every sliver of peculiarity with a diagnosis that can form an integral part of one's personal branding.

Where the social ideal used to be that above all you wanted to be 'normal' – but with a dash of quirkiness to just stand out a little! – it now often appears that in some of the social games being played it is about being as 'weird' as possible.
In a global competition for uniqueness, there's a constant game of one-upmanship running to find the next facet to highlight why we're actually special.
Maybe searching for uniqueness in the stratosphere of accomplishment was still superior to searching for it in an identity of oppression or diagnosis.
If we can't find a way to make being mostly ordinary okay, we're condemned to search for that 'something' in ever more pathological places.
posts.key_insight: Social media's collapse of local social hierarchies into one global arena has made ordinary uniqueness impossible, driving people toward increasingly pathological ways of standing out.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Facebook's mission to connect everyone is fundamentally toxic because it prevents the natural human process of growing apart and losing touch. He left Facebook in 2011 because staying connected to every past acquaintance creates a gravitational force that inhibits personal growth and reinvention. The platform's default of permanent connection forces people to actively sever ties rather than letting them fade naturally, which moderates self-expression and stifles evolution. He rejects Zuckerberg's idea that people should have one consistent identity across all contexts, embracing instead the freedom to be different people in different stages and circles of life.
key_points: Facebook reversed the natural default from 'lose touch over time' to 'stay connected forever,' requiring active effort to disconnect rather than passive drift
Personal growth depends on the freedom to leave behind old social circles and identities without the weight of a permanent audience
Zuckerberg's 'one self' philosophy is toxic because people are naturally different across social contexts and life stages
The permanent audience of past connections moderates what people share and discourages vulnerable experimentation with new identities
Severing synthetic connections in one stroke was liberating rather than antisocial
money_quotes: I quit Facebook back in 2011 for a lot of reasons, but perhaps the most crucial was to rebel against its core mission: Connecting the world.
Zuckerberg has repeatedly doubled down on the toxic idea that we should only have one self, one persona. That we should be the same person in all social circles, lest we be 'frauds'.
What allowed me to change and prosper was the freedom to grow apart and lose touch with people.
It's hard to change yourself if you're stuck in the same social orbit. There's a gravitational force that pulls you into repeating the same circular pattern over and over again.
Now the default is to stay connected with everyone you've ever friended forever. And to break that connection, you have to actively sever it.
Not a lot of people are so keen to premiere such vulnerable stages of their evolution in front of an audience that expects them to be that same person they always were forever.
Connected in the sense that an anchor is to a boat. A literal drag.
So I pressed the red button and nuked all these synthetic connections from Facebook's orbit in one go. It was liberating.
posts.key_insight: The natural human process of growing apart and losing touch is essential for personal growth, and social media's default of permanent connection is a subtle but powerful force against self-reinvention.

posts.summary: DHH argues against the 'bring your whole self to work' movement, contending that employees should maintain a professional 'work self' that is genuine but bounded. He suggests the demand to bring one's whole self to work is a consequence of excessive work hours colonizing personal life. The work self should be courteous, engaged, and appropriately reserved — sharing no more than you would with a kind stranger on a long flight. He notes that deeper friendships can still form at work, but they're special precisely because they develop beyond professional obligations. The blurring of work and personal life through shared devices and always-on culture has eroded healthy boundaries that previous generations understood instinctively.
key_points: The 'whole self at work' demand is a bad trade that stems from employers expecting 60-80+ hour weeks
A 'work self' isn't a facade — it's a genuine but bounded version of you, like what you'd share with a kind stranger on a plane
Workplace friendships are more meaningful precisely because they develop beyond the professional context, not because work demanded full vulnerability
Holding something back creates a smaller surface area for interpersonal friction and preserves emotional reserves
The collapse of boundaries between work and personal devices mirrors the collapse of boundaries between work and personal identity
money_quotes: What work and you really need is for everyone to show up with their "work self".
Your work self needn't be a facade, it can still be you — just not all of it.
No more of your outside-of-work self than you'd be comfortable sharing with a kind stranger on a long plane ride.
Your whole self is a vulnerable show. Far more easily rattled, disturbed, or offended than the much slimmer slice that work is owed and due.
Holding some of yourself back means having something in reserve. Not putting it all in play.
A smaller surface area to rub others the wrong way and vice versa.
Work isn't owed all of you. Don't offer it.
posts.key_insight: The 'bring your whole self to work' ethos is a bad deal for everyone — maintaining a bounded but genuine professional self preserves emotional reserves and healthier workplace dynamics.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates the end of easy-money funding conditions as interest rates rise and recession looms. He argues that cheap capital enabled a culture of short-termism, inflated valuations, and reckless cash-burning while ignoring business fundamentals like profitability. Startups justified endless losses by invoking Amazon, Facebook, and Google as precedents, despite the statistical improbability of replicating their outcomes. DHH sees the downturn as a healthy correction that forces entrepreneurs to learn sustainable business basics.
key_points: Rising interest rates and recession fears are ending the era of easy venture funding on soft terms
The cheap-money era enabled scams, cash-burning, and obsession with valuation over actual profitability
Startups justified perpetual losses by pointing to Amazon/Facebook/Google, ignoring survivorship bias
Economic downturns force entrepreneurs to learn fundamentals they ignored during boom times
Both dreamers and realists are needed — but the realist perspective has been suppressed during The Good Times
money_quotes: The Good Times™ all too often serve as an enabling backdrop for short-termism and hot potatoes.
It is most definitely, absolutely, not the time to talk about the boring fundamentals of business.
The Good Times™ teach entrepreneurs that the only thing that really matters is inflating the fantasy figure known as valuation.
Companies that wandered in the desert of unprofitability for longer than Moses, but eventually found the holy land of monopoly rents.
Just jam the pipeline full of more dreams, and surely it'll happen!
When the gravy gets spooked, and you have to get by on your own cooked cabbage until you can afford better, that's when you learn the basics.
It's good to dream. But it's also good to wake up.
posts.key_insight: Economic downturns are healthy corrections that force startups to abandon valuation theater and relearn the fundamentals of building profitable businesses.

posts.summary: DHH argues that senior leaders have a duty to exercise their judgment and authority rather than retreating into false egalitarianism where all opinions are treated as equally valid. He observes that some senior people become afraid to make decisive calls, hiding behind relativism instead of leveraging their hard-earned competence. This failure to lead hurts junior employees who learn slower without exposure to decisive, experienced decision-making. He clarifies this isn't about blind obedience—questioning authority should always be welcome—but legitimate power rooted in competence must actually be used to be valuable.
key_points: A senior title represents a recognition of competence and experience, which creates a duty to exercise judgment for the benefit of others
Some senior leaders retreat into false relativism where all opinions are treated as equally valid, which helps no one
Junior employees learn less and slower when not exposed to decisive action by experienced people
Exercising legitimate authority is compatible with a culture that allows questioning leadership
The goal is a middle ground between authoritarian command and leaderless egalitarianism
money_quotes: Just because you have a high-ranking title, doesn't mean you're automatically right about everything. But it does mean you should be mostly right, most of the time, or that title isn't vested in legitimate power.

Retreat into some relativistic cave where all opinions, ideas, and insights are presumed equally good, equally deserving of first-principles reasoning, and resulting in an arm's length "who am I to judge" distancing. This helps no one.
Legitimate power drawn from competence and experience is wasted if not exercised.
Junior folks learn less and slower if they are not exposed to decisive action by people who know stuff and have seen stuff.
The organization swerves in its mission if not built on a foundation of decisions and directions made and set by the people most qualified to do so.
We don't have to pick between these extremes.
posts.key_insight: Senior leaders who refuse to exercise their hard-earned judgment out of false egalitarianism fail everyone—their junior colleagues learn less, and the organization drifts without decisive direction.

posts.summary: DHH argues that founders have a unique obligation to make bold, risky decisions that professional managers at mature companies are structurally unable to make. As companies grow, the stakes appear higher and management becomes more risk-averse, creating the classic Innovator's Dilemma. Founders, having built the company from nothing, are less afraid of disruption because they remember life before the pillars of success existed. He cites several examples from 37signals where big gambles were taken on product strategy, pricing, culture, and fighting Apple. These decisions require faith and instinct rather than data, and while some bets will inevitably fail, the alternative — stagnation — is worse.
key_points: Professional management at mature companies becomes structurally incapable of making bold decisions as stakes grow higher
Founders have a psychological advantage in risk-taking because they built the company from nothing and don't fear the ruins
Big decisions are big precisely because they can't be resolved by data alone — they require a leap of faith guided by instinct
The arrogance required to start a business is the same quality needed to make difficult pivots later
Failing on some gambles is inevitable and acceptable — stagnation from not betting is the greater danger
money_quotes: The biggest risk of all is thinking that what worked yesterday is going to work tomorrow too.
If you were there before the pillars of wealth and progress stood solid, it's easier not to fear the ruins.
All are from the dust, and to dust all return.
They're big, difficult decisions because they require a leap of faith. A plunge into the unknown at a moment of peak criticality guided primarily by instinct.
This level of faith grows in a certain mold of arrogance. The kind that motivates someone to have the audacity to start a business in the first place.
Nobody is forever the oracle and all lucky streaks end. But that's the risk with taking risks. Native to the game. Part of the deal.
Someone needs to be comfortable starting the fire that can clear the path, despite the danger that it might also burn down your house, lest the company is to stagnate.
posts.key_insight: Founders have both the unique psychological capacity and the obligation to make bold, faith-driven decisions that professional managers are structurally unable to take, because stagnation is a greater risk than any individual failed bet.

posts.summary: DHH applauds Netflix's updated cultural guidelines that tell employees they must support the company's content breadth or leave, framed as a response to the failed employee campaign to cancel Dave Chappelle's comedy special. He argues this kind of boundary-setting used to be uncontroversial—workers at publishing houses didn't need to personally approve every book. DHH traces the problem to tech companies blurring work-life boundaries by calling themselves 'families' and demanding total devotion, which collapsed employees' personal identities into their employer's identity. He sees Netflix's move as a healthy reset of expectations.
key_points: Netflix codified that employees must support content breadth even if it conflicts with personal values, creating clear employment boundaries
The expectation that workers don't personally endorse everything their employer produces used to be uncontroversial and widely understood
Tech companies eroded work-life separation by demanding passion and calling themselves families, making employees feel morally liable for all company decisions
Overreach by activist employees invites institutional counter-responses that formalize boundaries more explicitly than before
money_quotes: That's what happens after a moment of overreach. It leaves you exposed for the riposte.

By codifying the fact that Netflix expects employees to be able to work on a wide breadth of content, which invariably means some of it might offend individuals from time to time, they've drawn the boundaries of employment.
Being, say, a proof reader at a publishing house did not mean you had to personally approve of every book that landed on your desk.

In our brave new world, it's become common place to consider your job an integral part of your identity. And thus feeling like you're personally and morally liable for all decisions made and actions taken by said company.

After a few decades of tech companies insisting they're really families, craving unbridled passion from employees, and demanding a work-life balance that was all work and no life, the space and time needed for a personal, private identity has effectively been under corporate occupation.
It's an admission in part that the strategy of boundless accommodation isn't working well for anyone.
posts.key_insight: When companies blur the line between work and identity by demanding total devotion, they shouldn't be surprised when employees treat every company decision as a personal moral matter—and resetting those boundaries is overdue.

posts.summary: DHH builds on David Graeber's concept of 'bullshit jobs' to argue that meaningless work proliferates far more easily in large companies than small ones. He recaps Graeber's thesis that pointless jobs cause psychological and spiritual damage, leading workers to seek meaning through pathological outlets. DHH's central addendum is that big companies create the conditions — excessive risk management, communication overhead, fiefdom building — where bullshit jobs thrive. In a ten-person company, it's nearly impossible to hide a role that adds no value, while in a ten-thousand-person company it's hard to avoid them. He argues this explains the unique draw of startups and small companies, even at lower pay. The post concludes with advice to escape golden handcuffs before a bullshit job erodes your soul.
key_points: Bullshit jobs cause deep psychological harm — self-loathing, guilt, and resentment that seeks outlets in exaggerated causes and conflicts
Big companies are breeding grounds for pointless roles due to excessive risk management, communication overhead, fiefdom building, and unconstrained hiring
Small companies provide natural immunity because the short distance between people makes it obvious who adds value and who doesn't
The risk of landing in a bullshit job may be the most important factor in white-collar employment decisions, outweighing compensation
money_quotes: The theater of productivity performed in pursuit of the pointless only offers a shallow distraction from the plot of existential anguish.
It's difficult to hold a bullshit job that contributes nothing of material value in a company of ten. In a company of ten thousand, it's often hard not to.
If that means inventing or exaggerating causes, enemies, and oppressions, so be it.
When the job is filled with intrinsic meaning and purpose, there's less left on the psychological budget to spend on exaggerations or outright fantasies.
The 'moral and spiritual damage' that the risk of landing in a bullshit job can cause is perhaps the key risk of all employment decisions for white-collar workers today.
Don't let the bullshit job eat your soul. We call them golden handcuffs for a reason. Find the key, then consider trying something smaller.
posts.key_insight: Large companies are structurally prone to generating meaningless jobs that corrode workers' sense of purpose, making small companies a psychologically healthier bet even at lower pay.

posts.summary: DHH surveys the 2022 tech downturn, noting massive layoffs at companies like Bolt, Klarna, and Robinhood that were recently valued at astronomical sums despite never turning a profit. He argues these companies were 'financial instruments first, real companies second' and that the market correction is exposing the folly of growth-at-all-costs thinking. Writing from the perspective of a bootstrapped, profitable company owner, he feels vindicated in his long-standing advocacy for sustainable business fundamentals. He concludes that periodic economic crises serve a necessary corrective function, tempering the 'natural forces of exuberance' in the startup ecosystem.
key_points: Companies like Bolt, Klarna, and Robinhood are cutting staff after achieving massive valuations without ever being profitable — they were financial instruments, not real companies
The downturn has barely begun and the real pain hasn't hit yet, making the anticipatory layoffs and rescinded offers just a preview
Bootstrapped, profitable companies face economic slowdowns calmly while venture-funded companies face existential crises
The gospel of profitability is impossible to sell during boom times when investors only value growth, turning every startup into a 'death march between funding rounds'
Regular economic crises serve a necessary corrective function, like controlled burns that prevent catastrophic wildfires
money_quotes: Of course none of the companies are making any profits, never have made any profits, and did not have any plans for profits in the foreseeable future. They were financial instruments first, real companies second.
And these are just the anticipatory moves! The funding winter has barely begun. The reserves are still there. Nothing is actually hurting yet.
I must say it's rather curious to watch this from the bootstrapped and profitable side of the fence.
It's not rocket science, but it does take a strong conviction to sit out the funding game as the rounds get bigger, bolder, and more preposterous during the good times.
This is how every damn startup ceases to be a company and just becomes another 'case'. When every damn 'journey' is actually just a death march between funding rounds.
The sad truth is that nothing recalibrates the conversation as effectively as a crisis.
Like timely recurring burns prevent The Big One, we should not look at this with undue dismay, but with a form of systemic gratitude.
posts.key_insight: The 2022 tech downturn is a necessary corrective that exposes the fundamental unsustainability of growth-obsessed, profit-free startups and vindicates the bootstrapped approach to building real businesses.

posts.summary: DHH condemns the growing adoption of employee surveillance software in remote work environments, calling it a sign of managerial failure. He argues that while early pandemic panic might have excused the impulse, two years later managers can no longer claim ignorance. He points to Basecamp's decision to ban surveillance integrations and notes that half of tech workers would quit rather than submit to monitoring. The post advocates for evaluating work quality rather than tracking screen time, arguing that surveillance destroys the mutual benefits that make remote work valuable for both employers and employees.
key_points: Employee surveillance software reveals managerial insecurity and incompetence rather than solving productivity problems
The pandemic excuse for adopting surveillance tools has expired — continued use reflects who these managers truly are
Remote work's value proposition is a two-sided deal: productivity for employers, flexibility for employees — surveillance poisons both sides
Managers must transition from line-of-sight supervision to evaluating quality of work output
Half of tech workers say they'd resign rather than accept surveillance, though economic downturns may weaken that resolve
money_quotes: Employee surveillance software is managerial bankruptcy.
Moving to remote work has brought out the worst in some managers. It's revealed their insecurities and paranoia, and caused them to address these in a spectacle of incompetence.

Two years after those frantic first months of the pandemic, you can't claim ignorance or fear as excusable reasons to carry on like this. Now you have to accept that if you're a manager reaching for surveillance systems to supervise employees, this is just who you are.
