These dreadful surveillance tools seek to entrench the former. They try to recreate management by counting hours in the seat in front of the screen – a method that never worked to begin with!

Remote work might have seemed like a mixed blessing during the pandemic, but it's a real breakthrough in work-life relations during more normal times. Don't you dare spoil the bargain with this surveillance poison bullshit.
posts.key_insight: Managers who reach for surveillance tools to monitor remote workers are admitting they cannot evaluate the quality of work itself — the only metric that ever mattered.

posts.summary: DHH explains his long-standing aversion to angel investing, rooted in distaste for the self-aggrandizing 'angel investor' label and the unicorn-obsessed startup culture. He acknowledges that while bootstrapping worked for Basecamp, not every entrepreneur can self-fund, and some non-builder types bring valuable skills like product management and sales. He's softened his stance enough to make small investments in Danish software companies, specifically ones open to sustainable growth rather than chasing venture capital and unicorn exits. He frames this as breeding 'zebras' instead of unicorns — profitable, grounded businesses that serve their communities.
key_points: The 'angel investor' persona is self-serving theater, often masking luck and connections as visionary skill
Not all entrepreneurs can bootstrap — some need modest funding to get started, especially non-builders with strong product or sales skills
If the only available funding comes from VC-pipeline angels, an entire class of sustainable businesses will never get the chance to exist
DHH is investing in Danish software companies that are open to unicorn-free growth plans, steering them away from the default VC route
Most businesses fail regardless of funding model — invest only what you're willing to lose
money_quotes: A winged hustler chasing mythical creatures with a dull trident, blind-folded. Occasionally nailing the throw, usually out of luck, connections, or happy timing, then heralding themselves as visionaries, demanding a parade.
While we bootstrapped Basecamp to the moon, not everyone has the capacity to build the thrusters needed in a shed behind an existing business.

Just as the world is full of bozo business types who think they're owed millions because they can dazzle a powerpoint with all the right keywords, it's also full of talented builder types who couldn't come up with a compelling product or find a way to sell it if their life depended on it.
We desperately need more tech companies that aren't solely focused on unicorn stardom, the big exit, or any of the other bullshit markers of success in that world.

If the only source of funding that's available to these entrepreneurs come from angels who are hoping to float the business down the stream to venture capitalists, we're never going to see whether this class of businesses might prosper outside that playpen.
All I had to do was tickle that hesitation and reservation... and of course offer an alternative (albeit significantly smaller) bag of money.
Even if you're aiming to breed zebras, most businesses fail. Only invest what you're willing to lose.
posts.key_insight: The tech startup ecosystem needs funding options beyond VC-pipeline angel investing so that sustainable, non-unicorn businesses have a viable path to existence.

posts.summary: DHH challenges the common assumption that there's always a deeper reason behind how things are. He argues that most outcomes aren't the result of careful planning or deliberate malice, but simply the consequence of unplanned actions taken in the moment. This applies especially in business, where we expect everything to be driven by mission statements and strategic plans. He suggests that most post-hoc explanations are rationalizations, and that accepting the lack of deeper reasoning is liberating.
key_points: People default to assuming outcomes are either carefully planned or the result of malice/incompetence, missing the simpler explanation: nobody thought about it at all
Business culture creates an expectation that every action has a clear 'why' derived from strategy, but most don't
Post-hoc rationalization masquerades as deliberate reasoning in most organizations
Accepting that there is no hidden depth — no iceberg beneath the surface — is freeing rather than frightening
money_quotes: It's human nature to assume there's a good reason for why things are the way they are.
Nobody thought about this at all.
There was no plan, good or bad. No road map, well designed or not. No projection, accurate or off.
All you see is the consequence of actions taken without foresight according to the needs of the moment by people living in the present.
Any attempt to pretend such reasoning happened is usually just a post-hoc rationalization.

Accepting the fact that there probably is no iceberg, just an ice tip bobbing at the surface, is the way to develop the fortitude to face and embrace this vast, beautiful empty space of the unconsidered.
posts.key_insight: Most business decisions have no deeper strategic reasoning behind them — they're unplanned responses to the moment, and accepting this void is more useful than inventing post-hoc explanations.

posts.summary: DHH uses the release of Top Gun: Maverick as a lens to discuss American cultural malaise and the value of optimistic storytelling. He contrasts current pessimism about America's direction with the reality that 1986—when the original Top Gun premiered—was objectively worse by many metrics, including triple the homicide rate in New York City. He argues that nostalgia makes the past seem brighter, but the real difference may be one of attitude and faith. The post concludes that America needs confident, unironic cultural touchstones like Top Gun to remind people that decline isn't destiny and turnaround is possible.
key_points: Top Gun: Maverick succeeds because it offers confident, unironic American optimism at a time of widespread cultural fatalism
America in 1986 was objectively worse on many metrics (e.g., 3x the homicides in NYC) despite being remembered as a happier era
The gap between nostalgia and reality suggests attitude and faith matter as much as material conditions
Cultural works that wallow in brokenness reinforce despair, while aspirational films can shift collective mindset
Historical comparison proves America can turn things around, but only if people believe it's possible
money_quotes: It's as subversive as an unironic American flag pin at a hipster café.
Confident, competent, and charismatic execution.
America was very cool to a Danish kid growing up in Copenhagen in the 80s.
There were over three times as many homicides in 1986 as there are today in New York City.
This moment right here, is just a snapshot. America isn't destined to head off the cliff.
But to turn things around, you need to have some faith that it can be done. Top Gun oozes that faith.
posts.key_insight: Cultural pessimism blinds Americans to historical progress, and regaining faith in the possibility of turnaround—as embodied by Top Gun's unironic confidence—is a prerequisite for actually achieving it.

posts.summary: DHH addresses the guilt managers feel when terminating employees, arguing that no amount of managerial intervention can transform someone into something they're not. He acknowledges that self-improvement is possible but insists it must come from the individual, not the manager. The post pushes back against the counterfactual thinking that leads managers to blame themselves for failed hires. He frames hiring as an inherently uncertain gamble that even the biggest companies haven't solved, and argues that accepting this frees managers from self-serving anguish. The conclusion reframes letting someone go as an act that benefits both parties, since a poor fit in one role may be a perfect fit elsewhere.
key_points: Managers cannot transform people into something they're not — growth and adaptation must come from the individual
Counterfactual regret ('if only I had...') is an obstacle to accepting the fundamental limits of managerial influence
Hiring is inherently unpredictable, and no company has solved the problem of reliably predicting fit
Holding onto a poor fit harms both the employee and the organization that would be a better match
Accepting these limits is liberating rather than defeatist — it replaces self-serving guilt with productive acceptance
money_quotes: You just don't have the power to turn someone into something they're not, in the short time that's available.
The best you can do is cheer them on, point them in the right direction, and give them the opportunities they need to prove that they have what it takes. You can't carry them there.
You have to stop well short of the line where you step onto the agency of others.
How do you predict whether someone will have the right mix to fit into this open situation? Nobody has yet nailed it. Nobody likely ever will.
Once you accept that as a fundamental boundary on your capacity as a manager, it's going to set you free.
The human pieces that don't fit into your puzzle will complete someone else's.
Learn to let go with grace. You're not here to save anyone. It's an illusion of grandeur to believe that you can.
posts.key_insight: The belief that a manager could have saved a failing employee is not humility but grandiosity — accepting the limits of your influence is what actually sets you free to manage well.

posts.summary: DHH draws on the famous chemical burn scene from Fight Club to argue that discomfort at work should be embraced rather than avoided. He applies Tyler Durden's philosophy about pain and sacrifice to professional challenges like disagree-and-commit situations and fundamental clashes over direction. The core message is that tuning out or reaching for quick fixes robs you of the deeper understanding that sustained discomfort can produce. He advocates sitting with the pain until it yields lasting insight.
key_points: Professional discomfort — disagreements, policy clashes, difficult projects — is analogous to pain that builds character
The instinct to tune out or reach for a quick fix forecloses the chance for deeper understanding
Staying with the discomfort opens 'doors of perception' that lead to better long-term decisions
Sacrifice and struggle are prerequisites for meaningful progress, not obstacles to it
money_quotes: I like to think of this exchange whenever I'm ready to tap out at work.
It doesn't really matter, as long as it burns. That's when I need to mentally slap myself, and stay with the "pain".
It's far easier to tune out, of course. To go to your happy place. But that's not where you're likely to make real progress.
It's also too easy just to reach for anything to stop the hurt.
Forgoing the opportunity to letting it guide you to a deeper understanding.
Some times you simply have to let it burn for a while. The scar is your sacrifice.
posts.key_insight: Sitting with professional discomfort rather than escaping it is what produces the deepest insights and the best decisions.

posts.summary: DHH describes his approach to reducing phone usage by removing all apps from his iPhone's Home Screen after a week-long phone sabbatical inspired by Cal Newport's Digital Minimalism. His Home Screen now shows only three essential apps against a cosmos wallpaper, requiring him to search for any other app. He explains how this adds helpful friction to frivolous usage while keeping intentional use easy, and uses the Photos app as a healthy fallback when the urge to scroll strikes. He acknowledges this isn't a complete solution, noting that replacing phone time with better activities is equally important.
key_points: Removing apps from the Home Screen adds friction that deters frivolous use while keeping intentional use easy
A nearly empty Home Screen prevents the muscle-memory tour of apps that happens when you open the phone for one task
The Photos app serves as 'digital methadone' — a finite, personal alternative to infinite scrolling feeds
Phone sabbaticals alone don't work; lasting change requires replacing phone time with better alternatives
money_quotes: Any use that's actually worth doing is easily worth the friction, but any frivolous use now faces a helpful hurdle to deter it.
I no longer open my phone to do one thing, then get dragged into a muscle-memory tour of other apps while I'm there.
It's far easier to simple do the thing I desired to do, then put the phone away, when I'm literally staring into the cosmos after completing any single task.
The photos app is there as a form of digital metadone.
The only slot machine available on the first row is my personal photo album.
There's no forever scrolling feed of novelty that can suck me in for hours on end. Just a small slice of happy memories.
Escaping the phone addiction takes more than just a quick or clever hack. You have to replace the time spent using the phone with better alternatives.
posts.key_insight: Adding friction to phone usage by clearing the Home Screen transforms the phone from an attention trap back into an intentional tool.

posts.summary: DHH argues that most hard problems in business, design, and programming don't actually need to be solved head-on. Instead, they can be restated as easy problems by questioning assumptions, reweighing trade-offs, and abandoning sunk costs. He presents this as 37signals' key principle for maintaining pace with small teams, citing their 6-week cycles and two-person teams as deliberate constraints that force simpler solutions. The post frames problem-avoidance as a learnable skill that runs counter to the natural instincts of ambitious people.
key_points: Almost all hard problems can be restated as easy ones by questioning assumptions and ignoring sunk costs
Ambitious people must overcome their ego-driven desire to solve hard problems as a point of pride
Artificial constraints like tight deadlines and tiny teams force teams to find simpler solutions rather than brute-forcing complexity
This mindset is rare because it's unnatural to creative people, but it can be taught through deliberate practice and structural constraints
money_quotes: You don't have to solve the majority of hard problems you encounter in either business, design, or programming.
Almost all of them can be restated as an easy problem, if you dare question the assumptions, reweigh the trade-offs, and stop diving after sunk cost.
To do this well, you have to cultivate an allergy to hard problems.
Park the part of the ego that thrives on doing hard things as a point of pride.
This is deeply unnatural to most ambitious, creative, and diligent people. Which is why it's also a rare attribute to encounter in the wild.
The right answer is not to throw more at the problem when push comes to shove, but to do just the opposite.
Less is the answer, less is the way.
Find your purpose in pace and progress. Not in heroic tales of climbing impossible mountains.
posts.key_insight: The highest-leverage skill in building products is refusing to solve hard problems and instead reframing them as easy ones through tight constraints and questioned assumptions.

posts.summary: DHH argues that letting tasks slide is not negligence but a rational acceptance of finite time and energy. With an essentially unlimited number of things that could be done, the difference between doing 8 or 12 tasks is a rounding error. He emphasizes that completed work creates more work — plans need updating, processes need monitoring — so doing less also reduces future burden. The post encourages people to stop apologizing for deliberately choosing not to do things and to accept that something bad will happen regardless.
key_points: The work is never done, so all you can control is what you're willing to let slide — and most days that should be most things
Completing tasks produces work products that must be maintained forever, making work a self-replicating process
Letting things slide is a rational acceptance of finite time, not a sign of neglect
There are always more good reasons not to do something than to do it
money_quotes: All you can control is what you're willing to let slide. Most days that should be most things.
Letting things slide is not a sign of neglect. It's an acceptance of our finite allotment of time, motivation, skill, and knowledge.
The difference between getting 8 or 12 things done in a day is not really a 50% increase in productivity, but a rounding error in the grand picture of completion.
The less you do, the less you accumulate.
Work begets work begets more work.
Stop apologizing for the deliberate act of letting it slide.
Something bad is bound to happen somewhere regardless of what you do.
posts.key_insight: Deliberately letting most things slide is not failure but wisdom — completed work self-replicates, so doing less today means carrying less forever.

posts.summary: DHH argues that corporate mission statements are not just useless but actively harmful. He contends that typical mission statements contain no real trade-offs or guidance, making them meaningless platitudes about customer satisfaction and excellence. Worse, they create impossible ideals that set companies up to fail by turning every normal shortcoming into a perceived betrayal. Happy customers see the mission as merely meeting the stated bar, while unhappy ones feel personally deceived. The only mission statement worth printing would be one that actually said something contrarian.
key_points: Mission statements that involve no trade-offs offer no actual guidance for decision-making
Lofty promises create a no-win scenario: happy customers see it as baseline expectation, unhappy ones feel betrayed
Corporate mission statements create impossible ideals disconnected from everyday business reality
Every normal business failing gets reframed as a transgression against the company's stated mission
money_quotes: Chances are far better that the rainbow springs straight out of a dumpster than a pot of gold, and that the mission statement is trash too.
These mission statements – that never contain neither a real mission nor any interesting statements – involve no trade-offs, and thus offer no guidance.
A mission statement that actually said "fuck the customers, we strive for mediocrity, and this is all about the short term"? Now that would be worth printing.
Waxing lyrically about all the happy customers, the happy employees, and all the other win-win bullshit is placing a bet you can only lose. Zero upside, unlimited downside.
When customers invariably are disgruntled, they'll point to your saccharine mission statement and not just feel aggrieved, but betrayed.
It creates an impossible ideal crafted in an abstract world that has no bearing on reality.
posts.key_insight: Mission statements that express no trade-offs provide no guidance, and by setting impossible ideals they guarantee that every normal failing feels like a betrayal rather than just business as usual.

posts.summary: DHH argues that businesses cannot codify every decision into written rules, and attempting to do so would create a constraining straightjacket. Instead, organizations must rely on cultural intuition — the ability to extrapolate from a company's explicit and implicit values to handle novel situations. This capacity develops primarily through osmosis, by observing how decisions are made and internalizing the patterns. When cultural intuition is working well, organizations move with less friction and greater alignment.
key_points: Written rules and decision trees can never fully capture how a business should operate, and over-codifying becomes a straightjacket
Cultural intuition is developed through osmosis — watching decisions, pace, and standards over time and internalizing them
New employees start with an 'empty stomach' for cultural intuition and must build it gradually
When cultural intuition is dialed in, the energy required to go from idea to action to results decreases significantly
money_quotes: We can't write down everything that factors into the conduct of the business.
Such a tome would become a straight jacket sooner than it would serve our drive and progress.
The root of good inference is cultural intuition.
The ability to extrapolate from the explicit and implicit gestalt of the business, and use that to decide how we might tackle the novel and unknown in its spirit.
You watch how decisions are made, how pace is set, how bars are cleared, and you internalize all these factors and more in your gut.
The energy required to get from idea to action to results is less. You're swimming with the stream, in your own way, with your own flair, but in the same direction as the rest of the school.
posts.key_insight: Organizations thrive not by codifying every rule but by cultivating cultural intuition — the shared instinct that lets people make good decisions in novel situations without being told what to do.

posts.summary: DHH warns about the dangers of consolidating all digital services with a single tech company, citing two alarming cases. A San Francisco father lost his entire Google account over a false AI-generated child pornography accusation, and an Apple customer had their digital life erased over a billing dispute. Even when proven wrong, these companies feel no obligation to reverse their decisions. DHH argues this represents a fundamental vulnerability in modern life where everyone must choose between two monopolists who hold absolute power over their digital existence.
key_points: A father lost all Google services after AI falsely flagged a medical photo of his toddler as child pornography, and Google refused to restore access even after police exoneration
Apple similarly destroyed a customer's entire digital life over a billing dispute, demonstrating this isn't isolated to one company
The smartphone duopoly means everyone must entrust their digital life to either Apple or Google with no real alternative
These companies act as judge, jury, and executioner with no meaningful accountability or appeals process
Users should diversify their critical digital services across multiple providers to reduce catastrophic risk
money_quotes: You pick either Apple or Google, and if they decide to pick you back, you're just fucked.
The judge, jury, and executioner had spoken, and the word was final.
Even when proven wrong, Google and Apple feel under no obligation to correct their mistakes.
This should make everyone think about what they might have to lose, if they run afoul of either of these monopolists.
Puff, iCloud gone, App Store gone, Apple ID accounts gone.
posts.key_insight: Consolidating your digital life with a single tech monopolist creates an existential vulnerability, because these companies can and do destroy users' entire digital existence over mistakes they refuse to correct.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the psychological utility of religious faith, specifically the concept of 'God's will' as a mechanism for accepting fate. He argues that Nietzsche's Amor Fati and Stoic philosophy offer intellectual equivalents but lack the effortless comfort of theological surrender. He frames religion as 'social software' that has been refined over millennia, offering scalable meaning-making that secular alternatives like Wokeness or MAGA fail to replicate. He concludes with sympathy for Nietzsche's declaration of God's death as an expression of loss rather than triumph.
key_points: Religious faith offers an easier path to accepting fate than secular philosophies like Stoicism or Existentialism, which demand intellectual effort and solitary strength
God's will functions as a 'scalable form of Amor Fati' — religion as social software that packages acceptance of fate for mass adoption
Modern secular replacements for religion (Wokeness, MAGA) are inferior to systems refined over thousands of years
Nietzsche's 'God is dead' was a statement of sorrow, not celebration — seeing the world clearly comes at a psychological cost
money_quotes: The ability to accept reality as it unfolds is a gift Christians is offered in their communal theology, but one that atheists have to struggle and strive to revive mostly in solitude.
What I miss about the faith-based embrace of God's will, and the supporting canon of The Mysterious Ways, is exactly that intellectual surrender.
It's just such an easier sell than the one about being thrown into an absurd, uncaring, and random world.
God's will as a concept is a scalable form of Amor Fati.
It's an example of a feature in the religious social software humankind has been debugging for a couple of millennium.
It's hard to create a somewhat coherent religion with overall better outcomes for more people than the systems that have been refined for thousands of years.
You may see the world As It Really Is, whatever that means, but you might soon enough wish you'd taken the blue pill, and stayed together with the tribe inside the ropes of the magic kingdom.
posts.key_insight: Religion's concept of God's will is a brilliantly scalable piece of social software that secular philosophy has never matched for helping ordinary people accept the uncontrollable.

posts.summary: DHH argues that taking on more responsibility is the path to finding meaning in life, counterintuitive as it may seem. He draws on a Danish school ship program that transformed struggling young adults by giving them real duties and consequences. Influenced by Jordan Peterson's 12 Rules for Life, he connects this idea to why people voluntarily contribute to open source software. The key distinction is that the responsibility must involve genuine agency—being able to control and influence outcomes. He suggests that what people diagnose as burnout may actually be 'under-purpose,' and that a heavier but more meaningful load could be the cure.
key_points: Many people struggle not from too much responsibility but from too little—not enough is asked of them
A Danish school ship program showed that giving struggling young adults real duties and consequences gave them purpose and gratitude
The voluntary nature of open source contribution illustrates how people seek out additional responsibility because it provides meaning
The responsibility must involve genuine agency and control—being a passenger doesn't count
What feels like burnout may actually be 'under-purpose' that could be addressed with more meaningful work, not less
money_quotes: That the antidote to a flailing existence might be more responsibility can come off as counterintuitive.
Lots of people are struggling exactly because not enough is asked of them.
Finally someone was counting on them, they mattered, and if they didn't do their work, there would be consequences. It gave them a purpose.
It simply feels good to take on more responsibility, and it fills your life with meaning.
If you assume responsibility over a process that you can't control, or barely even influence, then you're just a passenger, not an agent of action.
It's exactly assuming agency that provides the meaning. I'm doing stuff, that stuff matters, and the world is better off because of it.
Could you counter the burnout of under-purpose with a more meaningful load, even if it's heavier?
posts.key_insight: What people often experience as burnout or existential drift may actually be under-purpose, and the counterintuitive remedy is taking on more meaningful responsibility rather than retreating from it.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the coordinated deplatforming of Andrew Tate from social media is hypocritical and dangerous, drawing parallels between Tate's misogynistic rhetoric and decades of celebrated hip hop lyrics containing similar themes. He contends that the bar for 'unpersoning' someone from social media has rapidly lowered from Alex Jones to Tate, echoing the ACLU's historic principle that free speech must be defended in its most repugnant forms. He concludes that the ban is likely counterproductive, giving Tate a martyr narrative and more visibility than he had before.
key_points: Celebrated hip hop artists like Dr. Dre, Jay-Z, and Eminem have expressed views about women as bad or worse than Andrew Tate's, yet remain cultural icons
The threshold for deplatforming has rapidly dropped from Alex Jones (conspiracy theorist harassing Sandy Hook parents) to Andrew Tate (misogynist influencer) in just three years
The ACLU once defended literal Nazis' right to march in Skokie, understanding that free speech must be defended in repugnant cases or it erodes entirely
Banning Tate is counterproductive — it gave him far more visibility and a credible martyr narrative
The pattern mirrors conservatives' failed attempts to ban rap music in the 1990s
money_quotes: This unique, terrible, unaccountable power to unperson someone from the entire realm of social media was supposed to be reserved only for unique, terrible dangers.
It took just a few years to lower the bar from 'batshit conspiracy theorist harassing Sandyhook parents' to 'dude whose views on women we find abhorrent'.
Why on earth would you think the ride would stop with Tate?
Unless you take a stand for free speech in the cases most people would find repugnant, you'll gradually lose the right altogether.
The principled discussion is simply getting boiled away, and we're left with the dichotomous bones of 'are you for or against Andrew Tate'?
I'd literally never heard of Andrew Tate until he got banned. Now he's everywhere, and has a credible martyr role to drape his spiel in.
Did progressives learn nothing from conservatives trying to ban rap music in the 90s?
posts.key_insight: The coordinated deplatforming of controversial figures represents a dangerous erosion of free speech norms that is both hypocritical — given society's embrace of equally offensive artistic expression — and counterproductive, turning banned figures into martyrs.

posts.summary: DHH argues that being wrong is a valuable experience because it leads to learning and improved judgment. He frames decision-making as probabilistic — anyone making enough calls will inevitably be wrong some percentage of the time. He contends that a perfect track record simply means you never took risks. The post encourages swinging confidently while maintaining the humility to learn from mistakes.
key_points: Being wrong is a feature, not a bug — it corrects misconceptions and builds humility
Decision-making should be thought of in bets: some percentage of calls will inevitably be wrong
A perfect record means you never took real swings — staying safe isn't the same as being right
The goal is to keep making bold judgment calls while embracing mistakes as learning opportunities
money_quotes: It's a treat to be wrong.

If you hold a hundred opinions or make a hundred judgement calls, you're guaranteed that some percentage of those opinions will be misinformed and some of those calls will turn out poorly. That's just the math of being human.
Even when you think you're right. Especially when you're convinced you're right.
Anyone with a perfect batting record has never swung at a fast ball.
It's easy being right all the time if you stay out of the game.
Always swing confidently to the best of your ability. Always embrace a bad hit with the humility of a learner.
Loving the wrong makes the right.
posts.key_insight: Thinking in bets — accepting that a percentage of your confident judgments will be wrong — is not a reason to stop swinging but a reason to swing with humility.

posts.summary: DHH argues that society benefits from unreasonable people who pursue audacious ideas, using Elon Musk's electric car revolution as the primary example. He notes that after someone like Musk achieves something transformative, polite society tries to temper their unreasonableness, which he considers foolish. While acknowledging that not every unreasonable idea will pan out, DHH contends that the same irrational self-confidence that produces failures also produces world-changing breakthroughs. Society can simply ignore the ideas that fail and celebrate the ones that succeed.
key_points: Transformative achievements require unreasonable people with unreasonable self-confidence
Society wrongly tries to domesticate visionaries after their initial success, wanting them to become 'reasonable'
Not every unreasonable idea will succeed, but the mindset that produces failures also produces breakthroughs
The asymmetry of outcomes favors tolerating unreasonableness: we can ignore failures but benefit enormously from successes
money_quotes: It takes unreasonable people to do unreasonable things.
The unreasonable people who end up changing the world continue to be unreasonable after their crowning achievement has become a success.
Polite society is so eager to squeeze the unreasonable qualities out of them after the fact. It's foolish.
We should be grateful for the unreasonable self-confidence that makes Musk believe this to be true! That's where we get the big, interesting swings from.
The cone of confidence that protects his motivation from that of more reasonable people, and their oh-so-reasonable objections.
We're better off as a society when unreasonable people continue to be unreasonable, undeterred by the chatter and the criticism.
We, mostly, have the license to ignore those unreasonable ideas that peter out, and revel in the ones that soar.
posts.key_insight: Society benefits most when it stops trying to make visionaries reasonable after their first success, because the same irrational confidence that produces failures also produces world-changing breakthroughs.

posts.summary: DHH explores the idea that humans have an innate need for struggle, drawing on Orwell's observation that we can imagine hell but not heaven, and Dostoevsky's claim that people will reject rational bliss to preserve autonomy. He argues that modern society, having solved many existential problems, now generates invented struggles—from prolonged COVID culture wars to woke ideology and its conservative counter-reaction. DHH suggests that traditional religion may have served as a healthier containment system for this psychological need, channeling the yearning for struggle through predefined narratives rather than destructive cultural battles. He concludes by recommending readers engage with Orwell, Dostoevsky, and Sowell to navigate these tensions.
key_points: Humans have a deep psychological need for struggle and will invent one when real existential challenges recede
Modern culture wars—COVID debates, woke ideology, counter-reactions—are symptoms of manufactured struggle by spiritually malnourished populations
Traditional religion may have been a superior mechanism for channeling humanity's innate need for conflict and meaning
The collective wisdom embedded in tradition, culture, and law is difficult to articulate but structurally profound, and we're only now seeing consequences of its retreat
The rapid establishment of progressive orthodoxy has paradoxically made conservative traditionalism the new counterculture
money_quotes: While humans can imagine hell in minute detail, heaven escapes anything but the most fuzzy, vague descriptions.
This yearning for an identity-defining struggle is so great that humans will invent one if need be.

Bredding pathological attacks on the pillars of society, and even civilization itself, in the search for a struggle worthy of the self-actualization needs present in especially the over-educated, spiritually-malnourished part of the populace.
We were broadly better off when the big, existential struggles were derived mostly from religious roleplaying.
Without a broad religious operating system, we end up chasing the same bugs in our innate psyche, but the modern fixes are often way worse.

The collective wisdom of the ages, as passed down to us through tradition, culture, and law, is often very hard to reason specifically about after the fact. But that doesn't mean the wisdom isn't present.
Being transgressive is now defined as the act of wearing a cross and attending latin mass.
posts.key_insight: Humanity's hardwired need for struggle, once channeled through religion, now manifests as destructive culture wars because modern secular society offers no healthy substitute.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how expectations shape our experience of outcomes, using his 2nd place finish at the European Le Mans Series at Spa as a personal example. He draws a parallel to the well-known finding that Olympic silver medalists are less satisfied than bronze medalists. After a disastrous practice and qualifying session that left his team starting dead last in 39th, an extraordinary turnaround to 2nd place felt better than most wins. He concludes that this dynamic of expectations governing satisfaction applies universally across sports, business, and life.
key_points: Low expectations can make a good outcome feel better than a great outcome achieved under high expectations
The Olympic silver vs bronze medalist satisfaction paradox illustrates how relative framing matters more than absolute results
Dramatic turnarounds create euphoria precisely because they violate negative expectations
The principle applies universally: sports, business, and life outcomes are all filtered through the lens of what we expected
money_quotes: It's well established that Olympians who finish 2nd are less satisfied than the ones who finish 3rd.
Expectations at work!
The extreme turn-around made the whole team euphoric as we crossed the finish line.
You think you deserve to win? Anything but is now a loss. You think you're nowhere? Reaching the podium is now a triumph.
This is true in sports, this is true in business, this is true in life.
posts.key_insight: Satisfaction is determined not by the outcome itself but by the gap between expectations and results — low expectations can make a good result feel extraordinary while high expectations can make the same result feel like failure.

posts.summary: DHH argues against the lawsuit investigation targeting GitHub's Copilot AI, framing it as scarcity thinking that betrays the spirit of open source. He dismisses claims that Copilot will create a walled garden that starves open source communities, calling it classic zero-sum thinking. He views Copilot as exactly the kind of remix and reuse that open source was meant to enable. He draws a parallel to Oracle's attempt to copyright Java APIs, warning that the open source community risks becoming the gatekeepers it was meant to fight.
key_points: The lawsuit investigation against Copilot employs scarcity scarecrow tactics that contradict the collaborative spirit of open source
The claim that AI code completion will destroy open source communities is illogical because half the value of open source is ongoing community collaboration, not one-off code snippets
If we indict AI intermediation of open source code, the same logic would condemn CDNs, package bundlers, and other tools that serve code without directing users to project homepages
The investigation mirrors Oracle's attempt to copyright Java APIs—using law to lock software into scarcity thinking
Open source creators who share code freely should welcome innovations like Copilot that build upon their work in creative ways
money_quotes: I love the ethos of open source: Free code creating a true commons in software.
This is classic zero-sum thinking. The kind that open source at its best transcends for me, and Open Source at its worst entrenches.
Isn't this partly why we share our code to begin with? To enable others to remix, reuse, and regenerate with?

There's a funny mirror version of this investigation that looks an awfully lot like Oracle's tragic attempt to copyright the Java APIs. To use the law to lock critical software into this dungeon of scarcity thinking.
Aren't these the demons we in the open source community were meant to be fighting rather than become?
These are the ways of closed software. The greed with which free use is often curtailed, unless it's explicitly allowed.
To escape this trap of hoarding positive rights, lest someone, somewhere, might get away with some inspired feat of unauthorized creativity that builds upon my work.
posts.key_insight: The open source community risks betraying its own values by applying scarcity thinking to AI tools like Copilot that remix and build upon freely shared code—the very thing open source was designed to enable.

posts.summary: DHH admits to being a very late adopter of LinkedIn after nearly twenty years of avoiding it, having dismissed it as a lame business version of Facebook. He argues that LinkedIn's perceived lameness has become its greatest strength, as the professional norms constrain people's behavior in ways that make discourse more pleasant than Twitter's toxic free-for-all. He acknowledges the irony of celebrating compartmentalized personas after years of combative Twitter engagement. The post concludes that the 'business casual' nature of LinkedIn connections is preferable to the unfiltered chaos of platforms where people bring their whole selves.
key_points: LinkedIn's professional norms and social restraint have become virtues as other platforms descended into toxicity
The collapse of boundaries between personal and professional identity on Twitter made discourse worse for everyone
Even 'fake' professional personas are preferable to being subjected to people's full unfiltered personalities
DHH acknowledges the irony of celebrating compartmentalization after years of combative Twitter participation
A throttled, curated connection is more valuable than full-bandwidth authenticity online
money_quotes: The restraint that people show in their virtual khakis and button-downs on a business network is apparently exactly the antidote we need in 2022 to stay even remotely within the boundaries of respectable exchanges online.
It's a place where people are inclined to bring their work self to the conversation. A place almost entirely free of the endless, droning moral grandstanding that has taken over Twitter.
It just doesn't matter if you don't have to know!
The entirety of someone's personality and predilections were suddenly thrust upon you, whether you wanted to know or not. And mostly, I can assuredly say, you did not!
After a solid decade duking it out on Twitter in plenty campaigns that had more than a pale moral hue to them, I would celebrate the compartmentalization of someone's public persona.
If we absolutely must use the internet to 'connect', then I welcome a throttled connection.
Many of our brightest dreams have turned into our darkest nightmares.
Rather than the full-bandwidth bananas bonkers nonsense of Your Whole Self.
posts.key_insight: Social platforms benefit from constraints on self-expression — the professional norms that make LinkedIn feel 'fake' are precisely what make it more functional than platforms that encourage radical authenticity.

posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals is leaving the cloud after over a decade of running services on AWS and Google Cloud. He argues that renting computers is a bad deal for medium-sized companies with stable, predictable growth, noting that Basecamp and HEY are paying enormous premiums for elasticity they don't need. He dismantles the marketing narratives around cloud simplicity, pointing out that operations teams don't shrink just because you move to the cloud. Beyond cost, he frames the decision as a matter of internet decentralization, arguing it's tragic that a handful of mega corporations now control the infrastructure of a system designed to be distributed.
key_points: Cloud computing only makes economic sense at two extremes: very simple/low-traffic apps, or highly irregular/unpredictable loads — neither applies to 37signals today
HEY pays over $500K/year just for database and search services from Amazon, a premium DHH considers absurd given how much hardware that could buy outright
The promise that cloud simplicity would reduce operations staff never materialized — running major services in the cloud is not actually simpler overall
AWS prints nearly 30% profit margins renting servers, but the industry frames it as futuristic 'computing on demand' rather than the mundane reality of renting computers
The concentration of the internet on a few mega-corporations' infrastructure is a threat to the decentralized architecture the internet was built on
money_quotes: Renting computers is (mostly) a bad deal for medium-sized companies like ours with stable growth.
It's like paying a quarter of your house's value for earthquake insurance when you don't live anywhere near a fault line.
Do you know how many insanely beefy servers you could purchase on a budget of half a million dollars per year?
Anyone who thinks running a major service like HEY or Basecamp in the cloud is "simple" has clearly never tried.
The cloud is sold as computing on demand, which sounds futuristic and cool, and very much not like something as mundane as "renting computers", even though that's mostly what it is.
It strikes me as downright tragic that this decentralized wonder of the world is now largely operating on computers owned by a handful of mega corporations.

Don't let the entrenched cloud interests dazzle you into believing that running your own setup is too complicated. Everyone and their dog did it to get the internet off the ground, and it's only gotten easier since.
posts.key_insight: For companies with stable, predictable workloads, the cloud is an expensive rental arrangement dressed up in futuristic marketing — and its dominance threatens the decentralized nature of the internet itself.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on a decade-long mistake at 37signals: telling themselves that employees shouldn't feel surprised when let go. He argues that emotions don't follow logic, and trying to prescribe how someone should feel about being terminated is both presumptuous and callous. The post concludes that we can only control our own actions and intentions, not others' emotional responses.
key_points: Emotions operate on 'sovereign ground' and don't flow neatly from logic
37signals spent a decade wrongly believing they could prevent surprise at terminations through good process
Prescribing emotional responses to bad news adds an unfair burden on the person already suffering
Leaders can only control their own actions and intentions, not how others receive them
money_quotes: Emotions roam on sovereign ground, and foreign prescriptions feel like invasions.
We've made this mistake for at least a decade at 37signals by trying to tell ourselves that "nobody should feel surprised if they're let go".
Not only is it presumptuous to believe this is even possible, it's actually callous.
It attempts to place an additional burden of serenity and immediate acceptance on the shoulders of someone already carrying the weight of a termination.
Someone in that position should be free to be surprised. And mad. And sad. Regardless of the circumstances or the logic.
All we get to control are our own actions and intentions.
posts.key_insight: Expecting employees to respond rationally to being fired is not enlightened management — it's an additional cruelty that denies people their right to feel what they feel.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Twitter's 7,500-person workforce is absurdly bloated, comparing it unfavorably to WhatsApp's 50-person team that served nearly a billion users. He supports Musk's plan to cut Twitter's staff to 2,000, noting that the current massive team has failed to improve the platform while losing hundreds of millions of dollars. He frames Musk's acquisition as a bailout for shareholders and employees, and argues that radical restructuring is the least controversial path forward given Twitter's ongoing dysfunction.
key_points: WhatsApp served nearly 500 million users with 50 people while Twitter needs 7,500 for half the user count
Twitter's massive workforce has produced poor results — the platform is widely considered dysfunctional and lost over $200 million last year
Media coverage hysterically focuses on the dangers of staff cuts rather than questioning why so many employees haven't fixed obvious problems
Musk's offer to buy Twitter at over double WhatsApp's acquisition price is a financial bailout regardless of outcome
Twitter may be unfixable, but radical reimagining of operations is more rational than continuing the status quo
money_quotes: WhatsApp had almost half a billion monthly users, but a team of just 50 people running everything. Compare this to Twitter, which today has a staff of 7,500 to manage half the number of users.
I guess it's more like a clown town that just happened to be founded on a gold mine, yet somehow still manages to turn gold into lead.

If there's one thing everyone seems to agree on, it's that Twitter in its current form is an absolute dumpster fire. One that the more than seven thousand employees still haven't managed to put out after years and years of trying almost nothing.

Shareholders and employees should be grateful that Mr Musk made his historically ill-timed offer to buy the business for more than double what Zuckerberg paid for WhatsApp. It's a bailout for the ages.
Twitter needs a fundamental rethink. More of the same from many of the same will give you exactly what you got: shockingly little.

The problem with the thousands and thousands of people smashing those block buttons for spam, ideological transgressions, or whatever else gets you banned from the platform these days, is that there aren't enough of them and that they aren't smashing harder!
It's entirely possible that Twitter is unfixable. But the least controversial attempt to at least try is to radically reimagine how the service is operated and by whom.
posts.key_insight: Twitter's bloated headcount and persistent dysfunction prove that throwing more people at a broken organization produces nothing — radical downsizing and reimagination is the rational response, not a scandal.

posts.summary: DHH argues against continuing to invest in projects whose impact requires sophisticated measurement to detect. He advocates for focusing energy on efforts with clearly visible results, setting predetermined deadlines for evaluation, and having the discipline to abandon what isn't working. The post warns that accumulating micro-improvements creates complexity without meaningful progress. He frames letting go of underperforming projects as a virtue, not a failure.
key_points: If you need a statistical microscope to detect an improvement, it's probably not worth the effort
Set a proportionate future deadline to evaluate projects, then stick to the decision
Accumulating marginal micro-improvements creates heavy baggage that obscures the big picture
Abandoning failed experiments is a feature of the scientific method, not a sign of failure
Mental space for new possibilities requires letting go of mediocre existing commitments
money_quotes: The more sophisticated you have to be to tell whether there's a positive effect, the less likely it is to be worth the effort.
Spend your energy where it's plain as day when it works.
These "improvements" then accumulate until you have a bag full of feathers that still weigh a ton.
The more such micro improvements you collect, the greater the odds that the big picture drifts away.
There's no shame in trying something, seeing that it doesn't make enough of a difference to continue, and then letting it go.
The only way to make mental space for new ideas, new experiments, and new possibilities is to let go of mediocre ideas, unfulfilling experiments, and disappointing actualities.
Stop hoarding the insignificant.
posts.key_insight: If an improvement requires sophisticated measurement to prove it exists, it's not worth pursuing — focus on changes with obviously visible impact.

posts.summary: DHH dissects a jargon-laden Snap earnings statement, showing how corporate language obscures simple truths. He compresses Snap's wordy excuse into one plain sentence: they're selling fewer ads because of inflation and higher interest rates. He argues that clarity isn't valued when delivering bad news to Wall Street, because obfuscation distracts from the fundamental reality of losses. He concludes by expressing relief that 37signals, as a private company, never has to engage in this performative corporate communication.
key_points: Corporate jargon in earnings reports doesn't compress complexity — it obscures simple, unflattering truths
Snap's multi-clause excuse reduces to one plain sentence about selling fewer ads due to inflation and interest rates
The lack of clarity is a feature, not a bug, when companies need to soften bad financial news for impatient markets
The ritual is absurd because sophisticated investors see through the jargon anyway, making it a hollow performance
Being a private company means never having to perform this kind of corporate theater
money_quotes: The jargon isn't compressing any compelling complexity, the cliches aren't making the bad news any easier to digest.
We're selling fewer ads because of inflation and higher interest rates.
Clarity isn't a feature when explaining bad numbers to an impatient market.
The more hand-wavy, long-winded bullshit you can blow, the less likely the focus remains on the fundamental truth: You're losing money holding our stock.
This dance is fascinating. And by fascinating, I mostly mean deeply off-putting.
Holy hell I'm happy we never have to dress up and pretend like this at 37signals.
posts.key_insight: Corporate jargon in earnings reports is deliberately unclear because clarity would force companies to state uncomfortable truths in terms investors can't ignore.

posts.summary: DHH argues that social media has destroyed the once-normal practice of keeping political views private, to everyone's detriment. He recalls a time when you rarely knew the specific political leanings of friends and coworkers, and it didn't matter. Now constant political broadcasting on social media narrows our perception of others, hardens our stances, and poisons politically diverse relationships. He advocates for returning to political privacy as both a personal and societal good, suggesting we delegate political engagement to professional politicians and specific advocates.
key_points: Before social media, political affiliations were rarely known or relevant in personal relationships
Private voting exists not just to prevent intimidation but to protect relationships from political imposition
Constant political broadcasting creates a feedback loop of narrowing perception, hardening stances, and poisoning relationships
Political privacy is both a personal virtue and a societal good necessary for functioning democracy
money_quotes: Before social media, I couldn't tell you what narrow political box to put most of my friends or coworkers.
There's a reason we vote in private. It's not just to prevent overt intimidation in the booth, but also to avoid fracturing otherwise happy relationships with the imposition.
We've lost that wisdom in the social media age. Every political like, retweet, forwarded article, and indignant post broadcasts and entrenches exactly where we stand to ourselves and others.
This way of living is not only narrowing our perception of others, and hardening our stances on the issues, it's poisoning our ability to have politically diverse friendships and relationships.
A polity where everyone, everywhere constantly flaunt their partisan leanings is one that'll eventually careen off a cliff.
Speech is silver, silence is golden.
posts.key_insight: Social media's destruction of political privacy has created a toxic feedback loop that narrows perception, hardens positions, and poisons the politically diverse relationships a healthy society depends on.

posts.summary: DHH argues that European attempts to comply with the Schrems II ruling by relocating data to EU-based servers are a complete sham, because US intelligence services can compel any American company to hand over data regardless of where servers are physically located. He explains that FISA Section 702 gives US agencies legal power over any US-controlled entity, making server location irrelevant. The new Trans-Atlantic Data Privacy Framework is just another temporary fix that will likely be struck down again. DHH concludes that Europe must either accept American surveillance as the cost of using US services or erect a genuine privacy wall, rather than continuing the expensive legal charade.
key_points: US surveillance law (FISA Section 702) compels American companies to hand over foreign data regardless of server location, making EU data residency requirements meaningless

The European Court of Justice's Schrems II ruling logically implies a ban on European companies using American internet services, but the court left implementation to individual companies, creating an expensive legal industry
37signals investigated whether any corporate structure could shield European data from US authorities and concluded it was impossible
The new Trans-Atlantic Data Privacy Framework (Privacy Shield 2.0) offers vague commitments with enough caveats for the US to continue business as usual
The cycle of framework-ruling-framework creates billable hours for lawyers while changing nothing about actual surveillance practices
money_quotes: Those mass surveillance mechanisms don't care in the slightest where the data is physically located. They only care about who controls the data, and whether those entities can be compelled to comply with US law.
It is impossible for an American company to offer any guarantees of privacy to European companies that sidestep mechanisms like the FISA Section 702.
It simply shifted the burden of arriving at the logical conclusion – a ban on most American internet services – onto individual companies.

It includes a laundry list of vague commitments to European privacy principles while offering the Americans so many caveats that they can continue doing whatever the hell they want and have done all along.

One which invalidates the millions of billable hours invoiced by lawyers trying to find a bespoke way out of the uncertain liability since 2020. Brilliant. The perfect illustration of our age of bullshit jobs.

Meanwhile the real winners here are the companies that never bothered to engage in the charade to comply, the lawyers that played their expensive parts in the charade that was performed, and the American data spies who never skipped a byte.
American companies will never be able to resist the demands of American intelligence services. It doesn't matter if their servers are located in Virginia or Paris or on the damn moon.
posts.key_insight: No amount of server relocation or corporate restructuring can protect European data from US surveillance because American law targets who controls the data, not where it physically resides.

posts.summary: DHH reacts to Elon Musk's acquisition of Twitter and the open letter written by Twitter employees demanding job security, benefits preservation, and dignified treatment. He argues the employees' demands are delusional given that Musk bought the company specifically to change how it operates, and that demanding no layoffs from a new owner who overpaid for an unprofitable company is fundamentally unrealistic. He characterizes the letter as performance theater rather than genuine negotiation, noting the employees have no leverage against someone like Musk. While extending some sympathy for the turbulent acquisition process, he ultimately views the demands as entitled and self-defeating.
key_points: Twitter employees' open letter demanding preserved headcount and benefits from a new owner of an unprofitable company is fundamentally delusional
No new owner who massively overpaid for a money-losing company would commit to zero layoffs or perpetual preservation of existing arrangements
The employees have no leverage and are using self-defeating negotiation tactics against someone like Musk
The open letter is better understood as performance theater than a serious attempt at negotiation
The entire point of the $44 billion acquisition was to run Twitter differently
money_quotes: You'd think they were giving an account of working for the mob. Not the reality of being highly-paid tech workers at an unprofitable social media platform that just got a new owner who bought the place to turn the ship around.
What new owner, especially one who just massively overpaid for a company that's been losing hundreds of millions of dollars lately, would commit not to layoff a single employee?
The whole point of Musk buying the Twitter was to run it differently! That's what the $44 billion offer yield in return: The power to make new plans for the company.
This is not just a delusional list, but also a self-defeating negotiation tactic. The Twitter employees who conceived of this letter have little to no leverage.
Much easier to explain the delusional demands as performance theater. Acting out the righteous belief that We, The Twitter Workers, Stand United Before The Great Peril.
That's not a free pass to throw entitled tantrums or demands.
posts.key_insight: Employees demanding that a new owner preserve every job and benefit at an unprofitable company he bought specifically to overhaul reveals a fundamental disconnect between tech worker entitlement and business reality.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Twitter should stop trying to be a content moderation platform and instead embrace its role as a fundamental internet protocol, like HTTP or SMTP. He contends that Twitter's core problem is shoving unsolicited content in front of users rather than letting them control their own experience. His three-part fix: show only content from people you follow, let authors moderate their own threads, and replace negative content bans with positive editorial curation. He frames this as aligning Twitter with the existing wisdom of Section 230 and the open internet, where individuals and local jurisdictions handle speech rather than global content moderators.
key_points: Twitter should function as a neutral protocol like HTTP or SMTP, not as a curated content platform with centralized moderation
The core problem is Twitter shoving unsolicited content at users — if people only saw what they chose to follow, most moderation problems would disappear
Authors should be deputized as moderators of their own threads, similar to how blog owners manage their comment sections
Speech regulation should follow existing jurisdictional laws rather than a global content moderation council
Promoted content should go through a positive editorial process (picking what to feature) rather than a negative one (banning what's disliked)
money_quotes: The internet is full of the bland, the wonderful, the obscure, the sacrilegious, the offensive, the infuriating, and the nasty. The internet, as a whole, is governed by local laws on speech, not by a council of global content moderators, not by single individuals. This is a feature not a bug.
Twitter should embrace these features, and accept its role as a fundamental protocol. Just like HTTP, just like SMTP, just like FTP.

You know that you can find the most vile, nasty shit on the internet if you search for the right words on Google. So most people just don't do that! They manage their own exposure by moderating their own behavior.
If Twitter stopped shoving all this speech you never asked to see in front of you, there would be no need for an army of content moderators.
Authors should be deputized as content moderators of their own posts. Just like someone hosting a blog with comments on the internet is.
Accept that the price we pay for an open, free internet is one which permits the darker sides of humanity.
All the content moderators in the world couldn't put the utopia that a platform for hundreds of millions won't contain disagreeable speech together again.
posts.key_insight: Twitter's moderation crisis stems from its algorithmic feed pushing unsolicited content at users — fix that by making it a neutral protocol where individuals control their own exposure, and the moderation problem largely solves itself.

posts.summary: DHH argues that working under bad bosses is one of the most valuable career experiences because it teaches you what not to do more effectively than working in well-run organizations. He recounts an early career experience where a boss rearranged office seating for investor aesthetics, creating an uncomfortable fishbowl environment. This experience crystallized his commitment to letting people work where and how they choose. He acknowledges he's likely made bad decisions as a boss himself, and hopes those experiences similarly motivate others to form their own principles.
key_points: Negative experiences under bad management are more instructive than the subtle lessons of well-run companies
Bad bosses often lack the formative experience of having worked under bad bosses themselves, creating a cycle of poor management
Vanity-driven workplace decisions, especially ones the boss won't follow themselves, are particularly corrosive
The strongest management principles are forged in opposition to bad experiences rather than imitation of good ones
money_quotes: It takes a lot longer to learn how to run a good business, if your lessons are derived solely from the confines of calm, comfortable companies operated by kind and caring bosses.
I was very fortunate to have plenty of bad bosses early on in my career. Bosses who instilled in me an urge to operate differently.
It left me with a revulsion to dictating the physical working environment of others on the basis of vanity, especially if the boss wasn't willing to abide themselves.
And if I ever got to call the shots somewhere, I'd make damn sure that people were free to work where and how they saw fit.
I hope that perception at least serves as a spark for some of them to start their own businesses from chiseled principles that were carved in opposition.

Everyone deserves at least a few bad bosses in their career to teach them what not to do, illuminate what really matters to them, and hopefully endow them with a perspective on how they'd operate themselves if they ever got the chance.
posts.key_insight: The strongest leadership principles are forged not by imitating good bosses but by experiencing bad ones and resolving to do the opposite.

posts.summary: DHH argues that productive company culture requires a clearly set pace for shipping work, using 37signals' Shape Up methodology as his model: six-week cycles, two-person teams, with unfinished projects getting canceled. He connects this to Elon Musk's Twitter takeover, framing it as shock therapy for a company with an extraordinarily low pace where employees could go years without shipping anything. While he disagrees with Musk's specific work ethic demands, he finds the argument for dramatic pace correction compelling.
key_points: A clearly set pace is the foundation of productive company culture — when pace is wrong, everything else falters
37signals proves major features can ship in six weeks with two-person teams, sustainably and without burnout
Twitter exemplified extreme lethargy: a former 37signals hire had spent two years at Twitter without shipping anything
Musk's Twitter takeover is shock therapy — an entrepreneur's urgency colliding with a culture rewarded for inaction
DHH rejects Musk's specific methods (in-office mandates, insane hours) while endorsing the underlying goal of raising the pace
money_quotes: A productive company culture is one with a clearly set pace.
Projects that fail to ship within that timeframe are canceled.
This person had spent over two years at Twitter and never shipping anything.
It was mockups, meetings, and then eventual cancelations that propelled life in the product group at that time.

An entrepreneur and engineer used to making big moves quickly, and broadly being rewarded for such impatience with progress and success, and a company and its staff used to making no moves slowly, and also broadly being rewarded with such sloth by continued existence and employment.

I don't subscribe to Musk's concept of a work ethic (in-office only, insane hours, sleeping on the floor), but I think there's a cogent argument that this kind of shock therapy is simply what's needed to drag Twitter out of the hole its been in.
Musk took over a Twitter set to one, and now he's turning it up to eleven.
posts.key_insight: Every company needs a clearly set shipping pace, and Twitter's acquisition drama illustrates what happens when an organization's pace has been allowed to stagnate for years — shock therapy may be crude, but the underlying correction is necessary.

posts.summary: DHH draws a parallel between Elon Musk's takeover of Twitter and Steve Jobs' return to Apple in 1997. When Jobs came back, Apple was near death — stock at a 12-year low, hemorrhaging money — and he immediately cut 4,100 employees and killed projects. DHH argues that the press hysteria over Musk's staff cuts echoes the same dire predictions made about Apple's cuts, which proved wrong. He contends that decisive action, even with mistakes, beats organizational paralysis.
key_points: Apple cut 4,100 employees and killed numerous projects immediately after Jobs returned in 1997, when the company was near bankruptcy
Press coverage of Apple's cuts was overwhelmingly negative, warning of lost talent and shaky times ahead — predictions that aged poorly
DHH sees an 'uncanny' parallel between Jobs' Apple turnaround and Musk's Twitter takeover: urgency, customer focus, and willingness to make imperfect decisions
The core argument is that decisive leadership that makes some mistakes beats organizational drift that makes no decisions at all
DHH hedges the comparison while still clearly sympathizing with Musk's approach
money_quotes: Some mistakes will be made. That's good. Because at least some decisions will be made too.
The call to urgency, the focus on the customer, and the willingness to start making decisions knowing some of them will be wrong is uncannily similar.
While everyone seems to be collectively losing their shit right now over Elon's staff cuts, it's worth hearing the echo from Jobs' return back then.
It needed a fundamental reboot, and Steve Jobs delivered just that.
It required some savage cuts to the payroll, to the project portfolio, and to the pride of the company.
The alternative was more of the same, and the same clearly wasn't working.
Maybe before you declare the bird house dead, you ought to let your humility reflect history.
posts.key_insight: Dramatic corporate cuts under decisive new leadership look catastrophic in the moment but may be necessary reboots, and history suggests the naysayers are often wrong.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the era of unprofitable software companies riding venture capital to absurd valuations is over, using Asana, Monday.com, and Smartsheet as cautionary examples. These companies have lost hundreds of millions of dollars annually while their stocks have cratered 62-87%. He extends the warning to private companies like ClickUp, Airtable, and Notion that raised at peak 2021 valuations. DHH contrasts this with 37signals' approach of operating profitably within its means, having survived multiple boom-bust cycles since the original dot-com era. He frames the downturn as an opportunity for sustainable, profit-oriented business building.
key_points: Asana, Monday.com, and Smartsheet have collectively lost billions while their stocks dropped 62-87%, with some running low on cash reserves
The venture capital model accepts 90% mortality rates, enriching VCs at IPO while leaving public investors, employees, and customers exposed when reality hits
Private companies that raised at peak 2021 valuations (ClickUp at $4B, Airtable at $12B, Notion at $10B) face even harder reckonings ahead
37signals has survived every boom-bust cycle since the dot-com era by operating profitably and within its means
The downturn creates opportunity for entrepreneurs, employees, and customers to reconsider the growth-at-all-costs model
money_quotes: Operating within your means, taking profits along the way, and aiming for a stable, long-term business is not just dismissed, but frowned upon.
The logic of venture capital is to rush every business it touches through a steroid program that accepts a mortality rate of up to 90%.
That's closing in on a billion dollars in losses over the last four years. Madness. To use the glib insult that we've also enjoyed receiving over the years: They make fucking todo lists!!
The venture capitalists who invested in Asana, Monday.com, and Smartsheet surely all made out like bandits when these unprofitable software companies went public.
These are all crazy numbers from a crazy time, and now the economic hangover is due.
This recession isn't going to last forever, but it's also barely even begun!
You don't need to sign up for whole or half billion dollar losses over four years to have a shoot at building something awesome, something you can be proud of.
posts.key_insight: The collapse of unprofitable software company valuations vindicates the boring, profitable approach to building software businesses that Silicon Valley has long dismissed as unambitious.

posts.summary: DHH criticizes Spotify for continuing to display COVID-19 warning labels on Joe Rogan's podcast episodes that link to CDC guidance, particularly regarding child vaccination. He contrasts the CDC's aggressive push to vaccinate children as young as 6 months with Scandinavian countries' decisions not to recommend vaccines for children. He catalogs numerous instances where early pandemic consensus positions were later reversed or proven wrong, arguing this pattern should inspire humility rather than censorship. He concludes that while forgiveness for pandemic overreach may be warranted, it must come with the lesson that science is a process of discovery, not dogma.
key_points: Spotify still displays COVID warning labels linking to CDC guidance that contradicts the health policies of Sweden, Denmark, and Norway regarding child vaccination
The musician boycott over Joe Rogan largely collapsed, with most artists quietly returning to Spotify

Numerous early pandemic consensus positions — on masks, outdoor transmission, lockdown harms to children, vaccine transmission blocking, lab leak origins — were later reversed or significantly walked back
Capitalizing 'The Science' into an unquestionable consensus has damaged public trust in scientific institutions more than anything else
Forgiveness for pandemic overreach is possible, but only if the lesson that science is a fallible process — not revealed truth — is fully internalized
money_quotes: Capitalizing The Science and turning it into a consensus cult has done more to undermine public trust in the House of Francis Bacon than even the atom bomb.
Covid in America remains a political struggle rather than a medical one, of course. A flame that continues to attract partisan moths.
That's of course how lowercase science works. It produces hypotheses, which are subjected to the real world, and their ability to be disproven is literally the most important part of the process!
A journey of zig-zagging discovery that ought to have provoked more than just a little hesitation in those eagerly attempting to police any opposition to the temporary conclusions of The Science.
It's a process that often ends down blind alleys, and has to backtrack. A process that desperately needs the voice of an opposition to test, validate, and properly authenticate knowledge.
It's cracked the reputation of scientists who turned dogmatists, and centers for disease control and prevention captured by political directives.

I choose to believe that many of the people who went totalitarian did so in good faith, or that it at least started there. But this only works if the lesson that The Science is not truth is integrated fully.
posts.key_insight: The pandemic revealed that treating evolving scientific understanding as unchallengeable dogma — and censoring dissent accordingly — backfired catastrophically, and any forgiveness for that overreach must come with the hard lesson that science is a process, not a pronouncement.

posts.summary: DHH argues that modern culture has developed an accelerating addiction to 'The Current Villain' — a rotating scapegoat who absorbs collective outrage on social media. Drawing on René Girard's mimetic theory and scapegoating mechanism, he explains how figures like Chapelle, Kanye West, and Elon Musk serve as sacrificial targets to discharge group tension over unresolved cultural issues. He contends that these scapegoating cycles are accelerating because they fail to address underlying conflicts, requiring ever-more-frequent targets. He ends with dark humor, suggesting either the destruction of Twitter or the second coming of Christ as potential remedies.
key_points: Culture has shifted from shared moments of glory to shared villains, with 'The Current Villain' consuming more of collective attention than broader cultural topics
René Girard's scapegoating mechanism explains how communities project unresolved tensions onto individuals who violate group orthodoxy, regardless of actual guilt
The cycle is accelerating because targeting major public figures who can fight back provides less tension relief than early cancel culture's focus on minor characters
The shift from music and culture as shared centers to political outrage as the binding force reflects a fragmented society desperate for common ground
The scapegoating mechanism is self-perpetuating: unresolved underlying issues guarantee the need for new scapegoats with increasing frequency
money_quotes: But humans still need something to come together around, thus: The Current Thing.
Worse than the broad category of The Current Thing is the subcategory of The Current Villain.
This singular focus on an individual that has to absorb all our hate, our rage, our frustration with reality as we've come to see it.

The scapegoats barely release any tension any more, especially since the shift from the early day of cancel culture that focused on minor characters with limited power to resist the mob, to major characters who can actually fight back.
Mobs form with ever greater frequency and ferocity in ever-more desperate attempts to dissolve the tension and restore unification. But it's just not working.
So I guess all we need to resolve the situation is the second coming of Christ? Let me say a little prayer.
posts.key_insight: Modern social media scapegoating cycles are accelerating because targeting prominent figures fails to resolve the underlying cultural tensions driving collective outrage, creating an insatiable demand for new villains.

posts.summary: DHH argues that earning attention through genuine sharing and teaching is far more valuable than buying it through advertising. He rejects the term 'content marketing' as too contrived and transactional, insisting the best results come from authentically sharing what you're doing and learning. He points out that most businesses don't have secret recipes—they succeed on execution and timing—so openly sharing knowledge costs nothing. The deep connection formed when you genuinely teach someone or change their thinking creates long-lasting relationships that eventually convert to customers, even if the timeline is unmeasurable.
key_points: Most businesses have no secret recipe worth protecting; they succeed or fail on execution and timing
