The term 'content marketing' implies a contrived, transactional approach that undermines authenticity
Earning attention through genuine teaching creates far deeper connections than paid advertising
The ROI of authentic sharing is real but often unquantifiable—people may follow for years before becoming customers
Sharing knowledge should be intrinsically rewarding, not contingent on measurable returns
money_quotes: Most businesses are not fucking Coca-Cola. They don't have this secret recipe that's the foundation of their success.
The vast majority of businesses succeed or fail on the basis of their execution and their timing.
I absolutely loathe the term content marketing because it sounds so contrived.
The good stuff comes out when you're simply sharing what you're doing without this transactional approach to it.

The connection you establish when you truly teach someone something, when you truly change their mind on something, or at least provoke them to think about that topic in a new way, it's just so much deeper.
Oh man, I read all your stuff for years. And then, hey, I just signed up for Basecamp yesterday. How do you even track or quantify that?
Just you put good stuff into the universe and most of the time the universe will return good stuff to you.
posts.key_insight: Authentic sharing and teaching builds deeper, more valuable customer relationships than any advertising spend, even if the returns are impossible to quantify.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Elon Musk's Twitter acquisition is the most entertaining spectacle in the world, regardless of outcome. He compares Musk's divisiveness to Trump's but notes the stakes are far lower since it's just a social media platform. DHH contends the world doesn't need Twitter and would likely be better off without it. Therefore, both possible outcomes—Musk fixes Twitter or destroys it—represent a win for everyone else.
key_points: Musk has positioned himself at the intersection of countless cultural trends, making him the most compelling public figure of the moment
The stakes of the Twitter saga are refreshingly low compared to political power struggles, making it guilt-free entertainment
The world doesn't need Twitter and would almost certainly be better off without it
The binary outcome creates an asymmetric bet for the public: a better Twitter or no Twitter are both wins
money_quotes: It's the greatest show on earth, no work of fiction could ever hope to compete.
Nor has anyone been able to play the media like a fiddle with such virtuosity.
This reality show is playing out over something as disposable as a struggling social media website.
The world doesn't need Twitter. In fact, the world would almost certainly be better off without Twitter.
That's what makes this show so guiltlessly entertaining to watch: Whichever the outcome, the world wins.
This is a 44 billion dollar gamble where the rest of us will win whatever the outcome.
posts.key_insight: The Musk-Twitter saga is guilt-free entertainment because both possible outcomes—a reformed platform or its destruction—leave the world better off.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the DEI movement, which achieved near-total dominance in corporate America after 2020, is entering a period of rapid decline. He identifies four factors driving this: the Supreme Court's likely repeal of affirmative action, scandals discrediting allied organizations like BLM, Elon Musk's takeover of Twitter removing the platform's enforcement mechanism, and mass tech layoffs reducing activist employees' leverage. He contends that DEI's core premise—that demographic discrepancies prove discrimination—is false, and calls for a return to equality of opportunity and merit-based hiring rather than outcomes-based approaches.
key_points: DEI achieved cultural dominance in 2020 through converging forces—Trump backlash, George Floyd protests, pandemic isolation—making traditional meritocracy and color-blindness 'problematic'

Four factors are eroding DEI's power: likely Supreme Court repeal of affirmative action, BLM organizational scandals, Musk's Twitter takeover ending ideological enforcement, and mass tech layoffs reducing employee leverage
The majority of Americans support equality of opportunity but reject race-based preferential treatment in admissions and hiring
DHH distinguishes between DEI's 'worthwhile aspirations' of equal opportunity and its 'misguided' tactics of pursuing equality of outcomes through demographic ratios
He argues the real problems—like early childhood education gaps—should be addressed upstream rather than through preferential hiring downstream
money_quotes: Not many dared speak up back then, whatever their misgivings about the ideology or the actions it bred. Most wisely chose to hold their breath rather than waste it against a hurricane.
Americans still support equality of opportunity, and they still reject equality of outcomes.
The DEI movement has lost control of Twitter, which served as the main instrument to run ideological enforcement in the corporate sphere.
The idea of bringing Your Whole Self to Twitter may suddenly seem a fair bit less appealing as the job market turns.
Divorce the specific tactics, and especially the ideology, from the valiant drive to promote equal opportunities for all.
Give everyone a fair chance to advance in the world on the merit of their talent and content of their character. Regardless of their identity.
The central premise of DEI, that discrepancies between demography statistics and employment reality, is defacto proof of discrimination is simply false.
It's been a crazy couple of years in the throes of zealotry.
posts.key_insight: DHH argues that DEI's corporate dominance was a temporary phenomenon enabled by unique 2020 conditions, and that its collapse should prompt a return to equality of opportunity over equality of outcomes.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how TikTok's algorithm surfaced a compelling connection between Stoic philosophy and Christian teachings. After lingering on Stoic quotes, the algorithm served him Jim Rohn's self-help take on amor fati, followed by a Christian TikTok expressing the same idea through prayer. He marvels at how profound truths about embracing difficulty have been independently discovered across traditions and centuries, and finds it delightful that such wisdom can surface on a platform known for trivial content.
key_points: TikTok's algorithm connects ideas across unrelated domains, occasionally surfacing meaningful philosophical links
Jim Rohn's advice to wish for self-improvement rather than easier circumstances echoes Stoic amor fati and the dichotomy of control
The same core insight — that hardship is the vehicle for growth — appears independently in Stoic, self-help, and Christian traditions
Profound wisdom can reach people through unexpected, even frivolous channels like a short-form video app
money_quotes: Don't wish it was easier, wish you were better. Don't wish for less problems, wish for more skills. Don't wish for less challenges, wish for more wisdom.
So many of the most profound truths of life have been independently discovered by a great many thinkers from many great traditions over millennia.
And some of them will find us in between cute puppies, silly dance videos, and crass humor on a Chinese video app. Majestic!
What a wonderful way to combine the Stoic principles of amor fati (love your fate) and the separation of what you can control (getting better!) from what you can't (what the world throws at you!).
One of the fascinating aspects of the TikTok algorithm is its ability to connect similarities from alien domains, and thus feed you more of what you've liked, but from an alternate angle.
posts.key_insight: Life's most profound truths — that hardship is the path to growth — have been independently rediscovered across cultures and centuries, and modern algorithms can surface these connections in the most unlikely places.

posts.summary: DHH endorses John McWhorter's book 'Woke Racism' and its central thesis that people should refuse to be intimidated by baseless accusations of racism or white supremacy. He uses his own experience receiving such accusations after writing about DEI as a case study. DHH argues that the tactic of labeling dissenters as racists relies on shock value and 'transitive guilt' — where the accusation extends to anyone associated with the accused. He contends that these tactics have lost much of their power since 2020 through overuse, and that standing firm against them accelerates their decline.
key_points: McWhorter frames contemporary anti-racism activism as a quasi-religious movement ('The New Religion') that enforces orthodoxy through shaming
The tactic of 'transitive guilt' — extending accusations to anyone associated with the target — is designed to isolate dissenters and multiply the cost of speaking out
The shock value of being called a racist or white supremacist diminishes rapidly with repeated baseless use, undermining the tactic's effectiveness
DHH argues that the power of these social shaming tactics has weakened significantly since their peak around 2020
Collective refusal to submit to ideological shaming is presented as the mechanism that will force a recalibration
money_quotes: There is room in this society for speaking the truth and living to tell about it.
After the shock of being accused without cause of being a 'racist' or a 'white supremacist' once, it just doesn't have the same effect the next time.
The shock value rapidly diminishes exactly because it is such a serious charge. One that can't be sustained if the accusation isn't plugged into reality.
Transitive guilt is a terrifying tactic the first time you encounter it.
You're not only required to hold your own line, you also have to hold it for everyone else, or you will have brought a pox upon their houses too.
By refusing to bow to these tactics, they will inevitably lose their terror.
What a year or two ago might have been A Really Big Deal is now barely a twirl in a teapot.
posts.key_insight: Baseless accusations of racism lose their power through overuse and collective refusal to be intimidated, and the declining effectiveness of these shaming tactics since 2020 proves that standing firm works.

posts.summary: DHH argues that true responsibility often means restraint rather than constant involvement. He challenges the common assumption that running a company requires being busy all the time, explaining how he maintains a 40-hour work week despite having unlimited potential tasks. He describes how 37signals institutionalized this restraint through Shape Up's six-week cycles, where new ideas must wait rather than immediately disrupting ongoing work. The core message is that responsible leadership means setting direction and then stepping back to let people execute without interference.
key_points: Responsibility and busyness are commonly but incorrectly equated — sometimes the most responsible thing is to do nothing
A 40-hour week constraint forces prioritization and prevents the trap of endless involvement
Shape Up's six-week cycles institutionalize restraint by preventing new ideas from disrupting work in progress
The best thing a leader can do for their team is leave them alone after setting direction
The urge to react on every gut instinct of urgency is a trap that disciplined processes can counteract
money_quotes: Sometimes the responsibility that you hold comes with a serving of just sitting back and shutting the fuck up.
Are we on a good path right now? Great. Don't mess with it. Don't tinker with it. Keep your hands off. That's responsibility.
The best thing you can do for your team that you're responsible for is to leave them the hell alone.
That level of tying your hand behind your back is a way to fuse responsibility with a reservedness.
Not being able to react on every gut instinct of urgency when you get a new idea.
Once the direction is set, leave the people to simply travel that direction, and then spend the time between checkpoints getting bored, getting creative, coming up with my own damn stuff to do.
There's an unlimited amount of stuff that I could feel like, 'Well, I should do that because I have the obligation to do that.'
posts.key_insight: True leadership responsibility often means disciplined restraint — setting direction then stepping back — rather than the constant involvement that most people mistake for diligence.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the tech industry's decade-plus of easy money created a generation of weak, bloated companies with terrible habits—overspending, overhiring, and no financial discipline. Now that the business cycle is turning, these companies face a reckoning they're utterly unprepared for. He distinguishes between monopolies like Google that can survive inefficiency through tollbooth economics and lesser companies that merely mimicked big tech's lavish habits without the revenue moat. Drawing on the concept of antifragility, he urges founders to embrace the downturn as an opportunity to build leaner, stronger organizations, noting that many great companies were born during recessions precisely because hardship forced discipline from day one.
key_points: A decade of cheap capital created deeply ingrained bad habits—overhiring, wasteful marketing, and indifference to profitability—that most tech companies have never been forced to confront
Monopolies like Google can survive bloat because their tollbooth businesses print money regardless, but companies mimicking that lifestyle without a moat face existential risk
Business cycle downturns serve a necessary ecological function, clearing dead wood and zombie companies, and the longer the cycle is suppressed the more catastrophic the eventual correction
The best companies emerge from hard times because scarcity forces prudence, focus, and self-sufficiency from the start
Leaders should embrace the downturn as an opportunity for antifragility rather than waste energy lamenting lost conditions
money_quotes: The past decade+ of decadent funding has produced a generation of flappy and fragile tech companies.
The resource curse wrecks havoc on critical thinking, prudent projections, and risk management.
It clears the brush of dead wood and puts zombie companies out of their misery.
Google might have 2-3-4x as many employees as they really need, and many of those employees might be dilly-dallying the day away in luxury canteens.
It's another thing entirely, if you're merely mimicking the habits of such an obese monopoly, but you sell, say, enterprise business software with no viral vector or intrinsic moat.
Suddenly those hundreds of millions of dollars you've raised and burned will be judged as a capital vice.
Good times create weak companies. Weak companies create hard times. Hard times create strong companies.
I judge you unfortunate because you have never lived through misfortune. You have passed through life without an opponent — no one can ever know what you are capable of, not even you.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's long era of cheap capital bred deeply unhealthy companies, and the coming downturn—while painful—is a necessary corrective that will separate the disciplined from the delusional.

posts.summary: DHH praises Shopify for consistently refusing to deplatform politically controversial stores like Breitbart and Libs of TikTok, maintaining its common carrier principle despite organized cancellation campaigns. He argues that Shopify's refusal to cave has been vindicated by the company's continued growth and success. DHH contends that cancellation tactics are losing effectiveness as more companies and public figures survive them and become 'anti-fragile.' He warns that segregating commerce along political lines would be dystopian and argues the general public is growing tired of ideological pressure campaigns targeting platforms.
key_points: Shopify has withstood multiple organized cancellation campaigns over hosting politically controversial stores, starting with Breitbart in 2017 and continuing with Libs of TikTok
Companies that survive cancellation attempts develop 'organizational antibodies' that make future attempts less effective, creating an anti-fragile dynamic
The concept of 'stochastic terrorism' is dismissed as a rebranded version of transitive guilt — holding platforms responsible for hypothetical future violence by third parties
Segregating commercial platforms along political lines would create an exhausting dystopia where every vendor relationship requires political vetting
The general public is losing patience with cancellation extremists from both sides, whether election deniers or woke scolds
money_quotes: Shopify's leadership held the line, and withstood the immense pressure to compromise its common carrier principle. That was not cheap, especially in 2017, but in the years that have followed, it has proven priceless.

Stochastic terrorism. Which is really just a fancy term for the nonsense accountability leap that 'your political speech is directly responsible for violent outcomes that third parties might commit later'.
The more companies or individuals that survive and thrive after being chased for ideological cancelation, the stronger the overall societal immune system will be to protect future targets.
It would be a dystopian dark road to travel, if we were to segregate all forms of commerce along political fault lines.

Most people have a basic appreciation that if service companies are pushed to vet all customers for political beliefs, the day may soon come where service is denied them because of their views. And where does that end?
Enough companies like Shopify have developed organizational antibodies to fight off new cancelation attempts cultivated in the same stale ideological petri dish.
posts.key_insight: Companies that resist ideological pressure to deplatform customers build anti-fragile immunity that strengthens the broader ecosystem's resistance to political litmus tests on commerce.

posts.summary: DHH argues that we're living through an era of unprecedented predictive failure across politics, culture, technology, economics, and medicine. He observes that experts and institutions have lost credibility, with trust in media hitting historic lows while small businesses rank as America's most trusted institution. Rather than picking ideological sides, he advocates for intellectual humility and giving ideas time before judging them. The core prescription is to accept uncertainty while still pursuing truth through exposure to opposing viewpoints.
key_points: Expert predictions have been spectacularly wrong across every domain, making it nearly impossible to identify reliable forecasters
Journalists and intellectuals are more interested in protecting narratives than discovering truth, driving institutional trust to historic lows
Small businesses are now America's most trusted institution at 68%, while television news sits at just 11%
Truth emerges from the clash of opposing ideas, not from loyalty to prepackaged ideological camps
Intellectual humility requires giving ideas time ('five minutes') before rushing to judgment
money_quotes: I can't remember a time in my life when so many got so much wrong in their predictions about the future.
The best approximate conclusion I've been able to draw is this: Nobody Knows Anything.
The ones with the great recent track record often regress to the mean, and picking the upcoming stars is like a lottery.
So much of the inquiry is tuned not for getting closer to the truth, but for protecting narratives and ideologies.
The double whammy from this predicament is of course also that nobody knows what to do about the fact that Nobody Knows Anything. It's turtles all the way down.

Picking a permanent side in some grand ideological struggle – with its prepackaged set of principles and values, enemies and heroes – might bring psychological comfort in the short term, but it'll erode the capacity for intellectual honesty in the long term.
Accepting that Nobody Knows Anything does not render all truth relative. We can still validate knowledge.
posts.key_insight: In an era where experts and institutions have lost credibility across every domain, intellectual honesty requires abandoning ideological loyalty and embracing the discomfort of genuine uncertainty.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the twenty-year reign of SaaS is due for a correction, with software products (buy-once, self-hosted) poised for a resurgence. He outlines trade-offs from both the customer perspective—loss of data possession, forced upgrades, and perpetual subscriptions—and the vendor perspective—operational burden, obligation to tinker forever, and organizational mismatch between creation and maintenance. He points to Docker and similar breakthroughs as enabling easier distribution of installable software, and expresses a personal desire to both buy and sell finished software products rather than perpetual services.
key_points: SaaS customers sacrifice data possession, upgrade control, and the ability to pay once for finished software
SaaS vendors bear the burden of 24/7 operations, being a single point of failure, and the obligation to tinker with the same product forever
The creative energy of building new things is stifled by the service model's demand for perpetual maintenance
Technologies like Docker have dramatically eased software distribution, removing a key advantage SaaS held over installable products
The video game industry's historical separation of creative studios from publishers offers a model worth emulating
money_quotes: Possession in 9/10ths of the law, and with software services, you are just not in possession of your data.
Refusing service on individual moral, political, or other grounds is something people can do when they're offering a service, not after they've sold you a product.
Somehow we've all, buyers and sellers, swallowed the logic of planned obsolescence when it comes to software.
Most business software is bought because it serves a purpose. It's a tool, not entertainment. Novelty ought not be a top priority.
I wish there was a broad category of software that was offered in its final form.
Running a software service that has thousands of customers means you're the single point of failure for their dear data.

There's a reason many software makers pine for the rare opportunity to work on greenfield projects. That lush open space to start fresh with your best ideas, unencumbered by old promises and old decisions, is invigorating.
I dream of being able to both buy and sell finished business software products.
posts.key_insight: The SaaS model imposes serious trade-offs on both customers and vendors, and with modern distribution tools like Docker removing the old hassles of installable software, the industry is ripe for a return to buy-once software products.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the explosion of excitement around ChatGPT and AI capabilities in late 2022, acknowledging the genuinely impressive leaps in AI competency while cautioning against premature predictions. He challenges Paul Graham's suggestion that AI will only commoditize mediocre work, arguing that AI might actually surpass human creativity given how AI chess and go players already make moves of wild ingenuity that humans never would. He draws parallels between current AI enthusiasm and past technology hype cycles — 1950s AGI predictions, 1990s VR, and 2010s self-driving cars — none of which materialized on schedule. His core message is that while the moment is genuinely exciting and raises profound questions about consciousness and creativity, nobody can reliably predict what comes next.
key_points: ChatGPT represents a second data point on what appears to be an exponential curve of AI competency, following breakthroughs in image generation
Contra Paul Graham, AI may not just commoditize mediocre creativity but actually surpass the best human creativity, as it already has in chess and go with moves no human would consider
Human creativity may not be fundamentally different from a large language model — both are distillations of observations, inputs, and pattern recognition with some randomness
Historical parallels — 1950s AGI predictions, 1990s VR hype, 2010s self-driving euphoria — should temper expectations about timelines
The excitement is warranted because these developments go to the heart of what consciousness and creativity actually are
money_quotes: Because why would we assume that AI won't actually be more creative than mere humans?
In this domain, it's the humans executing mechanical moves based on memorized patterns, the computers making novel inferences.
Is what we call human creativity all that different from a large language model anyway? A distillation of observations, inputs, mimetic tendencies, and a wetware random generator?
The line between the absurd and the plausible is blending fast.
In the late 50s, the foremost thinkers in computer science thought we were just a few years away from general AI.
It's so hard for humans to predict the next epoch.
Things that seemed far-fetched in 2021 suddenly appear soon-to-be plausible as we close out 2022.
posts.key_insight: AI may not merely commoditize mediocre human output but surpass the best of human creativity, yet history's repeated failure to predict technological timelines means nobody truly knows what happens next.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on returning to in-person conferences after the COVID years and argues that physical gatherings produce far better discourse than online platforms like Twitter. He observes that people's ideological guards drop at in-person events, enabling genuine curiosity and good-faith debate that's nearly impossible online. He acknowledges the apparent irony given his decades of advocating remote work, but frames in-person events as battery-recharging complements to remote work rather than contradictions. He concludes that healing online community divisions requires periodic physical connection to build the good faith that sustains healthy digital interaction.
key_points: Twitter has become a reinforcement engine for existing beliefs and tribal identities, making good-faith debate nearly impossible
In-person events lower ideological guards and open minds to genuine curiosity and perspective change
Periodic in-person gatherings and remote work are complementary, not contradictory — events recharge the social batteries that sustain remote collaboration
Online community health depends on good faith that is built much faster through physical human connection
The post-COVID return to conferences has reinforced that some forms of intellectual exchange simply work better face-to-face
money_quotes: Twitter is a reinforcement engine for existing beliefs and tribes.
Everyone's guards are up so high when they engage in online, public forums.
The ideological guards are down, the mind is opened to new ideas, and most people show up with a genuine curiosity about the topics in play.

These industry events recharge social and intellectual batteries. Storing energy that can sustain working remotely and engaging with strangers over the internet in a healthier way for months afterwards.
The strength of online communities is drawn from the good faith of its participants.
Building that faith, broadly and deeply, just happens much faster when we tap into the human connections established by a disarming smile, eye contact, and the open minds that follow.
posts.key_insight: Online discourse runs on a social battery that can only be recharged through in-person human connection, making periodic physical gatherings essential to sustaining healthy digital communities.

posts.summary: DHH argues that humans are fundamentally bad at estimating complex software projects, so 37signals replaced estimates with fixed time appetites. Instead of asking how long something will take, they define a rough problem sketch and give teams six weeks to build a great version. The key mechanism is treating time like a stop-loss order — you cap your investment rather than chasing sunk costs. When time runs out, you cut scope or kill the project entirely, but you never ship bad software.
key_points: Replace estimates with appetites: define how much time you're willing to spend, not how long it will take
The last 10% of a project often contains another 90% of effort, making estimates systematically wrong
Sunk cost psychology — feeling like you must dig out of a hole — leads to the worst resource allocation decisions
Nothing kills team momentum like being unable to ship, and runaway projects are the main cause
Cut scope to fit the appetite, and if scope cuts would make it bad, kill the project instead
money_quotes: Humans are terrible at estimating anything complicated that involves novel attempts at problem solving.
Getting out of estimates and into appetites has been the single most important thing we have done for our software development process at 37signals.
Think of it like a stop loss.
It is so common to reach the end of an estimate, and go no, no, no, we're 90% done. We just need the last 10%. Yeah, except there's another 90% worth of effort in those last 10%.
Nothing will clog down the momentum and motivation of a software development team like not being able to ship.
This state of 'I gotta dig myself out of this hole'. Stop digging.
The thing you cut is scope, and if you have to cut too much scope, you cut the project. You don't ship bad software.
posts.key_insight: Fix the time and flex the scope — treat project budgets like stop-loss orders rather than chasing ever-expanding estimates.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Europe has become a 'digital colony' of American tech monopolies, having ceded sovereignty over its digital infrastructure, data, and economy. He traces how the decentralized early internet gave way to centralized trillion-dollar conglomerates that extract wealth, data, and attention from Europe's 750 million people. He proposes three radical solutions: embracing protectionism (citing China and Denmark's ejection of Uber as models), outlawing surveillance capitalism's targeted advertising business model to level the competitive playing field, and pursuing digital independence from American cloud and tech infrastructure. He frames this as an urgent matter comparable to Europe's energy dependency on Russia, arguing that globalism is dead and self-sufficiency is now essential.
key_points: Europe has become a digital colony where 750 million people generate data, attention, and revenue for American tech monopolies while receiving toxic public discourse and declining mental health in return

Protectionism is a legitimate and proven strategy — China achieved digital sovereignty through it, America practices it selectively, and Denmark successfully ejected Uber using regulatory requirements
Banning targeted advertising based on personal data would instantly level the playing field for European competitors by dismantling the business model that makes American tech giants unassailable
Europe's privacy efforts (GDPR, cookie banners, Privacy Shield) have been largely ineffective because Europe hasn't committed to what it actually wants — half-measures produce only kabuki theater
The death of globalism, evidenced by Russia's energy weaponization and China's supply chain disruptions, should be a wake-up call to reduce all forms of foreign digital dependency
money_quotes: Without digital sovereignty, we cannot claim to be free in the 21st century.
So what started out as a worldwide web of collaboration became a web of entanglement. We Europeans went from being part of producing the silk to ended up as prey in the net.
Europe became a digital colony. A region of 750 million people with few to no major, native tech services.

Free trade is a fantasy. Some times it's a useful fantasy, but often it's also just a convenient cover for opening new markets in sectors where your region is strong, and closing them off where it is weak or needs nurture.
Globalism is dead. If you didn't get the message from Wuhan, you hopefully got it from Moscow.
Services that have achieved the holy grail of alchemy: Turning the worst, most divisive, most enraging content on the internet into gold.
Europe should put up or shut up about privacy.
The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is today.
posts.key_insight: Europe must treat its dependency on American tech monopolies as the same existential threat as its dependency on Russian energy, and respond with equally radical protectionist measures including banning surveillance capitalism's business model entirely.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the trajectory of 'no politics at work' policies, noting that when 37signals adopted this stance in 2021, it caused massive public backlash and employee departures. Now, less than two years later, Meta has implemented virtually the same policy for its 70,000+ employees and the response has been near-silence. He argues this proves the cultural shift he predicted is well underway, with even former critics privately seeking advice on implementing similar policies. DHH frames this as genuine progress and connects it to what he sees as the declining influence of DEI initiatives in corporate culture.
key_points: 37signals' no-politics policy in 2021 caused weeks of drama and ~20 employee departures, but DHH considers it one of the most valuable decisions for long-term company health
Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong reports similar validation, with even vocal critics privately reaching out for advice on implementing the same approach
Meta quietly banned internal discussion of abortion, guns, vaccines, and elections with virtually zero media or public backlash
The contrast between the 60-person company causing a firestorm and the 70,000-person company causing silence illustrates how quickly the Overton window shifted
DHH connects this trend to the broader decline of DEI's corporate influence and predicts more companies will follow
money_quotes: Being early can be costly. But the results, despite the (very) difficult transition, have ultimately been worth it.
No comment thread on Basecamp is going to close the gap on fundamental philosophical and political differences. And we're left worse for wear when we try.

That's what real progress looks like. That we in less than two years can go from no-politics etiquettes being major industry news, when a company of sixty people does it, to being a complete nothingburger, when a company of 70,000+ does it.

Most people are simply ready to move on from this nonsense. They don't actually want to discuss abortion, gun rights, vaccine efficacy, or elections with their coworkers during work hours in work forums.

You'd think that such an important shift for a company that still has more than 70,000 employees would be A Very Big Deal to all the journalists, activists, and internet commentators who lost their shit when Coinbase and 37signals first took that position. But you'd be wrong. It's been crickets.
After living with this type of etiquette for some time now, I would not want to go back.
posts.key_insight: The near-silent reception of Meta's no-politics-at-work policy proves that what was once a career-risking corporate stance has become mainstream common sense in under two years.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates the EU's Digital Markets Act as a breakthrough in the fight against Apple and Google's mobile monopolies. After years of failed attempts through US state legislatures and individual countries, where Apple's lobbying power neutralized reform efforts, the EU has proven too large a market for Apple to ignore or defy. Reports indicate Apple is preparing to comply with alternative app stores, sideloading, third-party payment processing, and open NFC access. DHH argues this is the most exciting development in tech, even more than AI advances, and believes it will ultimately benefit Apple by restoring its relationship with developers.
key_points: US state-level antitrust efforts failed because Apple could buy lobbyists and influence politicians, while individual countries like South Korea and the Netherlands couldn't enforce their rulings
The EU's Digital Markets Act succeeds where others failed because Europe represents $95 billion in Apple sales — too large a market to abandon — and penalties reach 20% of global revenues
Apple is reportedly preparing full compliance: alternative app stores, sideloading, third-party payments, NFC access, and alternate browser engines
DHH argues Apple's monopolistic behavior damaged developer relationships more than even the butterfly keyboard debacle
The forced opening of the platform could paradoxically benefit Apple long-term by restoring developer goodwill
money_quotes: It's basically the most ambitious wishlist everyone could present to Santa Antitrust, and Apple is reportedly working – however begrudgingly – to comply with it all!
As dazzled as I am by ChatGPT and Stable Diffusion, I'm even more excited about the notion of setting the phones free.

Free for all the business models that couldn't work with a 30% haircut, free from the gatekeeper nonsense keeping developers from releasing software, and freedom for users to install the apps they damn well please.
Apple used to be an aspirational company for most developers, but then it turned out to just as bad of a boss as the last big boss (Microsoft), although somehow worse.
The EU just might make Apple lovable again by outlawing their monopolistic excesses. What a twist.
It's lucrative to be the greedy king hiking taxes on whims and leisure, but eventually you'll foment enough ill will to risk that head beneath the crown ends up on a pike.
posts.key_insight: The EU's Digital Markets Act is succeeding against Apple's mobile monopoly where US efforts failed, because Europe's $95 billion market is too large for Apple to abandon and the penalties too severe to ignore.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Elon Musk's takeover of Twitter has failed to deliver on its promise of principled management, as evidenced by the ElonJet controversy. While acknowledging that the Twitter Files revealed Old Twitter's Trust & Safety team operated with clear political bias, he contends that Musk's Twitter will likely replicate the same problems from the opposite direction. Using an extended Lord of the Rings metaphor, DHH compares Twitter's power over public discourse to the One Ring — a corrupting force no individual can wield responsibly. His conclusion is that the best outcome would be Twitter's destruction entirely.
key_points: Musk's handling of ElonJet showed he cannot resist the corrupting influence of controlling Twitter's platform
The Twitter Files confirmed that Old Twitter's moderation team operated with systematic political bias
New Twitter under Musk will likely replicate the same censorship problems, just from the opposite ideological direction — a difference in degree, not kind
No individual or faction should control a platform with Twitter's unique influence over public discourse
The best outcome of Musk's acquisition is Twitter's eventual destruction, freeing society from its influence
money_quotes: Nobody should have this much power over the town square. No human is strong enough to resist the corruption it brings.
The central characters in the Orwellianly-named Trust & Safety team were drunk on influence, power, and kept ruling from blatant bias, while draping themselves in bullshit experthood and victimology.
It's now clear that we're probably in store for much of the same, just from the other front. Perhaps it'll be less, but now we're arguing a difference in degree, not in kind.
Our best hope is that Twitter by accident, intent, or happenstance is thrown into the fire of Mount Doom.
No one can handle The One Ring. Nobody should.
posts.key_insight: Twitter's power over public discourse is inherently corrupting regardless of who controls it, and the best outcome for society is the platform's destruction rather than a change in management.

posts.summary: DHH argues against lowering standards for new employees, contending that high expectations are actually a form of respect. He acknowledges that nobody hits the ground running but insists the bar should remain visible and high from day one. Rather than coddling newcomers with gentle onramps, managers should be honest about the gap between where someone starts and where they need to be. He frames lowered expectations as 'ruinous empathy' and advocates for radical candor even with new hires.
key_points: Lowering standards for new people feels kind but actually disrespects their potential
Keeping the bar high isn't the same as expecting immediate perfection — it's about honesty regarding the gap
People are more motivated when assumed capable than when coddled with gentle expectations
The 'standard pace' of onboarding is an artificial ceiling that limits high performers
Ruinous empathy applies to onboarding just as much as to ongoing management
money_quotes: It's tempting to think you're doing someone new a favor by initially holding them to lower standards on work, effort, or decision making.
Making that distance obvious immediately is a sign of respect.
The standard pace is for chumps.
Don't bother too much with what a 'standard' ramp of learning or acclimation might look like.
If you assume that everyone new might well have the capacity to surprise you with their pace, you're likely to be just that – surprised.

There's more potential motivation available from being considered intrinsically capable of reaching that high bar quickly than there is from the kind but coddling instinct to make the onramp too gentle, too safe.
Smothering newcomers with lowered expectations is a strain of the poison that is ruinous empathy.
posts.key_insight: High standards from day one aren't harsh — they signal genuine respect for a newcomer's potential and provide the clarity needed to grow fast.

posts.summary: DHH explains why Basecamp makes cancellation effortless, with no guilt trips or dark patterns. When someone asked what data drove this 'strategy,' he argues it's not a strategy at all — it's a core value. He critiques the growth-hacking mindset that optimizes retention through hassle, pointing out that the easy-to-measure savings from preventing cancellations mask the harder-to-measure reputational damage inflicted on the vast majority of customers. Ultimately, he argues that some business decisions should be driven by principles, not metrics.
key_points: Basecamp lets customers cancel without friction, guilt, or retention tactics — and it leaves a positive lasting impression
The growth-hacking mindset demands data justification for every decision, but some decisions are about values, not optimization
Easy metrics create a tyranny: you can measure the $32K saved by hassling cancellers, but not the long-term reputational damage to the 99.9% who resented the experience
Even the ROI argument misses the point — running a business you're proud of means refusing to A/B test your principles
money_quotes: We don't hassle customers who want to cancel Basecamp because that simply feels like a shitty thing to do.
Few things in the world of business aggrieves me as much as being held hostage to a subscription I no longer desire to keep.
It always leaves an incredibly sour taste, ruins any residual affection I might have had for the service, and dials in a determination to spread that ill will far and wide.
See! We earned an extra $32,856 last year putting everyone who wanted to cancel through the wringer. Yes, but at what cost?
This is the tyranny of easy metrics.
It's easy to measure how much money is saved by preventing cancelations, it's much harder to measure how much long-term business is lost by poisoning your reputation.
This isn't just about the money, it's about running a business in a way you can feel proud about.
And the only way to do that consistently is by not A/B testing your core values.
posts.key_insight: Some business decisions should be driven by principles rather than metrics, because the tyranny of easy measurement leads companies to optimize for small, quantifiable gains while ignoring the massive, unquantifiable costs of treating customers poorly.

posts.summary: DHH recounts 37signals' failed attempt to use SUSE Rancher and Harvester as part of their cloud exit for HEY. After enduring multiple meetings where pricing was withheld, they received a $2 million/year licensing bid — nearly as much as their entire cloud spend. Unlike Dell's typical 80% discounts, SUSE offered just 3%, making the deal absurd. DHH channels his frustration into building their own infrastructure stack without enterprise software vendors.
key_points: SUSE's $2M/year licensing bid for Rancher and Harvester was nearly as expensive as 37signals' entire $3M cloud bill, defeating the purpose of leaving the cloud
Enterprise sales culture deliberately withholds pricing through multiple meetings, wasting everyone's time on deals that are DOA
Enterprise software discounting norms vary wildly — Dell offers 80% discounts while SUSE offered 3%, making list prices meaningless signals
The experience pushed 37signals toward building their own open-source infrastructure stack instead of relying on enterprise vendors
money_quotes: We should have smelled something was off when getting basic information about pricing took several online meetings.
The bid we received back from SUSE came in at an even more ridiculous two million dollars, just in licensing and support costs.
When we pressed them on the belly for the discount, the answer was 3. Three percent. Yeah, okay, but no.
BUT WHY ARE THEY WASTING OUR FUCKING TIME IN MEETING AFTER MEETING WITHHOLDING DEAL-BREAKER PRICING?
The haggling, the hoodwinking, the game-playing. The let's-see-what-we-can-get-away-with bullshit that brings meaning to the life of suits duking it out in a negotiation contest.
I can't stand it. In fact, I loathe it.
We're going to build our own theme park with blackjack and... no fucking enterprise sales people.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise software pricing games can be just as exploitative as cloud vendor lock-in, pushing companies toward building their own open-source infrastructure instead.

posts.summary: DHH traces a recurring pattern in software: merchants of complexity build elaborate, profitable ecosystems around simple problems, get defeated by simpler alternatives, then regroup in a new domain. He draws a direct line from J2EE's enterprise bloat (defeated by Rails) to WS-* specifications (defeated by JSON/REST) to the current Kubernetes ecosystem built atop Docker's original simplicity. The core argument is that complexity is manufactured because it's profitable—it creates demand for consultants, vendors, and specialized knowledge. DHH warns that the container orchestration world is repeating the same cycle, with the ratio of genuine innovation to bullshit once again completely out of whack.
key_points: Rails succeeded by extracting good patterns from J2EE and stripping away the enterprise complexity that made them inaccessible to most developers
The software industry follows a predictable cycle: simple breakthrough → complexity merchants pile on → simpler alternative wins → merchants migrate to the next frontier
Kubernetes and its ecosystem represent the latest reconstruction of the Death Star, taking Docker's elegant simplicity and burying it under layers of enterprise tooling
Complexity persists because it's profitable—it creates markets for consultants, vendors, certifications, and enterprise sales that simplicity cannot sustain
When you're in the thick of a complexity cycle, the 17 reasons why the simple approach is naive always sound convincing—until hindsight makes them look absurd
money_quotes: The ratio between Good Idea and Bullshit was completely out of whack.
The merchants of complexity don't close up shop just because a single market dries up. They migrate to the next.
The struggle between profitless simplicity and profitable complexity is eternal in the world of software.
When things become too easy, too approachable, there just isn't room for all the enterprisey services, sales, and solutions to prosper.

Damn if the merchants of complexity didn't see such simplicity as a bug to quickly correct, and use their impressive tractor beam of bullshit to pull us back towards the ever-reconstructing Death Star once more.

Try contrasting what's required to get going with Kubernetes, both within your application, and to operate control planes and what not, with the breakthrough simplicity of the original Docker advancement, and it's damn depressing.
The complexity that's been conjured up is immensely profitable.
Beware carnies dressed as storm troopers. The circus empire is always coming to town.
posts.key_insight: The software industry perpetually cycles between simplicity breakthroughs and profitable complexity bloat, because merchants of complexity always rebuild their Death Star in a new domain once the old one is destroyed.

posts.summary: DHH argues that speed of execution depends not just on competence but on risk tolerance, and most companies dramatically overestimate the criticality of their work. He presents a 'criticality ladder' ranging from life-threatening systems down to minor inconveniences, arguing that most software falls far closer to the low end. Using examples from Facebook's early 'move fast and break things' ethos and Basecamp's own evolution, he contends that organizations naturally slow down as they grow, often adding more process than the actual stakes warrant. The core message: even established companies should bias toward speed over excessive caution.
key_points: Risk tolerance matters as much as competence for development speed — you can't move fast if you treat every problem like rocket surgery
Most software sits far down the criticality ladder, closer to 'inconvenience' than 'loss of human life,' and should be treated accordingly
Executive overreaction to small mistakes causes organizations to become lethargic by discouraging appropriate risk-taking
Organizations naturally slow down as they grow, adding process that may be real or imagined in necessity — the trick is telling the difference
Young entrepreneurs have an advantage in risk-taking through 'bliss of ignorance,' while experienced veterans bring needed caution for high-stakes work
money_quotes: You can't go quickly if you treat every problem with the diligence required for rocket surgery.
The price you pay for making a mess is an instant surcharge on both quality and productivity.
That's the nature of the criticality bet. You need to have the stomach to keep playing, even if you occasionally lose a hand.

If executive wrath bears down upon you after any little mistake, you'll naturally just stop taking the appropriate risk, lest you tempt a small chance of getting chewed out. Organizations that work like this in the face of criticality that does not warrant it become lethargic.
We need youngsters blessed with the bliss of ignorance to push the envelope where the stakes are lower. And then some old-timers weathered by wisdom to pull the envelope when the stakes tip large.
Speed of execution is one of the most important organizational features in most areas of competition.

But if you must err, I contend that most companies, even us – two decades in and hundreds of millions in revenue later – are closer to Zuckerberg's sweet spot of moving fast and breaking things than we are to the rocket surgery.
posts.key_insight: Most companies overestimate the criticality of their work and should bias toward speed over excessive caution, because the real risk is usually going too slow, not breaking things.

posts.summary: DHH reviews the Asus Zenfone 9 as a surprisingly compelling Android phone after years of lukewarm Android experiences. He praises its relatively compact size, lighter weight compared to the iPhone 14 Pro, and significantly improved CPU performance over older Android chips. He highlights how much easier it is to sideload apps like Fortnite on Android 13 compared to previous versions. While acknowledging iOS still has better fit and finish, he argues Android's openness to installing software outside the official store represents a more important advantage than the polish gap.
key_points: The Zenfone 9 stands out for being smaller and lighter than most Android phones, making it feel better in hand than the heavier iPhone 14 Pro
Android CPU performance has improved significantly but still lags far behind Apple's chips — the Snapdragon 8+ Gen 1 scores 115 on Speedometer vs Apple's 365-400
Sideloading apps like Fortnite on Android 13 is dramatically easier than it was on Android 11, showing meaningful progress in openness
iOS ecosystem lock-in through iMessage, calendars, and photos remains the biggest barrier to switching, not hardware quality
The freedom to install software outside a curated app store matters more than the remaining quality gap between Android and iOS
money_quotes: The fidelity gap is real, but the freedom gap is bigger.
There's something deeply appealing about having a phone where at least it's actually possible to install Forbidden Software, like Fortnite, without wild interventions like jailbreaking.
It's also the first Android phone where the CPU doesn't feel like a total drag when it comes to web stuff.
Still miles off what Apple can do, but less noticeable day-to-day.
iMessage is the big one, but calendars, photos, and the other service hooks still make it difficult to just casually switch back and forth.
posts.key_insight: Android's freedom to sideload software outside curated app stores represents a more meaningful advantage over iOS than the remaining gap in polish and performance.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates the joy of an entirely empty calendar day dedicated to deep, focused work on a challenging project. He argues that his ability to juggle many pursuits comes not from productivity hacking but from deliberately keeping his schedule clear. He pushes back against the common objection that empty calendars are only possible after years of grinding, noting that he and Jason Fried have always worked this way. The secret lies in intentional organizational choices: hiring self-directed people, communicating asynchronously, and running structured development cycles.
key_points: Long stretches of uninterrupted time are essential for creative, meaningful work
Productivity comes from protecting empty calendar space, not from clever scheduling tricks or busyness
The common excuse that empty calendars require years of prior hustle is a myth — DHH says they worked this way from the start
Organizational design choices like hiring managers of one, async communication, and Shape Up enable calendar freedom
An empty calendar is a deliberate design decision, not a privilege earned through suffering
money_quotes: Nothing invigorates the soul like seeing something come together out of nothing, and to do that, you need these long stretches of uninterrupted time.
Not by clever productivity hacking, fancy calendar stacking, or endless bursts of busyness.
That answer is often met with a disbelief that disconnects the questioner from accepting this as a possible reality for them.
But Jason and I always worked like this. If anything, we used to work more like this.
I recall not keeping a calendar at all through much of the 2000s.
This is our reality because we designed it to be.
These are all choices. Ones you can make too.
posts.key_insight: An empty calendar isn't a reward for past hustle — it's a deliberate organizational design choice that anyone can make to unlock deep, meaningful work.

posts.summary: DHH argues that computer hardware has become exciting again after a stagnant decade in the 2010s. Apple's M1 chip broke the logjam, and now CPUs and NVMe storage are making massive generational leaps. He highlights how NVMe speeds have improved so dramatically that 37signals is exploring replacing RAM-based caching and job queuing with NVMe storage. He connects this hardware renaissance to his broader argument that companies should leave the cloud and run their own servers, suggesting that a new generation of developers will rediscover the joy of caring about physical hardware.
key_points: Hardware stagnated in the 2010s due to Intel's rut, but Apple's M1 reopened the floodgates for meaningful generational improvements across the industry
NVMe storage has seen order-of-magnitude leaps, going from ~500MB/sec to a promised ~13GB/sec with Gen 5, making it viable to replace RAM for caching and queuing
37signals is exploring petabyte-scale NVMe storage in single rack servers for around $200K, fundamentally changing infrastructure economics
Cloud-native developers are disconnected from hardware advances, but as companies leave the cloud, interest in physical hardware will resurge
Modern hardware is so powerful that running services with millions of users on a single server is becoming mathematically plausible
money_quotes: I lost interest in computer hardware during the 2010s. It seemed years would pass with only meager, marginal improvements.
That's the kind of order-of-magnitude leap forward that requires you to rethink your assumptions.
We're exploring moving both caching and job queuing at Basecamp to NVMe instead of RAM.
There's a whole generation of app developers who've only ever known the cloud, which separates them from appreciating the direct advances in hardware considerably.
As more companies start redoing the math on cloud, and pulling their applications off the rental racket, I think we'll see a resurgence of developers caring about the metal again.
We just bought some 12TB NVMe Gen 4 cards for $2,390. 12TB!
Run all our fancy services, with millions of users, on a single machine (or three, for redundancy).
posts.key_insight: Hardware improvements have become so dramatic that they demand rethinking fundamental infrastructure assumptions, making owned hardware increasingly compelling over cloud rentals.

posts.summary: DHH rails against the modern workplace as an 'interruption factory' that prevents people from doing meaningful work. He argues that pointless meetings, constant chat, and excessive check-ins drain workers not because the work itself is hard, but because they can never find uninterrupted time to do it. He positions Basecamp as the antidote to this chaos, built on twenty years of 37signals philosophy. The post doubles as the launch announcement for a $5 million Basecamp marketing campaign built around the slogan 'Just Let Me Do My Job.'
key_points: The modern workplace is an 'interruption factory' that fills days with meetings, chat, and check-ins while producing no real progress
Worker burnout often comes not from hard work but from the frustration of never having uninterrupted time to apply their skills
Flow state is the peak of workplace happiness, and it requires being left alone — individually or as a team
Basecamp's design philosophy is to give teams 'a little less than you think you might need' so everyone can actually use it
37signals is launching a $5M marketing campaign around this frustration in 2023
money_quotes: The modern workplace has become an interruption factory. Constantly cutting the work day into fleeting work moments. Keeping everyone busy producing everything but progress.
Half the people who feel zapped by work at the end of the week do so because they actually want to get the job done, but just can't seem to find the peace and calm to do so.
Drained by the lack of momentum on moving things forward, not from an intrinsic incapacity for motivation.
Work isn't always a picnic, but if you get to apply your skills to interesting problems for long, uninterrupted stretches of time, it can actually be rewarding and meaningful.
The height of happiness at work is finding that state of flow. But you'll be looking in vain unless you're mostly left alone.
Basecamp's mission since its launch nearly two decades ago has been to give you a little less than you think you might need, such that everyone on the team can actually figure out how to use it.
posts.key_insight: Most workplace exhaustion stems not from the difficulty of work itself but from the impossibility of finding uninterrupted time to actually do it.

posts.summary: DHH argues that corporate America's retreat from public statements on social and political issues proves his earlier prediction that DEI and corporate activism have peaked. He cites an Axios report showing companies have gone silent on national tragedies after years of vocal positioning. He dismisses the concern that corporate silence will harm civil discourse, calling it hypocritical from progressives who historically opposed corporate influence in politics. His core position is that corporations should stay out of social and political commentary entirely.
key_points: Corporate silence on social issues confirms DHH's earlier prediction about the waning of DEI's corporate dominance
Economic pressures and layoffs in tech have forced companies to gut DEI departments and stop public posturing
The concern that 'corporate silence could crater civil discourse' is contradicted by decades of progressive opposition to corporate influence in politics
Corporate activism was never neutral — it supported a specific political orientation, and the objection to silence reveals that bias
Individuals should engage in politics as citizens, but corporations should not issue sanctimonious statements outside their sphere of operations
money_quotes: There's a very distinct limit on how often most folks want to hear from the maker of the candy bar, the producer of their soap, or the purveyor of their software on societal and political topics. And we've clearly reached that limit!
Unless mega corporations weigh in on our politics, it'll crater civil discourse?
Haven't progressives in particular just spent the last half century at least hemming and hawing over the influence of big corporations on public discourse and politics?!

The problem with the lack of corporate involvement, just like the lack of Twitter's overt censorship regime, is obviously primarily that it marks an absence of support from a very particular narrative and political orientation.
I'm certain this same writer would be quick to call for corporations to shut up, if they replaced the 'corporate silence' with support for a counterposition.
THIS IS WHY WE SHOULDN'T WANT CORPORATIONS WEIGHING IN ON SOCIAL AND POLITICAL QUESTIONS OUTSIDE THEIR DIRECT SPHERE OF OPERATIONS.
But spare us the sanctimonious press releases, with their carefully rehearsed thoughts & prayers, on company letterhead, from the outfits that stock our commodities.
posts.key_insight: Corporate retreat from social activism isn't a crisis for civil discourse — it's a correction, and the objection to corporate silence reveals that the real concern was always about losing a megaphone for a particular political viewpoint.

posts.summary: DHH argues that bugs are an inevitable and normal byproduct of software development, and that users' emotional reactions to encountering them are disproportionate and counterproductive. He contends that the only way to achieve near-perfect software quality is to write less software, which is commercially unviable. Drawing on Brooks's law, he dismisses the idea that more developers can simply fix all bugs. He frames bug triage as rational prioritization, arguing that the value of software depends far more on the problems it solves than on its bug count. The post calls for realistic expectations and sympathy toward developers rather than outrage.
key_points: Bugs are an inevitable byproduct of writing software; complete eradication is impossible regardless of team size or methodology
The value of software depends far more on the problem it solves than the quality by which it does so — useful buggy software beats useless bug-free software
Bug prioritization through triage (users affected × criticality) is rational and necessary, not a sign of incompetence
Users suffer from myopia when they demand their specific bug be treated as a drop-everything emergency
Emotionally charged responses — demeaning developers, questioning professionalism — make everyone worse off including users
money_quotes: Disappointment occurs when expectations don't match reality. And our expectations for software quality are profoundly unrealistic.
The only reliable, widely used way to ensure impeccable software quality is to write less software that does less stuff, and then spend eons honing that tiny lot.

Bugs are an inevitable byproduct of writing software. Sure, there are all sorts of techniques and potions that promise to decrease how many of the damn critters run about, but only the comically hyperbole pretends that complete eradication is possible.
Useless software can be entirely bug free, yet remain entirely useless. Useful software can be ridden with bugs, yet remain highly valuable.
The value of software depends far more upon the problem it solves than the quality by which it does so.
It's really the pinnacle of myopia when we as users demand and demean software makers to fix our pet bug, just because we hit it.
Marvel at the miracle it is that anything as complex as a modern piece of software works at all!
posts.key_insight: Software bugs are normal and inevitable; the rational response is disciplined triage based on impact, not emotional outrage demanding every bug be treated as an emergency.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on a viral photo of his luxurious Malibu home office, contrasting it with his dim, cluttered Copenhagen office where Ruby on Rails and Basecamp were created. He argues that the creative work happening on the screen matters far more than the surroundings. Using a Coco Chanel quote about the best things in life being free, he emphasizes that meaningful, engaging work is the true source of fulfillment. The fancy office is nice but secondary to the intellectual satisfaction of building things. He wishes more people would find inspiration in the humble workspace rather than the glamorous one.
key_points: The most enjoyable part of any office is the creative work that happens there, not the view or aesthetics
Ruby on Rails and Basecamp were built in a dim, cluttered Copenhagen room — proof that environment is secondary to meaningful work
The gap between 'the best things' (free, creative fulfillment) and 'the second best things' (expensive luxuries) is existential
A good work life requires calm, interruption-free pursuit of interesting projects — not a glamorous setting
Secure the free, fulfilling aspects of work first; if the luxury chase fails, you'll still be winning
money_quotes: The best thing about work is getting the opportunity to exercise your human capacity for creativity, ingenuity, progress.
I would never contemplate to give up 'the best thing', a work life filled with engaging, challenging tasks of meaning, for more, or any!, of those 'second best things', like a nice view.
This dim room holds claim to some of the greatest intellectual achievements of my entire career. AND I LOVED IT!
Nobody is going to look at that office with envy because its allure is entirely internal.
A place of calm for interruption-free pursuits of interesting projects. A portal to the state of flow.
Your first mission in life ought to be to ensure that all those free, best things are solidly in place before caring to chase the rest of the list.
If that latter chase doesn't yield the spoils, so what, you'll still be #winning at life from a hand flush with aces.
posts.key_insight: The fulfillment of meaningful creative work vastly outweighs any luxury of environment, and a dim room where great things are built is worth more than a dream office with an ocean view.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on 20+ years of working with the same technologies and argues that the tech industry's narrative of constant, disruptive change is overblown. Drawing from his experience with Ruby, Rails, and Basecamp, he demonstrates that core web technologies have remained remarkably stable for decades. He acknowledges genuine paradigm shifts like mobile computing and AI but contends these are rare exceptions. Citing Jeff Bezos's advice to invest in things that don't change, he argues that compounding skills in stable technologies pays off far more than chasing every new trend.
key_points: Core web technologies (Ruby, SQL, HTTP, HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Linux) have remained fundamentally stable for 20+ years despite version changes
Genuine paradigm shifts like the iPhone or AI are rare; most technological progress is incremental improvement on enduring ideas
Media coverage creates a distorted impression of constant disruption because stability isn't newsworthy
Compound interest applies to skills — deep investment in stable technologies pays increasing dividends over time
Bezos's principle of investing in things that don't change applies to developer skills, not just business logistics
money_quotes: There are paradigm shifts, like the introduction of mainstream mobile computing with the iPhone, or the internet before that, and perhaps now the advent of serious AI, but these shifts are rare. Most of the time, it's incremental improvement on enduring ideas.
All the energy I've invested into learning Ruby, SQL, HTTP, HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Linux, and the other mainstays of web development, continues to propel my work to this day.

There's of course nothing interesting about "yep, in this sector of commercial development, things have been pretty much stable for two decades!", and a lots of interesting angles on "AI is coming for us all!!".
Most people working in technology today do so with tools and techniques that have been stable for twenty years or more.
Invest in things that don't change.
The magic of compound interest applies to skills as well as to investments.
If you can keep your cool, and not thrash about reacting to every dip or peak, you usually make out pretty well in the end.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's narrative of relentless disruption is largely a myth — most foundational technologies remain stable for decades, and deeply investing in them compounds like interest.

posts.summary: DHH shares a passage from REWORK about the perishable nature of inspiration, arguing that while ideas last forever, the motivation to execute them has an expiration date. He urges acting immediately when inspiration strikes, calling it a productivity multiplier that can compress weeks of work into hours. He then reflects on his own recent experience of riding a wave of motivation to pursue a new vision, accomplishing months of work in weeks.
key_points: Ideas are immortal but inspiration is perishable — it has an expiration date like fresh fruit
Acting immediately on inspiration can compress weeks of work into hours through sheer motivation
Inspiration cannot be shelved or scheduled — it demands action in the moment it arrives
DHH validates the principle with his own recent experience of intense productive output driven by a new vision
money_quotes: Ideas are immortal. They last forever. What doesn't last forever is inspiration.
Inspiration is like fresh fruit or milk: It has an expiration date.
If you want to do something, you've got to do it now.
When you're high on inspiration, you can get two weeks of work done in twenty-four hours.
Inspiration is a time machine in that way.
Inspiration is a now thing. If it grabs you, grab it right back and put it to work.
posts.key_insight: Inspiration is a fleeting productivity multiplier that demands immediate action — shelving it guarantees losing it.

posts.summary: DHH reveals that HEY email service originally started as an attempt to build Highrise 2, a successor to 37signals' 2007 CRM product. After several failed skunkworks attempts, the team realized email is the true center of CRM and built HEY first without the CRM features. Now they're adding CRM-like capabilities back with HEY Workflows and Contact Notes. The goal is to serve small businesses that need 'email++' rather than competing with enterprise tools like Salesforce.
key_points: HEY was originally conceived as Highrise 2, not a general-purpose email service — email turned out to be the foundation of CRM
HEY Workflows replaces the need for dedicated SaaS tools for small-scale processes like hiring funnels or invoice tracking
37signals deliberately avoids building for enterprise needs, staying focused on small business use cases
The philosophy is 'just-right' software that sits between an address book (too little) and traditional CRM (too much)
money_quotes: It was realizing that email is the true center of the CRM that paved the path toward a real product.

No, it's not going to replace Salesforce for huge enterprise teams that need to calculate their pipeline value for the VP of Finance for next quarter. We don't know anything about that world (and would prefer to keep it like that!).
For every workflow, there's probably a dedicated SaaS solution that you could add to your monthly nut, but do you really need it?
If you only hire a couple of times per year, you don't need a dedicated hiring app.
Your address book doesn't do enough. Traditional CRM software tries to do too much.
Small business users who just need email++ to run their shop – not a ton of individual, expensive services.
posts.key_insight: Email is the true center of CRM for small businesses, and most don't need dedicated SaaS tools when lightweight workflow features built into their email can do the job.

posts.summary: DHH tells the story of how he came to own his all-time dream car, the Pagani Zonda HH, inspired by Doug DeMuro's video about fulfilling his own dream car purchase. What started as a tourist visit to the Pagani factory during a 2010 European road trip turned into commissioning a custom one-million-euro hypercar after a chance connection arranged a personal meeting with Horacio Pagani himself. DHH uses the story to defend unapologetic enthusiasm for cars and hobbies against modern tendencies to problematize everything. He announces his return to Instagram specifically to share car photos, arguing that excessive self-critique about consumption and hobbies is more ostentatious than the indulgences themselves.
key_points: Doug DeMuro's authentic, unpretentious enthusiasm for cars inspired DHH to reflect on and publicly celebrate his own passion
A serendipitous chain of events during a 2010 European road trip led to DHH commissioning a custom Pagani Zonda HH with Horacio Pagani himself
DHH dreamed of the Zonda since 2004 — the same year Basecamp and Ruby on Rails launched — when he had no fortune or even a driver's license
He pushes back against the cultural tendency to problematize every hobby and indulgence, arguing people should be allowed to enjoy things without sanctimonious self-critique
He's returning to Instagram solely to share car content, reframing it as a tool for simple enjoyment rather than toxic comparison
money_quotes: I refuse to give up that affection, despite all the many ways it's possible to PROBLEMATIZE cars today.
We can argue the virtues of public transit, bikes, or pollution-free inner cities, and still let out a shriek of COUNTACH! when a bright yellow Lamborghini passes us by.
Life is full of contradiction, and the heart beats for passion before reason. No apologies needed.
This was the same year Basecamp and Ruby on Rails came into the world. From a small apartment in Copenhagen – before I even had a driver's license! – and with no fortune to my name.
Which is exactly the kind of impossible dream that bedroom posters of cool cars have permitted since the supercar was invented.
Can we just enjoy some of those second best things in life that the world has to offer? Without being a bore about every which way it's inevitably a symptom of late-stage capitalism or whatever?
I've come to consider that even more ostentatious than a bright yellow Lamborghini these days.
Not only do we still own the Pagani Zonda HH, but it is still my favorite car of all-time. The grail. The one we'll never sell.
posts.key_insight: Pursuing childhood dreams unapologetically and refusing to let cultural guilt drain the joy from passionate hobbies is itself a form of authenticity worth defending.

posts.summary: DHH details 37signals' plan to save approximately $7 million over five years by leaving the cloud. After spending $3.2 million on cloud services in 2022, they're purchasing around $600,000 in Dell hardware with AMD Zen4 chips to replace $2.3 million in annual cloud compute costs. The math works out to roughly $840,000 per year for owned hardware, power, and bandwidth versus $2.3 million in the cloud. He argues any mid-sized SaaS company with stable workloads that isn't benchmarking cloud costs against owned hardware is committing financial malpractice.
key_points: 37signals spent $3.2M on cloud in 2022, with $2.3M on compute they plan to replace with ~$600K in hardware amortized over five years
The total annual cost of owned infrastructure (hardware, power, bandwidth) comes to $840K versus $2.3M in the cloud
They're adding 4,000 vCPUs across two data centers using AMD Zen4 chips with Gen 4 NVMe drives, fitting into existing rack space
The cloud exit excludes S3 storage (8 petabytes) which will be addressed separately in 2024
They abandoned enterprise Kubernetes in favor of building their own tooling
money_quotes: We stand to save about $7m in server expenses over five years from doing so. Without changing the size of our ops team.
Spending $600,000 on a bunch of hardware might sound like a lot in the age of cloud. But if you amortize that over a conservative five years, it's just $120,000 per year!

Any mid-sized SaaS business and above with stable work loads that does not benchmark their rental bill for servers in the cloud against buying their own boxes is committing financial malpractice at this point.
That's a total of $840,000/year for everything. Bandwidth, power, and boxes on an amortization schedule of five years. Compared to $2.3m in the cloud.
In round numbers, let's call it saving a million and a half dollars per year.
I suggest you call Dell, then call Deft. Get some real world numbers. Make up your own mind.
posts.key_insight: For mid-sized SaaS companies with stable workloads, owning hardware instead of renting cloud compute can cut server costs by more than 60%, making cloud-only strategies a form of financial malpractice.

posts.summary: DHH outlines five core values driving 37signals' decision to leave cloud infrastructure, going beyond the cost argument he's previously made. The values center on independence from hyperscalers, serving the open internet, fiscal responsibility, industry leadership, and maintaining entrepreneurial adventure. He frames the cloud exit as both a practical business decision and an ideological stance against the consolidation of internet infrastructure. The post positions 37signals as pioneers showing other SaaS companies that owning hardware is superior to renting it.
key_points: Cloud dependency violates 37signals' core value of independence, making them subject to Amazon's pricing decisions
Consolidating servers under a few hyperscalers erodes the open, decentralized nature of the internet
For established SaaS businesses, renting cloud infrastructure is fiscally irresponsible when you're paying purchase price every few months in rent
The cloud-as-default narrative for SaaS companies is wrong — most businesses of sufficient size should own their infrastructure
