Long-running companies need ambitious projects to maintain energy and avoid stagnation
money_quotes: Being trapped in Amazon's cloud, and having to suffer the indignities of outrageous pricing for new experiments, has become an intolerable violation of this core value.
We will not contribute to the erosion of those ideals by voting with our dollars for further consolidation of the servers that run this beautiful free haven into the hands of a few hyperscalers.
We're paying as much to rent the donkey every few months as it would cost to buy it. When that's the case, you should own the donkey!
Cloud has been sold as The Answer to SaaS companies like ours for a decade or more. I bought the narrative. We bought the narrative. The narrative is not true.
Most SaaS business of our size and above should be owning their infrastructure rather than renting it.
To keep the fire alive, we should continue to set our standards high, keep our values honest, and press forward into new territories on all fronts. Otherwise we will shrivel.
posts.key_insight: Leaving the cloud is not just a cost optimization but a values-driven decision about independence, internet stewardship, and refusing to fund the consolidation of infrastructure under a few dominant players.

posts.summary: DHH argues that cost control deserves the same attention as revenue growth in tech companies. He pushes back against the Silicon Valley obsession with top-line growth, making the case that profits—not just revenue—deliver independence and sustainability. He distinguishes between smart cost discipline and counterproductive penny-pinching, like forcing employees through bureaucratic expense approvals for small purchases. At 37signals, the philosophy is to cut costs on vendors and overhead while investing generously in people.
key_points: Costs matter as much as revenue to the bottom line, but tech culture glorifies only the revenue side
Cost control should be everyone's concern, not delegated solely to finance departments
There's a critical difference between strategic cost discipline and penny-wise bureaucracy that wastes more than it saves
Profits equal independence, sustainability, and prosperity—they're not just a vanity metric
Companies should cut vendor costs aggressively but invest generously in employees and their wellbeing
money_quotes: Revenue gets all the glory in the land of tech. The unlimited upside! Growth cures all!
Profits equal independence, sustainability, and, ultimately, prosperity.
I have a distinct distaste for waste. Money spent on the frivolous or the ill-considered is money that can't be spent elsewhere.
Cost hammering left entirely to folks who like spreadsheets a little too much is a dangerous recipe.
I once had to spend several days navigating the expense maze at a company to buy a $35 book. It must have cost the company hundreds if not thousands of dollars in lost attention and motivation.
That's not cost control, that's cost myopia.
Don't spend money on vendors that you could spend on employees.
posts.key_insight: Smart cost control is a competitive advantage and a form of respect for your business, but it must target real waste—not nickel-and-dime employees into dysfunction.

posts.summary: DHH argues that when a team achieves productive momentum, the best managerial response is to do nothing and let it run. He contends that professional managers often feel compelled to tinker with processes, teams, and missions because that's their job identity, even when intervention is counterproductive. He credits 37signals' productivity to having very few full-time managers, with most people (including himself and Jason Fried) working in the trenches. The post advocates for management as an occasional hat to wear rather than a permanent role.
key_points: Team momentum is rare and fragile — once achieved, the worst thing a manager can do is intervene
Professional managers are structurally incentivized to tinker even when things are working well
37signals maintains high productivity with only a couple of full-time managers among eighty people
Management should be an occasional role, not a permanent identity — leaders should primarily do the actual work
Having fewer managers eliminates the temptation to fix what isn't broken
money_quotes: The easiest way to mess up a good thing is to mess with it at all.
Sitting on their hands, or at least just polishing the edges, is not a response that comes natural to most professional managers.
Management is not our first love. It's a hat to wear when the occasion calls, then one we happily put back on the shelve when the deed is done.
The vast majority of everyone at 37signals are working in the trenches, and leading from the front.
This relative lack of full-time managers means the temptation to mess with what ain't broken just largely isn't there.
Idle managerial hands run's the devil's workshop.
posts.key_insight: The most productive thing a manager can do when a team has momentum is nothing at all — and companies with fewer full-time managers are less likely to sabotage their own success.

posts.summary: DHH argues that serverless computing is the cloud industry's latest marketing mantra used to deflect criticism about cloud costs and complexity. He breaks down the economics of cloud computing as a bulk-buy-sell-by-piece model, showing that serverless simply slices the server more thinly, enabling cloud providers to extract even higher margins. For customers who need sustained compute, serverless is a worse deal than traditional cloud, compounded by severe vendor lock-in. The cloud only makes economic sense for companies with highly variable demand or those too small for the costs to matter.
key_points: Cloud computing works on the principle of buying in bulk and selling by the piece — serverless just slices the pieces thinner for higher provider margins
If you consistently need the computing power of a whole server, serverless makes you pay far more per clock cycle than owning or even renting a full machine
Serverless introduces deeper lock-in through proprietary cloud-native services, making it harder to leave when the economics stop working
The cloud is primarily suited for companies with large demand swings or early-stage companies spending too little for it to matter
money_quotes: Serverless has become a mantra to chant because it's still appears just magic enough that most people won't question the fundamentals. But you should.

Instead of renting out your one big server to seven customers at $200/month, you rent out individual function executions to 100 customers at $20/month. This now clears $1,000/month in profit instead of just $400/month. No wonder cloud providers love serverless!
There's no magic that can change the fundamental fact that if you need all the computing cycles of a computer, you ought to own that computer.
The further down the rabbit hole you go with 'cloud-native' services in serverless, the harder it'll be to climb out when you realize that you should own the donkey rather than rent it.
It's basically a credit agreement at a 25% APR. Tread wisely!
If you start off with a proprietary serverless setup, you might well find the lock-in impossible to escape by the time the rental math no longer works.
posts.key_insight: Serverless computing doesn't change the fundamental economics of the cloud — it just lets providers slice their hardware thinner for higher margins while locking customers deeper into proprietary ecosystems.

posts.summary: DHH introduces Kamal, a new deployment tool built by 37signals to replace both Capistrano and Kubernetes as part of their cloud exit strategy. Kamal combines Capistrano's procedural simplicity with Docker containerization, running deployments from your local machine rather than cloud-based pipelines. It deploys HEY in 20 seconds compared to minutes with Kubernetes, while dramatically reducing configuration complexity. The tool enables cloud-style containerization benefits on bare-metal hardware and is released as MIT-licensed open source software.
key_points: Kamal replaces both the 2005-era Capistrano and complex Kubernetes pipelines with a simpler Docker-based approach that runs locally
Deployment speed drops from minutes (Kubernetes) to as little as 20 seconds, with far fewer moving parts and lines of configuration
The tool makes infrastructure vendor-independent, working equally well on bare metal or cloud servers
Kamal is the key technical enabler of 37signals' cloud exit strategy for HEY and legacy applications
Released as free, MIT-licensed open source as part of 37signals' philosophy of infrastructure independence
money_quotes: Kamal marries the procedural simplicity of Capistrano with the advantages of modern containerization techniques.
It sits on top of basic Docker, and harvests all the benefits you get from isolated containers with a sliver of the complexity associated with most other solutions.
With Kamal, we can deploy a new version of HEY in as little as 20 seconds.
The bigger factor, and the reason we wrote Kamal, is because it allows us to use the cloud advantages of containerization on our own bare-metal hardware.
It makes your entire system setup vendor independent in a way we never managed to truly achieve with Kubernetes.
It's our gift, as the makers of Basecamp & HEY, to everyone who'd like more independence, to serve the internet, and to spend their money wisely on their infrastructure.
posts.key_insight: Modern containerization benefits don't require Kubernetes complexity — Kamal delivers Docker's advantages with Capistrano's simplicity, enabling cloud-exit independence.

posts.summary: DHH argues that confident predictions about Twitter's demise after Musk's takeover proved completely wrong, as the platform continued operating with far fewer employees. He extends this to broader failed prophecies including COVID lab leak dismissals, mask effectiveness claims, and lockdown policies, arguing that experts and pundits across all political sides consistently get major predictions wrong. The post uses these examples to advocate for epistemic humility and against internet censorship of so-called misinformation, since today's banned opinion may turn out to be tomorrow's accepted truth.
key_points: Twitter continued functioning normally through major events despite losing over 75% of its workforce, proving the company was vastly overstaffed
Expert predictions about COVID origins, mask effectiveness, and lockdown consequences have been systematically revised, vindicating positions that were once censored
Failed prophecies are not partisan — all sides consistently get big predictions wrong, and credentialed experts are no exception
Content moderation and censorship of 'misinformation' actively delayed society from reaching the truth on multiple important topics
Epistemic humility should replace confident prediction-making and the impulse to silence dissenting views
money_quotes: The prophecy failed and the farewells were in vain.
Elon has definitively proven that it did indeed does not take 7,000 people to operate Twitter.
Nobody knows anything! Most predictions are just wishful thinking on fancy paper!
When all these highly-credentialed people can get it so wrong for so long, what dearly-held beliefs might you sit with that aren't so?
If you're going to put on a mask, make it one that delivers a steady flow of humility.
Those attempts delayed us all in advancing closer to the truth at the fastest pace possible.
Your rock is bound to often be in the wrong place, so you should have some sympathy for those who pick another one.
Let the tide of truth submerge whatever rock the facts can swallow.
posts.key_insight: The consistent failure of expert predictions and mainstream consensus on major issues — from Twitter's survival to COVID policies — should humble us all and make us deeply skeptical of efforts to censor dissenting viewpoints as misinformation.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how Kent Beck's Extreme Programming and the Agile Manifesto shaped his approach to software development. He champions two mottos — 'the simplest thing that could possibly work' and 'you aren't gonna need it' — as the most powerful guides for productive programming. He argues that combining these principles with deliberate outsider ignorance enables paradigm-breaking work, citing Rails, Hotwire, and Kamal as examples. The post encourages developers to read Beck's work and resist the natural urge to overcomplicate.
key_points: Beck's XP methodology was revolutionary for its irreverent insistence on practicality over Big Upfront Design
The mottos 'simplest thing that could possibly work' and 'YAGNI' remain DHH's guiding principles decades later
Cultivated ignorance of existing paradigms enables first-principles thinking and breakthrough solutions
These principles explain how 37signals moves from concept to working software in days rather than months
Human nature resists simplicity — developers are primed to dwell on potentialities rather than act
money_quotes: In the battle for software development's soul, Beck was formulating the war cry for a rebel army tired of going in circles from commands up high.
It's so easy to get lost in sentiments like "but wouldn't it be nice if..." and "we'll probably also need...". You need pithy mental antidotes at the ready to provide a counter.
It took being an outsider to J2EE to pursue Ruby on Rails. And being an outsider to JavaScript to chase Hotwire. And now, again, being an outsider to Kubernetes to sail with Kamal.
It's easier to break the paradigms if you're not enmeshed in them on the daily.
It's how you go from nothing to working software in days, not weeks or months.
Human nature is primed to dwell on all potentialities vs the activating the instinct for action. So the mottos help.
Few changes in perspective will unlock that hallowed 10x potential like this.
posts.key_insight: The most productive software development comes from relentlessly pursuing the simplest possible solution while cultivating enough outsider ignorance to challenge entrenched paradigms.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the era of freely-flowing VC money that let SaaS startups ignore team productivity is over. With profitability now a survival necessity rather than a distant goal, startups must ship more with fewer people. He offers three concrete recommendations: delay native app development in favor of the mobile web, hire full-stack developers instead of splitting frontend and backend, and hire designers who can work directly with HTML and CSS. He holds up 37signals as proof that tiny teams using Rails and Hotwire can build major products, and urges entrepreneurs to rethink bloated team structures before conditions worsen.
key_points: VC funding contraction forces SaaS startups to treat hires as costs rather than trophies, making individual productivity critical again
Native app development should be delayed in favor of the mobile web, which is now capable enough for most B2B use cases
Full-stack developers who can take an idea to production alone are far more productive than specialized frontend/backend splits
Designers must work natively with HTML and CSS rather than operating solely through Figma handoffs
37signals ships features with 1 programmer + 1 designer using Rails and Hotwire, proving small teams can build major products
money_quotes: Entrepreneurs everywhere are suddenly having to count each hire as a cost rather than a trophy.
The same person has to be capable of taking an idea to production entirely on their own. If they can't, your setup is too complicated, and you need to simplify.
It almost feels like we're cheating when I talk to other shops and hear about how big the teams need to be in order to ship.
So many hats, so many people, so many tools. But during the go-go days, it just didn't matter.
Getting to profitability is no longer a distant, post-IPO nice-to-have, but a short-term necessity for survival.
Who cares if the stack you're using takes twice the number of people to ship meaningful updates? You can just hire four times as many heads!
It's easier to turn the ship around before you're in the middle of the storm. Right now it's just raining. But odds are worse is ahead.
posts.key_insight: The end of cheap VC money means SaaS startups must prioritize individual developer productivity by simplifying their tech stacks and shrinking their teams, not expanding them.

posts.summary: DHH argues that actions are far more persuasive than arguments alone. He contends that people are right to keep a high bar for changing their minds, since talk is cheap and plausible arguments exist for every position. Only by putting ideas to the test through real-world action can someone earn the credibility needed to change others' minds. The post frames this as 'skin in the game' — risking being wrong through action rather than safely opining from the sidelines.
key_points: Arguments alone cannot overcome entrenched convictions; only demonstrated actions can unlock closed minds
People are right to maintain a high bar for changing their positions, since plausible arguments exist for every side of any issue
Credibility is earned through successful actions and real-world risk-taking, not through rhetoric
Most people are willing to argue but few are willing to stake something real on their convictions
money_quotes: You can't convince someone invested in their convictions to the contrary by arguments alone.
Talk is cheap, and others are right to keep their considered positions from being for sale at a discount.
There are a thousand plausible arguments available for every possible side of any issue, but rarely more than a few that can be substantiated by reality, once put to the test.
This is why we listen more to those who do than those who merely opine.
Credibility is thus the consequence of successful actions.
There's an endless supply of people willing to stake nothing on their convictions in the realm of arguments, but only a few ready to risk the effort of being wrong by actually following through.
posts.key_insight: Credibility comes not from the quality of your arguments but from your willingness to risk being wrong by putting your ideas into action.

posts.summary: DHH recounts how 37signals hired a high-profile ad agency for Basecamp's first branding campaign, only to find their million-dollar proposal for a single 30-second spot wildly mismatched with the company's $5 million total marketing budget. After parting ways with the agency, their new head of marketing and visual storyteller produced an equally compelling commercial in-house for just $37,000. The story illustrates 37signals' longstanding ethos of doing more with less and rejecting the assumption that great results require massive spending.
key_points: A top-tier ad agency proposed spending $1 million on a single 30-second commercial involving renting an actual wind tunnel
The 4-5x placement-to-production rule meant a $1M production would consume the entire $5M annual marketing budget on one spot
Two in-house employees produced an equally well-received commercial for $37,000 — roughly 1/27th the agency's price
Hiring people accustomed to Apple/Nike budgets was a predictable mismatch with 37signals' constraints
Resourcefulness and creative ownership can substitute for lavish production budgets
money_quotes: They were smart, funny... and entirely out of sync with our budget.

That's a huge multiple of everything we've ever spent before on marketing in the entire 24-year history of the company, but a pittance when you work with a top-tier agency full of people who used to do campaigns for the like of Apple or Nike.
The agency wanted to spend one million dollars on a thirty-second spot, which, while funny and clever, involved renting an actual wind tunnel for a couple of days.
If we spent one million dollars on shooting the commercial, we'd literally have to spend the rest of the budget on running just that one spot. That didn't seem smart.

Together they came up with a new idea for a different brand commercial, did everything from storyboarding to location scouting to shooting to editing themselves, and in just a few weeks produced our amazingly well-received first commercial for a total all-in budget of $37,000.

No, it didn't have a freaking wind tunnel, but it was just as funny of a concept, even more on point for fit with the marketing message, and the final result looks every bit as ace to my eyes as anything you'd see produced at 10-20x the budget.
posts.key_insight: Constraints breed creativity — a small in-house team with skin in the game can match the output of an expensive agency at a fraction of the cost by staying scrappy and aligned with the company's identity.

posts.summary: DHH argues that angry customers provide the most valuable feedback a business can receive, because most dissatisfied customers leave silently without explaining why. When a customer is upset enough to voice their frustration, it represents a rare window into honest criticism. The challenge is separating the emotional delivery from the actionable information. He urges businesses to resist the defensive instinct and instead extract maximum insight from these confrontations.
key_points: Most customers who leave will never tell you the real reason, making honest feedback extremely scarce
Every angry, vocal customer represents at least a dozen silent ones lost to the same issues
The natural defensive instinct to dismiss emotional feedback is the enemy of improvement
Leaders must train themselves not to reflexively minimize complaints when customers escalate
money_quotes: The only time you're truly allowed a free peek at the naked truth of why your product or service didn't fit, is when the customer is really upset.
Every loud, angry customer represents at least a dozen silent ones you lost to the same issues.

You can either protect your ego from the sharp words of a frustrated customer by dismissing the feedback entirely OR you can be smart enough to filter out the emotion while retaining the crucial information that'll improve your offering.
We bond with our creations and our companies because that's the human thing to do if you like your work. So those sharp words can and often do hurt your feelings.
That's how you win. By building better rather than defending worse.
posts.key_insight: Angry customers are the rare exception to the silent majority of churned users, making their unfiltered frustration the most valuable feedback a business can receive.

posts.summary: DHH argues that tech companies facing budget pressures should reduce their cloud spending before resorting to layoffs. He distinguishes between big tech layoffs, which release captive talent back into the broader economy, and smaller company layoffs driven by cost-cutting necessity. He contends that cloud bills represent a grotesquely large expense that companies overlook when trimming costs, and points to 37signals' own cloud exit as proof that leaving the cloud is faster and easier than most assume. His core message is that companies owe it to their employees to explore cloud cost reduction before printing pink slips.
key_points: Big tech layoffs have a silver lining by releasing talent hoarded at inflated salaries back into the broader economy
Smaller company layoffs are more concerning because they often reflect genuine financial distress rather than overhiring corrections
Cloud spend is one of the two largest line items at most tech companies, yet it's often overlooked when cutting costs
37signals' cloud exit has been faster and easier than expected, suggesting other companies overestimate the difficulty
Companies have a moral obligation to cut infrastructure costs before cutting people
money_quotes: We need smart people focused on other problems beyond figuring out how to make people click more ads.
At the majority of tech companies I've talked to, there are basically two big line items: staff and cloud.
It just frustrates me to no end to see tech companies trying to cut costs by firing folks rather than reigning in their cloud spend.
Maybe you're also overestimating how difficult it would be to buy your own hardware and exit the cloud.
It's been way easier and way faster than I had dared hope for.
You certainly owe it to your employees before you start printing those pink slips.
posts.key_insight: Companies facing budget pressure should exhaust cloud cost reductions before resorting to layoffs, because infrastructure is easier to cut than people are to replace.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Musk's decision to charge $7/month for full Twitter participation is a bold and fascinating experiment worth supporting. He dismisses critics who reflexively oppose everything Musk does, calling their performative distancing from controversial figures 'pathetic.' The core argument is that nobody has ever tried to fund an established social network through user payments instead of targeted ads, and this experiment could illuminate a path away from ad-driven internet models. DHH also notes that Musk's changes have spurred healthy competition through alternatives like Mastodon, which is the market working as intended.
key_points: Musk's paid Twitter model is an unprecedented experiment in funding a social network at scale through user payments rather than ads
Critics who oppose everything Musk does while performatively distancing themselves from him are being intellectually dishonest
The experiment could reveal whether there's a viable alternative to the ad-driven business model that has degraded the internet
Musk's changes have created healthy market competition by motivating alternatives like Mastodon
Despite endless predictions of Twitter's demise after mass layoffs, the platform has not collapsed
money_quotes: This preemptive distancing from controversial figures when they do something you actually agree with is just so performative and pathetic.
Can you fund a social network with user payments? In contrast to the current established wisdom that only targeted ads will do it. We don't know! Nobody has ever tried to do it at this scale.
I for one am a fan of any experiment that might shine light on a path out of the ad infestation that's currently possessing the internet.
Musk gave his most ardent opponents the perfect motivation to seed and cultivate alternatives. And we've gotten those!

All the attempts have usually been baked in from the beginning, thus posing a real challenge to reaching critical mass. But Musk is now going to try the experiment on a network that already has critical mass. THAT'S INTERESTING!
I also absolutely commend the man for taking big, bold risks when it's primarily his own money on the line!
I'm happily voting with my seven dollars to see where this experiment goes. May you live in interesting times!
posts.key_insight: Musk's paid Twitter experiment is a uniquely valuable test of whether a social network at scale can be funded by users instead of ads, and deserves support regardless of one's opinion of Musk personally.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his return to Denmark after 15 years in America, arguing that the US can never replicate Scandinavian social democracy because it lacks the cultural homogeneity and conformity that makes those systems work. He observes that Denmark's high-trust, collectivist society depends on fierce pressure to assimilate and conform, while America's strength lies in its individualism and openness to immigrants. The post represents a shift in DHH's thinking: he now sees American Exceptionalism as genuine rather than delusional, recognizing that building a functional multicultural nation of 300+ million people is historically unprecedented. He concludes by choosing optimism about America and abandoning his desire for it to become more like Denmark.
key_points: Denmark's social democratic success depends on cultural homogeneity and intense conformity pressure that cannot be transplanted to a diverse nation like America
America's ease of integration for immigrants is an underappreciated strength — even immigration officers have foreign accents and nobody bats an eye
The individualism that prevents American collectivism is the same quality that makes its multicultural society possible, creating a fundamental trade-off
American Exceptionalism is more legitimate than DHH previously believed: sustaining a functional nation of 300+ million people from diverse backgrounds is historically unprecedented
Living in Denmark paradoxically made DHH appreciate America more and abandon his desire for the US to adopt Nordic-style governance
money_quotes: I didn't fully appreciate the tremendous, underpinning power of a homogenous culture to fasten all these planks of a socially-democratic state – until I returned after 15 years Over There.
It may sound silly, but it's this kind of fierce conformity that carries everything else.
I arrive at immigration, and the officers literally guarding entry to God's own country will have a thick, foreign accent. This is normal. Nobody bats an eye.
The cliché of the melting pot might be trite but it's still apt.

If we're going to live in a truly multicultural, heterogeneous country together, we can't also expect the levels of trust and collectivism that makes countries like Denmark capable of working like they do.
Creating a nation out of so many other nationalities, and making it not just work, but thrive on so many parameters absolutely is an exceptional achievement!
I choose to cheer for America. In all its contradictions, despite all its flaws. I no longer yearn for it to just WAKE UP, and become like Denmark. Let America be America.
posts.key_insight: The cultural homogeneity and conformity that enable Scandinavian social democracy are fundamentally incompatible with the multicultural openness that makes America exceptional, and accepting this trade-off is more productive than envying what the other system provides.

posts.summary: DHH advocates for a 'bucket' approach to time management: instead of responding to tasks as they arrive, let related tasks accumulate and then process them all in a focused batch. He illustrates this with email (using HEY's Reply Later to batch 30-50 replies into one hour), GitHub issue triage, and expense reviews. He argues that constant interruptions create an illusion of busyness without real progress, while batching preserves the focused attention needed for meaningful work. The core realization is that time isn't scarce—attention is.
key_points: Batch related tasks into 'buckets' and empty them in focused sessions rather than handling each interruption as it arrives
Inbox zero and continuous email checking feel productive but fragment attention and destroy focus
Batching is key to sustainable open source maintenance—reviewing issues on your own schedule prevents burnout
Multitasking is a mirage; humans are single-core CPUs that lose productivity to context-switching costs
The scarce resource isn't time but attention, and protecting it through intentional sequencing is the real productivity hack
money_quotes: Every email is a drop, and every drop ripples the surface of your mind. It's careless to let them hit without a filter.
So many movements, so little progress.
Rejecting this way of working is why I usually feel very content about the progress I'm able to make on the things that matter, without feeling overwhelmed or busy all the time.
Letting your attention be disturbed 30-40-50 times over a week or two vs accepting a single interruption in the form of a focused hour. It's a monumental difference.
I'm happy to look at your suggestion or your feedback, but I'll do it on my own schedule, thank you very much.
Multitasking is a mirage. Humans run single-core CPUs. All they can do is swap context in and out, losing ever more to switching costs as they do.
There's enough time. Time isn't the scarce resource, attention is.
posts.key_insight: Productivity comes not from responding to everything immediately but from batching tasks into focused sessions, because attention—not time—is the truly scarce resource.

posts.summary: DHH reviews Tim Urban's book 'What's Our Problem?' and endorses its analysis of American political dysfunction. The book adds a second dimension to the political spectrum—not just left-right positioning, but whether disagreements manifest through 'high-rung' intellectual discourse or 'low-rung' tribal thinking. DHH appreciates that Urban traces how both sides fell into low-rung thinking, starting with Republicans in the mid-90s and progressives more recently. He argues the book may be more accessible to fence-sitting progressives than direct critiques like McWhorter's 'Woke Racism' because of its lighter tone and broader scope. DHH connects the analysis to his own experience with political controversy at Basecamp and expresses cautious optimism that the tide is turning toward healthier discourse.
key_points: Tim Urban's framework adds a vertical dimension to politics: not just what you believe, but how you engage—through idea supremacy and intellectual discourse vs. tribal low-rung thinking
Both political sides have succumbed to low-rung thinking at different times, with Republicans starting in the mid-90s and progressives following in the past decade
The book may reach progressive readers more effectively than direct critiques because it starts by acknowledging 'the other side started it' before turning its lens inward
DHH draws on his own Basecamp experience to validate that standing up to ideological pressure is difficult but increasingly feasible, especially in business
The prescription from both Urban and McWhorter is the same: actively resist ideological conformity to restore genuine intellectual discourse
money_quotes: It's central contribution is adding a second dimension to the political spectrum.

Our current problem is not so much disagreeing about those two central elements. All democracies will and should continue to do that. It's how that disagreements manifest themselves in political tactics.

He also sums up the feeling of having been made politically homeless, as a long-term, progressive-leaning individual, once the low-rung thinking of the woke nonsense conquered one important institution after another.
We have to stand up to the ideological golems in order to rescue our political discourse.
Refuse to let idea supremacy cow us into submission.
It's far easier to stand up to the golems today than it was a couple of years ago. At least in the realm of business. Things still look pretty rough in academia!
I would love to return to the idea lab. Argue positions for the intellectual sport and societal enlightenment again.
posts.key_insight: The core problem in American political discourse isn't left vs. right disagreement but the collapse of both sides into tribal low-rung thinking that suppresses genuine intellectual debate.

posts.summary: DHH shares the arrival of new Dell R7625 servers at 37signals' data centers in Chicago and Ashburn, marking a major milestone in their cloud exit. The twenty servers bring nearly 4,000 vCPUs, 7,680 GB of RAM, and 384TB of NVMe storage. He contrasts this with Basecamp's humble 2004 origin on a single-core Celeron with 256MB RAM. The cloud exit is ahead of schedule, expected to complete by end of spring rather than end of summer, with half the cloud applications already migrated using Kamal instead of Kubernetes. DHH urges other SaaS entrepreneurs to reconsider their cloud bills, arguing that modern hardware economics make self-hosting compelling again.
key_points: Twenty Dell R7625 servers with dual AMD EPYC 9454 CPUs deliver nearly 4,000 vCPUs, 7,680 GB RAM, and 384TB NVMe storage across two data centers
The cloud exit is ahead of schedule — half the cloud apps already migrated, with full exit expected by end of spring instead of end of summer
Switching from Kubernetes to Kamal dramatically simplified and accelerated the migration, going from decision to halfway-done in under three months
The $38,000/week cloud spend serves as powerful motivation, with projected savings of $7M over five years likely to be conservative
Basecamp Classic remains a multi-million dollar business despite not being updated in 13 years, demonstrating SaaS durability
money_quotes: It's a staggering amount of computing power in a shockingly small footprint.

We launched Basecamp on a single-core Celeron server with just 256MB of RAM back in 2004. Spinning rust at 7,200 RPM. And that was good enough to get the business from part-time to full-time in about a year.
It's kinda wild to think that it's been less than three months since we decided to scrap Kubernetes and pursue a simpler solution for the cloud exit with Kamal.
Basecamp Classic — still a multi-million dollar business, even if it hasn't been updated in about 13 years – that SaaS magic!
I imagined getting out of the cloud was going to be as hard as getting in. But that just hasn't proven to be the case.
Once you've gone cloud, it might seem impossible to contemplate getting out again, but don't believe that for a second.
Modern server hardware is incredible. We've taken huge leaps forward in performance, density, and cost over the last few years.
I think we're going to find that the napkin math I did for our public calculation of savings will be highly conservative.
posts.key_insight: Leaving the cloud is faster and easier than expected, and modern server hardware economics make self-hosting a financially compelling alternative to cloud for established SaaS businesses.

posts.summary: DHH announces Rails World, a new conference organized by the Rails Foundation's executive director Amanda Perino, to be held in Amsterdam in October 2023. The event celebrates the 20th anniversary of Ruby on Rails and will offer tickets at accessible prices thanks to corporate founding members who seeded the foundation with a million dollars. DHH expresses enthusiasm about the framework's sustained momentum over two decades and teases new frameworks and libraries from 37signals to be unveiled at the conference. The post serves as both a promotional announcement and a celebration of the Rails ecosystem's longevity and corporate support.
key_points: Rails World is a new 650-attendee conference in Amsterdam at affordable prices (€199-€299), underwritten by the Rails Foundation's corporate funding
Eight founding companies including Shopify, GitHub, and 37signals funded the Rails Foundation with a million dollars in seed money
The conference celebrates the 20th anniversary of Ruby on Rails, which DHH started building in summer 2003
37signals plans to unveil new frameworks and libraries at the event, some not yet publicly discussed
DHH has abandoned the idea that Rails would ever be 'done,' embracing continuous evolution with the web
money_quotes: It never ceases to amaze me how much momentum, excitement, and passion we've been able to sustain for now two decades.
I used to think the day would come when we'd just be 'done', but I've surrendered to the notion that Ruby on Rails can always improve on it's mission to be a full-stack framework.
The web continues to evolve, and Rails will continue to evolve with it.
This is what believing in a bright future for Ruby on Rails looks like at the corporate level.
Way to make an entrance!
posts.key_insight: After twenty years, Ruby on Rails has enough corporate backing and community momentum to launch a major new conference series, signaling that the framework's ecosystem is thriving rather than fading.

posts.summary: DHH reveals that 37signals spent $300,000 on 98 billboards across Boston to promote Basecamp as part of a multi-channel marketing campaign, and it was a clear failure. While other channels like TV and podcast ads performed well, the billboard campaign produced only barely measurable results, with each attributable trial signup costing thousands of dollars. He acknowledges the emotional satisfaction of seeing the brand in physical spaces but concludes the ROI was indefensible. The post serves as a transparent postmortem, noting that their refusal to use privacy-invasive targeted ads pushed them toward conventional channels like billboards, which simply don't work for software products.
key_points: 98 billboards across Boston for a month cost $300K and produced only barely measurable results with absurdly high per-signup costs
37signals deliberately avoids privacy-invasive targeted ads, which limits their marketing channels and pushed them toward conventional options like billboards
Billboard advertising for B2B software likely works more as executive vanity than effective customer acquisition
The willingness to experiment publicly and transparently share failures is presented as part of their marketing philosophy
money_quotes: That's advertisement! It's hard to know what's going to work unless you give it a go.

Since we're fighting competitors with one hand behind our back by refusing to use privacy-invasive ads targeted on personal data, we thought it was worth trying some more conventional channels, like billboards.
We ended up spending thousands of dollars on every attributable trial signup. Way outside the realm of anything we could claim a return on.
It felt pretty cool to see the irreverent Basecamp marketing messages appear on building-hugging billboards. There was an emotionally gratifying return on the investment.
Perhaps that explains why it used to be common to see business software advertised in places like San Francisco where a lot of executives of such companies could feel good about seeing them.
But yeah, it didn't work. We won't be doing that again. But we'll always have the pictures.
posts.key_insight: Billboard advertising for software products delivers emotional satisfaction for the advertiser but not measurable customer acquisition, and the vanity factor likely explains why so many tech companies do it anyway.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on whether AI truly represents an existential threat to software development as a profession. He acknowledges that while most past predictions of transformative change (including No Code movements) failed to materialize, AI feels genuinely different and the uncertainty is novel even after thirty years in the industry. He advocates for holding both skepticism and humility simultaneously — not descending into nihilism, but accepting that dramatic change occasionally does happen. He concludes with a poetic metaphor: software developers today may be perfecting kerosene lamps while electricity is being invented in the next room, and that's still a beautiful, necessary mission.
key_points: AI's threat to software development feels qualitatively different from previous hype cycles like No Code, the internet, or mobile — this might actually be 'The Big One'
Three decades of unfulfilled 'Change Everything Forever' predictions justify healthy skepticism, but also risk making us complacent when real transformation arrives
The practical response is neither dismissive skepticism nor professional nihilism, but stoic acceptance through negative visualization
Even if software development as we know it is ending, the work remains necessary and meaningful — the world still needs to see in the dark while electricity is being invented
The internet and mobile, despite being genuinely transformative, didn't fundamentally change how most day-to-day programming looks
money_quotes: I'd probably still wager that the fears are overstated – that we also got prematurely euphoric about the imminent prospects of self-driving cars – and that AI generating code is different from it evolving existing systems. But I wouldn't want to bet the house on it.
That uncertainty, whether we truly are on the cusp of a total transformation, is a surprising novelty to me, despite thirty years in software.

Even the two properly big transformations we've had in that time, the internet and mobile, havn't made the kind of programming most people do today look all that different from the kind of programming done in the 90s.
The purpose of such visualization is not to descend into the catacombs of professional nihilism.
There's nothing to be gained from nurturing existential fears of what you cannot change.

We're improving the burn efficiency. We're finding ever clearer forms of glass to let the light through. We're tinkering with a formula that's been around for a long time. But as we do, Edison and Tesla are busy inventing electricity in the other room.
That just might be us, right now, lighting the path to the future with the ways of what could soon be the past.
That's a beautiful mission. Even if it's a one-way ticket.
posts.key_insight: Software developers should hold skepticism and humility in equal measure about AI — continuing to do meaningful work while accepting that this time, the revolution might actually be real.

posts.summary: DHH argues that America's problems are real but overstated, pushing back against the prevailing narrative of decline. He uses data on gun deaths, median incomes, and Millennial wealth to show that many metrics are better than popular discourse suggests. Drawing on his experience living in Denmark, he contends that Americans romanticize Nordic societies without understanding the material trade-offs involved. He concludes that much of America's malaise is a self-inflicted vibe problem rather than a reflection of unprecedented decline, and calls for a realistic restoration of national self-confidence.
key_points: Gun violence and homicide rates, while high, are below historical peaks from the 1970s-90s, contradicting narratives of unprecedented crisis
Millennial median incomes are significantly above past generations in constant dollars, and homeownership rates are roughly comparable
Americans romanticize Nordic countries without reckoning with the broad-based taxes, smaller living spaces, and material sacrifices that fund those systems
Much of America's current malaise is a vibe-driven phenomenon rather than a reflection of actual unprecedented decline
A strong, self-confident America matters not just domestically but for global stability, including for the Nordic societies often held up as alternatives
money_quotes: America is a violent country. It's been a violent country for a long time. This is not a novel situation.
On purely median-income terms, all boats are rising in the US, even if the biggest boats are rising faster.
I think some Americans have a tendency to romanticize the Nordics. And to think all that's needed to turn the US into a similar society is just to tax some billionaires more. That's just nonsense.
Plenty of it also just stems from the general vibe that things are terrible because they feel terrible. And that's the part that seems self-inflicted, unnecessary, and correctable.
America needs to get its mojo back. Its self-confidence. Not the starry-eyed, see-no-evil-hear-no-evil kind, but the realistic kind.
I don't actually want to see where the world might go if a strong America is replaced on the world scene by the current prospective alternatives.
It took a break from being in the thick of it to realize that, yes, I am actually on Team America. Proudly so.
posts.key_insight: America's malaise is largely a self-inflicted vibe problem that obscures genuinely strong long-term trends, and restoring realistic self-confidence matters both domestically and globally.

posts.summary: DHH reports that migrating Basecamp Classic from AWS back to owned hardware delivered major performance improvements alongside cost savings. Median request times dropped from 67ms to 19ms, and median query times were cut in half. The app moved from AWS EKS with managed Kubernetes to Dell R7625 servers with AMD EPYC CPUs. He notes the entire application could theoretically run on a single $20,000 machine, amortizing to just $333/month for an app generating millions in annual revenue. While performance wasn't the primary motivation for leaving the cloud, DHH frames the speed boost as a bonus feature for Basecamp Classic's loyal user base.
key_points: Median request time dropped from 67ms to 19ms after moving from AWS to owned hardware, with 95% of requests now under 300ms
The entire Basecamp Classic application could run on a single Dell R7625 server with 196 vCPUs, costing under $20,000 amortized to $333/month
Basecamp Classic has come full circle from launching on one machine in 2004 to being able to run on one machine again in 2023
The cloud exit was motivated by cost savings, but performance gains came as an unexpected bonus
HEY is next on the cloud exit schedule, representing the biggest migration yet
money_quotes: The median request now runs in just 19ms, compared to 67ms before.
Each of these machines were less than $20,000. Amortize that over five years. That's $333/month for all the hardware needed to run Basecamp Classic today.

And this is still a large SaaS app that's generating literally millions of dollars in revenue per year! The vast majority of SaaS businesses out there would require far less firepower to service their customers.
Basecamp launched in 2004 on a single machine (with 1 vCPU!) and can now, in 2023, run on a single machine again.
Speed is a feature too, so I guess you could say we just shipped one!
posts.key_insight: Leaving the cloud for owned hardware doesn't just save money — it can deliver dramatically better performance, with median response times dropping by over 70%.

posts.summary: DHH argues that taking responsibility for something meaningful is itself a reward, not a burden to be minimized. He draws on Jordan Peterson and Viktor Frankl to suggest that people feeling overwhelmed may need more purpose, not less work. He connects this to Denmark's volunteer culture and his own twenty-plus years in open source development. He pushes back against the 'unpaid labor' framing of open source contributions, arguing it would be tragic if contributors started viewing their work primarily as a poorly-paid second job.
key_points: Burnout can stem from under-purpose rather than overwork — sometimes the answer is more meaningful responsibility, not less
Denmark's volunteer club culture demonstrates how people seek out responsibility as a source of purpose and identity
The 'unpaid labor' framing of open source mischaracterizes what is fundamentally a voluntary pursuit of purpose
Purpose often needs a 'third place' beyond home and work, and open source serves that role for many programmers
money_quotes: One of the straightest paths to purpose in life is to take responsibility for something (or someone).

There's something inherently counterintuitive about this notion that people who feel overwhelmed by life, or lost in its endless possibilities, might not need a lighter load, but a more meaningful burden instead.
It reminds me of the version of burnout that stems not from overwork, but from under-purpose.
Sometimes, the answer to "it's just all too much" is, weirdly, "can I have some more, please".
The more tragic I think it would be if all the hundreds of thousands of programmers who find purpose in open source start seeing their contributions primarily as a poorly-paid second job.
What a well of resentment that would fill.
Some people are lucky to find a full serving of purpose at home or at work. But many more need to find it in a third place.
posts.key_insight: Voluntary responsibility — especially in open source — is a source of purpose, not exploitation, and reframing it as unpaid labor poisons the well of meaning people draw from.

posts.summary: DHH proposes the term 'sovereign clouds' to describe companies that own their hardware in colocated datacenters rather than renting from AWS or other cloud providers. He acknowledges that 'cloud exit' terminology can alienate cloud-native technologists, and 'private cloud' sounds too corporate. He argues that 80-90% of cloud skills transfer directly to owned hardware, making the transition easier than expected. The core distinction he draws is ownership vs. renting, framing it as a matter of digital independence against centralization.
key_points: 80-90% of cloud-native skills transfer directly to running owned hardware — containers, load balancers, monitoring, and performance tools are literally the same
The key distinction is not public vs private cloud but owning vs renting your infrastructure
Cloud exit eliminates FinOps complexity — no more forensic accounting to understand your bill
DHH frames infrastructure ownership as an ideological stance against internet centralization and monopoly rents
money_quotes: 80-90% of what you need to know to run your own hardware these days is the same as what you need to know to operate in the rental clouds.
We need a counter to the "you will own nothing and be happy" pitch of the rental clouds.
That sentiment is simply alien to the ethos of the internet.
You no longer have to be a forensic accountant to understand your bill or a bulldog to guard against it running wild, if you own your hardware!
An aspirational goal for those with material and fundamental computing needs to own their digital homesteads rather than rent a farm from the big tech cloud lords.
It surely must be an easier learning curve than getting up to speed with Kubernetes!
posts.key_insight: Companies should aspire to 'sovereign clouds' — owning their infrastructure rather than renting from cloud providers — because the skill overlap is massive, the cost savings are real, and digital independence is worth pursuing.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Amazon Prime Video's decision to abandon their serverless microservices architecture in favor of a monolith, which saved them 90% on operating costs. He argues this vindicates his long-standing position that microservices are a harmful pattern when applied outside their original context of massive cross-organizational coordination. He traces the pattern of distributed architecture hype through J2EE, WS-Deathstar, and now microservices/serverless, calling it a 'zombie architecture' that keeps returning. He concludes that replacing method calls with network invocations within a single team is madness, and that the industry must stay vigilant against this recurring bad idea.
key_points: Amazon Prime Video saved 90% on costs by replacing serverless microservices with a monolith, validating monolith-first architecture
Service-oriented architecture made sense at Amazon's organizational scale but became toxic when adopted as microservices inside single applications
Microservices are a recurring zombie pattern — the same distributed-everything mistake has resurfaced through J2EE, WS-Deathstar, and now serverless
The microservices craze was driven by theoretical benefits that collapsed under real-world conditions, hitting hard scaling limits at 5% of expected load
The hype cycle around distributed architectures is sustained by vendor spending, hiring, and marketing rather than genuine engineering need
money_quotes: In theory, this would allow us to scale each service component independently. However, the way we used some components caused us to hit a hard scaling limit at around 5% of the expected load.
Now the real-world results of all this theory are finally in, and it's clear that in practice, microservices pose perhaps the biggest siren song for needlessly complicating your system.
As with many good ideas, this pattern turned toxic as soon as it was adopted outside its original context, and wreaked havoc once it got pushed into the internals of single-application architectures.
In many ways, microservices is a zombie architecture. Another strain of an intellectual contagion that just refuses to die.
You went from writing bad code to building bad infrastructure.
Replacing method calls and module separations with network invocations and service partitioning within a single, coherent team and application is madness in almost all cases.
Some bad ideas simply refuse to die no matter how many times you kill them.
posts.key_insight: Microservices and serverless are a recurring zombie architecture that trades simple method calls for needless network complexity, and Amazon's own 90% cost reduction by returning to a monolith proves the pattern fails even at the scale that supposedly demands it.

posts.summary: DHH argues that microservices architecture is almost always premature and unnecessary, pointing to the Prime Video case study and examples like GitHub and Shopify running massive monoliths. He provides a five-step recovery guide for teams that adopted microservices too early: stop creating new ones, consolidate critical paths, leave isolated hotspots for last, prune esoteric language diversity, and learn to use modules instead of network boundaries. He cites John Gall's principle that complex working systems must evolve from simple working ones. The post concludes with specific criteria for when microservices actually make sense: large evolved systems with clear boundaries and proven performance or organizational needs.
key_points: GitHub and Shopify run millions of lines of code as monoliths with thousands of programmers, undermining the argument that scale requires microservices
The worst microservice damage comes from splintering coherent flows (signup, checkout) across multiple systems, making coordinated changes cumbersome and error-prone
Microservice culture leads to proliferation of programming languages and frameworks, destroying conceptual comprehension so nobody can work on the whole system
Network boundaries are not a substitute for proper modular architecture—if you can't organize code with modules and namespaces, distributing it won't help
Microservices are only justified for large systems that evolved from simple ones, with clearly modularized boundaries and proven performance or organizational needs
money_quotes: The vast majority of systems are much better served by starting and staying with a majestic monolith.
You can't clean up a mess before you stop making more of it.

So much of the motivation for microservices has been driven by the fallacy that if you can't figure out how to properly architect a large system using programming tools like modules and namespaces, then you can solve this problem by partitioning it with network boundaries. No, no, no.

A complex system that works is invariably found to have evolved from a simple system that works. The inverse proposition also appears to be true: A complex system designed from scratch never works and cannot be made to work.
This is murder for conceptual comprehension, and leads to the common microservices symptom of 'nobody understands or can work on the whole system'.
Yes, 'it depends' is technically correct. But we offer no guidance to those looking to design better systems by simply saying that; we need to be willing to say WHAT IT DEPENDS ON.
Our industry is full of bright, passionate people. Many of them eager to start an Iron Man before they've done a 5K run.
posts.key_insight: Microservices are a recovery destination for large evolved systems, not a starting architecture—most teams should build majestic monoliths and use modules instead of network boundaries to manage complexity.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on creative downturns — periods where productivity stalls after intense creative highs or disruptions to routine. His remedy is simply to wait it out, filling the time with menial tasks until boredom itself reignites the creative spark. He describes creativity as cyclical, following natural patterns of intensity and dormancy. With age, he's learned to accept these oscillations rather than fighting them.
key_points: Creative downturns are best escaped not by forcing productivity but by waiting them out while handling mundane tasks
Boredom with distractions is the mechanism that restarts creativity — when nothing else is appealing, the editor beckons
Downturns typically follow dopamine highs from intense creative periods, or prolonged breaks from routine
Accepting the cyclical nature of creativity becomes easier with age and experience
money_quotes: If I'm stuck in a creative downturn, there's usually only one remedy: keep going.
The curse is usually broken when I'm sufficiently bored with the menial, the managerial, and the internet.
When there is literally nothing better to do than open the editor and code.
As if the creative juices really are juices, and you can chug the jug.
It can't be eternal sunshine in the productive mind.
The grey, wet days of today soak the soil for the sun of tomorrow.
Don't argue with nature. Just wait. It'll be bright before you know it.
posts.key_insight: Creative downturns aren't problems to solve but natural cycles to endure — boredom with distractions is itself the mechanism that restores the urge to create.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the long-running debate between static and dynamic typing, arguing it's fundamentally a matter of mindset rather than objective superiority. He confesses to previously holding an evangelical position for dynamic typing, viewing skeptics as lacking education or competence. He now embraces 'the magnificence of multiplicity,' comparing programming paradigms to art styles — constraining everyone to one would ruin the craft. He concludes that while not all programming debates are purely subjective, static vs dynamic typing and functional vs OOP clearly fall within the realm of legitimate personal preference.
key_points: The static vs dynamic typing debate is a matter of faith and mindset, not reason — arguments rarely convert anyone
Recent converts to either camp tend to be the loudest evangelists, like omnivores turned vegan
Programming's richness depends on paradigm diversity, just as art depends on stylistic diversity
DHH is a self-described 'recovering solutionist' who now recognizes that different paradigms produce equally excellent (and awful) software
True ambidexterity across paradigms is rare — most programmers have a natural orientation
money_quotes: I've heard a million arguments from both sides throughout my entire career, but seen very few of them ever convinced anyone of anything. As rationalizations masquerading as reason rarely do in matters of faith.
The rider will always justify the way of the elephant.
The shorter the faith, the brighter the flame.
Ruby with explicit, static typing would be like a salad with a scoop of ice cream. They just don't go together.
These days, I've come to appreciate the magnificence of multiplicity.
It would ruin the magic of programming. This unique fusion of art and engineering.
I'm a recovering solutionist.
As long as I never have to soil my Ruby with type hints or write all front-end code in TypeScript, I can so happily coexist with those who adore Go or can't stand JavaScript.
posts.key_insight: The static vs dynamic typing debate is not a technical argument to be won but a reflection of different programming mindsets, and the field is richer for embracing that diversity.

posts.summary: DHH, a longtime remote work advocate who literally wrote a book on the topic, argues against the growing tendency of remote workers to declare offices morally inferior. He acknowledges that the modern world was built from offices and that in-person work is a perfectly legitimate choice. He praises Apple for sticking to their in-person culture and argues that the real victory for remote workers is having the freedom to choose, not eliminating offices entirely. The post warns against remote work advocates becoming the same kind of dogmatists they once opposed.
key_points: Remote work advocates risk becoming as dogmatic as the office-only advocates they once fought against
The modern world was designed and executed from offices, making in-person work a legitimate choice
The real victory is freedom to choose between remote and in-person companies, not eliminating one option
Companies like Apple deserve respect for making deliberate choices about their work culture, even at the cost of losing some talent
Social and technical movements often fail to recognize when they've won and keep fighting unnecessarily
money_quotes: You're never getting me back into an office. I credit much of my career to escaping that place in the early 2000s.
Let's be real here. The modern world we live in was designed and executed from an office. It's a perfectly legitimate way of working.
Don't spoil that victory by becoming an entitled, totalitarian whiner who won't let some companies make different choices than the one you now happen to like the very best.
The remote workers of the world have already won.
Isn't this what we wanted? The freedom to choose how we'd like to work by picking between a plentitude of companies offering the style of our preference?
It's not enough to carve out a clear passing in the forest of options for you and your compatriots to frolic in peace. No, the battle must rage on long after it's won.
For the love of all that is holy, learn to take the win!
posts.key_insight: Remote work advocates should declare victory and embrace workplace pluralism rather than insisting offices are universally wrong, which mirrors the same dogmatism they once fought against.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the massive tech layoffs, while painful for individuals, represent a necessary correction to a misallocation of talent. During the pandemic, big tech companies hoarded workers and put them on low-value projects, starving the rest of the economy of capable engineers. He contends that much of the work at these companies amounted to 'bullshit jobs' optimizing ads or building VR dead ends. The layoffs present an opportunity for talent to spread across startups and less glamorous but more productive businesses.
key_points: Big tech's pandemic hiring spree hoarded talent and deployed it on low-value projects, representing a tragic misallocation of productive capacity
Adding tens of thousands to already bloated bureaucracies made these companies slower and less capable, not better
The slow existential pain of wasting talent on a bullshit job may be worse than the immediate pain of getting fired
Talent hoarding by monopolistic companies priced out startups and less glamorous businesses that sorely needed tech workers
The layoffs create an opportunity for 300,000+ workers to spread across a broader, more productive set of companies
money_quotes: What's bad for the individual isn't always bad for the group.
This spree swallowed up a preposterous amount of talent, then put much of it to work on bullshit projects with little to no value for the company or the economy as a whole.
Such hoarding of tech talent represented a tragic misallocation of productive capacity.
There's only so much brain power that can productively be thrown at optimizing ad clicks or poured into VR black holes.

As much as getting fired sucks, and that's a lot, it's at least an immediate, explicit pain. Unlike the slow, creeping pain of having wasted your talent on a bullshit job that the world would have been oblivious to be without.
When these massive tech companies hoard all the talent, it goes without saying that there's less of it to go around.
All sorts of less glamorous, but sorely needed businesses were left stranded, and in return made all of us worse off.
May the three hundred thousand plus brains that have been released so far bloom a thousand new fields!
posts.key_insight: Big tech's pandemic hiring spree was a tragic misallocation of talent that starved the broader economy, and the layoffs—however painful—are an opportunity for that talent to find more productive and fulfilling work elsewhere.

posts.summary: DHH announces 37signals is dropping Datadog after their bill would have been $88,000/year, using it as a springboard to critique enterprise SaaS pricing broadly. He highlights absurd examples including a crypto company paying $65 million/year for Datadog and calculates how per-seat pricing for tools like Slack, Asana, and Dropbox can add up to $1.5 million yearly for a 2,000-person company. He attributes this to a zero-interest-rate environment and predicts a correction is coming. He contrasts this with Basecamp's flat $299/month pricing as a matter of principle.
key_points: Enterprise SaaS pricing has become detached from the value delivered, with monitoring tools alone costing tens of thousands to millions per year
Per-seat pricing models compound rapidly at scale — a few common tools at $65/month per seat costs $1.5M/year for 2,000 employees
Inflated SaaS spending is a symptom of cheap capital (zero-interest-rate phenomenon) and will face a market correction
Open-source alternatives could adequately replace most expensive enterprise SaaS with some in-house development
Basecamp's flat-rate pricing model is positioned as the ethical counter to exploitative per-seat enterprise pricing
money_quotes: Enterprise SaaS pricing is getting silly.
At the risk of invoking a tired cliche, this kind of outlay for a performance and monitoring service sounds like a 0% interest rate phenomenon.
There's just no universe I can imagine where that's a reasonable commitment. One that couldn't be more than adequately replaced by open-source alternatives.
Just for a chat tool!
It feels like we're in a service bubble waiting to pop. Someone is going to look at those margins and see an opportunity.
I can't in good conscience sell software at prices where I'd never pay.
We're due a correction here.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise SaaS pricing has ballooned to absurd levels propped up by cheap capital, and a market correction toward simpler, fairly-priced alternatives is inevitable.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the concept of 'underdogs' as the perfect descriptor for 37signals' core audience and identity. Inspired by Jason Fried's post, he argues that 'small business' never quite captured their allegiance — it's the underdog mentality that matters. He connects this to Nassim Taleb's concept of skin in the game, noting that underdogs have something on the line that makes them fundamentally different. Despite 37signals no longer being technically small, DHH insists they remain underdogs at heart, and that this shared DNA creates a deep bond with their audience.
key_points: The word 'underdog' captures 37signals' identity better than 'small business' ever did
Underdogs have skin in the game, which solves the principal-agent problem by eliminating the agent
37signals has grown beyond 'small' but maintains its underdog ethos and attitude
The underdog DNA — drive, grit, scrappiness, independence — creates a bond between 37signals and its audience
Giving voice to underdog sentiments and alleviating their fears is what keeps DHH motivated after twenty years
money_quotes: It's funny how finding just the right word unlocks the perfect mental image.
What I love most about underdogs is how they can't help having something on the line.
The easiest way to solve the agent-principle problem is to skip the agent, at least at the top.
We're just under eighty people. We've got over a hundred thousand customers. We have cloud bills in the millions, and revenue many times that. But we sure as hell are underdogs.
There's a deep bond in that attitude.
Being an underdog is an ethos: Drive. Grit. Scrappiness. Independence.
Helping to allay those sentiments, and encouraging someone to keep on, is what continues to give me the spark to stick with it twenty years in.
Underdogs stick together.
posts.key_insight: The underdog identity isn't about company size — it's an ethos of skin-in-the-game drive and scrappiness that creates a deep bond between like-minded builders.

posts.summary: DHH argues that while big tech companies offer attractive benefits, pay, and job security, most employees end up as interchangeable parts who don't meaningfully impact the organization. In contrast, small companies demand full engagement from every team member, creating both pressure and opportunity for growth. He frames this as a tradeoff between comfort and meaning, suggesting that being essential to a small team is more rewarding than being redundant on a large one.
key_points: Big tech offers compelling material benefits but makes most individual contributors redundant by design
Small companies require every person to contribute meaningfully — there's no hiding on the bench
The pressure of being essential at a small company accelerates talent development
Meaning comes from others depending on your contributions, not from compensation alone
Recent tech layoffs have weakened the job security argument for big companies
money_quotes: Odds are you'll be sitting on the bench if you do.
If you weren't there, there'd probably just be someone else to fill the slot, and it'd rarely make a big difference either way.
That's part of the resilience of a big ship, it's built to withstand whatever the weather, so much of the crew is redundant by design.
If you're not rowing, everyone will notice. You can't just rest and vest. You gotta play.
It's the fact that others, and the boat as a whole, count on you being there that rewards you with meaning.
Would you rather be on the bench in the big leagues or playing every game with an underdog team moving up?
posts.key_insight: The security and prestige of big tech come at the cost of personal impact and meaning — small companies offer the opposite tradeoff, where being indispensable drives both growth and fulfillment.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the three-year anniversary of the pandemic, arguing that most people—including himself—uncritically accepted false claims about COVID-19's origins, masks, and vaccines, and then aggressively attacked dissenters. He frames the pandemic response as a failure of scientific thinking, where the adversarial process of falsification was abandoned in favor of enforcing unproven consensus. He calls the prolonged lockdowns, vaccine mandates, and childhood masking mistakes that will take years to unwind. The post urges readers to cultivate intellectual humility by regularly asking 'what if I'm wrong?'
key_points: Multiple official pandemic claims—lab leak denial, mask efficacy, vaccine preventing infection and transmission—turned out to be false or overstated
The greater failure wasn't being wrong, but suppressing dissent and abandoning the adversarial process essential to science
Most people became 'foot soldiers' enforcing false consensus rather than questioning it, including DHH himself
Intellectual humility and willingness to recognize mistakes are prerequisites for learning from the pandemic
The pandemic was a 'grand intellectual test' that most people failed, and preparation for future crises requires practicing self-doubt
money_quotes: It's a humbling experience to be proven wrong. No wonder most of us would rather cover it up with excuses.
You can't learn from your mistakes unless you're willing to recognize them as such.
The essence of science is falsification. If your hypothesis cannot be proven wrong, it's a faith, not a predictor of fact.
We sacrificed the process of science to early, unproven, and ultimately false guesses at what was really what.
The pandemic doubled as a grand intellectual test, and most of us failed it.
Not only did most people buy the false claims, they became little foot soldiers in the crusade for them. Including me, for a while.
Falsification requires an adversarial process. We must subject our arguments to counterarguments, our past findings to new findings.
posts.key_insight: The pandemic's greatest failure wasn't getting things wrong—it was abandoning the adversarial process of science and persecuting those who dared to question the consensus.

posts.summary: DHH argues that 37signals has thrived for twenty years by deliberately avoiding metric-driven management. While acknowledging that KPIs like customer lifetime value and churn can be useful, he contends they ultimately serve a simple question: are we making money, and is it enough? He criticizes data-obsessed cultures like Google's famous 127-shades-of-blue test as the logical extreme of metrics worship. Instead, he advocates for intuition-driven decision making—fingerspitzengefühl—and warns that steering by derivative metrics leads to short-term thinking and questionable decisions.
key_points: Sophisticated KPIs all reduce to the same simple question: are we making money, and is it enough?
Product decisions should be guided by intuition (fingerspitzengefühl) rather than A/B testing every detail
The saying 'you can't manage what you don't measure' is management's greatest myth
Steering by derivative metrics creates pressure for short-term thinking and questionable decisions
Not needing metrics is a luxury afforded by profitability and having no outside investors
money_quotes: That's not to say that we never run these numbers, but it is to say that these numbers never run us.
The greatest myth in management is that you can't manage what you don't measure.
It's hot or cold outside regardless of how many digits of precision your thermometer is showing.
All these metrics are downstream from simply making something people want to buy.
Half the charm of making something to me is in letting your fingers drive the direction. Without articulation.
Rare is the dull business you can turn into a sparkle by obsessing about the derivatives. Common is the dread that's born from trying to do so.
If you steer your company according to a constellation of derivative metrics, it's really easy to end up in a swamp.
posts.key_insight: Metrics are downstream from making something people want to buy, and obsessing over derivative KPIs breeds short-term thinking while adding little to businesses already guided by good judgment.

posts.summary: DHH argues that companies can operate with very few full-time managers by replacing traditional managerial supervision with well-designed asynchronous processes. He outlines five specific practices at 37signals: automated daily/weekly check-ins replacing stand-ups, 6-week Shape Up cycles limiting planning overhead, Hill Charts for project visibility, time-boxed cycles that enforce scope discipline, and peer mentoring for quality review. These processes allow 37signals to run with just one full-time engineering manager across all technical teams, while other managers (including DHH and Jason Fried) split their time between management and individual contribution. The goal is enabling individual contributors to operate as autonomous 'managers of one.'
key_points: Daily stand-ups and weekly 1-1s can be replaced with automated asynchronous check-in questions that keep everyone informed
Time-boxed 6-week cycles with fixed scope budgets eliminate the need for managers to constantly make trade-off decisions — teams self-manage within constraints
37signals operates with a single full-time engineering manager across all programming, operations, and design teams
Management functions like career guidance and feedback can be separated from routine supervision work
Peer mentoring distributes quality review across the team, with senior staff held accountable for their mentee's output
money_quotes: If you want to run a company that's light on full-time managers, you have to focus on managing processes before people.

The traditional paradigm of a reporting manager that's constantly following up with their reports, conducting daily stand-up meetings, weekly 1-1s, and all other forms of intensive supervision, needs to be (mostly) replaced with an asynchronous, self-managing paradigm instead.

Everyone working on a feature knows that they have to find a way to hammer the scope enough to ship within that maximum budget. This deputizes them to make the necessary trade-offs without the need of a manager.
