These processes help us dramatically reduce the amount of managerial supervision needed at 37signals. They're the supporting pillars that make the Manager of One ideal actually hold up.
Jason and I are both moonlighting managers as well. Spending the majority of our time pushing the products forward with our direct work on design, programming, copywriting, or whatever.
These functions of management can be divorced from much of the other routine supervision work that fills the weeks of a manager under the traditional paradigm.
Get the processes right and the rest will follow.
posts.key_insight: Well-designed asynchronous processes and time-boxed work cycles can replace most traditional managerial supervision, allowing individual contributors to operate autonomously while the company runs with minimal full-time managers.

posts.summary: DHH argues that in-person meetups are essential for making remote work successful, drawing on 37signals' fifteen-year practice of twice-yearly company-wide gatherings. He introduces the concept of 'trust batteries' that get recharged through face-to-face time and carry teams through months of remote collaboration. He notes that the pandemic's forced absence of meetups highlighted their importance, and that many people who tried remote work for the first time during COVID were unfairly deprived of this crucial element. The post promotes a video from 37signals staff explaining their meetup logistics process.
key_points: In-person connection builds trust in ways that video calls cannot replicate, but these moments don't need to happen daily or weekly to be effective
37signals has run twice-yearly company-wide meetups for over fifteen years across nearly 80 people in 20 countries
The pandemic revealed that many first-time remote workers never experienced the meetup component, which skewed their perception of remote work negatively
Meetups may seem expensive but neglecting human connection costs more in the long run
money_quotes: Nothing can substitute for spending time together in person as a way to build bonds, create connections, and foster trust with your colleagues.
There's just a special kind of magic that comes from being together, which Zoom will never match or catch.
It's remarkable how nourishing it can be simply to see your coworkers every six months, and then rely on those fully-charged trust batteries to carry you through the interim.
Many people got their first experience with remote work during the pandemic, and were deprived of these recharging opportunities, so it's no wonder a fair number of them didn't like it.
They might seem expensive at first glance, but you're likely to pay far more for neglecting the value of direct, human connection without them.
posts.key_insight: Remote work succeeds not by eliminating in-person time but by concentrating it into periodic, high-impact meetups that recharge the trust batteries needed for months of effective distributed collaboration.

posts.summary: DHH argues that 360 performance reviews are a wasteful, inauthentic process that produces little value. After years of running them at 37signals, he found they devolved into forced congratulations with constant nagging required for compliance. The rare honest feedback caused more social dysfunction than improvement. He advocates replacing 360s with voluntary peer appreciation sessions at company meetups, where people authentically celebrate each other, while keeping corrective feedback in private manager conversations.
key_points: 360 reviews produced meaningful results only once across hundreds of reviews, while requiring weeks of nagging to complete
Honest peer feedback in 360s has no upside for the reviewer — minor grievances are best kept private, and serious issues shouldn't wait for a scheduled review
Mild critical feedback in 360s often caused disproportionate social drama and dysfunction
Voluntary peer appreciation at meetups produces genuinely moving, authentic moments that everyone loves
Processes requiring honest participation must be seen as intrinsically valuable by participants — forced compliance is a red flag
money_quotes: You couldn't design a better process for planting seeds of social dysfunction than to explicitly ask peers to share their minor annoyances with each other through the conduit of management.
Humans can smell inauthenticity a mile away. And if 360s are anything, it's inauthentic.
You can't force someone to be honest or authentic, if they don't see a personal upside.

Getting folks to fill out their reviews required constant nagging, resulting in weeks-long procrastination. That is not the hallmark of a happy process that serves the interests of the people involved.
Let peers celebrate each other in public, let managers deal with redirection directly and in private.
Even if someone acts in ways you think could be improved, are you really going to complain about it to management? Probably not, unless it's truly egregious, and if so, why wait for the 360?
posts.key_insight: Performance review processes that force peers to evaluate each other produce either inauthentic praise or unnecessary social friction — celebration should be voluntary and public, while corrective feedback should be direct and private.

posts.summary: DHH argues that new hires should be thrown into real, challenging work almost immediately rather than spending weeks on trivial onboarding tasks. He advocates pairing new employees with both a buddy (for cultural guidance) and a mentor (for quality standards) while giving them meaningful assignments within the first week. At 37signals, this approach frequently reveals strong performers quickly. He acknowledges not everyone will shine immediately, but argues that exposure to real challenges accelerates growth and also surfaces bad hires faster, which benefits both parties.
key_points: New-job enthusiasm is a finite resource that shouldn't be wasted on busywork and toy projects
Deep-end immersion must be paired with support structures: a buddy for culture and a mentor for quality standards
Early exposure to real work serves as both an accelerant for good hires and a filter for bad ones
Most people rise to the challenge when given meaningful work immediately, though some need more time
No hiring process is perfect, so the sooner you discover a mismatch the better for everyone
money_quotes: The kindest thing you can do to a new team member is to involve them in something real and challenging right away.
Don't squander weeks of new-job enthusiasm with baby rails and play tasks.
Get them into the deep end right from the start.
I'm perpetually pleased by how often someone shows themselves to be an excellent swimmer right from the get go when given the chance.
The more exposure to more challenges, the quicker most people will find themselves familiar with what's required of them.
Nobody, anywhere has a faultless hiring process that can bat a perfect ten out of ten.
The worst thing you can do to a new hire is leave them wandering the halls of the organization without something fulfilling, meaningful, and engrossing to do.
Everyone worth having on board wants to contribute as quickly as they can. Let them!
posts.key_insight: Throwing new hires into real, meaningful work immediately — with proper support structures — is both the kindest onboarding approach and the most effective way to reveal whether the hire was right.

posts.summary: DHH argues that promoting talented individual contributors into management roles often backfires, turning the promotion into a punishment. Many senior contributors excel at working alongside others but struggle with the emotional burden of managing people, particularly delivering corrective feedback or terminating employees. Effective management requires a specific emotional calibration—empathetic but not too empathetic—that's impossible to assess until someone faces a real crisis. DHH advocates for minimalist management as a solution, using processes and distributed mentorship to capture the benefits of senior leadership without forcing people into full managerial roles.
key_points: Talented individual contributors often don't want management responsibility, particularly the guilt associated with corrective feedback and terminations
Effective management requires a paradoxical emotional balance: enough empathy to connect but enough detachment to make hard decisions
Evidence supports that the best managers come from top individual contributors, yet the stress of managing can be paralyzing for exactly those people
Minimalist management—process-driven reporting, distributed mentorship—lets senior people contribute leadership without the full burden of managing careers
Organizations can function with fewer managers by making progress visible through processes rather than hierarchical oversight
money_quotes: The world is full of talented, capable people who'd rather put their own efforts to direct use than manage others.
The natural inclination is to promote senior contributors up a managerial ladder, and out of the trenches. This often ends poorly, turning the promotion into a punishment.
To be an effective manager, you have to be empathetic, but not too empathetic.
If you can't separate yourself emotionally from the process, at least in part, you can't make the difficult-but-necessary decisions required to service the broader team.
It's really hard to know whether you have this in you until you're faced with the reality of a tough situation.
You can condemn fewer of the most talented, capable, senior people to positions where they must reluctantly hold the careers of others in their hands.
posts.key_insight: Organizations should pursue minimalist management structures that let senior contributors provide leadership through process and mentorship without forcing them into the emotionally taxing role of managing people's careers.

posts.summary: DHH argues that digital communication apps connecting parents and schools have made the relationship worse, not better. Using Denmark's centralized school app Aula as his primary example, he describes how these tools create an endless stream of needless updates and parent threads that nobody actually wants. Despite his career building collaboration tools, he contends that some relationships benefit from friction and limited communication. He calls for eliminating these apps entirely, noting that both parents and teachers resent the constant connectivity they impose.
key_points: The limited school-home communication of the 80s and 90s (flyers, grade books, annual parent-teacher chats) worked well and didn't need digital replacement
School communication apps create obligation to monitor constant low-value updates just to catch the rare important message
Teachers suffer from the reduced friction too, getting inundated with direct messages from parents at a frequency and tone they'd never face in person
Not all connections benefit from removing friction — some barriers to interaction serve a valuable purpose
Both parents and teachers dislike these systems, yet everyone feels obligated to participate
money_quotes: Not all connections benefit from having the friction removed by digital systems. Some barriers to interaction are good, actually.
The fundamental premise that everything will be wonderful if we could just all be linked together everywhere, all the time is false.
I simply don't need the play-by-play from a teacher about what happened in class this week, every week. We talk to our kids at home.
I say that as someone who've spent a long career in the service of collaboration and communication tools. This is not the luddite perspective.
You're essentially obliged to partake in these non-stop chain mails, lest you miss a truly important or urgent message.

Parents and politicians are all, with some justification, so concerned about the dangers of screens these days. So let's start in our own interactions by burning the most useless use cases right away.
posts.key_insight: Some human relationships function better with communication friction intact, and the reflexive digitization of every interaction often creates obligation and noise rather than genuine value.

posts.summary: DHH argues that hybrid work arrangements represent the worst compromise between office and remote work. He contends that the push for hybrid is really a stealth reversal plot by managers who never liked remote work, starting with three days in-office before eventually eliminating remote days entirely. Hybrid destroys remote work's key benefits—geographic freedom, uninterrupted focus time, and wider hiring pools—while also undermining office work's advantages, since employees commute only to end up on video calls with absent colleagues. He advocates for a clear commitment to either fully remote or fully in-office work rather than a half-hearted middle ground.
key_points: Hybrid is a Machiavellian strategy by managers to gradually eliminate remote work while appearing to compromise
Hybrid destroys remote work benefits: employees must still live near the office, endure commutes, and lose uninterrupted focus time
Hybrid also undermines office benefits: employees commute in only to video-call colleagues who aren't there that day
The split between async and real-time communication creates a half-assed version of both information-sharing models
Companies should commit fully to either office or remote rather than choosing a cop-out middle ground
money_quotes: The honeymoon for remote work is over, and managers who never liked the concept to begin with are plotting its complete reversal.
This reversal plot is known as hybrid work, and combines the worst of in-office and remote work worlds.
By starting like this, managers who have no interest in remote work at all can claim to have "compromised", dulling the urge to revolt.
It also makes the interruption factory the norm once again.
A long commute can be crushing on the best of days, but it's twice as bad if it's in service of the same zoom fatigue you could have enjoyed from home.
When some of the information you need is written down and discussed online on the out-of-office days, and the rest lives only in the minds of people from oral tradition.
I say, do or do not. Hybrid is a cop-out.
Don't fiddle this Machiavellian middle.
posts.key_insight: Hybrid work is not a genuine compromise but a strategic trojan horse that strips away the core advantages of both remote and office work, and companies should commit fully to one model or the other.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his tenth participation in the 24 Hours of Le Mans during its centenary year. The race was marked by extreme drama — crashes, monsoon rain, mechanical failures, and a gravel trap incident that cost his team any chance at victory. Despite the disappointing result, DHH found satisfaction in his personal lap times, finishing as the fifth-fastest silver driver. He reflects on the cruel randomness of Le Mans, where hundreds of perfect laps can be undone by one split-second mistake, and wrestles with whether to continue racing given his recent four-year streak of bad luck after four podiums in his first six attempts.
key_points: The 2023 Le Mans centenary was exceptionally dramatic, with 22 cars retiring from crashes or mechanical issues throughout the 24-hour race
DHH's team went from leading the race to falling a lap behind through a sequence of tire debris, rain crashes, and a gravel trap incident
Driving without the pressure of winning can be liberating — DHH set his best lap times in the final stints when the result was already lost
DHH's last four Le Mans attempts have all been foiled by mechanical issues, contrasting sharply with four podiums (including a 2014 win) in his first six
Le Mans punishes premature optimism — the race is long enough that early leads mean nothing and one split-second mistake erases hundreds of clean laps
money_quotes: That's the danger of Le Mans. The race is so long that it's easy to start dreaming about results well before it's prudent.
Never do that at Le Mans.
You can do literally hundreds of laps across the French countryside with perfect poise, and then you make one tiny mistake, in a split second, and it all goes away.

It's a strange sensation, but in some ways it can be liberating to drive at Le Mans when you're no longer in it to win it. All the pressure is gone. It's just you and the track and the commitment to go flat out, damn the risks.
You never know whether you're going to get another shot at running at Le Mans.
Maybe Le Mans is trying to tell me that I've already had more than my fair share of luck at Circuit de la Sarthe.
When the pace is still solid, it's hard to commit to calling it quits.
posts.key_insight: Le Mans is a metaphor for the cruelty of long games — hundreds of laps of perfect execution can be erased by one moment of error, and the hardest part is deciding when you've pushed your luck far enough.

posts.summary: DHH pushes back against the 'act your wage' movement, arguing that deliberately underperforming at work hurts the employee more than the employer. He acknowledges that companies often fail to earn their employees' best effort, but contends that withholding effort causes personal skills to atrophy over time. His core argument is that people should always bring their best not as a gift to their employer, but as an investment in their own capabilities and future opportunities.
key_points: Unused abilities atrophy — you can't bank talent or energy for later, only lose it
The 'act your wage' movement is an understandable response to employer mistreatment, but it's ultimately self-defeating
Peak performance should be treated as a personal discipline, not a reward for good employers
Discontent should fuel self-improvement and eventual departure, not complacency
money_quotes: Abilities unused will atrophy, so putting in anything less than your best means giving up on what you're capable of.
You can't save talent or energy for better days, only watch it go to waste.
You can't avenge your grievances by squandering your potential.
If you wait for the perfect company to reveal your peak potential, it may never come.
Whatever the situation, whatever the company, you should show up with your very best. Not for them, but for you.
Use the discontent to become better, then bolt to another camp when the opportunity presents itself.
Don't play yourself by living down to their expectations.
posts.key_insight: Deliberately underperforming to spite a bad employer is a form of self-sabotage — skills atrophy without use, and the only person who loses is you.

posts.summary: DHH argues that original thinking requires deliberately reducing exposure to the constant noise of industry trends and social media. He credits 37signals' innovative work in product development and organizational design to being physically and mentally distant from Silicon Valley. He contrasts the pre-social-media era, when it was easier to tune out hype, with today's environment where every feed pushes you into predefined camps. The core message is that less exposure to conventional wisdom can paradoxically raise the bar for what you can achieve.
key_points: Original thinking is impossible when your senses are perpetually flooded with everyone else's ideas and established scripts
37signals' geographic and cultural distance from Silicon Valley was a key factor in their novel approaches to product and organizational design
Social media has made it exponentially harder to escape industry groupthink compared to the pre-social-media era
Taking sides for or against a trend (like AI) is still letting that trend dictate your thinking — both positions are 'plusses and minuses in front of the same thinking'
Sometimes knowing less about what everyone else is doing raises the ceiling on what you can create
money_quotes: It's hard to think original thoughts if your senses are being perpetually flooded with everything from everyone all the time.
To escape back to first principles, you need to consciously turn down the volume on the world.
Being in Chicago in those early years of the post-dot-com-bust internet was like living in a monastery.
Every feed is there to homogenize your thinking into a preconfigured camp. For or against, hype or denounce. These are all just plusses and minuses in front of the same thinking.
There are times when the less you know raises the bar for what you can do.
Don't drown out our inner drumbeat by living all your days inside the permanent intellectual festival of your profession.
posts.key_insight: Deliberately distancing yourself from industry noise and conventional wisdom is not ignorance — it's a prerequisite for original thinking.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Google is fundamentally untrustworthy as a product and service company because its business model is built entirely around advertising. He uses the sudden killing of Google Domains—a $180M/year business serving 10 million domains—as the latest evidence. He catalogs Google's pattern of abandoning products like Nest Secure and neglecting others like Google Home, arguing that even enterprise offerings like Workspaces and Google Cloud Platform are perpetually at risk. His conclusion: anything made by Google implicitly carries the disclaimer that it will be abandoned when Google loses interest.
key_points: Google is institutionally incapable of sustaining products that don't directly generate ad revenue at massive scale
Google Domains was killed after nine years despite being a $180M/year business with 10 million customers, proving no product is safe
Google's customer service is uniquely awful because helping product users is never as profitable as helping advertisers
The Nest/Google Home portfolio demonstrates how Google can acquire successful products and make them worse through neglect
Even enterprise offerings like Workspaces and Google Cloud Platform are vulnerable to internal reorganization or executive whims
money_quotes: Google will eventually kill every single service you care about, if they can't find a way to directly monetize it with ads at a scale of billions.
They're institutionally incapable of being in the product or service business, because neither products nor services butter Google's bread. Advertisement does.

Google has always been uniquely awful when it comes to customer service, because helping someone with a problem on Workspaces or even the Google Cloud Platform was never going to be as profitable as helping an advertiser carpet bomb your attention span.
This company is one major reorganization or recently promoted executive away from murdering any of these.
Anything that reads "Made by Google" implicitly has the subscript "until we don't give a fuck any more" printed below.
posts.key_insight: Google's advertising monoculture makes it structurally incapable of sustaining any product or service that doesn't generate ad revenue at scale, so users should always have a backup plan.

posts.summary: DHH argues that software companies should commit to maintaining their products indefinitely, contrasting the tech industry's habit of shutting down services with luxury brands like Leica, Rolex, and Porsche that service products for decades. He points out that maintaining software is actually easier than keeping physical spare parts in warehouses. He uses 37signals as an example, noting they still run every major service they've ever launched, including the original 2004 version of Basecamp, Highrise, Campfire, and even free tools like Ta-da List. He frames this long-term commitment as both a source of pride and a powerful marketing signal that builds trust.
key_points: Popular software services routinely get shut down, and even Google can't be trusted to maintain products long-term
Luxury brands like Leica, Rolex, and Porsche maintain decades-old products — software companies should find this even easier since there are no physical spare parts to warehouse
SaaS has a 'nasty secret': even when services survive, they often evolve in directions users don't want, with no recourse
37signals still runs every major service it ever launched, including the original 2004 Basecamp, generating millions from thousands of happy legacy customers
Keeping old services running is a marketing signal that builds long-term trust and differentiates the company
money_quotes: Popular services and crucial products get shut down all the time.
If anything, it should be easier for companies like Google to honor their legacy than the likes of Leica, Rolex, and Porsche.
That's the nasty secret of SaaS. Even if the service doesn't just disappear entirely, like it often does, it might well 'evolve' in a direction you don't care for one bit.
Almost all companies will simply tell you to get with the program, find where they hid the cheese now, and just be grateful that updates are even rolling out.
The revenue we make continuing to offer these services would never interest the likes of a Google, but so what? It makes us proud and our customers happy, do we need any other justification?
Keeping even free services like Ta-da List around might not make strict financial sense in isolation, but it sends a signal to the market that we're here for the long term.
We're here until the end of the internet.
posts.key_insight: Committing to run software services indefinitely is both easier than maintaining physical products and a powerful trust-building differentiator in an industry where abandonment is the norm.

posts.summary: DHH shares cost data from 37signals' company meet-ups, revealing that European locations cost roughly half as much as US ones per person. Amsterdam came in at $3,500 per person compared to $6,200 in Miami and $5,700 in New Orleans, with comparable or better quality. He argues that the savings from eliminating their Chicago office ($25,000/month) more than offset meet-up costs, and strongly recommends remote companies invest in regular in-person gatherings for the human connection they provide.
key_points: European meet-ups cost nearly half of US ones: Amsterdam was $3,500/person vs Miami's $6,200 and New Orleans' $5,700
Transportation costs in the US were a major factor, with Miami cabs and Ubers alone exceeding $10,000 vs a fraction of that for Amsterdam public transit
Eliminating their Chicago office lease ($25,000/month used by a handful of people) more than offsets the cost of biannual meet-ups
Despite 37signals being split 50/50 between US and international employees, staying in the US for meet-ups didn't save on travel costs
The human connection value of in-person meet-ups for remote teams far exceeds their cost
money_quotes: Would you have guessed that hosting a company meet-up in US was almost twice as expensive as Europe? I wouldn't!
I feel like Americans often complain about how expensive things are in Europe, but looking at our expenditures on meet-ups, that just isn't so.
In Miami, we spent well over $10,000 just on cabs and Ubers. Whereas in Amsterdam, the cost was a fraction for the public transit options.
We used to spend a good $25,000/month on the Chicago lease and associated bills. That was an office used by less than a handful of people on a regular basis!
These meet-ups leave such a lasting impression on everyone who participates. It's a super dose of human connection, combined with a fun trip, and some excellent eating.
If you're running a remote company, especially an internationally remote one, I strongly recommend finding a way to fit meet-ups into the budget.
The human dividends are huge.
posts.key_insight: Remote companies can get better value hosting team meet-ups in Europe than the US, and the savings from eliminating office leases make regular in-person gatherings an easy investment in human connection.

posts.summary: DHH argues that organizations are rarely destroyed by obviously terrible decisions but rather by the slow accumulation of mediocre ones. Because mediocrity isn't painfully obvious, leaders lack the urgency to act, and inertia compounds the cost of change over time. He notes that most people know what needs to be done but avoid difficult conversations and uncomfortable truths. The post concludes with a call to make hard choices proactively rather than letting mediocrity compound unchecked.
key_points: Mediocrity is more dangerous than outright failure because it lacks the urgency that forces action
Inertia compounds the cost of reversing bad decisions — a three-month problem is far easier to fix than a three-year one
People naturally rationalize delay, convincing themselves that more time will fix what needs to be cut
Mediocrity's real danger is in accumulation — no single mediocre element is fatal, but together they are
money_quotes: It's rarely the terrible decisions, processes, or even people that'll sink your organization. It's the accumulation and inertia of the mediocre ones.
Dealing with the truly bad is easy. It's painfully obvious to all that change is required.
The insidious nature of inertia is in its ability to compound the cost of action as time goes on.
Letting go of an employee that just didn't meet the bar after three months is endlessly easier than doing the same after three years.
Every additional month you let mediocrity fester, you make it harder to undo.
The sad truth is that most of us are cowards whenever we can be. We usually know what needs to be done, but we shrink from the responsibility to do it.
The inertia of mediocrity will not break unless you break it.
posts.key_insight: The greatest organizational threat isn't spectacular failure but the compounding inertia of tolerated mediocrity, because each day of inaction makes the eventual correction harder.

posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals has completed its exit from the cloud after six months of work, migrating all seven applications—including HEY email and six heritage services—back to their own hardware. The move is projected to save at least $1.5 million per year, with the total hardware investment costing less than a third of one year's savings. They accomplished this using an entirely open-source stack (KVM, Docker, Kamal) while avoiding Kubernetes complexity, and without increasing their operations team size. DHH argues that cloud benefits have been vastly overstated for established companies and urges others to do their own math.
key_points: All seven 37signals applications were migrated off AWS in six months, with the containerization done for cloud actually making the exit easier
The company expects to save $1.5M+ annually, having spent only ~$500K on hardware that will be amortized over five years
The operations team size didn't change—cloud never delivered the promised productivity gains that would allow a smaller team
The open-source stack of KVM, Docker, and Kamal allowed them to avoid both Kubernetes complexity and enterprise vendor lock-in
Cloud still makes sense for early-stage companies and those with highly variable workloads, but established companies are overpaying
money_quotes: The back of the napkin math is that we'll save at least $1.5 million per year by owning our own hardware rather than renting it from Amazon.
Running our applications in the cloud just never provided the promised productivity gains to do with any smaller of a team anyway.
And it was less than a third the cost of what we predict we'll be saving EVERY YEAR!
Just be careful that you don't look at those lavish cloud credits as a gift! It's a hook.
The benefits have been vastly overstated. The cloud is often just as complicated as running things yourself, and it's usually ridiculously more expensive.
If you only need a plough thrice a year, it doesn't make much sense keeping it in the barn unused for the remaining 363 days.
So don't just stay in the cloud because of the hype.
posts.key_insight: For established companies with predictable workloads, owning hardware with open-source tooling is dramatically cheaper than cloud rental, and the cloud's complexity and productivity benefits have been vastly overstated.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Rails World in Amsterdam selling out in under 45 minutes, calling it validation for the newly formed Rails Foundation and its corporate backers. He frames this as a triumphant moment for the Rails ecosystem, noting there hasn't been a major European Rails conference since 2008. The sellout validates the Foundation's decision to prioritize a conference series as its first major project. DHH positions this as a fresh start that leaves past community conflicts behind, with plans to expand the conference series in 2024.
key_points: Rails World in Amsterdam sold out in under 45 minutes, demonstrating strong pent-up demand for a major European Rails conference
The Rails Foundation, backed by companies like Shopify, GitHub, and 37signals, is validated by the conference's success as its first major undertaking
DHH frames the event as moving past old community squabbles and the end of RailsConf, positioning the Foundation as the new institutional home for Rails events
The conference coincides with Rails' 20th anniversary, and DHH uses the milestone to project momentum and longevity for the framework
money_quotes: There hasn't been a major, dedicated Rails conference in Europe since 2008, so perhaps it's no surprise that there was pent-up demand.
What an awesome validation that companies like Cookpad, Doximity, Fleetio, GitHub, Intercom, Procore, Shopify, and 37signals were right to back a new institution.
To have all of this come together, with Rails World, with the Rails Foundation, in the year we'll celebrate the 20th anniversary of the framework, is just icing on the birthday cake.
Especially because this feels like just the beginning.
Personally, it's gratifying to see that we've been able to leave old squabbles behind, route around the nonsense, and get back to the joyful collaboration on this fantastic framework.
I seriously feel #blessed to continue working with such an incredible ecosystem of developers after having spent literally half my life in its service. Bring on the next twenty years!
posts.key_insight: The rapid sellout of Rails World validates the Rails Foundation's corporate-backed institutional model as the right path forward for the framework's community and events.

posts.summary: DHH announces his family's return to America after three years in Denmark, originally prompted by pandemic school lockdowns. He acknowledges Denmark's superiority on many rational metrics, especially for family life and child safety in Copenhagen. However, invoking Dostoevsky, he argues that reason represents only 'one twentieth' of life's capacity, and that deeper, unarticulated feelings matter. He characterizes Denmark's success as rooted in cultural homogeneity and conformity, which creates a 'small box' for acceptable thinking and being that ultimately prevented it from feeling like home.
key_points: On a rational pro/con list, Denmark arguably beats America for family life, especially Copenhagen's child-friendly design
Life decisions shouldn't be purely rational — Dostoevsky's insight that reason is 'one twentieth of our capacity for life' applies to where we choose to live
Denmark's success stems from being a small, homogenous society with strong cultural conformity — a compelling capitalist-collectivist hybrid
That same conformity creates implicit barriers for outsiders and a narrow box for acceptable thinking and being
The expat bubble initially masked the cultural fault lines, which only emerged when they attempted genuine long-term integration
money_quotes: If you were to make a pro/con list contrasting family life in Denmark vs America, I think even fairly conservative parents from the US would find it difficult to compose a slam-dunk case for God's own country.
But life isn't just a set of rational arguments for or against.
Denmark works so well as it does because it's a small, homogenous society with strong norms that force a stringent cultural conformity.
It also breeds a small box for acceptable manners, thinking, and being.
If we realize that our benefits are reaped by our collective sameness, then any material otherness is a threat.
It wasn't until we gave the promise of long-term integration a proper go that the fault lines emerged.
In important ways, this country had a hard time becoming home, deep in our hearts, however persuasive the argument was to our heads.
Now I'm looking forward to being back in the whirlwind of America. Tickling many of the other nineteen tenths of our capacity for life.
posts.key_insight: A society can be rationally superior on every metric yet still feel constraining — belonging requires more than reason, and cultural homogeneity's benefits come at the cost of a narrow space for otherness.

posts.summary: DHH argues that wisdom is not about accumulating knowledge but about repeatedly practicing and internalizing lessons until they become instinctive. He reflects on how he once dismissed rereading books as wasteful, only to realize that repetition is essential for wisdom to stick. Drawing on Stoic philosophy, particularly Epictetus, he compares wisdom to physical exercise — knowing how to do a push-up is worthless without actually doing the reps. He extends this to his own habit of repeating the same ideas in writing and interviews, framing repetition not as redundancy but as necessary practice.
key_points: Wisdom is useless unless it can be recalled and applied when needed — knowledge alone has no value
Repetition is the mechanism by which wisdom becomes internalized, analogous to physical exercise
Stoic philosophy provides a practical 'exercise program' for wisdom, especially the ideas of imagined suffering and focusing only on what you can control
Repeating yourself in writing or speaking is not redundancy but a form of cognitive training for both speaker and audience
Reading foundational texts repeatedly (like Epictetus's Manual) keeps hard-won insights accessible in moments of need
money_quotes: The hallmark of great wisdom is not what you know, but what you know and can put to use.
The globe is full of learned idiots, unable or incapable of following the wisdom they have accumulated.
There's no prize for a closet full of axioms or insights, if you leave it all in there, and venture philosophically naked into the world.
You don't get in shape by knowing how to do a push-up but by doing a hundred a week. Accept that wisdom is a form of mental exercise.
As soon as you actually need this pithy little reminder, it's usually nowhere to be found. To have it emerge top of mind in a time of need requires immense training.
We're just doing the reps!
You are what you think. Become better by thinking better. Think better by repeating the few hard lessons you know you need the most.
posts.key_insight: Wisdom is not an intellectual achievement but a physical discipline — it requires constant repetition to be available when you actually need it.

posts.summary: DHH argues that people who achieve great financial success typically continue working not out of greed but because they genuinely enjoy the journey and find purpose in their work. He pushes back against the common reaction of 'I would never keep working if I were rich,' calling it a failure of imagination. Drawing on his own experience of having enough money to stop but choosing not to, he defends figures like Musk and Zuckerberg for staying in the arena. He concludes that the drive behind success isn't extinguished by material achievement, and that critics who reduce continued ambition to greed are making shallow projections.
key_points: The 'I would never keep working' reaction reveals a contradiction: people fantasize about financial freedom while failing to understand that the drive creating wealth is inseparable from the desire to keep working
Most successful people genuinely like what they do — the journey itself provides purpose beyond material rewards
DHH uses his own experience as evidence: despite financial comfort, his daily work routine is virtually identical to before
The biggest winners in capitalism often aren't defining their ambitions in terms of money, while their critics paradoxically are
Dismissing continued ambition as 'pure greed' or 'pure evil' is a shallow projection that fails to account for intrinsic motivation
money_quotes: It represents a death of imagination to be unable to visualize how someone who've secured the material success needed for life of quiet leisure could intentionally choose a path of continued work.
I've never met anyone that made it big who merely tolerated having a job, and who secretly wished they never had to work again.
There's something provocatively counterintuitive about the fact that the biggest winners in the game of capitalism aren't actually defining their ambitions in terms of money.
I just respect the hell out of people who stay in the arena. Who when given the choice by their good fortune refuses to settle for a lesser burden.
Sometimes what appears to the peanut gallery as pure greed or pure evil is but a shallow projection, which fails to account for the fact that some people simply live to stay in the arena.
The drive that got them to where they are isn't extinguished by achieving personal, material wellbeing.
I can also appreciate what a potentially infuriating mirror it presents to the meager ambitions of others.
posts.key_insight: The drive that creates extraordinary success is inseparable from the desire to keep working — dismissing continued ambition as greed says more about the critic than the ambitious.

posts.summary: DHH argues that web designers should work directly in HTML and CSS rather than relying on Figma as an intermediary abstraction layer. At 37signals, designers work with the native materials of the web, which powers their productive two-person team model. He pushes back against the common workflow where designers create mockups in tools like Figma and hand them off to programmers to implement. He contends that creativity thrives on constraints and that knowing the grain of web materials produces better results. While acknowledging Figma's value for early concepts and native mobile work, he advocates for designers becoming proficient in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript as core career skills.
key_points: Figma, like Photoshop before it, is an intermediary abstraction that slows collaboration between programmers and designers working on the web
37signals' two-person team productivity depends on designers working directly in HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and Ruby against real code and real data
The common handoff workflow where designers create mockups for programmers to implement is a holdover from the late 90s Photoshop era
Creativity thrives on constraints — knowing how to cut with the grain of web materials produces better outcomes than unconstrained mockup tools
Designers who can make their creations real are not unicorns but a deliberate hiring and development strategy that pays productivity dividends
money_quotes: No matter how good Figma is, it's an intermediary abstraction, like Photoshop before it.
If you're working with the web, you'll work faster without such an abstraction layer in the design process filtering the collaboration between programmer and designer.
Creativity thrives on constraints. Knowing how to cut with the grain makes getting the most out of the material much easier.
The design process and its iterations flow through updates to the real code that runs the real app, and, as quickly as possible, against real data.
You can be more creative if you're not constrained by what it takes to make it happen. I think that's exactly the wrong conclusion.
Great designers that can also make their creations real? Good luck finding THAT! And yet we have, repeatedly, at 37signals.
Embrace doing the bulk of the design for the web directly in the core elements of its periodic table.
posts.key_insight: Web designers should work directly in HTML and CSS rather than through abstraction tools like Figma, because creativity thrives on constraints and cutting with the grain of native web materials produces faster, better results.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates the Supreme Court's ruling striking down affirmative action in college admissions, framing it as part of a broader decline of DEI ideology that he sees as overdue. He argues that the 2020-2021 period represented peak DEI influence, particularly in tech, and that the pendulum is now swinging back. He contends that using racial preferences to correct past injustices amounts to present-day discrimination, particularly against Asian Americans. He predicts the ruling will accelerate DEI's decline in corporate settings and sees it as a vindication of meritocratic ideals.
key_points: The Supreme Court's affirmative action ruling generated a surprisingly contained reaction in tech, which DHH attributes to DEI's already-waning influence in the industry
The 2020-2021 peak of DEI ideology is compared to the 1950s Red Scare in its intensity and eventual reassessment
Corporate legal departments are already course-correcting in anticipation of lawsuits extending the ruling's logic beyond college admissions
Asian Americans bore disproportionate penalties under affirmative action, making the system's discriminatory nature most visible
While DEI is receding in tech and corporate worlds, academia, arts, and science remain in its grip
money_quotes: Correcting past injustices with present-day discrimination was never going to be a long-term stable project.

When you attempt to rig the rules to achieve such 'demographically proportionate' outcomes, you invariably end up penalizing better candidates on the basis of having the 'wrong', 'over-represented' racial background. That's not right.
It really was a crazy time. And I think we're only beginning to appreciate just how bananas it was.
With the legal basis drained, the ideological nature of this push will become all the more apparent and niche at the same time.
The original American promise that this is a country where you can make it regardless of where you came from has always been challenged in practice, but it's a beautiful ideal.
Using race, and other protected identity markers, to advantage some candidates over others is illegal.
posts.key_insight: The Supreme Court's affirmative action ruling marks a legal turning point that will accelerate DEI's decline from corporate and institutional life, vindicating merit-based evaluation over racial preferences.

posts.summary: DHH argues that while the S&P 500 has recovered, the gains are concentrated in profitable mega-cap tech companies like Apple and Microsoft, leaving unprofitable SaaS companies still deeply underwater. He contends that the era of cheap capital lifting all tech boats is over, and actual business fundamentals now determine valuations. Companies like Asana, Monday, and Smartsheet are burning through hundreds of millions annually, while private companies like ClickUp, Airtable, and Notion face brutal fundraising conditions after raising at peak valuations. DHH warns that while large-scale money-losing companies can survive for years on accumulated capital, the financial bleeding will eventually prove fatal.
key_points: The 2023 market recovery is concentrated in a handful of profitable mega-cap tech stocks, not a broad tech rebound
Unprofitable SaaS companies that raised at peak 2021 valuations face devastating down-rounds as they exhaust their runway
Public SaaS companies like Asana ($400M annual loss) and Smartsheet ($230M annual loss) have unsustainable burn rates now that capital is no longer free
Large-scale money-losing companies can survive for years on accumulated capital — Groupon is still alive 12 years after IPO despite collapsing from $25B to $200M
The shift back to fundamentals-based valuation means more pain is coming for unprofitable enterprise SaaS
money_quotes: We're now back in world where the actual business metrics and mechanics of individual operations determine the value assigned to their stocks and startups.
When fresh capital is inevitable needed, it'll be murder on the cap tables.
It's hard to know whether or how that'll all come to an end, but those aren't pretty numbers now that capital is no longer free.
At a large enough scale, there's substantial lag between the infliction of a mortal financial wound and the final burial of the bleeding company.
You'd be silly, as an investor, employee, or customer, not to pay attention to the red drain once it's begun.
It's a truism that if you bleed long enough, you will eventually die, though, and boy has there been a lot of bleeding already!
posts.key_insight: The return to fundamentals-based valuation means unprofitable SaaS companies face an existential reckoning — the bubble era subsidized their losses, and no recovery is coming to bail them out.

posts.summary: DHH argues that founders must preserve their initial sense of urgency even as their companies grow and succeed. He warns that success attracts smart advisors who counsel caution and slower pace, but following that advice risks organizational stagnation. Using the bike helmet analogy, he contends that obsessing over specific short-term risks often creates worse long-term outcomes. The core message is that the risk of losing momentum far outweighs the risk of moving too fast and occasionally failing.
key_points: Success attracts advisors who rationalize slower pace as prudent, but their narrow correctness masks a broadly wrong direction
The risk of organizational rigor mortis from success exceeds the risk of moving fast and making mistakes
Obsessing over specific short-term risks often worsens fuzzy long-term outcomes, like mandatory bike helmets reducing cycling and overall health
Founders are uniquely positioned to inject necessary risk into a business that others will naturally try to de-risk
Every company needs someone pushing to launch next Monday instead of next month, willing to eat occasional losses
money_quotes: Reaching escape velocity doesn't guarantee you'll keep going, unless you retain the propulsion from an unreasonable sense of urgency.
The irony of being successful in business is that it'll invariably attract smart people who can tell you all the good reasons for why you can no longer go as quickly as you once did.
The risk of succumbing to the rigor mortis of success is usually far greater than the risk of making a mistake by not considering and mitigating all possible bad outcomes up front.
Being obsessive about specific, short-term risks often sits in opposition to worrying about fuzzy, long-term risks.
Nobody will be as motivated as a founder to inject risk into the business.
Every company needs to have someone asking why we can't launch next Monday instead of next month.
posts.key_insight: The greatest threat to a successful company isn't moving too fast and making mistakes — it's losing the founder's unreasonable urgency and succumbing to the cautious paralysis that success invites.

posts.summary: DHH argues that focus is eroded not by dramatic failures but by accumulations of small, seemingly harmless commitments he calls 'mental barnacles.' These include mediocre initiatives, process theatre, and even modest successes that no longer deserve attention. He warns that each individually seems too minor to address, but collectively they kill momentum. The solution is ruthless, regular pruning — cutting the bottom 10% of attention-consuming activities before the drag becomes noticeable.
key_points: Small, inconsequential commitments accumulate imperceptibly until they destroy focus and momentum
The hardest barnacles to remove are things that appear to be working — past successes and modest accomplishments that no longer merit attention
Each individual barnacle presents as more hassle to deal with than to dismiss, which is exactly how they accumulate
You should proactively prune before reaching carrying capacity, leaving room for serendipity
money_quotes: The easiest way to squander your focus is by paying attention to a million inconsequential things at once.
These little mental barnacles add up in imperceptible ways until you suddenly feel like you're getting nowhere, no matter how hard you push.
All individually presenting as more hassle to deal with than to dismiss, but combined killing the efficiency and focus on progress.
You can't keep stuffing your head with ever more things to pay partial attention to every month and expect to keep a clear eye on the horizon and the future.
The hardest part about this is not to wait until it's too much, until the drag is actually there.
Keep your mind below carrying capacity, such that there's room for serendipity, at all times.
Be decisive about what you'll no longer do.
posts.key_insight: Ruthlessly and regularly cut the bottom 10% of things consuming your attention — especially modest successes — before they accumulate into an imperceptible drag on your focus.

posts.summary: DHH argues that after twenty years of hiring, he's learned it's impossible to truly know someone's capabilities upfront. He advocates for giving employees harder challenges before you're fully convinced they're ready, treating most projects as acceptable risks for developing talent. He contends that waiting for proof of capability wastes months or years of someone's potential, and that most projects are lower stakes than people admit. The core message is that team improvement depends on converting promising members into proven contenders through opportunity, not gatekeeping.
key_points: It's impossible to know what someone is truly capable of until they attempt real work, no matter how good the hiring process
Waiting until you're convinced someone can handle a bigger challenge means you've waited far too long, spoiling their potential
Most projects can afford to fail — the stakes are usually far lower than people care to admit
Every opportunity given to a proven employee is an opportunity kept from someone who might have risen to the occasion
Team improvement rate is a function of how many promising members you convert into proven contenders
money_quotes: After hiring people for twenty years, I've come to accept that it's impossible to know up front what someone is truly capable of.
I can't tell you the number of times I've been positively surprised by what someone is capable of when they've only been given a chance to prove it.
The only way to play it safe is to wait until you're convinced they're capable of that bigger challenge, but by then you've waited far too long.
The truth is that most projects can afford to fail. Much of the work we all do most of the time is far lower stakes than we all care to admit.
Every play you give to someone you know can do it, is a play you've kept from someone who might have been able to.
The rate of improvement for your team as a whole is a function of how many promising members you can convert into proven contenders.
Take more chances on promise.
posts.key_insight: The fastest way to build a strong team is to bet on people's promise before you have proof, because most projects are low-stakes enough to absorb the risk of someone learning on the job.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the return-to-office mandates from large tech companies have restored remote work as a competitive hiring advantage for smaller companies and startups. He notes that after the pandemic, many workers now consider remote work a non-negotiable job requirement. While the current soft job market gives big companies leverage, he predicts this will shift as the market tightens. He frames remote work as an enabling technology comparable to open-source software that lowers barriers to new company formation.
key_points: Large tech companies forcing return-to-office has reopened a competitive hiring gap that benefits remote-first smaller companies
Many workers now treat remote work as a defining, non-negotiable characteristic of a job, especially those outside major tech hubs
The current soft tech job market temporarily masks the hiring disadvantage of requiring office attendance
Remote work functions as an enabling technology that lowers the cost of starting new companies, similar to open-source software
The bifurcation between office-first big companies and remote-first small companies is a net positive for entrepreneurship
money_quotes: If you're trying to compete for talent with the likes of Google or Apple, you need all the help you can get, and these companies forcing everyone back into an office might just be your biggest lift.
For quite a few people, working remotely or not has become the defining characteristic of a job.
The world is full of wonderful, talented people who won't do this.
Eventually the tech job market is going to tighten again.
Remote work is an enabling technology, like free open-source software, which lowers the cost for new-company formation.
Granting more entrepreneurs more opportunities to take on the stodgy establishment without the need for other people's money.
posts.key_insight: Return-to-office mandates by big tech companies are inadvertently creating a structural hiring advantage for remote-first startups, functioning as an enabling technology that democratizes access to talent.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the discourse around Elon Musk's management of Twitter/X has devolved into pure motivated reasoning, with both supporters and detractors interpreting every development through their predetermined conclusions. He notes that countless predictions of Twitter's imminent demise have failed to materialize, yet critics never pause for introspection. Rather than engaging in the endless proxy war, DHH suggests people either enjoy the spectacle with detached amusement or tune out entirely. He acknowledges Musk may still fail but argues his survival through repeated supposed catastrophes demonstrates resilience.
key_points: Predictions of Twitter's collapse under Musk have repeatedly failed, yet critics never recalibrate their views
The discourse around Musk's decisions is a proxy war for ideological battles, not good-faith assessment
Both Musk's detractors and boosters engage in motivated reasoning, interpreting all evidence to confirm their priors
Surviving repeated supposed catastrophes demonstrates resilience, which is a key ingredient in success
The only rational responses are Camus-like detached amusement or tuning out entirely
money_quotes: Make sure you never miss a chance to be an expert on social media, advertising, team building, economics, or branding so you can tell the world's most successful entrepreneur what a total idiot he's being, and how much smarter, wiser, and kinder you'd be in his shoes.
However passionately you wish for the man to fail, he's not going to do so on account of your feeble bleating.
When you've survived so many supposed catastrophes, and you're still playing, you've at the very least shown tremendous resilience. That's one of the absolute key ingredients in success.
It's all turned into an endless proxy war, and every argument is wielded only in service of yet another petty ideological skirmish.
Musk could double the revenue and users of X, and his perma detractors would still find a path back to a prophesy of imminent ruin.
This is 5D chess being played by two parties shuffling pieces around in isolated realms of reality, each cheering every move as but two removed from checkmate.
Either embrace the absurdity of it all with the casual demeanor of Camus, and chuckle at the ever-grander spectacle, or tune out of the whole endeavor altogether.
posts.key_insight: The debate over Musk's Twitter/X has become pure motivated reasoning on both sides, where no outcome could possibly change anyone's mind, making the entire discourse an exercise in competitive catastrophizing detached from reality.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the software development community has an unhealthy reluctance to acknowledge differences in talent and competence among developers. He draws parallels to sports and medicine, where skill hierarchies are openly accepted, and contends that the lack of formal barriers to entry in programming has led people to wrongly conclude that anyone can reach the highest levels with enough effort. He criticizes top developers for downplaying their own achievements out of false humility, arguing this helps nobody. The post concludes that easy entry to the field offers a chance at pursuing competence, not a guarantee of it.
key_points: Software development lacks formal gatekeeping exams, which is good, but leads to a false belief that anyone can reach the top with enough effort
The best developers tend to downplay their talent out of false humility, unlike top athletes or surgeons who embrace their mastery
Celebrating exceptional talent attracts new people to a field rather than discouraging them, as seen in soccer with Messi and Ronaldo
There is an insecurity in the software community about whether being better even matters, which DHH calls nonsense
Low barriers to entry afford a chance at pursuing competence, not its inevitability
money_quotes: Many software developers seem to have a uniquely hard time accepting that not everyone who just tries real hard will become so good as to be among the best in this field.
The mistake comes from inferring that because there's no barrier to entry, it's within the capacity of anyone who keeps at it to ascent to the highest levels of competence possible.

In our eagerness to appear as welcoming and encouraging to the next generation of developers, we've often accidentally or intentionally debase the achievements of merit, and in the process help nobody.
More kids start playing soccer because we cherish the unique talents of Messi or Ronaldo, not fewer.
You don't see Messi or Ronaldo attribute their wins to 'just getting lucky' as a matter of course.
It reveals an unappealing insecurity about whether software development is domain where being better is even important.
What the easy entry to software development affords is a chance at the pursuit of competence, not it's inevitability.
Little worth having is not hard to get.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's reluctance to acknowledge talent differences, combined with top developers' false humility, undermines the meritocratic promise that low barriers to entry are supposed to provide.

posts.summary: DHH highlights the irony of Zoom, a company built on remote collaboration tools, mandating its employees return to the office. He argues this is part of a broader opportunistic squeeze by big tech companies who are exploiting a weakened job market and mass layoffs to force return-to-office policies. He notes that workers currently lack leverage to resist since hiring has cooled outside of AI. Despite the trend, he predicts remote work is too compelling to disappear and that companies committed to it will benefit from an oversupply of eager applicants.
key_points: Zoom mandating return-to-office is deeply ironic given its entire business is selling remote collaboration
Companies are exploiting a weak job market and post-layoff fear to push RTO policies they couldn't enforce during the talent war
The return-to-office wave has reached critical mass, giving individual companies safety in numbers to follow suit
Remote-first companies like Shopify, Automattic, and 37signals will benefit from increasingly oversubscribed job openings
Remote work's long-term trend remains upward despite the current corporate backlash
money_quotes: The company that literally lives to sell us all on the wonders of remote collaboration wants its own people back into the office again.
Which I guess is just a regression to the mean of productivity tool makers failing to believe their own marketing, but it's a bit sad none the less.
This is an opportunistic squeeze, because what's most tech workers supposed to do?
The option of simply hopping to another company has been severely limited.
So this is happening now because it can happen now.
I just hope that people who really did enjoy that remote work-lifestyle don't forget what it was like.
Remote work might be receding, but it's simply too compelling an option for too many to ever disappear.
The long-term trend line ain't going anywhere.
posts.key_insight: Big tech's return-to-office push is an opportunistic power play enabled by a weak job market, not a genuine indictment of remote work's effectiveness.

posts.summary: DHH draws a parallel between physical exercise and creative work, arguing that both reliably lift the human spirit but become hardest to start precisely when you need them most. He describes the gap between knowing creation is beneficial and actually doing it. He warns against 'simulacra of creation' — environments that mimic productive work without producing anything meaningful. The post concludes with an encouragement to push through inertia and start making things, because momentum builds on itself.
key_points: Creation and exercise share the same paradox: they're hardest to start when you need them most
Performative busyness — work that mimics creation without producing results — is especially corrosive to motivation
Negative streaks of inaction are self-reinforcing, making each day harder to break free from
The energy for creative work comes from doing it, not from waiting until you feel ready
money_quotes: It's hard not to see your spirits lifted when you're part of bringing something to life.
The hard part is getting started when you need it the most!
That's the curious chasm between knowledge and action.
Those periods were especially depressing when they felt like a simulacra of creation.
The pretending of purpose. The appearance of collaboration. Without any thrust towards completion.
For every day you stay there, the harder it is to get up, so best to muster everything you got to escape the dulling inertia now.
Once you're back to creating, the energy comes by itself.
posts.key_insight: Creative work is its own cure for malaise, but performative busyness masquerading as creation is a trap that deepens the inertia it pretends to address.

posts.summary: DHH uses Tesla's rise as a case study in strategic product differentiation, arguing that Tesla succeeded precisely because it ignored traditional automotive quality metrics like panel gaps and paint quality. Instead of competing in areas where legacy automakers had a century of advantage, Tesla focused on breakthrough innovations like EV charging infrastructure. The post frames this as a textbook example of Blue Ocean Strategy — finding success by choosing where not to compete. DHH argues this lesson applies broadly to any product maker: identify what customers don't actually care about, then redirect that energy toward underserved dimensions of value.
key_points: Tesla's legendary quality problems (shoddy paint, rattling interiors, mismatched doors) didn't matter because customers valued EVs over well-built gas cars
Deciding where you will NOT compete is as important as deciding where you will compete — this is the essence of Blue Ocean Strategy
Tesla avoided the red ocean of precision assembly where legacy makers had a century of advantage, and instead created blue ocean by owning EV charging infrastructure
When products are nearly identical, consumers obsess over minute differences — but offer something entirely different and half the shopping parameters disappear
The strategic move for new businesses is to build a thesis around what competing products' features you can drop, then invest that freed energy into an unattended axis
money_quotes: Consumers voted with their wallets for poorly built EVs because well-built gas-powered cars were deemed irrelevant in comparison.
To find a breakthrough angle in commerce, it's as important to decide where you will not compete, as it is to decide where you will compete.

The former is a red-ocean realm of competition against legacy car makers who've had a century to perfect their panel gaps, the latter is so blue-ocean as to appear preposterous to anyone from the gasoline past.
Figuring out what consumers truly care about is hard. Deciphering what they don't actually care about is harder still.
Give them something entirely different, and suddenly half the shopping parameters go out the window.
Build a thesis around which aspects of the competing products you could probably do without, and you'll be carving out opportunity to do something else with that liberated energy.
Then plow this energy into excelling on the underrepresented axis left unattended.
posts.key_insight: The most powerful competitive strategy isn't excelling at what incumbents already do well — it's identifying which traditional metrics customers don't actually care about and redirecting that energy into an entirely different dimension of value.

posts.summary: DHH argues that intelligent people are often the worst at accepting when reality proves their ideas wrong, because their intellect enables them to construct sophisticated rationalizations. He uses San Francisco's harm reduction policies as a primary example of a plausible thesis that failed in practice, yet whose proponents refuse to acknowledge defeat. He extends this pattern to technology and business, where people fall in love with their theories and can't course-correct. The core message is that treating reality as the ultimate referee and accepting intellectual losses is essential for long-term growth and better decision-making.
key_points: Smart people are paradoxically worse at accepting when reality disproves their theories because they can construct more sophisticated excuses
San Francisco's harm reduction approach is presented as a case study of a plausible thesis that failed but whose defenders refuse to acknowledge reality
The ability to believe your own eyes appears to diminish the higher you climb the intellectual hierarchy
This pattern of refusing to accept defeat against reality applies equally to technology, business, and politics
Long-term success requires eating intellectual losses and rolling learnings into the next thousand decisions
money_quotes: A great many smart people terminably hamper their ability to better understand the world by refusing to accept defeat when reality proves them wrong.
Believing your own eyes is a skill that actually appears less reliable the higher up you go the intellectual tree of knowledge.
The smarter we are, the more creative we can get at coming up with those self-deceivingly compelling rationalizations for why, actually, in this one case, the world is not what it appears.
There is never one single policy, one single business experiment, or one single technical argument so worth saving that you want to risk not learning from reality.
Going the distance means eating your intellectual losses. Accepting that reality is the referee.

Armed with an intellect that's at once both too proud to recognize it's own failings and cunningly capable of producing sophisticated excuses, they're adapt at spotting this failing in others but not in themselves.
posts.key_insight: Intelligence becomes a liability when it's used to rationalize away inconvenient realities rather than to learn from them, and long-term success depends on accepting defeat against reality quickly.

posts.summary: DHH argues that managers must distinguish between two types of problems with new hires: engagement (collaboration, communication style) and core competency (technical skills). Engagement problems can often be fixed with clear, candid feedback since they stem from unstated assumptions. Core competency problems, however, cannot be solved through conversations or performance plans, because skills built over years cannot be elevated to a new level in months. He advises hiring people at levels where they're solidly qualified rather than stretching, and recommends a quick parting of ways when competency is the issue rather than dragging both parties through futile performance improvement processes.
key_points: Problems with new hires fall into two categories: competency (skills) and engagement (collaboration/communication), and the remedy differs completely
Engagement issues stem from unstated assumptions and can be fixed with radical candor and clear feedback
Core competency gaps built over years of career development cannot be closed in weeks or months through feedback alone
Performance improvement plans for competency issues are often bureaucratic theater that wastes everyone's time
Hiring someone at a stretch level of seniority rarely does them a favor — better to hire where they can dazzle and promote from strength
money_quotes: If the problem is with core competency – their technical skills – you can't expect to solve that with a stern conversation.

If a programmer or a designer or a writer spent the last four-five-six years of their career getting their skills to where they are now, it's delusional to think that they can lift them to an entirely new level in just a month or two or even six just because you asked them firmly.
Problems with core competency is the pit where good intentions go to die.
The impossible walks in the desert that don't have any realistic chance of leading out. Motions of bureaucracy conducted to produce a paper trail. The worst.
Better to have them come in where they're solidly qualified, can dazzle against the expectations they can surely best, and then push a promotion from a surge of confidence and goodwill.
You do neither you nor them any favors by sugarcoating or delaying the feedback.
posts.key_insight: Managers must honestly distinguish between fixable engagement problems and unfixable competency gaps in new hires, because no amount of feedback or performance plans can rapidly close a skills deficit built over years.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Turbo 8, the next major release of the Hotwire Turbo framework, will drop TypeScript in favor of plain JavaScript. He argues that modern JavaScript (post-ES6) is enjoyable to write and doesn't need a compilation step, while TypeScript adds 'type gymnastics' that provide little joy and considerable grief. He frames the typing preference as a near-immutable personality trait rather than a rational choice, and emphasizes that TypeScript's compatibility design means this decision won't break interoperability. The post positions the move as a liberation from unnecessary tooling rather than an attack on TypeScript users.
key_points: Modern JavaScript (ES6+) is capable enough to be enjoyable without a compilation layer
TypeScript's type system creates friction where easy things become hard and hard things become 'any'
Typing preference is a deep-seated mindset formed early in a programmer's career, not a rational debate to be won
TypeScript's full compatibility with JavaScript means dropping it from a library doesn't force users to abandon it
JavaScript is a language of necessity on the client side, making freedom from mandatory tooling especially valuable
money_quotes: Things that should be easy become hard, and things that are hard become `any`. No thanks!
Very few programmers are typically interested in having their opinion on typing changed.
Most programmers find themselves drawn strongly to typing or not quite early in their career, and then spend the rest of it rationalizing The Correct Choice to themselves and others.
It pollutes the code with type gymnastics that add ever so little joy to my development experience, and quite frequently considerable grief.
JavaScript is a language of necessity when it comes to the client side.

So farewell, TypeScript. May you bring much rigor and satisfaction to your tribe while letting the rest of us enjoy JavaScript in the glorious spirit it was originally designed: Free of strong typing.
posts.key_insight: TypeScript's type system imposes compilation overhead and cognitive friction that isn't justified for developers who find joy in JavaScript's dynamic nature, and dropping it from an open-source library is a valid choice that doesn't diminish TypeScript's value for those who prefer it.

posts.summary: DHH responds to the backlash following his decision to remove TypeScript from the Turbo framework. He distinguishes between legitimate technical criticism from actual contributors and the mob behavior from outsiders with no stake in the project. The response included defacement of GitHub collaboration tools — fake pull requests, demands to delete the repository, and calls to remove DHH personally. He compares this to sports hooliganism and traces the intensity to the 'bikeshed' dynamic where low-stakes disagreements produce the nastiest fights. He acknowledges his own history of zealous advocacy but draws a line at defacing open source infrastructure. He closes by asking TypeScript enthusiasts to find better outlets for their passion.
key_points: The backlash came mostly from people with no stake in Turbo, crusading for TypeScript as an abstract cause rather than engaging with the specific technical decision
GitHub collaboration tools were weaponized — fake PRs to 'add TypeScript back,' a PR to delete the repository, and an issue to remove DHH from his own project
The bikeshed parable explains the intensity: low-stakes, unresolvable debates attract the nastiest fighting because participation is easy and consequence-free
TypeScript's identity crisis fuels the rage — it's a superset of JavaScript, so celebrating the subset feels like an existential threat to believers
DHH distinguishes between passionate evangelism and dogmatic crusading, acknowledging he's danced that line himself with Ruby on Rails
money_quotes: There's a fine line between being a passionate evangelist and becoming a dogmatic crusader.
People who showed up to crusade for a narrative that tells them only the incompetent or the malevolent would deny the divine glory of their superset.
Just absolutely unhinged open-source hooliganism.

This was one project, mostly overseen by one company, that removed TypeScript from their own project, and explained why, while allowing amble room and spirit for others to reach different conclusions for their own work.
TypeScript is JavaScript! It's a superset that sprinkles an appearance of type safety on top of a weakly- and dynamically-typed language.
To muster such rabid animosity towards anyone celebrating the use of the subset is but an illustration of the ferocity and viciousness of minor differences.
When the stakes are low, the debate more easily becomes nasty.
Please, for the love of open source culture, find another way to express your enthusiasm than by defacing collaboration organs or slandering those who JavaScript differently.
posts.key_insight: The TypeScript backlash exemplifies how low-stakes technical disagreements attract the worst behavior precisely because anyone can participate and nothing real is at risk — the bikeshed effect weaponized through open source collaboration tools.

posts.summary: DHH compares his four-year-old Tesla Model X against a new Mercedes EQS SUV and finds that his family unanimously prefers the Tesla despite its older age and lower price. While DHH personally appreciates Mercedes' build quality and quieter cabin, his family values Tesla's fun factor, entertainment features, and overall experience. He argues this reveals a fundamental challenge for legacy automakers: traditional automotive virtues like build quality matter less to a huge portion of the market. He concludes by noting even traditional car enthusiasts are won over by Tesla's absurd performance-to-price ratio, citing the Plaid's ability to beat a Bugatti at a fraction of the cost.
