key_points: DHH's family unanimously preferred an older, cheaper Tesla Model X over a brand-new, more expensive Mercedes EQS SUV
Traditional automotive virtues like build quality and cabin refinement are losing relevance to a large segment of buyers who prioritize fun, software, and personality
Tesla's sense of humor and entertainment features (games, UI personality) create loyalty that legacy brands struggle to match
The Model S Plaid's 1,020 horsepower at $90,000 — capable of beating a Bugatti Chiron — represents a price-performance ratio that's unprecedented in automotive history
money_quotes: Even though the Mercedes is considerably more money, and brand new, the rest of the family would rather keep the Tesla.
This is what the legacy car industry is up against.
Most of its traditional virtues under attack by a huge portion of the market who just don't give a damn.
The fact that Tesla has a sense of humor trumps the minor niggles it might still have compared to the legacy competition.
For a car that'll beat a Bugatti Chiron, along with virtually every other car on the planet, in the quarter mile. That's just ridiculous.
posts.key_insight: Tesla wins not by matching legacy automakers on traditional quality metrics but by making those metrics irrelevant to most buyers through fun, personality, and absurd performance-to-price ratios.

posts.summary: DHH reports that 37signals' cloud exit has already reduced their cloud spending by 60%, from $180,000/month to under $80,000/month, yielding approximately $1 million per year in savings. The hardware investment of about $500,000 in new servers will pay for itself in less than six months. He projects eventual savings could reach $2 million per year, or $10 million over five years. He argues this isn't an anomaly, pointing to Snapchat's $3 billion cloud spend as evidence that many companies are massively overpaying. The savings were delayed because reserved instance contracts had to expire first, but the numbers are now materializing as predicted.
key_points: Cloud spending dropped 60% from ~$180K/month to under $80K/month after migrating applications off AWS
The $500K hardware investment will pay for itself in under six months from monthly savings alone
Projected long-term savings could reach $2M/year or $10M over five years
Reserved instance contracts delayed the full savings realization, but the remaining spend will phase out through the year
The post-bubble era means profitability matters again, making cloud cost optimization newly urgent for many companies
money_quotes: The only way to get pricing in the cloud down from obscene to merely offensive is through reserved instances.
By the basic comparison of money saved vs money spent, we'll be in the money on the big purchase with the current monthly savings in less than six months. That's just astounding!
We don't have to squint hard to see the eventual savings climb all the way up to about $2m/year. That would be TEN MILLION DOLLARS over five years.
From having looked at the unoptimized cloud bills at other software companies, our savings may in fact be modest compared to what's possible.
Don't tell me there wasn't a billion or so in potential savings on that tab.
That didn't used to matter, because who cared if the business was profitable or not, but now it sorta does!
posts.key_insight: Leaving the cloud yielded a 60% cost reduction that will pay back the hardware investment in under six months, vindicating the case that owning your own infrastructure is dramatically cheaper for companies with predictable workloads.

posts.summary: DHH announces the 1.0 release of Kamal, a deployment tool born from 37signals' cloud exit. He frames the project as a happy accident triggered by SUSE's exorbitant enterprise pricing, which pushed them to build their own open-source tooling. Kamal handles zero-downtime deploys and rolling restarts for both cloud VMs and bare metal. The tool has already been used to migrate seven production apps off the cloud, with projected savings of $7M over five years. DHH positions Kamal as useful not just for cloud exits but for any startup wanting to preserve the option of leaving the cloud later.
key_points: SUSE's overpriced enterprise contracts inadvertently motivated 37signals to build their own open-source deployment tooling
Kamal 1.0 offers zero-downtime deploys, rolling restarts, and asset bridging for both cloud VMs and bare metal
37signals has already migrated seven production apps including HEY, projecting $7M in savings over five years
The project crystallized a core value: critical infrastructure must be open source and simple enough for the whole team to understand
Kamal is positioned as valuable even for cloud-native startups who want optionality to leave later
money_quotes: So many of the best opportunities in life only reveal themselves when the easy path is blocked.
We will not build our critical infrastructure on top of anything but open source, and it has to be simple enough that our whole team can understand it. If such tools do not exist, we will build them.
We are creatures in search of the least resistance, so to reach higher, we usually need a little push.
I count my lucky stars that SUSE's pricing for Rancher and Harvester was so ridiculous over the top for our situation.
It's for anyone who wants the option of ever being able to easily do so.
These startups can still profit from using Kamal on day 1, so that they'll be able to leave, on their own accord, when it makes financial sense to do so.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise vendor overreach can be a blessing in disguise, forcing teams to build simpler, open-source alternatives that deliver both independence and massive cost savings.

posts.summary: DHH announces Strada, the final piece of the Hotwire triptych alongside Turbo and Stimulus. Strada bridges native mobile controls with web apps, allowing hybrid applications to feel native while being primarily web-driven. The release completes Hotwire's vision of enabling individual developers to build and deliver features across all platforms from a single codebase. DHH emphasizes three benefits: blurring the line between web and native, reducing app store dependency, and empowering web teams to ship mobile features independently.
key_points: Strada bridges native controls (menus, etc.) with Hotwire web apps, completing the Turbo/Stimulus/Strada triptych
Hybrid apps like HEY and Basecamp are nearly indistinguishable from fully native apps to most users, with immense productivity benefits
Reducing app store reliance lets teams deploy fixes instantly without waiting for bureaucratic approval
Web developers can now drive changes on native controls without coordinating with mobile teams
The Majestic Monolith approach lets a comparably tiny team serve multiple platforms from a single codebase
money_quotes: Turbo Native makes it easy to have native mobile apps driven by fast web views, Strada brings that extra bit of polish only native controls, like native menus, can provide.

While true native nerds can probably still tell the difference, the vast majority of paying customers cannot. And the productivity benefits to not writing everything as custom, native screens are immense.
One of the reasons that developing for the web feels so magical is because you can roll out changes and fixes to everyone instantly without asking or waiting for permission.
Every time you submit a new build, you have to factor in the risk that a bureaucrat will reject your application for reasons unknown.

The more power we can give the web developers to introduce new features on the Majestic Monolith that Just Works inside the mobile apps, the less we need to grind gears coordinating releases with the mobile team.
Supercharge the productivity of individual developers by making them capable of building and delivering complete features across all platforms by themselves.
No frontend/backend separation, and as little web/native separation as possible too.
posts.key_insight: Strada completes Hotwire's vision of letting small web teams build fully native-feeling apps across every platform from a single codebase, maximizing developer productivity while minimizing platform-specific dependencies.

posts.summary: DHH reviews Walter Isaacson's Elon Musk biography, praising it as both a fine biography and an excellent business book. He highlights Musk's five-step 'algorithm' for shipping products as the book's most valuable contribution. While acknowledging Musk's controversial nature and maniacal work ethic, DHH argues readers can extract practical wisdom without becoming Musk disciples. He suggests most founders would benefit from adding even 10% of Musk's intensity to their approach, while maintaining that calmer work philosophies remain valid.
key_points: Musk's five-step algorithm (question requirements, delete, simplify, accelerate, automate) is a powerful and practical methodology applicable beyond his companies
You can learn from people you wouldn't want to be — extracting wisdom doesn't require becoming a disciple or mirror
Musk's extreme intensity works partly because his missions (energy transition, Mars colonization) motivate people to persevere despite his difficult management style
The modern inability to appreciate elements of a flawed character without swallowing it whole is a cultural loss
Most founders would benefit from adding a fraction of Musk's intensity while still maintaining healthier work philosophies
money_quotes: Like all the best business books, it's not merely an instruction manual, but an inspirational guide too.
His entrepreneurial achievements are as incontestable as his maniacal, even masochistic, commitment to a HARDCORE work ethic.
The latter seems as much motivated by escaping personal demons as by moving the needle. But the needle also does move!
You can absolutely learn from people you wouldn't want to be.
Extracting wisdom from Musk's success does not oblige you to become his disciple or his mirror.
A key reason Musk gets away with his madness is that the missions he pursue can motivate people to persevere in spite of him.
The ability to appreciate, applaud, and even partly appropriate elements of a character you'd never wish to swallow whole.
Most founders and executives would do well to add at least 10% of Musk's intensity to their personal recipe.
posts.key_insight: Musk's five-step algorithm for shipping products is universally applicable wisdom, and leaders can adopt elements of his intensity without embracing the extremes that make it a package deal.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the inaugural Rails World conference in Amsterdam, celebrating Ruby on Rails' twentieth anniversary with over 700 developers. He declares the event a resounding success and validation of The Rails Foundation, which was established just the year prior. The conference featured major releases including Rails 7.1, Solid Cache, Strada, and Kamal 1.0, alongside the largest-ever gathering of the Rails core team. DHH emphasizes that the energy came not just from new announcements but from genuine human connection, contrasting the positivity of in-person gatherings with the negativity of online discourse. He credits executive director Amanda Perino for pulling off the event in remarkably little time and thanks the foundation's corporate sponsors.
key_points: Rails World validated The Rails Foundation as an institution, proving real-world enthusiasm that online sentiment couldn't predict
The conference was packed with major releases and announcements — Rails 7.1, Solid Cache, Strada, Kamal 1.0, Turbo 8 previews — giving the ecosystem months of new material to digest
In-person connection provided an antidote to toxic online discourse, demonstrating that the Rails community is united around shared goals despite differences
The Rails core team assembled its largest-ever in-person gathering, recharging relationships built over a decade of remote collaboration
Amanda Perino executed the entire event in just months after being hired, handling hundreds of thousands of dollars in decisions
money_quotes: The internet is often a poor guide for what people really think or what they really want.
It's like making a new product. Until customers put their credit card in the form, you don't actually know what you've got.
I don't recall breathing such an air of excitement, of renewal, and of optimism since the very earliest days of Ruby on Rails.
When so much of our online discourse is sadly soaked in salty or sour interactions, we need the sweetness of being together like this to provide an antidote.
Coming together around our common goals and shared interests, rather than constantly marinating in our differences, whether they be technical, cultural, or political.
You know how they say social media isn't real life? Rails World was the perfect illustration of that.
posts.key_insight: The enthusiastic reception of Rails World proved that in-person community gathering reveals a vitality and optimism that online discourse systematically obscures.

posts.summary: DHH announces he's building a new Rails application (once.com) without any frontend build steps for the first time since the 2000s. The stack uses vanilla ES6 with import maps, vanilla CSS with nesting and variables, and a simple asset pipeline called Propshaft. He argues that while tools like esbuild and bun are great (and Rails supports them), the real state of the art is eliminating the build step entirely. By leveraging HTTP/2 and universal browser support for modern JavaScript and CSS features, developers can avoid bundling and transpiling altogether.
key_points: A new Rails app (once.com) is being built with zero frontend build steps using vanilla ES6, import maps, and vanilla CSS
Browser support for import maps, CSS nesting, and CSS variables has matured enough to eliminate transpilation and bundling
HTTP/2 and universal import map support make JavaScript bundling unnecessary for many applications
Rails 7.1 supports both approaches — build tools like esbuild/bun and the No Build path via Propshaft
The frontier of web development has shifted from faster builds to no builds
money_quotes: You can't get faster than No Build.
It's all just so... simple.
It's also fast. Really fast. Infinitely fast.
The state of the art is no longer finding more sophisticated ways to build JavaScript or CSS. It's not to build at all.

Lean on HTTP/2 and the now universal support for import maps to avoid bundling, and lean on the fundamental progress in support for modern JavaScript and CSS to avoid compiling what browsers already know how to read.
You just might want to shed the weight of complexity and enjoy the lighter stack.
posts.key_insight: Modern browser capabilities have advanced enough that the next frontier in frontend tooling isn't faster builds — it's eliminating the build step entirely.

posts.summary: DHH challenges the logical fallacy of dismissing new approaches because similar past attempts failed. He uses HEY's #NoBuild JavaScript approach as a concrete example — running without bundling or compiling JavaScript for three years with tens of thousands of paying customers and a perfect Lighthouse performance score. He argues that progress happens when people do things differently, and that import maps (available since 2020) changed what's feasible. Rather than evangelizing, he shares HEY's approach as proof that simplicity works, especially for small teams that benefit from conceptual compression.
key_points: The 'we tried that, didn't work' fallacy wrongly limits collective ambition based on others' past failures
HEY has run without JavaScript bundling or compiling for three years, achieving a perfect 100/100 Lighthouse performance score
Import maps (post-2020) changed what's technically feasible, invalidating mental models formed in the 2010-2020 era
Progress requires doing something different from predecessors, not dismissing new approaches based on outdated assumptions
Conceptual compression — removing entire steps from the development process — is especially valuable for small teams
money_quotes: In our quest for making programming simpler, faster, and prettier, no logical fallacy provides as much of an obstacle as "we tried that, didn't work".
There would be no human progress if we all quit trying after any unsuccessful attempt.
It's like insisting your map is correct even after it failed to record the massive mountain you're literally staring at.
Refusing to believe reality because it doesn't comport to your mental model of the world is an intellectual failure state.
That's how progress usually happens! By someone doing something different than whoever went before them in pursuit of the same goal.
Making programming better requires a willingness to test your priors. To question your assumptions. To recognize the half-life of facts.
Nothing compresses what you need to know like removing an entire step from the equation.
Don't get stuck in the mud of complexity.
posts.key_insight: Past failures don't define the boundaries of what's possible — technologies evolve, and dismissing proven results because they contradict your outdated mental model is an intellectual failure state.

posts.summary: DHH responds to critics who argued 37signals' cloud exit failed because they didn't properly leverage managed services. He counters that HEY was born in the cloud using Aurora/RDS, OpenSearch, and EKS extensively — and that's precisely why the bill was so high. Using OpenSearch as a case study, he shows they replaced $43,333/month in AWS spending with ~$5,000/month in self-hosted hardware, achieving nearly an order of magnitude in savings. The hardware paid for itself in three months, and no additional staff were needed.
key_points: HEY was cloud-native from launch, using Aurora/RDS, OpenSearch, and EKS — not a naive lift-and-shift
OpenSearch cost $43,333/month on AWS versus ~$5,000/month self-hosted, with hardware paying for itself in three months
The staffing argument doesn't hold: running on-premise is not materially different in operational scope from running in the cloud
The 'you're holding it wrong' defense of cloud computing doesn't survive contact with actual cost comparisons
money_quotes: It's basically the "you're holding it wrong" argument for the cloud, and it's hogwash.
It's exactly because we used all the cloud had to offer so extensively that the bill ended up so high.
There's no way the answer to this question was "just use more managed services" or wave the serverless magic wand over it.
That's almost an order of magnitude less than we were spending on OpenSearch!!
The $150,000 we spent on the hardware will have been repaid in just over three months, and from there on out, we'll be saving some $40,000/month.
Even if we had to hire someone FULL TIME just to look after search, we'd come out way ahead. But we haven't.
The team it takes to run our scale of operations in the cloud wasn't any less than what it takes to run that same scale on our own hardware.
That was the theory anyway. It's still surprising, startling even, to see it proven out in reality.
posts.key_insight: Managed cloud services don't reduce operational complexity or staffing needs — they just multiply costs, and self-hosting the same workloads can deliver near order-of-magnitude savings.

posts.summary: DHH argues that open source works best when understood as a gift exchange rather than a commercial transaction. He explains that his own open source contributions are sustainable precisely because they're selfishly motivated first—serving himself and his company—with broader benefit as a secondary outcome. He identifies mismatched expectations as the core problem in open source: maintainers who expect reciprocity from what should be gifts, and users who expect commercial-grade support from free software. The solution is simply reframing the relationship through the gift metaphor.
key_points: Open source contributions are most sustainable when selfishly motivated first, benefiting the creator before others
Maintainer resentment stems from expecting specific reciprocity for what are fundamentally gifts
Users develop 'vendoritis'—mistaking their relationship with open source maintainers for a commercial one and expecting customer service
Commercial software has explicit contractual terms; open source's vague expectations naturally breed resentment on both sides
The open source crisis is one of mismatched expectations, solvable by adopting the gift metaphor
money_quotes: It's an intentionally selfish drive first, then a broader benefit second. But, ironically, this is what's made my participation in the gift exchange of open source sustainable for twenty years and counting.
Putting my own mask on first, so I can keep helping others.
To expect specific forms of reciprocity from acts of altruism.
There's absolutely nothing wrong with developing software on commercial terms, and to expect to be paid for your work.
The terms of commercial reciprocity when it comes to open source are usually far more vague, and thus open to illusions of agreement.

Users of open source cultivating their own resentment because they mistake the relationship they have with maintainers for a commercial one. And thus expect a standard of customer service they've been used to with commercial vendors. I like to call this specific affliction 'vendoritis'.
If you're a recipient of a gift, one offered with no strings attached, you're not entitled to much beyond the freedom of choice of whether to accept it or not.
The primary crisis, if there is one, is mismatched expectations between givers and receivers. Solvable by a change in perspective.
posts.key_insight: Open source sustainability comes from treating contributions as selfish gifts rather than altruistic obligations, and the ecosystem's primary dysfunction is mismatched expectations on both sides of the exchange.

posts.summary: DHH explains why 37signals moved their podcast to Buzzsprout, a decision driven not by features or price but by a personal connection with cofounder Tom Rossi from the Rails community. He reflects on how modern commerce is usually impersonal, but when you can connect with the person running a business, it changes the buying dynamic entirely. He draws a parallel to choosing Braintree fifteen years ago because of a personal connection with CEO Bryan Johnson. The post argues that this ability to sell through personal relationships is a unique competitive advantage for small and medium-sized businesses, especially in commodity markets.
key_points: 37signals chose Buzzsprout for podcast hosting based on personal connection with its cofounder, not features or price
In-person encounters at events like Rails World can forge business relationships that transcend commodity comparisons
Small and medium businesses have a unique advantage in cultivating personal connections that large companies cannot replicate
Buying decisions can be a form of voting with your wallet for the kind of businesses you want to see succeed
money_quotes: Podcast hosting is to some extent a commodity market, so this was less about pining for a specific feature or even working to reduce the bill. This was about buying from Tom Rossi.
This in-person encounter made me reflect on modern commerce. How impersonal it is most of the time.
I usually would rather deal with a web form than a salesperson, but it's different when you can establish a connection to someone actually running the show.
He made it feel like we were buying from Bryan, more so than buying from Braintree.
You can far more easily cultivate and maintain these personal connections. Make the exceptions when that's needed. Be available when it matters.
By doing so, you allow your customers to buy the seller rather than just the product or service. That's a powerful advantage, especially in commodity markets.
This also represents an opportunity for buyers to directly vote with their wallet for what they'd like to see more of in this world.
posts.key_insight: In commodity markets, the most powerful differentiator for small businesses is the personal connection between buyer and seller — people buy people, not just products.

posts.summary: DHH highlights X (formerly Twitter) engineering team's announcement of 60% cloud cost reduction by moving infrastructure on-premises. He frames this as validation of the cloud exit movement, noting X achieved these savings while simultaneously reducing staff from 8,000 to under 2,000. DHH argues this proves that large organizations are massively overspending on cloud services. He predicts CFOs and investors will take notice, potentially pushing cloud exit into the mainstream.
key_points: X reduced monthly cloud costs by 60%, cloud data storage by 60%, and cloud data processing costs by 75% by moving to on-prem infrastructure
These savings were achieved with a workforce reduced to a quarter of its original size, undermining the argument that cloud exit requires more staff
Based on reported $100M/year AWS spend, X is on track to save $60M annually from cloud exit alone
DHH deliberately sidesteps the political debate around Musk to focus purely on the infrastructure economics
He positions X's results as a signal that cloud exit is about to go mainstream across large corporations
money_quotes: Musk has taken a cleaver to the costs and complexity at X. It hasn't always been pretty, but it sure has been effective.

Not only has the site stayed up, despite hysteric proclamations that it would crater soon after his personnel changes, but X has been able to increase the pace of experimentation and feature introductions at the same time.
However you're aligned, you'll find a chart to support your position that X is either thriving or has one foot in the grave.
Reduced the monthly cloud cost by 60%(!!) by moving work out of the cloud and onto their own servers.
What's even more impressive than the fact they've been able to cut their cloud bill so much, so quickly is that they've done it with a team a quarter the size of what it was.

If it's possible, and Musk is busy proving that it is, to run an operation like X with a quarter the staff, and STILL profit massively from exiting the cloud, you know there are gigantious savings waiting to be unlocked.
The #CloudExit might well be at the cusp of going mainstream. Have you run your numbers?
posts.key_insight: X's 60% cloud cost reduction achieved with a fraction of its former workforce proves that cloud exit can deliver massive savings even under constrained resources, signaling a potential mainstream shift away from cloud dependence.

posts.summary: DHH explains the Stoic practice of negative visualization — deliberately imagining worst-case scenarios not to breed anxiety, but to build resilience and gratitude. He argues this mental exercise prepares you for inevitable hardship and disconnects external events from internal responses. He then connects the practice to his own work, explaining that his 'One Person Framework' philosophy in open source is negative visualization put into action — preparing for the possible end of his business by building tools that empower individuals. He concludes that accepting impermanence is paradoxically liberating and makes it easier to enjoy the present.
key_points: Negative visualization is the opposite of anxiety: it's deliberate mental preparation for hardship that builds resilience and calm
The practice also generates gratitude by reminding you that things could always be worse than they currently are
DHH's 'One Person Framework' philosophy is negative visualization in action — preparing to start over from scratch with only tools and skills
Accepting that good times might end at any station dissipates the fear of not knowing when they will
Multiple philosophical traditions — Stoicism, Buddhism, modern self-help — independently arrived at the insight that accepting impermanence reduces suffering
money_quotes: The point of visualizing the consequences of an accident, the loss of a loved one, or the bankruptcy of your business is not to stew in despair, but to prepare yourself for the inevitability of hardship.
With enough training, you can disconnect the two, and become far better at choosing your response to almost any stimuli.
If you know and accept that the gravy train might stop at any station, you dissipate the fear associated with not knowing when or where it might do so.
I'm setting up my own situation for starting over from scratch with no capital, no staff, no advantages. Only the tools and the skills I've built and learned along the way.
It's ironic that it becomes so much easier to enjoy working on the business after you've accepted that it might end at any time, but it's true.
Things can always get worse and you can always get better.
posts.key_insight: Deliberately preparing for worst-case scenarios doesn't breed anxiety — it builds the calm and resilience needed to fully enjoy the present.

posts.summary: DHH shares his unexpectedly enthusiastic experience owning a Tesla Model S Plaid, comparing it favorably against his extensive collection of exotic supercars. Despite costing a fraction of his Lamborghinis and Ferraris, the Plaid's 1,000+ horsepower electric drivetrain delivers acceleration that surpasses them all while remaining perfectly controllable. He argues the car's handling, practicality, and software make it far more than a straight-line speed trick. The post frames the Plaid as democratizing progress — exotic-level performance becoming accessible to ordinary buyers.
key_points: The Tesla Model S Plaid delivers over 1,000 horsepower for $86K, matching the thrill of cars costing five to ten times more
EV traction control makes massive power safe and approachable in ways combustion engines never could, eliminating the scary loss-of-grip moments common in fast gasoline cars
The real joy of horsepower is acceleration, not top speed — every stoplight and onramp becomes an opportunity
The Plaid handles at 90% the level of a Porsche Taycan and is surprisingly livable as a daily driver
Exotic performance is being democratized: today's $86K car will be a $40K used car in a few years
money_quotes: The pleasure of power isn't about top speed, it's about acceleration, and I have never, ever owned a car that accelerates like the Model S Plaid in these intervals.
It's intoxicating and addictive, in the best, most wholesome possible way.

An old school gasoline-powered car can feel scary with as little as 300-400 horsepower. Traction breaks, you need quick hands to catch it. That's the frame of reference anyone who've driven a fast, regular car would have. But it doesn't apply at all in the EV world.
Very few people will ever end up owning a great Ferrari V12 or a Porsche V10. Lots and lots of people will either own or get a ride in Tesla Plaid. That's progress!
It's the kind of democratizing progress that Warhol talks about when he quips that the billionaire and the bum both have access to the best soft drink in the world: A Coca Cola.
I expected a one-trick pony. But this is so much more.
Even if you're an incarnate petrol head, you owe yourself a ride in a Plaid, and I will almost guarantee that you'll feel better about the future.
posts.key_insight: The Tesla Model S Plaid represents a democratization of exotic automotive performance, delivering thrills that once required half-million-dollar supercars at a fraction of the price and with far greater accessibility and safety.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Workfeed, a Danish employee scheduling startup he invested in, is expanding to the American market. During his three years in Denmark, he invested in five local startups on the premise of building profitable businesses without venture capital. Workfeed has already achieved profitability in Denmark, becoming the top-rated scheduling system there. The post explains how employee scheduling software is uniquely localized due to payroll integrations and labor law requirements, making international expansion harder than typical SaaS. DHH promotes their US launch by offering three months of free access to American service businesses willing to provide feedback.
key_points: Employee scheduling software is surprisingly localized due to payroll integrations, labor laws, and industry standards, unlike universal tools like Basecamp
Workfeed proved the bootstrap model works by becoming profitable and market-leading in Denmark without venture capital
Denmark's small market (under 6 million people) creates a natural ceiling that forces successful startups to expand internationally
DHH invested in five Danish startups specifically on anti-VC terms: profitability-focused, staying local, avoiding the venture capital path
money_quotes: All on the premise that we'd work towards becoming profitable, remaining in the country, and avoiding the VC timebomb.
That might sound pretty boring, but if you run or work at a restaurant still trying to do things with Excel spreadsheets or call sheets, you know this can be a huge pain and time suck.
That's exactly the kind of drudgery that software was meant to solve.
What I found interesting about employee scheduling software is how localized much of it is.
It's quite unlike the universality afforded Jason and I working on something like Basecamp.
It's a way to help a Danish startup fulfill the hypothesis that it's possible to build awesome SaaS solutions without going down the venture rabbit hole.
posts.key_insight: Bootstrapped, profitable SaaS companies can compete and expand internationally without venture capital, even in highly localized markets like employee scheduling.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the release of The Rails Documentary by Honeypot, marking twenty years of Ruby on Rails. He acknowledges the collaborative effort of thousands of contributors and the framework's impact, from tiny apps to Shopify's $77 billion business. Turning 44 prompts him to consider legacy and how to measure a life well-spent, drawing on Stoicism and Seneca's thoughts on mortality. He concludes that Rails has been unequivocally worth the effort, pushback, and drama.
key_points: The Rails Documentary prompted a rare moment of looking back on twenty years of Ruby on Rails development
Rails has scaled from small internal apps to powering $77 billion companies like Shopify, with over a million applications built
Turning 44 and passing life's statistical halfway point brought reflections on legacy and mortality
Stoicism and Seneca's philosophy on the shortness of life frame how DHH measures whether his time was well-spent
money_quotes: Together with 37signals, Rails has been my life's work.
I've been humbled to see well over a million applications be built with it. Everything from tiny internal apps to $77 billion behemoths like Shopify.
One of the reasons I don't spend too much time looking back is because how easily you might end up stewing in your own nostalgia and self-satisfaction.
That's past the halfway point for the average human in every society around the world.
To believe that I spent my time well, in service of pursuits worth the effort, in concert with people worth the relation, in honor of virtues worth upholding.
Looking back at Ruby on Rails, I can easily and emphatically say 'totally worth it'. Worth the effort, worth the pushback, worth the dramas, worth the impact.
posts.key_insight: Twenty years on, Rails stands as a life's work DHH can look back on with the conviction that the effort, conflict, and collaboration were entirely worth it.

posts.summary: DHH shares his rediscovery of high-fidelity audio after installing KEF LSX-II speakers at his desk. Growing up with a father who repaired electronics gave him an early appreciation for quality sound, but years of noise-canceling headphones and mediocre office speakers dulled the experience. After improving his office acoustics with a panel and stumbling onto the KEF speakers through X recommendations, he was blown away by the sound quality. He argues that HiFi doesn't require audiophile obsession—a good integrated setup can transform how you experience music you thought you already knew.
key_points: Growing up around repaired audio equipment seeded a lifelong appreciation for how equipment shapes the musical experience
Most everyday listening contexts (headphones on planes, mediocre office speakers) fail to deliver meaningful audio quality
You don't need perfect hearing or audiophile credentials to appreciate the difference a real HiFi setup makes
The discovery came through social media serendipity—strangers on X recommended the KEF LSX-IIs after seeing his desk setup
HiFi is music rendered so purely it can't stay in the background—it demands attention and reveals new depth in familiar tracks
money_quotes: It's as easy to tell the difference as that between watching a movie on a VHS tape and seeing it from blue-ray on a 4K projector.
That to me is HiFi. Music rendered so purely, so broadly, so detailed that it can't help but step out of the background and take center stage.
I came for the aesthetics, but I was blown away by the sound.
Constantly uncovering new facets and depth in the music.
Sure, you can get nuts with it, like everything, and start chasing gold-platted cables and purer forms of electricity to avoid interference. I mean, if that's you, great, it's fun to have a hobby!
But what a damn peak it is!
posts.key_insight: You don't need to be an audiophile to experience transformative sound quality—a well-chosen integrated speaker setup can turn familiar music into a revelation.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how material possessions inevitably lose their novelty through the hedonic treadmill, while nature never stops inspiring awe. He shares his experience of watching sunrises from his Malibu home office and references research on forest bathing to argue that regular exposure to nature improves health and happiness. He connects this to his advocacy for remote work, suggesting that trading commute time for time in nature is one of its most underappreciated benefits.
key_points: Material possessions lose their wow factor over time due to the hedonic treadmill, but nature never does
Moving from urban Copenhagen to wilderness California reinforced that nature ranks higher than city life on his personal scorecard
Research on forest bathing supports the idea that walking in nature reduces stress and blood pressure more than urban walking
Remote work enables trading stressful commute time for restorative time in nature
money_quotes: I've found that you eventually get bored or at least used to every form of material trapping you can buy. Cars, watches, cameras, computers, whatever.
It's not that fancy stuff stops being nice, but it does stop being wow.
If you literally stop to smell the flowers, you'll never stop being wowed by the awesomeness of this planet.
I've seen a sunrise like this in Malibu a million times before, but it never, ever grows old.
Your life will literally be longer, healthier, and happier, if you let nature wash over you regularly.

Imagine taking that hour or two of commuting – which, when done in rush hour by car, is perhaps one of the most blood pressure and stress raising activities known to man! – and trading that in for a walk in the forest or the garden or any form of wilderness.
posts.key_insight: The hedonic treadmill dulls every material purchase, but nature's capacity to inspire awe is inexhaustible — and remote work is the practical key to accessing it daily.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Progressive Web Applications have finally become a viable alternative to native mobile apps for B2B startups in 2024. He traces the long struggle of startups needing to build for web, iOS, and Android simultaneously, which forces them to raise capital and hire large teams. Apple's recent addition of web push notifications and improved PWA support in Safari has removed the last major blocker. He recommends B2B startups focus on web-first development and only pursue native apps after achieving product-market fit, noting that 37signals is going all-in on PWAs for their new ONCE products.
key_points: B2B startups face an unfair burden building for web, iOS, and Android simultaneously, forcing them into external funding and large teams
Apple's addition of web push notifications and badge counting in Safari finally removes the last major blocker for PWAs as a native app alternative
Apple likely delayed PWA support for years to protect App Store revenue, only relenting under pressure from the EU's Digital Markets Act
B2B startups should target web-first and only invest in native apps after achieving product-market fit and profitability
37signals is going all-in on PWAs for their ONCE products, pairing web-based delivery with customer self-hosting
money_quotes: We built Basecamp back in 2003 because we could do it ourselves. A single developer and a couple of designers was all it took.
Everything just takes longer on mobile because the materials are harder to work with than the web.
You don't have to be much of a conspiracy theorist to ponder why it took Apple almost a decade to bring web push notifications to the iPhone.

Apple makes billions of dollars in toll fees from charging app makers 30% of their revenue to live in the App Store, and gating push notifications behind that toll has been an incredibly effective tool to enforce that distribution monopoly.
The hallowed promise of web applications being viable and competitive alternatives to native efforts is finally here.
I'm now going to recommend that every B2B software startup begins by targeting the web first.
If technical founder teams can't do it themselves, they have to hire, and if they have to hire, they have to raise capital. I hate that.
posts.key_insight: Apple's belated embrace of web push notifications has finally made PWAs a realistic alternative to native mobile apps, freeing B2B startups from the capital-intensive burden of building for three platforms simultaneously.

posts.summary: DHH describes the final stages of preparing the first ONCE product for launch, emphasizing their commitment to exceptionally high code quality. Because ONCE ships its source code with purchase, the team has held themselves to watchmaker-level standards of internal polish. He argues that building for quality and readability actually accelerates delivery rather than slowing it down, countering the conventional wisdom that crunch and corner-cutting are necessary to meet deadlines. He personally reviews all 7,412 lines of code repeatedly to refine naming, spacing, and cohesion.
key_points: Shipping source code with the product creates accountability for internal code quality, preventing the temptation to cut corners
High internal quality standards serve commercial deadlines rather than opposing them — 'slow is smooth, smooth is fast'
Repeated full codebase reviews catch not just style issues but also functional quality problems that reduce customer issues
The ONCE business model (buy once, get the code) enables a new relationship with customers through code walkthroughs
money_quotes: We've handcuffed ourselves to the virtue of beautiful code by committing to shipping the code with purchase.
I want to show everyone that an extremely high level of care for the internals is not in opposition to the demands of commercial deadlines.
In racing, like other communities of competence, you'll often hear the saying: Slow is smooth. Smooth is fast.

Building for quality, building for readability, and sweating the details is a slow, smooth process. One that gets you to release quicker than the frantic thrashing you hear about from stereotypical crunch sessions.
We want ONCE #1 to have Patek levels of finishing, because we're shipping this product with an exhibition case back.
Every time I do, I usually also find other small quality issues that'll lead to a better product which fewer people will have issues with.
posts.key_insight: Shipping your source code to customers is a powerful forcing function for maintaining high internal quality, and that discipline pays off in faster, smoother delivery.

posts.summary: DHH compiles a comprehensive FAQ addressing the most common questions about 37signals' cloud exit, one year after completing the migration. He systematically dismantles cloud marketing claims — that it reduces headcount, that 'cloud native' saves money, that security is better, and that you need elite engineers to run your own hardware. The company spent $600,000 on Dell servers, already recouped that investment, and projects $7 million in savings over five years. He argues that running your own hardware in 2023 is operationally similar to cloud thanks to containerization, and that the cloud premium is increasingly unjustified as Moore's Law drives down hardware costs without corresponding cloud price reductions.
key_points: The cloud exit required no change in team composition — the claim that cloud reduces operations headcount is a 'central deceit' of cloud marketing
Hardware ownership is operationally similar to cloud: white-glove data center services handle physical setup, containerization handles deployment, and the same redundancy patterns apply
Moore's Law benefits hardware buyers directly but cloud providers rarely pass savings through, making the cloud a worse deal every year
Serverless and 'cloud native' architectures don't save money at scale — buying single-wrapped sugar cubes costs more than buying a pound
The $600,000 hardware investment was already paid off by cloud savings within months, making the five-year $7M projection conservative
money_quotes: This is the central deceit of the cloud marketing, that it's all going to be so much easier that you hardly need anyone to operate it. I've never seen it.
If you need a pound of sugar, you're not going to save any money buying single-wrapped cubes.

The internet got going in 1995. Cloud as the default choice didn't happen until maybe 2015. So for well over twenty years, companies have been operating hardware to run their applications. This isn't some archaic knowledge that's been lost to the ages.
We might not know exactly how the pyramids were built, but we do still know how to connect a Linux machine to the internet.

Moore's Law is hammering the prices and increasing the capabilities of stuff you buy from Dell and others. But it's scarcely moving the needle on the price you have to pay Amazon and others for their managed services.
Buy the baseline, rent the spike.
We bought the marketing pitch. That it was going to be cheaper, easier, and faster. Only the last promise ever really came through for us.
Typically if they have banks or governments on their client list, you ought to look elsewhere, unless you just like setting money on fire.
posts.key_insight: Running your own hardware in the modern era of containerization and white-glove data center services is operationally comparable to cloud, dramatically cheaper at scale, and the cost gap widens every year as Moore's Law benefits hardware buyers but not cloud renters.

posts.summary: DHH showcases five major open source projects 37signals released in 2023: Kamal (deployment tool for their cloud exit), Solid Cache (SSD-based caching replacing Redis), Solid Queue (database-backed job queuing), Strada (native mobile controls for hybrid apps), and Turbo 8 (with morphing). He emphasizes these tools are extracted from real production use at Basecamp and HEY, not speculative development. The post frames open source as 'gifts' funded by paying customers, while arguing that simpler alternatives to industry-standard tools like Kubernetes are both possible and preferable. He highlights that all this was accomplished by just 13 Ruby and 7 native programmers working remotely.
key_points: 37signals extracted five production-tested open source tools in 2023, all born from real product needs rather than speculative development
Solid Cache and Solid Queue represent a thesis that modern SSDs have made Redis unnecessary for caching and queuing, reducing infrastructure complexity
Kamal emerged from their cloud exit, offering simple Docker-based deployment as an alternative to Kubernetes complexity
The Hotwire ecosystem (Strada, Turbo 8) continues pushing server-rendered HTML as a viable alternative to JSON API + SPA architectures
A small team of 20 programmers shipped all this while also building new products like HEY Calendar and ONCE
money_quotes: We don't do speculative software development here. All these tools and frameworks have been forged in the fire of real customer usage, pressure, and demands.
You shouldn't need a PhD in container orchestration to be able to deploy applications.
Kamal is the result of taking two steps back from the industry standards and say 'do we really need all this?'.
It seems that a lot of folks finally realized that the future of the web doesn't have to be built by sending JSON back and forth.
Rails 8 will no longer be BYOQ (Bring-Your-Own-Queue). Jobs will just work out of the box.
The millions of dollars we invest every year in open source gifts has to come from paying customers of these products and services.
We'll try our best to continue to push the web forward to a simpler, easier place for new and existing business to build. A world where the lone developer remains competitive.
posts.key_insight: Real-world production software, not speculative projects, produces the best open source tools — and modern SSDs and simpler architectures can replace the complex infrastructure the industry has normalized.

posts.summary: DHH announces that ONCE #1 (Campfire) ships with zero JavaScript or CSS build steps, serving 68 individual JS files and multiple CSS files directly to browsers via import maps and HTTP/2. He argues that modern browsers now natively support everything developers previously needed transpilers and bundlers for — including CSS nesting and variables — while still achieving a perfect PageSpeed Insights score of 100. He acknowledges bundlers still have their place for large SPAs but celebrates that they're no longer necessary. The post frames this as the culmination of a long journey through webpack and esbuild, with simplicity as the ultimate destination.
key_points: ONCE #1 serves 68 unbundled JavaScript files via import maps with HTTP/2, paying minimal penalty for individual file loading while gaining fine-grained cache expiration
Modern vanilla CSS now supports nesting and variables natively across Chrome, Safari, and Firefox, eliminating the need for preprocessors
The application achieves a perfect 100 PageSpeed Insights score despite serving many small individual files without bundling
View Source once again shows the exact same code developers write in their editors, restoring a fundamental web transparency that was lost
Bundlers and TypeScript remain valid choices for large SPAs — the point is they're no longer mandatory
money_quotes: The dream has come true. It's now possible to build fast, modern web applications without transpiling or bundling either JavaScript or CSS.
Looking at any of those individual files via View Source reveals exactly the same source code that I see in my editor. It's been a long time since JavaScript developers and designers could say that.
To people who haven't seen modern, vanilla CSS, something like this might look like it's from the future. Nesting?! Variables?! Yes. Yes. It's good!
When some JavaScript people see arguments like this, it seems like their brain just turns into a red mush of rage. Like I've insulted their religion and all its holy prophets.
All I'm demonstrating here is that it's no longer necessary to live and work like this. You can choose something different.
I'm a firm believer that complexity ought to be a temporary price we pay for progress. The final destination, for me, has always been simplicity.
Nothing is simpler than sending a plain-text JavaScript or CSS file straight to a browser and watch the magic play.
posts.key_insight: Modern browsers have finally caught up to the point where transpiling and bundling are optional, not mandatory — and shipping plain JavaScript and CSS directly is both simpler and performant enough for production applications.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his year of challenging multiple tech industry orthodoxies simultaneously — cloud computing economics, SaaS dominance, and JavaScript build tooling. He argues that shipping working alternatives is more threatening to paradigm defenders than mere intellectual debate, because concrete proof is harder to dismiss than abstract arguments. He identifies the motte-and-bailey fallacy as a common tactic used by defenders of the status quo, such as shifting from 'the cloud is cheaper' to 'it's about agility' when challenged. He concludes by acknowledging everyone is susceptible to this kind of defensive thinking and advocates for reframing being wrong as learning.
key_points: Shipping working counterexamples is more powerful than intellectual arguments because concrete proof can't be dismissed with rhetoric
Paradigm defenders employ the motte-and-bailey fallacy, advancing bold claims then retreating to weaker but defensible positions when challenged
People who stake their identity on a particular view of reality perceive working alternatives as existential threats
The antidote to paradigm-guarding is reframing 'being wrong' as 'learning more' to overcome natural defensive instincts
money_quotes: If you've staked your identity on a certain view of reality, say, The Cloud Is The Future, or SaaS Will Reign Forever, or Bundling JavaScript Is A Must, it's a threat when someone dares not just argue, but ship an irrefutable counterpoint.
Shipping proof isn't so easily refuted by clever rhetoric or calls to authority.

Even if the shipping thesis is limited in scope, it still has the quality of a black swan against the postulate that "all swans are white". It takes just one flapping bird of the wrong color to undermine a categorical premise.
That's why you see such furious activity around policing the borders of the paradigm. Why the goal posts are constantly moving to ensure no striker can ever be said to have scored.

The bailey is "the cloud is always cheaper" and the motte is "actually it's about agility". You chase a compelling argument because you know it's persuasive, but then retreat to the less compelling argument when it comes under attack.
Changing our minds about things we believe is hard! Even if the evidence appears to warrant such a change.
I try to combat that natural human instinct by forcing myself to see "being wrong" as "learning more".
posts.key_insight: Shipping a working counterexample is the most powerful way to challenge an entrenched paradigm, because concrete proof cannot be deflected by the rhetorical retreats that paradigm guardians rely on.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the best New Year's resolution is to commit to improving competence at work, where you already spend 2,000 hours a year. Rather than forming new habits, he suggests dedicating even 10% of work time to deliberate practice and refinement. Using programming as his primary example, he advocates going beyond merely functional code to make it beautiful through clarity, cohesion, consistency, and conciseness. He emphasizes the pull request as a tool for self-collaboration and obsessive study of one's own work, arguing that time invested in refining technique pays dividends across a decades-long career.
key_points: Experience creates a trap: the more you've done things one way, the higher the perceived cost of trying something new, which is why paradigm shifts often come from outsiders

The standard 'make it work, make it right, make it fast' should be extended with 'make it beautiful' — pursuing aesthetic satisfaction forces you into unfamiliar techniques where real learning happens
Pull requests are primarily a tool for self-collaboration and studying your own work holistically, not just for team code review
Competence compounds over decades — time spent refining technique now pays back across a 20-60 year career, making 'I don't have time' a false economy
The write-revise loop is like gym reps: no single iteration matters, but relentless repetition over a year produces unmistakable results
money_quotes: Once you've been cooked in experience for too long, the cost of trying something new can seem prohibitive. But spend you must, if you aren't to stagnate.
When the bar isn't just a completed task but a delightful solution, you're frequently forced to find novel solutions in the wilderness of unfamiliar techniques.
Make it work, make it right, make it fast. And then, let me add one more: Make it beautiful.
Your career, and even your business, is not a single sprint. It's an intellectual iron man repeated until you're in the ground.
Whatever time you take to refine your technique now will be paid back to you for the next 20-40-60 years of your career.
Some people might see that primarily as a tool of collaboration with others, but I see it as a tool of collaboration with myself first.
The main blocker is your will to see it so.
posts.key_insight: The most effective path to career growth is dedicating a portion of your existing work hours to deliberate practice — not just shipping functional work, but obsessively refining it toward beauty through clarity, cohesion, consistency, and conciseness.

posts.summary: DHH argues that not all software needs continuous evolution, challenging the SaaS model's assumption that constant updates justify ongoing payments. He uses Dropbox as a prime example of software that was perfect in its original form but has been degraded by endless feature additions and upsell attempts. He points to legacy Basecamp versions that still generate millions in revenue despite being feature-frozen for over a decade, proving customers value finished products. The post introduces once.com as an experiment in selling software as a completed product rather than an ever-evolving service, with a clear path to being done.
key_points: The SaaS model creates perverse incentives to add unnecessary features and upsells, degrading originally great products
Dropbox exemplifies perfect software ruined by the compulsion to constantly evolve and juice growth metrics
Legacy Basecamp versions frozen for over a decade still generate millions in revenue, proving demand for finished software
Pre-internet software shipped on physical media was inherently finished, and that constraint had objective value
once.com is designed to sell software as products with a clear path to being DONE, not perpetual services
money_quotes: We've become so used to digital services being malleable that we've confused the possibility of software updates with their necessity.
Some software can simply be finished, and a lot would be better if it were.
I just want to pay for the original premise: All my files synched between all my computers, with a backup in the sky.
But SaaS can't stop, won't stop, ever being just enough.
It solves their problem, and that's enough.
You literally burnt the bits into a hard surface, and they weren't going to magically change after that.
This isn't Amish software. We're not swearing off all advantages of modernity.
We are designing these software products as products, not services.
posts.key_insight: The SaaS model's requirement for perpetual growth has confused the possibility of software updates with their necessity, and many products would be better if they were simply declared finished.

posts.summary: DHH draws on Victor Frankl, Erich Fromm, Dostoevsky, and existentialist philosophy to argue that purpose is essential to human well-being. He contends that modern comfort and the absence of struggle can be psychologically destructive, and that people must actively choose a purpose rather than drift into purposelessness. He connects this to taking pride in one's work, citing Japanese work culture as an example, while cautioning against making work the sole pillar of meaning. The post concludes with a call to deliberately decide that purpose matters and to find at least one way to pursue it.
key_points: Comfort and the absence of responsibility can be as psychologically destructive as deprivation, as Frankl and Fromm observed
Humans are problem-creating creatures who need struggle and tension, not paradise
Purpose is both preventive and healing — which explains why wealthy people often choose to keep working
Work pride is one path to purpose, but life should be diversified across multiple pillars of meaning
Purpose isn't discovered passively — it requires a conscious decision that it matters
money_quotes: Getting everything he wanted while having nobody asking him for anything. The satiation of every desire paired with a relief of every responsibility is a psychological death sentence to many.
Man is less a problem-solving creature than he is a problem-creating one. He needs the tension inherent in some degree of struggle.
Purpose is both a preventive tonic and a healing potion.
Anyone can choose to be needed, and thus choose to be happy.
If you don't diversify your life, if you put it all on work, you have just one fragile pillar to carry that heavy load of a universal purpose.
We're thrown into this world, and we're left with the significant burden of picking a purpose to make sense of it all.
All you need to do to get started with this logotherapy is to find one way, any way. And you do that by deciding it's important.
posts.key_insight: Purpose isn't something you discover — it's something you deliberately choose, and making that decision is itself the first act of meaning-making.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the tech discourse on X/Twitter has improved since Elon Musk's takeover, not because of better management but because politically-focused users migrated to other platforms like Mastodon and Threads. He describes the current vibe as 'vintage,' reminiscent of a decade ago when tech conversations stayed focused on technical topics rather than devolving into political struggle sessions. He acknowledges this is largely a matter of personal impression and algorithmic curation. He concludes that sharing every political opinion in every social circle was never productive and resolves to keep political talk private.
key_points: The old Twitter regime fostered an environment where politics infiltrated every tech discussion, turning them into 'struggle sessions'
The greatest gift of the Musk acquisition was 'The Rift' — politically-motivated users self-selecting onto other platforms
Tech discourse on X now feels like it did a decade ago: disagreements exist but stay focused on technical substance
DHH acknowledges this may have been an inevitable cultural shift that new management merely accelerated
He frames constant political sharing as a failed experiment that didn't advance anyone
money_quotes: There was scarcely a programming or product topic that couldn't be turned into a struggle session on account of some perceived transgression or privilege.
Perhaps the greatest gift it's given to the platform is The Rift.
The tech discourse on X feels like it's been able to recover its attention in large part because so many of those most interested in keeping it on politics went somewhere else.
Talking about tech and programming on X today feels like it did a decade ago.
Talking too much about politics, on X or elsewhere, is indeed a limiting prospective.
Before we were fooled into thinking that sharing every thought about every hot-button political issue in every social circle was going to advance us all somehow. It wasn't, it didn't, and it won't.
posts.key_insight: The best thing about Twitter's ownership change wasn't policy or features — it was driving away the people who turned every tech conversation into a political litmus test.

posts.summary: DHH announces the launch of HEY Calendar, the most-requested feature for their email service HEY.com. The calendar was designed from scratch rather than copying existing grid-based designs, drawing inspiration from paper family calendars. Originally planned as a separate paid product, 37signals decided to bundle it free with the existing HEY subscription at no price increase, betting that removing the calendar objection will push fence-sitters to switch from Gmail or Outlook.
key_points: The lack of an integrated calendar was the single biggest blocker preventing potential customers from switching to HEY
Rather than shipping a checkbox feature, they spent a full year rethinking calendar design from first principles, inspired by paper calendars
They pivoted from a separate paid product to bundling it free with HEY, making it a more compelling all-in-one package
The strategy explicitly targets user inertia — people stuck on Gmail/Outlook need serious force to change habits
money_quotes: If we were going to do a calendar for HEY, it had to be as interesting, inspiring, and innovative as HEY itself.
What should a calendar look like and how should it work if you designed it from a clean sheet of paper?
Which lead us to break the boring grid of a typical computer calendar, and add back a lot of that personality and whimsy that's possible with paper.
Inertia runs most people's lives. If you want to change that trajectory, you better apply some serious force.
We didn't just want to tick a box on a feature chart.
posts.key_insight: Bundling a rethought calendar free with HEY is a deliberate strategy to overcome the inertia that keeps people locked into Gmail and Outlook.

posts.summary: DHH argues that code style matters deeply and that Ruby's expressive syntax is its greatest strength. He pushes back against the idea that enforced, universal linting—like Go's built-in formatter—is desirable, comparing it to Orwell's Newspeak. He views code as a form of literary expression where individual style should flourish, not be flattened. Rails will ship with RuboCop and his own default style preferences, but explicitly as a starting point, not a mandate. The post positions Ruby's multiple ways of expressing the same logic as a feature, not a flaw, contrasting it with Python's 'one obvious way' philosophy.
key_points: Universal, language-enforced code formatting (like Go's gofmt) is too totalitarian and kills expressive style
Code is a form of prose and Ruby's rich syntax makes it uniquely suited for 'writer-programmers'
Linters are valuable when teams choose their own style, not when style is imposed from above
Rails will ship RuboCop with DHH's default preferences as a starting point, not a permanent edict
Ruby's 'many ways to do things' philosophy is the opposite of Python's and is a strength
money_quotes: Imagine every novel written in the same style, Hemingway indistinguishable from Dickens, Tolkien from Rowling. It would be awfully gray to enjoy the English language if there was only a single shade of prose.
The best code to me is indeed its own form of poetry, and style is an integral part of such expression. And in no language more so than Ruby.
There are many ways to do almost everything, and it's why reading and writing Ruby is such a delight to people like me who strive to write code as an author more often than as an engineer.
That to me is the essence of style, it's invariably idiosyncratic. We wouldn't even be talking about style if it was all the same for everyone all the time.
Ruby affords us such rich and expressive syntax that it would be a damn shame if we narrowed all possible paths down to a single, pre-approved trail. Ruby is for writers.
I think they're excellent tools for authors and teams to enforce their style with consistency on their codebases.
posts.key_insight: Code style is a form of literary expression, and Ruby's multiple ways of doing things make it uniquely suited for programmers who think of themselves as writers rather than engineers.

posts.summary: DHH examines the Danish welfare state that American liberals idealize, arguing they overlook the strict compromises underpinning it. Free education is meritocratically gated by grades alone, healthcare is heavily rationed with long waits, and taxes exceed 50% for middle earners. He details Denmark's fiercely restrictive immigration policies, cultural homogeneity, enforced social conformity, and zero tolerance for vagrancy and drug use. He concludes that Denmark's benefits are inseparable from its tall fences—ethnic homogeneity, strict norms, and obligations—and challenges Americans to honestly reckon with whether they'd accept those trade-offs.
key_points: Danish education and healthcare, while free, are heavily rationed and meritocratically gated—no legacy admissions, but also no accommodations for disadvantage
Denmark's welfare state is sustained by extreme tax rates (37-50%+ income tax plus 25% VAT) and strict cultural expectations that everyone contribute through work
Denmark pursues one of Europe's most restrictive immigration policies with broad political support, contrasting sharply with Sweden's open-door approach and its consequences
Danish society enforces rigid conformity: no tolerance for vagrancy, drug use, panhandling, or stay-at-home motherhood, backed by both cultural norms and law enforcement
The trade-off produces a remarkably safe, egalitarian society where children roam freely—but one that is essentially closed to true outsiders
money_quotes: To pine for a society like the Danish purely on the basis of the benefits is delusional. These benefits are fenced by a myriad of compromises and obligations.
Despite the fact that Danes proudly declare themselves to be a social-democratic state, it pursues perceived freeloaders with a savagery worthy of its Viking roots.
The egalitarian ethos only applies to those who contribute and those who conform.
To generalize broadly, Danes are content paying high taxes for high services, as long as the recipients look like them, and all do their outmost to pitch in.
The fruits of Danish society are meticulously guarded by a strong, tall fence that surrounds the garden. In Denmark, this is self-evident, and not even up for serious debate.
Don't you for a second think this unity isn't contingent on the homogeneity of ethnicity, values, norms, and perhaps even religion.

Almost any Dane, with the right will and gumption, could make it in America. Even become American. But almost no American, regardless of their honest intentions to integrate, will ever be able to truly become a Dane in the eyes of the Danish.
posts.key_insight: Denmark's enviable welfare state is inseparable from its strict cultural homogeneity, enforced conformity, and restrictive immigration—trade-offs that most American admirers would never accept.

posts.summary: DHH reports that Apple rejected the HEY Calendar companion app from the App Store, claiming it 'doesn't do anything' because it requires an existing account login. He argues this is hypocritical since major apps like Salesforce, Netflix, JPMorgan, and Google Calendar all require existing accounts. He points out that HEY already fought and won a similar battle with Apple in 2020, securing a specific carve-out for email services, and that HEY Calendar is a free companion to that same service. DHH frames this as evidence of Apple's unchecked monopoly power and expresses hope that the EU's Digital Markets Act and an imminent DOJ antitrust lawsuit will force change.
key_points: Apple rejected HEY Calendar after a 19-day review, claiming it 'doesn't do anything' because it requires an existing account — a standard practice for thousands of apps
HEY already fought and won an App Store battle in 2020, securing a specific carve-out for email services under rule 3.1.3(f)
Apple itself uses the same one-subscription-many-apps strategy with iCloud (Mail, Calendar, Files), making the rejection hypocritical
Apple selectively enforces rules, granting exceptions to mega corporations while inventing new rules for smaller companies
The EU Digital Markets Act and an imminent DOJ antitrust lawsuit represent potential regulatory relief
money_quotes: There is no rule of law with the app stores, except that of the jungle, and Apple is the 800 lbs gorilla, ruling as it sees fit.
After spending 19 days to review our submission, causing us to miss a long-planned January 2nd launch date, Apple rejected our stand-alone free companion app 'because it doesn't do anything'.
Apple has become so emboldened by a decade of free monopoly reign that they don't even feign a superficial adherence to their own rules.
They carve out exceptions left and right to mega corporations they'd rather not anger, then invent entirely new rules that aren't codified anywhere when it suits them.
We're never going to roll over and pay Apple 30% in protection money to be left alone.
The last time the DOJ sued Microsoft in the late 90s/early 2000s, it inflicted a serious wound on Redmond's capacity to capture markets, which gave us the rise of Google, Apple, and others.
You don't get something for nothing.
posts.key_insight: Apple's App Store review process operates as an arbitrary monopoly power, selectively enforcing rules against smaller companies while exempting mega corporations, and only regulatory intervention can restore fair competition.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the shift from desktop to mobile computing has been a regression for developer freedom, as Apple and Google's app stores replaced the open web's free market with arbitrary gatekeeping. He draws parallels between app store control and pre-internet walled gardens like AOL, contending that most people simply don't know what they're missing. He advocates for Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) as the best route around app store monopolies, pointing to 37signals' ONCE product as proof the approach can work. He calls on developers to collectively push PWA technology to close the remaining fidelity gap with native apps, making it his main objective for Rails 8.
key_points: App stores replaced the free market's wisdom with arbitrary bureaucratic gatekeeping, making store approval riskier than building something people want
Most mobile users have never experienced an open software market on their phones, making them easy to frighten with security stories that justify walled gardens
PWA technology is rapidly improving and represents the best practical route around app store monopolies, even if regulatory enforcement falls short
37signals' ONCE product is built entirely around a PWA strategy with no native apps, proving the approach is viable today
The current state of PWAs is like early games on a new console — developers haven't yet maximized what the platform can already do
money_quotes: The most risky part of running a software business is no longer just in making something people want, but making something the app store bureaucrats won't reject.
The wisdom of the market has been supplanted by the idiocy of the reviewer.
Comparing the kindness of kings is myopic in contrast to the distinction between free markets and even the fairest sovereign.
The rules of commerce aren't even rules, they're "guidelines", and they're as vague as they are shifty. A fig leaf covering absolute power, wielded with absolute impunity.
Imagine if your access to the Internet was governed by the same principle. If you could only visit sites pre-approved by AOL or Yahoo?
The internet treats gatekeepers as a bug and finds a way around them. Whether it be censorship or monopolies.
Don't tell me we've maximized what's doable with HTML, JavaScript, and CSS on chips ten orders of magnitude faster in 2024.
posts.key_insight: The open web and PWAs represent developers' best path to reclaiming the freedom that app store gatekeepers took away when computing moved to the pocket.

posts.summary: DHH reports that 37signals achieved exceptional uptime across all major applications in 2023, the same year they moved seven apps out of the cloud. HEY email hit 99.99% uptime despite the migration, and Basecamp 2 achieved 100% uptime for the second consecutive year. He attributes this success to choosing boring, mature technologies and resisting unnecessary complexity. The post serves as evidence for his ongoing argument that most companies don't need cloud infrastructure to achieve reliability.
key_points: All seven major 37signals applications achieved at least 99.99% uptime in 2023 despite migrating off the cloud
Basecamp 2 hit 100% uptime for two consecutive years running on owned hardware
Their stack is deliberately boring: F5s, Linux, KVM, Docker, MySQL, Redis, Elasticsearch, and Rails
Programmers' attraction to complexity is the real enemy of reliability, not self-hosted infrastructure
Only true hyperscalers like Netflix or Google face problems that justify cloud-scale complexity
money_quotes: So hitting the hallowed four nines in a year when everything had to change about how we run HEY is a major accomplishment that makes me proud.
To the question about whether you can ensure reliability and stability outside the cloud, no application makes this point clearer for us than Basecamp 2.
But programmers are attracted to complexity like moths to a flame. The more convoluted the systems diagram, the greater the intellectual masturbation.
Our commitment to resisting that is the key ingredient in this uptime success.
There's nothing fancy about our stack, and very little complexity either. We don't need people with PhDs in Kubernetes or specialists in exotic data stores.
But for the other 99.99% of us? It's a siren song to model our infrastructure in their image.
You don't need the cloud to get good uptimes. You need mature technologies run on redundant hardware with good backups. Same as it ever was.
posts.key_insight: Exceptional uptime comes not from cloud infrastructure or complex systems, but from choosing boring technologies and resisting the programmer instinct toward unnecessary complexity.

posts.summary: DHH declares that the pervasive pessimism of recent years is finally lifting, replaced by a growing cultural optimism. He characterizes the last half-decade as uniquely dark and nihilistic, distinct from earlier periods of cultural upheaval. Without attributing specific causes, he notes the vibe has shifted toward movements like e/acc and a general sense that things are improving. He admits to having been caught in the pessimistic rut himself and urges readers to embrace optimism heading into 2024.
key_points: The last half-decade has been characterized by a uniquely depressing, nihilistic pessimism unlike previous cultural weirdness
Cultural optimism is returning, with movements like e/acc seeping into the broader discourse
Pessimism is self-reinforcing and existentially suffocating, snuffing out the hope needed to move forward
Optimism vs pessimism may be cyclical, with pendulum swings correcting previous excesses
The shift is partly a matter of personal choice — you can force yourself toward optimism
money_quotes: Not dreaming broadly of a better world, singing songs, smoking mild pot, and submitting to flower power weird. No, a pessimistic weird.
An everything-sucks-all-the-time-and-there's-nothing-to-do-about-it weird. A depressing, nihilistic, no-end-in-sight weird.
I think maybe the fever is finally breaking. The dark clouds of pessimism are parting from optimistic rays of light.
And now, like a former smoker, these heavy clouds of endless pessimism just seem extra suffocating.
Snuffing out the hope we need to carry forward.
I don't know where you are right now on the 'it's over' vs 'we're so back' spectrum, but if it isn't near the latter, I think you should just force yourself to try it out in 2024.
It's mostly in your head anyway.
