posts.key_insight: The cultural tide is turning from nihilistic pessimism back toward optimism, and embracing that shift is both a collective trend and a personal choice worth making.

posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals has resubmitted the HEY Calendar app to Apple after it was rejected for 'not doing anything' without a login. He argues this rejection contradicts Apple's own guidelines, which explicitly exempt free companion apps for email services from in-app purchase requirements. To comply with Apple's demand, the team worked through the weekend to add an Apple History feature accessible without login — a pointed, satirical response. DHH frames the situation as Apple selectively enforcing rules against smaller companies while exempting powerful players like Netflix and Salesforce.
key_points: Apple rejected HEY Calendar for requiring login, despite their own guidelines explicitly exempting email service companion apps from this requirement
Thousands of apps on the App Store require login before functioning, making Apple's enforcement selective and inconsistent
37signals added a satirical Apple History calendar feature as malicious compliance to satisfy the 'must do something' requirement
Apple's 85% device share among HEY customers gives them effective monopoly power over the business
Apple strategically times rejections for Friday evenings to minimize controversy coverage
money_quotes: Apple waited until end of business on Friday to send us the formal rejection. It seems they love to play these little games to try to drown any controversy with the cover of a weekend.
We don't roll over that easy, so the team worked through the weekend to prepare a new build to appease the App Store's bullying bureaucrats.

That list of exemptions is now very long, as they attempt to cling on to as many profitable app categories with their shakedowns for the 30% cut as possible, while buying off their strongest adversaries.
Something like 85% of all HEY customers use Apple devices, so not being present on the iPhone with the new HEY Calendar would be a death sentence for that part of the business.
So here's what we did to comply with the 'it has to do something' bullshit.
There are literally thousands of such apps that 'don't do anything' before you login with credentials you already have.
Despite the fact that this requirement exists nowhere in the Apple App Store Guidelines. Despite the fact that the store is chockfull of apps that also doesn't 'do anything' without a login.
posts.key_insight: Apple selectively enforces App Store rules against smaller companies while exempting powerful ones, using its monopoly position as leverage to extract compliance or revenue.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Apple approved the HEY Calendar app for the App Store after a tense submission process where they cleverly included Apple's own history as preview content to satisfy the 'app must do something' requirement. He highlights the existential risk Apple poses to businesses dependent on iOS distribution, noting that 85% of HEY's paying customers use Apple devices. He celebrates their independence as a company that can stand on principle, while pointing to upcoming antitrust action from the DOJ and Europe's Digital Markets Act as longer-term solutions. The post also previews their ONCE product line built on PWA technology to bypass app store gatekeeping entirely.
key_points: Apple's App Store approval process represents an existential threat to businesses where most paying customers use iPhones
Being a fully independent company without investors or board members enables 37signals to take principled stands others can't afford
Regulatory relief is coming from both the US DOJ antitrust case and Europe's Digital Markets Act mandating sideloading and alternative app stores
HEY is positioning its ONCE product line around PWA technology specifically to route around app store gatekeeping
Despite competing against free giants like Gmail and iCloud, HEY only asks to serve the niche of users willing to pay for email and calendar
money_quotes: About 85% of paying customers for HEY use Macs and iPhones, so if Apple denied us the ability to distribute our app, we'd be dead.
With stakes this high, it's no surprise that scarcely anyone dares to fight them. Most, wisely, just lower their head, stay quiet, and grit their teeth.
There's no board, no MBAs, no venture capitalists in the background running the cost-benefit analysis. Just two looney tunes on banjo and bass who love making great software.
All we're asking is to be allowed to sell the niche product that is a paid email and calendar system to people who really care.
There'll always be a legion of Apple fans to defend every abusive tactic the company employs, but there absolutely is also a huge tribe of developers who recognize monopoly bullshit when they see it.
posts.key_insight: Apple's App Store gatekeeping poses an existential threat to independent software businesses, and the only durable solutions are regulatory intervention or building on open web technologies that bypass the gatekeepers entirely.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Google Cloud's decision to eliminate egress fees for customers migrating away from GCP. He notes that 37signals faces $300,000-$400,000 in fees just to leave AWS S3, making Google's move significant. He's particularly surprised that the cloud exit discourse, which only gained momentum in 2023, is already influencing a major hyperscaler. While skeptical of Google's altruism, he offers two theories: preempting EU regulation and a competitive strategy to steal marketshare from AWS and Azure. Regardless of motive, he views the move as unambiguously good for cloud competition and cloud exits alike.
key_points: Major cloud providers charge absurd egress fees that effectively trap customers — 37signals faces $300k-$400k just to leave AWS S3
Google Cloud eliminated all data transfer fees for customers migrating away, effective immediately and globally
The cloud exit movement that gained steam in 2023 is already influencing hyperscaler pricing decisions
Google's likely motivations are preempting EU anti-competitive regulation and gaining competitive advantage as the distant third-place cloud provider
Regardless of Google's motives, eliminating egress fees improves both cloud competition and the viability of leaving the cloud entirely
money_quotes: The absurd egress fees charged by major hyperscalers have been one of the biggest impediments for companies to leave the cloud.
The price for taking our data elsewhere is in the crazy-land region of $300,000-$400,000!
I wouldn't have guessed that the cloud exit discourse, which only really gained steam in 2023, would already be influencing one of the three major hyperscalers!
I don't know how many people will actually buy the idea that Google is just doing this out of the goodness of their heart.
Google is a distant third in the cloud wars, and they stand to gain far more from goading competitors into following suit than they'll lose on their end.
But who cares! I commend Google for doing this, regardless of whatever ulterior motives or self-interest is guiding it.
Cutting the egress racket absolutely will improve competition in the cloud and make it easier for companies to leave it entirely.
posts.key_insight: Cloud egress fees function as a data hostage racket, and Google's elimination of them — whatever the motive — validates the cloud exit movement and pressures AWS and Azure to follow suit.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the ongoing wave of tech layoffs in early 2024, drawing parallels to the dot-com bust of the early 2000s. He shares personal experiences of being fired and having to fire others, acknowledging the pain on both sides. He argues that layoffs, while painful, are a necessary corrective mechanism for businesses that over-hired. Using Denmark's flexible labor market as a model contrasted with Spain's rigid employment laws, he makes the case that easier firing actually leads to more hiring and healthier businesses. He concludes optimistically that the current wave of layoffs may seed the ground for the next leap forward in tech.
key_points: A quarter million tech workers were laid off in 2023, with major cuts continuing into early 2024 at Google, Twitch, Discord, and Unity
Layoffs are a painful but necessary corrective when businesses have hired ahead of the revenue needed to support their workforce
Denmark's flexible labor market, which makes it easy to lay off workers, actually encourages more hiring and healthier businesses compared to rigid systems like Spain's
The alternative to allowing layoffs — as illustrated by the Spanish car dealership example — is often total business failure where everyone loses their jobs
Historical precedent suggests that periods of mass layoffs can seed the ground for the next wave of innovation
money_quotes: There are no good firings, just degrees of awful ones.
The last time I remember seeing such brutal cuts, so broadly was after the dot-com bubble in the early 2000s.
It's relatively easy to lay off people, which makes companies less afraid of hiring when the going is good.
I think the Danes have correctly realized that however much it sucks when companies lay off workers, you can't magically fix that without consequences that are even worse.
It's the difference between what we wish was true (nobody ever has to get fired!) and what reality demands (sometimes firings are necessary).
Either way, if history is any guide, this forest fire of layoffs that seems to continue this year in tech may just seed the ground for the next big leap forward.
posts.key_insight: Layoffs are painful but necessary market corrections, and labor markets that allow easier firing paradoxically create healthier hiring and more resilient businesses.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates the success of Rails World 2023 in Amsterdam and previews the 2024 edition in Toronto, framing it within a broader narrative of Rails renaissance. He highlights Rails 8's upcoming features like Solid Queue, praises Shopify's outsized contributions to both Ruby and Rails ecosystems, and argues that a cultural 'vibe shift' away from pandemic-era divisiveness is enabling renewed collaboration. The post positions Rails as perfectly suited for a new era of capital-constrained startups that need productive, integrated tools, and connects web platform advances like PWAs and #nobuild to Rails' full-stack philosophy.
key_points: Rails World 2024 in Toronto will nearly double capacity, with Rails 8 features like Solid Queue becoming production defaults
Shopify is positioned as the 'patron saint' of the Ruby on Rails ecosystem through YJIT, Rails Core members, and Foundation support
The Rails Foundation keeps conference tickets affordable by operating at a loss, subsidized by founding member contributions
Tech layoffs will seed a new generation of capital-constrained startups that favor productive, integrated frameworks like Rails over scattered toolchains
DHH frames a cultural recovery from pandemic-era divisiveness as enabling renewed community collaboration and optimism
money_quotes: Shopify is more than just the first founding member of The Rails Foundation, though, they're the damn patron saint of the whole Ruby on Rails ecosystem at this point.
The pendulum is swinging in the direction of Ruby on Rails. Technically, culturally, and economically.
More and more programmers are just getting tired of the scattered, complicated, and unintegrated approach to web development that's been the norm for a while now.
When money isn't free, you can't just throw warm bodies at the problem. You have to work with better, simpler, more productive tools.
We need the web more than ever, and the web hasn't been this exciting in a very long time.
We went through some awful years during the pandemic that made everyone a little crazy, and we didn't have the in-person gatherings to renew social connections, so bad ideas were allowed to fester.
2024 is the year optimism makes a big comeback. The Rails Renaissance is fueled by this optimism.
posts.key_insight: A convergence of web platform advances, post-pandemic cultural healing, and capital-constrained startup economics is creating the ideal conditions for a Rails renaissance built on productive, integrated full-stack development.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the real complexity in modern web services comes not from raw computational demands but from multi-tenancy—serving millions of users from a single system. He contends that modern hardware is powerful enough that any single organization could run on a single server with SQLite, and that the industry's SaaS multi-tenancy paradigm creates unnecessary operational complexity. This insight underpins 37signals' ONCE product line, which sells software as single-tenant products rather than renting them as services. He frames this as a pendulum swing back toward distributed, single-tenant architectures and expresses excitement about the conceptual simplifications it could unlock.
key_points: Modern computers are so fast that virtually no single organization can overwhelm a web service on one server—multi-tenancy is the source of all scaling complexity
SQLite's resurgence for non-embedded use cases reflects a growing interest in single-tenant architectures that sidestep multi-tenancy challenges
37signals' ONCE product line bets on selling software as single-tenant products you run yourself, rather than renting multi-tenant SaaS
The cloud's original promise to make scaling easy never materialized—large multi-tenant systems still require serious operations teams
The shift between centralized services and distributed clients is a recurring pendulum in tech, and revisiting old paradigms is how progress happens
money_quotes: Computers have gotten so ridiculously fast that there is scarcely any organization in the world that can overwhelm a web-based information system running on a single server.
All the complexity and sophistication required to run web services today stem from multi-tenancy.
No multi-tenancy, no intermingling of data, much easier scaling dynamics.
Part of the original cloud pitch was that it would become easy, but that never panned out.
There's no major web-based information system with millions of users that's "easy" to run.
That's what's so alluring about single-tenancy, sqlite, and turning services back into products. The possibility of sidestepping much of this complexity for both development and operations.
Our beloved industry consists of a handful of pendulums that continuously swing back and forth, and distributed clients vs centralized services is one of the biggest.
Progress usually requires revisiting your priors.
posts.key_insight: The vast majority of web service complexity exists to serve multi-tenancy, and single-tenant architectures running on modern hardware could eliminate most of that burden by turning services back into products.

posts.summary: DHH excoriates Apple for its bad-faith compliance with the Epic v. Apple court ruling that required allowing developers to link to their own websites for purchases. Despite losing at the Supreme Court, Apple immediately unveiled terms that impose a 27% commission on external link purchases, mandatory financial reporting, audit rights, and the threat of App Store expulsion. DHH argues this response is more predatory than organized crime and represents unprecedented arrogance from a tech monopolist.
key_points: Apple lost its Supreme Court appeal in the Epic case but immediately rolled out compliance terms designed to nullify the ruling's intent
The new terms impose a 27% commission on external link purchases, financial reporting requirements, audit rights, and expulsion threats
Apple's public messaging frames these punitive terms as benevolent, which DHH sees as deeply cynical
DHH argues this represents the most arrogant behavior from a tech monopolist in computing history
money_quotes: Apple revealed an elaborate bad-faith compliance with the ruling.
Not even the mob is that greedy, capricious, or meticulous in its shakedown efforts!

Apple can't stop padding itself on the back for just how awesome and benevolent they are, as they threaten to ram these poisonous terms down the throat of anyone even thinking about taking advantage of the judge's ruling.
I don't believe we've ever seen this level of arrogance from a major tech monopolist in the history of the computing industry.
And that's saying something, because we've had to contend with a few!
posts.key_insight: Apple's malicious compliance with the Epic ruling — imposing a 27% commission and onerous reporting requirements on external links — reveals a monopolist that would rather subvert a court order than cede any control over its ecosystem.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Apple is repeating Microsoft's monopolistic mistakes from the late 1990s and early 2000s, particularly in how it wields App Store power and pursues vindictive legal action against Epic Games. He traces the irony that Apple's modern existence was saved by Microsoft's $150M investment, made under DOJ pressure to prop up a competitor. Microsoft's aggressive monopoly tactics turned an entire generation of developers against them, a debt that came due when Windows Phone failed because no developer would help. DHH warns that Apple is now the developer community's enemy #1, having squandered the goodwill it earned as the Unix-embracing underdog, and will eventually pay the same price Microsoft did.
key_points: Apple owes its modern existence to Microsoft's DOJ-pressured $150M investment in 1997, making its current monopoly behavior deeply ironic
Microsoft's brutal monopoly tactics poisoned developer relations for a generation, and the cost materialized when Windows Phone failed without developer support
Apple has swapped positions with Microsoft in developer sentiment — from beloved underdog to monopoly villain
Vindictive moves like pursuing Epic for $73M in legal fees reveal Apple's short-sighted prioritization of dominance over developer goodwill
The price of alienating developers lags behind the current platform but inevitably comes due on the next one
money_quotes: Apple is protecting its App Store racket with the same kind of indignant entitlement that characterized Microsoft during its darkest monopoly days.
Apple owes its entire modern existence to the fact that the DOJ was breathing down Microsoft's neck in the late 90s.
Microsoft's brutish tactics also managed to turn an entire generation of developers against them. And the bill for that didn't come due until Windows Phone.
Nobody, and I mean nobody, wanted to lift a finger to help Microsoft gain a foothold in mobile.
Microsoft is no longer developer's enemy #1, Apple is.
Learn that you can win the battle, say, against Epic, and end up losing the war for the hearts and minds of developers.
The price for that loss lags beyond the current platform, it'll eventually come due, and they'll rue the day they chose this wretched path.
posts.key_insight: Apple is repeating Microsoft's monopoly playbook and will pay the same generational price in developer alienation — you can win every battle against competitors and still lose the war for the next platform.

posts.summary: DHH announces Campfire as the first product in the ONCE line — installable, self-hosted chat software sold for a one-time price of $299. He argues that while SaaS makes sense for complex, evolving products like Basecamp and HEY, many tools like team chat don't need to be services and organizations are overpaying dramatically for Slack. Campfire offers dead-simple chat with full data ownership, no user metrics reporting, and source code included. The product uses PWAs for mobile to bypass Apple and Google app store restrictions and fees.
key_points: SaaS is great for complex evolving software but unnecessary for simple tools like team chat, where organizations pay thousands for basics
Campfire sells for a one-time $299 price with source code, bug fixes for three years, and full data sovereignty on your own server
Self-hosting means full GDPR compliance and suitability for sensitive data like children's or patients' records
PWAs replace native mobile apps, routing around Apple and Google app store fees and bureaucracy
The product deliberately excludes enterprise complexity in favor of doing the basics really well
money_quotes: SaaS has been ruling the world of web-based software for two decades now, and made a lot of people wealthy in the process.
There's also a lot of SaaS that does not need to be a service. That could just as well be a simple product. A finished product, even.
It's simply ridiculous to hear people paying hundreds of dollars a month, if not thousands, if not TENS OF THOUSANDS, for a system like Slack.
Dead-simple chat. Without all the endless configuration screens, without all the enterprisey compromises. Just the basics, done really well.
YOU OWN IT ALL! All your data, both the messages and the file uploads, live on YOUR server. We never see any of it.
This means routing around the damage Apple and Google are causing app makers and consumers with their draconian app store bureaucracies.
posts.key_insight: Much of the software sold as SaaS subscriptions doesn't need to be a service — simple tools like team chat can and should be sold as installable, self-hosted products at a fraction of the cost.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the primary benefit of having senior employees is the managerial bandwidth they free up by operating independently. Juniors require frequent feedback and direction, which consumes scarce managerial attention. Senior employees should be 'managers of one' who take rough direction and deliver results without hand-holding. The ideal organization mixes just enough junior talent to prepare for the future with enough senior talent to handle the present, while keeping managerial bandwidth deliberately scarce to prevent process churn.
key_points: Juniors are judged on effort and require frequent feedback; seniors are judged on outcomes and should operate independently
Excess managerial bandwidth is dangerous — it leads to constant churning of plans and processes
Senior employees who consume more bandwidth than they contribute back are failing at their role
Organizations should use 'moonlighting managers' who'd rather be building to naturally constrain management overhead
The right mix of junior and senior talent should yield just enough productive bandwidth — not too much, not too little
money_quotes: Juniors are judged on effort, seniors are judged on outcomes.
There's nothing more dangerous than excess managerial bandwidth.
If you want just enough management, but absolutely no more, hire managers who'd rather be building.
The best of them all encompass the ideal of being managers of one.
Capable to finding the right path independently to a destination worth heading. Interested in taking fuzzy problems and turning them into crisp solutions.
You should be wary of adding more lest you end up with too much.
The goal of the organization is to mix just enough junior talent that it's preparing for the future, with enough of the seniority needed to tend to the present.
Simple. But not easy.
posts.key_insight: Senior employees should be net producers of organizational bandwidth rather than consumers of it, and excess managerial capacity is more dangerous than too little.

posts.summary: DHH pushes back against the narrative that tech layoffs are driven by corporate greed, arguing that greed is a constant force in capitalism rather than a seasonal affliction. He invokes Adam Smith's concept of self-interest as the engine of economic progress and argues that uncertainty, not malevolence, better explains why companies over-hired and then laid people off. He acknowledges layoffs are painful but contends that blaming greed is a coping mechanism that refuses to accept the fundamental unpredictability of markets. He reframes greed as 'enlightened self-interest' — a permanent feature of capitalism that drives aggregate progress despite individual hardship.
key_points: Greed is a constant in capitalism, not a seasonal affliction — it can't explain why layoffs happen at specific times
A truly greedy executive would have avoided over-hiring in the first place, making greed a poor explanation for the current layoff wave
The real explanation is radical uncertainty: nobody — economists, CEOs, analysts — can predict what happens next during periods of turmoil
Blaming greed is a coping mechanism because it assigns a villain, while accepting uncertainty requires accepting the fallibility of everyone involved
Self-interest, properly understood, is the engine that produces economic progress even if it's indifferent to individual hardship
money_quotes: The idea that greed is this seasonal affliction that only seem to infect companies and executives occasionally is not a good explanation for these layoffs or, well, anything at all.

The most greedy executive would have been shrewd enough to foresee all this, never hired too many people in the first place, and booked all the savings from not having to pay out severance as profits.
Nobody, not you, not highly-paid executives, not stock analysts, have a perfect crystal ball to predict tomorrow.
At large scales of coordination, greed is a constant. It doesn't wax or wane, and it absolutely does not sleep.
No matter how much money you pay a professional CEO, they'll make bad bets, like hiring too many people in anticipation of growth extrapolations based on yesterday's weather.
While that's a good explanation for choices that turn out poorly, it's a terrible coping mechanism for grieving layoffs. Because it accepts the humanity of everyone involved.
posts.key_insight: Tech layoffs aren't explained by greed — which is a constant in capitalism — but by the fundamental inability of anyone to predict the future during turbulent times.

posts.summary: DHH argues that first-time entrepreneurs overvalue exclusive access to business advisors, investors, and board members, believing these connections hold secret knowledge. In reality, every worthwhile business insight has already been shared publicly through books, podcasts, blogs, and social media. He draws on his own experience as both a prolific advice-sharer and as someone who had Jeff Bezos as an equity partner in Basecamp. Despite having direct access to Bezos, DHH found that Jeff shared the same great ideas publicly with everyone. The real insight Bezos offered was that entrepreneurs need confidence more than advice, and that nobody knows your business better than you do.
key_points: All valuable general business knowledge is already publicly available — great thinkers can't help but share their best ideas
Having elite investors or advisors on your board doesn't grant access to secret knowledge unavailable elsewhere
DHH's experience with Jeff Bezos as an equity partner confirmed that Bezos shared the same insights publicly that he shared privately
The scarcest thing entrepreneurs need is confidence in their own judgment, not more advice
Entrepreneurs building something novel will always know their own business better than any outside advisor
money_quotes: Everything worth knowing is already public.
You couldn't make most business advisors shut up about their best ideas if you paid them!
A million lifetimes of entrepreneurial learnings have already been compressed for you by people eager to share.
All the insights I can offer have already been offered, usually in a better-edited form than any on-the-fly account I could produce on the spot.
What entrepreneurs need most is confidence, not advice.
Nobody will know your business better than you do, if you're attempting anything novel.
The magic is in your own fingers.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable thing an entrepreneur can develop is confidence in their own judgment, because all the business advice worth having is already freely available to everyone.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Apple's response to the EU's Digital Markets Act is designed to sabotage alternative app stores through punitive fees rather than comply in good faith. He calculates that Meta would owe Apple $135 million per year just for Instagram if distributed through a competing app store, with no fee for staying in Apple's store exclusively. He characterizes the 'Core Technology Fee' as a poison pill designed to ensure no large developer ever leaves Apple's ecosystem. DHH contends this is the most blatant extortion attempt ever committed to public policy by a technology company, and hopes the EU will push back.
key_points: Apple's Core Technology Fee would cost Meta $135M/year per major app just to distribute through alternative stores, with no fee for staying in Apple's App Store
The fee structure is a poison pill designed to ensure alternative app stores never attract major apps and therefore never gain traction
Apple's DMA compliance appears designed to maintain the status quo while technically satisfying the letter of the law
Without big apps in alternative stores, the EU's entire effort to create digital market competition will fail
money_quotes: At first glance, it could seem like Apple actually attempted some semblance of good faith compliance with the Digital Markets Act that goes into effect March 7 in the EU. But once you start peeling the onion, you realize it's stuffed with poison pills so toxic you can scarcely believe Apple's chutzpah.
Holy shakedown, batman! That might be the most blatant extortion attempt ever committed to public policy by any technology company ever.
You add a hundred million here and a hundred million there, and suddenly you're talking about real money!
Apple doesn't want Meta, or anyone, to actually use these alternative app stores. They want everything to stay exactly as it is, so they can continue with the rake undisturbed.
This poison pill is therefore explicitly designed to ensure that no second-party app store ever takes off.
If you interrupt our toll-booth operation, we'll make you regret it, and we'll make you pay. Don't resist, just let it be.
posts.key_insight: Apple's Core Technology Fee is not a compliance measure but an economic weapon designed to make alternative app stores unviable by making it ruinously expensive for any major developer to leave Apple's store.

posts.summary: DHH argues that smartphones are computers and owners should have the fundamental right to install any software they choose without platform gatekeepers' permission. He frames the App Store dispute as a question of whether the iPhone is a computer or an appliance, contending that most people view their $1,000 smartphone as a real computer. He points to the Mac as Apple's own proof that open software installation is compatible with a safe, secure platform. He warns that Apple is signaling a future where all software requires app store approval, and calls for enshrining a 'Right to Compute' before that freedom disappears.
key_points: The App Store dispute hinges on whether an iPhone is a computer (with rights to install software) or an appliance (with restricted access)
Apple's own Mac platform proves that open software installation is compatible with security and stability
Apple faces a paradox: arguing iPhones need app store gatekeeping undermines the reputation of their open Mac platform
Mac developers are right to worry that Apple is moving toward requiring app store approval for all software on all platforms
The right to install software freely should be enshrined now, while people still remember what computing freedom looks like
money_quotes: Is the iPhone a computer or not? If it's a computer, we ought to have the right to compute.
They want to escape from the freedom of owning a computer.
After paying $1,000 for this computer, they should be able to install whatever software they should so choose. Without having to ask Apple or Google for permission.

They should be able to have a direct relationship with companies like Adobe or Epic or Netflix or 37signals, which isn't intermediated by some toll-booth operator telling them what's allowed or asking for an outrageous cut.
Now is the time to enshrine this right to compute. While people still remember what it's like to be free to install software of their own choice.
In order to advance their scarecrow case for an iPhone you can install your own software on, they have to sully the reputation of their other computing platform.
It's a strategy tax applied as reputational harm.
posts.key_insight: If smartphones are computers—and they are—then owners deserve the same freedom to install any software they choose, and Apple's own Mac proves this freedom is compatible with security.

posts.summary: DHH argues that 2023 marked a dramatic reversal for DEI in corporate America, following its peak dominance from 2020-2022. He cites the Supreme Court's ruling against race-based college admissions, DEI department cuts across tech, and an EEOC commissioner's public clarification that race-based hiring is illegal under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act. He contends that the 'antiracist discrimination' philosophy is fundamentally incompatible with civil rights law, and predicts lawsuits targeting companies that engaged in race-conscious hiring. He concludes that corporate legal departments, not DEI offices, will now drive hiring practices.
key_points: The EEOC has clarified that using race as even 'part of the equation' in hiring decisions violates Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964
S&P 100 companies added 300,000 jobs in 2021, with 94% going to people of color — which DHH calls plainly illegal under civil rights law
The 'antiracist discrimination' philosophy advocated by Ibram X. Kendi is fundamentally incompatible with colorblind hiring law
Corporate legal departments will replace DEI bureaucracies as the drivers of hiring practice going forward
A flood of lawsuits targeting documented race-conscious hiring patterns is likely coming
money_quotes: After the ideology completely conquered both the corporate and cultural world from 2020-2022, the reversal last year was astounding.
You can't hire people on the basis of race, not even if that consideration is only 'part of the equation'. That's exactly as it should be.

These aspirations are not compatible. You can't both follow the law, and not consider race in your hiring decisions, while somehow actively correcting for real or perceived disparities in the racial makeup of a company during that same process.

The Civil Rights Act is from 1964. It was passed to address the explicit and discriminatory use of race in hiring practices. Not to apply discrimination in the opposite direction to get desirable statistical outcomes.
During 2020-2022, this colorblind principle was sacrificed on the new altar of 'antiracist discrimination'.

In 2021, the S&P 100 added 300,000 jobs. 94% of these positions went to people of color, 6% to white applicants. This was antiracism in action. And, according to the Civil Rights Act of 1964, plainly illegal.
With corporate DEI bureaucracies getting dismantled, I think it'll be corporate legal departments who drive the agenda for hiring practices going forward.
posts.key_insight: Race-conscious 'antiracist discrimination' in corporate hiring is legally incompatible with Title VII, and the post-2023 legal landscape will force companies to abandon DEI-driven hiring in favor of colorblind compliance.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Campfire, 37signals' group chat product, is now available for purchase under the ONCE brand. The product represents a deliberate challenge to the SaaS model that 37signals helped popularize with Basecamp in 2004. Priced at a one-time $299 payment with no subscription, Campfire is positioned as a 99.9% cheaper alternative to Slack for companies that just need basic chat. DHH argues that company chat has become a commodity but is still priced like a luxury, and that technological progress now makes installable web software viable again.
key_points: Campfire launches under the ONCE brand as installable, one-time-purchase web software — a deliberate counter to the SaaS model 37signals helped create
The 3-year total cost of ownership for 500 users is $479 (Campfire + Hetzner hosting) versus $270,000 for Slack Business+, a 99.9% cost reduction
Company chat has become a commodity thanks to Slack and Teams but remains priced like a novel luxury
SaaS still makes sense for complex systems like email (HEY), but simpler tools like chat don't need the service model
You need extreme price disruption, not incremental savings, to unseat entrenched software incumbents
money_quotes: We're trying to validate an entirely new-old way of selling software.
Almost every major web system sold in the last 15-20 years has been Software as a Service. We helped kickstart that craze with Basecamp back in 2004. And my lord has it eaten the world.
ONCE is the argument that thanks to technological progress, it's now both viable and desirable to make software products again – rather than services.
Company chat has become a commodity, thanks to Slack and Teams. But it's still priced like a novel luxury.
The ONCE solution is literally 99.9% cheaper. It's crazy.
You're not going to unseat Slack being 10% cheaper. But 99.9% cheaper? Yeah, that might get people's attention!
Of course you're getting 'more' with an enterprise product like Slack. There's screen after screen of enterprisey settings.
posts.key_insight: Software that has become a commodity shouldn't be sold as a luxury service — one-time purchases at radically lower prices can disrupt entrenched SaaS incumbents by making the cost difference too dramatic to ignore.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the tech industry's 'merchants of complexity' profit by convincing people that running their own servers is too dangerous, when in reality it's never been easier or more secure. He points to Docker and hardened base images as evidence that baseline computing security has dramatically improved. The post connects to 37signals' cloud exit and their ONCE product line, making the case that self-hosting is a viable alternative to paying recurring cloud fees. He urges readers to reject the fear, uncertainty, and doubt spread by those who profit from complexity, and to dare to own their own infrastructure and software.
key_points: The tech industry profits by making simple problems appear dangerously complex, scaring people away from self-hosting
Docker and widely-used base images have made secure server deployment trivial compared to the manual hardening of the past
Cloud providers have a financial incentive to maintain FUD about self-hosting, even as the underlying technology has gotten dramatically easier
Not everything needs to be a recurring SaaS subscription — owning infrastructure and software is a legitimate alternative
We're in a golden age of secure baseline computing but the industry refuses to celebrate it because there's no profit in admitting progress
money_quotes: The merchants of complexity thrive when they can scare you into believing that even the simplest things are too dangerous to even attempt by yourself these days.
It's incredibly profitable to dress up what used to be simple problems into complicated solutions, if you can somehow convince the punter that it's necessary.
It's never been easier to confidently connect a computer to the internet, and have it serve up a web app securely on port 443.
This is a golden age of secure, baseline computing. We should be celebrating!
But where's the profit in admitting to such progress?
You can run your own server. You can connect it to the internet. You can own it.
Not everything needs to be a service, not everyone wants a monthly, recurring cost on a million line items to run their business or their life.
Dare to embrace the underlying progress of containers and security. Dare to question the financial equation of renting vs buying. Dare to connect a server to the internet.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry manufactures fear about self-hosting to sell cloud services, but containerization and modern security have made running your own servers easier and safer than ever.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Basecamp's 20th anniversary, reflecting on how the product launched in 2004 to solve the simple problem of project collaboration over email. He argues that Basecamp's longevity is rare among independent software companies and that the product is more relevant than ever as companies suffer from tool sprawl across too many SaaS products. DHH contrasts Basecamp's all-in-one simplicity with the modern mess of fragmented collaboration tools. He credits the company's success to out-sharing and out-teaching competitors rather than spending venture capital on ads, and expresses confidence that Basecamp will endure for decades more.
key_points: Basecamp launched February 5, 2004 with just messages, todo lists, and milestones — solving a real problem immediately
Most independent software companies stumble, get acquired, or become irrelevant over 20 years — Basecamp's longevity is exceptional
Modern companies suffer from split-brain existence with data scattered across too many SaaS tools, making Basecamp's all-in-one approach more relevant than ever
37signals built its brand by out-sharing and out-teaching rather than outspending competitors with venture capital
Basecamp solves a perpetual problem — human collaboration — which gives it evergreen relevance
money_quotes: That kind of longevity is rare on the internet. It's even more rare amongst independent software makers.
Technological progress has brought many blessings, but boy has it also brought new hassles.
Too many companies now live a split-brain existence with data and collaboration scattered over half a dozen tools.
That simplicity sadly seems like somewhat of a secret cheat code these days.
We're never going to have hundreds of millions of dollars in venture capital to light on fire with targeted ads or a huge sales force.
We damn well have the energy, the enthusiasm, and, most importantly, the stamina to keep teaching you the interesting lessons we learn along our way.
Basecamp is the kind of ever-green product that solves a perpetual problem, which will be needed as long as humans need to collaborate with each other.
posts.key_insight: Building a simple product that solves a real, perpetual problem — and sustaining it through teaching rather than spending — is a more durable business strategy than chasing venture-funded growth.

posts.summary: DHH argues that AI doesn't just need to match human performance to replace humans — it needs to be virtually perfect, because people hold robots to a fundamentally different psychological standard. He walks through self-driving cars, medical diagnosis, and customer service to show that even when AI is statistically better than humans, the remaining errors create intolerable legal and reputational consequences. A self-driving car that's 50% better than humans still produces thousands of deaths attributable to a single company. The core problem isn't technical but psychological: we forgive human mistakes in ways we never will for machines.
key_points: AI being 'as good as a human' isn't sufficient — robots are held to a near-perfection standard that humans never face
Even dramatic statistical improvements (50% fewer deaths) become unacceptable when a single company bears legal responsibility for the remainder
The malpractice and liability math makes AI adoption in medicine and driving potentially impossible at current error rates
Customer service AI may need error rates 100x lower than human agents before the psychology works in its favor
Human fallibility carries a built-in advantage: we are psychologically wired to forgive other humans but not machines
money_quotes: Being as good as a human isn't good enough for a robot. They need to be computer good. That is, virtually perfect.
The math is easy, the human element is hard, and the legal ramifications perhaps impossible.
That's also the kind of effective-altruism math that just doesn't fly in The Real World.
How many misdiagnosed patients could Healthcare AI handle before the malpractice lawsuits sink the business? A dozen? A hundred?
Maybe the customer service robot actually has to get to 0.01% error rate before it'll beat a human that gets it wrong 100x more often.

We humans can look at two situations where answer A is clearly better than answer B on a litany of objective measures, and then we'll still go with B, because it's psychologically compatible with our mental constitution.
Maybe fallible humans have an inherent advantage over AI by being forgivable?
posts.key_insight: AI doesn't just face a technical bar to replacing humans — it faces a psychological one, because we forgive human error in ways we never will for machines.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates REWORK reaching one million copies sold, 14 years after publication. He reflects on the book's unconventional approach: 87 short essays designed to be read in an afternoon, with the manuscript literally cut in half during editing. The publisher balked at the thin page count, so they added drawings, increased font size, and adjusted margins rather than padding the content. DHH attributes the book's enduring success to its brevity, directness, and rejection of typical business book bloat.
key_points: REWORK sold over 200,000 copies in its first two years and has now passed one million copies worldwide
The manuscript was cut in half during final editing — the authors refused to pad content and instead used design tricks to fill pages
The book's lasting appeal comes from its rejection of jargon, filler, and conventional business book format
DHH frames REWORK as an antidote to corporate culture and inflated business speak
money_quotes: We didn't just kill our darlings, we went on a murder spree.
No jargon, no filler, no bullshit.
It's a business book for people who don't have the patience to read a handful of ideas stretched to fit 300 pages.
We increased the font size, we shrunk the margins, and we bumped the line spacing. Oh, and we added a picture for every essay.
REWORK was and is an antidote to the poison of not only ASAP, but business as usual.
An uppercut to pompous MBA heads droning on and on about 'synergies' or some shit like that.
When everything is high priority, nothing is.
posts.key_insight: Brevity and directness beat conventional padding — in business books and in business itself — which is why a 14-year-old book of short essays keeps selling.

posts.summary: DHH reports that Campfire, sold under the new ONCE installable software model, has already generated over $250,000 in sales in its first week. The installable model has worked smoother than expected, delivering a SaaS-like experience without the recurring subscription. However, the biggest challenge is marketing: the product is so much cheaper than competitors that prospective customers assume there's a catch. Drawing on Rory Sutherland's insight about how budget airlines had to emphasize what they removed to seem credible, DHH argues ONCE needs a similar story about what Campfire deliberately lacks to make the value proposition believable.
key_points: Campfire sold over $250,000 in its first week under the ONCE installable model, replacing systems costing tens of thousands per year
The installable software model has been far smoother than anticipated, with almost no deployment headaches
The ONCE model delivers SaaS-quality experience without recurring subscription costs — 'SaaS without the aaS'
The core marketing challenge is that the product is so cheap customers suspect a gimmick rather than trusting the value
Like budget airlines emphasizing removed amenities to justify low prices, ONCE needs a credible story about what Campfire deliberately omits
money_quotes: People are using it to replace existing systems costing tens of thousands of dollars per year.
Over-delivering on an under-promise.
This has essentially allowed us to have our cake and eat it too. For Campfire to deliver a very SaaS-like experience, but without the aaS.
A lot of prospective customers look at this setup and say 'it's too good to be true, where's the catch?'.
When you say stuff like 'it's 99.9% cheaper than Slack on a 500-seat account', they rightfully suspect that it's a gimmick, even though, it really isn't.
So what we need is a credible story about how Campfire is worse to make it credible to people that we didn't skimp on the core experience.
posts.key_insight: When your product is dramatically cheaper than competitors, the biggest marketing challenge isn't convincing people it's good — it's convincing them the low price is real by giving a credible reason for what you left out.

posts.summary: DHH argues that creativity is perishable and cannot be saved for later use. He learned early in his career that spending eight hours on uninspiring work drained his creative energy rather than preserving it for personal projects. The key insight is that full creative engagement at work, even on boring problems, compounds over time and grows your overall creative capacity. Those who discharge creativity daily end up with far more of it than those who try to conserve it.
key_points: Creativity is perishable — it must be used regularly or it spoils, contrary to the intuition that you can save it up
Full engagement at work plants seeds for personal creative endeavors rather than depleting a finite resource
At least half the creative equation is under your control — you can find sharp angles on even the most boring problems
People who apply full creativity to trivial problems consistently outgrow those who hold back, compounding their abilities over years
money_quotes: Creativity is perishable, just like inspiration. It has to be discharged regularly or it will spoil.
If you let enough of it go to waste, eventually your talents will sour and shrivel with it.
Spending eight hours a day spinning my intellectual wheels would get me nowhere at night when I finally tried to move.
Being fully engaged at work is how you plant the best seeds for your own adventures.
Nothing spoils them both like a bullshit job.
At least half the creative equation is completely under your control.
The ones who are able to judo seemingly boring problems into interesting pursuits always end ahead on creativity and competence.
As you sow, so shall you reap.
posts.key_insight: Creativity compounds through use rather than depletes — fully engaging your creative energy on even mundane work grows your capacity, while conserving it causes it to atrophy.

posts.summary: Danish school mock elections in 2024 showed a surprising rightward shift among young voters, with the center-right Liberal Alliance winning a third of votes. DHH connects this to the post-Greta Thunberg generation growing up under predictions of imminent climate catastrophe that never materialized. He argues this has bred healthy skepticism toward expert predictions generally. Rather than analyzing the full phenomenon, he focuses on the idea that each generation rejects the apocalyptic narratives of its predecessors.
key_points: Danish youth mock elections showed a startling shift toward center-right politics, challenging assumptions that young people are inherently left-leaning
The current cohort of young voters grew up during the Greta Thunberg era but never saw the predicted climate catastrophe materialize, potentially dulling climate alarmism as a motivating issue
Repeated failures of expert predictions across climate, economics, and politics breed healthy skepticism in those who live through enough missed forecasts
Each generation may naturally reject the apocalyptic narratives inherited from the previous one
money_quotes: The results for 2024 were a startling refutation of the idea that young people must be inherently left-leaning.
The ones who failed to see the world come to an end in short order, as at times breathlessly predicted, and, perhaps as a result, no longer list climate alarmism as a central concern.
The weather, the money, and the people have a funny way of proving experts wrong time and again.
Just when they think they have it all figured out, the story takes an unexpected turn.
You really don't have to live through too many of such turns before you start developing a healthy skepticism for the people who are so sure about what's going to happen next.
Having lived their entire childhood under a story of imminent climate calamity, they've become young adults without seeing that doom's day come to their calendar.
Maybe no generation wants to inherit the favorite apocalypse of the one that went before it.
posts.key_insight: Generations raised under apocalyptic predictions that fail to materialize develop a natural skepticism toward those narratives and the experts who promoted them.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his switch from iPhone to Android, finding it surprisingly easy once he'd already moved away from Apple's ecosystem services like Mail and Calendar to HEY. He discovers that major apps are indistinguishable across platforms, Android Auto is actually better than CarPlay, and Samsung hardware has caught up in quality. The main friction point was Apple Photos, which he still needs to migrate. He frames the switch as a broader lesson about avoiding vendor lock-in and distributing trust across multiple companies rather than living under one tech giant's rule.
key_points: Platform switching difficulty is almost entirely determined by how deep you are in the vendor's proprietary services, not the hardware itself
Apple's lock-in strategy relies on services like iMessage, Apple Mail, and in-app payment subscriptions that create friction for leaving
Android hardware and software have reached parity with iPhone — scroll acceleration, font rendering, and chip performance are no longer differentiators
Android Auto is actually superior to CarPlay in connection speed, voice recognition, and app integration
Computing freedom comes from distributing your dependencies across multiple competing companies rather than going all-in on one ecosystem
money_quotes: Dangerously easy, you might say, if you were in Apple's shoes.
It's surprisingly easy to make that not the case, and then switching becomes trivial.
No wonder Apple is so keen to infuse friction into this kind of mobility by forcing customers to be stuck through subscriptions bought via in-app payments that won't easily transfer!
It really doesn't matter whether you run X or TikTok or Instagram or YouTube on iPhone or Android. You won't be able to tell the difference in most cases.
Computing freedom requires that you don't put all your eggs in one basket. That's how you get stuck with a bunch of rotten eggs, if the basket suddenly falls apart.
I'm not switching from Apple to Google as much as I'm switching from Apple to a constellation of strong but distinct alternatives.
Tech is better when power is distributed between multiple, competing companies. Living under the absolute rule of a single king is absolutely awful.
Apple has been falling apart for me in terms of trustworthiness, stewardship, and just overall likability over the past few years.
posts.key_insight: Platform lock-in is a choice you can undo by replacing proprietary services with cross-platform alternatives, and computing freedom comes from distributing your dependencies across competing providers rather than surrendering to a single ecosystem.

posts.summary: DHH reports being genuinely surprised that Windows has become a viable, even superior alternative to macOS for web development. Through testing a Dell desktop with an i9-14900K and a Framework 13 laptop, he found that WSL combined with VSCode provides seamless Linux integration without performance penalties. While Apple still holds a laptop advantage, desktop Windows machines beat the M3 Max in developer-relevant benchmarks at lower cost. He ultimately didn't switch due to his attachment to TextMate and dislike of Windows font rendering, but argues most web developers would have no such barriers.
key_points: A $2,349 Dell XPS with i9-14900K outperforms a $3,200 M3 Max MacBook in web development benchmarks, including running the HEY test suite in 1m37s vs ~2m16s
WSL + VSCode integration is seamless — files live inside the Linux subsystem, eliminating cross-filesystem performance penalties that plagued earlier attempts
Framework laptops offer a compelling repairability and upgradeability story that makes Apple's forced-upgrade cycle feel wasteful
The tech industry has come full circle: Microsoft was the villain of the 1990s, and now daring to run Windows makes you the odd one out at developer conferences
Apple desperately needs this competition for web developer mindshare, and the Mac finally has a no-apologies-needed alternative
money_quotes: I'm kinda shocked. Windows actually got good for web developers.
WSL lets you run a real Linux distribution natively, so you can use the same package manager that you'll deploy against.
I had somehow bought the premise that an M3 Max was the undisputed king of quick in mainstream computers today. Not so.
It's wild how we've come full circle. Microsoft was the top villain of the late 1990s for everyone I knew in tech, and Apple was the underdog darling. Now it's the other way around.
Switching to OSX in 2001 felt like the rebel move. Now daring to run Windows in 2024 would make you the odd one out in a lot of communities.
Apple desperately needs the competition for the mindshare of web developers.
By burying the hatchet with Linux, and open source in general, you've arrived at an incredibly productive combination of tools for developers like me.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's embrace of Linux through WSL, combined with competitive x86 desktop hardware, has made Windows a genuinely excellent web development platform that challenges Apple's long-held dominance among developers.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his twenty-year commitment to TextMate as his programming editor and why he's now considering a switch. He values simplicity over IDE features, preferring a plain text editor that forces disciplined code design. However, TextMate's Mac-only limitation has become a problem as he increasingly wants independence from Apple. After dismissing VSCode as too IDE-like and Sublime as uncanny valley, he's experimenting with neovim as a potential 'last editor' — one that's platform-independent, has decades of staying power, and aligns with his minimalist philosophy.
key_points: A plain text editor acts as a valuable constraint, forcing better program and framework design by making you feel every extraneous element
Platform independence matters more than feature richness — being tied to a single company's ecosystem is an increasingly unacceptable dependency
VSCode's trajectory toward becoming a full IDE makes it wrong for someone who explicitly wants less, not more
Neovim represents the ultimate in longevity and ubiquity — an editor with roots in the 1970s that's still actively evolving
The best tool isn't the most popular or powerful one, but the one whose philosophy matches your own
money_quotes: It's amazing how sensitive you become to extraneous elements when you literally have to type all of them by hand.
It's choosing for something to be harder, because the constraint has value.
Most of The New Stuff they brought to the party was stuff I'd pay to forgo.
Apple has turned into the kind of company that I just don't want to have to rely on.
I absolutely do not want to feel like I have to. I want to have the independence where walking away is always an option.
Its heart is beating to become a full-fledged IDE, and all I want is a text editor.
Who knows what kind of villainy they may once again descent into, if you hope to still be writing code for another 20-30 years.
posts.key_insight: The best programming tools are the ones that constrain you productively and don't chain you to any single platform or company.

posts.summary: DHH discovers that the long-maligned font rendering on Windows isn't actually a software problem but a hardware one — specifically, display pixel density. Mac fonts look great because every Mac ships with retina-class displays running at integer 2x scaling, while Windows PCs often use lower-density monitors with fractional scaling that destroys font quality. When connected to a proper high-PPI display at 200% scaling, Windows renders fonts identically to macOS. He argues this misconception has unfairly tarnished Windows' reputation among Mac users for years.
key_points: Font rendering quality is determined by display hardware (PPI) and integer scaling, not the operating system itself
Fractional scaling (e.g., 150%) ruins font rendering on any OS because you can't split pixels, forcing ugly typographic compromises
Apple controls its entire hardware lineup to enforce retina-class displays, giving it a structural advantage in default text appearance
Gaming-optimized monitors prioritize refresh rate and color over pixel density, creating a real trade-off between text quality and gaming performance
Microsoft's new Aptos font is a welcome improvement over the 'atrocious' Segoe UI and could help make Windows more appealing to Mac switchers
money_quotes: I always thought it was a software problem — or at least a difference of aesthetics expressed in software. But it turns out the reason many Mac owners, including yours truly, so strongly dislike how fonts typically look on Windows is actually a hardware problem!
You can't split a pixel, so the system has to do all sorts of typographically nasty tricks when doing non-integer scaling, and the end result is awful font rendering.
Apple has the power to enforce retina-class displays across its entire lineup, so as a result everyone who looks at a Mac sees beautifully rendered fonts.
Windows, on the other hand, runs on everything from rinky-dingy $200 laptops with super low-resolution screens and up to the best computers money can buy.
I just wish I knew about all this before dismissing Windows out of hand as a suitable alternative to macOS on account of font rendering for years.
As I've been tweeting about comparing Windows and macOS, I've gotten a ton of replies that were essentially 'yeah, Windows sucks at fonts'. And that's just wrong.

And that's the fact that the default system font is the atrocious Segoe UI. Now that's surely a difference of aesthetics, not hardware or software limitations, but I absolutely hate this tinny, thin font.
posts.key_insight: Windows font rendering looks bad not because of software deficiency but because most Windows PCs lack the high-density displays and integer scaling that Apple mandates across its lineup.

posts.summary: DHH recounts how Apple's threat to kill progressive web apps in the EU pushed him to explore alternatives outside the Apple ecosystem. Though Apple reversed that decision, the experience taught him the danger of being locked into one platform. He moved off Apple services entirely, adopted multi-platform tools, and started using Windows and Android alongside his Mac. He argues that software makers owe it to their customers to actually use the platforms they build for, noting that his own team discovered numerous improvements needed for Basecamp on Windows.
key_points: Apple's attempted PWA removal in the EU was the catalyst for exploring non-Apple platforms, and their reversal only reinforced the lesson about vendor lock-in
DHH completed a full exit from Apple services, moving to multi-platform alternatives like Google Photos, iA Writer + Dropbox, and HEY to guarantee freedom of movement
Using Windows daily revealed concrete product improvements needed for Basecamp and HEY, from scrollbars to fonts
The Mac has become an echo chamber among web designers and programmers, creating blind spots for how products look and work on other platforms
Owning multiple types of computers is not just acceptable but beneficial, especially for people who make multi-platform software
money_quotes: Apple gave me an excellent reminder that having all your computing eggs in one basket leaves you mighty vulnerable to predatory behavior.
If you're ALL-IN on Apple hardware, software, and services, you're as good of a captive consumer as it gets. You might complain, but you won't leave.
Leaving nothing on the service side tying me to one platform or another. Everything important is multi-platform, which guarantees freedom of movement.

I make multi-platform software for a living. Basecamp, the most important product we sell at 37signals, has Windows as its #1 platform. It's kinda embarrassing how little we've paid heed to that fact over the years.
I think it's fair to say that within certain parts of the web communities, the Mac has become an echo chamber with both designers and programmers.
You'll go through 24 hours of withdrawals switching to Android or Windows for a while, but it's remarkable how quickly it all subsides.
posts.key_insight: Software makers who are locked into one platform develop blind spots about their own products, and the best antidote to vendor lock-in is to actually live on multiple platforms.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Apple under Tim Cook mirrors Microsoft's stagnant Ballmer era — profitable but culturally toxic and strategically misguided. He points to Apple's combative response to the EU's $1.8 billion antitrust fine over Spotify as evidence of deep institutional hubris. Meanwhile, he credits Satya Nadella with transforming Microsoft into a developer-friendly, open-source-embracing company that now sits atop the market. DHH contends that Apple won't fundamentally change until it undergoes a similar leadership transition, replacing its current defensive posture with genuine engagement with the developer community.
key_points: Apple's response to the EU Spotify ruling — claiming credit for Spotify's success — reveals a level of corporate hubris comparable to Microsoft's worst antitrust-era behavior
Microsoft under Nadella has undergone a genuine cultural transformation by embracing open source, WSL, VSCode, and GitHub stewardship
Apple remains anchored in a victim mentality and adversarial relationship with developers and regulators despite its financial success
Financial performance alone doesn't indicate corporate health — Ballmer's Microsoft was profitable but strategically lost
Apple needs a leadership transition to unlock the kind of cultural reboot that transformed Microsoft
money_quotes: Sure, they're making gobs of money these days, like Ballmer did so well during his time, but, also like Ballmer, they're anchored in an aggrieved past.
Nothing will fundamentally change at Apple until they have the kind of leadership transition that unlocked so much value at Microsoft.
Until they find their Nadella to replace their Ballmer, we should expect more indignant press releases, more threats, more evasion, more malicious compliance.
It's incredible how similar the posture of Gates and the posture of Apple's writing in that press release on the Spotify verdict shimmers with the same light.
They've managed to literally get to the top of the world while also engaging with the broader development community in an incredibly productive way.
Either way, Apple needs a cultural reboot.
It's truly an incredible document. One that I hope marks the sad high water point for Apple's hubris.
posts.key_insight: Apple's financial success masks a Ballmer-era cultural rot — adversarial toward developers and regulators — that only a leadership transition can fix.

posts.summary: DHH announces his commitment to switching from Mac to Windows as his primary development platform, using WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux) to run Linux inside Windows. While initially motivated by Apple's hostile moves against PWAs in the EU and their authoritarian treatment of Epic Games, he emphasizes wanting to compute out of passion rather than spite. He's found surprising delights in the Windows/Linux combination, including good font rendering and growing appreciation for VSCode. He also sees practical value in countering the Mac monoculture at 37signals, given that over half of Basecamp's users are on Windows.
key_points: Windows with WSL provides a viable development environment that DHH would never have considered possible a few years ago
Apple's authoritarian App Store behavior — punishing public criticism — provided the push to leave, but DHH wants positive rather than spite-driven motivation
Long-held prejudices about Windows (like poor font rendering) turned out to be false with modern hardware and configuration
Switching platforms at 37signals counters the Mac monoculture and better aligns the team with their users, over half of whom use Windows
VSCode became enjoyable once he stopped trying to recreate TextMate and stripped out the IDE features
money_quotes: So public criticism of Apple is now motivating grounds for being denied access to the App Store? What kind of overtly authoritarian bullshit is this?
I don't want to compute purely out of spite. I want to compute out of passion.

I've gone around the computing world in the past eighty hours. I've been flowing freely from Windows to Linux, sampling text editors like VSCode, neovim, Helix, and Sublime, while surveying PC laptops and desktops. It's been an adventure! But it's time to stop being a tourist. It's time to commit.

I would never have contemplated a switch to Windows without being able to run Linux inside it. But it's still a change of scenery you could not possibly have convinced me was in the cards a few years ago!
I had this prejudice that Windows simply didn't know how to render fonts, and it turned out to be false.
The key turned out to resist recreating TextMate, and something as simple as picking a radically different color theme helped break the constant comparison.
One encouraged and sanctioned by yours truly, mind you, but also one at odds with the fact that more than half the users on our biggest product, Basecamp, live on Windows.
posts.key_insight: Breaking free from platform monoculture — whether motivated by principle or passion — reveals that long-held computing prejudices often don't survive actual experience.

posts.summary: DHH examines Apple's increasingly hostile responses to EU regulation, including the Digital Markets Act and the Spotify fine, and raises the provocative question of whether Apple might actually withdraw from the European market. He notes that Tim Cook's Apple has shown a surprising level of spite and obstinacy that exceeds even Steve Jobs-era confrontations. While the EU represents only 8-10% of Apple's revenue, DHH argues that Cook's recent combative posture suggests he might be capable of such a dramatic move. He urges EU bureaucrats to at least entertain the possibility.
key_points: Apple's responses to EU regulation have been uniquely spiteful, surpassing even Steve Jobs-era power moves in their institutional hostility
Tim Cook's public persona as a conservative bean counter is at odds with the increasingly aggressive and personal nature of Apple's regulatory pushback
The EU represents only 8-10% of Apple's revenue (~$35B of $383B), making a withdrawal financially survivable if not strategically sound
Facebook's threat to leave Australia over newspaper royalties provides precedent for big tech threatening market withdrawal, though no one has followed through at this scale
The last decade of unprecedented events should make regulators take even unlikely scenarios seriously
money_quotes: We never saw such an institutional commitment to flipping off legislators and platform partners.
His cool cucumber public persona seems to be turning into more of a hot potato with every aggrieved move Apple pulls.

If they're already lashing out with malicious compliance, vindictive App Store evictions, and pissy press releases on account of where we are today, what might they do if the regulatory pressure in Europe doesn't relent?
A CEO that signs off on public letters like the one in response to their loss in the Spotify case might actually have it in them to do something big.

We've gone through a lot of surprises in the last decade. Major, world-affecting events and decisions almost nobody would have contemplated as realistic possibilities just a few years prior to them happening.
I hope there are bureaucrats within the EU at least entertaining the possibility. Stranger things have happened.
posts.key_insight: Apple's unprecedented level of institutional spite toward EU regulators suggests that even the once-unthinkable scenario of withdrawing from Europe deserves serious consideration.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Google's disastrous Gemini AI launch was the predictable result of ideological capture within the company. He points to Gemini's historically inaccurate image generation and moral equivocations as symptoms of an organization where social justice ideology has become a prime directive, displacing accuracy and usefulness. Drawing parallels to his own experience at Basecamp in 2021, he argues that such cultural capture grows worse if left unchecked. He contends that addressing this is existential for Google's AI competitiveness, and urges founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page to make the painful but necessary correction, noting it's easier to do in 2024 than it was in 2021.
key_points: Google squandered its AI leadership position—from acquiring DeepMind to publishing the Transformer paper—by releasing a product warped by ideological overcorrection rather than optimizing for accuracy
The real AI safety threat isn't perpetuating existing biases but injecting the biases and ideology of its overseers into AI systems
DHH draws a direct parallel between Google's situation and Basecamp's 2021 crisis, arguing that ideological capture only worsens if leadership avoids confrontation
Google's AI Principles prioritize social justice and anti-bias as top directives while failing to mention accuracy or usefulness
The broader tech industry has already begun correcting course on these issues, giving Google more cover to act than existed in 2021
money_quotes: The threat from AI will come less from 'perpetuating existing biases in the world' and more from 'injecting the biases and ideology of its overseers'.
An asylum run entirely by its most deranged inmates, holding everyone else captive.
The problem with ideological dragons like this is that they're never content with the political scalps or capital accumulated. There's always a hunger for more, more, more.
You end up with a search engine that wants to lecture people rather than finding the facts. There's no mainstream market for such a bullshit product in the long run.
Hard choices, easy life. Easy choices, hard life.
Every little victory is an opportunity to move the goal posts further north. Make ever smaller transgressions punishable by ostracization and shame.
It could coast on the fumes of its former glory for a long time. Let's hope that there's more than an empty, hollowed out shell of a company left by the time they get this right.
posts.key_insight: Ideological capture within organizations produces products that prioritize ideology over usefulness, and the longer leadership avoids the painful correction, the more existential the damage becomes.

posts.summary: DHH argues that overly gentle, 'precious' workplace language and policies—like dedicated mental health days for feeling 'overwhelmed'—actually make employees more fragile rather than supporting them. He uses 37signals' old mental health day policy as an example of 'ruinous empathy' that confuses the message and sets counterproductive expectations. Drawing on the concept from Radical Candor, he contends that coddling language plants the idea that office work is unbearably taxing, which he rejects. He advocates for clarity over gentleness, arguing the opposite of precious isn't cruel—it's clear and fair.
key_points: Ruinous empathy—couching feedback in overly gentle language—extends beyond individual reviews to company-wide policies and culture
Dedicated mental health days for feeling 'overwhelmed' set counterproductive expectations and normalize fragility rather than resilience
Precious workplace language actually makes people more fragile by planting the idea that normal work is mentally unbearable
The opposite of being precious is not being cruel, but being clear and setting reasonable baseline expectations
37signals removed these policies and claims the organization has been better off since
money_quotes: The idea that employees are so mentally fragile that 'feeling overwhelmed' is a reason to be absent is bonkers.
Could you imagine if the rest of the world ran on something like this? That your trash cans wouldn't get emptied if the truck driver 'felt overwhelmed'? No. Just no.
Not every day is going to be magical at work. Some days you really do just have to power through.
I've come to believe that this type of language and expectation setting actually makes people more fragile.
The opposite of precious is not being cruel, but being clear.
Being too precious is the same as being fragile. That's not a goal to aspire to. We should all be chasing higher resilience instead.
It's expecting a reasonable baseline from people that anyone out in ThE ReAL wORlD could recognize as fair.
posts.key_insight: Overly gentle workplace policies and language don't protect employees—they breed fragility by setting the expectation that normal work stress is unbearable.

posts.summary: DHH observes that developers face a double anxiety from ongoing tech layoffs and the emerging threat of AI tools like Devin. He contextualizes this by noting that despite recent losses, programmers remain far better off than a decade ago, but human psychology fixates on the delta rather than absolute status. He draws an ironic parallel: programmers who prospered by automating others' jobs now face automation themselves. His prediction is that we've likely seen the peak of manual programming as a mass profession, comparing it to agriculture's workforce shrinkage, and advises developers to stop worrying about forces beyond their control.
key_points: Tech layoffs have been the worst since the dot-com bust, but the industry remains enormous and wages are still historically high
Human psychology responds to trajectory, not absolute position — developers feel anxious because prospects are declining even if they remain privileged
Programmers who prospered by automating other people's jobs now face the irony of their own profession being automated
AI will likely make tech more valuable and integrated into society, but with far fewer human programmers needed
Past predictions of imminent disruption (self-driving cars, dot-com recovery timelines) suggest The Future is notoriously hard to time
money_quotes: Humans don't react to absolute status or wealth. All the anxiety or exhilaration is in the delta.

Programmers, as a group, have prospered tremendously by automating other people's jobs over the past half century. But when it's other people's livelihoods, we naturally have a much easier time seeing the big picture.
That's the trouble with The Future. It's awfully difficult to predict when it'll actually arrive. All we're doing is making bets and taking guesses.
I do think we've probably seen the high-water mark of the manual programmer.
We just might not need as many human programmers pounding code with their little meat fingers.
No profession has ever successfully resisted automation or redundancy in the face of technological advancement over the long term.
