Screaming at Devin will only distract you from enjoying the last glorious years of a golden run.
posts.key_insight: Programmers who built their fortunes automating others' work now face the uncomfortable irony of AI automating theirs, and no amount of resistance will change that trajectory.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the unintended consequences of government antitrust action against Big Tech. While he has long advocated for breaking Apple and Google's app store monopolies, he now worries that the EU's Digital Markets Act and similar efforts may not disband concentrated power but rather transfer it to governments eager to control speech and online platforms. He draws parallels between Apple's frustrating experience with vague DMA compliance rules and the arbitrary app review process developers have endured for years. Ultimately, he finds himself genuinely torn — unable to fully endorse either government intervention or the status quo, concluding with a Kierkegaard-inspired admission that both paths lead to regret.
key_points: The EU's DMA is giving Apple a taste of its own medicine — vague rules, arbitrary enforcement, and post-hoc compliance judgments mirror what developers face in app store review
Government antitrust action risks not dismantling Big Tech's power but co-opting it, deputizing platforms as enforcement arms for speech regulation and censorship
Political overreach justified by one side inevitably gets turned against them when power changes hands — emergency powers and speech restrictions always expand
The Microsoft antitrust precedent is ambiguous: market disruption from Firefox, Chrome, and mobile may have mattered more than DOJ intervention
Both tackling and ignoring Big Tech monopolies carry serious risks, making this a genuinely difficult question without a clear principled answer
money_quotes: My concern is rather that the sovereign leviathans of the world, be it the EU or the US, might not exactly share as many interests with free market advocates as it appears on the surface.
Welcome to our world, Apple. This is exactly what it feels like to be a developer knocking on the door of your app store bureaucracy.
If you give up on hope, you're bound to become cynical, and that's a curse worse than any commercial dispossession.
The DMA is convoluted and complicated because the EU is trying to have its cake and eat it too.
Every overreach you find justified when its your team wielding the advantage is one you'll rue when it's turned back against you.

A future where this intolerable concentration of power is not so much disbanded as simply subsumed into the growing arsenal of oppressive powers that are increasingly being collected by Western democracies.
Tackle Big Tech, and you will regret it. Don't tackle Big Tech, and you will regret that too. Either way, you will regret it.
posts.key_insight: Government antitrust action against Big Tech may not liberate the market but instead transfer monopoly power to states eager to weaponize platforms for speech control and censorship.

posts.summary: DHH distills executive responsibility into three core functions: charting the course (deciding what to build and when), setting the pace (demanding more from work hours without demanding more hours), and holding the line (maintaining quality standards across everything). He argues against long-term planning in favor of short 6-8 week cycles, and insists executives must stay close to the actual work to make credible decisions. The framework emphasizes that boldness without insight is delusion, and that quality culture is built one decision at a time. He concludes that consistently executing these three responsibilities is the best an executive can do, regardless of outcome.
key_points: Course-charting should happen in short 6-8 week cycles, not five-year plans — long-term thinking invites intellectual self-deception
Setting the pace means demanding more from hours, not more hours — executives must know the work deeply enough to push credibly
Quality requires personal investment and willingness to halt work and redo what isn't right, or standards will erode
Executives who drift too high above the work lose the ability to make grounded, credible decisions
Success is largely out of your hands, but great execution on these three responsibilities tends to find the gold
money_quotes: If you don't have a clue where you're going, any road can take you there, and running in circles is as good as making progress.
Being a "long-term thinker" is an invitation to smell your own intellectual exhaust fumes.
It's much easier to bullshit from 30,000 ft than it is when imminent decisions stare you in the face.
The slower you're moving, the less you think you can do, the slower you're moving.
Setting the pace isn't about demanding more hours, it's about demanding more from those hours.
So to be bold, you must have insight – or you're just delusional.
If you're constantly pushing for the impossible, and none of it happens, you're a clown. Get out of here.
A culture of quality is built one product and process decision at a time.
posts.key_insight: Executive leadership reduces to three disciplines — choosing what to build in short cycles, pushing for ambitious but credible pace by staying close to the work, and personally enforcing quality standards before they erode.

posts.summary: DHH enthusiastically endorses Abigail Shrier's book 'Bad Therapy,' which argues that modern American parenting—with its over-monitoring, therapy culture, and excessive intervention—is failing children rather than helping them. He connects it to his personal frustration with hyper-involved parenting culture and the erosion of childhood independence. DHH praises Shrier's willingness to challenge sacred cows, noting her track record of surviving cancellation attempts after her previous book 'Irreversible Damage.' He argues the mental health crisis among young Americans proves that the current approach of more therapy, accommodations, and interventions is not working.
key_points: Modern American parenting has replaced childhood independence and moderate dangers with overprotective 'cotton-ball cocoons' that prevent kids from handling even minor setbacks
The culture of gentle parenting, social-emotional learning, and trauma-focused thinking has spread everywhere despite producing worsening mental health outcomes in children
Shrier's credibility is bolstered by surviving cancellation attempts over her previous book, with subsequent evidence validating her earlier concerns about gender medicine
Parents, administrators, and teachers are all complicit in an escalating cycle of hyper-involvement in children's social lives
The deteriorating mental health statistics for kids and young adults demonstrate that more therapy, pills, and interventions are not the answer
money_quotes: Replaced with cotton-ball cocoons to keep kids from dealing with even the most minor of setbacks themselves.
The hyper-active, hyper-involved parents who have hair-trigger intervention fingers ready to be pulled at the slightest appearance of social discomfort or disharmony.
Combined with the fact that we, me, us are all complicit. All so easily sucked into it all. I hate it.
It reads like it was written by a writer entirely unafraid of slaughtering the sacred cows of the upper-class zeitgeist. Because it is!
There is perhaps nothing quite as fortifying as staring down a censorship mob, and then later seeing your testimony confirmed.
All the therapy, the accommodations, the pills, and the interventions have failed to produce a generation of happy, independent kids.
There's no way the answer is more of the same.
posts.key_insight: The modern American parenting apparatus of therapy, monitoring, and intervention has produced the opposite of its intended effect—a generation with worse mental health outcomes—and parents need to radically reverse course toward childhood independence.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on securing his 11th Le Mans entry through Nielsen Racing after a last-minute seat opening. He describes the complex economics of motorsport where drivers must balance money and talent to secure competitive seats. The post explores the paradox of peak manufacturer involvement in racing amid uncertainty about the automotive industry's electric and AI-driven future. DHH meditates on aging as a 44-year-old amateur competing at the highest level, accepting the inevitability of physical decline. He finds peace in finality through fatherhood and the continuation of human lineage, before concluding that racing's greatest gift is forcing total presence in the moment.
key_points: Motorsport economics require a 'curious mix of money and talent' — most seats are funded by passionate privateers, even in Formula 1
Despite massive uncertainty about the future of cars (electric, AI, who wins between Silicon Valley and China), manufacturer spending on racing is at an all-time high
At 44, DHH is acutely aware that drivers don't improve after forty, and he's waiting for the inevitable decline in lap times
Fatherhood provides a biological acceptance of finality — the human lineage continues regardless of individual decline
Racing demands total present-moment focus, leaving no room for existential deliberation
money_quotes: Motorsports relies on a curious mix of money and talent. It's usually not enough just to be good at driving to get a seat in a competitive car.
I bring some of the money and some of the talent, but never enough to single-handedly make a program worthy of a Le Mans entry happen.
You'd think this level of uncertainty about the future would have dampened the will among car makers to spend lavishly on big-budget racing programs. And yet we're at an all-time high.
Drivers don't get better after forty, that's just a fact.
I'm at peace with the circle of life, it's array of inevitable declines, and with it's finality. Nobody gets to be their best at almost anything forever.
If anything, there's a sense of relief in knowing that none of it -- not racing, not programming, not entrepreneurship -- is meant to go on forever.
There's no time for existential deliberation! All your attention must be focused on making the perfect inputs for the next corner.
Everything else simply must vanish for your attention to be devoted to the task at hand: Go fast, don't crash.
posts.key_insight: The inevitability of decline — in racing, programming, and life — is not something to fear but to accept, and the discipline of racing forces a liberating presence that strips away existential worry.

posts.summary: DHH argues that while the 'we're like family' rhetoric at work is rightly criticized, people can't then expect that underperforming colleagues should be protected from being cut. He endorses Netflix's 'professional team' metaphor, where people earn their spot through competence and fit. Using soccer as an extended analogy, he argues that finding the right level matters more than clinging to a team where you don't belong. He frames letting someone go as potentially merciful rather than cruel, and positions decisive personnel decisions as a manager's most important job.
key_points: The 'we're a family' workplace rhetoric is cynically used to extract sacrifice without mutual obligation
Netflix's 'professional team' metaphor is more honest — you earn your spot and can lose it
You can't reject family rhetoric while also expecting underperformers to be protected from cuts
Being on the wrong team hurts the individual too — cutting them can be an act of mercy, not cruelty
A manager's most important job is knowing when to make the cut and having the strength to do it
money_quotes: I've always hated the saying "we're like family here" when it comes to work. Because it's obviously not true, and it's usually cynically invoked by management to entice an undue obligation of sacrifice.
You can't have it both ways. You can't scorn the use of "we're a family" and then also expect that colleagues who can't keep up are spared the cut.
Now the natural delusion of every business owner is to think that they're running Manchester United when in fact they're barely hanging on in third division.
Cutting them from the team would be an act of mercy, not cruelty.
This is perhaps the most important job of the coach or manager. Knowing when to make the cut, and having the strength to do it.
Not everyone is meant to be Messi or to play on his team. But we wouldn't have much of a sport if his level was the only level.
Find your level, find a team that fits with it, and play to the best of your ability. That's the beautiful game.
posts.key_insight: Rejecting the 'family' metaphor at work means accepting the 'team' metaphor's corollary: not everyone is guaranteed a spot, and decisive cuts are a manager's most important responsibility.

posts.summary: DHH argues that companies with too little meaningful work are worse than those with too much. Drawing on David Graeber's Bullshit Jobs thesis, he contends that when real problems are scarce, people invent fictitious ones that drain morale. He acknowledges the paradox that humans want lighter loads but become unhappy when they achieve them. His prescription: keep teams slightly understaffed relative to meaningful problems, because idle knowledge workers will fill the void with made-up busywork.
key_points: Companies with insufficient real work breed worse dysfunction than overworked ones, because people invent meaningless problems to fill the void
Perspective matters — the same job can feel meaningful or pointless depending on framing, as illustrated by the zookeeper analogy
Humans are biologically wired to conserve energy yet cognitively wired to crave challenge, creating a fundamental paradox
Permanent leisure (mojito island) is a curse, not a blessing — retirees who lose purpose often decline rapidly
The solution is to keep meaningful problems slightly exceeding available capacity, preventing the invention of busywork
money_quotes: The worst kind of company is usually not the one where there's too much real work to do, but the kind where there's not enough.
Without enough real problems to go around, humans are prone to invent fictitious and dreadful ones.
We are biologically tuned to conserve energy while being cognitively tuned to crave a challenge.
Mojito island, all the time, is a curse, not a blessing.
The worst injury you can inflict on knowledge workers is leaving them with too little of consequence to contest with.
Meaningful problems are the most valuable human motivators. Made-up problems are a blight.
Ensure you have not quite enough time and people available to tackle the former lest you start inventing the latter.
posts.key_insight: Keep your team slightly understaffed relative to meaningful problems — idle knowledge workers don't rest, they invent bullshit to fill the void.

posts.summary: DHH argues that imperfections and quirks in products create deeper emotional connections than polished perfection does. He draws parallels between the Porsche 911's rear engine, mechanical watches, and vintage computers to explain why he's fallen in love with the Framework 13 laptop running Linux. Despite acknowledging its drawbacks compared to modern MacBooks—inferior battery life, adequate-but-not-great screen, less polish—he finds the machine's quirky design choices and compromises endearing. He encourages programmers to try Linux, arguing it's finally good enough for desktop use, and suggests that embracing imperfection can lead to a more meaningful computing experience.
key_points: Products that achieve near-perfection paradoxically become harder to form emotional connections with, while flawed but distinctive products inspire loyalty and affection
The Framework 13 laptop recreates the quirky appeal of classic computers through its unusual 3:2 display, deep-travel keyboard, and modular interchangeable ports
Linux desktop has quietly become viable for daily use, with Ubuntu and a few extensions providing a delightful experience that's surprisingly close to macOS
Most people rightly prefer polished, frictionless tools—but for those who appreciate mechanical engagement and distinctiveness, imperfect alternatives offer something deeper
The drawbacks of the Framework 13 and Linux (battery life, screen resolution, polish) are real but ultimately don't matter much in practice
money_quotes: The engine is in wrong place in a Porsche 911. It's hanging out the back, swinging the car like a pendulum. And that's key to why it's the most iconic sports car ever made.
The mac in particular has been massaged to within an inch of perfection, and has thus become harder to connect with.
We strive to make things better and better, but if we succeed, we reminisce of the quirks that used to be.
It was so good because it was also kinda bad.
This quirky, flawed machine has created a connection I haven't had with a piece of physical computer hardware in a very long time.
I've smirked about 'This Is The Year of Linux on the Desktop' for over twenty years, but now that I've been actually running it for over a month, I've realized it's actually here.
Dare to add a little imperfection into your computing setup, and you might just find a deeper connection to the bits and electrons running it all.
posts.key_insight: Perfection breeds indifference while imperfections create the friction and character that forge genuine emotional connections with the tools we use.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the tech industry's shift from 'master' to 'main' in Git branch naming, arguing he went along with it in good faith but now views it as an exploit of collective goodwill. He says the change accomplished nothing meaningful because the demands for linguistic changes never stop. He frames his compliance as naivety and promises to resist similar pressures in the future, using the metaphor of patching a security vulnerability.
key_points: DHH didn't personally care about the master/main naming but agreed to change as a gesture of good faith
He now views the renaming campaign as an 'exploit' that produced no meaningful change while enabling escalating demands
He argues the pattern of endless linguistic policing means no concession is ever sufficient
He frames the experience as a learning moment, comparing it to patching a security vulnerability against future exploitation
money_quotes: This was a classic exploit of good faith, and I fell for it.
Changing master to main changed less than nothing.
Nothing was or is ever enough in this arena.
As soon as this word battle was won, it was just on to the next and the next.
The upside of being hit by an exploit like this is that you eventually end up with a patch that closes the hole.
People are not going to be this gullible twice. I am not going to be this gullible twice.
Next time the firewall will be ready.
posts.key_insight: DHH frames the master-to-main renaming as a cautionary tale about how good-faith compliance with symbolic demands leads not to resolution but to escalating expectations, and vows to resist such pressures going forward.

posts.summary: DHH applauds Google's decision to immediately fire 28 employees who occupied the Google Cloud CEO's office to protest the company's contract with Israel. He frames this as a vindication of the position he and Basecamp took three years earlier: that workplaces should not be platforms for political activism. He argues that Google's follow-up policy banning political activism at work signals that corporate America has passed the peak of ideological capture. DHH connects this to a broader pattern including Coinbase's similar stance and sees it as evidence that common-sense workplace norms are being restored.
key_points: Google fired 28 employees immediately for occupying an office in political protest, with no drawn-out investigation or sympathetic statements
Google followed up by explicitly banning political activism in the workplace, echoing policies from Coinbase and Basecamp
DHH sees this as proof that corporate America has moved past peak 'ideological capture' that he says nearly swallowed companies whole
The specific political cause is irrelevant — DHH argues the principle applies equally to any activist cause used to disrupt business
Three years ago this stance drew massive backlash; now DHH predicts it will barely cause a ripple outside activist echo chambers
money_quotes: Anyone watching that shit show would be right to wonder whether one of America's great technology companies had fallen completely into the hands of the new theocracy.

But then the most amazing thing happened. There was no drawn-out investigation. No saccharine statements about employee's rights to occupy offices, preventing work from happening, or advance their political agenda at work. Nope. They were just fired. Immediately. All 28 of them.
Bravo.

Three years ago, taking a common-sense position like this would have been met with drama and outage in the media and on Twitter. Today I doubt it'll bring more than a ripple outside of a few activist echo chambers on Mastodon. Amazing progress!
We've not just passed the peak of the nonsense that nearly swallowed corporate America whole, but we're now seeing them repudiate it head on.
If Google, with it's employment roster still packed with people sympathetic to the new theocracy, can put its foot down, so can the rest of the Fortune 500 and beyond.
posts.key_insight: Google's swift firing of activist employees and adoption of a no-politics-at-work policy validates what DHH and others advocated years earlier: workplaces are for business, not political platforms.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the aftermath of 37signals' 2021 decision to ban political discussions at work, now vindicated by Google adopting a similar policy. He describes the internet mob backlash as ultimately a gift that revealed true friends and allies. He argues that the social justice movement of the late 2010s swept up many well-meaning people into divisive ideology, and that the path forward is forgiveness rather than retaliation. He calls for a broad societal rejection of the ideas themselves while extending grace to individuals who were temporarily caught up in them.
key_points: Google's adoption of a no-politics-at-work policy echoes what 37signals and Coinbase did years earlier, suggesting vindication
Being the target of internet mob outrage, while awful in the moment, served as a clarifying gift that revealed true allies
The social justice movement of the late 2010s through 2022 swept otherwise kind people into divisive oppressor/oppressed frameworks
Pursuing vendettas against individuals who were momentarily swept up in bad ideas will slow the broader rejection of those ideas
The winning strategy against ideological rigidity is to be broader, more forgiving, and less punitive — the opposite of eating your own
money_quotes: In the moment, it was awful, but in retrospect, it was a gift.
We walk through life much of the time without really knowing who'll be there when the going gets tough -- and our guesses are often wrong.
It brought unexpected friends and allies out in the light, and it revealed which friends and acquaintances would rather crawl back in the woodwork than stand by my side.
It swept up so many otherwise kind and caring people into an ideology predicated on dividing us all into oppressors/oppressed, privileged/not, and other false dichotomies and identities.
The worst thing we can do to slow down the rejection of these bad ideas is by forever tarring people who were momentarily taken in by them.
It kept eating its own for ever-smaller transgressions against an ever-shifting doctrine.
To beat that nonsense back, the side of sanity has to do the opposite. Be broader, more forgiving, and less high strung.
posts.key_insight: The most effective way to defeat a divisive ideology is not retaliation against its followers but broad forgiveness that makes it easy for people to walk away from bad ideas.

posts.summary: DHH argues that ambition is a rare and essential gift that gives work meaning, contrasting it with the existential dread of bullshit jobs. He pushes back against the idea that his 'calm company' philosophy at 37signals means coasting, insisting that calm is a method for achieving ambitious goals, not an end in itself. He defines ambition as a leap of faith that requires uncertainty and the possibility of failure. While acknowledging that leaders like Musk and Jobs inspire through ambitious projects, he argues you don't need to be abrasive to stir people's hearts. The core tension he explores is that people crave challenge and discomfort even as they pursue comfort and security.
key_points: Modern office workers are starved for ambition and adventure, which explains why people willingly work for demanding leaders like Musk despite harsh conditions
A calm company is a method for achieving ambitious goals faster, not a destination of comfortable coasting
True ambition requires uncertainty and the possibility of failure — if you can quantify everything before starting, the ambition disappears
Being ambitious and calm are complementary, not opposing: smooth and fast beats big and erratic
People don't actually know what fulfills them at work — it's meaningful ambitious projects, not perks like ping pong or free massages
money_quotes: It's the lack of ambition that fuels the malaise of a bullshit job.
Being ambitious and calm is like being smooth and fast. Big, erratic, dramatic movements might feel like they're getting you somewhere, but the stop watch usually reveals the opposite.
I'd rather work in place where it was crazy all the time, if we're trying to get somewhere, than I'd work somewhere perfectly calm that's just spinning the wheels.
If we can quantify it all before we even begin, the ambition disappears.
It's exactly the possibility of failure that gives the effort its meaning and its value.
Without a dash of the unpredictable, we all wither away. The chase for security and surety only works as a thrill if you never truly get there.
Our competency only grows when we stretch it slightly beyond its breaking point from time to time.
posts.key_insight: A calm company isn't about coasting — it's a disciplined method for pursuing ambitious goals, because meaningful work requires uncertainty and the possibility of failure.

posts.summary: DHH traces the redemption arcs of tech companies he once opposed, focusing on Microsoft and Meta. Microsoft transformed from the sworn enemy of open source in the 1990s to one of its biggest champions through GitHub, VSCode, and WSL. Meta, through Zuckerberg's embrace of open-source AI with Llama 3, now serves as a critical counterweight to closed AI cartels and government-controlled content moderation. DHH argues that self-interest, not altruism, drives these positive shifts, invoking Adam Smith to make the case that competition and free markets matter more than trusting any single company's motives. He concludes that defending free markets, free speech, and open AI outweighs his earlier concerns about targeted advertising.
key_points: Microsoft completed a remarkable villain-to-hero arc by becoming a major open source sponsor, steward of GitHub, and creator of VSCode and WSL
Meta's open-source release of Llama 3 is a vital counterbalance to closed AI development and government-industry regulatory capture
The fusion of state power and platform censorship through 'fact checkers' and content moderation poses a greater threat than targeted advertising
Self-interest and market competition, not altruism, are the reliable forces that produce good outcomes — per Adam Smith
Defending free markets for AI, speech, and ideas should be prioritized over holding grudges against former tech villains
money_quotes: They've gone from being the sworn enemy of open source to one of the biggest sponsors of it.

Zuckerberg's embrace of open source AI is an invaluable counter to the cartel-adjacent bullshit of 'AI safety & ethics' that would see the likes of OpenAI and Google conspire with governments around the world to determine what math should be allowed to predict the next token.
I've seen this movie before, and I'm not interested in a rerun.
Whatever trust I may once have had in objective third-party 'fact checkers' have long since evaporated from the catastrophic track record of these anything-but-neutral, would-be arbiters of truth.
We don't need altruism to save us, we need incentives. We need competition. We need free markets for products, platforms, services, ideas, speech, and AI.
We need to put the locus of control with consumers and individuals, not bureaucrats and monopolists.
I'm more than willing to commute the sentencing of former villains who've served their time and changed their minds.
Let's go, Zuck. Give 'em hell.
posts.key_insight: Tech companies should be judged by their current alignment with open markets and open source, not by past sins — and self-interested competition, not regulation or altruism, is the most reliable guardian of freedom.

posts.summary: After twenty-plus years on Mac, DHH describes his journey away from Apple, first to Windows (which he found surprisingly capable thanks to WSL), then to Linux on a Framework laptop. He discovered that Windows is a legitimate alternative to Mac for web developers, but ultimately fell in love with running Ubuntu despite its rough edges. He found the tinkering and troubleshooting inherent to Linux nostalgic and genuinely enjoyable rather than frustrating, comparing it to the IKEA effect. He concludes that we now have three viable personal computing platforms and encourages adventurous users to try the switch.
key_points: Windows has become a genuinely good web development platform thanks to WSL, and most anti-Windows sentiment is based on outdated facts
Linux's rough edges and need for tinkering are features, not bugs — the hands-on experience creates a deeper connection with the hardware
The Framework laptop's hardware and AMD chip running Linux outperformed expectations and became his daily driver
Having three viable computing platforms (Mac, Windows, Linux) is healthy for the industry and consumers
Apple's missteps provided the motivation needed to overcome decades of platform inertia
money_quotes: It felt nice. Nice knowing that there was a real, realistic, and compelling alternative to the mac, and that most of my aversion to Windows was based on outdated facts or misconceptions.
Most of the jokes about Linux are true! There are more rabbit holes, more gotchas, and less polish.
Rather than being frustrated with things not just working out of the box, I embraced the adventure.
Computers required some assembly, and as IKEA knows, it made us love them more.
It's hard to know whether you'll find some zen of motorcycle maintenance in knowing how to tighten the timing chain of Ubuntu before you try.
We ought to have AT LEAST three good options for personal computing in the modern age, and now I've come to realize that we do.
Do it not because it is easy, but because it is hard. See what kind of computing stuff you're made of.
posts.key_insight: Breaking free from Apple's ecosystem revealed that both Windows and Linux have matured into legitimate alternatives, and the hands-on tinkering required by Linux can be a source of joy rather than frustration.

posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals is moving continuous integration from remote BuildKite runners back to local developer machines. Modern multi-core CPUs like the Intel 14900K and Apple M3 Max can run their full test suite in under half the time of their cloud CI setup. He argues this represents a broader pattern of using hardware improvements to simplify development stacks rather than adding capability. He acknowledges this won't work for massive codebases like Shopify or GitHub, but contends that 99.99% of web apps are small enough to benefit from this approach.
key_points: Local developer machines now outperform remote CI runners for HEY's test suite, completing in under 2.5 minutes vs 5.5 minutes remotely
Modern CPUs with 8-20+ cores make parallelized local testing feasible in ways that were unthinkable in the mid-2010s
The real benefit isn't speed but simplification — eliminating the operational complexity of remote CI infrastructure
Hardware progress should be spent on simplifying stacks, not adding complexity: #nobuild, local CI, dropping Spring
Most web apps are small enough to run CI locally, even if tech giants with millions of lines of code cannot
money_quotes: Getting to flush all of it down the simplification drain is an amazing step forward.
Never aspire to a more complicated stack than what your application calls for.
Progress is a bonfire.
Small teams ought to remove all the moving parts possible.
Installing, operating, and caring for a remote CI setup is a substantial complication.
The simplified future is not evenly distributed.
Running all these checks and validations in a reasonable time on a local machine would have been unthinkable not too long ago.
posts.key_insight: When hardware gets faster, the best use of that performance gain is eliminating infrastructure complexity rather than scaling up what you build on top of it.

posts.summary: DHH argues that programmers irrationally trust cloud and SaaS infrastructure over their own machines, a phenomenon he links to imposter syndrome. He uses the example of moving CI back to developer machines, where critics invoked 'it works on my machine' as if cloud computers were fundamentally different. He contends that all computers are equally fallible, testing is about confidence rather than certainty, and running tests on multiple developer machines is actually a feature that uncovers hidden dependencies. The post concludes that the perceived superiority of cloud operators is an illusion created by opaqueness, not actual competence.
key_points: Programmers suffer from a form of imposter syndrome that makes them trust remote infrastructure over their own machines
Cloud computers are not magically more reliable — they're just other computers with the same potential for faults
Running CI on multiple developer machines is a feature that helps uncover undeclared dependencies and environmental issues
Testing provides degrees of confidence, not certainty — the pursuit of 100% certainty leads to programmer misery
The perceived competence of cloud operators comes from opaqueness, not actual superiority
money_quotes: There are no magic machines and no magic operators. Just the same kind of potentially faulty bits and brains.
The computer running tests remotely is indeed just that: Another computer. It isn't magical, and it's no less prone to be reliant on unaccounted for dependencies or environmental factors.
When it comes to testing, it's a feature not a bug to have the suite run on multiple machines.
The road to programmer misery is paved with delusional aspirations that you can ever be fully, truly certain that any sufficiently complicated system will ever work as intended in production.
All you have is degrees of confidence to trade-off against increasingly cumbersome protocols and procedures.
If only you knew what kind of frazzled mechanical turk it takes to run most cloud institutions or SaaS operations, you wouldn't be so quick to doubt your own abilities.
"It works on my computer" is just the midwit version of "it works on THAT computer". It's all just computers.
posts.key_insight: The trust programmers place in cloud infrastructure over their own machines is an illusion born from imposter syndrome and the opaqueness of remote operations, not from any real difference in reliability.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the announcement that RailsConf 2025 will be the last RailsConf, framing it as a positive transition rather than a loss. He argues that with The Rails Foundation now organizing Rails World (which sold out in twenty minutes), Ruby Central can focus on its core Ruby mission while the Foundation handles Rails-specific advocacy. He reminisces about the early explosive growth of RailsConf from 400 attendees to 1,800, and the memorable keynotes he delivered. He briefly alludes to pandemic-era controversies but pivots to optimism about Rails' future as a counter to ZIRP-era complexity.
key_points: RailsConf 2025 will be the final RailsConf, replaced by Rails World under The Rails Foundation
The split clarifies responsibilities: Ruby Central handles Ruby/RubyGems/Bundler, The Rails Foundation handles Rails
Rails World sold out its thousand-attendee conference in under twenty minutes, proving strong demand
The post-ZIRP complexity hangover makes Rails' simplicity philosophy more relevant than ever
DHH frames pandemic-era community controversies as a catalyst that helped motivate creating The Rails Foundation
money_quotes: We bootstrapped something from nothing, turned it into an epoch-defining event.
The world has woken up from the ZIRP years with a complexity hang-over, and Rails is the perfect painkiller.
The ideal of the one-person framework has never been more relevant.
We never went away, but the renaissance is palpable none the less.
That kind of nonsense is thankfully now largely behind us, not just in the Ruby world, but in tech in general.

Let's go out with a bang in 2025, celebrate the legacy, and then keep plucking away on spreading the joy of beautiful code, incredible productivity, and programmer-centric development with Ruby on Rails.
posts.key_insight: The end of RailsConf isn't a decline but an institutional maturation, with The Rails Foundation taking the reins at the precise moment Rails' simplicity philosophy is most needed in a post-ZIRP tech landscape.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the backlash against Apple's iPad 'Crush' ad represents something far bigger than one bad commercial — it signals that Apple has exhausted its reservoir of public goodwill. He draws parallels to Microsoft's fall from grace in the 1990s, noting that Apple's fan-boy defenders have gone conspicuously quiet. The company's transition from beloved underdog to near-three-trillion-dollar incumbent battling regulators has left it vulnerable to exactly this kind of tipping point. DHH predicts Apple's era of easy street is over, though the company likely won't adapt until after several more embarrassments.
key_points: The iPad ad's destruction of musical instruments is a perfect metaphor for how tone-deaf technologists can be about digitization's cultural costs
Apple has spent down its goodwill over five years and reached a tipping point where its usual apologists are no longer defending it
The pattern mirrors Microsoft's sudden fall from grace in the 1990s — reputations evaporate seemingly overnight once enough critical stories accumulate
Companies that train themselves to ignore haters can't distinguish between normal criticism and a genuine shift in public sentiment
Apple's identity has fundamentally changed from underdog innovator to incumbent monopolist shipping incremental updates
money_quotes: It symbolizes everything everyone has ever hated about digitization.
The real story is just how little saved up goodwill Apple had in the bank to compensate for the outrage.
Apple has lost its presumption of good faith over the last five years with an ever-larger group of people, and now we've reached a tipping point.
It's awfully quiet on the fan-boy front.
Companies like Apple almost have to ignore the haters as the cost of doing business, but then they also can't easily tell when the sentiment has changed from 'the usual number' to 'one too many'.
Nobody is lining up with a tent to buy a new iPhone any more.
Everything is great until it isn't.
posts.key_insight: Apple's backlash over a single bad ad reveals that the company has quietly exhausted decades of accumulated goodwill, and like Microsoft before it, may not recognize its own tipping point until it's too late.

posts.summary: DHH declares that DEI (Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion) as a corporate movement is finished, citing declining mentions in earnings reports as evidence. He frames the DEI era as a period of 'temporary insanity' driven by the premise that racial disparities could only be explained by systemic racism. He argues this led to a culture of intimidation where dissenters were punished, and characterizes the movement's decline as a welcome correction. He calls for vigilance against similar movements in the future.
key_points: DEI mentions in corporate earnings reports have plummeted, which DHH treats as proof the movement is over
DHH frames DEI as ideological sanctimony built on the false premise that all racial disparities stem from systemic racism
He argues DEI led to compensatory discrimination against Asians and white people
The DEI era created a culture of intimidation where questioning orthodoxy led to social punishment
He calls for hardening defenses against similar future movements
money_quotes: DEI, as an obsessive, ideological preoccupation of the corporate world, is done.
This graph perfectly captures the temporary insanity of what those nutty years were really like in corporate America.
An explosion of sanctimony, triggered by the fallacy that racial disparities in the office (and elsewhere) could only be explained by systemic racism.
With this explosion of sanctimony came a brief but suffocating culture of intimidation.
Anyone who dared question the theology of high priests of this new religion, like DiAngelo or Kendi, were hounded and frequently banished by ideological thugs.
There were pitchforks enough to chase all transgressors, big or small.
The People are sick of this shit.
All there's left to do is mop up, then make sure we harden our defenses for the next time agitating embers come flying.
posts.key_insight: DHH declares victory over corporate DEI, framing its decline in earnings reports as vindication that it was always ideological sanctimony rather than genuine progress.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the conventional wisdom of buying from big tech companies for safety is outdated and wrong. Meta's decision to shut down Workplace, despite millions of paying users, demonstrates that big tech treats non-core products as disposable. Companies like Spotify and McDonalds now face forced migration because Meta's ad business dwarfs Workplace revenue. DHH contends that smaller vendors who depend on your business are actually the safer bet.
key_points: The 'nobody got fired for buying IBM' mentality is a false sense of security in the modern tech landscape
Big tech's massively profitable core businesses make even million-user products seem negligible and expendable
Meta's Workplace shutdown and Google Domains' closure form a pattern of big tech abandoning paying customers
Smaller companies that depend on your revenue are inherently more motivated to maintain their products
money_quotes: The saying "nobody ever got fired for buying IBM" is at its essence about risk management.
Any company that hitched their wagon to Workplace just got served with an eviction notice.
Simply because Meta can't be bothered to maintain a platform that's merely used by millions when their consumer business is used by billions.
This, right here, is the risk of buying anything from big tech like Meta and Google.

Their main ad-based cash cows are so fantastically profitable that whether it's the millions of paying accounts on Workplace or the millions of live websites once hosted by Google Domains, it all just pales in comparison, and is thus one strategy rotation away from being labeled "non-core" and killed off.
Buying from big isn't the sure bet they want you to believe. Buy from someone who actually needs your business to make the wheels go round.
posts.key_insight: Big tech's enormous core revenue makes every secondary product disposable, making smaller vendors who depend on your business the actually safer long-term bet.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the definition of 'normal' has narrowed dramatically since the 1980s and 90s, when kids with various quirks and differences were simply considered part of the normal spectrum. He contends that society now incentivizes identifying with marginalized categories, making it socially desirable to acquire diagnostic labels rather than be seen as ordinary. Referencing Abigail Shrier's 'Bad Therapy,' he points to a therapy industry eager to pathologize normal childhood behaviors. He calls for re-expanding the definition of normal to encompass a broader range of human variation, with fewer labels and fewer medical interventions.
key_points: Behaviors once considered normal childhood variation are now routinely pathologized with diagnostic labels
'Centering the margins' has made it socially desirable to identify as non-normal and low status to be regular
A therapy industry has grown eager to supply diagnoses and pills for what were once seen as normal phases of development
The pursuit of individuality through marginalized identity has replaced older forms of social differentiation like brand preferences
The definition of normal should be re-expanded to fit a broader spectrum, with labels reserved for severe cases
money_quotes: Nobody seems to qualify (or desire to be seen) as being normal these days.
This is the natural consequence of 'centering the margins'. Of making it socially desirable to be not normal and low status to be regular.
There's cachet of cool to be had in identifying with some margin. Preferably one that can claim to be oppressed by society via heteronormativity or neurotypicality or other big words for 'normal'.
There's a large industry of therapists and other 'mental health professionals' eager to accommodate this flight to the margins.
I think we've given up on something important in this pursuit of individuality through the spectrum of marginalization.
When fitting in is less about having the right brand of backpack and more about having some medical prescription.
We're better off re-expanding the definition of normal to fit a much broader spectrum of quirky, weird, and natural varieties of humans.
posts.key_insight: Society has narrowed the definition of normal to the point where ordinary human variation is pathologized, and we should reverse course by expanding what counts as normal rather than labeling every difference.

posts.summary: DHH argues that open source projects are not democracies and using open source software doesn't entitle users to influence project direction. He contends that the word 'community' misleadingly implies egalitarian participation, when in reality projects are run by small groups of elite contributors who do the actual work. He defends this hierarchy as morally correct — productive effort should be the basis of power. He suggests 'ecosystem' as a better metaphor than 'community,' and frames open source as a gift exchange rather than an entitlement program.
key_points: The term 'community' creates false expectations of democratic governance in open source projects
Open source elitism is good — projects should be driven by those who demonstrate dedication and competence through actual work
Open source burnout is often caused by user entitlement, not lack of compensation
The democratic ideal in open source is fulfilled through freedom to fork and choose alternatives, not through voting
Open source is better understood as a gift exchange or ecosystem than a community
money_quotes: Using open source software does not entitle you to a vote on the direction of the project.
Elitism is good, when it comes to open source. You absolutely want projects to be driven by the people who show up to do the work, demonstrate their superior dedication and competence.
Productive effort is the correct moral basis of power in these projects.
It is not, and never will be, one user, one vote. That is, it will never be democratic. And this is good!
Don't like how they're running a given project? Use one of the usual countless alternatives. Or start your own!
When people talk about open source burnout, it's often related to this entitlement syndrome. Although it's frequently misdiagnosed as a problem of compensation.
Ecosystems aren't egalitarian. There are big fish and little fish. Sometimes the relationships are symbiotic, but they're also potentially parasitic.
Open source is first and foremost a method of collaboration between programmers who show up to do the work. Not an entitlement program for petulant users.
posts.key_insight: Open source governance is rightfully meritocratic, not democratic — the people who do the work earn the authority, and the gift of free software is the only entitlement users receive.

posts.summary: DHH criticizes the AI gold rush mentality driving companies to make desperate, privacy-violating decisions. He uses Salesforce's announcement that Slack will train AI models on users' private messages by default as a prime example. He argues companies are acting out of fear of missing out rather than genuine excitement about AI's potential. The opt-out process—sending a specially formatted email to a feedback address—reveals how little these companies respect user consent. DHH predicts this pattern of trust and privacy violations will accelerate as companies scramble for any competitive edge in the AI race.
key_points: The AI boom is driven more by corporate fear of being left behind than genuine enthusiasm about the technology's promise
Salesforce training Slack AI on private messages by default, with a deliberately cumbersome opt-out process, exemplifies this desperation
Companies that can't compete on public data are resorting to exploiting confidential user communications for AI training
The race for AI advantage will likely produce more flagrant violations of user trust and privacy
money_quotes: I've never seen so much paranoia in technology about missing out on The Next Big Thing as with AI.
Companies seem less excited about the prospects than they are petrified that its going to kill them.
Presumably this is because some Salesforce executives got the great idea in a brainstorming sesh that the way to catch up to the big players in AI is by just ignoring privacy concerns all together.

If you can't beat the likes of OpenAI in scanning the sum of public human knowledge, maybe you can beat them by scanning all the confidential conversations about new product strategies, lay-off plans that haven't been announced yet, or private financial projections for 2025?
All you have to do is edit out, say, Asana in your layoff completion, and voila, you'll have saved at least 8 minutes typing out the corporate slop yourself.

Things are moving fast enough that we'll probably see more such flagrant transgressions of trust and privacy, if there's even a sliver of a chance that it can provide an edge in the race for a better chatbot.
posts.key_insight: The AI gold rush is producing a corporate paranoia so intense that companies like Salesforce will sacrifice user privacy and trust for even marginal competitive advantage in the race to build AI products.

posts.summary: DHH declares that system tests, which he helped introduce as a default in Rails 5.1 in 2016, have not lived up to their promise. Despite the theoretical advantages of driving a headless browser through the actual interface, he finds them slow, brittle, and plagued by false negatives. He argues that model and controller tests handle business logic better, while UI testing is best done by humans clicking around in a real browser. HEY is cutting its 300+ system tests down significantly, acknowledging the sunk cost fallacy that kept them running too long.
key_points: System tests remain slow, brittle, and full of false negatives despite years of investment, making them not worth the effort for most use cases
Model and controller tests handle business logic testing better and more cheaply than system tests
System tests should be retained only as top-level smoke tests to catch configuration or loading issues
Human testing provides more confidence for UI logic because it captures both 'does it work' and 'does it feel right'
HEY is undergoing a grand review to drastically cut its 300+ system tests, overcoming sunk cost fallacy
money_quotes: System tests remain as slow, brittle, and full of false negatives as they did a decade ago.
I've wasted far more time getting system tests to work reliably than I have seen dividends from bugs caught.
We do it for the quick feedback loop on changes, we do it to catch regressions, but most of all, we do it to become confident that the system works.
System tests work well for the top-level smoke test.
The method that gives me the most confidence that my UI logic is good to go is not system tests, but human tests. Literally clicking around in a real browser by hand.
Half the time UI testing is not just about 'does it work' but also 'does it feel right'. No automation can tell you that.
The sunk cost fallacy has kept us running this brittle, cumbersome suite for too long.
posts.key_insight: System tests have failed to deliver on their promise of reliable confidence, and most should be replaced by focused unit tests and human exploratory testing.

posts.summary: DHH argues that programmers wrongly dismiss aesthetics by associating it with superficial veneering. He contends that beauty is not just about surface appearance but serves as a powerful motivational force that drives great work. The pursuit of beauty requires extra care and effort that elevates the entire creation process. He calls on programmers to rediscover beauty in code, patterns, and tools—not as a monoculture of taste, but as a practice of making incremental improvements that fuel personal motivation and mission.
key_points: Programmers are right to be skeptical of cheap veneers, but wrong to discard aesthetics entirely because of them
Beauty requires extra effort that correlates with deeper care throughout the creation process
Motivation—not time or attention—is the rare fuel behind career breakthroughs, and beauty is its most potent source
Modern culture has deconstructed and problematized beauty to the point where pursuing it feels harder to justify
Beauty is not binary but a journey of a thousand small decisions to make something marginally better
money_quotes: Programmers are often skeptical of aesthetics because they frequently associate it with veneering.
Discarding the value of aesthetic on behalf of cheap imitations is a mistake.
The primary reason I appreciate aesthetics so much is its power to motivate.
It's not time, it's even attention. It's motivation.
Nothing quite motivates me like using and creating beautiful things.
We've managed to deconstruct and problematize so much of what is self-evidently beautiful that it's harder to take the chase for granted.
Beauty isn't binary. It's the journey of a thousand little decisions and investments in making something marginally prettier than it was before.
Resist the urge to just make it work, and not stop until you make it shine.
posts.key_insight: Beauty in software is not superficial decoration but a powerful motivational force that drives programmers to produce their best work through accumulated care in a thousand small decisions.

posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals is shifting its default developer platform from Mac to Linux after over twenty years of Apple monoculture. He frames this as aligning the company's tools with its open-source values while gaining practical benefits like better understanding Windows-using customers. The change is a default, not a mandate — mobile developers still need Macs, and personal preferences are respected. DHH emphasizes supporting companies like Framework that represent the independent future he wants to see, while acknowledging Apple still makes great hardware.
key_points: After 20+ years of Mac monoculture, 37signals is making Linux the default for developers and system operators
The shift aligns the company's tooling with its open-source values and reduces dependency on Apple's ecosystem
Defaults matter even when they're not mandates — they set institutional tone and direct spending toward preferred vendors like Framework
Running Mac, Windows, and Linux across the company better reflects how customers actually use their products
The move is partly motivated by growing philosophical disagreements with Apple's direction on computing freedom
money_quotes: My entire career has been spent in the service and sun of open source, both as a contributor and a beneficiary, and closing the loop with a desktop operating system is very satisfying.
Default doesn't mean edict, though.
I love voting with my dollars for more of a future I'd like to see, and Framework represents just that.
Apple has evolved into a company that's much harder to recommend for people who care about the future of computing, but they still make great hardware.
None of this has to be binary. Hate/love. Yes/no.
Going forward, we'll spend more of our money and attention on platforms that align better with the independence and freedom we so cherish in all other aspects of our business.
The year of Linux on the desktop. Who would have thought!
posts.key_insight: A company's default tools should reflect its values, and for an open-source-driven shop increasingly at odds with Apple's closed ecosystem, Linux is the natural realignment.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his evolution from an aggressive tech evangelist to a more accepting stance on programming language diversity. He recounts how Rails' success came not from pointed arguments against competitors, but from the famous 15-minute demo video that simply showed what was possible. He argues that you can't convert people through dunking or debate — you can only attract those already dissatisfied with their current tools. He concludes that programmers come in different intellectual shapes, and finding the language that clicks with your mind is more important than any objective trade-off analysis.
key_points: Rails succeeded because of a demo showing what was possible, not through arguments against competing frameworks
You can't dunk someone into submission — attraction works better than persuasion in technology adoption
Programmers have different intellectual shapes and sizes, and ecosystem diversity is a feature not a bug
Finding a language that connects you to an enduring source of motivation matters more than objective trade-off analysis
Technical discussions are more effective when framed as 'here's what I like and why' rather than crusading against alternatives
money_quotes: You simply can't dunk someone into submission, and it's usually counterproductive if you try.
I've never seen someone who was head'over'heels in love with, say, functional programming be won over by arguments for objected-oriented programming.
The fact that working for the web allows for such diverse ecosystem choices is an incredible feature, not a bug.
Half the programming conundrum lies in connecting to an enduring source of motivation.
There is no universal set of trade-offs that'll make something objectively 'work best'.
I would give up all the fancy cars I have in a heartbeat, if I was made to choose between them and programming.
Programming is one of those 'best things' that is virtually free to everyone in the Western world.
posts.key_insight: Technology adoption is driven not by winning arguments against alternatives, but by demonstrating something compelling and letting dissatisfied programmers discover it for themselves.

posts.summary: DHH introduces Omakub, an opinionated, one-command setup that transforms a fresh Ubuntu installation into a fully-configured web development environment. He argues that Linux's power comes with a paradox of choice and a steep learning curve that deters newcomers. Omakub applies the 'omakase' philosophy—strong, curated defaults chosen by someone with taste—to lower the barrier for developers switching from Windows or Mac. The project targets future Linux converts who would trade bespoke customization for a cohesive, ready-made experience.
key_points: Linux's customizability creates a paradox of choice that intimidates newcomers and shouldn't require a rite-of-passage initiation
Omakub bundles curated applications, tools, and dotfiles into a single command for fresh Ubuntu installs, solving both package management and configuration
The project applies DHH's 'omakase' philosophy: opinionated defaults that provide a cohesive experience without requiring deep expertise
It targets developers coming from Windows or Mac who want Linux without the configuration legwork, not existing Linux power users
Good defaults can serve as a gateway—users may later customize fully or simply keep what works
money_quotes: Linux can look and feel so good, but it often doesn't out of the box.

It's almost like there's a rite of passage in certain parts of the community where becoming an expert in the intricacies of every tool and its theming is required to prove you're a proper nerd. I think that's a bit silly.
Linux gets great power from how customizable it is, but that also presents a paradox of choice and a tall learning curve.
Nothing in Omakub provides solutions to problems you couldn't also solve a million other ways.
The idea that an entire setup experience can benefit from being tailored upfront by someone with strong opinions about what works and looks good together.
There's a large constituency of developers who are more than willing to trade ultimate bespoke customization for a cohesive package of goods.
posts.key_insight: The biggest barrier to Linux adoption isn't capability but curation—developers need opinionated, cohesive defaults more than infinite customization options.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the thrill of discovering technologies and ideas before they reach mainstream adoption. He draws a line from his early embrace of Mac, Ruby, and Campfire through to his current enthusiasm for Linux on the desktop and web development simplification via #nobuild and Hotwire. He argues that recognizing potential in rough, unpolished ideas requires a certain contrarian disposition and willingness to bet on being early. The post frames this pattern of early adoption as both intellectually rewarding and personally energizing.
key_points: DHH sees Linux as currently underappreciated the same way Mac was in 2001 and Ruby was in 2003
Web development has undergone an epoch-altering simplification through the convergence of better browsers, faster mobile CPUs, #nobuild, and Hotwire
Seeing potential in unpolished ideas requires disagreeableness and even arrogance — a belief that the marketplace of ideas isn't perfectly efficient
The compounding effect of multiple incremental improvements matters more than any single breakthrough
money_quotes: Nothing gets me quite as fired up as discovering the future early and undistributed.
That feeling of realizing that something is simply better, and the only reason it hasn't taken off yet is because the world hasn't realized it.
When I discover a path that seems like a clear shortcut, it doesn't really matter if it's poorly paved at first.
It's incredible how much conceptual complexity we've been able to compress in the last decade.
Following such a sense of wanderlust requires a certain disagreeableness. Even arrogance.
A steadfast belief that it's possible that you might actually have found a better way.
You have to believe that the market place of ideas isn't perfectly efficient or perfectly rational.
A life spent without taking at least a few bets on being early on an idea is one not lived to the fullest.
posts.key_insight: The greatest rewards in technology come from recognizing the potential in rough, unpolished ideas before the mainstream catches on, which requires a willingness to be contrarian and bet on your own judgment.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his successful transition from Apple's ecosystem to Linux and Android after two decades. He emphasizes that the switch was far easier than expected and has been genuinely enjoyable rather than merely an act of rebellion. He promotes his Omakub Linux setup and highlights Android's real openness advantages like sideloading apps and replacing default launchers. Rather than trying to convince satisfied Apple users to switch, he addresses those already dissatisfied, assuring them the alternatives are genuinely good on their own merits.
key_points: Switching away from Apple after 20 years proved far easier than anticipated, and entrenched habits create a false belief that change is impossible
Falling in love with something new is a healthier motivator for switching than hating something old
Running open source software feels right for someone whose career was built on open source, and Linux has become genuinely compelling
Android's openness is real and practical: sideloading apps, replacing launchers, and customizing the experience in ways iOS forbids
Changing platforms forces you to discover new tools and approaches you'd never have considered otherwise
money_quotes: What seemed so difficult at first now appears trivial, since it's been done.
Falling in love with something new is a better and healthier motivator than hating something old.
Running an open source operating system simply feels right for someone who owes their entire career to open source.
Their value isn't defined as not-Apple. There's no valor in that.
Linux is wonderful, is flawed, is messy, is beautiful, is nerdy, is different.
Don't think of the journey as a way to find the same home in a different place. Be open to a new home, in a new way, in a new place.
That's what a change of scenery can do for you. Force you to open your eyes to what else is out there.
posts.key_insight: Leaving Apple's ecosystem after two decades revealed that the perceived difficulty of switching was mostly habit-driven fear, and that Linux and Android offer genuinely compelling experiences defined by their own strengths rather than as Apple alternatives.

posts.summary: DHH shares his recent discovery of mechanical keyboards and the satisfying 'thock' sound they produce. After years of contentment with Apple's Magic Keyboard, he fell into the mechanical keyboard rabbit hole and found unexpected daily joy in the typing experience. He celebrates the incredible variety and affordability in the mechanical keyboard market as a triumph of capitalism and competition. He recommends two specific boards — the Varmilo Milo 75 and Lofree Flow 84 — as entry points for anyone curious about upgrading their typing experience.
key_points: Mechanical keyboards don't solve problems — they add sensory delight to the mundane act of typing, which is its own reward
The mechanical keyboard market showcases capitalism at its best: hundreds of manufacturers competing on niche details at increasingly affordable prices
The hobby has evolved from requiring DIY assembly to offering excellent prebuilt options, making it far more accessible
DHH had false starts with Keychron boards before finding keyboards he truly enjoyed, suggesting first impressions of the hobby can be misleading
money_quotes: Mechanical keyboards don't solve any problems. In fact, if anything, they bring quite a few with them!
Taking greater joy from such a mundane activity as typing is one of those rare daily treats that just makes life a little better, a little more interesting.
It's also a benign rabbit hole of lingo, knowledge, aesthetics, and preference. Kinda like mechanical watches, but mercifully more affordable.
The sheer variety and depth of this market would make Adam Smith blush.
I absolutely love leveling up on a new domain like this!
Both look amazing, feel amazing, and make a mockery of the $100 Apple Magic Keyboard that I thought was all I ever needed for so long.
posts.key_insight: The best tools aren't always about solving problems — sometimes they're about adding unexpected delight to the daily grind of work you're already doing.

posts.summary: DHH uses the persistence of cookie banners as evidence that the EU is fundamentally incapable of effective tech regulation. He argues that billions spent on cookie banner compliance have produced no meaningful privacy improvements, yet the EU has no mechanism to self-correct this legislative failure. He extends this critique to newer regulations like the Digital Markets Act and AI legislation, suggesting that if the EU can't get something as basic as cookies right, more complex regulation is doomed. He concludes that Europe's regulatory approach is actively squandering its entrepreneurial potential and driving innovation elsewhere.
key_points: Cookie banners cost billions in compliance while providing no material privacy improvement to users
The EU lacks any mechanism for self-correcting legislative failures, even with years of evidence
Europe's AI legislation is already drawing second thoughts from its own architect, following the same pattern of vague, overreaching regulation
Europe's tech innovation is largely incubation-stage, with companies leaving for America once they're ready to scale
The cookie banner failure should serve as a cautionary lens for evaluating all EU tech legislation
money_quotes: Companies have spent billions on cookie banner compliance only to endlessly annoy users with no material improvement to their privacy.
The bureaucratic maze almost guarantees that all the noble intentions eventually find a dead end in which to get stuck.
If the EU can screw up something as basic as cookie banners this bad, and prove so incapable and uninterested in re-calibrating its approach, what hope is there for the thornier issues?
Like many modern European laws, it's a vague, posturing, and suffocating mess that's way too specific about things it fails to prove the ability to understand.
So little of the core tech innovation that's driving the future, in AI or otherwise, is happening in Europe.
If you want different outcomes, you have to start doing different things.
posts.key_insight: The EU's inability to fix its own cookie banner failure after years of evidence reveals a regulatory system structurally incapable of governing technology effectively.

posts.summary: DHH reviews the new high-resolution screen upgrade for the Framework 13 laptop, which resolves his last major complaint about the device. The new 2880x1920 display delivers 256 PPI — beating the MacBook Pro — while retaining the matte finish and 3:2 aspect ratio he loves. He highlights that the screen can be retrofitted to existing Framework laptops for $269, taking about five minutes to install. He declares it the best laptop screen he's ever used for programming and argues the Framework 13 AMD version is a better deal than a MacBook Pro for developers.
key_points: The new Framework 13 screen upgrades to 2880x1920 (256 PPI), surpassing MacBook Pro's display density while keeping the matte finish, 3:2 ratio, and adding 120Hz
The screen is retrofittable to existing Framework laptops for $269, embodying the right-to-repair and anti-waste philosophy
For programming specifically, the combination of high PPI, matte coating, and 3:2 aspect ratio beats Apple's offerings
The fully configured AMD Framework 13 costs roughly half what a comparable MacBook Pro does
Linux still lacks mature variable refresh rate support, so 120Hz on battery has a cost, but 60Hz is a workable compromise
money_quotes: The combination of 256 PPI, matte covering, 3:2 aspect ratio, and 120hz is amazing for working with text.
It takes the last reservation I had about the Framework 13 and turns it into a key strength.
It's a $269 upgrade, so it's not cheap, but it's a hellavu lot cheaper than buying a whole new computer. Not to mention way less wasteful.
The installation is involved enough that you feel like you've made a real personal contribution to your hardware, but without feeling complicated or onerous.
I'd go so far as to say that this is the best looking laptop screen I've ever used for programming.
But for programmers? The Framework is better.
That's a ton of amazing computer for the money (and almost half of what a MacBook Pro with 24GB of RAM and 1TB of storage costs!).
posts.key_insight: The Framework 13's new retrofittable high-res matte screen eliminates its last weakness and makes it a better programming laptop than a MacBook Pro at half the price.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his journey from dismissing American patriotic self-image as delusional to genuinely appreciating the First Amendment's value. He argues that recent crackdowns on speech in the UK, EU, and Canada—punishing 'misinformation' and 'hate speech' with harsh sentences and regulatory threats—have vindicated America's constitutional free speech protections. He uses the campy patriotic song 'God Bless the USA' as a framing device, admitting it now rings true to him as a Danish immigrant. His core argument is that America's First Amendment has proven a uniquely resilient bulwark against the new wave of censorship spreading across Western democracies.
key_points: The UK's response to riots includes fast-track tribunals handing out harsh sentences for speech offenses like 'anti-establishment rhetoric' and criticism of migration policy
The EU is using the Digital Markets Act—originally meant to counter tech monopolies—as a tool to pressure platforms into censoring speech
Western democracies that criticized the Patriot Act are now embracing similar crisis-driven authoritarian measures around speech
America's First Amendment stands out as uniquely protective compared to other Western nations' approaches to regulating speech
The right to 'be full of shit' is preferable to any government claiming authority to define truth and falsehood a priori
money_quotes: I used to find the American self-image of being this uniquely freedom-loving, freedom-having people delusional. Turns out I just needed to wait a little longer for the patriotic hymns to begin ringing true.
There's a shockingly draconian crackdown on 'misinformation' and 'hate speech', the two stalwart euphemisms for 'speech we don't like', going on in Britain in particular at the moment.
Weren't The Enlightened Ones supposed to recognize the dangers of using a crisis to push through police-state measures?

America's first amendment, the constitutional right to free speech, including the right to 'misinformation' and even 'hate speech', has proven a surprisingly effective and resilient bulwark against the new rise of censorship and blasphemy laws gaining steam across the Atlantic.
I'd much rather live in a country that embraces everyone's right to BE FULL OF SHIT than one that pretends it can declare a priori what's true and what's false.
I'm not an American. But I sure am becoming a lot more appreciative of the core principles my adopted country was founded on.
The Digital Markets Act, which I've in the past applauded for its attempt to counter tech monopolies, is being wielded as the cudgel for this authoritarian crackdown.
posts.key_insight: Recent speech crackdowns across Western democracies have vindicated America's First Amendment as a uniquely effective protection against government-imposed censorship, even for a skeptical immigrant.

posts.summary: DHH argues that software estimation is fundamentally broken and has been for sixty years, yet the industry keeps trying the same failed approach. He contends that estimatable work gets commoditized into products like WordPress or Shopify, leaving only novel work that's inherently unpredictable. Instead of better estimates, he advocates replacing them with fixed budgets and flexible scope — what 37signals calls 'appetites' in their Shape Up methodology. The key insight is that programmers deliver great results when given a time budget but allowed to negotiate scope during development.
key_points: Software estimation has failed consistently for sixty years, making continued attempts at it delusional
Estimatable software development gets commoditized into products, so remaining work is inherently novel and unpredictable
You can't specify novel work upfront — real requirements only emerge after building the wrong solution first
The solution is fixed budgets with flexible scope, not better estimation techniques
Programmers are surprisingly good at delivering on time when scope remains negotiable
money_quotes: Estimating even medium-sized projects is devilishly difficult, and estimating large projects is virtually impossible.
Yet the industry keeps insisting that the method that hasn't worked for sixty years will definitely work on this next project, if we all just try a little harder. It's the definition of delusional.

The fundamental problem is that as soon as a type of software development becomes so routine that it would be possible to estimate, it turns into a product or a service you can just buy rather than build.
The thing about novel work is that nobody knows exactly what it should look like until they start building.

Nobody cared for the outcome. Because it invariably didn't end up solving the real problems. The ones you could only articulate after building half of a wrong solution, changing direction, and then coming up with something better.
It turns out that programmers are actually surprisingly good at delivering great software on time, if you leave the scope open to negotiation during development.
What you asked for before you began building was based on the absolute worst understanding of the problem.
Great software is the product of trade-offs and concessions made while making progress.
posts.key_insight: Instead of trying to estimate unpredictable software work, fix the budget and let scope flex — programmers deliver better results when they can negotiate what to build within a time constraint.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the tech industry thrives on selling unnecessary complexity to people who equate sophistication with value. He contends that vendors exploit insecurities—fear, vanity, pride—to convince buyers that simple, ownable solutions are beneath them, when in reality most problems don't require expensive, subscription-based tools. The post frames this as a psychological trap that people can break free from simply by choosing to. He closes by encouraging readers to embrace simplicity across their tech stack.
key_points: People claim to want simplicity but consistently buy complexity because it makes them feel special
The tech industry manufactures fear and insecurity to sell subscription-based solutions to basic problems
The shift from ownership to rentals (subscriptions) is a deliberate strategy to extract ongoing revenue
The complexity spell only works as long as you believe in it—opting out is always within your power
money_quotes: It's hard to sell simple, because simple looks easy, and who wants to pay for that?
Everyone says they want something simple, but the way they buy reveals that they usually don't.
Few people have the courage to admit their life and work isn't that complicated.
Basic problems people could easily solve for themselves, cheaply and quickly, getting turned into scary and insurmountable challenges that only a sophisticated solution will cure.
They'll own nothing and you'll convince them they should be happy.
By what sorcery? Fear, mostly. Vanity, sometimes. Sloth, occasionally. Pride, definitely.
The spell only binds as long as you choose to believe in it.
Dare to be basic, babe.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry profits by making simple problems look complex, exploiting insecurity to sell sophisticated subscriptions for what people could easily solve themselves.

posts.summary: DHH addresses the global decline in birth rates, noting that no comprehensive theory explains why it's happening simultaneously across vastly different countries.
