Rather than proposing a political or economic explanation, he makes a personal case for having children based on the profound, almost inexpressible emotional transformation parenthood brings. He distinguishes between happiness and meaning, arguing that while children may reduce day-to-day happiness, they provide a deep, lasting sense of purpose. He contends that the case against having kids is easy to articulate (less time, money, freedom) while the case for it is nearly impossible to put into words, and this asymmetry is hurting prospective parents' decision-making.
key_points: Birth rates are dropping globally with no consensus explanation, and most theories merely reflect political priors that collapse under cross-country comparison
The emotional transformation of parenthood is so profound it cannot be adequately conveyed in words, creating an asymmetry where the costs are easily articulated but the rewards are not
Parenthood provides deep meaning rather than happiness — a distinction that surveys measuring parental 'unhappiness' fail to capture
DHH uses the trolley problem to illustrate the absolute, unhesitating commitment parents feel: he'd sacrifice himself or a million strangers for his children without a second thought
The inability to communicate parenthood's rewards may itself be contributing to declining birth rates
money_quotes: There's no comprehensive theory as to why this is happening all over the world and at the same time.
Before having kids, I did not understand what it would be like. That might sound obvious, but I think it's actually not.
I imagine it's like explaining 'green' to a person who can't see color.
If that lever controls a train heading for my kids or a million strangers, it's not a hard choice at all. I don't even have to think about it for a second.
It illustrates the difference between 'meaning' and 'happiness' perfectly.
Happiness comes and goes, but the meaning of being directly connected by DNA to a human you brought into this world is ever lasting.
It's easy to describe in words how having less time, less money, less freedom sounds like a raw deal. It's very hard to describe the purpose granted by parenthood.
Contributing with your own DNA to the continuation of the species, and watching your own flesh and blood grow up, is a sublime experience.
posts.key_insight: The case for having children is losing to the case against because parenthood's profound rewards are nearly impossible to articulate, while its costs are obvious and easy to enumerate.

posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals has returned to its original configuration of having no full-time managers, after experimenting with traditional management roles for several years. Instead of dedicated managers, managerial duties like performance reviews, onboarding, and mentoring are distributed among senior individual contributors. He argues this minimalist approach dodges Parkinson's Law by preventing managerial work from expanding to fill available time. DHH acknowledges limitations—particularly around handling conflict and poor performance—but contends the arrangement works brilliantly at their scale of 60 people when you hire self-directed 'managers of one.' He concludes that after twenty-plus years, and having tried the alternative, he's fully committed to this model.
key_points: Managerial duties are distributed among senior ICs rather than concentrated in dedicated manager roles, with each lead responsible for one mentee
The arrangement dodges Parkinson's Law: without 40 hours dedicated to management, the work naturally compresses to only what's necessary—sometimes zero hours per week
The model depends on hiring 'managers of one'—self-directed people who don't need weekly check-ins to stay on track
The hardest part to distribute is handling conflict and poor performance, which must bubble up to leadership
Experimenting with traditional management structure for a few years actually reinforced DHH's conviction that the flat model is superior
money_quotes: We once more have no full-time managers whose sole function is to organize or direct the work of others.
It's impossible for a full-time manager to scale their interventions down to zero hours per week and still feel like they're doing their job.
This incentivizes a minimalist approach to management.
Some weeks that means the managerial overhead is literally zero, as everyone is just busy getting their work done, and nobody needs anything.
Small teams of highly competent managers of one can progress at an unbelievable pace.
If cases of poor performance keeps bubbling up, there's a fundamental problem somewhere in the process anyway, and that can usually only be definitively addressed by the top.
We're going to make this company work without full-time managers or die trying.
Spending a couple of years with a more traditional structure was a wonderful experience because it cemented this fact.
posts.key_insight: Distributing managerial duties among senior individual contributors rather than hiring dedicated managers creates a minimalist management culture that dodges Parkinson's Law and lets skilled, self-directed teams move at maximum speed.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his relationship with goal-setting, distinguishing between treadmill-style material goals (hit a revenue number, then chase the next) and transformative goals aimed at personal growth. He shares two examples: aspiring to match Kent Beck's public speaking mastery after seeing him at JAOO 2003, and pursuing the 24 Hours of Le Mans after only recently starting racing. He argues that the best goals are ambitious but not delusional, and that the real value lies not in reaching the destination but in who you become through the pursuit.
key_points: Material checkpoint goals (revenue milestones, etc.) create an endless treadmill with fleeting satisfaction
The best goals are transformative — chosen for what the pursuit will make of you, not what you'll get
There's a fine line between ambitious and delusional goals; impossible odds make a pursuit vain
Both mastery of public speaking and racing required years of deliberate growth, not shortcuts
Identity transformation ('becoming a racer' vs 'winning a race') is more valuable than achievement
money_quotes: I don't like goals that feel like checkpoints on a treadmill.
They make you reach for a million dollars in revenue, celebrate for a second, and then turn the chase to five million the minute after.
Now there's a fine line between goals that are ambitious and goals that are delusional.
If I had set a goal to be the fastest driver in the world at age 30, after only earning my driver's license at 25, I would have been delusional.
I intuitively knew that it wasn't just about the destination. If it was, there'd probably be many shortcuts I could have taken. But I wanted to take the long road. For what it would make of me.
It was not about winning a race, but about becoming a racer. It was not about giving a speech, but about becoming a speaker.
Think about what you'd like to become more often than thinking about what you'd like to get.
posts.key_insight: The most meaningful goals are chosen not for what you'll achieve but for who you'll become through the pursuit.

posts.summary: DHH argues that free speech protections in the West are under serious threat, drawing a parallel to the impermanence of all historical eras. He points to recent actions by Brazil's judiciary banning X, the EU threatening censorship of the platform, calls for Elon Musk's arrest, and the UK's crackdown on online speech as evidence of a rising authoritarian censorship movement. He warns Americans not to assume the First Amendment makes them immune, using a Game of Thrones analogy about how sixty years of peace can end abruptly. His conclusion is that the concepts of 'misinformation' and 'hate speech' are fig leaves for political censorship, and the only defense is rejecting the premise of benevolent censorship entirely.
key_points: Historical eras of stability and freedom always feel permanent to those living through them, but they never are
The political left, once the champion of free speech in the West, has shifted toward embracing censorship under the banners of fighting 'misinformation' and 'hate speech'
Multiple recent events — Brazil banning X, EU threats, calls for Musk's arrest, UK speech crackdowns — represent a coordinated authoritarian momentum across the West
Americans should not assume the First Amendment provides permanent protection against censorship trends
The only viable defense of free speech is to reject the concept of benevolent censorship entirely, since no one can consistently distinguish misinformation from truth without bias
money_quotes: I'm old enough to remember when it was the left of the west that carried the banner of free speech.
That long stretch of peaceful rule and commitment to protecting free speech seems to have evaporated from much of the left today.
In its place, we have the fig leaves of 'misinformation' and 'hate speech' failing to adequately cover the naked persecution of political adversaries.
We're just one succession crisis away from losing rights that have been taken for granted for several generations of Americans.
Yet just cause you're paranoid doesn't mean they're not after you.

Nobody has a monopoly on the truth, nobody can discern 'misinformation' from truth consistently or without bias, and nobody can define 'hate speech' in universally acceptable terms that don't recall blasphemy laws of centuries past.
The only way to defend free speech is to nuke the idea of benevolent censorship from orbit.
The alternative, betting on more speech to counter bad speech, isn't a guaranteed win every time, but it's by far the best option we've found so far.
posts.key_insight: Free speech protections are not permanent fixtures of Western civilization but historically contingent achievements that are actively being eroded by censorship movements disguised as fights against misinformation and hate speech.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Linux's low adoption rate among general users is analogous to why people don't exercise or read more — it requires effort that most people avoid. He acknowledges that casual computer users have no obligation to use Linux, but contends that programmers are different. Like firefighters who maintain physical fitness as a prerequisite for their job, developers should embrace Linux as a training ground for deeper computing proficiency. He points to his Omakub project as a way for developers to give Linux another look.
key_points: Linux's low adoption follows the same pattern as other beneficial-but-effortful activities like exercise and reading — most people choose the path of least resistance
Linux serves as a 'dojo' that forces users to develop deeper understanding of computing fundamentals through hands-on problem solving
Programmers have a professional obligation to develop computing proficiency that casual users don't, making Linux a worthwhile investment for them
Developers who can navigate JavaScript build pipelines or AWS IAM already have the technical stamina needed for Linux
money_quotes: The world is full of free invitations to self-improvement that are ignored by most people most of the time.
Putting it crudely, it's easier to be fat and ignorant in a world of cheap, empty calories than it is to be fit and informed.
Linux isn't minimal effort. It's an operating system that demands more of you than does the commercial offerings from Microsoft and Apple.
It serves as a dojo for understanding computers better. With a sensei who keeps demanding you figure problems out on your own in order to learn and level up.
They're like firefighters. Fitness isn't the purpose of firefighting, but a prerequisite.
If you're able to figure out how to setup a modern build pipeline for JavaScript or even correctly configure IAM for AWS, you already have all the stamina you need for the Linux journey.
Not because it is easy, but because it is worth it.
posts.key_insight: Programmers should use Linux not because it's easy or convenient, but because the effort of mastering it builds foundational computing skills that make them better at their craft.

posts.summary: DHH argues that programmer productivity should be optimized over computational efficiency, since programmers are far more expensive than computing resources. He calculates that one programmer's salary equals roughly 3,663 server cores, making even small productivity gains enormously valuable in silicon terms. This has been Ruby on Rails' philosophy for twenty years: trade cheap compute time for expensive programmer time. He acknowledges the aesthetic appeal of squeezing maximum performance from hardware but argues it rarely makes economic sense for business software. The post positions this argument in the context of AI hype, suggesting that productivity tools like Rails have been solving the same economic problem AI promises to address.
key_points: One $200K/year programmer costs the equivalent of 3,663 AMD EPYC cores, making bio core optimization dramatically more cost-effective than silicon optimization
Many programmers have a misplaced moral sympathy for silicon efficiency that overrides the economic math favoring their own productivity
Ruby on Rails has been betting on programmer productivity over machine efficiency for 20 years, and the economics have only gotten more favorable
AI excitement in companies is driven by the same economic logic Rails always exploited: reducing the cost of expensive human programmers
Most business software doesn't need heroic optimization — computers have been fast enough for that work for years
money_quotes: Programmers continuing to become more expensive, computers continuing to become less so. Therefore, the smart bet was on making those programmers more productive EVEN AT THE EXPENSE OF THE COMPUTER!
They are in effect very expensive biological computing cores, and the real scarce resource.
The price of one biological core is the same as the price of 3663 silicon cores.
If you manage to make the bio core 10% more efficient, you will have saved the equivalent cost of 366 silicon cores.
Many of these squishy, biological programming cores have a distinctly human sympathy for their silicon counterparts that overrides the math.
There is something beautifully wholesome about making computers do more with fewer resources.
Most software around the world is priced on their inputs, not on the silicon it requires.
Best you deal in the economics of the present day, pick the most productive tool chain available to you, and bet that happy programmers will be the best bang for your buck.
posts.key_insight: Since one programmer costs as much as thousands of server cores, even marginal improvements to programmer productivity vastly outweigh any gains from optimizing computational efficiency.

posts.summary: DHH explains why 37signals abandoned their initial plan to build ONCE/Campfire's authentication entirely around passkeys. Despite investing significant engineering effort into a passkey-based system, they ripped it all out due to poor user experience. The core problem is that passkeys are tied to specific platforms and devices in ways users don't understand, creating lockout scenarios worse than traditional passwords. He argues that passkeys are essentially a halfway house to password managers but with less portability. Instead, they're sticking with passwords plus email-based second-factor authentication on first login from new devices. He concludes that passkeys aren't worth the implementation complexity or the UX pitfalls they introduce.
key_points: Passkeys are platform-locked in non-obvious ways, easily leaving users unable to access accounts across devices and operating systems
The cross-device solutions like QR code scanning are cumbersome and alien to most users
If you have to educate users about passkey pitfalls, you might as well teach them to use a cross-platform password manager instead
Email as a second factor on first login from a new device is a simpler, more universally understood alternative
Having a real problem (password reuse) doesn't mean every proposed solution is actually better
money_quotes: The problem with passkeys is that they're essentially a halfway house to a password manager, but tied to a specific platform in ways that aren't obvious to a user at all.

If you're going to teach someone how to deal with all of this, and all the potential pitfalls that might lock them out of your service, you almost might as well teach them how to use a cross-platform password manager like 1password.
But just because we have a real problem doesn't mean that all proposed solutions are actually going to be better.
Unless you accept the idea that all your passwords should be tied to one computing ecosystem, and thus make it hard to use alternative computers.
Passkeys seemed promising, but not all good intentions result in good solutions.
I'm disappointed to report that passkeys don't appear worth the complexity of implementation (which is substantial!) nor the complexity and gotchas of the user experience.
posts.key_insight: Passkeys solve a real problem but create worse ones by locking authentication to specific platforms and devices, making a good password manager the better practical choice.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his frustrating experience as a longtime Sonos megafan whose multi-room music system became unreliable, pushing him to explore competitors like Bluesound and WiiM. After months of debugging hardwired Sonos equipment with no resolution, he discovered that cheaper alternatives worked instantly and that Sonos's historic app advantage had been eroded by Spotify Connect. He draws a broader business lesson: once you force satisfied customers back into the market to sample competitors, you risk losing them permanently. He cites Basecamp's approach of maintaining legacy versions for happy customers as the right model, arguing that the cost of keeping existing customers content is far less than the cost of losing them.
key_points: Sonos's reliability problems—not just the app but firmware and connectivity—destroyed the 'it just works' experience that justified customer loyalty
Spotify Connect and cheaper competitors like WiiM ($299 vs $599) have eliminated Sonos's historic moat of app superiority and multi-room grouping
The worst thing a business can do is push satisfied customers back into the market where they discover viable alternatives
37signals runs three versions of Basecamp, including one unchanged for 14 years, specifically to avoid disrupting happy customers
The cost of maintaining backward compatibility and legacy products is worth paying compared to the cost of customer defection
money_quotes: When you want to listen to some music at dinner, you want play to mean PLAY RIGHT NOW. Not "won't connect". Not "try restarting the app". Just play.
If you've managed to win over a customer, the last thing you want to do is get them back into the market to sample the competition.
If you can keep them content with what they got, they aren't interested in whatever anyone else is offering.
That's why it's worth going the extra effort not to fuck with existing customers.
I'd been boiled by the Sonos problems for so long that I didn't even remember what instant does for enjoyment. It does a lot!
Don't push customers back into the market. They might just find that Spotify Connect is pretty awesome, and that amps half the price can do the job.
They signed up for a system they liked in the mid-to-late 2000s, and they're not in the market for a change. THAT'S JUST FINE BY US!
posts.key_insight: The greatest competitive advantage is customer inertia—never force satisfied customers to re-evaluate their options, because the market they return to may have changed dramatically since they last looked.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates the enduring brilliance of vi-style text editing, marveling that a tool created in 1976 remains fundamentally unchanged and deeply productive in 2024. He explains vi's core appeal through its grammar-based approach to text manipulation, where actions, scopes, and objects combine into powerful combos. Drawing an extended analogy to Street Fighter combo moves, he argues that vi transforms mundane text editing into a satisfying, game-like experience. He recommends Neovim with the LazyVim distribution as the best entry point for newcomers, and shares his own positive experience after switching from TextMate earlier in the year.
key_points: Vi's fundamental design of splitting insert mode from command mode has survived nearly 50 years essentially unchanged, defying tech's obsession with constant reinvention
Vi's real power is that it's a composable language for editing, built on an action-scope-object grammar that lets users construct unlimited combinations from a small set of primitives
The experience of mastering vi combos is game-like and dopamine-inducing, analogous to learning Street Fighter special moves
LazyVim provides an accessible entry point to Neovim, avoiding the daunting from-scratch configuration process
Despite the rapid pace of AI and modern tooling, some foundational tools persist because their core design was right from the start
money_quotes: For as much as some things in technology change, many other things stay the same. Like vi!
In 1976, most computers didn't even have a mouse. In 2024, most vi-successor users don't even need one when programming.
Vi makes manipulating text into a key-based form of Street Fighter.
It's not just an editor, it's a language for editing.
Once you learn the basic grammar of vi text manipulation, and you learn a few actions, scopes, and objects, you can string it all together in any combination possible.
It's just uniquely satisfying to string a handful of these combos together and see the text beneath you radically manipulated. In a way you just know would have been a drag to do in any other editor.
Neovim from scratch is like climbing Mount Everest without oxygen. If all you're interested in at first is a peek at the view, book that LazyVim helicopter tour before bothering the sherpas.
May vi reign for another fifty years and beyond!
posts.key_insight: Vi endures because it's not merely a text editor but a composable language for text manipulation, whose action-scope-object grammar makes editing feel like mastering a fighting game's combo system.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how establishing rapport and personal connection dramatically affects whether people will genuinely listen to controversial ideas. He uses his appearance on ThePrimeagen's stream as an example of how warm introductions and gracious hosts signal to audiences that a guest is worth hearing out. He acknowledges that many of his longstanding arguments have been dismissed by people who encountered them cold, without the rapport that warrants charitable listening. He concludes that while he wishes arguments were judged purely on logic, he's come to appreciate the power of set, setting, and personal vouching.
key_points: Cold contexts like X/Twitter or one-off blog posts make it nearly impossible to land controversial takes because there's no established rapport
Writing can't compete with podcasts or streams for persuasion because face, voice, and vibe change the emotional reception of arguments
Most people evaluate arguments based on emotions rather than pure logic or rhetoric
Having others vouch for your earnestness is the best way to move past stranger skepticism
money_quotes: It's hard to open cold with a controversial take to a bunch of strangers.
Just like comedy, half the battle of winning over the audience comes from a solid introduction, good timing, and a broad smile to warm the room.

That's the kind of introduction that makes all the difference as to whether someone is willing to grant you the grace of charitable listening -- or whether they'll immediately set their defenses to Max Stranger Skepticism.
That's where writing just can't compete with a podcast or a stream. Putting a face, a voice, and a vibe to the argument absolutely changes its tone, and in turn, the emotions it evokes.
I sometimes do struggle with that. Thinking that the strength of an argument should be gauged purely by its logic or at least its rhetoric.
We might not all become friends on the internet, but we needn't be strangers either.
posts.key_insight: Controversial ideas are judged more by the rapport and emotional context in which they're delivered than by their logical merit, making warm introductions and personal vouching essential for persuasion.

posts.summary: DHH recaps Rails World 2024 in Toronto, celebrating it as a triumphant event for the Rails community. Over a thousand attendees from 57 countries gathered at an outdoor venue, coinciding with the Rails 8 beta release featuring the #NOPAAS movement, Solid adapters with SQLite defaults, and Kamal 2.0. He argues Rails has found renewed confidence as a counter-melody to the industry's trend toward specialization, doubling down on the one-developer full-stack framework. The post credits the Rails Foundation, Shopify, and numerous sponsors for underwriting the event at a loss of over $100,000 to keep it affordable and mission-driven.
key_points: Rails World 2024 sold out in twenty minutes and drew 1,000+ attendees from 57 countries to a unique indoor/outdoor Toronto venue
Rails 8 beta launched at the conference with #NOPAAS philosophy, Solid adapters powered by SQLite, and Kamal 2.0 for deployment without PaaS subscriptions
Rails is deliberately charting a contrarian course as a one-developer framework while the rest of the industry slices web development into thinner specialties
The Rails Foundation and its member companies willingly underwrote a $100,000+ loss on the conference to subsidize accessibility and grow the ecosystem
Matz received the Rails Lifetime Award — reportedly his first award ever — in a session moderated by Shopify CEO Tobi Lütke
money_quotes: While everyone seems resigned to slice the expertise of web development into ever thinner specialties, Rails is doubling down on the one-developer framework, the full stack, and the compression of complexity.
The web does not have to be as complicated as we've let it become, and here's a corner of the programming world pushing back in unison.
You should never need a PaaS subscription to go live with Rails!
After spending a few years in the wilderness with webpacker and whatnot, Rails has found a new stride and a new confidence to pursue its counter melody to the rest of the industry.

While venture capitalists are moving in on developer tech from all sides, Rails is reaffirming its commitment to open source generics, and deployment strategies that don't require paying big premiums for wrappers around AWS.
The bold leap from #NOBUILD to #NOPAAS made for perhaps the most exciting shipping season in well over a decade for Rails.
posts.key_insight: Rails is experiencing a confident renaissance by rejecting the industry's push toward specialization and platform dependency, doubling down on simplicity and the one-developer full-stack framework.

posts.summary: DHH announces the release of Kamal 2, an upgraded deployment tool that lets developers deploy containerized applications to their own hardware without Kubernetes or PaaS providers. The new version supports multiple applications on a single server and automatic SSL certificate provisioning via Let's Encrypt. He positions Kamal as the key enabler of 37signals' cloud exit and frames it as a tool for everyone from $5 VPS users to dedicated hardware operators. The core argument is that vendor lock-in from proprietary PaaS setups stifles competition, and Kamal removes migration friction across deployment stages.
key_points: Kamal 2 adds multi-app-per-server support and automatic Let's Encrypt SSL, significantly reducing setup complexity
The tool scales from a single cheap VPS to dedicated hardware fleets without migration costs
DHH highlights Hetzner's 96-thread EPYC servers at $220/month as an example of what you can run outside the cloud
The chief mission is eliminating vendor lock-in to enable real competition in hosting and deployment
money_quotes: Kamal was our ticket out of the cloud.
A simple tool for deploying containerized applications onto our own hardware, without the need for the complexity of something like Kubernetes.
Imagine just how you can run on one of those amazing Hetzner EPYC 9454 boxes with 96 threads and 256GB of RAM that they sell for $220/month!
Our move out of the cloud would not have been nearly as smooth or as fast without Kamal.
We can't have competition in the cloud as long as folks are locked into proprietary or overly-complicated setups that makes moving from one vendor to another a huge hassle and expense.
It's chief mission is to allow you to move through all those stages of a production deployment without onerous migration costs or delays.
posts.key_insight: Developers don't need PaaS or Kubernetes — Kamal 2 makes deploying to your own hardware simple enough to eliminate cloud vendor lock-in at any scale.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Automattic's demand for 8% of WP Engine's revenues fundamentally violates open source principles and the GPL license. He contends that open source's success depends on the clarity and certainty of its licensing regime, where obligations are spelled out in the license and nothing more. Despite having tribal reasons to side with WordPress, DHH prioritizes the integrity of open source licensing over personal allegiances. He draws a parallel to Rails, noting that many billion-dollar companies built on it contribute nothing back, and that's their right under the license. He concludes that Automattic wants the prestige of open source while extracting payments like a proprietary vendor.
key_points: Open source licensing derives its power from clarity and certainty — there cannot be shadow obligations beyond what the license states
Automattic's revenue demand against WP Engine sets a dangerous precedent that extends far beyond WordPress to all open source projects
DHH acknowledges the frustration of seeing companies profit enormously from your open source work without contributing back, but insists that's the deal
Automattic's trademark enforcement is especially hypocritical given they were part owners of WP Engine until 2018
If Automattic wants different terms, they can change their license going forward, but they can't retroactively impose obligations on GPL-licensed code
money_quotes: Automattic is completely out of line, and the potential damage to the open source world extends far beyond the WordPress.
A key part of why open source has been so successful over the last several decades is the clarity and certainty of its licensing regime.

None of the major licenses, however, say anything close to 'it's free but only until the project owners deem you too successful and then you'll have to pay 8% of your revenues to support the project'.
That's a completely bonkers and arbitrary standard based in the rule of spite, not law.
I don't even have a dog in this fight, only a set of principles.
That's the deal. That's open source. I give you a gift of code, you accept the terms of the license.
There cannot be a second set of shadow obligations that might suddenly apply, if you strike it rich using the software.
They want to retain WordPress' shine of open source, but also be able to extract their pound of flesh from any competitor that might appear, whenever they see fit. Screw that.
posts.key_insight: Open source licenses must be the complete and final word on obligations — allowing project owners to impose retroactive revenue demands on successful users destroys the clarity and trust that made open source viable.

posts.summary: DHH defends the Benevolent Dictator For Life (BDFL) model of open source governance but argues that Matt Mullenweg has crossed critical lines in his dispute with WP Engine. He traces the escalation from trademark claims to blocking WPE engineers from WordPress.org and expropriating the Advanced Custom Fields plugin. While acknowledging Mullenweg's legitimate achievements with WordPress, DHH argues that weaponizing open source registries sets a dangerous precedent that threatens decades of open source peace. He makes a personal plea for Mullenweg to de-escalate before causing irreparable damage to open source norms.
key_points: The BDFL model works well for open source but is not infallible — unpopular decisions are a feature, but abuse of power is a real risk
Automattic's expropriation of WPE's ACF plugin after blocking their access to WordPress.org represents an unprecedented breach of open source norms
Open source code registries must remain neutral territory regardless of commercial disputes between companies
The WordPress-WPE conflict threatens to create SCO-Linux-level fear, uncertainty, and doubt about open source fundamentals
Gratitude grievances about unequal contributions must remain moral critiques, not commercial crusades enforced through platform control
money_quotes: Half the advantage of the BDFL model is exactly in allowing for unpopular decisions to be made without the lethargic mores of committees and bureaucracy!
Weaponizing open source code registries is something we simply cannot allow to form precedence. They must remain neutral territory. Little Switzerlands in a world of constant commercial skirmishes.
Matt, don't turn into a mad king.

I recognize the temptation of gratitude grievances, arising from beneficiaries getting more from our work than they return in contributions. But that must remain a moral critique, not a commercial crusade.
You can't just extract by force that which you believe to be owed beyond the license agreement on a whim.

Not since the SCO-Linux nonsense of the early 2000s have we faced such a potential explosion in fear, doubt, and uncertainty in the open source realm on basic matters everyone thought they could take for granted.
Please don't make me cheer for a private-equity operator like Silver Lake, Matt.
posts.key_insight: Open source registries must remain neutral infrastructure immune to commercial disputes, because weaponizing them — even by a project's rightful BDFL — threatens the foundational trust that makes the entire open source ecosystem work.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates SpaceX's successful Starship booster catch and argues that people should marvel at genuine technological progress rather than letting social media commoditize spectacular achievements. He praises Elon Musk as a 'crazy one' whose ambitious vision drives humanity forward, referencing Tesla's Cybercab reveal and robotic developments alongside the SpaceX milestone. DHH criticizes people who claim to admire rule-breaking visionaries but recoil when confronted with actual nonconformists. He argues that partisan politics should not cloud appreciation for tangible engineering breakthroughs.
key_points: Social media has commoditized spectacle, compressing historic achievements to the same level as viral cat videos
People claim to celebrate 'the crazy ones' in the abstract but resist actual nonconformists who challenge the status quo
Partisan politics — Musk shifting from 'blue team' to 'red team' — shouldn't diminish appreciation for genuine technological progress
Transformative progress depends on people with outsized, seemingly irrational ambition willing the future into existence
Most people are aggressively or passively conformist and see transgressors rather than genius
money_quotes: The spectacle has been completely commoditized. The grand gesture is gone.
It now takes effort to actually marvel when something truly spectacular occurs.
Don't be cynical. Don't let transient party politics cloud the moment.
This is exactly the kind of willing the future into existence that leaps of progress depend on.
Ambition this crazy is only likely to emerge from someone equally and sufficiently nuts.
Everyone wants to believe that they'd support 'the misfits, the rebels, the troublemakers'.
Most people are either aggressively or passively conformist. They squirm when The Crazy Ones actually attempt to change the world.
Not crazy for the sake of crazy, but crazy for the sake of progress. Demonstrable, undeniable, awe-inspiring progress.
posts.key_insight: People romanticize visionary nonconformists in the abstract but resist them in practice, and we should override that instinct to appreciate genuine technological progress regardless of politics.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the enormous economic value created by Ruby on Rails, noting that companies worth hundreds of billions were built with his framework. He acknowledges the temptation to feel shortchanged for not capturing more of that value personally, but argues this thinking is poisonous to the open source ethos. Instead, he finds satisfaction in the thriving ecosystem of happy programmers and successful businesses. He frames contentment with 'enough' as a special kind of wealth and defines the open source spirit as deliberately capturing far less than you create.
key_points: Companies like Shopify, GitHub, Coinbase and others built billion-dollar businesses on Rails, representing enormous value DHH helped enable
The temptation to calculate what you're 'owed' from open source contributions is lethal to the open source spirit
Gratitude grievances create a haunting, never-satisfied mindset where everyone else's success diminishes your own
The real reward of open source is the growing ecosystem of programmers and businesses it enables
Having 'enough' is a special kind of wealth that frees you from value-capture envy
money_quotes: This line of thinking is lethal to the open source spirit.
The moment you go down the path of gratitude grievances, you'll see ungrateful ghosts everywhere.
Don't let the success of others diminish your satisfaction with your own efforts.
I want for very little, because I've been blessed sufficiently. That's a special kind of wealth: Enough.
To let a billion lemons go unsqueezed. To capture vanishingly less than you create.
To marvel at a vast commons of software, offered with no strings attached, to any who might wish to build.
Thou shall not lust after thy open source's users and their success.
posts.key_insight: The open source spirit means deliberately capturing far less value than you create, finding wealth in sufficiency rather than maximizing personal extraction from communal tools.

posts.summary: DHH reports that 37signals' cloud exit savings have exceeded original estimates, projecting over $10 million in savings over five years. The 2024 cloud bill dropped from $3.2 million to $1.3 million annually, with the remaining spend on AWS S3 set to end next summer when they migrate to Pure Storage. The $700K hardware investment was fully recouped during the transition year. He argues the industry's all-cloud-everything mentality has peaked and companies should do the math on their cloud spending.
key_points: Annual cloud spending dropped from $3.2M to $1.3M, with $700K in new hardware fully paid off from 2023 transition-year savings
The remaining $1.3M S3 spend will be replaced by an 18PB Pure Storage setup at roughly the same cost as one year of S3, saving another $4M over five years
No hidden operational costs materialized — the same team manages everything without needing to hire additional staff
The cloud still makes sense for early-stage companies and highly variable workloads, but established companies with substantial bills should run the numbers
37signals plans to fully delete their AWS account by summer 2025
money_quotes: For 2024, we've brought the cloud bill down from the original $3.2 million/year run rate to $1.3 million.
Think about that for a second. This is gear we expect to use for the next five, maybe even seven years! All paid off from savings accrued during the second half of 2023.
There were no hidden dragons of additional workload associated with the exit that required us to balloon the team, as some spectators speculated when we announced it.
The motto of the 2010s and early 2020s – all-cloud, everything, all the time – seems to finally have peaked. And thank heavens for that!
But as soon as the cloud bills start to become substantial, I think you owe it to yourself, your investors, and common business sense to at least do the math.
The potential savings from these answers can be shocking.
At 37signals, we're looking forward to literally deleting our AWS account come this summer.
posts.key_insight: 37signals' cloud exit proved even more profitable than projected, saving over $10 million in five years while maintaining the same team size, challenging the industry assumption that cloud is always the right choice for established companies.

posts.summary: DHH argues that business decisions are inherently uncertain and that even successful experiments don't produce universal truths. He warns against treating past wins or losses as definitive proof of what works, since most business experiments are imperfect and context-dependent. The post cautions that locking in conclusions from past bets—whether wins or losses—makes you a prisoner of flawed narratives. Instead, he advocates maintaining an open mind that treats all past results as provisional and subject to reinterpretation.
key_points: Business experiments rarely produce clean, definitive answers—confounding variables and second-order effects make perfect experiments nearly impossible
Winning a bet doesn't mean you've discovered truth; the conclusions drawn from success should remain suspect
The phrase 'we tried that, didn't work' is dangerously reductive—it forecloses future experimentation with different approaches
Good business people make calculated bets on incomplete information, but great ones avoid hardening those bets into rigid beliefs
Resisting the urge to write a definitive narrative keeps you open to reinterpretation and future opportunity
money_quotes: The fun bit about business is in all the answers you don't have.
The hallmark of a good business person is their ability to make calculated bets based on half a story and a hunch.
The key is to remember that what you won was a bet, not a trophy made of truth.
We tried SOMETHING and that SOMETHING didn't work. We could try SOMETHING ELSE and THAT might work.
If you don't, you'll eventually become a prisoner to every bet you've made.
It's better than okay to resist coming up with a definitive story either which way. Because as soon as you write that story down, it tends to become rigid rather than open to reinterpretation.
An open mind is always willing to let the story twist, willing to revisit the setup, and willing to test everything from timing to premise on another day.
posts.key_insight: Business outcomes are bets won or lost, not truths discovered—hardening them into rigid narratives closes you off to future opportunities.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates what he sees as the collapse of progressive social accusations (racist, misogynist, fascist) as effective political tools, using the 2024 US presidential election as evidence. He argues these labels were overused to the point of meaninglessness, comparing them to rubber checks that eventually bounce. He frames the decline of DEI and 'woke orthodoxy' as the end of a preference falsification where people went along with prevailing norms out of fear. He concludes this is a universal win regardless of one's opinion on Trump.
key_points: Accusations of racism, misogyny, and fascism have lost their power through overuse and lack of substantiation
The DEI movement and critical-theory thinking were amplified by preference falsification — people conforming publicly while disagreeing privately
The 2024 presidential election result proves these social pressure tactics have lost their political effectiveness
What appeared to be widespread consensus was actually distorted perception — 'shadows on the wall' made larger by the angle of light
money_quotes: They just don't work any more, those baseless accusations that anyone we disagree with is a racist, misogynist, fascist.
The problem with accusations like that is that they eventually have to be backed by proof or they'll bounce like a rubber check.
When even the most mundane political or moral positions were able to earn one of those *ist-y labels, the entire enterprise of throwing them around was bound to go bankrupt.

Being professionally offended by every little thing, seeing micro-aggressions around every corner, and rendering the entire world through a narrow oppressed/oppressor lens is back to the niche disposition once more.
This particular preference falsification, where people just go along with what's in vogue to keep their head down, has left the zeitgeist.
That was shadows on the wall, distorted to thrice their size from the angle of the light.
That big scary mob that once was has been reduced to near impotence in most arenas, including the biggest of all, the US presidential election.
posts.key_insight: The overuse of social stigma labels without substantiation has bankrupted their credibility, and the 2024 election proved the cultural moment that sustained them has passed.

posts.summary: DHH argues that therapy culture has infiltrated the workplace to its detriment, drawing from a personal experience where a company meeting devolved into a group therapy session. He contends that while emotions at work are natural, the expectation that managers should process employees' feelings during work hours is misguided. He points out that licensed therapists operate under strict ethical boundaries that can't be replicated in workplace settings. DHH concludes that the rise of therapeutic language and methods in corporate culture has made people more fragile and less capable of handling normal workplace adversity.
key_points: A company meeting about a COO departure turned into an unproductive group therapy session, illustrating the dangers of therapy-infused corporate culture
Therapeutic processing of emotions belongs with licensed professionals, not managers during 1-on-1s or all-hands meetings
Action-oriented coping is a legitimate strategy that gets dismissed by therapy culture, particularly for men who prefer doing over talking
Licensed therapists operate under ethical boundaries (independence, competence, relationship constraints) that cannot exist in workplace settings
The increase in therapy thinking at work has made people more fragile and less capable of handling normal disappointments
money_quotes: It's a natural extension of "holding the space", of too much navel-gazing "mindfulness", of a posture of nurture and care that borders the patronizing.
It's okay to be disappointed or frustrated or anxious at work. That's part of the experience working with and especially for other people!
It's also based on a fundamental misconception that everything can be made better for everyone by talking more about it.
There's a reason licensed therapists are bound by a whole host of ethical boundaries when working with clients. They can't be intertwined with the client's friends or family or colleagues.
It's possible to share the same genuine care for people while disagreeing on the methods and the context that's due to serve them best.
An increase that's making people more fragile, more precious, more incapable of coping with the most basic expectations of disappointments or adversity on the job.
Get therapy out of the office and let's hand it back to the shrinks.
posts.key_insight: Workplace therapy culture undermines resilience by importing therapeutic methods into a context that lacks the ethical boundaries and professional competence required to use them responsibly.

posts.summary: DHH explains why Fortnite is his family's favorite game, arguing it teaches kids valuable life skills through cooperative squad play. He enforces two simple house rules — stay together and no complaining — which teach teamwork and emotional regulation. Unlike competitive family games like Mario Kart, Fortnite puts the whole family on the same team working toward shared objectives. He concludes that not all screen time is equal, and that well-chosen games can be remarkably effective teaching tools.
key_points: Two simple rules — stay together and no complaining — teach cooperative discipline and emotional regulation
Fortnite's team-based structure makes it superior to competitive family games for teaching collaboration
Learning to lose gracefully and temper frustration are life skills that transfer well beyond gaming
Screen time quality varies enormously — cooperative gaming can be genuinely educational
money_quotes: All screen time is not created equal.
If you get all pissy and blamey when you're knocked out, the session ends, because nobody wants to listen to that (especially dad!).
If you want to play more, and get better, you better start tempering your frustrations. Wonderful life skill to develop early.
It's ever-so tempting to stray from the squad to chase your own goal of getting better gear, and it comes easy to blame your brother when the other team gets you.
Unlike something like Mario Kart or Smash Brothers, which pits the family against itself (also fun, but less learning!), Fortnite puts us all on the same team, striving for the same objective.
posts.key_insight: Cooperative video games with enforced rules about teamwork and emotional control can be powerful tools for teaching children discipline, collaboration, and resilience.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his creative process, arguing that his best work comes from intense, obsessive sprints on a single problem followed by periods of aimless wandering. He traces how his recent major projects — Omakub, Kamal, cloud departure, and Rails 8 — each emerged from curiosity-driven exploration rather than planned roadmaps. He argues that keeping your schedule free of recurring commitments is essential to being available when the next obsessive opportunity arrives. The post concludes that not knowing what's next is a feature, not a bug, because the best work emerges from following hunches rather than planning destinations.
key_points: Big leaps forward require unsustainable sprint-level intensity, which is why they must be short bursts rather than marathons
Each of DHH's major recent projects (Omakub, Kamal, Rails 8) traced back to unplanned curiosity rather than strategic planning
Keeping your calendar and commitments clear is essential to being available when obsessive inspiration strikes
Company-level work is a marathon, but executive-level breakthroughs are sprints inspired by hunches
Not knowing what's next is the correct state — if you already knew, you'd already be doing it
money_quotes: My best work is a series of sprints punctuated by periods of wandering.
At the executive level, almost all big leaps forward are sprints inspired by a hunch.
The level of intensity required to get that hunch over the hill is just too high to sustain for long.
That to me is the best argument for making sure my plate isn't full of half-eaten commitments.
There are few things more satisfying to me than following a hunch and seeing where it leads, without a commitment to a specific, final destination.
Every essay starts with a hunch, but the final shape is rarely clear until the mind has been left to wrestle with the words for a while.
If I did, I'd already be half-way done doing it. And then it wouldn't really be next, it'd be now.
Finding the next now is the art of wandering, and wandering well takes practice and patience.
posts.key_insight: The best creative and technical breakthroughs come not from planning but from keeping your mind and schedule open enough to follow obsessive hunches when they arrive.

posts.summary: DHH announces he's joining Shopify's board of directors, framing it through his twenty-year relationship with founder Tobi Lütke dating back to the earliest days of Ruby on Rails. He highlights Shopify's enormous contributions to the Rails ecosystem, including employing a third of the Rails Core Team and developing the YJIT Ruby compiler. Beyond gratitude, DHH admits a selfish curiosity to engage with engineering problems at a scale far beyond his work at 37signals—a five-million-line monolith and thousands of programmers. He positions Shopify as a counterforce to web enshittification, enabling small entrepreneurs to compete with Amazon through fast, trustworthy online stores.
key_points: Tobi Lütke was an early Rails contributor who single-handedly killed the 'Rails can't scale' argument by building Shopify into the world's most popular hosted e-commerce platform
Shopify is the most generous benefactor in the Ruby on Rails ecosystem, employing a third of the Rails Core Team and developing the YJIT compiler
DHH is motivated partly by curiosity to wrestle with engineering problems at a scale several orders of magnitude beyond 37signals
Shopify is framed as a democratizing force against enshittification, giving small merchants a competitive shopping experience against Amazon
The Shopify monolith at five million lines of code represents a real-world vindication of the 'majestic monolith' architecture DHH has long advocated
money_quotes: Tobi more or less single-handedly killed the zombie argument that Rails couldn't scale by building the world's most popular hosted e-commerce platform and routing a sizable portion of all online sales through it.
They are without a doubt the most generous benefactor in the Ruby on Rails world.
It was also to satisfy a selfish curiosity to wrestle with problems at a scale that none of my own work has ever touched.
While much of the web is decaying to enshitification and entropy, Shopify stores stand out by being faster to browse, quicker to checkout, and easier to trust.
That's the Shopify magic: Leveling the commercial playing field between some obscure keyboard maker and the consolidated titans of e-commerce.
Shopify deals with a scale several orders of magnitude beyond what I do day-to-day at 37signals. That's interesting!
posts.key_insight: DHH frames his Shopify board appointment as both a tribute to Rails' most important commercial success story and a personal opportunity to engage with engineering scale he's never experienced at 37signals.

posts.summary: DHH argues that ADHD is massively overdiagnosed, comparing the diagnostic process to cold reading by psychics. He points out that the commonly listed ADHD symptoms—forgetfulness, attraction to novelty, difficulty with habits—are universal human experiences, not pathologies. He notes the explosion of Adderall prescriptions (45 million in 2023, up from 35 million in 2019) as evidence of a cottage industry convincing normal people they have a medical condition. He concludes that people want a medical explanation for ordinary human struggles rather than accepting mundane solutions like better sleep and exercise.
key_points: Common ADHD symptom lists describe universal human experiences like forgetfulness and attraction to novelty, not distinct pathology
The ADHD diagnostic criteria are so broad that most people could qualify, undermining their usefulness as a clinical standard
Adderall prescriptions jumped from 35 million to 45 million between 2019 and 2023, suggesting overdiagnosis
The ADHD diagnosis industry functions like cold reading—throwing out vague enough symptoms that everyone finds hits
People prefer a medical label over mundane lifestyle fixes like sleep, exercise, and diet
money_quotes: No wonder we've seen an explosion of ADHD diagnosis. This list applies to most humans at least part of the time!
You don't need a diagnosis to be a flawed human. It goes for all of us.
There clearly is also an entire cottage industry cropping up around convincing perfectly normal people that they suffer from ADHD.
I think a lot of people desperately want to hear that there's a medical reason for why they sometimes can't focus, don't feel motivated, forget things, or find breaking bad habits hard.
If you want to supercharge your morning's productivity routine by popping a pill or two of amphetamines, own it! Don't hide behind some label.
Sure, you may recognize all those symptoms, but for true ADHD sufferers, they're just, like, worse! Okay, but what kind of diagnostic standard is that?!
Now that's crazy.
posts.key_insight: The broad, universally relatable nature of ADHD symptom lists functions like psychic cold reading, enabling a cottage industry that medicalizes ordinary human imperfection.

posts.summary: DHH contrasts Jaguar's widely mocked rebrand with Volvo's emotionally powerful advertising to make a point about authentic branding. Jaguar's campaign relies on hollow, cliché slogans and diversity pandering that feel completely disconnected from what a car company should stand for. Volvo, meanwhile, builds its brand on decades of genuine commitment to safety, backed by real actions like giving away the seatbelt patent and running crash response teams since the 1970s. The key lesson is that great branding requires congruence between what you say and what you actually do, repeated consistently over time. DHH argues that brand strength comes from authenticity and commitment, not clever marketing slogans. The result: he wants to buy a Volvo despite his natural inclinations toward Jaguar's speed and design heritage.
key_points: Jaguar's rebrand with empty slogans like 'CREATE EXUBERANT' and 'DELETE ORDINARY' is tone-deaf and likely signals the death of the brand

Volvo's branding works because it rests on genuine actions: giving away the seatbelt patent, running crash response teams since the 1970s, and designing for real-world safety rather than just test ratings
Brand is like culture — it requires repetition and authenticity, being who you say you are over and over again
The strongest brands have congruence between their marketing message and their actual behavior and history
Great advertisement has the power both to lift and to diminish a brand
money_quotes: You'd think these were slogans from a Will Ferrel bit about insufferable marketing trons, but nope, that's the 2024 campaign for a car maker that won't be selling any cars until 2026.
Brand is like culture: It's all about repetition and authenticity. Being who you say you are, over and over.
Volvo is safety, safety is paramount to parents, so Volvo is for parents. It's that simple, and it's that powerful. But only because it's actually true!
Their cars do very well when the test expands to include new scenarios because it's designed to be best-in-reality not just best-in-test.
The branding strength of Volvo rests on congruence, consistency, and commitment to doing the same thing, a little better every year, for basically an eternity.
I wouldn't be caught dead in a Jaguar now. That's the power of advertisement: To lift and to diminish.
posts.key_insight: Authentic branding requires congruence between what you claim and what you actually do — no amount of clever slogans can substitute for decades of genuine commitment to a real value.

posts.summary: DHH argues that AI coding assistants currently function like superb junior programmers — encyclopedic in syntax knowledge but prone to overly-complicated, subtly-defective solutions. He identifies the 'premise trap' as the core problem: the fundamental architectural assumptions in AI-generated code go unquestioned until too much time has been invested improving implementation details. He attributes this limitation to AI's training data being dominated by tutorials and simplified answers rather than proprietary codebases where real trade-offs are learned. He advises senior programmers to treat AI output with the same scrutiny they'd apply to junior code, and warns against letting AI sap programmers' own learning and stamina.
key_points: AI coding assistants exhibit the same 'premise trap' as junior programmers — their fundamental architectural assumptions are often wrong even when syntax is correct
Non-programmers using AI to build systems experience an initial rush followed by a one-step-forward-two-steps-back cycle when trying to make things production-ready
AI models are limited by their training data: tutorials, Stack Overflow answers, and content often made by 'the blind leading the blind'
The next big leap for AI coding likely requires exposure to proprietary, corporate codebases where real-world trade-offs are navigated
Senior programmers risk having AI sap their learning stamina and lure them into signing off on subpar work
money_quotes: The fundamental assumptions baked into the first draft of the code aren't questioned until you've already spent far too long improving the implementation.

AI at the moment is like a superb junior programmer. One with an encyclopedic knowledge about syntax and APIs, but also one saddled with the same propensities to produce overly-complicated, subtly-defective solutions.

It's an incredible rush to see a prototype come to life in mere minutes, but actually moving this forward to something that works reliably often turns into a one-step-forwards-two-steps-backwards dance.
The unfortunate reality that a fair chunk of internet programming content is made by the blind leading the blind.
Senior human programmers all got started on the same information diet, but eventually graduated to higher levels of understanding and mastery by working on proprietary code bases.
To verify the assumptions that have been baked into its path will require spending as much time to understand the choices as it would doing the work yourself.
Alert to the danger of luring more senior programmers, including myself, into signing off on slop, while it saps our stamina for continued learning.
posts.key_insight: AI's coding weakness isn't syntax or knowledge but architectural judgment — it falls into the same 'premise trap' as junior programmers, building confidently on flawed assumptions that cost more to fix than doing the work yourself.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the existential crisis that comes with achieving wealth and total freedom, inspired by the Loom founder's $60M windfall and subsequent loss of purpose. He argues that unlimited freedom and material success often fail to deliver the meaning people expect. Drawing parallels to retirees who struggle without work and rich kids who flounder without responsibility, he suggests that the pursuit of goals provides more fulfillment than their achievement. The core paradox: chasing the carrot is more satisfying than catching it.
key_points: A startup founder's $60M exit led to existential crisis rather than fulfillment, illustrating that wealth doesn't solve the problem of meaning
Retirees often struggle with excess freedom, with many Danish pensioners returning to work to reclaim purpose and usefulness
Being needed and useful is more attractive than unlimited leisure, contradicting the common fantasy of total freedom
The belief that material success will fix existential problems serves as a useful motivating illusion, depressing to lose
There's a painful irony in achieving your dreams: the chase itself was the real reward
money_quotes: It's harder than you think to suddenly have all the freedom you ever desired land in your lap.
You may just realize that you don't actually know what to do with it all!
Some literally wither away from all that excess freedom.
Being needed, being useful is far more attractive than leaning back in leisure.
Believing in a material fix to the problem of existence dangles a carrot of hope that's depressing to go without.
Once you know that the material carrot is just like the spoon that bends because it doesn't actually exist, you're condemned to a life of knowing that what you imagine as nirvana probably isn't.
What beautiful irony: That the prize for catching the carrot is the realization that chasing it was more fun.
posts.key_insight: The pursuit of wealth and freedom provides more meaning than their achievement, because purpose comes from being needed and useful, not from the absence of obligation.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Meta's announcement to end third-party fact-checking and relax content moderation on its platforms, framing it as the end of a social media censorship era. He argues that the pandemic period saw governments directly instruct platforms to suppress legitimate speech under the guise of fighting 'misinformation' and 'hate speech.' He credits Elon Musk's acquisition of Twitter as the pivotal moment that broke the censorship consensus, and gives Trump's election win credit for accelerating the shift. He contrasts America's move toward free expression with Europe and the UK's continued embrace of speech restrictions, arguing the world needs America's First Amendment principles.
key_points: Meta's pivot to community notes and relaxed moderation across Facebook, Instagram, and Threads affects three billion users and represents a seismic shift in online speech policy

The pandemic-era censorship regime used 'hate speech' and 'misinformation' as rhetorical fig leaves that quickly lost objective meaning and became tools for suppressing politically inconvenient speech
Musk's acquisition and transformation of Twitter into X was the pivotal moment that broke the global censorship consensus and enabled Meta's follow-on pivot
A growing chasm exists between America's recovery from speech restrictions and Europe/UK's doubling down on censorship, with UK police threatening arrests over social media posts
Free speech protections must extend to speech you disagree with — a principle illustrated by the ACLU defending Nazis' right to march in Skokie
money_quotes: Terms that almost immediately lost all objective content, and turned into mere descriptors of 'speech we don't like'.
This is why the world needs America. From the UK to the EU to Brazil, China, Russia, and Iran, political censorship is very popular.
His acquisition of Twitter and its transformation into X was the pivotal moment for both American and global free speech.
It's hard to divorce principles from people! We're all liable to mix and confuse the two.
You don't need to love every champion of your principles to quietly appreciate their contributions.
In order for you to voice your dissent on Trump or Musk or whatever, you need the protection of the first amendment to cover those who want to dissent in the opposite direction too.
It's entire purpose is to protect speech that's unpopular with the rulers of the moment.
I will cheer for Zuckerberg without reservation when he works in their service.
posts.key_insight: The collapse of the pandemic-era censorship consensus — triggered by Musk's Twitter acquisition and cemented by Meta's pivot — marks a historic victory for free expression, but the principle only endures if defended for speech you oppose.

posts.summary: DHH argues that decades of mass immigration in Europe have demonstrated that cultural integration between radically divergent cultures has largely failed, and that blaming host countries for not trying hard enough is no longer a credible explanation. He contends that multiculturalism as a compromise has poisoned national unity across Europe, and that suppressing dissent by labeling it racist has fueled populist movements. While acknowledging the dangers of combative nationalism and that correct diagnoses don't guarantee correct prescriptions, he calls for replacing naive hope with realistic new strategies for immigration policy.
key_points: When every European country has struggled identically with integration for decades, the failure can't be attributed to insufficient effort by any single nation
Multiculturalism as parallel societies has undermined national unity and trust across Europe
The suppression of dissent on immigration through accusations of racism has directly fueled populist movements
Correct diagnosis of a problem doesn't guarantee a correct prescription — many who identified integration failures early proposed unsound solutions
Immigration can benefit societies but must account for cultural compatibility and be guided by realism rather than idealism
money_quotes: As Thomas Sowell tells us, this is because there are no solutions to intractable, hard problems like cultural integration between wildly different ways of living. Only trade offs.
Heartwarming anecdotes don't settle trade offs, though. They don't prove a solution or offer a conclusion either.
Allowing parallel cultures to underpin parallel societies is poison for the national unity and trust.
I actually have a nostalgic appreciation for the beautiful ideals behind such hope for humanity, but I also think that at this point it is as delusional as it is dangerous.
World history is full of people who've had the correct diagnosis but a terrible prescription.
The old accusations have finally lost their power from years of excessive use, and suppressing the reality that many people can see with their own eyes is getting harder.
But you can hold both considerations in your head at the same time without losing your wits.
posts.key_insight: Europe's decades-long experiment with mass immigration and multiculturalism has failed on a statistical level, and replacing naive idealism with clear-eyed realism about cultural integration is urgent — even if the right prescriptions remain unclear.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Trump's return to the presidency, noting that two-thirds of young Americans (18-29) are optimistic about the next four years. He frames the moment as bigger than Trump himself—a cultural shift away from excessive self-criticism toward renewed American confidence. He contrasts American optimism with European pessimism, expressing hope that Europe will eventually follow suit. He acknowledges the piece is more faith than analysis, but declares optimism the only viable stance.
key_points: Young Americans (18-29) are the most optimistic demographic about Trump's second term, with two-thirds expressing positive outlook
The cultural shift transcends politics—it's economic, entrepreneurial, and technological in nature
Europe is stuck in deep pessimism and regulatory thinking, falling further behind America's dynamism
Europe was economically competitive with America as recently as 15 years ago but lacks America's ability to course-correct
DHH openly admits this is faith-based optimism rather than rigorous analysis
money_quotes: After spending so long lost in the wilderness of excessive self-criticism and self-loathing, there's finally a broad coalition of the willing working to get the mojo back.
This is what's so exhilarating about America. The big, dramatic swings. The high stakes. The long shots.
I like this country much better when it's confident in that inherent national character.
As someone with a foot on both the American and European continent, I can't help being jealous with my euro leg.

I can also well imagine Europe sticking with Eurocrat thinking, spinning its wheels with grand but empty proclamations, issuing scorning but impotent admonishments of America, and doubling down on the regulatory black hole.
Optimism must win. Pessimism is literally for losers.
This is far more an article of faith than of analysis, mind you.
posts.key_insight: American optimism under Trump represents a broader cultural shift beyond politics—toward entrepreneurial confidence and away from self-loathing—that Europe desperately needs but may lack the agility to replicate.

posts.summary: DHH uses the Danish habit of waiting at red lights even when no cars are coming as a metaphor for how consistent norm enforcement creates profound second-order effects in society. He argues that Copenhagen's safety—where nine-year-olds ride the metro alone—comes from vigorously enforcing even minor infractions and having zero tolerance for antisocial behavior. He contrasts this with American cities where tolerance of disorder has driven families from public spaces. His core argument is that Denmark's progressive outcomes (paternity leave, public transit, child independence) are built on a highly conservative foundation of enforced social norms, and American progressives who admire Denmark must reckon with this paradox.
key_points: Consistent enforcement of even minor social norms (jaywalking, bike infractions, fare evasion) creates compounding second-order effects that make cities safe
Freedom from antisocial behavior and freedom to engage in antisocial behavior are oppositional forces—granting one necessarily restricts the other
Denmark's progressive policy outcomes depend on conservative norm enforcement, a paradox American liberals rarely acknowledge
The veneer of civil society is thin—norms fray quickly when unguarded and are much harder to reestablish than to maintain
Parenthood sharpens awareness of how public safety norms directly determine whether children can be independent in cities
money_quotes: Doing the right thing every time can be put on autopilot, and when most people follow even the basic norms consistently, the second-order effects are profound.

When you take away the freedom from crackheads and bums to smoke up on the train or sleep in the park, you grant the freedom to nine-year olds to roam the city and for families to enjoy the park at dusk.
This is the fundamental error of suicidal empathy. That tolerance of the deranged and dangerous few can be kept a separate discussion from the freedom and safety of the many.
The veneer of civil society is surprisingly thin. Norms fray quickly if left unguarded.
It's much harder to reestablish their purchase on society than to protect them from disappearing in the first place.
The progressive Nirvana of Denmark is built on a highly conservative set of norms and traditions. It's yin and yang.

Many liberals in America keep Denmark as some mythical place where all their policy dreams have come true, without ever wrestling much with what it takes to maintain the social trust that allows those policies to enjoy public support.
posts.key_insight: Denmark's progressive social outcomes that American liberals admire are inseparable from the conservative norm enforcement they tend to reject—you cannot have one without the other.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his family's experience during the January 2025 Los Angeles wildfires that devastated Malibu and the Palisades. After narrowly avoiding the Franklin fire in December, his family evacuated again on January 7th as the Palisades fire destroyed nearly 100 of 150 houses in their immediate area. Though their home survived, the surrounding infrastructure was obliterated, forcing them to relocate to Copenhagen with their children. He reflects on how humans chronically underestimate disaster risk through bad pattern matching, and the bittersweet irony of leaving America just as he feels it's entering its most exciting period.
key_points: Humans are dangerously prone to wishful thinking and bad pattern matching when assessing disaster risk, assuming the past predicts the future
The Palisades fire destroyed over 10,000 houses and leveled entire neighborhoods, far exceeding anything in recent memory
A surviving house in a disaster zone is still a disaster — destroyed infrastructure, burnt landscaping, and national guard roadblocks made the area uninhabitable
After living in Malibu since 2009 and knowing the fire risk since Woolsey in 2017, the family retreated to Copenhagen for the children's stability
DHH feels conflicted about leaving America at what he considers its most exciting and optimistic moment
money_quotes: Humans have a strange relationship with risk and disasters. We're so prone to wishful thinking and bad pattern matching.
Because the past had failed to predict the future.
Beautiful enough to be worth the risk, we said.

But even if it wasn't a surprise, it's still a shock. The sheer devastation, especially in the Palisades, went far beyond our normal range of comprehension. Bounded, as it always is, by past experiences.
But a house left standing in a disaster zone is still a disaster.
After Woolsey in 2017, Malibu life always felt like living on borrowed time to us.
Of the twenty years I've spent in America, this feels like the most exciting time to be part of the exceptionalism that the US of A offers.
And that burns.
posts.key_insight: Our comprehension of catastrophic risk is always bounded by past experience, which makes us chronically underprepared when reality finally exceeds those bounds.

posts.summary: DHH reveals that 61 servers purchased in 2015 for Basecamp 3's launch are only now being retired after nearly a decade of service, at a fully amortized cost of just $142/day. The fleet cost about half a million dollars and supported a service that grossed hundreds of millions. He argues that server lifetimes are typically underestimated—their cloud exit accounting assumed five years, but these lasted nearly ten. The database servers will continue serving heritage apps even after the main fleet is refreshed. DHH uses this as further evidence that owning hardware is vastly more economical than cloud rental, potentially saving millions more than initially projected.
key_points: 61 Dell servers bought for ~$500K in 2015 lasted nearly a decade, working out to just $142/day fully amortized
Server lifetime assumptions in cloud-exit ROI calculations are often too conservative—five years is a floor, not a ceiling
The retiring database servers will be handed down to heritage apps, extracting even more value from the original investment
Hardware ownership combined with lean team management forms the foundation of 37signals' cost advantage
Companies should re-run their cloud-exit calculations with longer server lifetimes, which could tip the economics decisively
money_quotes: That's over 3,500 days of service from this fleet, at a fully amortized cost of just $142/day.
Owning our own iron has been a fantastically profitable proposition. Millions of dollars saved over renting in the cloud.
The dollar you keep at the end of the year is just as real whether you earn it or save it.
Most servers can easily power your applications much longer than that.
So you just might want to run those cloud-exit numbers once more with a longer server lifetime value. It might just tip the equation, and motivate you to become a server owner rather than a renter.
The database servers are getting handed down to some of our heritage apps. They will keep on trucking until they give up the ghost.
posts.key_insight: Server hardware routinely outlasts the conservative five-year depreciation schedules used in cloud-vs-own calculations, making the economics of owning your infrastructure even more favorable than initially projected.

posts.summary: DHH draws a historical parallel between WWII production chief William Knudsen and Elon Musk, arguing that Musk's proven track record of radical efficiency gains at SpaceX, X/Twitter, and Tesla makes him uniquely qualified to tackle the US government's two-trillion-dollar budget deficit through DOGE. He acknowledges Musk's erratic personal behavior and controversial tweets but argues his positive contributions to mankind overwhelmingly outweigh the negatives. DHH frames the US fiscal situation as a crisis requiring exactly the kind of ruthless efficiency optimization Musk has demonstrated in the private sector.
key_points: The US budget deficit approaching $2 trillion with $36 trillion in total debt and $881 billion in annual interest payments represents an unsustainable fiscal crisis
Musk's track record includes 100x SpaceX cost reductions, doubling X/Twitter profitability with 80% fewer staff, and scaling Tesla production 50x in a decade
