DHH compares Musk to William Knudsen, the Danish-born GM president who Roosevelt recruited to run WWII military production and achieved a 100x increase in plane manufacturing
DHH concedes Musk's erratic tweets and support for fringe political figures are legitimate concerns but argues they're vastly outweighed by his achievements
Government efficiency reform requires bringing in exceptional private-sector talent, just as Roosevelt did during wartime
money_quotes: Elon Musk is a modern day William S. Knudsen. Only even more accomplished in efficiency management, factory optimization, and first-order systems thinking.
The US was knocking on a two-trillion-dollar budget deficit in 2024! Adding to a towering debt that's now north of 36 trillion.
Whatever you think of Musk and his personal tweets, it's impossible to deny what an insane achievement of efficiency this has been!
Musk's magic is balanced with mania and even a dash of madness. This is usually the case with truly extraordinary humans.
Expecting Musk to do what he does and then also be a 'normal, chill dude' is delusional.
Who wouldn't want such a capacity involved in finding the waste, sloth, and squander in the US budget? Well, his political enemies, of course!
Musk is exactly the kind of formidable player you want on your team when you're down two trillion to nothing, needing a Hail Mary pass for the destiny of America.
He's a modern-day Knudsen on steroids (or Ketamine?). Let him cook.
posts.key_insight: America's fiscal crisis demands the same kind of radical private-sector efficiency expertise that won WWII's production war, and Musk's track record of dramatic cost-cutting makes him uniquely suited for the job despite his personal flaws.

posts.summary: DHH endorses JD Vance's Munich Security Conference speech criticizing Europe's lack of genuine free speech protections and its mass immigration policies. He argues that Europeans fundamentally misunderstand free speech, believing they have American-style protections when laws across the UK, Germany, and Denmark criminalize various forms of expression. He connects suppressed speech to Europe's immigration crisis, arguing that a narrow Overton window prevented honest debate about the downsides of mass immigration for decades. He contrasts Denmark's earlier willingness to question immigration with Sweden's sustained suppression of debate, which he blames for Sweden's current violence problems.
key_points: Europeans lack First Amendment-style protections and delude themselves into thinking their speech is as free as Americans', despite laws criminalizing hate speech, insults to officials, and religious mockery
The Biden administration's censorship through social media platforms violated the First Amendment, as revealed by the Twitter Files
Topics once labeled misinformation or hate speech — lab leak theory, vaccine efficacy questions, trans policy debates — have since been vindicated or mainstreamed
Europe's inability to openly discuss mass immigration consequences is a direct result of its restricted speech norms, with Sweden serving as the worst-case cautionary tale
Denmark's earlier willingness to break the immigration taboo, despite accusations of racism, positioned it better than Sweden which kept debate suppressed through three decades
money_quotes: Europeans by and large simply do not understand 'free speech' as a concept the way Americans do.

It's quite like how every dictator around the world pretends to believe in democracy. Sure, they may repress the opposition and rig their elections, but they still crave the imprimatur of the concept. So too 'free speech' and the Europeans.
In America, your speech is free to be wrong, free to be hateful, free to insult religions and celebrities alike.
The founding fathers correctly saw that asserting the power to determine otherwise leads to a totalitarian darkness.
It's the lack of free speech, and a catastrophically narrow Overton window, which has led Europe into such a mess with mass immigration in the first place.
The state of Sweden today is a direct consequence of suppressing any talk of the downsides to mass immigration for decades.
It's never too late to fix tomorrow. You can't fix today, but you can always fix tomorrow.
posts.key_insight: Europe's lack of genuine free speech protections created a fatally narrow Overton window that prevented honest debate about mass immigration, leading to the crisis now engulfing countries like Sweden.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Europe has simultaneously failed in three critical domains: military capability, immigration policy, and economic entrepreneurship, creating a compounding crisis. He points to America negotiating Ukraine peace without European involvement as evidence of military irrelevance, the rise of right-wing parties as a consequence of failed multicultural integration policies, and the absence of major new European companies as proof of economic decline. Germany's energy policy decisions serve as a particularly damning example of self-inflicted economic harm. Despite the grim diagnosis, DHH insists Europe has the talent and capital to reverse course but currently lacks political will. Writing as a Dane, he frames this as a tough-love assessment rather than anti-European sentiment.
key_points: Decades of underfunding European military has left the continent excluded from major geopolitical negotiations like Ukraine peace talks
Mainstream European parties have failed to address immigration concerns, instead dismissing critics as racist, fueling right-wing surges
Europe's entrepreneurship decline and Germany's energy policy decisions have gutted economic competitiveness and accelerated brain drain to America
The simultaneous failure across military, immigration, and economic domains creates a compounding disaster rather than manageable individual problems
Europe has the resources to change course but remains stuck in the early stages of grief rather than taking action
money_quotes: Europe has become a third-rate power economically, politically, and militarily, and the price for this slowly building predicament is now due all at once.
The never-ending ridicule of America, for spending the supposedly "absurd sum" of 3.4% of its GDP to maintain its might, coming home to roost.
Rather than respond to this growing public discontent, mainstream parties all over Europe run the same playbook of calling anyone with legitimate concerns "racist".
The European economy lost parity with the American after 2008, and now the net-zero nonsense has lead Europe's old manufacturing powerhouse, Germany, to commit financial harakiri.
A single failure in any of these three crucial realms would have been painful to deal with. But failure in all three at the same time is a disaster, and it's one of Europe's own making.
Europe has the people, the talent, and the capital to choose a different path. What it currently lacks is the will.
I don't want Europe to become American. But I want Europe to be strong, confident, and successful. Right now it's anything but.
The best time for Europe to make a change was twenty years ago. The next best time is right now.
posts.key_insight: Europe's simultaneous failures in military investment, immigration integration, and economic entrepreneurship have compounded into an existential crisis that only radical political will can reverse.

posts.summary: DHH reacts to a 60 Minutes segment showing German police conducting dawn raids on citizens for online speech offenses, framing it as validation of JD Vance's Munich speech criticizing European free speech restrictions. He describes German prosecutors openly admitting that online insults and retweeting 'lies' are criminal offenses, with fifty raids conducted in a single day for speech violations. DHH extends his criticism beyond Germany to all of Europe, including his native Denmark, arguing the continent fundamentally misunderstands free speech. He concludes that European leaders are humiliated by Vance's public callout because they know he's right, while European citizens are being gaslit about the true state of their rights.
key_points: A 60 Minutes segment showed German police conducting dawn raids with armed officers to confiscate devices over online speech offenses, with fifty such raids in a single day
German prosecutors openly told American journalists that online insults are crimes and retweeting lies is prosecutable, requiring no hidden cameras to expose
European leaders' outrage at Vance's Munich speech is defensive because they know the criticism is accurate
Many Europeans sincerely believe they have free speech while simultaneously accepting that insulting people or posting unflattering statistics should be illegal
The trend toward speech prosecution extends across Europe, not just Germany, including Denmark's own draconian laws
money_quotes: This is German prosecutors telling an American journalist in an open interview that insulting people online is a crime and retweeting a "lie" will get you in trouble with the law. No hidden cameras! All out in the open!
Even typing out this description of what happens sounds like insane hyperbole, but you can just watch the clip for yourself.
Fifty raids in a day! For wrong speech, spicy memes, online insults of politicians, and other utterances by German citizens critical of their government or policies!
I suppose this is why European leaders are in such shock over Vance's wagging finger. Because they know he's dead on, but they're not used to getting called out like this.
But the humiliation of the European people is infinitely greater as they're gaslit about their right to free speech.

I keep hearing from Europeans who with a straight face will claim that of course they have free speech, but that doesn't mean you can insult people, hurt their feelings, or post statistics that might cast certain groups in a bad light.
Madness.
posts.key_insight: Germany's open, unapologetic prosecution of online speech offenses — conducted with armed dawn raids — validates American criticisms of European free speech and reveals a continent in denial about its own authoritarian tendencies.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Trump's blunt treatment of Europe is exposing a fundamental truth: Europe is militarily weak and dependent on American security guarantees, yet expects to be treated as an equal power on the world stage. He compares Trump's directness to a child's honesty, noting that while impolite, the observation about Europe's weakness is accurate. DHH contends that Europe's incoherent position — viewing Russia as simultaneously collapsing and an existential threat — stems from this weakness. He concludes that Europe should treat Trump's bullying as a cheap lesson, since Russia or China could teach the same lesson far more painfully.
key_points: Europe holds an incoherent position on Russia, seeing it as both about to collapse and as an unstoppable superpower threatening all of Europe
Military strength, not moral principles, is what earns a seat at the table in international relations — Europe lectures weaker countries while standing powerless before stronger ones
Europe cannot simultaneously enjoy a peace dividend, depend on American security, and expect to influence global affairs
Trump's rude treatment of Europe is a relatively cheap lesson compared to what Russia or China could impose
The rules-based international order is maintained by self-preservation and strength, not by higher principles alone
money_quotes: Trump is just as blunt when he essentially asks: "Why is Europe so weak?".
The big cats in the international jungle don't stick to a rules-based order purely out of higher principles, but out of self-preservation.
All too happy to lecture weaker countries they do not fear on high-minded ideals of democracy and free speech, while standing aghast and weeping powerlessly when someone stronger returns the favor.
Europe simply can't have it both ways. Be weak militarily, utterly dependent on an American security guarantee, and also expect a seat at the big-cat table.
You either get your peace dividend -- and the freedom to squander it on net-zero nonsense -- or you get to have a say in how the world around you is organized.
We're being punked by someone from our own alliance. That's a cheap way of learning the lesson that weakness, impotence, and peace-dividend thinking is a short-term strategy.
Get jacked, Europe, and you'll no longer get punked. Stay feeble, Europe, and the indignities won't stop with being snubbed in Saudi Arabia.
posts.key_insight: Europe's dependence on American military protection while expecting equal geopolitical influence is an unsustainable contradiction that Trump is crudely but accurately exposing.

posts.summary: DHH explores the difficult question of when to abandon a failing endeavor rather than persisting. He argues that while 'never give up' is a good default, the most dangerous trap isn't total failure but partial success — things that sorta-kinda work but aren't worth the continued investment. He uses Rails system tests as a concrete example, where 37signals recently deleted 5,000 lines of system tests from Basecamp after years of trying to make the approach work at scale. He draws parallels between startups, development projects, and societal policies, suggesting time boxing as a practical antidote and concluding that the right time to quit is probably sooner than you think.
key_points: The most dangerous trap isn't outright failure but partial success — ideas that sorta-kinda work keep people invested far longer than they should be
Even the best VCs are wrong more often than right because nobody can consistently predict disruption, making the decision of when to fold inherently difficult
Time boxing (like Shape Up's six-week cycles) provides a hard stop that counteracts the human tendency to throw good effort after bad
The ability to fold and preserve remaining resources applies universally — to startups, projects, methodologies, and even societal policies
Knowing when to give up requires humility, and the right answer is almost always 'less than you think, less than you want'
money_quotes: The trouble was that it sorta-kinda did work! Some of the time. With great effort. But ultimately wasn't worth the squeeze.
Having the grit to stick with the effort when the going gets hard is a key trait of successful people. But having the humility to give up on good bets turned bad might be just as important.
Stuck with an idea that sorta-kinda does work, but not well enough to be worth a decade of their life. That's a tragic trap.
The only antidote I've found to this on the development side is time boxing.
Whatever the answer, it's 'less than you think, less than you want'.
The idea ought to look a bit daft at first glance or it probably doesn't carry the counter-intuitive kernel needed to really pop.
Programmers are just as liable as anyone to believe a flawed design can work if given just a bit more time. And then a bit more. And then just double of what we've already spent.
posts.key_insight: The most dangerous failure mode isn't something that doesn't work at all, but something that sorta-kinda works — just enough to keep you trapped far longer than the investment is worth.

posts.summary: DHH argues that startup founders make a critical mistake by delegating customer-facing roles too early. He contends that direct interaction with customers through support and sales provides irreplaceable insights that get destroyed when paraphrased or summarized. He uses Basecamp's history as proof, where Jason Fried personally handled customer service for three years while the business grew to millions in ARR. The post also advocates for 'Everyone on Support' rotations, where programmers and designers regularly answer customer emails, leading to faster bug fixes and product improvements.
key_points: Founders should stay in customer-facing roles (support and sales) far longer than feels comfortable
Hiring salespeople or support agents too early strips the nutrients from customer feedback
Jason Fried personally handled 150 emails/day for three years before Basecamp hired its first support agent
Rotating all team members through support ('Everyone on Support') yields high dividends because programmers fix problems rather than apologize for them
The eagerness to replace support with AI-only interactions is another form of quitting on customers too early
money_quotes: The absolute worst thing you can do is hire a sales person or a customer service agent too early.
Seeing these reasons paraphrased or summarized destroy all the nutrients in their insights. You want that whole-grain feedback straight from the customers' mouth!
We fixed an incredible number of minor niggles and annoying bugs because programmers found it easier to solve the problem than to apologize for why it was there.
Customers often offer their valuable insights wrapped in rude language, unreasonable demands, and bad suggestions.
That's why there's such eagerness to reduce support to an AI-only interaction.
You don't have to do everything that every customer demands of you, but you should certainly listen to them.
You can't listen well if the sound is being muffled by early layers of indirection.
posts.key_insight: Direct, unfiltered customer interaction is a competitive advantage that founders sacrifice too early, and every layer of indirection between decision-makers and customers destroys the nutritional value of their feedback.

posts.summary: DHH argues that The New York Times functions as a gatekeeper of liberal orthodoxy, and its recent publication of a long piece on Denmark's immigration policy signals permission for the center-left to pivot on mass immigration. He uses Denmark as a case study showing that high crime rates among certain immigrant groups, welfare dependency, and cultural incompatibility have eroded public trust in the social safety net. DHH criticizes the article for crediting Danish Social Democrats with the policy shift rather than the Danish People's Party, which he says forced the issue. He concludes that this is democracy working as intended: voters chose parties addressing their immigration concerns, and the center-left is now pivoting because losing elections is forcing a reckoning.
key_points: The New York Times serves as gatekeeper of the liberal Overton window, and publishing counter-arguments to liberal orthodoxy signals a permitted pivot
Denmark is an effective case study because it's seen as a progressive ideal, yet it adopted restrictive immigration policies after confronting crime, welfare, and cultural integration problems
The NYT article credits Danish Social Democrats for the immigration pivot, but DHH argues the Danish People's Party deserves credit for forcing the issue starting in 1995
The tension between diversity and a generous welfare state is now being openly acknowledged, with academic research cited showing the trade-off
The pattern of mainstream parties ignoring immigration concerns until right-wing parties surge is repeating across Europe and the US, which DHH frames as democracy functioning correctly
money_quotes: When The New York Times allows the counter argument to liberal orthodoxy to be published, it signals to its readers that it's time to pivot.
Denmark is an incredibly effective case study in such arguments, because it's commonly thought of as the holy land of progressivism.
Diversity or welfare! That again would have been an absolutely explosive claim to make not all that long ago.
The real reason the Danes took this turn is that 'the far right' won in Denmark, and The Danish People's Party deserve the lion's share of the credit.
It's also hilarious that this process, where voters choose parties that champion the causes they care about, has been labeled The Grave Threat to Democracy in recent years.
If your political class is out of step with the mood of the populace, they're supposed to lose.
Losing sucks, and if you're on the center left, you'd like to see that end.
posts.key_insight: The New York Times publishing Denmark's immigration story signals the center-left is being given permission to pivot on mass immigration because continuing to ignore voter concerns means continuing to lose elections.

posts.summary: DHH traces his history with AMD from dismissing them as budget parts in the 1990s to now running AMD in every machine he owns. After 37signals switched from Intel to AMD EPYC servers in the early 2020s, DHH went all-in on AMD for desktops, laptops, and even his kids' gaming PCs after switching to Linux. He argues that TSMC's manufacturing is the key enabler lifting AMD and Qualcomm to near-parity with Apple's M-series chips, while Intel falls further behind by insisting on its own fabrication. The post celebrates AMD's decades-long journey from perpetual underdog to legitimate competitor.
key_points: 37signals switched entirely from Intel to AMD EPYC servers, running 96-core machines that outperform Intel on price/performance
TSMC is the hidden protagonist — its advanced manufacturing nodes are what enabled Apple's M-series lead and are now closing that gap for AMD and Qualcomm
Intel is falling behind because it insists on its own chip fabrication rather than leveraging TSMC's superior processes
Qualcomm's Snapdragon 8 Elite now matches or beats Apple's A18 Pro, ending years of Android CPU inferiority
Framework laptops paired with AMD chips represent a credible, repairable alternative to MacBooks for Linux developers
money_quotes: Today, the bulk of our new fleet run on dual EPYC 9454s for a total of 96 cores(!) per machine. They're awesome.
TSMC really is lifting all the boats behind its innovation locks.
It was so bad for a while that we had to program our web apps differently for Android, because they simply didn't have the power to run JavaScript fast enough! But that's all history now.
After dumping Apple, it's a delight to see that the M-series advantage is shrinking to irrelevance fast.
By focusing on repairability, upgradeability, and superior keyboards, we finally have an alternative for developer laptops that doesn't just feel like a cheap copy of a MacBook.

The company was founded just one year after Intel, back in 1969, but for its entire existence, it's lived in the shadow of its older brother. Now, thanks to TSMC, great leadership from Lisa Su, and a crack team of chip designers, they're finally reaping the rewards.
There's of course still the software reason for why someone would pick Apple, and they continue to make solid hardware, but the CPU playing field is now being leveled.
posts.key_insight: TSMC's manufacturing dominance is democratizing chip performance, enabling AMD and Qualcomm to close Apple's lead while Intel falls further behind by going it alone.

posts.summary: DHH argues that human customer service is becoming a luxury good as AI replaces commodity-level support. While acknowledging AI's promise, he contends that current AI customer service often traps users in frustrating loops when they just need a human. He distinguishes between low-end support that nobody will miss and skilled human agents who tailor interactions to both the problem and the customer's temperament. Basecamp's thesis is that treating customer service as a career produces agents who deliver this luxury experience.
key_points: Current AI customer service frequently traps users in frustrating loops, making human access feel like a premium feature
Humans need more than correct answers — they need understanding, reassurance, and genuine connection
AI will replace commodity support that was already done poorly by disengaged workers at cost-obsessed companies
Skilled human agents read temperament as well as problems, delivering bespoke interactions that AI cannot replicate
Treating customer service as a career rather than a cost center is Basecamp's competitive differentiator
money_quotes: I can't tell you how often I've been stuck in some god-forsaken AI loop or phone tree WHEN ALL I WANT IS A HUMAN.
In our excitement about the technical promise, I think we're forgetting that humans need more than correct answers.
Customer service at its best also offers understanding and reassurance. It offers a human connection.
Nobody is going to cry a tear for losing that.
A customer service agent who's good at their job knows how to tailor the interaction not just to your problem, but to your temperament.
They can spot an angry-but-actually-just-frustrated fit a thousand miles away.
That's luxury.
By treating customer service as a career, we'll end up with the kind of agents that embody this luxury, and our customers will feel the difference.
posts.key_insight: As AI commoditizes basic customer support, companies that invest in skilled human agents will turn customer service into a luxury differentiator rather than a cost center.

posts.summary: DHH describes how he suffered from seasonal allergies for years, cycling through medications and allergy vaccinations recommended by specialists, none of whom addressed the root cause: the air he was breathing. He solved the problem by installing HEPA air purifiers throughout his home, which eliminated most of his symptoms. He uses this personal experience to critique the medical profession's focus on mitigation over prevention. The broader point is that experts can overcomplicate problems that have simple environmental solutions.
key_points: Multiple allergy specialists prescribed medications and vaccinations but never asked about the air quality in his environment
HEPA air purifiers combined with air quality sensors effectively eliminated his chronic allergy symptoms for over a decade
The medical profession's focus on treating symptoms rather than addressing environmental causes represents a systemic blind spot
The dose makes the poison — reducing allergen exposure most of the time is sufficient even if you can't eliminate it entirely
Expertise can become a liability when specialists are too focused on complex solutions to see simple ones
money_quotes: But never once did an allergy doctor ask the basic question: What kind of air are you breathing?
That's what's carrying all this particulate matter, so if your idea of proper ventilation is merely to open a window, you're inviting in your nasal assailants.
That's it. That was the answer!
The difference between breathing in some allergens, some of the time, is entirely different from breathing all of it, all of the time.

Here was this entire profession of allergy specialists, and I saw at least a handful of them while I was trying to find a medical solution. None of them even thought about dealing with the environment.
Their entire field of view was restricted to dealing with mitigation rather than prevention.
Not every problem, medical or otherwise, has a simple solution. But many problems do, and you have to be careful not to be so smart that you can't see it.
posts.key_insight: Experts can become so focused on sophisticated interventions that they overlook simple environmental fixes — sometimes the best solution is eliminating the cause rather than treating the symptoms.

posts.summary: DHH argues that running a business purely by quantifiable metrics is impoverished, and that the best products come from leaders who care deeply about what they make. He critiques MBA-style management that reduces everything to abstract categories and spreadsheets. Founder-led companies maintain an edge because taste and judgment drive countless decisions that can't be captured in numbers. He advocates for 'vibe management' — trusting instincts and pursuing beauty alongside basic financial discipline.
key_points: Evaluating everything exclusively by quantifiable metrics is an impoverished way to run a business
Founder-led companies maintain an edge because genuine passion for the product shows up in countless value judgments that spreadsheets can't capture
Strong financial results are often derivatives of doing unquantifiable things well
Business owners should embrace 'vibe management' — following instincts and having fun — without apology
Financial basics (profitability, not overextending) provide the cushion needed to lead primarily on vision and vibes
money_quotes: Weighing everything exclusively by its quantifiable properties is an impoverished way to view business (and the world!).
The death of all that makes for a breakout product or service happens when the generic lingo of management theory takes over.

When there's someone at the top who actually gives a damn about cars, watches, bags, software, or whatever the hell the company makes, it shows up in a million value judgments that can't be quantified neatly on a spreadsheet.
Much of the time, those figures are derivatives of doing all the stuff that you can't compute and that won't quantify.

No business owner should ever apologize for having fun, following their instincts, or trusting that the numbers will eventually show that doing the right thing, the beautiful thing, the poetic thing is going to pay off somehow.
Once there's a bit of margin, you owe it to yourself to lean on that cushion and lead the business primarily on the basis of good vibes and a long vision.
posts.key_insight: The best businesses are built on taste and conviction that can't be quantified — financial metrics are the byproduct, not the driver, of caring deeply about what you make.

posts.summary: DHH draws a parallel between Microsoft's dismissal of the iPhone in 2007 and Apple's current fumbling of AI. He argues that Apple executives are exhibiting the same delusional confidence that cost Ballmer-era Microsoft the mobile market. Apple Intelligence has delivered nothing but vaporware and terrible image generation, yet leadership keeps promoting it. DHH blames the failure on Tim Cook's lack of product savvy and fiefdom management under AI VP John Giannandrea, concluding that Apple needs a leadership reboot.
key_points: Apple's AI confidence mirrors Steve Ballmer's infamous iPhone dismissal — incumbents assuming past dominance guarantees future success
Apple Intelligence has been marketed aggressively but delivered poorly, amounting to vaporware that won't materialize as promised
Tim Cook lacks the product judgment to distinguish genuine AI progress from internal bullshit, enabling years of failure under Giannandrea
Apple has made costly hardware decisions (RAM spec bumps) for AI features that don't work, hurting margins for nothing
The problem is structural: a logistics-minded CEO running a product company leads to regression to the mean
money_quotes: Nobody at Apple seems able or courageous enough to face the music: Apple Intelligence sucks. Siri sucks. None of the vaporware is anywhere close to happening.
Cook evidently does not have the product savvy to be able to tell bullshit from benefit, so he keeps giving Giannandrea more rope.
This is what regression to the mean looks like. This is what fiefdom management looks like. This is what having a company run by a logistics guy looks like.

Now the fella has hung Apple's reputation on vaporware, promised all iPhone 16 customers something magical that just won't happen, and even spec-bumped all their devices with more RAM for nothing but diminished margins.
Exuding the same delusional confidence that lost Ballmer's Microsoft any serious part in the mobile game.
Apple needs a leadership reboot, stat. That asterisk is a stain.
posts.key_insight: Apple's AI failures aren't a technical problem but a leadership one — Tim Cook's inability to evaluate product quality has let a fiefdom of vaporware promises erode Apple's reputation, mirroring exactly how Ballmer's Microsoft lost mobile.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Denmark's strict immigration policies, while widely praised and emulated by Sweden and Germany, have not solved the integration problems caused by decades of immigration from MENAPT countries. He presents evidence from 2025 Danish media showing ongoing issues with school violence, women's safety in nightlife, and sports violence linked to immigrant descendants. The core argument is that second-generation immigrants are statistically more problematic than their parents, undermining the thesis that time and assimilation will resolve these issues. He concludes that even closing borders is insufficient because existing populations of maladjusted descendants will continue causing problems for decades.
key_points: Denmark is praised as an immigration policy model, but thirty years of strict policies haven't solved integration problems with MENAPT descendants
Data shows descendants of MENAPT immigrants are more likely to be violent and troublesome than their parents, contradicting the assimilation thesis
Sweden's dramatic reduction in new arrivals hasn't reduced problems because the existing immigrant population continues growing through family reunification
Three 2025 Danish cases illustrate persistent failures: school violence, women's safety in nightlife, and sports violence from immigrant-background communities
Even countries that 'close the borders' face decades of consequences from existing maladjusted populations
money_quotes: That's an explosive point because it blows up the thesis that time will solve these problems. Showing instead that it actually just makes it worse. And then what?
Meaning even if European countries 'close the borders', they're still condemned to deal with the damning effects of maladjusted MENAPT immigrant descendants for decades to come.
Obviously, if you're in a hole, you should stop digging. And Sweden has done just that. But just because you aren't compounding the problem doesn't mean you've found a way out.
Denmark proves to be both a positive example of minimizing the digging while also a cautionary tale that the hole is still there.
The very same countries that until recently condemned the lack of open-arms/open-border policies they would champion as Moral Superpowers.
Descendants of MENAPT immigrants are more likely to be violent and troublesome than their parents.
posts.key_insight: Closing borders to new immigration is necessary but insufficient — European countries face decades of compounding integration failures because second-generation MENAPT immigrants are assimilating less, not more, than their parents.

posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals has completed migrating every application in their fleet to Kamal, the deployment tool he built in six weeks out of frustration with Kubernetes complexity and enterprise pricing. The migration covers everything from active development apps to heritage applications dating back to 2003. He frames this through 37signals' 'Until The End Of The Internet' philosophy — a commitment to never abandoning products customers depend on. Docker's ability to encapsulate entire systems makes long-term maintenance feasible, and Kamal provides a simple wrapper around that capability. The post celebrates that Basecamp's original 2004-era codebase still runs for paying customers two decades later.
key_points: Kamal was built in six weeks as a reaction to enterprisey pricing and Kubernetes complexity during 37signals' cloud exit
Every 37signals web app — including the original 2003 Basecamp codebase — now deploys uniformly through Kamal
37signals' 'Until The End Of The Internet' philosophy means never forcing customers off working products, even decades-old ones
Docker enables long-term software preservation by encapsulating entire systems with all dependencies
Simplicity is framed as the prerequisite for durability — complexity is inherently fragile
money_quotes: I refused to accept that running our own hardware had to be that expensive or that convoluted. So I got busy building a cheap and simple alternative.
The modern build-boost-discard cycle of internet applications is a scourge.
Users ought to be able to trust that when they adopt a system like Basecamp or HEY, they don't have to fear eviction from the next executive re-org.
That's the OG code base from 2003! Which hasn't seen any updates since 2010, beyond security patches, bug fixes, and performance improvements.
Docker allows you to encapsulate an entire system, with all of its dependencies, and run it until the end of time.
Complexity is but a bridge — and a fragile one at that. To build something durable, you have to make it simple.
Kamal seeks to be but a simple wrapper and workflow around this wondrous simplicity.
posts.key_insight: Durable software requires simplicity — Kamal and Docker let 37signals run every app they've ever built, proving that the industry's build-and-discard cycle is a choice, not an inevitability.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Denmark's obsessive recycling and sustainability rituals have become a secular replacement for religion, filling the spiritual void left by declining Christianity. Despite being officially Lutheran, fewer than 2% of Danes attend church, yet they dutifully sort trash into seven bins and wash plastic containers before recycling. He contends these acts are largely performative and inefficient — trash-sorting robots could do the job better — and serve primarily as moral absolution rather than meaningful environmental action. The rituals function like Catholic indulgences, letting people buy a clean conscience while still setting airport travel records.
key_points: Denmark remains constitutionally Christian with a church tax, yet church attendance has collapsed to under 2%, creating a spiritual vacuum
Climate consciousness has filled the role of religion, complete with guilt, repentance, rituals, and heretics like Bjorn Lomborg
Recycling theater — washing trash, sorting into seven bins — is performative suffering that robots could handle more effectively
Small symbolic acts function as modern indulgences, absolving guilt over larger environmental impacts like record-breaking air travel
DHH distinguishes between genuine environmental progress (nuclear energy, bikes replacing cars) and meaningless ritualistic compliance
money_quotes: God is as dead here as anywhere in the Western world. Less than 2% attend church service on a weekly basis. So one way to fill the void is through climate panic and piety.
This leads to the grotesque daily ritual of washing trash (and wasting water galore in the process!).
This is exactly what Nietzsche meant by "God is dead" — his warning that we'd need to fill the void with another centering orientation toward the world.
The point of it all seems to be self-inflicted, symbolic suffering — solely to remind you of your good standing with the sacred lord of recycling, refuting the plastic devil.
We buy a good conscience washing trash so we don't have to feel guilty setting new records flying for fun.
I'm not religious, but I'm starting to think it'd be nicer to spend a Sunday morning in the presence of the Almighty than to keep washing trash as pagan replacement therapy.
It's got guilt, repentance, and plenty of rituals to spare. Oh, and its heretics too.
posts.key_insight: Modern recycling rituals in secular societies function as religious substitutes — offering guilt, penance, and moral absolution — while doing little to address the environmental problems they claim to solve.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on why having children is worthwhile, arguing that the value isn't in children being objectively remarkable but in them becoming the most interesting people in the world to their parents. He acknowledges that this experience is inherently personal and impossible to convey convincingly to others. The post is a brief, personal meditation rather than his typical tech commentary, ending with the admission that parenthood requires a leap of faith.
key_points: Having kids no longer has a default cultural justification — it now requires an explanation
The value of children isn't objective interestingness but subjective fascination — they're the most interesting people *to you*
Watching your children's growth is an unparalleled privilege that can't be adequately described to others
The case for parenthood is fundamentally non-transferable — it requires a leap of faith
money_quotes: We didn't used to need an explanation for having kids. That was just life. That's just what you did. But now we do, because now we don't.
Having kids means making the most interesting people in the world.
There are no humans on earth I'm as interested in as my children.
Their maturation and growth are the greatest show on the planet.
Having a front-seat ticket to this performance is literally the privilege of a lifetime.
I could never convince a stranger that my children are the most interesting people in the world, because they wouldn't be, to them.
So words don't work. It's a leap of faith. All I can really say is this: Trust me, bro.
posts.key_insight: The case for having children can't be made with arguments — it's an irreducibly personal experience where your own kids become the most fascinating people in your world, but only to you.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Apple's leadership has a severe age problem, with board members averaging 68 years old and executives around 60. He contends this gerontocracy is directly contributing to Apple's missteps, particularly in AI, where leadership lacks genuine engagement with the products they're shipping. He draws a contrast with Meta's younger board and Steve Jobs' own relatively young tenure, arguing Apple needs a blend of fluid and crystallized intelligence to course-correct.
key_points: Apple's board averages 68 years old with nearly half over 70, and its executive team averages around 60
The age imbalance is manifesting in poor product decisions, particularly Apple's AI strategy and features like Genmoji that feel disconnected from actual user needs
Effective leadership requires a mix of fluid intelligence (younger, adaptive thinking) and crystallized intelligence (wisdom from experience)
Meta's board averages 55 with members in their 40s, and Steve Jobs himself led Apple's greatest era while in his 40s and 50s
Apple's 'AI for the rest of us' messaging inadvertently reveals a leadership that views AI as something bewildering rather than transformative
money_quotes: It's ridiculous that the world's premier tech company is now run by a gerontocracy.
I can picture the board presentation on Genmoji: "It's what the kids want these days!!".
It's a dumb feature because nobody on Apple's board or in its leadership has probably ever used it outside a quick demo.
You need to blend fluid and crystallized intelligence.
You need some people with a finger on the pulse, not just some bravely keeping one.
It's as if AI is like programming a VCR, and you need the grandkids to come over and set it up for you.
Apple literally needs some fresh blood to turn the ship around.
posts.key_insight: Apple's aging leadership lacks the fluid intelligence and genuine product engagement needed to navigate the AI era, and the company's recent missteps are a direct consequence of its gerontocratic governance.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Apple's AI failures stem from a leadership culture that prioritizes feelings and effort over outcomes. He contrasts the current Apple AI lead's empathetic response to the delayed Apple Intelligence features with Steve Jobs' famous MobileMe tirade, where Jobs berated his team for tarnishing Apple's reputation. DHH contends that competitive technology companies need leaders willing to be 'assholes' — people who demand results over comfort. He concludes that Tim Cook isn't up to the task and that Apple's VP fiefdom structure is poisoning the integrated product magic the company was known for.
key_points: Apple's AI unit is failing because its leadership prioritizes employee feelings and effort over shipping outcomes
The most successful tech leaders (Jobs, Gates, Musk) were 'assholes' who demanded results in competitive markets
The contrast between the current Siri team lead defending his group's effort and Jobs' MobileMe tirade illustrates Apple's cultural decline
Apple's leadership structure of senior VP fiefdoms prevents the integrated product vision that made Apple great
Tim Cook is not the right leader to fix Apple's product execution problems
money_quotes: When things are going well, managers can fool themselves into thinking that people trying their best is all that matters.
In management parlance, an asshole is someone who cares less about feelings or effort and more about outcomes.
Most top technology chiefs who've had to really fight in competitive markets for the top prize fall into this category.
Apple's AI management is missing an asshole.
Little fiefdoms. This is poison to the integrated magic that was Apple's trademark for so long.
Apple needs to find a new asshole and put them in charge of the entire product line.
Poor outcomes are just another opportunity for learning! But that delusion stops working when the wheels finally start coming off.
posts.key_insight: Companies in competitive markets need leaders who prioritize outcomes over effort and feelings, and Apple's current empathy-first management culture is why its AI strategy is failing.

posts.summary: DHH contrasts his free-roaming 1980s childhood in Copenhagen with the hyper-supervised upbringing common in modern American parenting. He argues that Denmark still offers children the independence to bike across town and manage their own social lives, which he credits as a key reason Denmark ranks as the best country for raising children. While acknowledging Jonathan Haidt's work on childhood fragility, DHH pushes back on the idea that smartphones are the primary culprit, noting that Danish kids have phones just as early but use them as tools to coordinate their independence. He concludes that American parents are scapegoating smartphones to avoid confronting the real problem: the death of unsupervised free play.
key_points: Copenhagen still offers children the 1980s-style independence that has largely vanished from American parenting culture
The expectation that parents arrange and supervise all childhood interactions is a bigger problem than smartphones
Denmark's high smartphone adoption among children hasn't produced the same anxious generation, undermining the phone-as-primary-cause argument
Banning smartphones without restoring unsupervised free play may further isolate children from managing their own social lives
Upper-middle-class American parents prefer blaming technology over interrogating their own over-involvement in children's lives
money_quotes: My parents rarely had any idea where I went after school, as long as I was home by dinner.
Play dates are commonly arranged via parents, even for fourth or fifth graders.

Upper-middle-class American parents have decided that blaming the smartphone for everything is easier than interrogating the lack of unsupervised free play, rough-and-tumble interactions for boys, and early childhood independence.
It also just doesn't track in countries like Denmark, where the smartphone is just as prevalent, if not more so, than in America.

My oldest had his own phone by third grade, and so did everyone else in his class — and it was a key tool for them to coordinate the independence that The Coddling of the American Mind called for more of.

I think it's delusional of American parents to think that banning the smartphone — further isolating their children from independently managing their social lives — is going to be the one quick fix that cures the anxious generation.
What we need is more 80s-style freedom and independence for kids in America.
posts.key_insight: The American childhood anxiety crisis stems more from the death of unsupervised free play and parental over-involvement than from smartphones, as Denmark demonstrates that kids with early phone access but genuine independence thrive.

posts.summary: DHH argues that immortality is a curse, not a gift, and that the finitude of life is what gives it beauty and meaning. Drawing on cultural touchstones like Highlander, Queen, and Björk, he makes the case that satiation applies to life itself just as it does to individual pleasures. He reflects on passing the halfway point of his own life expectancy and finds comfort rather than dread in eventual endings — of racing, coding, and life itself. He concludes with the Stoic idea of holding life with a loose grip, embracing both fate and death.
key_points: People intuitively understand satiation for individual pleasures but irrationally resist applying the same logic to life itself
Immortality brings loneliness, repetition, and cynicism — the curse of forever is worse than the mercy of finality
Even novelty eventually becomes variation on existing themes; surprise itself succumbs to familiarity
The knowledge that specific activities will end — racing, coding, writing — adds meaning rather than diminishing it
Holding life with a loose grip, like drumsticks, lets the music flow better until the song naturally ends
money_quotes: Immortality always sounded like a curse to me.

It's funny, people seem to have no problem understanding satiation when it comes to the individual parts of life. Enough delicious cake, no more rides on the rollercoaster, the end of a great party. But not life itself.
The eventual end strikes me as beautiful relief. Framing the idea that you can see enough, do enough, be enough.
It nails the sadness of forever. The loneliness, the repetition, the inevitable cynicism with humanity.
It's a great big world, but eventually every unseen element will appear as but a variation on an existing theme. Even surprise itself will succumb to familiarity.
One day I will write the last line of Ruby code, too. Sell the last subscription. Write the last tweet. How merciful.
One way to think of this is to hold life with a loose grip. Like a pair of drumsticks.
Amor fati. Amor mori.
posts.key_insight: Life's finitude is not a tragedy to be overcome but a mercy that gives meaning to every experience, and the wisest response is to hold it all with a loose grip.

posts.summary: DHH details the final phase of 37signals' cloud exit: migrating nearly six petabytes of files from AWS S3 to on-premise Pure Storage hardware. They're currently spending $1.5 million per year on S3 under a four-year contract expiring June 30, and missing that deadline would cost $5,000 per day. The replacement infrastructure—18 petabytes of NVMe storage across two data centers—is already racked and ready. Over five years, the move will save roughly $5 million compared to continued S3 costs, with hardware and support totaling about $2.5 million. The post frames the migration as a concrete, deadline-driven operation with real financial stakes.
key_points: 37signals spends nearly $1.5M/year on S3, locked in only by a four-year contract expiring June 30
Pure Storage's S3-compatible API eliminates the need for CEPH or MinIO, simplifying the application-side migration
AWS now offers a free 60-day egress window for departing customers, reducing transfer costs to zero
Transferring six petabytes over a 40-Gbit pipe will take at least three weeks with overhead
The five-year savings total nearly $5 million against roughly $2.5 million in hardware, warranty, and support costs
money_quotes: We're spending just shy of $1.5 million/year on AWS S3 at the moment to host files for Basecamp, HEY, and everything else.
A combined 18 petabytes, securely replicated a thousand miles apart. It's a gorgeous rack full of blazing-fast NVMe storage modules.

In an earlier age, that egress alone would have cost hundreds of thousands of dollars in fees alone. But now AWS offers a free 60-day egress window for anyone who wants to leave, so that drops the cost to $0.
If we don't get this done in time, we'll be paying a cool five thousand dollars a day to continue to use S3.
That's $35,000/week! That's $150,000/month!
Over five years, it'll now be almost five million!
The idea of paying $5,000/day, if we miss our departure date, is awfully concrete in comparison.
posts.key_insight: The cloud exit's final S3 migration has a hard deadline with a $5,000/day penalty for delay, but the five-year savings of nearly $5 million against $2.5 million in hardware costs make the urgency entirely justified.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the approaching AI singularity and argues that rather than fearing the unpredictable future it brings, we should embrace it with philosophical serenity. He traces the concept from von Neumann to today's rapid AI advances, noting that recent progress in image generation, robotics, and language models has made even skeptics take notice. He compares this moment to past paradigm shifts like the agricultural and industrial revolutions. Drawing on Christianity's Serenity Prayer, Stoic philosophy, and Buddhism, he argues that all great intellectual traditions converge on the same insight: let go of what you cannot control. He concludes that since nobody can pause or redirect AI progress, the wisest response is amor fati — loving whatever fate brings.
key_points: Recent AI advances in style-consistent image generation, video, and robotics represent a potential paradigm shift comparable to the agricultural or industrial revolutions
Nobody can predict what the world will look like four years from now, and there is no pause button or off-ramp for AI progress
The uncertainty of the AI future should be received as exhilarating rather than frightening, since worry over the uncontrollable is wasted energy
Christianity, Stoicism, and Buddhism all converge on the same principle: release attachment to outcomes you cannot control
The concept of amor fati should extend beyond personal fate to embracing the fate of the world and its technological transformations
money_quotes: What good is it to fret about a future you don't control anyway?
That's the marvel and the danger of progress: nobody is actually in charge!
This is all being driven by a million independent agents chasing irresistible incentives. There's no pause button, let alone an off-ramp.
I've never felt less able to predict what the next token of reality will look like.
This is one of those paradigm shifts with the potential for Total Change. Like the agricultural revolution, the industrial revolution, the information revolution.
You don't get to know the script to life in advance, but what an incredible show, if you just let it unfold.
The singularity may be here soon, or it may not. You'd be a fool to be convinced either way. But you'll find serenity in accepting whatever happens.
posts.key_insight: The wisest response to AI's unpredictable trajectory is not fear or false certainty but philosophical acceptance — embracing the uncontrollable future as the great traditions have always taught.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on his first year using Linux, Neovim, and a Framework laptop after leaving Apple's ecosystem. He describes how the transition was difficult for the first couple of weeks but quickly became second nature. Rather than evangelizing Linux, he takes a deliberately modest stance, acknowledging that most developers may not have the will or desire to switch. He frames the move as a personal choice driven by curiosity and the joy of learning, comparing it to his discovery of Ruby.
key_points: Habits are hard to break initially but once broken are quickly forgotten — the two-decade Apple habit faded in weeks
Mental flexibility decreases with age, making it harder to start over as a beginner, but the reward remains equally satisfying
Aggressive advocacy and religious arguments about technology choices are counterproductive — planting seeds and leading by example works better
The switch is a personal computing choice, not a universal prescription — there are legitimate reasons to stay with Apple
The mimetic engine of persuasion (showing rather than telling) is more effective than crusading
money_quotes: Humans are rigid in the short term, but flexible in the long term. Blessed are the few who can retain the grit to push through that early mental resistance and reach new maxima.
It takes more of me now to wipe a mental slate clean and start over. To go back to being a beginner. But the reward for learning something new is as satisfying as ever.

I don't know if most developers are better off on Linux. I mean, I believe they are, at some utopian level, especially if they work for the web, using open source tooling. But I don't know if they are as humans with limited will or capacity for change.
It's a dead end trying to convince anyone of an alternative with the sharp end of a religious argument.
What I've found to work much better is planting seeds and showing off your plowshare. Let whatever curiosity that blooms find its own way towards your blue sky.
The mimetic engine of persuasion runs much cleaner anyway.
The sun is still shining, the wind is in my hair, and the smile on my lips hasn't been this big since the earliest days of OS X.
posts.key_insight: The best technology advocacy isn't persuasion but demonstration — lead by example, stay humble about your choices, and let others find their own path.

posts.summary: DHH argues that AI's creative development follows the same trajectory as human artists and national economies: starting with poor copies, progressing to good copies, then achieving originality. He draws parallels to Japan's and China's economic rise through imitation, and to how artists learn by copying masters. He contends that AI is currently in the 'good copy' phase and that true AI creativity is inevitable. Critics fixating on minor flaws in AI-generated art, like the Ghibli trend, will only accelerate the push toward genuine originality.
key_points: All creativity builds on imitation — learning to copy well is the direct path to mastery, not a detour
Japan and China followed the same copy-to-original arc economically that AI is now following creatively
AI has already passed the 'cheap copy' phase and is firmly in 'good copy' territory, with originality likely imminent
Whether AI creativity is desirable is a moral question, but it's going to happen regardless
Critics nitpicking AI's current flaws are inadvertently motivating AI builders to push past imitation faster
money_quotes: Stealing good ideas isn't a detour on the way to becoming a master — it's the straight route. And it's nothing to be ashamed of.
You start with a cheap copy, then you learn how to make a good copy, and then you don't need to copy at all.
AI has sped through the phase of cheap copies. It's now firmly established in the realm of good copies. You're a fool if you don't believe originality is a likely next step.
Whether we want AI to become truly creative is a fair question — albeit a theoretical or, at best, moral one. Because it's going to happen if it can happen, and it almost certainly can.
AI builders, like the Japanese and Chinese economies before them, eager to demonstrate an ability to exceed.
All that is to say that AI is in the 'Good Copy' phase of its creative evolution. Expect 'The Great Artist' to emerge at any moment.
posts.key_insight: AI's creative trajectory mirrors how artists and economies have always evolved — from cheap imitation to good imitation to originality — and the leap to genuine AI creativity is now imminent and unstoppable.

posts.summary: DHH argues that universal basic income is a dead end because work provides not just income but existential purpose. He contends that most people, especially men, deteriorate psychologically without the external structure and validation that employment provides. Drawing on Frankl, Dostoevsky, and Orwell, he suggests that humans are poorly built for idle leisure and that the intellectual fantasy of liberated artists freed from capitalism ignores how most people actually respond to joblessness. While acknowledging that AI may eliminate jobs as inevitably as previous revolutions, he warns that replacing work's 'why' will be harder than building the AI itself.
key_points: Work provides existential purpose and social belonging, not just income — most people who lose jobs spiral into despair rather than liberation
UBI advocates project an intellectual fantasy of freed artists onto a population that mostly needs prepackaged purpose from work
The comfort paradox: the more frictionless life becomes, the harder people struggle with 'the problem of being,' as evidenced by the pathology of the idle rich
Previous technological revolutions (agricultural, industrial) displaced workers but led to broadly better outcomes — the AI revolution may be different
Even meaningless work offers something; a completely blank page with no deadline is worse than a bullshit job
money_quotes: The purpose of work is not just about earning your keep, but also about earning a purpose and a place in the world.
It's a great irony that the more comfortable and frictionless our existence becomes, the harder we struggle with the 'the problem of being'.
While the masses can easily identify the pathology of that when it comes to the idle rich, and especially their stereotyped trust-fund offspring, they still crave it for themselves.

In the Universal Basic Income scenario of having to design your own adventure entirely from scratch, there is nothing. Just a completely blank page with no deadline to motivate writing the first line.
If we kill the old 9-5 'why', we better find a new one.
Musk's craving for the constant chaos of grand gestures is Dostoevsky's prediction underwritten by the wealth of the world's richest man.
We just aren't built for a life of easy leisure. Not in mass numbers, anyway.
Even a stupid email job offers something. Maybe it isn't much, maybe it doesn't truly nourish the soul, but it's something.
posts.key_insight: Universal basic income fails not as economics but as existential policy — most people need work's prepackaged purpose, and eliminating it without a replacement 'why' is a civilizational dead end.

posts.summary: DHH explains why 37signals is hiring a junior programmer at $145,849 — a salary high enough to attract experienced developers — but strictly limiting applicants to those with less than three years of experience. The company is hiring for 'peak promise' rather than existing knowledge, arguing that a programmer with five years of experience has already revealed their trajectory. Junior developers represent untapped potential whose growth curve is still unknown and could be steep. He compares it to NFL scouting, where teams pay premiums for rookies who might become all-stars rather than veterans whose ceiling is known.
key_points: 37signals pays top-10% San Francisco salary for juniors because they're hiring for extraordinary potential, not current knowledge
A programmer with five or more years of experience has already revealed their growth trajectory — what you see is roughly what you'll get
Junior hiring is a deliberate bet on upside: fresh developers are 'essentially all potential' with steep possible growth curves
The approach mirrors sports scouting combines — paying a premium for unproven rookies who could become the best
This is explicitly acknowledged as risky: many promising juniors will fail to reach the level the company is betting on
money_quotes: We're hiring for peak promise — and such promise only remains until it's revealed.
A programmer who's been working professionally for five years has likely already revealed their potential. What you're going to get is roughly what you see.
The trajectory by which they improve has already been plotted.
A programmer who's either straight out of school or fresh off their first internship or short-stint job is essentially all potential.
You draw their line on the basis of just a few early dots, but the line can be steep.
That's the chance you take to land someone extraordinary.
posts.key_insight: Hiring juniors at premium salaries is a deliberate bet on unrevealed potential — experienced programmers have already shown their ceiling, but fresh talent's growth trajectory is still unwritten.

posts.summary: DHH explores the paradox founders must embrace: believing their venture will succeed while knowing statistically it probably won't. He argues this isn't contradictory — belief fuels the energy needed to compete, while accepting the odds keeps you grounded. The resolution is to build work you'd be proud of regardless of outcome, so that failure doesn't destroy your self-worth. He warns that the deepest regret comes not from failing, but from compromising yourself in the process.
key_points: Founders must hold two contradictory beliefs simultaneously: conviction they'll succeed and acceptance that most ventures fail
Belief isn't delusion — it's the fuel that generates the energy, fortitude, and determination required to even have a chance
The way to reconcile these opposing truths is to build something you'd be proud of regardless of the outcome
Deep regret comes from betraying your own standards for nothing, not from honest failure
Self-worth should be decoupled from business outcomes
money_quotes: To be a successful founder, you have to believe that what you're working on is going to work — despite knowing it probably won't!
Believing that what you're building is going to work is an essential component of coming to work with the energy, fortitude, and determination it's going to require to even have a shot.
Not because they don't know the odds, but because they've chosen to believe they're special.
If it doesn't work, you still made something you wouldn't be ashamed to put your name on.
Your worst sin is that you have destroyed and betrayed yourself for nothing.
Build it in a way that makes you proud to sign it.
Base your worth as a human on something greater than a business outcome.
posts.key_insight: The founder's paradox — needing simultaneous belief and realism — is resolved not by hedging your bets, but by doing work worthy of your name regardless of whether it succeeds.

posts.summary: DHH argues that ADHD is massively over-diagnosed in American boys, with nearly a quarter of seventeen-year-olds now carrying the diagnosis. Drawing on a New York Times Magazine investigation, he contends that ADHD medications like Adderall don't actually improve learning outcomes and their benefits fade within a year. He characterizes most DSM-5 ADHD symptoms as normal boyhood behavior and frames the epidemic as a 'pathology of normalcy' driven by a narrow educational ideal that demands all children sit still and study. He draws parallels to the opioid crisis and puberty-blocker controversies as examples of medical establishments being swept up in harmful paradigms.
key_points: ADHD medications make studying tolerable but don't improve actual learning, with benefits fading within a year and disappearing within three years
The DSM-5 diagnostic criteria for ADHD largely describe normal childhood behavior, especially in boys, requiring only six symptoms over six months
The over-diagnosis stems from a narrow educational ideal that pathologizes high-energy, kinetic boys who don't fit the sit-still-and-study mold
The ADHD medication trend parallels other medical overcorrections like the opioid crisis and puberty-blocker prescriptions
Long-term amphetamine use carries real costs: growth inhibition, emotional flattening, and loss of spirit
money_quotes: Nearly a quarter of seventeen-year-old boys in America have an ADHD diagnosis. That's crazy.
Taking Adderall or Ritalin doesn't actually help you learn more, it merely makes trying tolerable.
It's Dunning-Kruger — the phenomenon where low-competence individuals overestimate their abilities — in a pill.
The majority of these so-called symptoms are what I'd classify as 'normal boyhood'.
I think we're looking at a pathology of normalcy, where healthy boys are defined as those who can sit still, focus on studies, and suppress kinetic energy.
This is all downstream from an obsession with getting as many kids through as much safety-obsessed schooling as possible.
Doctors are supposed to first do no harm, but they're as liable to be swept up in bad paradigms, social contagions, and ideological echo chambers as the rest of us.
posts.key_insight: America's ADHD epidemic is not a medical crisis but a cultural one, where normal boyhood energy has been pathologized to fit a narrow educational ideal, and the medical establishment has been complicit in drugging a generation of boys with amphetamines that don't even work.

posts.summary: DHH reviews the new Framework 13 laptop with AMD HX370 chip, noting its strong performance gains for developers including beating an M4 Pro MacBook in running the HEY test suite. He acknowledges the top spec has gotten expensive at nearly $2,000, approaching MacBook Pro pricing, but argues the Framework's keyboard, 3:2 matte screen, repairability, and Linux compatibility justify it. He suggests the mid-range Ryzen AI 7 350 may be the sweet spot at $1,600. The post doubles as an endorsement of Linux for web development, contrasting its speed and simplicity with Apple's increasingly bloated software direction.
key_points: The HX370 Framework 13 runs the HEY test suite faster than an M4 Pro MacBook Pro, validating AMD+Linux as a serious developer platform
Top-spec Framework pricing now rivals MacBook Pro, but mid-range options like the Ryzen AI 7 350 at $1,600 may offer the best value
Linux on the desktop with Framework hardware has become DHH's preferred development environment after over a year of daily use
Apple's software quality is declining with more bloat, ads, and gimmicks, while Linux offers a clean, fast alternative especially for Docker-heavy web work
Framework's commitment to 100% backwards-compatible upgrades across generations sets it apart from disposable laptop culture
money_quotes: This remains my favorite laptop of at least the last decade.
The 3:2 aspect ratio combined with Linux's superb multiple desktops that switch with 0ms lag and no animations means I barely miss the trusted 6K Apple XDR screen when working away from the desk.
Apple still has a strong hardware game, but their software story is falling apart.
It seems like without an asshole in charge, both have move towards more bloat, more ads, more gimmicks, more control. Linux is an incredible antidote to this nonsense these days.
For any work that requires even a minimum of Docker, it's so fast.
Watching Framework perfect their 13" laptop while remaining 100% backwards compatible in terms of upgrades with the first versions is heartwarming.
It's in my opinion the best Linux laptop you can buy today, which by extension makes it the best web developer laptop too.
posts.key_insight: The Framework 13 with Linux has matured into a genuine MacBook Pro alternative for web developers, offering superior performance in Docker workloads while embodying the repairability and openness that Apple has abandoned.

posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals received over 2,200 applications for a junior programmer position, all reviewed by humans without AI screening. He pushes back against the narrative that AI has eliminated the need for junior developers, arguing that humans who understand code will always be necessary to vet AI-generated work. He also notes that landing a job isn't about meeting a checklist of criteria but about being the best candidate in the applicant pool.
key_points: 37signals received 2,200+ applications for one junior role, all reviewed by hand without AI screening
Getting a job isn't about meeting a fixed checklist — it's about being the best candidate relative to everyone else who applied
The narrative that the industry no longer needs junior programmers is wrong
Even as AI improves, humans who understand code will always be needed to vet machine-generated work
Great cover letters that don't read like AI slop are a baseline requirement
money_quotes: We received over 2,200 applications for our just-closed junior programmer opening, and now we're going through all of them by hand and by human. No AI screening here.
Yes, you have to present a baseline of relevant markers to even get into consideration, like a great cover letter that doesn't smell like AI slop.
To actually get the job, you have to be the best of the ones who've applied!
Almost every job opening is grading applicants on the curve of everyone who has applied. And the best candidate of the lot gets the job. You can't quantify what that looks like in advance.
It would be great to help counter the narrative that this industry no longer needs juniors.
However good AI gets, we're always going to need people who know the ins and outs of what the machine comes up with.
It's truly utopian thinking that mankind won't need people capable of vetting the work done by AI in five minutes.
posts.key_insight: No matter how capable AI becomes, the industry will always need junior programmers who can grow into people capable of understanding and vetting what the machines produce.

posts.summary: DHH argues against forcing Google to sell Chrome as an antitrust remedy, contending it would harm the open web more than help it. He maintains Chrome won the browser war through genuine technical merit, not anticompetitive behavior. Google's massive investment in Chrome is driven by economic self-interest—a thriving web means more search traffic and ad revenue—and that alignment of incentives is precisely why it works. He warns that without an 800-pound gorilla championing web standards and capabilities, proprietary platforms like iOS App Store would gain ground. While acknowledging Google's legitimate ad-market monopoly abuses deserve remedy, he argues Chrome divestiture is the wrong punishment.
key_points: Chrome won through technical merit and investment, not anticompetitive acquisition, and viable alternatives like Firefox, Safari, Brave, and Ladybird already exist
Google's self-interest in a thriving, searchable, ad-supported web is exactly why Chrome receives massive investment—capitalism runs on incentives, not benevolence
Proprietary platform owners like Apple would benefit from a weakened web, making a strong Chrome essential to keeping the open web competitive
Forcing a Chrome sale is a wealth-vs-riches mistake: Chrome's value comes from sustained investment, not a point-in-time snapshot, and would evaporate without it
Google should face consequences for ad-market monopoly abuse, but Chrome divestiture is the wrong remedy
money_quotes: Chrome won the browser war fair and square by building a better surfboard for the internet.
Google's incredible work to further the web isn't an act of charity, it's of economic self-interest, and that's why it works.
Capitalism doesn't run on benevolence, but incentives.
We want an 800-pound gorilla in the web's corner!
Microsoft fought the web tooth and nail back in the 90s because they knew that a free, open application platform would undermine lock-in — and it did!
A Chrome left to languish with half the investment will evaporate as quickly as a lottery winner's riches. Wealth requires maintenance to endure.
Google should not get away with rigging the online ad market, but forcing it to sell Chrome will do great damage to the web.
posts.key_insight: Google's economic self-interest in a thriving open web makes it the web's most effective champion, and severing that incentive through a forced Chrome sale would harm the open platform it sustains.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the appeal of 'vibe coding' with AI stems from programmers being stuck in unpleasant development environments, not from coding itself being inherently tedious. He contrasts this with Ruby's philosophy of 'programmer happiness,' which makes writing code genuinely enjoyable. He suggests AI-assisted coding is like stuffing mess under the bed — it hides bad developer experiences rather than fixing them. While he embraces AI as a pair programming tool, he has no interest in ceding the keyboard entirely, and sees the vibe coding trend as validating Ruby's founding insight that programming should favor humans.
key_points: The vibe coding movement is driven by developers trapped in unpleasant languages and frameworks, not by an inherent problem with coding itself
Ruby's 'programmer happiness' philosophy anticipated and already solves what AI coding promises — making development enjoyable
Using AI to hide bad code and ugly syntax is like cleaning by stuffing mess under the bed; the problems remain
AI works best as a pair programmer and collaborator, not as a replacement that turns developers into project managers
The core insight behind vibe coding's appeal is the same wisdom Ruby was founded on: programming should favor the human
money_quotes: If I found writing code a chore, I'd be looking for retirement too.
That's the appeal of AI: having it hide away all that unpleasantness. Only it's like cleaning your room by stuffing the mess under the bed — it doesn't make it go away!
Programming should be a vibe! It should be fun! It should resemble English closely enough that line noise doesn't obscure the underlying ideas and decisions.
I have no interest in giving up writing code. That's not the unpleasant part that I want AI to take off my hands. Just so I can — what? — become a project manager for a murder of AI crows?
AI is a superb pair programmer, but I'd retire before permanently handing it the keyboard to drive the code.
I've had the option to retreat up the manager ladder for most of my career, but I've steadily refused, because I really like writing Ruby! It's the most enjoyable part of the job!
A big part of their appeal to programmers is the wisdom that Ruby was founded on: Programming should favor and flatter the human.
posts.key_insight: The vibe coding movement validates what Ruby always knew — programming should be joyful — but AI hides bad developer experience rather than fixing it, making pleasant languages more relevant than ever.

posts.summary: DHH reviews the Minisforum UM870, a $550 mini PC that he finds surprisingly capable as a developer workstation. Running Linux, the tiny AMD-powered machine completes HEY's full test suite faster than a $2,199 M4 Pro Mac Mini, thanks largely to native Docker support. He argues that AMD's chips, manufactured on the same TSMC 5nm process as Apple's M2, deliver comparable single-core performance at a fraction of the cost. The post celebrates how small, cheap, and powerful developer machines have become, positioning Linux and AMD as compelling alternatives to Apple's ecosystem.
key_points: A $550 Minisforum UM870 with AMD 8745H beats a $2,199 M4 Pro Mac Mini on HEY's test suite due to native Docker/Linux advantages
AMD's Zen 4 cores, built on the same TSMC 5nm process as Apple's M2, match Apple's single-core Geekbench performance at a quarter the price
Mac's Docker performance suffers from filesystem virtualization overhead, giving Linux a significant real-world advantage for containerized development workflows
The upcoming AMD Ryzen AI Max 395+ in the Framework Desktop completes HEY tests more than twice as fast as the M4 Pro
Synthetic benchmarks like Geekbench can be misleading — real-world workload performance depends heavily on the specific software stack and OS
money_quotes: I'm shocked to report that I really don't need more than this!
Somehow I thought Apple had some special sauce that made this possible, and that PCs were forever condemned to be bigger, louder, and slower. How 2020 of me.
The UM870 is a little beast. It runs our full HEY test suite in just 2m28s. In comparison, it takes a 14-core M4 Pro 2m49s, and such an Apple costs $2,199.
At less than a quarter the cost, it's hard to complain.
I love that open-source Linux is able to run laps around Apple in the workloads that many developers need.
