I love that this tiny little silent $550 mini PC on my desk is capable of putting out computing power that only a decade ago would have been reserved to loud, honking metal in a data center.
The irony is that it was partly Apple's volume with and investment in TSMC that got us these incredible AMD chips.
posts.key_insight: Cheap AMD mini PCs running Linux now outperform Apple hardware costing four times as much for real-world developer workloads, thanks to native Docker support and TSMC manufacturing advances.

posts.summary: DHH advocates for regularly testing your assumptions by considering the exact opposite of what you believe or practice. He argues that helpful assumptions have a half-life and can become outdated as circumstances change. He illustrates this with 37signals' experiment of hiring full-time managers after nearly two decades without them, only to ultimately return to their managers-of-one culture. The core message is that intellectual humility requires genuinely believing you might be wrong.
key_points: Considering the opposite of your current approach is a powerful way to stress-test assumptions and values
Helpful assumptions have a half-life — what worked before may not work now, and it's easy to miss the shift
37signals hired full-time managers after 20 years without them as a deliberate experiment in trying the opposite
Testing the opposite requires genuine intellectual humility, not just performative open-mindedness
money_quotes: Have you thought about doing the opposite of whatever you're doing or considering?
Helpful assumptions have a half-life, just like facts.
It's ever so easy to miss the shift when circumstances change, if you're not regularly stress-testing your core beliefs.
That doesn't mean you're just a flag in the wind, blowing whichever way.
To try the opposite, you really have to believe that you might have been wrong.
Because you're wrong about something. I guarantee it. We all are.
posts.key_insight: Regularly challenging your deepest assumptions by genuinely trying the opposite is essential to avoiding stagnation, but it requires real intellectual humility — not just openness in theory.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates that HEY email can finally be purchased directly on the iPhone in the US, following a years-long battle with Apple over their 30% App Store commission and control of customer relationships. He credits Epic Games' $100M+ lawsuit and Judge Gonzalez Rogers' ruling for forcing Apple to allow developers to link to external payment systems. The post recounts the awkward workarounds HEY was forced to use, including bizarre burner accounts, after refusing Apple's in-app purchase demands. DHH frames this as a near-total victory while urging Apple to accept the ruling worldwide and return to competing on product quality rather than platform taxation.
key_points: Epic Games spent over $100M suing Apple, and Apple's possibly criminal contempt of court ultimately forced the resolution allowing developers to link to external payment systems
The fight was about more than the 30% cut — retaining the direct customer relationship for refunds and multi-platform billing was equally important to 37signals
Apple's App Store restrictions created countless customer-hostile experiences, including inability to buy Kindle books or sign up for Netflix subscriptions within apps
The ruling currently only applies to the US store, and DHH hopes Apple will voluntarily extend it worldwide or that European regulators will adopt a similar mechanism
DHH expresses hope that Apple will pivot back to competing on product quality rather than relying on platform lock-in and developer taxation
money_quotes: It was the kind of test that calls you to account for what you believe and asks what you're willing to risk to see it through.
The 30% toll gets all the attention, and it is ludicrously egregious, but to us, it's just as much about retaining that direct customer relationship.
Apple's obsession with squeezing every last dollar from developers has produced countless customer-hostile experiences on the iPhone.
Thanks to Apple's gag orders, developers were not even allowed to explain the confusing situation.

We've always been happy to pay Apple for hosting our application on the App Store. But being forced to hand over 30% of the business, as well as the direct customer relationship, was always an unacceptable overreach.
I really want Apple to return to being a shining city on the hill. To show that you can win in the market merely by making better products.
They don't need these gangster extortion techniques to make the numbers that Cook has promised Wall Street.
They've lost their way and moral compass over the last half decade or so, but that's only one leadership pivot away from being found again.
posts.key_insight: Apple's App Store monopoly was broken not by regulation or market forces but by one company willing to spend $100M on litigation and a judge willing to hold Apple in contempt — and the result is a fairer marketplace where developers can maintain direct customer relationships.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his weekend diving into Arch Linux + Hyprland, a notoriously difficult but highly customizable Linux setup. Building on his earlier Omakub project for Ubuntu, he was drawn by the hype around Hyprland's tiling window manager and its aesthetic focus. Despite the steep setup cost—requiring manual configuration of every component from login screens to notification systems—he found the experience intoxicating. He announces Omarchy, a new project to package this setup so others can enjoy Arch + Hyprland without investing 10-20 hours of configuration.
key_points: Arch + Hyprland is 'Linux on hard mode' but tools like archinstall have reduced the initial installation barrier
Arch's AUR package system solves many of the manual scripting workarounds needed on Ubuntu, making developer tool installation much easier
Hyprland is a tiling window manager with rare aesthetic sensibility in the Linux world, but ships with absolutely nothing preconfigured
DHH frames inspiration as something to act on immediately rather than question, using it as the justification for weekend deep-dives
Omarchy will be a preconfigured Arch + Hyprland distribution, following the same philosophy as Omakub but for power users
money_quotes: Over the years, I've learned not to question inspiration. To simply let it drive when it shows up with a full tank.
While Ubuntu is all about being a friendly place for newcomers to Linux, Arch + Hyprland is the exact opposite: It's Linux on hard mode!
The Arch ISO literally just dumps you into a terminal with scarcely any direction.
It looks like it was written by visual artists rather than neckbeards.

Hyprland comes with absolutely nothing out of the box. No login screen, no menu bar, no notification system, no file manager, no visual settings application. Just a text-based configuration file, a wiki, and an outline on a map for how to design your own adventure.
The core window tiling magic of Hyprland is one of the most intoxicating ways of using a computer that I've ever experienced.
I'm not going through all this work to set up a beautiful, preconfigured, fully-functional, out-of-the-box Arch + Hyprland combo and then not share it with anyone who's curious.
posts.key_insight: The extreme customizability of Arch + Hyprland—where every detail requires a deliberate choice—is both its greatest barrier and its greatest appeal, and packaging that experience for others is worth the effort.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Denmark and Europe must urgently pursue digital sovereignty by replacing American Big Tech infrastructure, particularly Microsoft products. He points to the US administration's disconnection of the ICC's chief prosecutor as proof that American sanctioning power poses a real threat to European digital infrastructure. Copenhagen and Aarhus counties have announced plans to eliminate Microsoft products for their combined workforce, which DHH sees as a promising first step. He dismisses defeatist bureaucrats who claim replacing Microsoft is impossible, comparing digital sovereignty to other difficult but achievable goals like energy independence and military rebuilding.
key_points: The US sanctioning of the ICC's chief prosecutor proved that American administrations can and will cut off access to digital infrastructure, undermining Microsoft's assurances to Europe
Denmark faces uniquely heightened risk due to US-Denmark tensions over Greenland, including increased American spying on Denmark
Copenhagen and Aarhus counties are moving to eliminate Microsoft products for over 40,000 employees, marking a concrete step toward digital sovereignty
Digital sovereignty means rejecting services that can be turned off from Washington, not cutting off all American tech
Bureaucratic defeatism is the biggest obstacle, with IT leaders declaring alternatives 'utopian' while simultaneously admitting the problem could have been solved if started earlier
money_quotes: Microsoft's assurances met reality and reality won.
All American administrations have the power to disconnect any individual, company, or foreign government from digital infrastructure provided by American Big Tech.
We certainly can blame them for gaslighting Europe about the risk.
Digital sovereignty doesn't mean cutting off all American tech, but it does mean rejecting any services that can be turned off from Washington.
Denmark is full of unambitious, defeatist bureaucrats who can't imagine a world without Microsoft.
Whatever has always done too often seems like the only thing that ever could be done.
Digital sovereignty isn't easy, but neither was securing a sovereign energy supply.
The bureaucrats who aren't interested in making it happen should find employment elsewhere.
posts.key_insight: The ICC sanctions proved that European dependence on American Big Tech is a genuine geopolitical vulnerability, not a theoretical one, and Denmark's moves to drop Microsoft show that digital sovereignty is achievable if bureaucratic defeatism is overcome.

posts.summary: DHH criticizes Copenhagen International School for implementing gender identity and pride-related programming in its primary school curriculum. He describes activities including trans-themed drawing exercises for young children and Gender and Sexuality Alliances for students as young as eight, which he characterizes as indoctrination. After parent pushback, the school progressively scaled back the programming. DHH argues schools should remain politically neutral and focus on education rather than advancing any specific organization's agenda, drawing parallels to Copenhagen Pride losing corporate sponsors over partisan political stances.
key_points: CIS planned a week of morning meetings focused on gender dysphoria, transgenderism, and pride activities for primary school students including kindergarteners
Parent complaints led to progressive rollbacks: removing kindergarten/first grade, pulling specific activities, and reducing the program to a single session
The school runs Gender and Sexuality Alliances for children as young as eight, facilitated by staff despite being billed as student-led
DHH argues schools should maintain political neutrality and not serve as subsidiaries of interest organizations, comparing this to expectations around climate change and Gaza
He draws a parallel to Copenhagen Pride losing major sponsors like Novo Nordisk and Maersk after taking partisan political positions
money_quotes: We thought Copenhagen would offer a respite from the woke nonsense that's been plaguing California — where some schools in our social circle ended up with a quarter or more of the student body identifying as trans or gender nonconformative — but it seems ideological contagions travel as fast as airplanes these days.

There's simply no responsible explanation for having kids as young as eight, or even as old as 11, in lunch and recess meetings with CIS staff to discuss gender and sexuality on school campus. It's preposterous, if not outright creepy.
CIS, like any other school, should not be a subsidiary of any specific interest organization.
We don't want our kids to get their information about climate change from either Extinction Rebellion or fossil-fuel lobbyists.

The commitment to an inclusive school does not offer a license to indulge in this kind of overt indoctrination or inappropriate lunch meetings where minors discuss gender and sexuality with school staff.
It's bizarre that those same companies now have the children of their employees programmed by this organization's agenda at school.
posts.key_insight: Schools should maintain political neutrality and focus on education rather than serving as vehicles for any interest group's ideological programming, especially when targeting young children.

posts.summary: DHH announces Omarchy, his opinionated setup of Arch Linux with the Hyprland tiling window manager, designed to provide a fully configured developer environment out of the box. He positions it as a sister project to Omakub (his Ubuntu setup), acknowledging that Arch and Hyprland have steeper learning curves. The project aims to solve Hyprland's bare-bones default experience by providing beautiful configurations and common tooling pre-installed. DHH argues that Linux desktop adoption is closer than ever, driven by growing dissatisfaction with Apple and Microsoft, Valve's Steam Deck running Arch, and major creators switching to Linux.
key_points: Omarchy is an opinionated Arch Linux + Hyprland setup that provides DHH's exact daily developer environment out of the box
Hyprland's difficulty comes not from the core tiling manager but from its extremely bare-bones defaults requiring manual configuration of basic features like lock screens and bluetooth
The project serves dual purposes: a ready-to-use environment and a paved path into Linux ricing and customization
DHH believes Linux desktop adoption is approaching critical mass due to dissatisfaction with major OS vendors and growing ecosystem support from Valve and content creators
money_quotes: My Platonic ideal of what a developer environment should look like.
I do think that Hyprland deserves its reputation of being difficult! Not because the core tiling window manager is hard, but because it comes incredibly bare-boned in the box.
The flip side of Hyprland being so atomized is that it's infinitely configurable.
I don't know when we'll literally get "The Year of Linux on the Desktop", but I've never been as convinced that its coming as I am now.
There's enough dissent in the water. Enough dissatisfaction with both Apple and Microsoft.
incredible projects like Hyprland — which offer not just a cheap visual copy of the two major commercial operating systems, but something much more unique and compelling
I'm in love with Linux, and thrilled to share my work to make it easier to enjoy.
posts.key_insight: Linux is finally offering something genuinely unique rather than imitating commercial operating systems, and opinionated pre-configured setups like Omarchy can bridge the gap between Linux's infinite customizability and practical usability.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his experience riding in a Tesla with Full Self-Driving (FSD) and declares that self-driving technology has finally arrived after years of broken promises. He recalls the hype of 2017 when the auto industry predicted imminent autonomy, followed by years of disappointment. After being skeptical even recently, a ride with Jason Fried convinced him that Tesla's FSD has made dramatic improvements in fluidity and reliability. He credits the achievement to AI advancement and Elon Musk's persistence, calling the transition from vaporware to credible reality remarkable.
key_points: The auto industry overpromised self-driving in 2017, creating justified long-term skepticism even as the technology improved
Tesla's FSD has made dramatic improvements in driving fluidity and smoothness in just nine months
DHH experienced a full 30-minute drive each way with zero driver interventions on pedals or steering wheel
The achievement was accomplished with camera-based AI alone, without LiDAR technology
money_quotes: If you promise them the moon in eight months, but don't end up delivering until eight years later, most will justifiably be skeptical that it's actually here — even in the face of gushing anecdotes and video evidence.
That's the problem with delayed promises.
How much could it really have improved in nine months or so? A lot, it turns out.
His new Model Y anticipated the red light with the manners of a drives-for-the-queen-level chauffeur.
The self-driving dream has flipped from vaporware to credible near-term reality.
Elon, that son of a bitch, seems to have done it again! Proven everyone wrong. Proven me wrong.
posts.key_insight: Self-driving technology has crossed the threshold from overhyped vaporware to genuinely impressive reality, proving that delayed promises can still eventually deliver even after exhausting public trust.

posts.summary: DHH argues that 'consent morality' — the idea that anything consenting adults choose is beyond criticism — has become a cultural dead end, particularly regarding parenthood. He contends that declining birth rates are partly caused by a culture that refuses to advocate for parenthood as an objective good, instead treating any lifestyle choice as equally valid. Drawing on Louise Perry's critique of the sexual revolution and Jordan Peterson's advocacy for embracing responsibility, he argues that modern malaise comes from carrying too little burden, not too much. He calls for culture to actively celebrate parenthood and family life rather than centering edge cases and pathologies, arguing this requires moving beyond consent morality to assert that some ways of living are genuinely better than others.
key_points: Consent morality — the refusal to judge any choice made by consenting adults — prevents culture from guiding people toward meaningful lives
The birthrate crisis is partly a cultural failure: society has systematically undermined the appeal of parenthood by focusing on its costs and burdens
Modern unhappiness stems not from too much responsibility but from too little — personal freedom without obligation becomes spiritual emptiness
A well-functioning culture should guide (not force) people toward higher values, including the value of raising children
Reembracing parenthood requires the moral courage to assert that some life paths are better than others for most people
money_quotes: It's the role of a well-functioning culture to guide people towards The Good Life. Not force, but guide.
Giving up on the whole idea of objective higher values and virtues is a nihilistic and cowardly alternative.
You think you want the paradise of nothing ever being asked of you, but it turns out to be the hell of nobody ever needing you.
Much of our modern malaise does not come from carrying too much, but from carrying too little.
A myopic focus on personal freedom — the nights out, the 'me time', the money saved — is a spiritual mirage.
The worst thing in the world is not to have others ask more of you.

Giving up on the total freedom of the unmoored life is a small price to pay for finding the deeper meaning in a tethered relationship with continuing a bloodline that's been drawn for hundreds of thousands of years before it came to you.
You're never going to be 'ready' before you take the leap. If you keep waiting, you'll wait until the window has closed, and all you see is regret.
posts.key_insight: A culture that treats all lifestyle choices as equally valid cannot guide people toward the deeper meaning found in parenthood and responsibility, contributing to both demographic decline and widespread spiritual emptiness.

posts.summary: DHH observes a notable vibe shift among developers toward Linux desktop adoption, driven by better hardware options like mini PCs, Apple's deteriorating developer relations, and cultural influencers like PewDiePie and ThePrimeagen embracing Arch Linux. He highlights Arch Linux and Hyprland as the surprising center of this movement, despite Arch being around since 2003. While acknowledging Linux remains a niche desktop OS by market share, he argues the mind share is shifting rapidly away from Apple and Microsoft. He positions his own Omarchy project as part of this wave and sees conditions ripe for a 'gradually, then suddenly' adoption shift.
key_points: Multiple converging factors — better Linux hardware, Apple missteps, and cultural influencers — are driving unprecedented developer curiosity about Linux
Arch Linux, a notoriously difficult distro from 2003, is paradoxically at the center of the new wave thanks to Hyprland, Steam Deck, and prominent YouTubers
The 'Year of Linux on the Desktop' joke from the 90s may finally be materializing alongside other long-promised tech like self-driving cars
Linux is winning mind share even while Apple and Microsoft still dominate market share, creating conditions for rapid future adoption
money_quotes: I've never seen so many developers curious about leaving the Mac and giving Linux a go.

People have been joking about 'The Year of Linux on the Desktop' since the late 90s. Just like self-driving cars were supposed to be a thing back in 2017. And now, in the year of our Lord 2025, it seems like we're getting both!
The perfect, free, and open alternative that was just sitting around waiting for developers to finally have had enough of the commercial offerings from Apple and Microsoft.
Apple and Microsoft sit on the lion's share of the market share. But the mind share? They've been losing that fast.
The window is open for a major shift to happen. First gradually, then suddenly. It feels like morning in Linux land!
posts.key_insight: A convergence of better hardware, Apple missteps, and cultural influencers has created the conditions for Linux desktop adoption to finally shift from perennial joke to genuine developer movement.

posts.summary: DHH recounts his six-hour marathon conversation on the Lex Fridman podcast, comparing the endurance required to his stints driving at the 24 Hours of Le Mans. He describes how the conversation flew by thanks to a flow state, covering topics from Ruby's beauty to AI, small teams, leaving the cloud, Elon Musk, fatherhood, and money. He praises Fridman's exceptional preparation and deep questioning style. The post serves as both a personal reflection on great conversation and a promotion of the episode.
key_points: DHH sat for over six hours straight with only three bathroom breaks, comparing it to his Le Mans driving stints
He credits flow state for making the six hours feel like sixty minutes
Lex Fridman's preparation stood out as the most thorough of any podcast DHH has appeared on
Topics ranged widely: Ruby, AI, small teams, cloud exit, Elon Musk, fatherhood, money and happiness
money_quotes: Talking with Lex Fridman in Austin on his podcast? Over six hours straight!
The magic of a good conversation, like the magic of driving at Le Mans, is that time flies by.
Those six hours felt more like sixty minutes. This is what flow does: it compresses the moment.
Lex prepares like no other podcast I've ever been on. Pages and pages of notes. Deep questions, endless attention for tangents.
We covered the beauty of Ruby for half an hour alone!
posts.key_insight: A great conversation, like peak performance in any domain, induces a flow state that compresses hours into what feels like minutes.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Linux reaching 5% desktop market share in the US, double what it was three years ago. He notes Windows still dominates at 63% with Apple at 26%, down from a 33% peak in 2023. He contrasts the US trend with Denmark, where Linux sits under 2% and Windows commands nearly 70%. DHH connects Denmark's slow adoption to the same cultural conservatism that drove him to move to the US in 2005 when Rails was taking off.
key_points: Linux desktop market share in the US hit 5%, doubling in three years according to Statcounter
Apple's US desktop share has dropped from a 33% peak in June 2023 to 26%
Denmark lags significantly with Linux under 2% and Windows at nearly 70%, reflecting cultural conservatism
DHH draws a parallel between Denmark's slow Linux adoption and its earlier reluctance to embrace Ruby on Rails
money_quotes: Linux has claimed 5% market share of desktop computing in the US. That's double of where it was just three years ago! Really impressive.
Danish companies were too conservative, too complacent, too married to Microsoft to really pay attention.
It's hard to change culture!
So while the Danes continue to fiddle, the Americans continue to push forward. Linux is on the up and up!
posts.key_insight: Linux's doubling to 5% US desktop market share signals meaningful momentum for open-source adoption, while culturally conservative markets like Denmark continue to lag behind.

posts.summary: DHH argues that America's unique culture of hype is a genuine competitive advantage, contrasting it with Europe's instinctive skepticism toward commercial optimism. He acknowledges his complicated relationship with venture capital, having built his career proving bootstrapping works, but concedes that VC excess is integral to America's hype engine. Using the dot-com boom as precedent, he suggests that AI's current hype cycle, despite its absurdities, will likely produce transformative outcomes. The core argument is that collective optimism, even when irrational, creates the conditions for real progress.
key_points: America's hype culture is a genuine structural advantage, not a flaw — it creates the critical mass needed to shift public vision toward the future
Europe's cultural allergy to commercial optimism prevents it from capitalizing on its own talent and inventiveness
Venture capital excess, despite producing spectacular failures like Pets.com, builds infrastructure that later enables real transformation
The current AI hype cycle mirrors the dot-com era and will likely produce both spectacular busts and genuine breakthroughs
Optimism in the face of unknown potential is a rational bet, even when individual claims are absurd
money_quotes: There's no country on earth that does hype better than America.
Most Europeans are allergic to anything that even smells like a commercial promise of a better tomorrow.

Europeans are just as smart, just as inventive as their American brethren, but they don't do hype, so they're rarely the ones able to sell the sizzle that public opinion requires to shift its vision for tomorrow.
The lavish overspending during the dot-com boom led directly to a spectacular bust, but it also built the foundation of the internet we all enjoy today.
Pets.com and Webvan flamed out such that Amazon and Shopify could transform ecommerce out of the ashes.
You don't have to throw away your critical reasoning to accept that in the face of unknown potential, optimism beats pessimism.
Americans fundamentally believe this. They believe the hype, so they make it come to fruition.
posts.key_insight: America's culture of irrational optimism and venture capital excess, while producing spectacular failures, is the engine that turns hype into real transformation — and skeptical Europe pays the price for its prudence.

posts.summary: DHH enthusiastically recommends Beelink mini PCs as the ideal hardware for running Linux, particularly his Omarchy distribution. He profiles three models at $289, $499, and $929 price points, highlighting their AMD processors that rival or exceed Apple M-chip performance. He praises Beelink's build quality, silent operation, and Linux compatibility compared to competitors. The post notes that his advocacy on X has driven significant sales as programmers explore Linux on affordable hardware.
key_points: Beelink stands out among mini PC competitors for build quality, cooling, silent operation, and full Linux compatibility
The $499 SER8 hits a price-to-performance sweet spot with AMD Zen4 delivering M4-class multi-core performance
The $929 SER9 with AMD Zen5 HX370 runs the HEY test suite faster than any Apple M chip DHH has tested
All Beelink models draw under 10 watts idle and under 100 watts peak, making them remarkably efficient
DHH's advocacy has created a symbiotic relationship between Beelink hardware and the growing Linux-curious programmer community
money_quotes: Beelink stands out with their superior build quality, their class-leading cooling and silent operation, and their use of fully Linux-compatible components.
That's the price-to-performance sweet spot in the range.
It uses the incredible 12-core AMD Zen5 HX370 that hits 2990/15611 in Geekbench, and runs our HEY test suite faster than any Apple M chip I've ever tested.
It's frankly incredible that we have this kind of performance and efficiency available at these prices with all of these Beelinks drawing less than 10 watt at idle and no more than 100 watt at peak!
There are a ton of programmers who have become Linux curious, and Beelink offers no-brainer options to give that a try at a bargain.
The perfect intersection of hardware, software, and timing.
posts.key_insight: Beelink mini PCs offer Apple M-chip-rivaling performance at a fraction of the price, making them the ideal on-ramp for programmers exploring Linux.

posts.summary: DHH argues that people shouldn't be disappointed when public companies shift their marketing and values with cultural trends, because that's exactly how capitalism is supposed to work. He contrasts the owner-operator model (where he can afford unpopular principles) with professional managers who answer to shareholders and must follow demand. Using Nike and American Eagle's dramatic pivot from early-2020s progressive messaging to 2025's traditional imagery, he illustrates how corporations are weathervanes, not moral actors. He concludes that the 'woke theocracy' temporarily captured culture and thus capitalism, but couldn't hold it, and commerce has now reverted to giving people what they actually want.
key_points: Public companies run by professional managers have no deep principles — they follow demand, and that's a feature, not a bug
Owner-operators like DHH can afford unpopular principles because they play with their own money; professional CEOs cannot
Nike and American Eagle's dramatic marketing reversals from progressive to traditional imagery prove corporations are cultural weathervanes
The 'woke' cultural movement succeeded by capturing culture to capture capitalism, but couldn't sustain its hold
The free market functions as an information system where demand directs production, making corporate principle-lessness a self-correcting mechanism
money_quotes: It's in everyone's interest that corporations set their sails to the wind and whims of the prevailing culture.

As an owner-operator, I can afford to have unpopular principles from time to time. I can afford to earn less than the theoretical maximum from the market as a consequence of these principles. That's because I'm playing with my own money.

Shareholders, in their Platonic ideal, only care about maximizing profits. That can occasionally be an incentive for amoral behavior, but more often, it's simply a driver towards making stuff that people want.
Selling 2020 woke platitudes into a 2025 market is a clear way to lose your job as a professional manager.
Blond babies and boobs are back in business!
If you can capture culture, you can capture capitalism. But it's one thing to capture culture, it's quite another to hold it.
By course-correcting the culture, we've course-corrected commerce. No corporate principles required.
posts.key_insight: Corporate principle-lessness is actually capitalism working as intended — corporations follow demand, so changing the culture automatically changes commerce without requiring companies to have principles at all.

posts.summary: DHH argues that building competency through hobbies and educational pursuits is a more effective treatment for mild depression than talk therapy, especially for men. He cites studies showing exercise outperforms therapy and pharmaceuticals for garden-variety depression, then extends the argument to competency-building activities like learning Linux, history, or motorcycle repair. Drawing on Csikszentmihalyi's concept of flow, he contends that stretching your abilities through challenging pursuits produces genuine happiness. He emphasizes that these pursuits also build community through topic-based connections, and holds up open source as the ideal combination of mastery, community, and meaningful purpose.
key_points: Studies show exercise is more effective than talk therapy or pharmaceuticals for mild depression, suggesting therapy is overprescribed
Pursuing competency in challenging domains produces flow states and endorphins that counter depression
Topic-based communities formed around shared learning interests provide meaningful human connection that combats isolation
Open source perfectly combines mastery, community, and purpose — the three key antidepressants DHH identifies
Talk therapy should be reserved for severe pathological cases rather than treated as the default for everyone
money_quotes: Most people just don't need therapy, they need purpose, they need competence, they need exercise, and they need community.
Peak human happiness arrives exactly in these moments of flow when your competence is stretched by a difficult-but-doable challenge.
At least for men who'd prefer to feel useful over being listened to, which, in my experience, is most of them.
You're not just learning, you're learning with others.
Leave it available for the severe, pathological cases that exercise and the pursuit of competence can't cure.
People are starved for the kind of topic-based connections that, say, learning about Linux offers in spades.
There are endless paths of discovery and mastery available. There are tons of fellow travelers with whom to connect and collaborate.
posts.key_insight: Mild depression is better treated through building competency, finding community, and pursuing purpose than through talk therapy — and open source offers all three.

posts.summary: DHH argues that ideals exist to inspire humanity toward excellence and that the cultural movement to dismantle beauty and achievement standards is misguided. He traces a line from 90s grunge and loser culture through Naomi Wolf's critique of beauty standards to modern body-positive advertising campaigns like Calvin Klein's. Using a Copenhagen billboard featuring an obese model as his central example, he argues that 'representation' culture patronizingly validates mediocrity rather than inspiring improvement. He concludes that the human yearning for excellence is reasserting itself after decades of postmodern deconstruction.
key_points: Ideals are meant to be aspirational and unattainable for most — that's their purpose and value
The 1990s cultural movement toward celebrating mediocrity (grunge, critical theory on beauty) was a wrong turn
The 'representation' narrative is patronizing because it validates people as they are rather than inspiring them to improve
Body-positive advertising campaigns like Calvin Klein's are the logical endpoint of deconstructing beauty ideals
The intrinsic human desire for excellence cannot be permanently suppressed by postmodern theory
money_quotes: Ideals are supposed to be unattainable for the great many. If everyone could be the smartest, strongest, prettiest, or best, there would be no need for ideals — we'd all just be perfect.
It was a decade that made it cool to be a loser.
The last thing we need is a patronizing message that however little you try, you're perfect just the way you are.
The beauty of ideals is that they ask more of us.
It proposes we're all better off if all we see is a mirror of ourselves, however obese, lazy, ignorant, or incompetent, because at least it won't be 'unrealistic'. Screw that.
No amount of post-modern deconstruction or academic theory babble seems capable of suppressing the intrinsic human yearning for excellence forever.
posts.key_insight: Ideals exist to inspire people toward excellence, and cultural movements that replace aspiration with mere representation are patronizing and ultimately futile against humanity's intrinsic drive to admire and pursue the best.

posts.summary: DHH argues that executives with packed calendars have made a poor trade, sacrificing availability for the illusion of efficiency. He points to old-school executives who kept open schedules, ready to act when critical moments arose. Modern executives, he contends, are insecure about appearing busy and damage their businesses by grinding constantly. He uses the Mad Men character Bert Cooper and even Elon Musk as examples of executives who maintain availability despite their responsibilities. The core message is that open calendars signal intentional readiness, not laziness.
key_points: Back-to-back executive calendars eliminate room for fires, customers, and unexpected opportunities
Traditional executives understood that availability and network access were their primary value
Modern executive busyness is driven by insecurity rather than effectiveness
Even Elon Musk, despite 100-hour weeks across multiple companies, likely spends only 20-30 hours per company
Open calendars should be reframed as intentional availability, not laziness
money_quotes: If your executive calendar is packed back to back, you have no room for fires, customers, or serendipities.
You've traded all your availability for efficiency. That's a bad deal.
But modern executives are so insecure about seeming busy 24/7 that they'll wreck their business trying to prove it.
Sacrificing themselves thin so they can run a squirrel-brain operation that's constantly chasing every nutty idea.
Wide open calendars should not be seen as lazy, but as intentional availability.
posts.key_insight: Executive value lies in availability for critical moments, not in performative busyness that crowds out the capacity to respond when it matters most.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates the rapid progress of Omarchy, his Arch Linux + Hyprland distribution, since its launch six weeks prior. The project has seen 18 releases, 3,500 Discord members, and 250 pull requests from a growing community. The latest release introduces a keyboard-driven control menu that embodies his vision for mouse-free computing. He argues that Linux is having a moment, with Hyprland making it viable for people who never considered leaving Windows or macOS.
key_points: Omarchy has shipped 18 releases in roughly six weeks with significant community contribution
The new unified keyboard-driven control menu represents DHH's ideal of hands-off-the-mouse computing
Hyprland has revitalized Arch Linux for a new audience who previously dismissed desktop Linux
Linux remains an adventure not suited for everyone, but growing disillusionment with Microsoft and Apple is expanding its potential audience
Omarchy targets computer enthusiasts who want an alternative but need a lower barrier to entry
money_quotes: We have about 3,500 early adopters on the Omarchy Discord, 250 pull requests processed, and one heck of an awesome Arch + Hyprland Linux environment to show for it!
It's exactly the kind of hands-off-the-mouse operation that I've always wanted, and with Linux, I've been able to build it just to my tastes.
Folks who'd never thought that open source could be able to deliver a desktop experience worth giving up Windows or macOS for.
It's still an adventure! An awesome, teach-you-about-computers adventure, but not everyone is into computer adventures.
Plenty of people are content with a computer appliance where they never have to look under the hood.
There are a growing number of computer enthusiasts who've grown very disillusioned with both Microsoft and Apple.
posts.key_insight: Growing disillusionment with Microsoft and Apple is creating a window for polished Linux distributions like Omarchy to capture enthusiasts who previously saw no viable alternative.

posts.summary: DHH reviews the Framework Desktop, a tiny 4.5L silent PC powered by AMD's Ryzen AI Max 395+ chip. He finds it crushes multi-core workloads like running the HEY test suite, beating both competing mini PCs and Apple's M4 Max by significant margins. While acknowledging Apple's single-core advantage, he argues multi-core performance is what developers actually feel in daily work. At roughly half the price of a comparable Mac Studio, he considers it an exceptional value for Linux developers, though he notes the cheaper Beelink SER9 remains a solid alternative for those less sensitive to multi-core speed.
key_points: The Framework Desktop with AMD 395+ is nearly twice as fast as the Beelink SER8 and 40-50% faster than M4 Max/Pro in multi-core developer workloads
At $1,876 for 64GB/2TB, it costs nearly half the $3,299 Mac Studio with similar specs while outperforming it for Docker-based development
Single-core performance differences between modern machines are imperceptible in daily use — multi-core workloads are where you actually feel the wait
The completely silent operation is a dealbreaker feature that separates it from cheaper alternatives like GMKTec
Local LLM capability with 128GB unified memory is impressive but frontier SaaS models still far outperform what runs locally
money_quotes: It's completely quiet, even under heavy, stress-all-cores load.
I can't listen to roaring fans anymore.
Docker is an integral part of the workflow for tons of developers. So this is what Real World benchmarks reveal.
I've found that all these computers feel fast enough in single-core performance these days.
The only time I actually feel like I'm waiting on anything is in multi-core workloads like the HEY test suite.
To get a Mac Studio with similar specs you'll literally spend nearly twice as much at $3,299!
I want the best models running at full speed, and right now that means SaaS.
What a great time to love open source software and tinker-friendly hardware!
posts.key_insight: The Framework Desktop delivers superior multi-core developer performance at half the price of a Mac Studio, making Linux on tinker-friendly hardware the best value proposition for developers today.

posts.summary: DHH praises YouTube as an invaluable internet archive, despite his frequent criticism of Google for killing products. He notes that when Google commits to something strategically important, the results are exceptional. After reviving his own YouTube channel, he was struck by how the platform has preserved video content spanning nearly two decades, including his own Rails demos and conference talks from 2006. He argues that people in small tech shouldn't let anti-big-tech ideology blind them to genuine contributions like YouTube.
key_points: YouTube stands as a rare example of Google successfully committing to a product long-term, contrasting with their habit of killing services
The platform serves as an irreplaceable internet archive, preserving video content from nearly two decades ago that exists nowhere else
Decentralized content is subject to link rot and disappearing content, giving centralized platforms like YouTube an archival advantage
People in small tech should be able to praise and criticize big tech selectively without feeling hypocritical
YouTube has become a primary learning platform, from programming to hardware reviews to niche hobbies
money_quotes: The company seems institutionally incapable of standing behind the majority of the products they launch for longer than a KPI cycle.
YouTube: The King of Internet Archives (Video Edition).
Decentralization is wonderful in many ways, but it's very much subject to link rot and disappearing content.
When you're working in small tech, it's really easy to become so jaded with big tech that you become ideologically blind to the benefits they do bring.
Any company as large as Google is bound to be full of contradictions, ambitions, and behaviors.
We ought to have the capacity to cheer for the good parts and boo at the bad parts without feeling like frauds.
It's an international treasure of learning, enthusiasm, and discovery.
posts.key_insight: Intellectual honesty requires the capacity to criticize and praise the same company for different things, and YouTube is a case where Google genuinely earned the praise as an irreplaceable archive of internet culture and learning.

posts.summary: DHH argues that American culture is finally shaking off the ironic, cynical, deconstructive mood of the 1990s and returning to the earnest, optimistic spirit of the 1980s. He contrasts 90s postmodern irony and moral relativism with 80s sincerity, using examples from Rocky IV, Weird Science, and pop music. He sees current cultural moments—Sydney Sweeney's unapologetic ad campaigns, the F1 movie's sincere storytelling—as evidence of a new era of confidence and levity. Citing David Foster Wallace's critique of postmodernism, DHH contends that culture has finally embraced Wallace's conclusions decades later. He calls for earnest values, grand narratives, and gratitude over the reflexive deconstruction and pessimism he associates with the post-90s era.
key_points: The 1990s cultural dominance of irony, cynicism, and postmodern deconstruction was a creative and moral dead end
1980s American pop culture succeeded through earnest, sincere storytelling with clear moral frameworks—Rocky IV, Weird Science, and pop music embodied this
Current cultural moments like Sydney Sweeney's campaigns and the F1 movie signal a return to unapologetic sincerity and optimism
David Foster Wallace diagnosed the exhaustion of postmodern irony in 1997, but it took until the mid-2020s for culture to act on his conclusions
Culture needs grand narratives, moral confidence, and fun rather than reflexive deconstruction of everything as problematic
money_quotes: It feels like we're finally emerging from this constant 90s Seattle drizzle to sunny 80s LA vibes in America.
The constant pessimism, the cancellation militias, and the walking-on-eggshells atmosphere have given way to something far brighter, bolder, and, yes, better.
We need earnest values and virtues. We need sincere stories that are not afraid of grand narratives.
We need to throw away these shit-tinted glasses that see everything in the world as a problematic example of some injustice or oppression.
Not a shred of irony. Just pure 'if you work real hard, you can do the right thing' energy.
Wallace had the diagnosis nailed back in 1997. But it's taken us until these mid-2020s to fully embrace its conclusions.
As much as I love the croissants of the Old World, I find myself craving that uniquely American brand of optimism, enthusiasm, and determination more whenever I've been back in Europe for too long.
posts.key_insight: American culture is finally recovering from decades of postmodern irony and rediscovering the earnest optimism and moral confidence that made 1980s storytelling magnetic.

posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals is switching its Ops and Ruby programming teams entirely to Omarchy, their custom Arch-derived Linux distribution, over the next three years. The move also means replacing MacBooks with Framework laptops and desktops. He argues that Linux offers superior control over your computing destiny compared to closed-source commercial operating systems. While acknowledging trade-offs like shorter battery life, he points to significant productivity gains from running Rails test suites much faster on Linux with native Docker support. He draws parallels to the early days of Ruby adoption, framing Omarchy as another contrarian bet that feels special before it goes mainstream.
key_points: 37signals will transition its Ops and Ruby teams from Macs to Omarchy (Arch Linux) and Framework hardware over three years
Nobody questions mandating Macs or Windows, but mandating Linux is treated as surprising — DHH sees this double standard as illogical
AMD processors and PC hardware have closed much of the gap with Apple Silicon, especially on performance if not yet on power efficiency
Rails test suites run almost twice as fast on a Framework Desktop with Linux compared to the fastest Mac, thanks to native Docker support
DHH compares the excitement of Omarchy to early Ruby and early OSX — a feeling of discovering something special before widespread adoption
money_quotes: It's funny how nobody bats an eye when the company mandate is to use Macs or Windows, but when the prescription is Linux, it's suddenly surprising.
Your ability to control your own destiny with Linux is far superior to what you'll get from a closed-source, commercial operating system. Of course it is! The code is literally all there!
I started using Ruby to build Basecamp when we could barely fill a room in American with professional Ruby programmers. This is what we do here!
A small concession on a rare occasion, but nothing like the performance AND battery deficit we willingly endured for decades on the Mac before Apple switched to their own chips.
Even the fastest Mac, an M4 Max, is almost twice as slow as a Framework Desktop machine running Linux, which can do Docker natively.
Omarchy is already by far-and-away my favorite computing environment. Right up there in joy and wonder with the old Amiga days or early OSX.
Very reminiscent of the excitement in the early Ruby days. Knowing you'd found something super special that wasn't yet widely distributed (but poised to be).
posts.key_insight: DHH is betting that Linux on open hardware can replace Macs for professional Rails development, trading minor battery life concessions for vastly superior performance, openness, and control.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the exhilarating feeling of working on Omarchy, his Linux project, comparing it to the concept of seizing a chance from Kobi Yamada's children's book. He describes the current moment as feeling like Linux itself fluttered into his hands with an invitation to do something extraordinary. Drawing parallels to the 'Quickening' scene from Highlander, he conveys the raw creative energy and excitement that drives him to the keyboard each morning. The post is a short, personal meditation on recognizing and chasing creative momentum when it arrives.
key_points: DHH uses Kobi Yamada's children's book 'What Do You Do With a Chance?' as a framework for understanding creative opportunity
His Linux project Omarchy represents a galvanizing creative moment that he compares to a chance 'fluttering' toward him
He likens the feeling to 'The Quickening' from Highlander — sensing a profound opportunity and being compelled to seize it
The post captures DHH's philosophy that the best work comes from recognizing and riding waves of creative energy
money_quotes: Right now, Omarchy feels like that chance. Like Linux fluttered into my hands and said "let's take a trip to the moon".
What a mesmerizing mental image of a chance, fluttering about.
That's the feeling I have when I wake up in the morning at the moment: The Quickening.
There's something so exciting here, so energizing, that I simply must get to the keyboard and chase wherever it flutters to.
A poetic reminder of what being open to chance looks like, and what to do when it shows up.
posts.key_insight: When a creative opportunity arrives with unmistakable energy, the only right response is to seize it completely and follow wherever it leads.

posts.summary: DHH announces that Omarchy, 37signals' Linux distribution, has micro-forked Chromium to add a single feature needed for live theming. The entire process from idea to release took just four days, demonstrating the practical power of open source. The fork is available as an AUR package for any Arch Linux user. DHH uses this as a concrete example that open source's promise of 'you can change anything' is not merely theoretical but achievable with skill, will, and perhaps a bounty.
key_points: Omarchy micro-forked Chromium to add live theming support, completing the entire process in four days
The fork is publicly available on AUR, making it accessible to any Arch Linux user
Open source power is practical, not theoretical — even massive projects like Chromium can be modified and shipped quickly
A $5,000 bounty was used to incentivize the patch, showing pragmatic approaches to open source contribution
money_quotes: You can just change things! That's the power of open source.
But for a lot of people, it might seem like a theoretical power. Can you really change, say, Chrome? Well, yes!
There are no speed limits in open source. Nobody to ask for permission.
You have the code, so you can make the change. All you need is skill and will.
We got it all done in just four days. From idea, to solicitation, to successful patch, to release, to incorporation.
posts.key_insight: Open source's promise that you can change anything — even something as massive as Chromium — is not theoretical but practically achievable with skill, will, and urgency.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Danish culture's ubiquitous and casual use of the national flag for everyday occasions like birthdays and airport arrivals, contrasting it with the US and UK where national symbols have become politically coded. He argues that progressive Americans have ceded the flag to the right wing, and that in the UK, flying the English flag has become almost transgressive. He concludes that national pride and strong national identity are prerequisites for building high-trust societies.
key_points: Danish culture integrates the national flag into everyday celebrations like birthdays and homecomings, making it a universal rather than political symbol
The American flag has become 'right-wing coded,' with progressives abandoning national symbols rather than reclaiming them
Criticizing your country is healthy, but tipping into hatred of national symbols undermines the ability to actually improve things
The UK has gone further, where flying the English flag or expressing certain political views risks being treated as transgressive or even criminal
National pride and shared identity are presented as foundations of high-trust societies
money_quotes: What a shame to feel such shame about the country you live in.
Being a Dane means something to the Danish. It's a unique identity, separate from everyone else in the world. It's local, it's close, it's personal.
It seems like the American flag, for example, has now been solidly right-wing coded.
Once that search for better tips over into disliking or outright hating the national symbols, you're off the rails, and much less likely to actually fix anything.
National pride is a cornerstone of building a high-trust society.
It seems flying the English flag is now as transgressive as posting you're not a big fan of mass immigration on Facebook.
posts.key_insight: When a society's progressive citizens abandon national symbols out of shame, they cede patriotism to one political faction and erode the shared identity that high-trust societies depend on.

posts.summary: DHH announces the release of Omarchy 2.0, his Linux distribution built on Arch and Hyprland, timed to coincide with Linux's 34th birthday. He reflects on how Linux has matured into a polished, capable desktop environment that rivals Apple's ecosystem. What started as a post-install script has grown into a full ISO with a dedicated package repository and a community of thousands. He invites curious users to try a different computing experience, provided they're willing to learn.
key_points: Omarchy 2.0 released on Linux's 34th birthday as a tribute to the open-source OS
DHH spent over 30 years in Apple's ecosystem before discovering Linux desktop had surpassed his expectations
Omarchy evolved from a post-install script into a full Linux distribution with ISO, package repo, and active community
Over twenty releases since late June demonstrate rapid iteration and community collaboration
money_quotes: Not only does Linux run 95% of all servers on the web, billions of devices as an embedded OS, but it also turns out to be an incredible desktop environment!
It's crazy that it took me more than thirty years to realize this, but while I spent time in Apple's walled garden, the free software alternative simply grew better, stronger, and faster.
The Linux of 2025 is not the Linux of the 90s or the 00s or even the 10s. It's shockingly more polished, capable, and beautiful.
What began as a post-install script has turned into a full-blown ISO, dedicated package repository, and flourishing community of thousands of enthusiasts all collaborating on making it better.

If you've been curious about giving Linux a try, you're not afraid of an operating system that asks you to level up and learn a little, and you want to see what a totally different computing experience can look and feel like, I invite you to give it a go.
posts.key_insight: Modern Linux has quietly evolved into a polished desktop OS that can rival proprietary alternatives, and DHH's Omarchy project aims to make that accessible to adventurous users.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the best engineering teams run their dependencies on edge (unreleased versions) in production, actively contributing to the frameworks they use rather than passively consuming them. He points to Rails 8.1 as proof, where Shopify, GitHub, and 37signals ran pre-release code in production for nearly a year before the beta announcement. He contends this practice transforms teams from open source consumers into real-time co-creators, producing a step function improvement in engineering competence. The post is a call to action for more companies to adopt this frontier approach.
key_points: Running production code on edge (unreleased versions) is how the best teams take ownership of their tools and foster engineering excellence
Shopify, GitHub, and 37signals ran Rails 8.1 in production for almost a year before beta release, proving edge adoption works at massive scale
Being a real-time co-creator of open source rather than just a consumer represents a step function in team competence
Programmers who can directly influence and immediately use improvements to their tools are more motivated, go deeper, and build connections with experts elsewhere
money_quotes: The best engineering teams take control of their tools.
Do you think bugs either just magically appear or disappear? No, they're put there by programmers and removed by the very same.
If you want bug-free frameworks and libraries, you have to work for it, but if you do, the reward for your responsibility is increased engineering excellence.
One that isn't just a consumer of open source software, but a real-time co-creator. This is a step function in competence and prowess for any team.

When your programmers are able to directly influence the tools they're working with, they're far more likely to do so, and thus they go deeper, learn more, and create connections to experts in the same situation elsewhere.
It doesn't work if you sit around waiting patiently for the next release before you dare dive in.
posts.key_insight: Engineering excellence comes not from safely consuming stable releases but from running on edge, finding bugs yourself, and becoming a co-creator of the open source tools you depend on.

posts.summary: DHH recaps the third Rails World conference in Amsterdam, celebrating its growing success with tickets selling out in under two minutes and expanded capacity. He reflects on the thriving Rails ecosystem, noting an influx of new programmers and renewed excitement around the framework. He credits multiple factors including his Lex Fridman appearance, Shopify's success, and the Rails Foundation's execution, but ultimately attributes the momentum to Rails simply being good. He announces next year's conference will move to Austin with dramatically expanded capacity, and expresses deep personal gratitude for finding his life's work early in his career.
key_points: Rails World tickets sold out in under two minutes, up from 45 minutes at the first edition, showing surging demand
Rails is experiencing a generational renewal with new programmers discovering the framework alongside veterans who've used it for decades
The Rails Foundation, founded in 2022, has proven its value through professional conference execution and community building
Next year's Rails World moves to Austin with plans to dramatically expand capacity as RailsConf has ended
DHH attributes Rails' current momentum to multiple factors but insists the core reason is that Rails is simply really good
money_quotes: Not everyone is so lucky as I've been to find their life's work early in their career, and see it continue to blossom over the decades. I'm eternally grateful that I have.
There are many auxiliary reasons why we're riding a wave at the moment, but key to it all is also that Ruby on Rails is simply really, really good!
It actually took guts to sign on back then. I approached quite a few companies at that time who could see the value, but couldn't find the courage to support our work.
We're enjoying a period of peak unity, excitement, progress, and determination to continue to push for end-to-end problem solving, open source, and freedom.
It's an honor and a privilege. I hope to carry this meaningful burden for as long as my intellectual legs still let me stand.
This year, tickets sold out in less than two minutes.
posts.key_insight: Rails is experiencing a genuine renaissance driven by new developer adoption, institutional maturity through the Rails Foundation, and the framework's enduring quality after 22 years.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on thirty years of internet debate and argues that words must never be equated with violence. He recounts how the 2021 37signals controversy exposed him to real threats and mob behavior, which changed him but also prompted self-reflection about his own rhetorical sharpness. He condemns the trend of labeling ideological opponents as nazis or fascists to justify violent rhetoric, citing the morbid celebrations following Charlie Kirk's death as a sickening example. He concludes by urging people to keep showing up for earnest debate rather than retreating from public discourse.
key_points: The modern conflation of words with violence creates a dangerous permission structure where deep disagreement is used to justify actual violence
The 2021 37signals controversy was a personal turning point that revealed how online mobs use ideological disagreement to threaten and intimidate
Labeling opponents as 'nazis' or 'fascists' to rationalize violent fantasies has become normalized even in technology communities
The celebration of Charlie Kirk's death by technologists exemplifies the moral decay of treating political opponents as subhuman
The correct response to intimidation is not retreat but renewed commitment to good-faith debate
money_quotes: What I cannot come to terms with, though, is the modern equation of words with violence.
The growing sense of permission that if the disagreement runs deep enough, then violence is a justified answer to settle it.

There are plenty of factions here who've taken to justify their violent fantasies by referring to their ideological opponents as 'nazis', 'fascists', or 'racists'. And then follow that up with a call to 'punch a nazi' or worse.
It's sickening. Deeply, profoundly sickening.
My first instinct was exactly what such people would delight in happening. To watch the rest of us recoil, then retract, and perhaps even eject.
Instead, we should double down on the opposite. Continue to show up with our ideals held high while we debate strangers in that noble search for the truth.
The earnest exploration of the topics in a live exchange with another human is as fundamental to our civilization as Socrates himself.
Don't give up, don't give in. Keep debating.
posts.key_insight: Equating speech with violence erodes the foundation of civilized debate, and the proper response to intimidation is not silence but a renewed commitment to showing up for honest argument.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Apple has lost its ability to maintain product quality because there's no one left in leadership willing to say no to subpar releases. He uses CarPlay Ultra's buggy, laggy launch as the primary example, alongside Apple Intelligence's underwhelming gimmicks, pulled advertisements, and the persistent iPhone alarm bug. He attributes the decline to the absence of founder-led leadership obsessed with quality, contrasting Tim Cook's logistics-oriented management with Steve Jobs' product perfectionism. His conclusion is blunt: Cook has been a 'bozo on product' and needs to go.
key_points: CarPlay Ultra shipped in a laggy, buggy, and potentially dangerous state despite being the salvageable remnant of the $10 billion Project Titan failure
Apple's quality problems are systemic — from pulled ads to underwhelming Apple Intelligence to a years-old iPhone alarm bug that still hasn't been fixed
The root cause isn't bugs themselves but a leadership vacuum where no one takes ownership or has the authority to halt bad releases
Founder-led companies tend to produce better products because founders feel personal pain when quality slips
Tim Cook excelled as a logistics operator under Jobs but has failed as a product and quality leader
money_quotes: Apple no longer has anyone left who can say no.

I guarantee you there are programmers and designers inside Apple who know CarPlay Ultra wasn't ready to ship, but were overruled by managers who felt they needed to stick to their contractual obligations, quality be damned.
That's what happens when there's a lack of leadership who actually care about quality, about customers, and about the product.
Nobody reaches for the emergency brake, nobody wants to take responsibility to avoid disaster.
The problem is not the bug, it's the lack of ownership.
When we talk the type that has the criticality of making someone miss a flight or lose the gauge cluster on the road at night, you need to treat that like a CODE RED.
If you leave the bozos in charge for too long, the entire organization will be shaped in their image.
Tim Cook was a masterful logistics hand to Jobs, but he's been a bozo on product, quality, and care. He's gotta go.
posts.key_insight: Apple's quality decline isn't a talent problem but a leadership problem — without someone at the top who feels personal pain over shipping broken products, no organization can maintain its standards.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on how London has changed since his youth, arguing that mass immigration has transformed the city beyond recognition, with native Brits dropping from over 60% to about a third of the population. He expresses sympathy for Tommy Robinson's march and the broader British frustration over demographic change and erosion of free speech, citing police arrests for online speech and the Pakistani grooming gang scandals. He draws parallels to Denmark, where the Social Democrat prime minister admitted that assumptions about immigrant integration were wrong. He concludes with encouragement that Britain can course-correct, pointing to Denmark's political evolution as a model.
key_points: London's native British population dropped from over 60% in 2000 to roughly a third by 2024, fundamentally changing the city's character
British authorities have become increasingly censorious, making 30 arrests per day for online speech while failing to address street crime and grooming gangs
Denmark's Social Democrat prime minister represents a template for mainstream politicians acknowledging that assumptions about mass immigration and integration were wrong
The 'far right' label has lost its power as a political weapon because it's been applied too broadly to mainstream positions
National identity and demographic continuity are legitimate concerns, not inherently racist or xenophobic
money_quotes: You can sometimes be forgiven for thinking that all of Britain is lost in self-loathing, shame, and suicidal empathy. But of course it's not.

British police are now making 30 arrests a day for wrongthink, wrongspeech, and other online transgressions against 'the regime narrative', as the BBC would have reported, if this were a statistic from a foreign nation.
Like hurling 'nazi' and 'fascist' at the most middle-of-the-road political figures and positions, it's finally lost its power.

There's absolutely nothing racist or xenophobic in saying that Denmark is primarily a country for the Danes, Britain primarily a united kingdom for the Brits, and Japan primarily a set of islands for the Japanese.
Not due to some unavoidable cosmic destiny, but due to equal parts policy and apathy.
Unwilling to just let their society wither away while their bobbies chase bad tweets instead of the rampant street thefts or those barbaric rape gangs.
Don't give up. You survived the Blitz. Britain will be back.
posts.key_insight: The demographic transformation of London and the British state's suppression of dissent represent a failure of policy that mainstream politics must confront honestly, as Denmark's Social Democrats eventually did.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Boeing, Intel, and Apple all followed the same pattern of decline: installing CEOs without engineering or product backgrounds, then watching the company hollow out over roughly a decade. Boeing's troubles accelerated after 2005 with cost-cutting and outsourcing that led to the 737 MAX tragedies. Intel similarly installed a non-engineer CEO in 2005 and now teeters on the edge. Apple under Tim Cook initially appeared immune thanks to Jobs's pipeline, but the ten-year curse has arrived with failed bets on self-driving cars, embarrassing AI efforts, and the Vision Pro flop. DHH concludes that great companies need hands-on leaders who live and breathe their products, not bean counters who delegate product vision down the org chart.
key_points: Boeing, Intel, and Apple each declined roughly ten years after installing CEOs without engineering or product backgrounds
Apple's strong product pipeline from the Jobs era temporarily masked Cook's limitations, but failures in AI, the car project, and Vision Pro reveal the hollowing out
Boards of public companies mistakenly believe product care and competence can be delegated while the CEO focuses on hitting numbers
Profits from past glories can mask cultural rot for years before the decline becomes undeniable
Great companies need leaders like Andy Grove or Scott Forstall who are professionally invested in the work itself
money_quotes: It takes ten years for the culture of a great company to fall apart once the CEO seat is given to someone without an engineering or product background.
Legendary American companies that all got lost when a bean counter, marketing man, or logistics hand took over.
The profits still gush from glories past, and the tollbooth operation on the App Store, but the soul has left the machine.
Great companies need bold, hands-on leaders who live and breathe the stuff they make or they'll eventually hollow out.

It's tempting for boards of public companies to think that all care and competence around product can be delegated down the org chart. That someone who can hit the numbers is all you need at the top. But it's not.
You need someone so professionally invested in the work and the culture that they'll refuse to let the search for surface-level efficiencies drain the foundation of its strength.
You need an engineer or a product person as CEO.
posts.key_insight: When companies replace visionary engineer-CEOs with operations-focused leaders, they may coast on past momentum for a decade, but the inevitable cultural hollowing will eventually destroy what made them great.

posts.summary: DHH argues that labeling political opponents as 'nazis' is an escalation tactic by what he calls the 'woke' movement, intended to justify violence against those who resist their orthodoxies. He frames this as a desperate response to declining institutional power, citing the dismantling of DEI programs, the shift in social media dynamics, and the 2024 political realignment. He contends that accusations of racism and misogyny no longer carry the social consequences they once did, so critics have escalated to the most extreme label available. DHH urges readers not to be intimidated, arguing that the movement behind these tactics is in decline.
key_points: The 'nazi' label functions as implicit authorization of violence against the accused, building on the 'punch a nazi' slogan
This escalation is a response to declining institutional leverage: DEI dismantlement, loss of social media narrative control, and political shifts
Previous accusations of racism and misogyny have lost their power to inflict professional and social consequences
The threat of violence is the 'final resort' of a movement that has lost political and philosophical ground
DHH frames resistance to these labels as both necessary and achievable, pointing to prior victories against 'wokeism in the workplace'
money_quotes: Calling someone a "nazi" is a permission slip for violence.
If you can't intimidate people into silence and compliance with the woke orthodoxies by threatening their job or their social circle, you might be able to threaten them with actual violence or worse.
The threat of violence, however, is evergreen. It's the final resort of a movement that has lost a political and philosophical path to victory in the public square.
Lost terrain means lost leverage. Which means the usual threats have stopped working because they relied on that institutional and broad social leverage to be effective.
The defeat of wokeism in the workplace should give you comfort. These people are not invincible.
The wheels have been falling off their political project for years now. You can and should say "no" when they come with the "nazi" nonsense too.
posts.key_insight: DHH frames the escalation to 'nazi' accusations as a sign of weakness from a declining political movement that has lost its institutional leverage and now resorts to implicit threats of violence.

posts.summary: DHH describes the latest failed attempt by Ruby community critics to 'cancel' him from Rails, contrasting it with a successful effort in 2022 when Ruby Central uninvited him from RailsConf. He argues that this time the campaign backfired spectacularly, with thousands of people publicly rallying to his defense on X. He frames this as the collapse of 'preference falsification,' where a silent majority finally feels empowered to push back against what he characterizes as a small group of aggrieved activists. He concludes with a dismissive call to ignore the critics and keep building.
key_points: A group of Ruby community members launched another campaign against DHH, naming their effort after a French WWII resistance action, which DHH mocks as absurd overreach
Unlike 2022 when he was successfully uninvited from RailsConf, this attempt was met with overwhelming public support for DHH from prominent tech figures
DHH frames the shift as the collapse of 'preference falsification' — people were always on his side but previously afraid to say so publicly
One of the campaign organizers expressed sadness that the Ruby community responded more negatively than positively, questioning whether 'MINASWAN' was always a lie
DHH's prescribed response is to reject, ignore, and keep building — treating the critics as irrelevant
money_quotes: Every few years, the same sad contingent of Ruby malcontents tries to cancel me from Rails.
Now RailsConf is dead, Rails World is thriving, and the cancellation nonsense is over.
This is what it looks like when preference falsification finally falls.
When normal people are no longer afraid to say no to these people. Then it's revealed just how small and isolated these aggrieved individuals actually are.
I guess I would be sad too if I had named my group after THE GOOD GUYS and then it turned out that everyone thought I was THE BAD GUYS.
We're going to reject and ignore these nut jobs. Then we're going to keep building.
posts.key_insight: DHH declares victory in the culture war within the Ruby community, arguing that the collapse of preference falsification has revealed his critics to be a tiny, powerless minority.

posts.summary: DHH argues that knowledge workers must prioritize personally meaningful work over the endless stream of emails, meetings, and updates that could consume their entire workweek. He shares his own practice of frequently skipping check-ins to dive into programming, experimenting, and researching. He frames this as a virtuous cycle: by solving your own problems and chasing your own curiosity, you develop competency that earns you more privilege to do even more meaningful work. The post rejects any guilt about this privilege and urges readers to carve out time for their own intellectual priorities.
key_points: Reactive work (emails, meetings, updates) will expand to fill all available time if you let it
Staying sharp requires prioritizing work that is personally meaningful over organizational busywork
Pursuing your own curiosity builds competency, which earns more autonomy — a virtuous cycle of merit
Privilege to do meaningful work should be embraced and expanded, not apologized for
money_quotes: There'll always be more emails in need of reply, more meetings to attend, and more updates to read. A person can fill the entire workweek with these tasks over and over again.
To stay sane and sharp, you must pay yourself first by doing the work that actually means something to you.
So quite frequently, I just don't. Don't check in, don't check up, and instead dive into the work that checks my own intellectual boxes.
In another age, I might have been tempted to apologize for such privilege, but screw that. Privilege is wonderful.
By solving your own problems, tickling your own interests, chasing your own curiosity. That's where you'll find the motivation to elevate your talent.
This is the virtuous circle of merit.
You'll never get through it all and onto your own priorities, if you continue to put them at the bottom.
posts.key_insight: Prioritizing personally meaningful work over reactive busywork isn't selfish — it's the engine that builds competency and earns greater autonomy.

posts.summary: DHH argues that the hand-wringing over AI-generated content ('slop') ignores the fact that humans have been producing equally terrible content ('sludge') for years. He points to SEO-driven content mills, clickbait, and conversion-optimized garbage as proof that human-made content was already degraded. He contends that letting AI handle this soul-crushing work is actually more humane than forcing people to produce it. The post concludes with a fatalistic observation that most people won't opt out of consuming low-quality content regardless of whether humans or machines produce it.
key_points: Human-produced SEO content, clickbait, and conversion-optimized writing is just as bad as AI slop — and more degrading because it wastes human potential
Letting machines produce low-quality content is more humane than forcing humans to do soul-crushing creative drudgery
The demand for endless low-quality content isn't going away — people's screen time and consumption habits guarantee a market for it
The option to disengage from low-quality content has always existed, but most people won't take it
money_quotes: It's an affront to humanity to make sentient beings do this work.
Turning human potential, creativity, and ingenuity into content sludge is a process no more dignified than turning pink slime into chicken nuggets.
