Let the little robots barf up tokens to unlock the next basis point of incremental conversion. Better them than us, I say.
This is exactly the soul-crushing, creative drudgery that machines were made to munch through without complaint.
But couldn't we do without sludge or slop, you say? Sure, right after we reach a shared state of nirvana.
So stop whining about the AI slop. You're already steeped in human sludge.
The door to exit both was always there. But you're not going to open it, are you?
posts.key_insight: AI slop is a more humane replacement for the soul-crushing content drudgery humans were already doing, and the people complaining about it were never going to stop consuming garbage content anyway.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Omarchy, his new Linux distribution, hitting a petabyte of downloads (about 150,000 installs) in its first month. He argues this explosive growth represents genuine product-market fit, driven by Apple and Microsoft alienating developers and computer enthusiasts through forced obsolescence, AI bloat, and declining software quality. He makes the case that Linux has never been more viable as a desktop OS because web apps have replaced most native software that once kept people tethered to Windows or Mac. Omarchy succeeds by packaging Arch Linux and the Hyprland window manager into an opinionated but fully customizable distribution that removes the traditional gatekeeping around Linux setup.
key_points: Omarchy grew faster than any open-source project DHH has been involved with, including Ruby on Rails, reaching 150,000 installs in its first month
Apple and Microsoft are driving users away by cutting off older hardware, forcing AI features, and declining in software quality
The historic gap in native GUI apps that kept people on Windows/Mac has nearly closed because the web has become the dominant computing platform
Linux offers superior developer performance — a $500 Beelink Mini PC can compete with an M4 Max machine costing thousands for real-world workloads
Omarchy's appeal lies in combining preconfigured usability with full user agency, rejecting both corporate lock-in and Linux gatekeeping culture
money_quotes: This is what product-market fit looks like. Doesn't matter if the product is free or not. The fit is obvious.
Microsoft is killing off Windows 10, which in turn cuts off a whole slew of perfectly fine computers made prior to around 2017–2018.
A Mac Mini sold as recently as 2023 is now end-of-life!
The historic gap in native GUI apps has never mattered less either. The web has conquered all as the dominant computing platform.
In the past, missing, say, Photoshop was a big deal. Now it's Figma — a web app! — that's driving designers.
The argument that owning your computer, fully and deeply, is starting to resonate.

All it took was someone to actually put all the pieces together, ignore the Linux neckbeards who insist you aren't worthy to run Arch or Hyprland without spending a hundred hours setting it up from scratch, and invite everyone to the party!
posts.key_insight: Apple and Microsoft's tightening grip on their platforms has created a historic opening for Linux, and Omarchy proves that packaging an opinionated, beautiful experience — rather than demanding users earn their way in — is what mainstream Linux adoption actually needed.

posts.summary: DHH reflects on the inevitability of haters emerging whenever a project achieves real success, prompted by the growing backlash against his new Omarchy project. He recalls experiencing the same phenomenon twenty years ago with Ruby on Rails, when he naively believed logical arguments could convert critics. Citing Kathy Sierra's framework of love-vs-hate as opposing forces, he argues that passionate supporters and detractors are inseparable — you can't have one without the other. He concludes that haters should be seen as a milestone worth celebrating, since their presence signals you've created something that actually matters.
key_points: Success inevitably generates both passionate fans and passionate detractors — they are inseparable forces
Trying to logically argue with haters is naive and futile; DHH learned this lesson over twenty years
The safe middle ground where nobody hates you is also where nobody cares about your work
Not all criticism comes from haters, but once sufficient success is achieved, a large portion will
The arrival of haters should be celebrated as a milestone proving your creation matters
money_quotes: Nothing of note in this world is allowed to succeed without spawning a counteracting force of haters.

Back then I still thought you could argue your way to understanding. That if you just made a logical case to counter whatever objections were raised, you'd be able to persuade most haters to change their perspective. How naive.
If you want people to love your creation, you have to accept the opposing force. Yin and yang.
It's safe there in the gray middle. Nobody is mad at you, nobody is making any bad-faith arguments, but also, nobody cares.
When escape velocity is achieved, you can't avoid drawing energy from both sides.
Have you even made something worth cheering for, if there isn't a contingent there to boo at it too? Probably not.
So embrace the boos as you embrace the cheers. They come as a pair.
posts.key_insight: Haters aren't a problem to solve but a signal to celebrate — their emergence proves your work has escaped the gray zone of irrelevance.

posts.summary: DHH argues that sabbaticals are a simple but powerful tool for employee retention in tech, where the average tenure is just 18 months because workers quit to get extended breaks. 37signals has offered six-week sabbaticals every three years for fifteen years, which he credits with keeping attrition low. He positions this as a way to combine American-level tech pay with European-style time off. He also argues sabbaticals benefit founders by curing burnout that might otherwise lead them to sell their companies prematurely.
key_points: The 18-month average tenure in tech is partly driven by workers quitting just to get a long break, which sabbaticals could prevent
A six-week break allows a mental reset that two-week vacations cannot achieve — you need enough time to actually get bored
The retention argument is purely economic: replacing smart, trained people is expensive
Sabbaticals cure founder burnout that leads to premature company sales — by week five, most founders are antsy to return
American tech workers can have both high pay and regular resets without adopting the European model of lower compensation
money_quotes: The only way many tech workers in the US can get a long break is by quitting their job.
It's been magical for retention because a break like that allows the mind to reset in a way a two-week vacation never could.
I think it's entirely possible to have it both ways: Work for an American tech company with American pay levels, but also enjoy a regular full reset, without having to quit to get it.
It's very expensive to see smart, trained people walk out the door.
Six weeks is also just long enough to remind tired founders that selling their company isn't likely to be the bliss they imagine.
There are endless stories of founders who regret selling their business when all they needed was a six-week break from the startup sprint.
Not just two weeks on Mallorca, but time enough to get bored.
posts.key_insight: Sabbaticals are a cheap retention tool that prevents both employees from quitting and founders from selling — most people don't want to escape work permanently, they just need enough time off to get bored and hungry for it again.

posts.summary: DHH explains why he stopped doing full-system backups, instead treating every computer as a stateless, disposable unit. By storing all important data in Dropbox and code in Git, he maintains distributed local copies across multiple machines. This approach means any single machine can be lost or destroyed without consequence. He extended this philosophy to create Omarchy, encoding his entire system configuration into an ISO that installs in two minutes.
key_points: Every machine should be treated as a stateless, disposable unit with full-disk encryption
Dropbox and Git provide distributed local copies — not just cloud storage — so you're protected even if the services go offline
The discipline required is routing all important data through Dropbox; everything outside it is considered temporary and disposable
System configuration should be codified too, which motivated the creation of Omarchy as a two-minute install ISO
money_quotes: Every machine I now own is treated as a stateless, disposable unit that can be stolen, lost, or corrupted without consequences.
The combination of full-disk encryption and distributed copies of all important data means there's just no stress if anything bad happens to the computer.
But don't mistake this for just a 'everything is in the cloud' argument.
Everything outside of Dropbox is essentially treated as a temporary directory that's fully disposable.
It seemed so unreasonable that the configuration needed for a fully functional system still took hours on end.
There ought to be no reason why a busted computer is a calamity of data loss or a long restore process.
posts.key_insight: By treating every computer as disposable and routing all important data through distributed sync services, you can eliminate the need for traditional backups entirely.

posts.summary: DHH argues that local LLMs are the modern nerd's excuse to buy expensive hardware they don't actually need. While acknowledging the technical achievement of running AI models locally, he contends that local models are vastly inferior to frontier cloud models and remain a curiosity rather than a practical tool. He suggests developers are fooling themselves when evaluating computers based on local LLM performance. To prove his point, he's switched from a $2,000 Framework Desktop to a $500 mini PC and barely notices the difference in daily work.
key_points: Local LLMs are impressive technically but vastly behind frontier cloud models for practical developer work
Evaluating hardware purchases based on local model performance is misguided since all local models underperform
Rising RAM prices driven by AI demand make overpowered hardware even less justifiable
Most developers can get by with far less computing power than they think, especially on Linux
DHH personally switched from a $2,000 desktop to a $500 mini PC with barely noticeable difference
money_quotes: Local LLMs are how nerds now justify a big computer they don't need
Let's get real here: they're all vastly behind the frontier models available for rent, and thus for most developers a curiosity at best.
But that day is not today.
I find it spurious to hear developers evaluate their next computer on the prospect of how well it's capable of running local models. Because they all suck!
Whether one sucks a little less than the other doesn't really matter.
It means you really don't need a 128GB VRAM computer on your desk.
I bet you likely need way less than you think too.
posts.key_insight: Developers using local LLM performance to justify expensive hardware are rationalizing a want as a need, since cloud frontier models are superior and most dev work requires far less compute than people assume.

posts.summary: DHH celebrates Shopify's record-breaking $6.2 billion Black Friday sales as validation of both the company and the Ruby on Rails ecosystem. He highlights the massive technical scale—31 million API requests per minute—as proof that Rails can handle frontier-level loads. DHH argues that Shopify's dominance in the Rails and Ruby communities should be seen as a blessing, not a concern, citing contributions like YJIT and Ractor improvements. He credits Shopify's success to founder-led leadership, noting that CEO Tobi Lütke still actively programs. While acknowledging his bias as a Shopify board member and friend of Tobi, DHH maintains that the company deserves celebration as the 'patron saint' of Ruby on Rails.
key_points: Shopify processed $6.2 billion in Black Friday sales (up 25% YoY), with databases handling 53 million reads and 2 million writes per second
Shopify's dominance in Rails and Ruby core teams should be celebrated, not feared, because their contributions (YJIT, Ractors, battle-tested releases) benefit the entire ecosystem
Founder-led companies produce unique advantages—Tobi Lütke still actively programs despite leading a $200 billion company
The alignment of a massive, successful company contributing back to open source is rare and shouldn't be taken for granted
money_quotes: Just crazy high growth on a crazy big base. The law of big numbers clearly hasn't found a way to apply itself here yet!
It's this kind of frontier load and criticality that makes Shopify the ideal patron saint of the Rails framework and the Ruby programming language.
How many CEOs of companies worth two hundred billion dollars still program like that?
Seeing this as anything but a blessing is silly, though.
Any programming community should be so lucky as to have a Shopify!
I'm both socially and economically incentivized to cheer for this extraordinary company. But that doesn't mean it isn't all true too!
Its infrastructure team is the backbone of our ecosystem, and its continued success the best case study of how far you can take this framework and language.
posts.key_insight: Shopify's massive scale and continued open-source contributions make it an unparalleled benefactor to the Ruby on Rails ecosystem, and the community should embrace rather than resent its dominance.

posts.summary: DHH introduces Fizzy, 37signals' new Kanban tool, positioning it as a fresh, simplified take on a concept that has grown cumbersome in existing tools. The product is available both as a paid SaaS and as source-available code under the O'Saasy License, which allows self-hosting but reserves commercial SaaS rights. He argues that software ecosystems need recurring cycles of renewal as even great products accumulate cruft over time. The post emphasizes data portability between SaaS and self-hosted instances, and celebrates the View Source ethos applied to backend code. DHH frames Fizzy as both a useful product and a learning resource for modern Rails development.
key_points: Good software ideas inevitably accumulate cruft and complexity, creating a need for periodic fresh resets of proven concepts
Fizzy uses the O'Saasy License — functionally MIT but with a carve-out reserving SaaS commercialization rights for the creators
Data portability between SaaS and self-hosted instances gives users an escape hatch from vendor lock-in
The full source code and pull request history serves as a learning resource for modern Rails application development
Community contributions flow into both the SaaS product and self-hosted versions equally
money_quotes: Very little software is ever the final word on solving interesting problems.
Even products that start out with great promise and simplicity tend to accumulate cruft and complexity over time. A healthy ecosystem needs a recurring cycle of renewal.
Good ideas can grow cumbersome and unwieldy surprisingly quickly. Fizzy is a fresh reset of an old idea. We need more of that.
That means it's not technically Open Source™, but the source sure is open.
In an age where SaaS companies come and go, pivot one way or the other, I think it's a great reassurance that the source code is freely available.
I'm also just a huge fan of being able to View Source.
posts.key_insight: Software ecosystems need recurring cycles of renewal, and combining source-available code with SaaS gives users both convenience and the reassurance of data portability and self-hosting as an escape hatch.

posts.summary: DHH argues that Europe is suffering from a dangerous gap between its self-image and reality across multiple dimensions: free speech, tech competitiveness, energy policy, defense spending, and economic growth. He frames the EU's €120 million fine on X as emblematic of censorship rather than consumer protection, noting thousands of yearly arrests across the UK, Germany, and France for online speech. He highlights Europe's failure to produce major tech companies, its stagnating GDP relative to America's, and its dependency on others for energy and security. Despite the grim assessment, he concludes Europe isn't doomed if it pursues nuclear energy, merit-based immigration, deregulation, and drops its censorship apparatus.
key_points: The EU's fine on X is selective enforcement serving censorship goals, since Meta runs the same verification scheme without consequences
Europe's tech sector is so anemic that EU tech fines generated more revenue than all income taxes paid by European public internet companies in 2024
Germany hasn't grown real GDP in five years, partly due to energy costs 2-3x those of the US and China after spending €700 billion on green energy
The EU-US GDP gap has widened from near-parity in 2008 to America being 50% larger, with growth trajectories suggesting America's economy could be nearly twice Europe's within a decade
Europe retains enormous human capital potential but its policy environment drives talent emigration rather than retention
money_quotes: The gap between Europe's self-image and reality has grown into a chasm of delulu.
The EU's tech-fine operation produced more income for European coffers than all the income taxes paid by its public internet tech companies in 2024.
Europe basically stopped creating new, large companies more than half a century ago.

These should all be sobering numbers to any European. Whether it's the 10,000 yearly arrests in the UK for social media posts or the risk of an economy that's half the size of the American one in a decade.

There are plenty of reasons to prefer living in Europe, but that doesn't offset the fact that America is simply a vastly richer country, and that matters when it comes to everything from commercial dominance to military power.
There are plenty of paths to take that do not end in a low-growth, censorious regime that continues to export many of its best brains to America and elsewhere.
posts.key_insight: Europe's compounding failures in free speech, tech competitiveness, energy policy, and defense spending are creating dangerous dependencies, but reversing course on regulation, energy, and immigration could still avert decline.

posts.summary: DHH introduces the O'Saasy License, a new open source license modeled on MIT but with SaaS commercial rights reserved for the copyright holder. He nostalgically recalls the early web's View Source culture and argues that back-end code for commercial applications has always been hidden. The license aims to encourage more production-grade code to be open sourced by letting creators retain SaaS revenue while making code freely available for learning, self-hosting, and contribution. He announces that Fizzy uses this license and that it now has its own home at osaasy.dev.
key_points: The early web's View Source culture was invaluable for learning but only ever applied to front-end code
The O'Saasy License is MIT-like but reserves SaaS commercial rights for the copyright holder, creating a middle ground between fully open and proprietary
Production-grade open source code is needed to teach both junior developers and LLMs
The license enables a 'view source for the back-end' while still allowing creators to sustain their business
money_quotes: Before View Source was ruined by minification, transpiling, and bundling, you really could just right-click on any web page and learn how it was all done. It was glorious.
Learning how to write great web applications still meant piecing together lessons from books, tutorials, and hello-world-style code examples, not from production-grade commercial software.
It's basically the do-whatever-you-want MIT license, but with the commercial rights to run the software as a service (SaaS) reserved for the copyright holder.
We need more production-grade code to teach juniors and LLMs alike.
A view source that extends to the back-end along with the open source invitation to fix bugs, propose features, and run the system yourself for free.
posts.key_insight: A license that reserves only SaaS rights while opening everything else can unlock production-grade code for learning and contribution without sacrificing the creator's business model.

posts.summary: DHH describes his evolving relationship with AI coding agents, explaining that autonomous terminal-based agents finally won him over after he was cold on the earlier autocomplete-style AI coding pioneered by Copilot and Cursor. He compares working with agents to managing a team rather than pair programming with an overzealous partner. While he's ready to promote AI agents to producing production-grade code, he pushes back on claims of 90%+ AI-written code and pure vibe coding, positioning 'supervised collaboration' as the realistic current paradigm. He urges programmers not to tune out the AI revolution despite legitimate skepticism about hype.
key_points: Autonomous terminal-based AI agents represent a fundamentally different and superior experience compared to in-editor autocomplete tools like Copilot and Cursor
The key shift is from AI as pair programmer stealing your keyboard to AI as a team member doing autonomous work you review
Supervised collaboration is production-ready today, but pure vibe coding remains aspirational for professional work
Claims of 90%+ AI-written code are wildly overstated if you hold the line on quality and cohesion
The improvement trajectory from 2025 is the most exciting computing development since the internet in the '90s
money_quotes: Reality is fast catching the hype!
When I code, I want to finish my own thoughts and sentences.
It's more like working on a team and less like working with an overly-zealous pair programmer who can't stop stealing the keyboard to complete the code you were in the middle of writing.
They're fully capable of producing production-grade contributions to real-life code bases.
Yet pure vibe coding remains an aspirational dream for professional work for me, for now. Supervised collaboration, though, is here today.

I'm nowhere close to the claims of having agents write 90%+ of the code, as I see some boast about online. I don't know what code they're writing to hit those rates, but that's way off what I'm able to achieve, if I hold the line on quality and cohesion.
This is the most exciting thing we've made computers do since we connected them to the internet back in the '90s.
Download OpenCode, throw some real work at Opus or the others, and relish the privilege of being alive during the days we taught the machines how to think.
posts.key_insight: AI coding agents have earned a promotion from learning aids to production-grade team members, but supervised collaboration—not pure vibe coding—is the realistic paradigm for professional work today.

posts.summary: DHH shares his enthusiasm for cloud gaming, arguing it has finally reached a tipping point of quality and practicality. He draws parallels to how streaming displaced physical media for music and movies, noting that NVIDIA's GeForce NOW service now delivers impressive performance even for competitive shooters. He also highlights local game streaming via Apollo and Moonlight as a way to repurpose a powerful gaming PC as a household streaming server. The post positions cloud and local game streaming as a practical alternative to expensive dedicated hardware, especially for Linux users.
key_points: NVIDIA GeForce NOW has reached a quality threshold where even competitive shooters are playable at high resolution and frame rates with minimal input lag
Cloud gaming follows the same trajectory as music and movie streaming — practical convenience wins over ownership purists
At $20/month vs $2,000+ for a gaming PC, cloud gaming offers compelling economics with a 100-month payback period
Local game streaming via Apollo/Moonlight eliminates input lag entirely and lets any device in the house borrow a powerful GPU
Linux and Mac gamers benefit most since cloud/local streaming bypasses platform-specific limitations like anti-cheat software
money_quotes: But do you know what I like more than collecting? Playing! Anywhere. Anything. Anytime.
Not 'better' in some abstract philosophical way (ownership vs rent) or even in a concrete technical way (bit rates), but in a practical way.
Google Stadia appears to have been just a few years ahead of reality (eerie how often that happens for big G, like with both AI and AR!)
You can legitimately play Fortnite in 2880x1800 at 120 fps through a remote 4080, and it looks incredible.
Funny how NVIDIA is better at offering the promise of cheap cloud costs than the likes of AWS!
No need for a honking big PC on my primary desk. I did not know this was an option!!
posts.key_insight: Cloud and local game streaming have quietly crossed the quality threshold where they can replace dedicated gaming hardware for most players, following the same inevitability as music and movie streaming before them.

posts.summary: DHH describes his experience with OpenClaw, a tool that gives AI agents their own machine, persistent memory, and autonomous execution capabilities. He set up an agent named Kef on an isolated virtual machine and tested whether it could navigate human-designed web interfaces without any special accommodations like MCPs, APIs, or skills. The agent successfully signed up for a HEY email account, registered for Fizzy, created boards with content, and joined Basecamp—all by navigating the web like a human would. DHH argues this points to a future where AI agents won't need special machine-readable interfaces, drawing an analogy to self-driving cars dropping LIDAR in favor of vision-only systems.
key_points: OpenClaw gives AI agents persistent execution, long-term memory, and the ability to autonomously interact with web services on an ongoing basis
DHH's agent completed complex multi-step tasks (email signup, service registration, collaboration) with zero corrections or special API accommodations
The experiment worked across multiple models—both Claude Opus 4.5 and the open-weight Kimi K2.5—suggesting this isn't model-specific
DHH predicts that agent accommodations like MCPs and APIs are temporary crutches, analogous to LIDAR being dropped from self-driving cars
Human-designed web interfaces will be sufficient for AI agents to operate, making special machine accommodations unnecessary
money_quotes: The model is no longer confined to a prompt-response cycle, but able to check its own email, Basecamp notifications, and whatever else you give it access to on a running basis.
I didn't install any skills, any MCPs, or give it access to any APIs. Zero machine accommodations.
After it had procured its own email address, it continued on with the task of signing up for Fizzy.

I'm thoroughly impressed. All the agent accommodations, like MCPs/CLIs/APIs, probably still have a place for a bit longer, as doing all this work cold is both a bit slow and token-intensive. But I bet this is just a temporary crutch.
If I was going to skate to where the puck is going to be, it'd be a world where agents, like self-driving cars, don't need special equipment, like LIDAR or MCPs, to interact with the environment.
The human affordances will be more than adequate.
What a time to be alive.
posts.key_insight: AI agents can already navigate human-designed web interfaces without special accommodations, suggesting that purpose-built machine APIs for agents are a temporary crutch rather than the future.

posts.summary: DHH announces OMACON, the first in-person conference for the Omarchy Linux distribution community, to be held April 10 at Shopify's SoHo space in New York City. The event features speakers from the Linux and TUI ecosystem including Vaxry, ThePrimeagen, TJ DeVries, and Dax Raad. He highlights the rapidly growing momentum around Linux on the desktop, driven by improving x86 hardware, terminal UIs from tools like OpenCode and Claude Code, and the Omarchy project itself. DHH emphasizes the irreplaceable value of in-person community gatherings for nerds with shared passions, drawing parallels to Rails World. He celebrates Omarchy's growth from a summer project to 50,000 ISO downloads per week and 300+ contributors.
key_points: OMACON is the first in-person gathering for the Omarchy Linux community, limited to 130 attendees at $299 offered at cost
The Linux desktop ecosystem is experiencing a momentum shift driven by better x86 hardware, TUI renaissance via OpenCode/Claude Code, and Omarchy's omakase approach
Omarchy has grown rapidly since last summer to 50,000 ISO downloads per week, 30,000 Discord members, and 300+ contributors
In-person community connection remains scarce and valuable despite abundant online information
Shopify and CEO Tobi Lütke are hosting the event, which DHH praises as an example of nerd-led corporate generosity
money_quotes: There's an endless amount of information and instruction available online, but a sense of community and connection is far more scarce. We nerds need this.
We also need people to JUST DO THINGS.
This is open source at its best. People from all over, coming together, making cool shit.

You gotta love when a hundred-plus billion dollar company like this is run by an uber nerd who can just sign off on doing something fun and cool for the community without any direct plausible payback.
The vibes around Linux are changing fast. Companies of all shapes and sizes are paying fresh attention.
posts.key_insight: The Linux desktop is experiencing a genuine cultural moment, and Omarchy's rapid community growth proves that opinionated, aesthetics-first open source projects can galvanize passionate in-person communities.
